title: MergeDJD: A Fast Constructive Algorithm with Piece Merging for the Two-Dimensional Irregular Bin Packing Problem
summary: The two-dimensional irregular bin packing problem (2DIBPP) aims to pack a given set of irregular polygons, referred to as pieces, into fixed-size rectangular bins without overlap, while maximizing bin utilization. Although numerous metaheuristic algorithms have been proposed for the 2DIBPP, many industrial applications favor simpler constructive heuristics due to their deterministic behavior and low computational overhead. Among such methods, the DJD algorithm proposed by L'opez-Camacho et al. is one of the most competitive constructive heuristics for the 2DIBPP. However, DJD is less effective for cutting instances, in which many pieces can be seamlessly combined into larger polygons. To address the issue, we propose MergeDJD, a novel constructive algorithm that integrates and extends the DJD framework. MergeDJD first preprocesses the instance by iteratively identifying groups of pieces that can be combined into larger and more regular piece. It then employs an improved version of DJD, in which the placement strategy is enhanced to better handle non-convex and combined shapes, to pack all resulting pieces into bins. Computational experiments on 1,089 well-known benchmark instances show that MergeDJD consistently outperforms DJD on 1,083 instances while maintaining short runtimes. Notably, MergeDJD attains new best known values on 515 instances. Ablation studies further confirm the effectiveness of the proposed components. To facilitate reproducibility and future research, we have open-sourced the complete implementation and provided interfaces for visualizing packing results.

AI.tldr: MergeDJD is a constructive refinement of the DJD algorithm for 2D irregular bin packing that adds a pre-processing merging step to consolidate pieces into larger, more regular shapes, combined with an enhanced placement strategy for non-convex/merged shapes. It consistently outperforms DJD on 1,083 of 1,089 benchmark instances and achieves new best-known values on 515 instances, with open-source implementation for reproducibility.
AI.motivation: Address the limitations of existing constructive heuristics for 2D irregular bin packing, particularly DJD's weaker performance on cutting-like instances, by increasing piece regularity and improving placement of non-convex/merged shapes to improve solution quality while maintaining low runtime.
AI.method: Preprocess the instance by iteratively identifying and merging groups of pieces into larger, more regular shapes. Apply an improved DJD-based placement that better handles non-convex and merged shapes to pack all resulting pieces into fixed-size bins. Validate on 1,089 benchmark instances, perform ablation studies, and provide open-source implementation and visualization interfaces.
AI.result: Algorithm outperforms DJD on 1,083 of 1,089 instances and achieves new best-known values on 515 instances, with short runtimes and reproducible results via open-source code.
AI.conclusion: Merging step plus enhanced DJD placement yields substantial performance gains, with empirical evidence from large-scale benchmarks and ablation studies supporting the contribution; the open-source release facilitates further research and adoption.

title: Towards Computing Average Merge Tree Based on the Interleaving Distance
summary: The interleaving distance is a key tool for comparing merge trees, which provide topological summaries of scalar functions. In this work, we define an average merge tree for a pair of merge trees using the interleaving distance. Since such an average is not unique, we propose a method to construct a representative average merge tree. We further prove that the resulting merge tree indeed satisfies a natural notion of averaging for the two given merge trees. To demonstrate the structure of the average merge tree, we include illustrative examples.

AI.tldr: Proposes an averaging framework for merge trees using the interleaving distance. Because the average under this metric is not unique, it provides a construction of a representative average merge tree and proves that it satisfies a natural averaging criterion, supported by illustrative examples.
AI.motivation: To enable meaningful aggregation of topological summaries (merge trees) by defining and constructing an average that reflects the interleaving distance, addressing non-uniqueness and providing theoretical justification.
AI.method: Introduce a distance-based averaging approach in the space of merge trees endowed with the interleaving distance. Develop a constructive procedure to select a representative average merge tree (e.g., a geodesic midpoint or minimizer of the sum of distances) despite non-uniqueness, and prove it satisfies a natural averaging property.
AI.result: A representative average merge tree is constructed and proven to satisfy the intended averaging criterion. The authors also demonstrate properties of the average and illustrate its structure with examples.
AI.conclusion: The work establishes a concrete, theoretically justified method to average merge trees and demonstrates its behavior through examples, contributing to the toolkit for aggregating topological summaries of scalar functions.

title: Compatible Triangulations of Simple Polygons
summary: Let $P$ and $Q$ be simple polygons with $n$ vertices each. We wish to compute triangulations of $P$ and $Q$ that are combinatorially equivalent, if they exist. We consider two versions of the problem: if a triangulation of $P$ is given, we can decide in $O(n\log n + nr)$ time if $Q$ has a compatible triangulation, where $r$ is the number of reflex vertices of $Q$. If we are already given the correspondence between vertices of $P$ and $Q$ (but no triangulation), we can find compatible triangulations of $P$ and $Q$ in time $O(M(n))$, where $M(n)$ is the running time for multiplying two $n\times n$ matrices.

AI.tldr: Two n-vertex simple polygons P and Q admit combinatorially equivalent triangulations. If a triangulation of P is given, one can decide Q's compatibility in O(n log n + n r) time (r = number of reflex vertices of Q). If a vertex correspondence between P and Q is given, compatible triangulations can be found in O(M(n)) time, where M(n) is the time to multiply two n×n matrices.
AI.motivation: To understand when two polygons have triangulations that are topologically identical, enabling reliable morphing, mesh generation, and geometric processing where triangulation structure must align.
AI.method: (i) Given a triangulation of P, check compatibility with Q by analyzing combinatorial structure relative to Q's reflex vertices, achieving O(n log n + n r) time. (ii) With a vertex correspondence but no triangulations, reduce the problem to matrix-product computations and solve in O(M(n)) time.
AI.result: Algorithms with concrete complexity: (i) decision of compatibility in O(n log n + n r); (ii) construction of compatible triangulations in O(M(n)) given vertex correspondence.
AI.conclusion: Efficiently decide and construct combinatorially equivalent triangulations for simple polygons under two input models, leveraging reflex-vertex structure and fast matrix multiplication.

title: Beyond Static Instruction: A Multi-agent AI Framework for Adaptive Augmented Reality Robot Training
summary: Augmented Reality (AR) offers powerful visualization capabilities for industrial robot training, yet current interfaces remain predominantly static, failing to account for learners' diverse cognitive profiles. In this paper, we present an AR application for robot training and propose a multi-agent AI framework for future integration that bridges the gap between static visualization and pedagogical intelligence. We report on the evaluation of the baseline AR interface with 36 participants performing a robotic pick-and-place task. While overall usability was high, notable disparities in task duration and learner characteristics highlighted the necessity for dynamic adaptation. To address this, we propose a multi-agent framework that orchestrates multiple components to perform complex preprocessing of multimodal inputs (e.g., voice, physiology, robot data) and adapt the AR application to the learner's needs. By utilizing autonomous Large Language Model (LLM) agents, the proposed system would dynamically adapt the learning environment based on advanced LLM reasoning in real-time.

AI.tldr: AR-based robot training interface evaluated; proposes a multi-agent AI framework with LLMs to adapt AR learning in real-time from multimodal inputs; baseline usability high but task duration varies across learners.
AI.motivation: Address static AR visualizations that do not account for diverse learner cognitive profiles; move toward pedagogical intelligence in AR training.
AI.method: Baseline AR interface tested with 36 participants on a robotic pick-and-place task; proposes a multi-agent architecture to preprocess multimodal inputs (voice, physiology, robot data) and adapt the AR application; envisions autonomous LLM agents for real-time reasoning and adaptation.
AI.result: Usability was high overall; notable disparities in task duration and learner characteristics; indicates a need for dynamic adaptation.
AI.conclusion: A multi-agent framework with autonomous LLM agents could coordinate preprocessing and real-time adaptation of the AR learning environment, enabling personalized pedagogy in industrial robot training.

title: Transit Network Design with Two-Level Demand Uncertainties: A Machine Learning and Contextual Stochastic Optimization Framework
summary: Transit Network Design is a well-studied problem in the field of transportation, typically addressed by solving optimization models under fixed demand assumptions. Considering the limitations of these assumptions, this paper proposes a new framework, namely the Two-Level Rider Choice Transit Network Design (2LRC-TND), that leverages machine learning and contextual stochastic optimization (CSO) through constraint programming (CP) to incorporate two layers of demand uncertainties into the network design process. The first level identifies travelers who rely on public transit (core demand), while the second level captures the conditional adoption behavior of those who do not (latent demand), based on the availability and quality of transit services. To capture these two types of uncertainties, 2LRC-TND relies on two travel mode choice models, that use multiple machine learning models. To design a network, 2LRC-TND integrates the resulting choice models into a CSO that is solved using a CP-SAT solver. 2LRC-TND is evaluated through a case study involving over 6,600 travel arcs and more than 38,000 trips in the Atlanta metropolitan area. The computational results demonstrate the effectiveness of the 2LRC-TND in designing transit networks that account for demand uncertainties and contextual information, offering a more realistic alternative to fixed-demand models.

AI.tldr: A two-level rider-choice based framework (2LRC-TND) that combines ML-driven mode-choice models with contextual stochastic optimization via CP-SAT to design transit networks under core and latent demand uncertainties; evaluated on the Atlanta area, showing more robust designs than fixed-demand approaches.
AI.motivation: Fixed-demand transit network design (TND) methods fail to account for demand uncertainty and contextual factors affecting adoption. There is a need to model two layers of demand (core and latent) and to integrate ML-based demand information into a robust optimization framework to produce realistic, implementable networks.
AI.method: Develop two level of choice models: (i) core demand identification (transit users) and (ii) latent demand adoption conditional on service availability/quality. Use multiple ML models to build these mode-choice predictions. Integrate these models into a contextual stochastic optimization (CSO) formulation solved via a CP-SAT solver to design the network.
AI.result: Computational evaluation on a large Atlanta case study (≈6,600 travel arcs and >38,000 trips) demonstrates that 2LRC-TND can design transit networks that account for demand uncertainty and contextual information, offering a more realistic alternative to fixed-demand designs.
AI.conclusion: 2LRC-TND provides a practical, scalable framework that fuses ML-based demand modeling with contextual stochastic optimization for transit network design under uncertainty; suitable for large-scale real-world networks and adaptable to different contexts and ML models.

title: Learning Under Extreme Data Scarcity: Subject-Level Evaluation of Lightweight CNNs for fMRI-Based Prodromal Parkinsons Detection
comment: Methodological case study cs.LG on subject-level evaluation and model capacity under extreme data scarcity; 9 pages, 1 figure. Experiments use 40-subject PPMI fMRI cohort; no external validation
summary:
Deep learning is often applied in settings where data are limited, correlated, and difficult to obtain, yet evaluation practices do not always reflect these constraints. Neuroimaging for prodromal Parkinsons disease is one such case, where subject numbers are small and individual scans produce many highly related samples. This work examines prodromal Parkinsons detection from resting-state fMRI as a machine learning problem centered on learning under extreme data scarcity.
  Using fMRI data from 40 subjects, including 20 prodromal Parkinsons cases and 20 healthy controls, ImageNet-pretrained convolutional neural networks are fine-tuned and evaluated under two different data partitioning strategies. Results show that commonly used image-level splits allow slices from the same subject to appear in both training and test sets, leading to severe information leakage and near-perfect accuracy. When a strict subject-level split is enforced, performance drops substantially, yielding test accuracies between 60 and 81 percent.
  Models with different capacity profiles are compared, including VGG19, Inception V3, Inception ResNet V2, and the lightweight MobileNet V1. Under subject-level evaluation, MobileNet demonstrates the most reliable generalization, outperforming deeper architectures despite having significantly fewer parameters. These results indicate that in extreme low-data regimes, evaluation strategy and model capacity have a greater impact on performance than architectural depth. Although the analysis is limited to a single cohort of 40 subjects and does not include external validation or cross-validation, it provides a concrete case study and practical recommendations for evaluating deep learning models under severe data scarcity.

AI.tldr: In Parkinson’s prodrome detection from resting-state fMRI with extreme data scarcity, image-level splits cause leakage and near-perfect accuracy, while strict subject-level splits yield robust but modest accuracy (60–81%). Among tested models, MobileNet generalizes best under subject-level evaluation, highlighting that data-scarcity evaluation strategy and model capacity matter more than depth.
AI.motivation: Examine how evaluation strategy and model capacity influence deep learning performance in extreme data-scarce neuroimaging settings, where correlated samples and small subject counts challenge generalization.
AI.method: Fine-tune ImageNet-pretrained CNNs (VGG19, Inception V3, Inception-ResNet V2, MobileNet V1) on resting-state fMRI data from 40 subjects (20 prodromal PD, 20 controls). Compare two data partitioning schemes: image-level splits (potential leakage) and strict subject-level splits, assessing generalization under each. Report performance and compare model capacities.
AI.result: Image-level splits yield near-perfect accuracy due to information leakage; subject-level splits yield 60–81% test accuracy. MobileNet V1 shows the strongest generalization under subject-level evaluation, outperforming deeper networks despite fewer parameters.
AI.conclusion: In extreme low-data regimes, evaluation strategy and model capacity have greater impact on performance than architectural depth. The work provides practical recommendations for evaluating deep learning models under severe data scarcity but is limited by a single-cohort design without external validation or cross-validation.

title: Multi-Sourced, Multi-Agent Evidence Retrieval for Fact-Checking
summary:
Misinformation spreading over the Internet poses a significant threat to both societies and individuals, necessitating robust and scalable fact-checking that relies on retrieving accurate and trustworthy evidence. Previous methods rely on semantic and social-contextual patterns learned from training data, which limits their generalization to new data distributions. Recently, Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG) based methods have been proposed to utilize the reasoning capability of LLMs with retrieved grounding evidence documents. However, these methods largely rely on textual similarity for evidence retrieval and struggle to retrieve evidence that captures multi-hop semantic relations within rich document contents. These limitations lead to overlooking subtle factual correlations between the evidence and the claims to be fact-checked during evidence retrieval, thus causing inaccurate veracity predictions.
  To address these issues, we propose WKGFC, which exploits authorized open knowledge graph as a core resource of evidence. LLM-enabled retrieval is designed to assess the claims and retrieve the most relevant knowledge subgraphs, forming structured evidence for fact verification. To augment the knowledge graph evidence, we retrieve web contents for completion. The above process is implemented as an automatic Markov Decision Process (MDP): A reasoning LLM agent decides what actions to take according to the current evidence and the claims. To adapt the MDP for fact-checking, we use prompt optimization to fine-tune the agentic LLM.

AI.tldr: WKGFC: a knowledge-graph-grounded, LLM-enabled fact-checking framework that retrieves structured subgraphs from an authorized open KG, augmented with web content, and operates as an automatic MDP with prompt-optimized LLMs to improve evidence-based veracity judgments.
AI.motivation: Address limitations of text-only, semantics- and social-context-based fact-checking and RAG approaches that rely on textual similarity, by leveraging structured, multi-hop knowledge graphs to capture relational evidence and improve generalization to unseen data.
AI.method: Retrieve relevant subgraphs from an authorized open knowledge graph using an LLM-enabled retriever; form structured evidence; augment with additional web content; model the process as an automatic Markov Decision Process where the reasoning LLM selects actions based on current evidence and claims; fine-tune the agent LLM via prompt optimization to adapt to fact-checking tasks.
AI.result: Not specified in the abstract.
AI.conclusion: Proposes a novel, structured evidence-grounded fact-checking framework (WKGFC) that combines knowledge-graph evidence with LLM-based reasoning in an MDP formulation, potentially improving multi-hop reasoning and generalization over purely text-based retrieval.

title: A User Study on the Suitability of Teleoperation Interfaces for Primitive Manipulation Tasks
comment: Accepted at 21st ACM/IEEE International Conference on Human-Robot Interaction (HRI'26), Late Breaking Report
summary: The application of teleoperation to control robotic arms has been widely explored, and user-friendly teleoperation systems have been studied for facilitating higher performance and lower operational burden. To investigate the dominant factors in a practical teleoperation system, this study focused on the characteristics of an interface used to operate a robotic arm. The usability of an interface depends on the characteristics of the manipulation tasks to be completed; however, systematic comparisons of different interfaces across different tasks remain limited. In this study, we compared two widely used teleoperation interfaces, a 3D mouse and a VR controller, for two simple yet broadly applicable tasks with a six-degree-of-freedom (6DoF) robotic arm: repetitively pushing buttons and rotating knobs. Participants (N = 23) controlled a robotic arm with 6DoF to push buttons and rotate knobs as many times as possible in 3-minute trials. Each trial was followed by a NASA-TLX workload rating. The results showed a clear connection between the interface and task performance: the VR controller yielded higher performance for pushing buttons, whereas the 3D mouse performed better and was less demanding for knob rotation. These findings highlight the importance of considering dominant motion primitives of the task when designing practical teleoperation interfaces.

AI.tldr: VR controller yields higher button-pushing performance, while a 3D mouse excels at knob-rotation; performance is strongly task-dependent and linked to dominant motion primitives; study supports task-driven interface design in teleoperation.
AI.motivation: To identify the dominant factors influencing usability in practical teleoperation by comparing two common input devices (VR controller and 3D mouse) across representative 6DoF manipulation tasks, addressing the lack of systematic cross-task interface comparisons.
AI.method: Experiment with 23 participants controlling a 6DoF robotic arm to perform two tasks (button pushing and knob rotation) in 3-minute trials per condition, using two interfaces (VR controller and 3D mouse); outcomes include task performance and NASA-TLX workload after each trial.
AI.result: There is a clear interface–task interaction: the VR controller yields higher performance for button pushing, while the 3D mouse performs better for knob rotation; the findings underscore the importance of aligning interface capabilities with task motion primitives.
AI.conclusion: Design teleoperation interfaces by matching input devices to the dominant motion primitives of the task; the results imply that no single interface excels across all tasks, highlighting the need for task-driven interface design in practical teleoperation systems.

title: StaTS: Spectral Trajectory Schedule Learning for Adaptive Time Series Forecasting with Frequency Guided Denoiser
summary: Diffusion models have been used for probabilistic time series forecasting and show strong potential. However, fixed noise schedules often produce intermediate states that are hard to invert and a terminal state that deviates from the near noise assumption. Meanwhile, prior methods rely on time domain conditioning and seldom model schedule induced spectral degradation, which limits structure recovery across noise levels. We propose StaTS, a diffusion model for probabilistic time series forecasting that learns the noise schedule and the denoiser through alternating updates. StaTS includes Spectral Trajectory Scheduler (STS) that learns a data adaptive noise schedule with spectral regularization to improve structural preservation and stepwise invertibility, and Frequency Guided Denoiser (FGD) that estimates schedule induced spectral distortion and uses it to modulate denoising strength for heterogeneous restoration across diffusion steps and variables. A two stage training procedure stabilizes the coupling between schedule learning and denoiser optimization. Experiments on multiple real world benchmarks show consistent gains, while maintaining strong performance with fewer sampling steps. Our code is available at https://github.com/zjt-gpu/StaTS/.

AI.tldr: StaTS jointly learns a data-adaptive noise schedule and a denoiser for probabilistic time series forecasting, using Spectral Trajectory Scheduler (STS) and Frequency Guided Denoiser (FGD) to improve structure preservation and invertibility with efficient sampling.
AI.motivation: Fixed noise schedules in diffusion models hinder invertibility and deviate from near-noise assumptions; existing methods focus on time-domain conditioning and neglect schedule-induced spectral degradation, limiting structure recovery across noise levels.
AI.method: Alternating updates to learn the noise schedule and denoiser. Introduces Spectral Trajectory Scheduler (STS) with spectral regularization to preserve structure and enable invertibility; introduces Frequency Guided Denoiser (FGD) that estimates schedule-induced spectral distortion and modulates denoising strength across diffusion steps and variables. A two-stage training procedure stabilizes the coupling between schedule learning and denoiser optimization.
AI.result: Experiments on multiple real-world benchmarks show consistent gains and robustness, with strong performance maintained even with fewer sampling steps.
AI.conclusion: StaTS demonstrates effective joint optimization of schedule and denoiser with spectral-aware components for probabilistic time series forecasting, improving accuracy and efficiency; code is available at the project page.

title: Automated Quality Check of Sensor Data Annotations
summary: The monitoring of the route and track environment plays an important role in automated driving. For example, it can be used as an assistance system for route monitoring in automation level Grade of Automation (GoA) 2, where the train driver is still on board. In fully automated, driverless driving at automation level GoA4, these systems finally take over environment monitoring completely independently. With the help of artificial intelligence (AI), they react automatically to risks and dangerous events on the route. To train such AI algorithms, large amounts of training data are required, which must meet high-quality standards due to their safety relevance. In this publication we present an automatic method for assuring the quality of training data, significantly reducing the manual workload and accelerating the development of these systems. We propose an open-source tool designed to detect nine common errors found in multi-sensor datasets for railway vehicles. To evaluate the performance of the framework, all detected errors were manually validated. Six issue detection methods achieved 100% precision, while three additional methods reached precision rates 96% and 97%.

AI.tldr: An automatic data-quality assurance framework for railway multi-sensor datasets; an open-source tool detects nine common errors; evaluated via manual validation with high-precision results (six detectors at 100% precision; three detectors at 96–97%).
AI.motivation: High-stakes safety context for automated rail systems requires high-quality training data; reducing manual validation workload accelerates AI development for GoA2–GoA4.
AI.method: Developed an open-source tool to detect nine common errors in multi-sensor railway datasets. All detected errors were manually validated to assess performance; reported precision for each detection method.
AI.result: Six detection methods achieved 100% precision; three additional methods achieved 96% and 97% precision respectively.
AI.conclusion: The tool effectively supports data quality assurance for railway AI pipelines and, due to its open-source nature, can be adopted to improve data reliability while reducing manual effort.

title: TraderBench: How Robust Are AI Agents in Adversarial Capital Markets?
comment: Equal Contribution: Xiaochuang Yuan and Hui Xu contributed equally to this work. All correspondence should be directed to yxc20098@gmail.com. Submitted to Agents in the Wild Workshop, ICLR2026
summary: Evaluating AI agents in finance faces two key challenges: static benchmarks require costly expert annotation yet miss the dynamic decision-making central to real-world trading, while LLM-based judges introduce uncontrolled variance on domain-specific tasks. We introduce TraderBench, a benchmark that addresses both issues. It combines expert-verified static tasks (knowledge retrieval, analytical reasoning) with adversarial trading simulations scored purely on realized performance-Sharpe ratio, returns, and drawdown-eliminating judge variance entirely. The framework features two novel tracks: crypto trading with four progressive market-manipulation transforms, and options derivatives scoring across P&L accuracy, Greeks, and risk management. Trading scenarios can be refreshed with new market data to prevent benchmark contamination. Evaluating 13 models (8B open-source to frontier) on ~50 tasks, we find: (1) 8 of 13 models score ~33 on crypto with <1-point variation across adversarial conditions, exposing fixed non-adaptive strategies; (2) extended thinking helps retrieval (+26 points) but has zero impact on trading (+0.3 crypto, -0.1 options). These findings reveal that current agents lack genuine market adaptation, underscoring the need for performance-grounded evaluation in finance.

AI.tldr: TraderBench combines expert-verified static tasks with adversarial trading simulations to provide a performance-grounded, variance-free evaluation for AI agents in finance, addressing the limitations of static benchmarks and LLM judges.
AI.motivation: Static finance benchmarks are costly and fail to capture dynamic decision-making; LLM judges introduce uncontrolled domain variance. A performance-grounded benchmark is needed to drive genuine market-adaptive behavior.
AI.method: Integrates expert-verified static tasks (knowledge retrieval, analytical reasoning) with adversarial trading simulations scored solely on realized performance metrics (Sharpe ratio, returns, drawdown). Features two tracks: crypto trading with four market-manipulation transforms and options derivatives scoring across P&L accuracy, Greeks, and risk management. Scenarios refreshed with new market data to prevent benchmark contamination.
AI.result: Evaluated 13 models (8B open-source to frontier) on ~50 tasks. 8 of 13 models score ~33 on crypto with <1-point variation across adversarial conditions, indicating fixed non-adaptive strategies. Extended thinking improves retrieval (+26 points) but has negligible impact on trading (+0.3 crypto, -0.1 options).
AI.conclusion: Current agents lack genuine market adaptation; performance-grounded evaluation is essential to drive finance-focused AI development.

title: Designing Social Robots with Ethical, User-Adaptive Explainability in the Era of Foundation Models
comment: Companion Proceedings of the 21st ACM/IEEE International Conference on Human-Robot Interaction
summary: Foundation models are increasingly embedded in social robots, mediating not only what they say and do but also how they adapt to users over time. This shift renders traditional ``one-size-fits-all'' explanation strategies especially problematic: generic justifications are now wrapped around behaviour produced by models trained on vast, heterogeneous, and opaque datasets. We argue that ethical, user-adapted explainability must be treated as a core design objective for foundation-model-driven social robotics. We first identify open challenges around explainability and ethical concerns that arise when both adaptation and explanation are delegated to foundation models. Building on this analysis, we propose four recommendations for moving towards user-adapted, modality-aware, and co-designed explanation strategies grounded in smaller, fairer datasets. An illustrative use case of an LLM-driven socially assistive robot demonstrates how these recommendations might be instantiated in a sensitive, real-world domain.

AI.tldr: Foundation-model-driven social robots require user-adapted, modality-aware explanations; the paper analyzes explainability challenges, proposes four recommendations, and demonstrates with an LLM-driven socially assistive robot in a sensitive domain.
AI.motivation: Traditional one-size-fits-all explanations are inadequate for foundation-model-based social robotics because models are trained on diverse opaque data and interact with users over time; ethical considerations demand explanations tailored to user, context, and modality.
AI.method: Analytical identification of open challenges; development of four recommendations to guide explainability; illustration via a real-world use case showing practical instantiation using smaller, fairer datasets.
AI.result: A concrete set of four recommendations for user-adapted, modality-aware explanation strategies grounded in smaller, fairer datasets; an illustrative use case demonstrating feasibility in a sensitive domain.
AI.conclusion: Explainability should be a core design objective for foundation-model-driven social robotics; move toward user-adapted explanations and fairer datasets; future work includes implementing and evaluating the proposed recommendations in varied contexts.

title: CARE: Confounder-Aware Aggregation for Reliable LLM Evaluation
summary: LLM-as-a-judge ensembles are the standard paradigm for scalable evaluation, but their aggregation mechanisms suffer from a fundamental flaw: they implicitly assume that judges provide independent estimates of true quality. However, in practice, LLM judges exhibit correlated errors caused by shared latent confounders -- such as verbosity, stylistic preferences, or training artifacts -- causing standard aggregation rules like majority vote or averaging to provide little gain or even amplify systematic mistakes. To address this, we introduce CARE, a confounder-aware aggregation framework that explicitly models LLM judge scores as arising from both a latent true-quality signal and shared confounding factors. Rather than heuristically re-weighting judges, CARE separates quality from confounders without access to ground-truth labels. We provide theoretical guarantees for identifiability and finite-sample recovery under shared confounders, and we quantify the systematic bias incurred when aggregation models omit confounding latent factors. Across 12 public benchmarks spanning continuous scoring, binary classification, and pairwise preference settings, CARE improves aggregation accuracy, reducing error by up to 26.8\%. Code is released in \href{https://github.com/SprocketLab/CARE}{https://github.com/SprocketLab/CARE}.

AI.tldr: CARE introduces a confounder-aware aggregation for LLM judges that accounts for latent confounding factors, improving ensemble evaluation accuracy without ground-truth labels; it offers identifiability and finite-sample guarantees and shows up to 26.8% error reduction across 12 benchmarks.
AI.motivation: The standard LLM-as-a-judge paradigm assumes independent judge estimates, but shared latent confounders (e.g., verbosity, style, training artifacts) induce correlated errors, making naive aggregation suboptimal or harmful.
AI.method: Propose a generative model where observed judge scores arise from a latent true-quality signal plus shared confounding factors. Without ground-truth labels, derive identifiability and finite-sample recovery guarantees, quantify bias from omitting confounders, and validate across 12 benchmarks with continuous, binary, and pairwise tasks. Release code for practical adoption.
AI.result: CARE achieves improved aggregation accuracy across diverse tasks, reducing error by up to 26.8%. Demonstrates theoretical guarantees and practical effectiveness without requiring ground-truth labels.
AI.conclusion: A principled framework for confounder-aware aggregation that mitigates correlated-jury bias in LLM evaluations, with theoretical guarantees and broad empirical validation; release of open-source code enables adoption and further research.

title: VoxelDiffusionCut: Non-destructive Internal-part Extraction via Iterative Cutting and Structure Estimation
summary: Non-destructive extraction of the target internal part, such as batteries and motors, by cutting surrounding structures is crucial at recycling and disposal sites. However, the diversity of products and the lack of information on disassembly procedures make it challenging to decide where to cut. This study explores a method for non-destructive extraction of a target internal part that iteratively estimates the internal structure from observed cutting surfaces and formulates cutting plans based on the estimation results. A key requirement is to estimate the probability of the target part's presence from partial observations. However, learning conditional generative models for this task is challenging: The high dimensionality of 3D shape representations makes learning difficult, and conventional models (e.g., conditional variational autoencoders) often fail to capture multi-modal predictive uncertainty due to mode collapse, resulting in overconfident predictions. To address these issues, we propose VoxelDiffusionCut, which iteratively estimates the internal structure represented as voxels using a diffusion model and plans cuts for non-destructive extraction of the target internal part based on the estimation results. Voxel representation allows the model to predict only attributes at fixed grid positions, i.e., types of constituent parts, making learning more tractable. The diffusion model completes the voxel representation conditioned on observed cutting surfaces, capturing uncertainty in unobserved regions to avoid erroneous cuts. Experimental results in simulation suggest that the proposed method can estimate internal structures from observed cutting surfaces and enable non-destructive extraction of the target internal part by leveraging the estimated uncertainty.

AI.tldr: Diffusion-based voxel completion for non-destructive disassembly. A diffusion model iteratively completes a voxel grid representing the internal structure conditioned on observed cutting surfaces to plan safe cuts and capture multi-modal uncertainty.
AI.motivation: Recycling/disassembly requires locating target internal parts (e.g., batteries, motors) across diverse products; conventional conditional generative models struggle with high-dimensional 3D shapes and multi-modal uncertainty, hindering reliable cut planning.
AI.method: VoxelDiffusionCut uses a diffusion model to iteratively infer the voxel representation of the internal structure, conditioned on observed cutting surfaces. It predicts part types at fixed grid positions, with the diffusion process capturing uncertainty in unobserved regions and informing cut planning.
AI.result: Simulation experiments show the method can estimate internal structures from observed cutting surfaces and enable non-destructive extraction by leveraging estimated uncertainty.
AI.conclusion: Diffusion-based voxel completion provides a tractable approach to model uncertainty in 3D internal structures for disassembly planning, with promising simulation results and avenues for real-world validation and efficiency improvements.

title: DIG to Heal: Scaling General-purpose Agent Collaboration via Explainable Dynamic Decision Paths
summary: The increasingly popular agentic AI paradigm promises to harness the power of multiple, general-purpose large language model (LLM) agents to collaboratively complete complex tasks. While many agentic AI systems utilize predefined workflows or agent roles in order to reduce complexity, ideally these agents would be truly autonomous, able to achieve emergent collaboration even as the number of collaborating agents increases. Yet in practice, such unstructured interactions can lead to redundant work and cascading failures that are difficult to interpret or correct. In this work, we study multi-agent systems composed of general-purpose LLM agents that operate without predefined roles, control flow, or communication constraints, relying instead on emergent collaboration to solve problems. We introduce the Dynamic Interaction Graph (DIG), which captures emergent collaboration as a time-evolving causal network of agent activations and interactions. DIG makes emergent collaboration observable and explainable for the first time, enabling real-time identification, explanation, and correction of collaboration-induced error patterns directly from agents' collaboration paths. Thus, DIG fills a critical gap in understanding how general LLM agents solve problems together in truly agentic multi-agent systems. The project webpage can be found at: https://happyeureka.github.io/dig.

AI.tldr: Dynamic Interaction Graph (DIG) provides a time-evolving causal map of emergent collaboration among unstructured LLM agents, enabling observability, explainability, and real-time detection/correction of collaboration-induced errors in agentic multi-agent systems.
AI.motivation: Address interpretability and controllability challenges in unstructured, autonomous multi-agent systems where agents interact without predefined roles or workflows; reduce redundant work and cascading failures as the number of agents grows.
AI.method: Define and instantiate a time-evolving graph capturing agent activations and interactions (the DIG); treat collaboration as graph dynamics to trace collaboration paths, explain failures, and guide real-time corrections.
AI.result: DIG proposes an observable and explainable model of emergent collaboration, enabling real-time identification, explanation, and correction of errors arising from agents’ collaboration, and providing a framework to understand how general LLM agents solve problems together.
AI.conclusion: DIG fills a crucial gap in understanding truly agentic multi-agent problem solving; offers a basis for improved reliability, debuggability, and governance of unstructured LLM agent systems.

title: Autonomous Block Assembly for Boom Cranes with Passive Joint Dynamics: Integrated Vision MPC Control
summary: This paper presents an autonomous control framework for articulated boom cranes performing prefabricated block assembly in construction environments. The key challenge addressed is precise placement control under passive joint dynamics that cause pendulum-like sway, complicating the accurate positioning of building components. Our integrated approach combines real-time vision-based pose estimation of building blocks, collision-aware B-spline path planning, and nonlinear model predictive control (NMPC) to achieve autonomous pickup, placement, and obstacle-avoidance assembly operations. The framework is validated on a laboratory-scale testbed that emulates crane kinematics and passive dynamics while enabling rapid experimentation. The collision-aware planner generates feasible B-spline references in real-time on CPU hardware with anytime performance, while the NMPC controller actively suppresses passive joint sway and tracks the planned trajectory under continuous vision feedback. Experimental results demonstrate autonomous block stacking and obstacle-avoidance assembly, with sway damping reducing settling times by more than an order of magnitude compared to uncontrolled passive dynamics, confirming the real-time feasibility of the integrated approach for construction automation.

AI.tldr: Autonomous articulated boom crane control for block assembly using vision, collision-aware B-splines, and NMPC to counter pendulum-like sway; validated on lab-scale with significant sway damping and real-time planning.
AI.motivation: Address precise placement under passive joint dynamics causing pendulum-like sway in crane-based prefabricated block assembly; enable autonomous pickup, placement, and obstacle avoidance in construction environments.
AI.method: Real-time vision-based pose estimation of blocks; collision-aware B-spline path planning on CPU with anytime performance; nonlinear model predictive control (NMPC) to suppress passive-joint sway and track the planned trajectory using continuous vision feedback; validated on a lab-scale crane testbed that emulates kinematics and passive dynamics.
AI.result: Autonomous block stacking and obstacle-avoidance demonstrated; sway damping reduces settling times by more than an order of magnitude compared to uncontrolled passive dynamics; real-time feasibility of the integrated framework for construction automation.
AI.conclusion: Integrating vision-based perception, collision-aware planning, and NMPC yields robust, real-time autonomous crane-based assembly under passive dynamics; demonstrates potential for scalable, automated construction workflows with improved accuracy and efficiency.

title: Attn-QAT: 4-Bit Attention With Quantization-Aware Training
summary: Achieving reliable 4-bit attention is a prerequisite for end-to-end FP4 computation on emerging FP4-capable GPUs, yet attention remains the main obstacle due to FP4's tiny dynamic range and attention's heavy-tailed activations. This paper presents the first systematic study of 4-bit quantization-aware training (QAT) for attention. We find that "drop-in" QAT, which naively combines an FP4 forward pass with a high-precision Flash Attention (FA)-style backward pass, leads to training instability. We identify two key principles for stable FP4 attention: (1) matching low-precision recomputation of attention scores in the backward pass, and (2) resolving implicit precision assumptions in FA's gradient calculation. Based on these insights, we propose Attn-QAT and implement fused Triton kernels for training as well as FP4 inference kernels. Across diffusion and language models, Attn-QAT recovers the quality drop from FP4 attention without explicit outlier-mitigation heuristics used in prior FP4 attention, and delivers up to a 1.5x speedup on an RTX 5090. Video demos can be found at https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/190F6xbBDUF2kGQYIcXBt3ehSYij5jlim?usp=sharing.

AI.tldr: Systematic study of 4-bit quantization-aware training for attention; introduces Attn-QAT to stabilize FP4 attention, with fused kernels enabling end-to-end FP4 training and inference, achieving quality recovery and speedups up to 1.5x on RTX 5090.
AI.motivation: FP4-capable GPUs promise reduced precision compute but attention is hindered by FP4's tiny dynamic range and heavy-tailed activations; a reliable FP4 attention training method is needed for end-to-end FP4: training and inference on FP4.
AI.method: Identify two stability principles for FP4 attention during QAT: (1) align low-precision recomputation of attention scores in the backward pass, (2) resolve implicit precision assumptions in Flash Attention's gradient calculation. Propose Attn-QAT and implement fused Triton kernels for both training and FP4 inference.
AI.result: Attn-QAT recovers the quality degradation typically seen with FP4 attention across diffusion and language models, without relying on explicit outlier-mitigation heuristics. It also delivers up to 1.5× speedup on an RTX 5090 and provides video demos demonstrating the approach.
AI.conclusion: A principled FP4 QAT framework for attention enables reliable end-to-end FP4 training/inference on modern GPUs, removing reliance on ad-hoc heuristics and offering practical speedups; the approach is demonstrated on diffusion and language models and is supported by optimized kernels.

title: Efficient Image Super-Resolution with Multi-Scale Spatial Adaptive Attention Networks
summary: This paper introduces a lightweight image super-resolution (SR) network, termed the Multi-scale Spatial Adaptive Attention Network (MSAAN), to address the common dilemma between high reconstruction fidelity and low model complexity in existing SR methods. The core of our approach is a novel Multi-scale Spatial Adaptive Attention Module (MSAA), designed to jointly model fine-grained local details and long-range contextual dependencies. The MSAA comprises two synergistic components: a Global Feature Modulation Module (GFM) that learns coherent texture structures through differential feature extraction, and a Multi-scale Feature Aggregation Module (MFA) that adaptively fuses features from local to global scales using pyramidal processing. To further enhance the network's capability, we propose a Local Enhancement Block (LEB) to strengthen local geometric perception and a Feature Interactive Gated Feed-Forward Module (FIGFF) to improve nonlinear representation while reducing channel redundancy. Extensive experiments on standard benchmarks (Set5, Set14, B100, Urban100, Manga109) across $\times2$, $\times3$, and $\times4$ scaling factors demonstrate that both our lightweight (MSAAN-light) and standard (MSAAN) versions achieve superior or competitive performance in terms of PSNR and SSIM, while maintaining significantly lower parameters and computational costs than state-of-the-art methods. Ablation studies validate the contribution of each component, and visual results show that MSAAN reconstructs sharper edges and more realistic textures.

AI.tldr: Introduces MSAAN, a lightweight SR network with MSAA module (GFM + MFA), plus LEB and FIGFF, achieving strong SR performance with lower parameters across x2/x3/x4 on standard benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Address the trade-off between high reconstruction fidelity and low model complexity in single-image SR; need to capture fine local details and long-range context efficiently.
AI.method: Propose MSAA with two components: GFM for coherent texture via differential feature extraction; MFA for adaptive multi-scale feature fusion using pyramidal processing; plus LEB to enhance local geometry and FIGFF for improved nonlinearity and reduced channel redundancy.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on Set5, Set14, B100, Urban100, Manga109 across x2/x3/x4 show MSAAN and MSAAN-light achieve competitive or superior PSNR/SSIM while significantly reducing parameters and FLOPs; ablations confirm contributions; visuals show sharper edges and realistic textures.
AI.conclusion: MSAAN provides state-of-the-art efficiency and accuracy in SR; MSAA components effectively balance local/global representation; the approach is validated through thorough ablations and benchmarks, indicating strong practical potential for lightweight SR deployment.

title: How Well Do Multimodal Models Reason on ECG Signals?
summary: While multimodal large language models offer a promising solution to the "black box" nature of health AI by generating interpretable reasoning traces, verifying the validity of these traces remains a critical challenge. Existing evaluation methods are either unscalable, relying on manual clinician review, or superficial, utilizing proxy metrics (e.g. QA) that fail to capture the semantic correctness of clinical logic. In this work, we introduce a reproducible framework for evaluating reasoning in ECG signals. We propose decomposing reasoning into two distinct, components: (i) Perception, the accurate identification of patterns within the raw signal, and (ii) Deduction, the logical application of domain knowledge to those patterns. To evaluate Perception, we employ an agentic framework that generates code to empirically verify the temporal structures described in the reasoning trace. To evaluate Deduction, we measure the alignment of the model's logic against a structured database of established clinical criteria in a retrieval-based approach. This dual-verification method enables the scalable assessment of "true" reasoning capabilities.

AI.tldr: A two-component framework to evaluate ECG reasoning in multimodal health AI by separately validating Perception (pattern recognition) via code-generated empirical checks of temporal structures and Deduction (clinical reasoning) via retrieval-based alignment with established criteria, enabling scalable true-reasoning assessment.
AI.motivation: Address the inability to reliably verify the semantic correctness of clinical reasoning in AI; move beyond manual review and proxy metrics toward a scalable, reproducible evaluation of true reasoning in ECG interpretation.
AI.method: Decompose reasoning into Perception and Deduction. Perception: agentic framework that generates code to empirically test temporal structures described in the reasoning trace. Deduction: retrieval-based alignment of the model's logic with a structured database of established clinical criteria.
AI.result: Proposes dual-verification framework enabling scalable assessment of true reasoning capabilities.
AI.conclusion: The framework provides a scalable, reproducible method for evaluating reasoning in ECG signals by separately validating perception and deduction.

title: SurgFusion-Net: Diversified Adaptive Multimodal Fusion Network for Surgical Skill Assessment
summary: Robotic-assisted surgery (RAS) is established in clinical practice, and automated surgical skill assessment utilizing multimodal data offers transformative potential for surgical analytics and education. However, developing effective multimodal methods remains challenging due to the task complexity, limited annotated datasets and insufficient techniques for cross-modal information fusion. Existing state-of-the-art relies exclusively on RGB video and only applies on dry-lab settings, failing to address the significant domain gap between controlled simulation and real clinical cases, where the surgical environment together with camera and tissue motion introduce substantial complexities. This work introduces SurgFusion-Net and Divergence Regulated Attention (DRA), an innovative fusion strategy for multimodal surgical skill assessment. We contribute two first-of-their-kind clinical datasets: the RAH-skill dataset containing 279,691 RGB frames from 37 videos of Robot-assisted Hysterectomy (RAH), and the RARP-skill dataset containing 70,661 RGB frames from 33 videos of Robot-Assisted Radical Prostatectomy (RARP). Both datasets include M-GEARS skill annotations, corresponding optical flow and tool segmentation masks. DRA incorporates adaptive dual attention and diversity-promoting multi-head attention to fuse multimodal information, from three modalities, based on surgical context, enhancing assessment accuracy and reliability. Validated on the JIGSAWS benchmark, RAH-skill, and RARP-skill datasets, our approach outperforms recent baselines with SCC improvements of 0.02 in LOSO, 0.04 in LOUO across JIGSAWS tasks, and 0.0538 and 0.0493 gains on RAH-skill and RARP-skill, respectively.

AI.tldr: SurgFusion-Net with Divergence Regulated Attention (DRA) enables multimodal fusion of RGB, optical flow, and tool segmentation for surgical skill assessment, addressing domain gaps between dry-lab and clinical settings. It introduces two large clinical datasets (RAH-skill, RARP-skill) with M-GEARS annotations, optical flow, and tool masks, and reports state-of-the-art improvements on JIGSAWS (LOS0/LOUO) and on the new datasets.
AI.motivation: Overcome RGB-only limits, domain shift between simulated and real surgeries, and lack of robust cross-modal fusion methods for surgical skill assessment.
AI.method: SurgFusion-Net employs Divergence Regulated Attention (DRA) with adaptive dual attention and diversity-promoting multi-head attention to fuse three modalities (RGB, optical flow, tool segmentation) in a context-aware manner. Two clinical datasets (RAH-skill, RARP-skill) with M-GEARS annotations and auxiliary modalities are introduced. Evaluation includes JIGSAWS, RAH-skill, and RARP-skill.
AI.result: On JIGSAWS, achieving SCC improvements of 0.02 (LOSO) and 0.04 (LOUO). On RAH-skill and RARP-skill, improvements of 0.0538 and 0.0493 in SCC, respectively.
AI.conclusion: DRA-based multimodal fusion improves accuracy and reliability of surgical skill assessment across real-world clinical data, highlighting the practicality of cross-modal learning in robotic surgery analytics.

title: Econometric vs. Causal Structure-Learning for Time-Series Policy Decisions: Evidence from the UK COVID-19 Policies
summary: Causal machine learning (ML) recovers graphical structures that inform us about potential cause-and-effect relationships. Most progress has focused on cross-sectional data with no explicit time order, whereas recovering causal structures from time series data remains the subject of ongoing research in causal ML. In addition to traditional causal ML, this study assesses econometric methods that some argue can recover causal structures from time series data. The use of these methods can be explained by the significant attention the field of econometrics has given to causality, and specifically to time series, over the years. This presents the possibility of comparing the causal discovery performance between econometric and traditional causal ML algorithms. We seek to understand if there are lessons to be incorporated into causal ML from econometrics, and provide code to translate the results of these econometric methods to the most widely used Bayesian Network R library, bnlearn. We investigate the benefits and challenges that these algorithms present in supporting policy decision-making, using the real-world case of COVID-19 in the UK as an example. Four econometric methods are evaluated in terms of graphical structure, model dimensionality, and their ability to recover causal effects, and these results are compared with those of eleven causal ML algorithms. Amongst our main results, we see that econometric methods provide clear rules for temporal structures, whereas causal-ML algorithms offer broader discovery by exploring a larger space of graph structures that tends to lead to denser graphs that capture more identifiable causal relationships.

AI.tldr: Econometric time-series causal methods provide clearer temporal structure rules; traditional causal ML explores broader graph structures, often denser, with complementary strengths; study compares four econometric methods to eleven causal ML algorithms using UK COVID-19 data and provides bnlearn translation; implications for policy decision-making and hybrid approaches.
AI.motivation: To systematically compare causal discovery from time-series data across econometrics and machine learning, evaluate practical implications for policy, and bridge outputs to bnlearn; assess whether econometric insights can inform and improve causal ML in dynamic settings.
AI.method: Empirically evaluate four econometric causality methods and eleven causal ML algorithms on time-series data (UK COVID-19 case), comparing graphical structure, model dimensionality, and causal effect recovery; translate econometric outputs to bnlearn representation; analyze policy relevance.
AI.result: Econometric methods yield explicit rules for temporal structure and relatively parsimonious graphs; causal ML reveals broader, denser graph spaces with potentially more identifiable causal relations but less temporal constraint; results suggest complementary strengths and trade-offs; bnlearn translation enables interoperability.
AI.conclusion: There is value in combining econometric temporality with the exploratory breadth of causal ML for time-series causal discovery, enhancing policy-supporting insights; cross-domain learning can improve interpretability and actionability of causal graphs.

title: BiSe-Unet: A Lightweight Dual-path U-Net with Attention-refined Context for Real-time Medical Image Segmentation
comment: Submitted to IEEE EMBC 2026. This work has been submitted to the IEEE for possible publication
summary: During image-guided procedures, real-time image segmentation is often required. This demands lightweight AI models that can operate on resource-constrained devices. One important use case is endoscopy-guided colonoscopy, where polyps must be detected in real time. The Kvasir-Seg dataset, a publicly available benchmark for this task, contains 1,000 high-resolution endoscopic images of polyps with corresponding pixel-level segmentation masks. Achieving real-time inference speed for clinical deployment in constrained environments requires highly efficient and lightweight network architectures. However, many existing models remain too computationally intensive for embedded deployment. Lightweight architectures, although faster, often suffer from reduced spatial precision and weaker contextual understanding, leading to degraded boundary quality and reduced diagnostic reliability. To address these challenges, we introduce BiSe-UNet, a lightweight dual-path U-Net that integrates an attention-refined context path with a shallow spatial path for detailed feature preservation, followed by a depthwise separable decoder for efficient reconstruction. Evaluated on the Kvasir-Seg dataset, BiSe-UNet achieves competitive Dice and IoU scores while sustaining real-time throughput exceeding 30 FPS on Raspberry Pi 5, demonstrating its effectiveness for accurate, lightweight, and deployable medical image segmentation on edge hardware.

AI.tldr: BiSe-UNet: a lightweight dual-path U-Net with an attention-refined context path and a shallow spatial path, followed by a depthwise separable decoder; achieves real-time segmentation on edge hardware (≥30 FPS on Raspberry Pi 5) on Kvasir-Seg with competitive Dice/IoU.
AI.motivation: The need for real-time, deployable medical image segmentation on resource-constrained devices during image-guided procedures, where existing lightweight models often sacrifice boundary fidelity and contextual understanding.
AI.method: BiSe-UNet uses a dual-path architecture: an attention-refined context path to capture global information and a shallow spatial path to preserve fine details, coupled with a depthwise separable decoder for efficient reconstruction; evaluated on the Kvasir-Seg dataset with real-time performance measured on Raspberry Pi 5.
AI.result: Competitive Dice and IoU scores on Kvasir-Seg, with real-time throughput exceeding 30 FPS on Raspberry Pi 5, demonstrating effectiveness for deployable, lightweight medical image segmentation.
AI.conclusion: BiSe-UNet achieves an effective balance between segmentation accuracy and computational efficiency, enabling real-time inference on edge hardware for endoscopy-guided procedures.

title: EmCoop: A Framework and Benchmark for Embodied Cooperation Among LLM Agents
summary: Real-world scenarios increasingly require multiple embodied agents to collaborate in dynamic environments under embodied constraints, as many tasks exceed the capabilities of any single agent. Recent advances in large language models (LLMs) enable high-level cognitive coordination through reasoning, planning, and natural language communication. However, fine-grained analyses of how such collaboration emerges, unfolds, and contributes to task success in embodied multi-agent systems are difficult to conduct with existing benchmarks. In this paper, we introduce EmCoop, a benchmark framework for studying cooperation in LLM-based embodied multi-agent systems. Our framework separates a high-level cognitive layer from a low-level embodied interaction layer, allowing us to characterize agent cooperation through their interleaved dynamics over time. Given a cooperation-constrained embodied task, we propose generalizable, process-level metrics that diagnose collaboration quality and failure modes, beyond final task success. We instantiate our framework in two embodied environments that scale to arbitrary numbers of agents and support diverse communication topologies, and use these instantiations to demonstrate how EmCoop enables systematic analysis of cooperation dynamics across team sizes and task settings. The project web page can be found at: https://happyeureka.github.io/emcoop.

AI.tldr: EmCoop introduces a benchmark framework to study and diagnose cooperation dynamics in LLM-enabled embodied multi-agent systems by decoupling cognitive planning from embodied interaction and providing process-level metrics across scalable environments.
AI.motivation: To move beyond final task success and understand how collaboration emerges, unfolds, and fails in embodied multi-agent tasks; existing benchmarks offer limited fine-grained diagnostics of cooperative dynamics.
AI.method: Proposes EmCoop framework with a two-layer design separating high-level cognitive planning from low-level embodied interaction, along with generalizable process-level metrics. It instantiates the framework in two scalable embodied environments supporting arbitrary numbers of agents and diverse communication topologies to analyze cooperation dynamics across team sizes and task settings.
AI.result: Demonstrates that EmCoop enables systematic analysis of cooperation dynamics—diagnosing collaboration quality and failure modes—across different team sizes and task configurations using the two instantiated environments.
AI.conclusion: EmCoop provides a reusable benchmark for studying cooperation in LLM-based embodied multi-agent systems, enabling rigorous, process-level analysis of collaboration dynamics; the project page is provided for access.

title: Learning Physics from Pretrained Video Models: A Multimodal Continuous and Sequential World Interaction Models for Robotic Manipulation
summary: The scarcity of large-scale robotic data has motivated the repurposing of foundation models from other modalities for policy learning. In this work, we introduce PhysGen (Learning Physics from Pretrained Video Generation Models), a scalable continuous and sequential world interaction framework that leverages autoregressive video generation to solve robotic manipulation tasks. By treating the pretrained video model as a proxy for a physics simulator, PhysGen models the dynamic interplay between the external environment and robot actions. We introduce a multimodal continuous representation that unifies video and action into shared physical tokens, bridging the gap between discrete video generation and continuous robotic control. This approach enables the seamless transfer of implicit physical knowledge-such as object permanence and dynamics-from video pretraining to downstream manipulation.To ensure efficient convergence, we incorporate causal masking, inverse kinematics, Lookahead Multi-Token Prediction (L-MTP), and key-value (KV) caching. Experimental results on the Libero and ManiSkill benchmarks demonstrate that PhysGen consistently outperforms robust baselines, surpassing OpenVLA and WorldVLA by margins of 13.8% and 8.8%, respectively. Notably, in real-world scenarios, PhysGen matches the performance of large-scale action-pretrained models like $π_0$ without requiring prior action-specific pretraining, demonstrating superior capability in physically complex tasks such as grasping transparent objects. These findings validate the potential of extracting physical intuition from pretrained video generators to facilitate generalizable robotic manipulation.

AI.tldr: PhysGen leverages pretrained video generation models as a scalable physics engine for robotic manipulation, proposing a multimodal representation and forecasting techniques to enable video-to-action transfer; it outperforms baselines on benchmarks and rivals action-pretrained models in real-world tasks.
AI.motivation: Address the lack of large-scale robotic data by repurposing pretrained video models to learn physics and dynamics for policy learning.
AI.method: Treats autoregressive video generation as a proxy physics simulator, unifies video and action into shared physical tokens, and employs causal masking, inverse kinematics, Lookahead Multi-Token Prediction (L-MTP), and KV caching to enable efficient, continuous control.
AI.result: On Libero and ManiSkill, PhysGen beats strong baselines OpenVLA and WorldVLA by 13.8% and 8.8% respectively; in real-world settings it matches large action-pretrained models like π0 without action-specific pretraining, and demonstrates robust grasping of transparent objects.
AI.conclusion: Shows that physical intuition can be extracted from pretrained video generators to support generalizable robotic manipulation.

title: Maximizing the Spectral Energy Gain in Sub-1-Bit LLMs via Latent Geometry Alignment
summary: We identify the Spectral Energy Gain in extreme model compression, where low-rank binary approximations outperform tiny-rank floating-point baselines for heavy-tailed spectra. However, prior attempts fail to realize this potential, trailing state-of-the-art 1-bit methods. We attribute this degradation to Latent Geometry Misalignment: standard singular vectors exhibit high coherence (spiky distribution), the worst-case geometry for binary quantization. To realize this gain, we propose LittleBit-2, a framework employing Internal Latent Rotation and Joint Iterative Quantization (Joint-ITQ). This approach acts as a geometric preconditioner, aligning coherent latent distributions with the binary hypercube with zero inference overhead. Empirically, LittleBit-2 establishes a new state-of-the-art in the sub-1-bit regime (1$\sim$0.1 bpp) on Llama-2 and Llama-3, matching the fidelity of leading 1-bit baselines.

AI.tldr: LittleBit-2 leverages low-rank binary approximations with a geometry-aware preconditioning to surpass tiny-rank FP baselines in heavy-tailed spectra. It uses Internal Latent Rotation and Joint ITQ to align latent vectors with a binary hypercube, enabling efficient sub-1-bit quantization.
AI.motivation: Binary quantization of heavy-tailed spectra suffers from high coherence of standard singular vectors (Latent Geometry Misalignment). This degrades potential gains from low-rank binary approximations. A geometric preconditioner can align latent distributions to the binary hypercube with no inference cost.
AI.method: Internal Latent Rotation plus Joint Iterative Quantization (Joint-ITQ) to reorient latent representations before binary quantization, serving as a zero-overhead geometric preconditioner.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance in the sub-1-bit regime (1 to 0.1 bits per parameter) on Llama-2 and Llama-3, matching fidelity of leading 1-bit baselines.
AI.conclusion: Latent geometry alignment via Internal Latent Rotation and Joint-ITQ enables effective sub-1-bit binary quantization for heavy-tailed spectra, offering practical compression gains without extra inference cost.

title: Monotropic Artificial Intelligence: Toward a Cognitive Taxonomy of Domain-Specialized Language Models
summary: The prevailing paradigm in artificial intelligence research equates progress with scale: larger models trained on broader datasets are presumed to yield superior capabilities. This assumption, while empirically productive for general-purpose applications, obscures a fundamental epistemological tension between breadth and depth of knowledge. We introduce the concept of \emph{Monotropic Artificial Intelligence} -- language models that deliberately sacrifice generality to achieve extraordinary precision within narrowly circumscribed domains. Drawing on the cognitive theory of monotropism developed to understand autistic cognition, we argue that intense specialization represents not a limitation but an alternative cognitive architecture with distinct advantages for safety-critical applications. We formalize the defining characteristics of monotropic models, contrast them with conventional polytropic architectures, and demonstrate their viability through Mini-Enedina, a 37.5-million-parameter model that achieves near-perfect performance on Timoshenko beam analysis while remaining deliberately incompetent outside its domain. Our framework challenges the implicit assumption that artificial general intelligence constitutes the sole legitimate aspiration of AI research, proposing instead a cognitive ecology in which specialized and generalist systems coexist complementarily.

AI.tldr: Monotropic AI: a proposition for highly specialized, domain-focused models that prioritize depth over generality, demonstrated by Mini-Enedina achieving near-perfect results on Timoshenko beam analysis, with deliberate incompetence elsewhere; advocates a cognitive-ecology of coexisting specialized and generalist systems.
AI.motivation: Challenging the prevailing AI paradigm that equates progress with scale and generality. The abstract argues that breadth and depth represent distinct epistemological strategies, drawing on monotropism to justify specialized cognition as potentially safer and more reliable in safety-critical domains.
AI.method: Define and formalize the characteristics of monotropic models, contrast with polytropic generalist architectures, and instantiate the concept with Mini-Enedina (37.5M parameters) that excels in Timoshenko beam analysis while being intentionally poor in other tasks; discuss implications for safety, reliability, and AI ecosystem design.
AI.result: Empirical demonstration that a small, domain-focused model can achieve near-perfect performance in a specific engineering domain (Timoshenko beam analysis) while remaining incompetent in unrelated tasks, thus validating the viability of monotropic architectures as a complement to generalist models.
AI.conclusion: Specialized, monotropic models offer a viable cognitive architecture for safety-critical tasks and should coexist with generalist AI, forming a complementary cognitive ecology rather than pursuing AGI as the sole legitimate AI goal.

title: PEPA: a Persistently Autonomous Embodied Agent with Personalities
summary: Living organisms exhibit persistent autonomy through internally generated goals and self-sustaining behavioral organization, yet current embodied agents remain driven by externally scripted objectives. This dependence on predefined task specifications limits their capacity for long-term deployment in dynamic, unstructured environments where continuous human intervention is impractical. We propose that personality traits provide an intrinsic organizational principle for achieving persistent autonomy. Analogous to genotypic biases shaping biological behavioral tendencies, personalities enable agents to autonomously generate goals and sustain behavioral evolution without external supervision. To realize this, we develop PEPA, a three-layer cognitive architecture that operates through three interacting systems: Sys3 autonomously synthesizes personality-aligned goals and refines them via episodic memory and daily self-reflection; Sys2 performs deliberative reasoning to translate goals into executable action plans; Sys1 grounds the agent in sensorimotor interaction, executing actions and recording experiences. We validate the framework through real-world deployment on a quadruped robot in a multi-floor office building. Operating without reliance on fixed task specifications, the robot autonomously arbitrates between user requests and personality-driven motivations, navigating elevators and exploring environments accordingly. Quantitative analysis across five distinct personality prototypes demonstrates stable, trait-aligned behaviors. The results confirm that personality-driven cognitive architectures enable sustained autonomous operation characteristic of persistent embodied systems. Code and demo videos are available at https://sites.google.com/view/pepa-persistent/.

AI.tldr: PEPA introduces a three-layer personality-driven cognitive architecture to achieve persistent autonomy in embodied agents, validated on a quadruped robot exploring a multi-floor office without fixed task specs.
AI.motivation: Current embodied agents rely on externally scripted objectives, limiting long-term deployment in dynamic, unstructured environments. The work argues that intrinsic personality traits can structure autonomous goal generation and behavioral evolution.
AI.method: PEPA consists of Sys3 (personality-aligned goal synthesis, episodic memory, daily self-reflection), Sys2 (deliberative reasoning translating goals into action plans), and Sys1 (sensorimotor grounding and experience recording). The system is deployed on a real quadruped robot across multiple floors, with five personality prototypes; it autonomously arbiters between user requests and personality motivations and navigates elevators and exploration tasks.
AI.result: Five personality prototypes yield stable, trait-aligned behaviors; the robot operates without fixed tasks, demonstrating continued autonomy and adaptive navigation in a real environment; quantitative analyses support persistence and alignment with traits.
AI.conclusion: Personality-driven cognitive architectures can enable sustained autonomous operation in embodied systems, offering a viable path beyond externally scripted tasks; the work provides open code and demos for verification.

title: Reinforcement Learning for Control with Probabilistic Stability Guarantee: A Finite-Sample Approach
summary: This paper presents a novel approach to reinforcement learning (RL) for control systems that provides probabilistic stability guarantees using finite data. Leveraging Lyapunov's method, we propose a probabilistic stability theorem that ensures mean square stability using only a finite number of sampled trajectories. The probability of stability increases with the number and length of trajectories, converging to certainty as data size grows. Additionally, we derive a policy gradient theorem for stabilizing policy learning and develop an RL algorithm, L-REINFORCE, that extends the classical REINFORCE algorithm to stabilization problems. The effectiveness of L-REINFORCE is demonstrated through simulations on a Cartpole task, where it outperforms the baseline in ensuring stability. This work bridges a critical gap between RL and control theory, enabling stability analysis and controller design in a model-free framework with finite data.

AI.tldr: A finite-data RL framework that offers probabilistic stability guarantees via Lyapunov methods, introducing a stability theorem, a stabilizing policy gradient, and the L-REINFORCE algorithm, validated on Cartpole; claims to bridge RL and control for model-free stability with finite data.
AI.motivation: Address the lack of finite-data, model-free stability guarantees in RL for control systems and to fuse control-theoretic stability with data-driven policy learning.
AI.method: Develops a Lyapunov-based probabilistic stability theorem with finite-sample guarantees; derives a policy-gradient theorem for stabilizing policy learning; introduces L-REINFORCE, an extension of REINFORCE tailored for stabilization tasks; analyzes how stability probability increases with more/longer trajectories.
AI.result: The proposed framework yields probabilistic mean-square stability with finite data; L-REINFORCE demonstrates improved stability on Cartpole tasks relative to a baseline.
AI.conclusion: Offers a principled bridge between RL and classical control by enabling stability analysis and controller design in a model-free setting with finite data, where stability becomes more certain as data grows.

title: CT-Flow: Orchestrating CT Interpretation Workflow with Model Context Protocol Servers
summary: Recent advances in Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) have shown strong potential for multi-modal radiological reasoning, particularly in tasks like diagnostic visual question answering (VQA) and radiology report generation. However, most existing approaches for 3D CT analysis largely rely on static, single-pass inference. In practice, clinical interpretation is a dynamic, tool-mediated workflow where radiologists iteratively review slices and use measurement, radiomics, and segmentation tools to refine findings. To bridge this gap, we propose CT-Flow, an agentic framework designed for interoperable volumetric interpretation. By leveraging the Model Context Protocol (MCP), CT-Flow shifts from closed-box inference to an open, tool-aware paradigm. We curate CT-FlowBench, the first large-scale instruction-tuning benchmark tailored for 3D CT tool-use and multi-step reasoning. Built upon this, CT-Flow functions as a clinical orchestrator capable of decomposing complex natural language queries into automated tool-use sequences. Experimental evaluations on CT-FlowBench and standard 3D VQA datasets demonstrate that CT-Flow achieves state-of-the-art performance, surpassing baseline models by 41% in diagnostic accuracy and achieving a 95% success rate in autonomous tool invocation.
