Moreover, even at scale, several continual learning methods outperform naive continual pretraining. The CoPeP benchmark offers an exciting opportunity to study these methods at scale in an impactful real-world application.

AI.tldr: Introduces CoPeP, a continual pretraining benchmark for protein language models (pLMs) across a decade of UniProt-derived data; evaluates continual learning methods on 31 protein tasks; shows temporal meta-information improves perplexity and some methods beat naive continual pretraining; provides scalable real-world evaluation platform.
AI.motivation: Address how pLMs can continually adapt to growing, temporally ordered protein data and leverage temporal information to improve performance.
AI.method: Assemble CoPeP by curating a sequence of UniProt-derived protein datasets spanning a decade, define 31 protein-understanding tasks, and evaluate continual learning methods (replay, unlearning, plasticity-based) on pLMs at scale.
AI.result: Temporal meta-information yields up to 7% perplexity improvement over joint training on all data; several continual learning methods outperform naive continual pretraining even at scale.
AI.conclusion: CoPeP enables scalable, real-world evaluation of continual learning for protein language models and highlights the value of incorporating temporal information for improved performance.

title: FlowPortrait: Reinforcement Learning for Audio-Driven Portrait Video Generation
summary: Generating realistic talking-head videos remains challenging due to persistent issues such as imperfect lip synchronization, unnatural motion, and evaluation metrics that correlate poorly with human perception. We propose FlowPortrait, a reinforcement-learning framework for audio-driven portrait animation built on a multimodal backbone for autoregressive audio-to-video generation. FlowPortrait introduces a human-aligned evaluation system based on Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) to assess lip-sync accuracy, expressiveness, and motion quality. These signals are combined with perceptual and temporal consistency regularizers to form a stable composite reward, which is used to post-train the generator via Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO). Extensive experiments, including both automatic evaluations and human preference studies, demonstrate that FlowPortrait consistently produces higher-quality talking-head videos, highlighting the effectiveness of reinforcement learning for portrait animation.

AI.tldr: FlowPortrait: an RL-based audio-driven portrait animation framework using a multimodal autoregressive video generator, with MLLM-based lip-sync/expressiveness/motion evaluation and GRPO-based post-training, yielding superior talking-head videos according to automatic metrics and human preferences.
AI.motivation: To overcome persistent problems in talking-head generation—perturbed lip synchronization, unnatural motion, and evaluation misalignment with human perception—by introducing an RL-based framework that optimizes for perceptually aligned rewards.
AI.method: A multimodal autoregressive audio-to-video generator (FlowPortrait) is fine-tuned via reinforcement learning using a composite reward: (i) signals derived from Multimodal Large Language Models (for lip-sync, expressiveness, motion), (ii) perceptual and temporal consistency regularizers. The generator is updated through Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) as a post-training step.
AI.result: Experiments show FlowPortrait produces higher-quality talking-head videos than baselines, with improvements reflected in automatic evaluations and human preference studies, demonstrating the effectiveness of RL for portrait animation.
AI.conclusion: RL optimization with MLLM-aligned evaluation signals can substantially improve lip-sync accuracy, expressiveness, and motion quality in audio-driven portrait animation, making FlowPortrait a strong framework for realistic talking-head generation.

title: K^2-Agent: Co-Evolving Know-What and Know-How for Hierarchical Mobile Device Control
summary: Existing mobile device control agents often perform poorly when solving complex tasks requiring long-horizon planning and precise operations, typically due to a lack of relevant task experience or unfamiliarity with skill execution. We propose K2-Agent, a hierarchical framework that models human-like cognition by separating and co-evolving declarative (knowing what) and procedural (knowing how) knowledge for planning and execution. K2-Agent's high level reasoner is bootstrapped from a single demonstration per task and runs a Summarize-Reflect-Locate-Revise (SRLR) loop to distill and iteratively refine task-level declarative knowledge through self-evolution. The low-level executor is trained with our curriculum-guided Group Relative Policy Optimization (C-GRPO), which (i) constructs a balanced sample pool using decoupled reward signals and (ii) employs dynamic demonstration injection to guide the model in autonomously generating successful trajectories for training. On the challenging AndroidWorld benchmark, K2-Agent achieves a 76.1% success rate using only raw screenshots and open-source backbones. Furthermore, K2-Agent shows powerful dual generalization: its high-level declarative knowledge transfers across diverse base models, while its low-level procedural skills achieve competitive performance on unseen tasks in ScreenSpot-v2 and Android-in-the-Wild (AitW).

AI.tldr: K2-Agent is a hierarchical mobile-control framework that learns long-horizon planning and precise execution via a SRLR loop for declarative knowledge and a curriculum-guided GRPO for procedural skills; it achieves strong performance and transfers across models and tasks.
AI.motivation: Address poor performance of existing mobile agents on long-horizon tasks by separating declarative (what) and procedural (how) knowledge and bootstrapping from minimal demonstrations.
AI.method: A two-level architecture: a high-level reasoner with SRLR loop to distill and refine task-level declarative knowledge; a low-level executor trained with curriculum-guided Group Relative Policy Optimization (C-GRPO) using decoupled rewards and dynamic demonstration injection to generate successful trajectories.
AI.result: On AndroidWorld, K2-Agent attains 76.1% success using only raw screenshots and open-source backbones; high-level declarative knowledge transfers across base models; low-level skills show competitive performance on unseen tasks in ScreenSpot-v2 and Android-in-the-Wild (AitW).
AI.conclusion: The framework demonstrates dual generalization—declarative knowledge transfers across base models and procedural skills generalizing to unseen tasks—making it a promising approach for scalable, long-horizon mobile task solving.

title: UniHM: Unified Dexterous Hand Manipulation with Vision Language Model
summary: Planning physically feasible dexterous hand manipulation is a central challenge in robotic manipulation and Embodied AI. Prior work typically relies on object-centric cues or precise hand-object interaction sequences, foregoing the rich, compositional guidance of open-vocabulary instruction. We introduce UniHM, the first framework for unified dexterous hand manipulation guided by free-form language commands. We propose a Unified Hand-Dexterous Tokenizer that maps heterogeneous dexterous-hand morphologies into a single shared codebook, improving cross-dexterous hand generalization and scalability to new morphologies. Our vision language action model is trained solely on human-object interaction data, eliminating the need for massive real-world teleoperation datasets, and demonstrates strong generalizability in producing human-like manipulation sequences from open-ended language instructions. To ensure physical realism, we introduce a physics-guided dynamic refinement module that performs segment-wise joint optimization under generative and temporal priors, yielding smooth and physically feasible manipulation sequences. Across multiple datasets and real-world evaluations, UniHM attains state-of-the-art results on both seen and unseen objects and trajectories, demonstrating strong generalization and high physical feasibility. Our project page at \href{https://unihm.github.io/}{https://unihm.github.io/}.

AI.tldr: UniHM introduces a unified framework for open-vocabulary, cross-morphology dexterous hand manipulation using a shared codebook, a language-conditioned policy trained on human–object interaction data, and physics-guided refinement to ensure physically feasible motions, achieving state-of-the-art results on seen and unseen objects and trajectories.
AI.motivation: The field struggles with reliance on object-centric cues and the need for extensive teleoperation data; there is a demand for open-vocabulary instructions and generalization across diverse hand morphologies while maintaining physical plausibility.
AI.method: 1) Unified Hand-Dexterous Tokenizer maps heterogeneous hand morphologies into a single shared codebook to improve cross-morphology generalization. 2) Vision-language action model trained solely on human–object interaction data to translate open-ended language into manipulation sequences, avoiding large teleoperation datasets. 3) Physics-guided dynamic refinement performs segment-wise joint optimization under generative and temporal priors to produce smooth, physically feasible sequences.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on multiple datasets and real-world evaluations for both seen and unseen objects and trajectories, with strong generalization to new morphologies and high physical feasibility in generated manipulation sequences.
AI.conclusion: UniHM provides a scalable, generalizable framework for open-vocabulary dexterous manipulation across diverse hand morphologies, with reduced data requirements and demonstrated physical realism; the project page is available at the provided URL.

title: Scalable Gaussian process modeling of parametrized spatio-temporal fields
summary: We introduce a scalable Gaussian process (GP) framework with deep product kernels for data-driven learning of parametrized spatio-temporal fields over fixed or parameter-dependent domains. The proposed framework learns a continuous representation, enabling predictions at arbitrary spatio-temporal coordinates, independent of the training data resolution. We leverage Kronecker matrix algebra to formulate a computationally efficient training procedure with complexity that scales nearly linearly with the total number of spatio-temporal grid points. A key feature of our approach is the efficient computation of the posterior variance at essentially the same computational cost as the posterior mean (exactly for Cartesian grids and via rigorous bounds for unstructured grids), thereby enabling scalable uncertainty quantification. Numerical studies on a range of benchmark problems demonstrate that the proposed method achieves accuracy competitive with operator learning methods such as Fourier neural operators and deep operator networks. On the one-dimensional unsteady Burgers' equation, our method surpasses the accuracy of projection-based reduced-order models. These results establish the proposed framework as an effective tool for data-driven surrogate modeling, particularly when uncertainty estimates are required for downstream tasks.

AI.tldr: A scalable Gaussian process with deep product kernels for continuous, parametric spatio-temporal fields, enabling arbitrary-coordinate predictions and scalable uncertainty quantification. It uses Kronecker algebra for near-linear training complexity, achieves competitive accuracy with operator-learning methods, and outperforms projection-based ROMs on Burgers' equation.
AI.motivation: Address the need for uncertainty-aware, data-driven surrogates that (i) predict at arbitrary spatio-temporal coordinates, (ii) scale to large grids, and (iii) remain competitive with modern operator-learning methods.
AI.method: Employ deep product kernels within a Gaussian process framework, leveraging Kronecker matrix algebra to ensure near-linear training complexity in the number of grid points. Enables efficient posterior variance computation (cost comparable to the posterior mean: exact for Cartesian grids, bounded for unstructured grids). Supports fixed or parameter-dependent domains and continuous representation for coordinate-free predictions.
AI.result: Empirically competitive with Fourier neural operators and deep operator networks; on the 1D unsteady Burgers' equation, surpasses projection-based reduced-order models; demonstrates scalable uncertainty quantification suitable for downstream tasks.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework is an effective data-driven surrogate modeling tool, offering scalable uncertainty quantification and accurate predictions at arbitrary coordinates, suitable for complex spatio-temporal problems.

title: DINOv3 Meets YOLO26 for Weed Detection in Vegetable Crops
summary: Developing robust models for precision vegetable weeding is currently constrained by the scarcity of large-scale, annotated weed-crop datasets. To address this limitation, this study proposes a foundational crop-weed detection model by integrating heterogeneous datasets and leveraging self-supervised learning. A total of 618,642 crop-weed images were initially collected and subsequently refined to 199,388 filtered images for fine-tuning a DINOv3 vision transformer (ViT-small) through a sequential curation strategy. The fine-tuned DINOv3 backbone was then integrated into YOLO26, serving either as a primary backbone or part of a dual-backbone architecture. A feature alignment loss was introduced in the dual backbone framework to enhance feature fusion with minimal computational overhead. Experimental results show that the proposed DINOv3-finetuned ViT-small-based YOLO26-large achieved up to a +5.4% mAP50 gain on in-domain images collected in the 2025 season. Moreover, it demonstrated strong cross-domain generalization with mAP50 improvements of +14.0% on the 2021-2023 season dataset and +11.9% on the 2024 season dataset, compared to the standard YOLO26-large. Although the DINOv3-YOLO26-large model has 45.6% more parameters and a 2.9x increase in inference latency, it maintains real-time performance at ~28.5 frames per second (fps). The curated dataset and software programs developed in this study will be made publicly available.

AI.tldr: A DINOv3-finetuned ViT-small backbone is integrated into YOLO26 for crop-weed detection, achieving improved mAP50 in-domain and cross-domain performance via a dual-backbone with feature alignment, at real-time speed albeit with higher parameter count.
AI.motivation: Address the lack of large, annotated weed-crop datasets hindering robust precision weeding models.
AI.method: Collect a large heterogeneous dataset (618,642 images; refined to 199,388) to fine-tune a DINOv3 ViT-small backbone, then integrate it into YOLO26 as a primary or dual-backbone; introduce a feature alignment loss to improve fusion with low overhead.
AI.result: In-domain mAP50 improved up to +5.4%; cross-domain gains of +14.0% (2021–2023) and +11.9% (2024) over standard YOLO26-large. Model has 45.6% more params and 2.9x latency, yet runs at ~28.5 fps.
AI.conclusion: The curated dataset and software will be publicly released, establishing a robust foundation for crop-weed detection with better cross-domain generalization, suitable for real-time precision weed control.

title: MemPO: Self-Memory Policy Optimization for Long-Horizon Agents
summary: Long-horizon agents face the challenge of growing context size during interaction with environment, which degrades the performance and stability. Existing methods typically introduce the external memory module and look up the relevant information from the stored memory, which prevents the model itself from proactively managing its memory content and aligning with the agent's overarching task objectives. To address these limitations, we propose the self-memory policy optimization algorithm (MemPO), which enables the agent (policy model) to autonomously summarize and manage their memory during interaction with environment. By improving the credit assignment mechanism based on memory effectiveness, the policy model can selectively retain crucial information, significantly reducing token consumption while preserving task performance. Extensive experiments and analyses confirm that MemPO achieves absolute F1 score gains of 25.98% over the base model and 7.1% over the previous SOTA baseline, while reducing token usage by 67.58% and 73.12%.

AI.tldr: MemPO enables a policy to autonomously summarize and manage its memory, improving long-horizon task performance while greatly reducing token usage.
AI.motivation: Long-horizon agents face magnifying context requirements that degrade performance and stability. External memory with retrieval keeps the policy from proactively shaping memory and aligning it with global task objectives, leading to inefficiencies and increased token cost.
AI.method: Introduce MemPO, a self-memory policy optimization algorithm that lets the policy model summarize and curate its memory online. It strengthens credit assignment based on memory effectiveness to selectively retain crucial information, reducing token usage without sacrificing task performance.
AI.result: Absolute F1 score gains of 25.98 percentage points over the base model and 7.1 percentage points over the previous SOTA baseline, while token usage is reduced by 67.58% and 73.12%, respectively.
AI.conclusion: MemPO demonstrates that enabling policy-level memory management is an effective and efficient approach for long-horizon agents, achieving higher performance with substantially lower memory token costs.

title: Online Generation of Collision-Free Trajectories in Dynamic Environments
comment: Submitted to IEEE Robotics and Automation Letters (RA-L)
summary: In this paper, we present an online method for converting an arbitrary geometric path represented by a sequence of states, generated by any planner (e.g., sampling-based planners like RRT or PRM, search-based planners like ARA*, etc.), into a corresponding kinematically feasible, jerk-limited trajectory. The method generates a sequence of quintic/quartic splines that can be discretized at a user-specified control rate, and then streamed to a low-level robot controller. Our approach enables real-time adaptation to newly captured changes in the environment. It can also be re-invoked at any time instance to generate a new trajectory from the robot's current to a desired target state or sequence of states. We can guarantee that the trajectory will remain collision-free for a certain amount of time in dynamic environments, while allowing bounded geometric deviation from the original path. The kinematic constraints are taken into account, including limited jerk. We validate the approach in a comparative simulation study against the competing method, demonstrating favorable behavior w.r.t. smoothness, computational time, and real-time performance, particularly in scenarios with frequent changes of target states (up to 1 [kHz]). Experiments on a real robot demonstrate that the proposed approach can be used in real-world scenarios including human presence.

AI.tldr: Online, jerk-limited trajectory generation from arbitrary planner outputs using quintic/quartic splines, enabling real-time re-planning and collision avoidance with bounded deviation.
AI.motivation: Enable kinematically feasible, smooth, and reactively replannable trajectories in dynamic environments from any planned path, with strict jerk and other kinematic constraints.
AI.method: Convert path to quintic/quartic splines, discretize at control rate, stream to controller; re-invocation from current to target state; ensure collision-free for a bounded time in dynamic scenes; handle target changes up to 1 kHz; compare to existing methods; validate in simulation and on real robot; account for jerk constraints.
AI.result: Shows improved smoothness, faster computation, and real-time performance; robust to frequent target changes; successful real-world experiments including human presence.
AI.conclusion: Provides a practical, real-time, jerk-limited, collision-aware trajectory generation framework from arbitrary planner outputs, suitable for dynamic environments.

title: Polynomial Surrogate Training for Differentiable Ternary Logic Gate Networks
comment: 28 pages, 13 figures. Submitted to 3rd International Conference on Neuro-Symbolic Systems (NeuS) 2026
summary: Differentiable logic gate networks (DLGNs) learn compact, interpretable Boolean circuits via gradient-based training, but all existing variants are restricted to the 16 two-input binary gates. Extending DLGNs to Ternary Kleene $K_3$ logic and training DTLGNs where the UNKNOWN state enables principled abstention under uncertainty is desirable. However, the support set of potential gates per neuron explodes to $19{,}683$, making the established softmax-over-gates training approach intractable. We introduce Polynomial Surrogate Training (PST), which represents each ternary neuron as a degree-$(2,2)$ polynomial with 9 learnable coefficients (a $2{,}187\times$ parameter reduction) and prove that the gap between the trained network and its discretized logic circuit is bounded by a data-independent commitment loss that vanishes at convergence. Scaling experiments from 48K to 512K neurons on CIFAR-10 demonstrate that this hardening gap contracts with overparameterization. Ternary networks train $2$-$3\times$ faster than binary DLGNs and discover true ternary gates that are functionally diverse. On synthetic and tabular tasks we find that the UNKNOWN output acts as a Bayes-optimal uncertainty proxy, enabling selective prediction in which ternary circuits surpass binary accuracy once low-confidence predictions are filtered. More broadly, PST establishes a general polynomial-surrogate methodology whose parameterization cost grows only quadratically with logic valence, opening the door to many-valued differentiable logic.

AI.tldr: PST enables scalable, differentiable many-valued logic by replacing a combinatorial gate search with a polynomial surrogate for ternary neurons, reducing parameters 2,187x and enabling unknown/abstention. Demonstrates faster training, diverse true ternary gates, and Bayes-optimal uncertainty proxy on CIFAR-10, synthetic, and tabular tasks; opens door to many-valued differentiable logic.
AI.motivation: Extend differentiable logic gate networks from binary two-input gates to ternary Kleene logic (K3) and enable principled abstention via an UNKNOWN state. Address the combinatorial explosion of candidate gates per neuron (19,683) with a scalable surrogate training method.
AI.method: Represent each ternary neuron as a degree-(2,2) polynomial with 9 learnable coefficients, yielding a 2,187× parameter reduction from the naive gate-binomial approach. Prove a data-independent commitment loss bounds the gap between the trained network and its discretized logic circuit, vanishing at convergence. Scale experiments from 48K to 512K neurons on CIFAR-10 to study overparameterization effects. Compare training speed (2–3× faster than binary DLGNs) and assess gate diversity and the role of UNKNOWN as an uncertainty proxy.
AI.result: PST achieves a reduced hardening gap with increased overparameterization. Ternary networks train 2–3× faster than binary DLGNs and discover diverse true ternary gates. The UNKNOWN output effectively acts as a Bayes-optimal uncertainty proxy, enabling selective prediction in which ternary circuits outperform binary accuracy after filtering low-confidence predictions. Parameterization cost grows quadratically with logic valence, enabling scalable many-valued differentiable logic.
AI.conclusion: PST provides a general polynomial-surrogate framework for differentiable many-valued logic, maintaining tractable parameter growth (quadratic in logic valence) and enabling principled abstention through an UNKNOWN state. This approach broadens the scope of differentiable logic beyond binary gates and paves the way for robust, scalable many-valued logical models.

title: SKINOPATHY AI: Smartphone-Based Ophthalmic Screening and Longitudinal Tracking Using Lightweight Computer Vision
summary: Early ophthalmic screening in low-resource and remote settings is constrained by access to specialized equipment and trained practitioners. We present SKINOPATHY AI, a smartphone-first web application that delivers five complementary, explainable screening modules entirely through commodity mobile hardware: (1) redness quantification via LAB a* color-space normalization; (2) blink-rate estimation using MediaPipe FaceMesh Eye Aspect Ratio (EAR) with adaptive thresholding; (3) pupil light reflex characterization through Pupil-to-Iris Ratio (PIR) time-series analysis; (4) scleral color indexing foricterus and anemia proxies via LAB/HSV statistics; and (5) iris-landmark-calibrated lesion encroachment measurement with millimeter-scale estimates and longitudinal trend tracking. The system is implemented as a React/FastAPI stack with OpenCV and MediaPipe, MongoDB-backed session persistence, and PDF report generation. All algorithms are fully deterministic, privacy-preserving, and designed for non-diagnostic consumer triage. We detail system architecture, algorithm design, evaluation methodology, clinical context, and ethical boundaries of the platform. SKINOPATHY AI demonstrates that multi-signal ophthalmic screening is feasible on unmodified smartphones without cloud-based AI inference, providing a foundation for future clinically validated mobile ophthalmoscopy tools.

AI.tldr: A smartphone-first, on-device, explainable multi-signal ophthalmic screening platform (SKINOPATHY AI) with five modules (redness, blink rate, PIR, scleral color, iris lesion measurement) that runs without cloud AI, using deterministic, privacy-preserving algorithms; architecture and evaluation framework described; aims for non-diagnostic triage in low-resource settings and longitudinal tracking.
AI.motivation: Address access barriers for early ophthalmic screening in low-resource and remote settings by removing reliance on specialized equipment and cloud-based AI, enabling on-device, explainable, multi-signal screening with privacy preservation.
AI.method: Five modules implemented on commodity devices: redness quantified via LAB a* normalization; blink-rate via MediaPipe Eye Aspect Ratio with adaptive thresholding; PIR time-series for pupil light reflex; scleral color indexing via LAB/HSV statistics; iris-landmark-calibrated lesion encroachment with mm-scale estimates and longitudinal tracking. Stack: React/FastAPI, OpenCV, MediaPipe, MongoDB for sessions, PDF reporting. All algorithms are deterministic and privacy-preserving; no cloud inference; evaluation methodology and ethical boundaries discussed.
AI.result: Demonstrates feasibility of multi-signal ophthalmic screening on unmodified smartphones without cloud AI inference; provides system architecture, algorithm design, and evaluation framework; establishes groundwork for future clinically validated mobile ophthalmoscopy tools.
AI.conclusion: Shows that comprehensive, explainable, on-device ophthalmic screening is feasible in low-resource settings and can form the basis for clinically validated tools, while emphasizing non-diagnostic use and strict privacy/ethical considerations.

title: AIoT-based Continuous, Contextualized, and Explainable Driving Assessment for Older Adults
summary: The world is undergoing a major demographic shift as older adults become a rapidly growing share of the population, creating new challenges for driving safety. In car-dependent regions such as the United States, driving remains essential for independence, access to services, and social participation. At the same time, aging can introduce gradual changes in vision, attention, reaction time, and driving control that quietly reduce safety. Today's assessment methods rely largely on infrequent clinic visits or simple screening tools, offering only a brief snapshot and failing to reflect how an older adult actually drives on the road. Our work starts from the observation that everyday driving provides a continuous record of functional ability and captures how a driver responds to traffic, navigates complex roads, and manages routine behavior. Leveraging this insight, we propose AURA, an Artificial Intelligence of Things (AIoT) framework for continuous, real-world assessment of driving safety among older adults. AURA integrates richer in-vehicle sensing, multi-scale behavioral modeling, and context-aware analysis to extract detailed indicators of driving performance from routine trips. It organizes fine-grained actions into longer behavioral trajectories and separates age-related performance changes from situational factors such as traffic, road design, or weather. By integrating sensing, modeling, and interpretation within a privacy-preserving edge architecture, AURA provides a foundation for proactive, individualized support that helps older adults drive safely. This paper outlines the design principles, challenges, and research opportunities needed to build reliable, real-world monitoring systems that promote safer aging behind the wheel.

AI.tldr: AURA is a privacy-preserving AIoT edge framework for continuous, real-world assessment of driving safety in older adults, converting in-vehicle data into actionable age-related indicators while accounting for context.
AI.motivation: With an aging population, reliance on episodic clinical assessments fails to capture real-world driving safety. Continuous monitoring in everyday driving can reveal how aging affects driving and support proactive interventions.
AI.method: An AIoT architecture that fuses richer in-vehicle sensing, multi-scale behavioral modeling, and context-aware analysis. It structures fine-grained actions into behavioral trajectories, separates age-related changes from situational factors (traffic, weather, road design), and uses privacy-preserving edge computation to enable real-world monitoring and personalized support.
AI.result: Conceptual framework and design blueprint. Outlines design principles, architectural choices, and key research challenges; identifies opportunities, evaluation strategies, and potential use cases for reliable real-world monitoring of older drivers.
AI.conclusion: Provides a foundation for proactive, individualized support to maintain safe driving in aging populations. Emphasizes privacy, reliability, and real-world applicability, while calling for further development and validation.

title: Hippo: High-performance Interior-Point and Projection-based Solver for Generic Constrained Trajectory Optimization
summary: Trajectory optimization is the core of modern model-based robotic control and motion planning. Existing trajectory optimizers, based on sequential quadratic programming (SQP) or differential dynamic programming (DDP), are often limited by their slow computation efficiency, low modeling flexibility, and poor convergence for complex tasks requiring hard constraints. In this paper, we introduce Hippo, a solver that can handle inequality constraints using the interior-point method (IPM) with an adaptive barrier update strategy and hard equality constraints via projection or IPM. Through extensive numerical benchmarks, we show that Hippo is a robust and efficient alternative to existing state-of-the-art solvers for difficult robotic trajectory optimization problems requiring high-quality solutions, such as locomotion and manipulation.

AI.tldr: Hippo is an IPM-based trajectory optimizer with adaptive barrier updates that handles inequalities and hard equalities, offering robust, efficient performance for challenging robotic tasks.
AI.motivation: To address slow, inflexible, and poorly convergent trajectory optimizers (SQP/DDP) when solving constrained robotics problems, especially those with hard equality and inequality constraints.
AI.method: Develop Hippo: interior-point method for inequality constraints with adaptive barrier updates; hard equality constraints enforced via projection or IPM; designed for trajectory optimization in robotics.
AI.result: Extensive numerical benchmarks indicate Hippo is robust and efficient, competing favorably with state-of-the-art solvers on difficult tasks such as locomotion and manipulation.
AI.conclusion: Hippo provides a versatile, high-quality solver for constrained robotic trajectory optimization, expanding the practical capabilities of model-based planning and control.

title: When does Chain-of-Thought Help: A Markovian Perspective
summary: Chain-of-Thought (CoT) prompting is a widely used inference-time technique for improving reasoning, yet its gains are uneven across tasks. We analyze when and why CoT helps by modeling the step-wise reasoning trajectory as a Markov chain. Each intermediate step is a state and the dependence between steps is captured by a transition kernel. Our theory identifies transition alignment, whether instances share a common step-wise transition kernel, as the key determinant of CoT's effectiveness. When transitions are identical across steps, CoT reduces inference-time sample complexity: fewer context sample trajectories suffice to recover the final decision. In contrast, when transitions differ across steps, these gains can vanish. We further quantify how noise in intermediate steps modulates CoT's benefit. Beyond theory, we design synthetic benchmarks that isolate these factors to complement prior results on real-world tasks and to empirically validate our predictions.

AI.tldr: CoT effectiveness hinges on transition alignment in a step-wise reasoning Markov model; identical step transitions yield sample-efficiency gains in inference, while heterogeneous transitions can erase them; noise modulates benefits; synthetic benchmarks validate theory.
AI.motivation: To understand why Chain-of-Thought prompting yields uneven gains by formalizing reasoning as a Markov chain and identifying the conditions under which CoT provides improvements.
AI.method: Model each intermediate reasoning step as a state in a Markov chain with a transition kernel capturing step-to-step dependencies. Define transition alignment as the sharing of a common step-wise kernel across steps. Derive implications for inference-time sample complexity and quantify the role of noise. Create synthetic benchmarks isolating transition alignment and noise factors to empirically validate theoretical predictions.
AI.result: The theory identifies transition alignment as the key determinant of CoT efficacy: when step transitions are identical, CoT reduces the number of context trajectories needed to recover the final decision (lower sample complexity); when transitions differ across steps, CoT gains can vanish. Noise in intermediate steps modulates the benefit, and synthetic benchmarks corroborate the predictions alongside prior real-world task results.
AI.conclusion: CoT is advantageous primarily under transition-aligned (kernel-identical) stepwise reasoning. Practically, promoting kernel-consistent transitions or recognizing when stepwise dependencies diverge can set expectations for CoT gains; synthetic benchmarks provide a controlled validation framework for these hypotheses.

title: A Boundary-Metric Evaluation Protocol for Whiteboard Stroke Segmentation Under Extreme Imbalance
summary: The binary segmentation of whiteboard strokes is hindered by extreme class imbalance, caused by stroke pixels that constitute only $1.79%$ of the image on average, and in addition, the thin-stroke subset averages $1.14% \pm 0.41%$ in the foreground. Standard region metrics (F1, IoU) can mask thin-stroke failures because the vast majority of the background dominates the score. In contrast, adding boundary-aware metrics and a thin-subset equity analysis changes how loss functions rank and exposes hidden trade-offs. We contribute an evaluation protocol that jointly examines region metrics, boundary metrics (BF1, B-IoU), a core/thin-subset equity analysis, and per-image robustness statistics (median, IQR, worst-case) under seeded, multi-run training with non-parametric significance testing. Five losses -- cross-entropy, focal, Dice, Dice+focal, and Tversky -- are trained three times each on a DeepLabV3-MobileNetV3 model and evaluated on 12 held-out images split into core and thin subsets. Overlap-based losses improve F1 by more than 20 points over cross-entropy ($0.663$ vs $0.438$, $p < 0.001$). In addition, the boundary metrics confirm that the gain extends to the precision of the contour. Adaptive thresholding and Sauvola binarization at native resolution achieve a higher mean F1 ($0.787$ for Sauvola) but with substantially worse worst-case performance (F1 $= 0.452$ vs $0.565$ for Tversky), exposing a consistency-accuracy trade-off: classical baselines lead on mean F1 while the learned model delivers higher worst-case reliability. Doubling training resolution further increases F1 by 12.7 points.

AI.tldr: Evaluation protocol and loss analysis for imbalanced whiteboard stroke segmentation; finds boundary-aware metrics and higher resolution improve performance, but there are trade-offs between mean F1 and worst-case reliability.
AI.motivation: Extreme foreground class imbalance (stroke ~1.79% of pixels; thin-stroke ~1.14% ±0.41%), making region metrics misleading; need boundary-aware metrics and equity analysis to reveal hidden trade-offs.
AI.method: Train five losses (cross-entropy, focal, Dice, Dice+focal, Tversky) on DeepLabV3-MobileNetV3; 3 runs each; 12 held-out images split into core and thin; evaluate using region metrics (F1, IoU), boundary metrics (BF1, B-IoU); core/thin-subset equity analysis; per-image robustness statistics (median, IQR, worst-case); seeded multi-run with non-parametric significance; evaluate adaptive thresholding and Sauvola binarization at native resolution; double training resolution.
AI.result: Overlap-based losses improve F1 by >20 points (0.663 vs 0.438, p<0.001); boundary metrics show contour precision gain; Sauvola binarization achieves mean F1 0.787 but worst-case F1 0.452 vs 0.565 for Tversky; consistency-accuracy trade-off; doubling training resolution raises F1 by 12.7 points.
AI.conclusion: An evaluation protocol that jointly reports region and boundary metrics plus thin-subset equity reveals hidden trade-offs; learned models can offer better worst-case reliability, while classical baselines may yield higher mean F1; resolution increase further boosts F1; boundary-aware metrics are essential for imbalanced segmentation tasks.

title: MO-MIX: Multi-Objective Multi-Agent Cooperative Decision-Making With Deep Reinforcement Learning
comment: 15 pages, 10 figures, published in IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence (TPAMI)
summary: Deep reinforcement learning (RL) has been applied extensively to solve complex decision-making problems. In many real-world scenarios, tasks often have several conflicting objectives and may require multiple agents to cooperate, which are the multi-objective multi-agent decision-making problems. However, only few works have been conducted on this intersection. Existing approaches are limited to separate fields and can only handle multi-agent decision-making with a single objective, or multi-objective decision-making with a single agent. In this paper, we propose MO-MIX to solve the multi-objective multi-agent reinforcement learning (MOMARL) problem. Our approach is based on the centralized training with decentralized execution (CTDE) framework. A weight vector representing preference over the objectives is fed into the decentralized agent network as a condition for local action-value function estimation, while a mixing network with parallel architecture is used to estimate the joint action-value function. In addition, an exploration guide approach is applied to improve the uniformity of the final non-dominated solutions. Experiments demonstrate that the proposed method can effectively solve the multi-objective multi-agent cooperative decision-making problem and generate an approximation of the Pareto set. Our approach not only significantly outperforms the baseline method in all four kinds of evaluation metrics, but also requires less computational cost.

AI.tldr: MO-MIX uses centralized training with decentralized execution for multi-objective multi-agent RL, conditioning agents on a weight vector and using a parallel mixing network to estimate joint Q, plus an exploration guide to cover the Pareto front, achieving Pareto-approximation with lower cost.
AI.motivation: Extends MOMARL to the multi-objective, multi-agent setting, addressing the gap where methods either handle multi-agent single-objective or single-agent multi-objective problems.
AI.method: Under CTDE, each agent receives a weight-conditioned local action-value estimate; a parallel mixing network aggregates to form a joint Q-value. An exploration guide promotes uniform coverage of the non-dominated solutions, enabling Pareto-front approximation.
AI.result: Empirically, MO-MIX outperforms baselines across four evaluation metrics, yields a close approximation to the Pareto set, and requires less computational cost.
AI.conclusion: MO-MIX effectively solves MOMARL by integrating weight-conditioned decentralization, a parallel joint-Q mixer, and exploration-guided Pareto-front approximation, with improved performance and efficiency.

title: A Novel Reconfigurable Dexterous Hand Based on Triple-Symmetric Bricard Parallel Mechanism
comment: 8 pages, 14 figures, 2026 IEEE International Conference on Robotics & Automation
summary: This paper introduces a novel design for a robotic hand based on parallel mechanisms. The proposed hand uses a triple-symmetric Bricard linkage as its reconfigurable palm, enhancing adaptability to objects of varying shapes and sizes. Through topological and dimensional synthesis, the mechanism achieves a well-balanced degree of freedom and link configuration suitable for reconfigurable palm motion, balancing dexterity, stability, and load capacity. Furthermore, kinematic analysis is performed using screw theory and closed-loop constraints, and performance is evaluated based on workspace, stiffness, and motion/force transmission efficiency. Finally, a prototype is developed and tested through a series of grasping experiments, demonstrating the ability to perform stable and efficient manipulation across a wide range of objects. The results validate the effectiveness of the design in improving grasping versatility and operational precision, offering a promising solution for advanced robotic manipulation tasks.

AI.tldr: A robotic hand with a reconfigurable palm using a triple-symmetric Bricard linkage enables versatile grasping; topological/dimensional synthesis and screw-theory kinematic analysis support improved workspace, stiffness, and motion/force transmission, validated by a prototype and grasping tests.
AI.motivation: Address variability in object shapes and sizes requiring a dexterous, adaptable hand; overcome limitations of fixed-palm grasping by introducing a reconfigurable palm mechanism.
AI.method: Adopt a triple-symmetric Bricard linkage as reconfigurable palm; perform topological and dimensional synthesis to achieve an appropriate degree of freedom and link configuration; conduct kinematic analysis via screw theory and closed-loop constraints; evaluate workspace, stiffness, and transmission efficiency; build and test a prototype through grasping experiments.
AI.result: Prototype demonstrates stable, efficient manipulation across a wide range of objects; enhanced grasping versatility and precision; validation that the reconfigurable palm achieves favorable DOF and configuration.
AI.conclusion: The design offers a promising approach for advanced robotic manipulation, showing that a tri-symmetric Bricard reconfigurable palm can improve dexterity, stability, and load capacity in grasping tasks.

title: Vectorized Adaptive Histograms for Sparse Oblique Forests
summary: Classification using sparse oblique random forests provides guarantees on uncertainty and confidence while controlling for specific error types. However, they use more data and more compute than other tree ensembles because they create deep trees and need to sort or histogram linear combinations of data at runtime. We provide a method for dynamically switching between histograms and sorting to find the best split. We further optimize histogram construction using vector intrinsics. Evaluating this on large datasets, our optimizations speedup training by 1.7-2.5x compared to existing oblique forests and 1.5-2x compared to standard random forests. We also provide a GPU and hybrid CPU-GPU implementation.

AI.tldr: Dynamic split strategy for sparse oblique random forests that switches between histograms and sorting, with vectorized histogram construction and GPU/CPU-GPU implementations; yields significant training speedups over oblique and standard RF.
AI.motivation: Oblique random forests offer uncertainty guarantees but require deeper trees and runtime sorting/histogramming, leading to higher data and compute costs. Reducing training time while preserving guarantees is desirable for scalability.
AI.method: Introduce a dynamic mechanism to switch between histogram-based and sorting-based splits to find the best split. Optimize histogram construction with vector intrinsics. Extend implementations to GPU and hybrid CPU-GPU architectures. Evaluate on large datasets.
AI.result: Training speedups of 1.7–2.5x compared to sparse oblique forests and 1.5–2x compared to standard random forests. GPU and hybrid CPU-GPU implementations demonstrated.
AI.conclusion: The approach achieves substantial training-time efficiency gains for oblique forests with maintained or improved scalability, enabling practical deployment of uncertainty-guaranteed ensembles on large-scale data.

title: ConFoThinking: Consolidated Focused Attention Driven Thinking for Visual Question Answering
summary: Thinking with Images improves fine-grained VQA for MLLMs by emphasizing visual cues. However, tool-augmented methods depend on the capacity of grounding, which remains unreliable for MLLMs. In parallel, attention-driven methods to crop the Region of Interest (ROIs) are proposed but they are constrained by (1) fragmented attention signals scattered across layers, leading to suboptimal localization and (2) relying on question- or redundant-text-conditioned attention extraction. Our analysis reveals three patterns: MLLMs may attend to the correct region yet generate incorrect coordinates, where-to-look attention is often fragmented across layers, and attention extraction is query-sensitive. Motivated by these, We propose ConFoThinking, a Consolidated-Focused-Attention-Driven Thinking framework that learns to aggregate attention into a designated intermediate layer, from which we mine and zoom in salient regions for downstream visual understanding. Moreover, we extract attention using concise semantic cues of what to look into, which mitigates the semantic noise introduced by question- or redundant-text-based attention extraction. Experiments across five VQA benchmarks demonstrate ConFoThinking significantly improves perception performance. The code, checkpoints, and dataset will be released after being accepted.

AI.tldr: ConFoThinking consolidates attention into an intermediate layer to mine salient regions for VQA in MLLMs, and uses concise semantic cues to extract attention, improving perception across five benchmarks.
AI.motivation: To address unreliable grounding in tool-augmented methods and the limitations of ROI-attention approaches, including fragmented, layer-distributed attention and query-sensitive extraction.
AI.method: Aggregate attention into a designated intermediate layer (ConFoThinking), mine and zoom salient regions for downstream visual understanding, and extract attention using concise semantic cues rather than question/text-conditioned cues.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation across five VQA benchmarks shows significant improvements in perception performance.
AI.conclusion: The approach mitigates attention fragmentation and semantic noise, yielding robust improvements; code/checkpoints/dataset to be released post-acceptance.

title: The Synthetic Web: Adversarially-Curated Mini-Internets for Diagnosing Epistemic Weaknesses of Language Agents
comment: Submitted to ICML 2026, currently under review
summary: Language agents increasingly act as web-enabled systems that search, browse, and synthesize information from diverse sources. However, these sources can include unreliable or adversarial content, and the robustness of agents to adversarial ranking - where misleading information appears prominently in search results - remains poorly understood. Existing benchmarks evaluate functional navigation or static factuality but cannot causally isolate this vulnerability, and current mitigation strategies for retrieval-augmented generation remain largely untested under such conditions. We introduce Synthetic Web Benchmark, a procedurally generated environment comprising thousands of hyperlinked articles with ground-truth labels for credibility and factuality, process-level interaction traces, and contamination filtering to eliminate training-data leakage. By injecting a single high-plausibility misinformation article into a controllable search rank, we measure the causal effect of adversarial exposure in six frontier models. The results reveal catastrophic failures: accuracy collapses despite unlimited access to truthful sources, with minimal search escalation and severe miscalibration. These findings expose fundamental limitations in how current frontier models handle conflicting information, with immediate implications for deployment in high-stakes domains. Our benchmark enables systematic analysis of these failure modes and provides a controlled testbed for evaluating mitigation strategies under adversarial ranking - a gap in current research. This work establishes a reproducible baseline for developing search-robust and epistemically humble agents capable of resisting manipulation in high-stakes domains.

AI.tldr: A procedurally generated Synthetic Web Benchmark tests robustness of language agents to adversarial ranking by injecting a high-plausibility misinformation article; measures causal impact on six frontier models and finds catastrophic failures under manipulation.
AI.motivation: Addresses the lack of causal, isolable evaluation of adversarial information ranking in retrieval-augmented agents; fills a gap in robust, reproducible benchmarks for high-stakes information synthesis.
AI.method: Procedurally generate thousands of hyperlinked articles with ground-truth credibility/factuality labels, provide process-level interaction traces, and filter contamination. Inject a single high-plausibility misinformation article into search results and measure the causal effect on six frontier models.
AI.result: Across six frontier models, accuracy collapses despite access to truthful sources, with minimal escalation of search and severe miscalibration, revealing fundamental limitations in handling conflicting information.
AI.conclusion: The benchmark enables systematic analysis of failure modes under adversarial ranking, provides a controlled testbed for evaluating mitigation strategies, and establishes a reproducible baseline for developing search-robust, epistemically humble agents suitable for high-stakes domains.

title: Minimalist Compliance Control
summary: Compliance control is essential for safe physical interaction, yet its adoption is limited by hardware requirements such as force torque sensors. While recent reinforcement learning approaches aim to bypass these constraints, they often suffer from sim-to-real gaps, lack safety guarantees, and add system complexity. We propose Minimalist Compliance Control, which enables compliant behavior using only motor current or voltage signals readily available in modern servos and quasi-direct-drive motors, without force sensors, current control, or learning. External wrenches are estimated from actuator signals and Jacobians and incorporated into a task-space admittance controller, preserving sufficient force measurement accuracy for stable and responsive compliance control. Our method is embodiment-agnostic and plug-and-play with diverse high-level planners. We validate our approach on a robot arm, a dexterous hand, and two humanoid robots across multiple contact-rich tasks, using vision-language models, imitation learning, and model-based planning. The results demonstrate robust, safe, and compliant interaction across embodiments and planning paradigms.

AI.tldr: Minimalist Compliance Control uses only standard actuator signals (motor current/voltage) to estimate external wrenches and drives a task-space admittance controller, enabling safe, compliant interaction without force/torque sensors, current control, or learning; embodiment-agnostic and plug-and-play with diverse planners.
AI.motivation: Compliance control is essential for safe physical interaction but is often limited by hardware requirements such as force/torque sensing. While RL approaches aim to remove sensors, they can suffer from sim-to-real gaps and safety concerns. A lightweight, sensorless method could broaden deployment across platforms.
AI.method: Estimate external wrenches from actuator signals and Jacobians; integrate these estimates into a task-space admittance controller to achieve compliant behavior. The approach does not rely on force sensors, current control loops, or learning, and is designed to be embodiment-agnostic and compatible with a variety of high-level planners.
AI.result: Empirically validated on a robot arm, a dexterous hand, and two humanoid robots across multiple contact-rich tasks. Demonstrations cover diverse planning paradigms (vision-language models, imitation learning, model-based planning), showing robust, safe, and compliant interaction across embodiments.
AI.conclusion: Offers a practical, sensor-light path to compliant manipulation, preserving stability and responsiveness across devices and planning frameworks, and enabling broader adoption of compliant interaction in real-world robotics.

title: Detecting Transportation Mode Using Dense Smartphone GPS Trajectories and Transformer Models
comment: Accepted for publication in the International Journal of Geographical Information Science, February 2026. This is the accepted manuscript. The final version of record will appear in IJGIS (Taylor and Francis)
summary: Transportation mode detection is an important topic within GeoAI and transportation research. In this study, we introduce SpeedTransformer, a novel Transformer-based model that relies solely on speed inputs to infer transportation modes from dense smartphone GPS trajectories. In benchmark experiments, SpeedTransformer outperformed traditional deep learning models, such as the Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) network. Moreover, the model demonstrated strong flexibility in transfer learning, achieving high accuracy across geographical regions after fine-tuning with small datasets. Finally, we deployed the model in a real-world experiment, where it consistently outperformed baseline models under complex built environments and high data uncertainty. These findings suggest that Transformer architectures, when combined with dense GPS trajectories, hold substantial potential for advancing transportation mode detection and broader mobility-related research.

AI.tldr: SpeedTransformer uses speed-only GPS trajectories with a Transformer to detect transportation modes, showing superior accuracy and transferability across regions and robust real-world performance.
AI.motivation: Accurate transport mode detection from GPS with limited features; leverage Transformer\'s sequence modeling for speed signals; improve transfer learning and deployment in diverse environments.
AI.method: Introduce SpeedTransformer, a Transformer-based model trained on speed features from dense GPS trajectories; compare to LSTM and other baselines; evaluate on cross-region transfer with fine-tuning; deploy in real-world test.
AI.result: Outperforms LSTM and baselines; strong cross-regional transfer with small fine-tuning; robust under complex urban environments and data uncertainty.
AI.conclusion: Transformer architectures are well-suited for transportation mode detection from dense GPS speed data and can advance mobility research.

title: Exploring the AI Obedience: Why is Generating a Pure Color Image Harder than CyberPunk?
summary: Recent advances in generative AI have demonstrated remarkable ability to produce high-quality content. However, these models often exhibit "Paradox of Simplicity": while they can render intricate landscapes, they often fail at simple, deterministic tasks. To address this, we formalize Obedience as the ability to align with instructions and establish a hierarchical grading system ranging from basic semantic alignment to pixel-level systemic precision, which provides a unified paradigm for incorporating and categorizing existing literature. Then, we conduct case studies to identify common obedience gaps, revealing how generative priors often override logical constraints. To evaluate high-level obedience, we present VIOLIN (VIsual Obedience Level-4 EvaluatIoN), the first benchmark focused on pure color generation across six variants. Extensive experiments on SOTA models reveal fundamental obedience limitations and further exploratory insights. By establishing this framework, we aim to draw more attention on AI Obedience and encourage deeper exploration to bridge this gap.

AI.tldr: Formalizes 'Obedience' as instruction alignment with a hierarchical grading system, identifying common obedience gaps in generative AI, and introduces VIOLIN, a benchmark for pure color generation across six variants, to quantify high-level obedience and guide future research.
AI.motivation: Address the mismatch where powerful generative models handle complex tasks but struggle with simple, deterministic instructions; provide a unified framework to categorize and assess obedience in AI systems to guide improvements.
AI.method: Define a hierarchical Obedience grading system (semantic to pixel-level precision); perform case studies to reveal obedience gaps where priors override constraints; propose VIOLIN benchmark for pure color generation across six variants; conduct extensive experiments on state-of-the-art models.
AI.result: Identifies fundamental obedience limitations, demonstrates cases where generative priors override logical constraints, and provides exploratory insights from VIOLIN experiments highlighting gaps in current models.
AI.conclusion: The framework centers AI Obedience, aims to raise emphasis on the issue, and encourages deeper research to close the obedience gap in generative AI.

title: MetaMind: General and Cognitive World Models in Multi-Agent Systems by Meta-Theory of Mind
summary: A major challenge for world models in multi-agent systems is to understand interdependent agent dynamics, predict interactive multi-agent trajectories, and plan over long horizons with collective awareness, without centralized supervision or explicit communication. In this paper, MetaMind, a general and cognitive world model for multi-agent systems that leverages a novel meta-theory of mind (Meta-ToM) framework, is proposed. Through MetaMind, each agent learns not only to predict and plan over its own beliefs, but also to inversely reason goals and beliefs from its own behavior trajectories. This self-reflective, bidirectional inference loop enables each agent to learn a metacognitive ability in a self-supervised manner. Then, MetaMind is shown to generalize the metacognitive ability from first-person to third-person through analogical reasoning. Thus, in multi-agent systems, each agent with MetaMind can actively reason about goals and beliefs of other agents from limited, observable behavior trajectories in a zero-shot manner, and then adapt to emergent collective intention without an explicit communication mechanism. Extended simulation results on diverse multi-agent tasks demonstrate that MetaMind can achieve superior task performance and outperform baselines in few-shot multi-agent generalization.

AI.tldr: MetaMind introduces a meta-theory of mind (Meta-ToM) to enable a general cognitive world model for multi-agent systems. Agents bidirectionally and self-supervisedly infer their own beliefs and others' goals/beliefs from observed trajectories, enabling zero-shot reasoning and improved few-shot generalization without explicit communication.
AI.motivation: A central challenge in multi-agent world models is understanding interdependent agent dynamics, forecasting interactive trajectories, and planning over long horizons without centralized supervision or explicit communication. Existing models struggle with generalization and coordinating emergent collective intent.
AI.method: Propose MetaMind, a general world model leveraging Meta-ToM. Each agent learns to predict and plan over its own beliefs while inversely inferring goals and beliefs from its behavior trajectories in a self-reflective, bidirectional loop. The metacognitive ability is learned in a self-supervised manner and transferred from first-person to third-person via analogical reasoning, enabling zero-shot reasoning about other agents' goals/beliefs from limited trajectories, without explicit communication.
AI.result: Extended simulations show MetaMind achieves superior task performance and outperforms baselines in few-shot multi-agent generalization across diverse tasks.
AI.conclusion: MetaMind enables active, self-supervised metacognition about self and other agents, transferring to third-person perspectives to adapt to emergent collective intention without explicit communication; demonstrates strong few-shot generalization in multi-agent settings.

title: DAM-VLA: A Dynamic Action Model-Based Vision-Language-Action Framework for Robot Manipulation
summary: In dynamic environments such as warehouses, hospitals, and homes, robots must seamlessly transition between gross motion and precise manipulations to complete complex tasks. However, current Vision-Language-Action (VLA) frameworks, largely adapted from pre-trained Vision-Language Models (VLMs), often struggle to reconcile general task adaptability with the specialized precision required for intricate manipulation. To address this challenge, we propose DAM-VLA, a dynamic action model-based VLA framework. DAM-VLA integrates VLM reasoning with diffusion-based action models specialized for arm and gripper control. Specifically, it introduces (i) an action routing mechanism, using task-specific visual and linguistic cues to select appropriate action models (e.g., arm movement or gripper manipulation), (ii) a dynamic action model that fuses high-level VLM cognition with low-level visual features to predict actions, and (iii) a dual-scale action weighting mechanism that enables dynamic coordination between the arm-movement and gripper-manipulation models. Across extensive evaluations, DAM-VLA achieves superior success rates compared to state-of-the-art VLA methods in simulated (SIMPLER, FurnitureBench) and real-world settings, showing robust generalization from standard pick-and-place to demanding long-horizon and contact-rich tasks.

AI.tldr: DAM-VLA merges VLM-based reasoning with diffusion-based action models for arm and gripper control, using routing, dynamic fusion, and dual-scale weighting to coordinate high-level cognition with low-level actions; outperforms SOTA VLA methods in SIMPLER, FurnitureBench, and real-world tasks, including long-horizon and contact-rich scenarios.
AI.motivation: In dynamic environments, robots must bridge high-level planning with precise manipulation; existing Vision-Language-Action frameworks adapted from pre-trained Vision-Language Models struggle to balance general task adaptability with the precision required for manipulation.
AI.method: 1) Action routing mechanism selects between arm movement and gripper manipulation using task-specific visual and linguistic cues; 2) Dynamic action model fuses high-level VLM cognition with low-level visual features to predict actions; 3) Dual-scale action weighting enables dynamic coordination between arm-movement and gripper-manipulation models.
AI.result: Superior success rates compared to state-of-the-art VLA methods in simulated (SIMPLER, FurnitureBench) and real-world settings; robust generalization from standard pick-and-place to long-horizon and contact-rich tasks.
AI.conclusion: DAM-VLA demonstrates effective integration of VLM reasoning with diffusion-based action models and dynamic routing/weighting to achieve robust, coordinated manipulation across varied tasks.

title: StethoLM: Audio Language Model for Cardiopulmonary Analysis Across Clinical Tasks
summary: Listening to heart and lung sounds - auscultation - is one of the first and most fundamental steps in a clinical examination. Despite being fast and non-invasive, it demands years of experience to interpret subtle audio cues. Recent deep learning methods have made progress in automating cardiopulmonary sound analysis, yet most are restricted to simple classification and offer little clinical interpretability or decision support. We present StethoLM, the first audio-language model specialized for cardiopulmonary auscultation, capable of performing instruction-driven clinical tasks across the full spectrum of auscultation analysis. StethoLM integrates audio encoding with a medical language model backbone and is trained on StethoBench, a comprehensive benchmark comprising 77,027 instruction-response pairs synthesized from 16,125 labeled cardiopulmonary recordings spanning seven clinical task categories: binary classification, detection, reporting, reasoning, differential diagnosis, comparison, and location-based analysis. Through multi-stage training that combines supervised fine-tuning and direct preference optimization, StethoLM achieves substantial gains in performance and robustness on out-of-distribution data. Our work establishes a foundation for instruction-following AI systems in clinical auscultation.

AI.tldr: A novel audio-language model (StethoLM) for cardiopulmonary auscultation enabling instruction-driven tasks across seven categories, trained on a large synthetic benchmark (StethoBench); demonstrates improved performance and robustness, enabling AI-assisted auscultation.
AI.motivation: To overcome limited interpretability and decision-support in existing deep-learning auscultation systems by enabling instruction-following, clinically relevant analysis.
AI.method: Integrates audio encoding with a medical language model backbone. Trained on StethoBench’s 77,027 instruction–response pairs, synthesized from 16,125 labeled cardiopulmonary recordings across seven task categories (binary classification, detection, reporting, reasoning, differential diagnosis, comparison, location-based analysis). Employs multi-stage training with supervised fine-tuning followed by direct preference optimization.
AI.result: Achieves substantial gains in performance and robustness on out-of-distribution data.
AI.conclusion: Sets a foundation for instruction-following AI systems in clinical auscultation, enabling broader, interpretable, task-driven analysis beyond simple classification.

title: Image-Based Classification of Olive Species Specific to Turkiye with Deep Neural Networks
summary: In this study, image processing and deep learning methodologies were employed to automatically classify local olive species cultivated in Turkiye. A stereo camera was utilized to capture images of five distinct olive species, which were then preprocessed to ensure their suitability for analysis. Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) architectures, specifically MobileNetV2 and EfficientNetB0, were employed for image classification. These models were optimized through a transfer learning approach. The training and testing results indicated that the EfficientNetB0 model exhibited the optimal performance, with an accuracy of 94.5%. The findings demonstrate that deep learning-based systems offer an effective solution for classifying olive species with high accuracy. The developed method has significant potential for application in areas such as automatic identification and quality control of agricultural products.

AI.tldr: EfficientNetB0 with transfer learning on stereo-camera olive images achieves 94.5% accuracy across five species, outperforming MobileNetV2; demonstrates practicality of DL for automated olive species identification and quality control.
AI.motivation: Enable automatic, non-destructive, high-accuracy olive species identification for agricultural management and quality control in Türkiye.
AI.method: Capture stereo images of five olive species, perform preprocessing, compare CNN architectures (MobileNetV2 and EfficientNetB0) using transfer learning, train/test evaluation, report accuracy.
AI.result: EfficientNetB0 achieved top accuracy of 94.5%; MobileNetV2 performance is lower (not specified).
AI.conclusion: Deep learning-based systems are effective for olive species classification with high accuracy and show potential for automatic identification and quality control in agricultural products.

title: MC-Search: Evaluating and Enhancing Multimodal Agentic Search with Structured Long Reasoning Chains
summary: With the increasing demand for step-wise, cross-modal, and knowledge-grounded reasoning, multimodal large language models (MLLMs) are evolving beyond the traditional fixed retrieve-then-generate paradigm toward more sophisticated agentic multimodal retrieval-augmented generation (MM-RAG). Existing benchmarks, however, mainly focus on simplified QA with short retrieval chains, leaving adaptive planning and multimodal reasoning underexplored. We present MC-Search, the first benchmark for agentic MM-RAG with long, step-wise annotated reasoning chains spanning five representative reasoning structures. Each example specifies sub-questions, retrieval modalities, supporting facts, and intermediate answers, with fidelity ensured by HAVE (Hop-wise Attribution and Verification of Evidence), resulting in 3,333 high-quality examples averaging 3.7 hops. Beyond answer accuracy, MC-Search introduces new process-level metrics for reasoning quality, stepwise retrieval and planning accuracy. By developing a unified agentic MM-RAG pipeline, we benchmark six leading MLLMs and reveal systematic issues such as over- and under-retrieval and modality-misaligned planning. Finally, we introduce Search-Align, a process-supervised fine-tuning framework leveraging verified reasoning chains, showing that our data not only enables faithful evaluation but also improves planning and retrieval fidelity in open-source MLLMs.

AI.tldr: A long-hops, agentic multimodal RAG benchmark (MC-Search) with HAVE verification; 3,333 examples across five reasoning structures; introduces process-level metrics and a fine-tuning framework (Search-Align) for improved planning and retrieval fidelity.
AI.motivation: Addresses the gap in existing benchmarks that emphasize short retrieval chains by introducing long, stepwise, cross-modal reasoning with verification to evaluate and guide agentic MM-RAG.
AI.method: Construct MC-Search with five representative reasoning structures, annotate sub-questions, retrieval modalities, supporting facts, and intermediate answers; enforce fidelity via HAVE (Hop-wise Attribution and Verification of Evidence); compile 3,333 examples averaging 3.7 hops; define process-level metrics for reasoning quality and stepwise retrieval/planning; build a unified agentic MM-RAG pipeline; benchmark six leading MLLMs; diagnose retrieval/planning issues; propose Search-Align for process-supervised fine-tuning.
AI.result: Reveals systematic issues (over-/under-retrieval, modality-misaligned planning) across models; MC-Search provides a high-quality data/resource for faithful evaluation and benchmarking; Search-Align improves planning and retrieval fidelity in open-source MLLMs.
AI.conclusion: MC-Search and Search-Align push forward the evaluation and training of agentic MM-RAG, enabling more faithful, stepwise, and multimodal reasoning, with practical improvements in planning and retrieval fidelity.

title: DRIFT: Diffusion-based Rule-Inferred For Trajectories
summary: Trajectory generation for mobile robots in unstructured environments faces a critical dilemma: balancing kinematic smoothness for safe execution with terminal precision for fine-grained tasks. Existing generative planners often struggle with this trade-off, yielding either smooth but imprecise paths or geometrically accurate but erratic motions. To address the aforementioned shortcomings, this article proposes DRIFT (Diffusion-based Rule-Inferred for Trajectories), a conditional diffusion framework designed to generate high-fidelity reference trajectories by integrating two complementary inductive biases. First, a Relational Inductive Bias, realized via a GNN-based Structured Scene Perception (SSP) module, encodes global topological constraints to ensure holistic smoothness. Second, a Temporal Attention Bias, implemented through a novel Graph-Conditioned Time-Aware GRU (GTGRU), dynamically attends to sparse obstacles and targets for precise local maneuvering. In the end, quantitative results demonstrate that DRIFT reconciles these conflicting objectives, achieving centimeter-level imitation fidelity (0.041m FDE) and competitive smoothness (27.19 Jerk). This balance yields highly executable reference plans for downstream control.

AI.tldr: A diffusion-based trajectory generator (DRIFT) reconciles trajectory smoothness and precision for mobile robots in unstructured environments by combining global topological guidance with local, obstacle-aware control. It uses a GNN-based Structured Scene Perception (SSP) for relational constraints and a Graph-Conditioned Time-Aware GRU (GTGRU) for temporal attention, yielding high imitation fidelity and competitive smoothness.
AI.motivation: Balancing kinematic smoothness (safe execution) with terminal precision (fine-grained tasks) is challenging; existing generative planners often sacrifice one objective for the other in unstructured settings.
AI.method: A conditional diffusion framework (DRIFT) with two inductive biases: (1) Relational Inductive Bias via a GNN-based Structured Scene Perception (SSP) module encoding global topological constraints for holistic smoothness; (2) Temporal Attention Bias via a Graph-Conditioned Time-Aware GRU (GTGRU) attending to sparse obstacles and targets for precise local maneuvering.
AI.result: Quantitative results show high fidelity and competitive smoothness: 0.041 m FDE (centimeter-level imitation fidelity) and 27.19 Jerk (smoothness metric), yielding executable reference plans for downstream control.
AI.conclusion: DRIFT successfully reconciles the trade-off between smoothness and precision, producing reference trajectories suitable for real-time control in unstructured environments.

title: Quantifying Catastrophic Forgetting in IoT Intrusion Detection Systems
summary: Distribution shifts in attack patterns within RPL-based IoT networks pose a critical threat to the reliability and security of large-scale connected systems. Intrusion Detection Systems (IDS) trained on static datasets often fail to generalize to unseen threats and suffer from catastrophic forgetting when updated with new attacks. Ensuring continual adaptability of IDS is therefore essential for maintaining robust IoT network defence. In this focused study, we formulate intrusion detection as a domain continual learning problem and propose a method-agnostic IDS framework that can integrate diverse continual learning strategies. We systematically benchmark five representative approaches across multiple domain-ordering sequences using a comprehensive multi-attack dataset comprising 48 domains. Results show that continual learning mitigates catastrophic forgetting while maintaining a balance between plasticity, stability, and efficiency, a crucial aspect for resource-constrained IoT environments. Among the methods, Replay-based approaches achieve the best overall performance, while Synaptic Intelligence (SI) delivers near-zero forgetting with high training efficiency, demonstrating strong potential for stable and sustainable IDS deployment in dynamic IoT networks.

AI.tldr: Continual domain-learning for IDS in RPL IoT networks; framework supports various continual learning strategies; replay-based methods perform best; SI minimizes forgetting; tested on 48-domain dataset; shows improved stability-plasticity-efficiency trade-offs for IoT.
AI.motivation: Static IDS models fail to generalize to evolving attack patterns and suffer catastrophic forgetting when updated with new threats; IoT networks require continual adaptation under resource constraints.
AI.method: A method-agnostic IDS framework integrating diverse continual learning strategies; benchmarks five representative approaches across domain-ordering sequences on a comprehensive 48-domain multi-attack dataset.
AI.result: Continual learning reduces forgetting while balancing plasticity, stability, and efficiency; replay-based approaches yield best overall performance; Synaptic Intelligence (SI) achieves near-zero forgetting with high training efficiency.
AI.conclusion: Continual learning is a viable approach for robust, sustainable IDS deployment in dynamic IoT networks; replay-based methods and SI show particular promise for managing distribution shifts in attack patterns.

title: A Novel Evolutionary Method for Automated Skull-Face Overlay in Computer-Aided Craniofacial Superimposition
summary: Craniofacial Superimposition is a forensic technique for identifying skeletal remains by comparing a post-mortem skull with ante-mortem facial photographs. A critical step in this process is Skull-Face Overlay (SFO). This stage involves aligning a 3D skull model with a 2D facial image, typically guided by cranial and facial landmarks' correspondence. However, its accuracy is undermined by individual variability in soft-tissue thickness, introducing significant uncertainty into the overlay. This paper introduces Lilium, an automated evolutionary method to enhance the accuracy and robustness of SFO. Lilium explicitly models soft-tissue variability using a 3D cone-based representation whose parameters are optimized via a Differential Evolution algorithm. The method enforces anatomical, morphological, and photographic plausibility through a combination of constraints: landmark matching, camera parameter consistency, head pose alignment, skull containment within facial boundaries, and region parallelism. This emulation of the usual forensic practitioners' approach leads Lilium to outperform the state-of-the-art method in terms of both accuracy and robustness.

AI.tldr: Proposes Lilium, an automated evolutionary method (Differential Evolution) to improve skull-face overlay by modeling soft-tissue variability with a 3D cone-based representation and enforcing anatomical and photographic constraints to outperform current SFO methods.
AI.motivation: Uncertainty in skull-face overlay due to individual variation in soft-tissue thickness undermines accuracy; needs robust, automated, physiologically plausible solution.
