AI.method: 3D cone-based soft-tissue model with parameters optimized via Differential Evolution; constraints include landmark matching, camera parameter consistency, head pose alignment, skull containment within facial boundaries, and region parallelism; integrates with automated SFO pipeline.
AI.result: Outperforms state-of-the-art methods in accuracy and robustness for SFO, demonstrating improved handling of soft-tissue variability.
AI.conclusion: Explicit soft-tissue variability modeling via a cone-based representation and evolutionary optimization enhances SFO reliability and aligns with forensic practice; suggests broader applicability and robustness.

title: HierKick: Hierarchical Reinforcement Learning for Vision-Guided Soccer Robot Control
summary: Controlling soccer robots involves multi-time-scale decision-making, which requires balancing long-term tactical planning and short-term motion execution. Traditional end-to-end reinforcement learning (RL) methods face challenges in complex dynamic environments. This paper proposes HierKick, a vision-guided soccer robot control framework based on dual-frequency hierarchical RL. The framework adopts a hierarchical control architecture featuring a 5 Hz high-level policy that integrates YOLOv8 for real-time detection and selects tasks via a coach model, and a pre-trained 50 Hz low-level controller for precise joint control. Through this architecture, the framework achieves the four steps of approaching, aligning, dribbling, and kicking. Experimental results show that the success rates of this framework are 95.2\% in IsaacGym, 89.8\% in Mujoco, and 80\% in the real world. HierKick provides an effective hierarchical paradigm for robot control in complex environments, extendable to multi-time-scale tasks, with its modular design and skill reuse offering a new path for intelligent robot control.

AI.tldr: HierKick: a vision-guided, dual-frequency hierarchical RL framework for soccer robots with a 5 Hz high-level planner (YOLOv8 + coach) and a 50 Hz low-level controller, achieving high success rates in simulation and real-world tasks: approaching, aligning, dribbling, kicking.
AI.motivation: Address multi-time-scale decision making in dynamic robot soccer; conventional end-to-end RL struggles in complex environments; need modular, reusable skills and perception-guided control.
AI.method: Hierarchical control: high-level policy at 5 Hz using YOLOv8 for real-time detection and a coach model to select tasks; low-level controller at 50 Hz pre-trained for joint control; four-step pipeline: approaching, aligning, dribbling, kicking.
AI.result: Success rates 95.2% IsaacGym, 89.8% Mujoco, 80% real world; modular, reusable skills; extendable to other multi-time-scale tasks.
AI.conclusion: HierKick demonstrates an effective hierarchical paradigm for robot control in complex environments; architecture enables multi-time-scale control and skill reuse; potential for broader intelligent robot control.

title: Deep Learning-Based Meat Freshness Detection with Segmentation and OOD-Aware Classification
summary: In this study, we present a meat freshness classification framework from Red-Green-Blue (RGB) images that supports both packaged and unpackaged meat datasets. The system classifies four in-distribution (ID) meat classes and uses an out-of-distribution (OOD)-aware abstention mechanism that flags low-confidence samples as No Result. The pipeline combines U-Net-based segmentation with deep feature classifiers. Segmentation is used as a preprocessing step to isolate the meat region and reduce background, producing more consistent inputs for classification. The segmentation module achieved an Intersection over Union (IoU) of 75% and a Dice coefficient of 82%, producing standardized inputs for the classification stage. For classification, we benchmark five backbones: Residual Network-50 (ResNet-50), Vision Transformer-Base/16 (ViT-B/16), Swin Transformer-Tiny (Swin-T), EfficientNet-B0, and MobileNetV3-Small. We use nested 5x3 cross-validation (CV) for model selection and hyperparameter tuning. On the held-out ID test set, EfficientNet-B0 achieves the highest accuracy (98.10%), followed by ResNet-50 and MobileNetV3-Small (both 97.63%) and Swin-T (97.51%), while ViT-B/16 is lower (94.42%). We additionally evaluate OOD scoring and thresholding using standard OOD metrics and sensitivity analysis over the abstention threshold. Finally, we report on-device latency using TensorFlow Lite (TFLite) on a smartphone, highlighting practical accuracy-latency trade-offs for future deployment.

AI.tldr: A two-stage meat freshness classification pipeline combining U-Net segmentation and multiple backbones for ID accuracy with an OOD abstention mechanism and edge deployment considerations.
AI.motivation: To achieve robust meat freshness classification across packaged and unpackaged datasets while reducing background noise and enabling safe deployment on devices through OOD abstention and latency awareness.
AI.method: U-Net segmentation to isolate meat regions as preprocessing; five backbones (ResNet-50, ViT-B/16, Swin-T, EfficientNet-B0, MobileNetV3-Small) used for classification; nested 5x3 cross-validation for model selection and hyperparameter tuning; OOD scoring with abstention threshold; TensorFlow Lite-based on-device latency evaluation.
AI.result: Segmentation IoU 75%, Dice 82%. ID test accuracy: EfficientNet-B0 98.10%; ResNet-50 97.63%; MobileNetV3-Small 97.63%; Swin-T 97.51%; ViT-B/16 94.42%. OOD scoring and sensitivity analysis performed; on-device latency demonstrated with practical accuracy-latency trade-offs (exact figures not provided).
AI.conclusion: The framework yields high ID accuracy with effective segmentation to stabilize inputs, supports OOD-aware abstention for safer predictions, and addresses deployment readiness via TFLite latency evaluation; EfficientNet-B0 is the top performer among tested backbones.

title: AdaFocus: Knowing When and Where to Look for Adaptive Visual Reasoning
summary: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) are shifting towards "Thinking with Images" by actively exploring image details. While effective, large-scale training is computationally expensive, which has spurred growing interest in lightweight, training-free solutions. However, existing training-free methods suffer from two flaws: perceptual redundancy from indiscriminate cropping, which adds overhead and noise; and a drift between semantic intent and spatial attention, which prevents accurate localization of user-focused regions. To address these challenges, we propose AdaFocus, a novel training-free framework designed for adaptive visual reasoning. AdaFocus follows a two-stage pipeline: a confidence-based module decides when to crop, and a semantic-guided localization module determines where to crop. This enables adaptive visual reasoning without additional training. Experimentally, AdaFocus delivers substantial performance gains while achieving approximately 4.0\times speedup inference speedup than the SOTA method ZoomEyes, representing a significant advance in both accuracy and efficiency.

AI.tldr: AdaFocus is a training-free adaptive cropping framework for Multimodal LLMs, using a two-stage pipeline (confidence-based crop decision and semantic-guided localization) to focus on relevant image regions. It achieves about 4× faster inference than ZoomEyes with improved accuracy.
AI.motivation: To reduce the computational cost of large multimodal models while maintaining or improving accuracy. Existing training-free approaches suffer from perceptual redundancy due to indiscriminate cropping and misalignment between semantic intent and spatial attention, which harms localization.
AI.method: A two-stage pipeline: (1) a confidence-based module decides when to crop the image to avoid unnecessary processing, (2) a semantic-guided localization module determines where to crop based on semantic cues, enabling adaptive, training-free visual reasoning without additional training.
AI.result: AdaFocus yields substantial performance gains and about 4.0× inference speedup over the state-of-the-art ZoomEyes, indicating improved efficiency and accuracy in adaptive visual reasoning.
AI.conclusion: AdaFocus demonstrates that training-free adaptive cropping can substantially boost both accuracy and efficiency in MLLMs by aligning cropping decisions with semantic intent and reducing redundant processing.

title: HiMAC: Hierarchical Macro-Micro Learning for Long-Horizon LLM Agents
summary: Large language model (LLM) agents have recently demonstrated strong capabilities in interactive decision-making, yet they remain fundamentally limited in long-horizon tasks that require structured planning and reliable execution. Existing approaches predominantly rely on flat autoregressive policies, where high-level reasoning and low-level actions are generated within a single token sequence, leading to inefficient exploration and severe error propagation over extended trajectories. In this work, we propose HiMAC, a hierarchical agentic RL framework that explicitly decomposes long-horizon decision-making into macro-level planning and micro-level execution. HiMAC models reasoning as a structured blueprint generation process followed by goal-conditioned action execution, enabling robust long-horizon planning within LLM-based agents. To train this hierarchy efficiently, we introduce a critic-free hierarchical policy optimization paradigm that extends group-based reinforcement learning to bi-level structures through hierarchical relative advantage estimation. Furthermore, we propose an iterative co-evolution training strategy that alternates between planner exploration and executor adaptation, mitigating the non-stationarity inherent in hierarchical learning. Extensive experiments on ALFWorld, WebShop, and Sokoban demonstrate that HiMAC consistently outperforms strong prompting and reinforcement learning baselines, achieving state-of-the-art performance and substantially improved sample efficiency across both text-based and visually grounded environments. Our results show that introducing structured hierarchy, rather than increasing model scale alone, is a key factor for enabling robust long-horizon agentic intelligence.

AI.tldr: HiMAC introduces a hierarchical RL framework for LLM agents, separating macro planning from micro execution, with a critic-free hierarchical policy optimization and iterative co-evolution training, achieving state-of-the-art results and improved sample efficiency on ALFWorld, WebShop, and Sokoban.
AI.motivation: LLM agents struggle with long-horizon tasks that require structured planning; flat autoregressive policies lead to inefficient exploration and error accumulation over long trajectories.
AI.method: HiMAC decomposes decision-making into macro-level blueprint planning and micro-level goal-conditioned execution. It uses a critic-free hierarchical policy optimization via hierarchical relative advantage estimation and extends group-based RL to bi-level structures. An iterative co-evolution training strategy alternates planner exploration with executor adaptation.
AI.result: HiMAC consistently outperforms strong prompting and RL baselines, achieving state-of-the-art performance and substantially improved sample efficiency across text-based and visually grounded environments (ALFWorld, WebShop, Sokoban).
AI.conclusion: Structured hierarchy—not merely larger model scale—is crucial for robust long-horizon agentic intelligence.

title: MiniUGV$_2$: A Compact UAV-Deployable Tracked Ground Vehicle with Manipulation Capabilities
summary: Exploring and inspecting \emph{Hidden Spaces}, defined as environments whose entrances are accessible only to aerial robots but remain unexplored due to geometric constraints, limited flight time, and communication loss, remains a major challenge. We present miniUGV$_2$, a compact UAV-deployable tracked ground vehicle that extends UAV capabilities into confined environments. The system introduces dual articulated arms, integrated LiDAR and depth sensing, and modular electronics for enhanced autonomy. A novel tether module with an electro-permanent magnetic head enables safe deployment, retrieval, and optional detachment, thereby overcoming prior entanglement issues. Experiments demonstrate robust terrain navigation, self-righting, and manipulation of objects up to 3.5 kg, validating miniUGV$_2$ as a versatile platform for hybrid aerial-ground robotics.

AI.tldr: Proposes miniUGV2, a compact UAV-deployable tracked ground robot with dual articulated arms, integrated LiDAR/depth sensing, and a tether module featuring an electro-permanent magnetic head for safe deployment/retrieval, enabling hybrid aerial-ground operation in confined spaces; demonstrates navigation, self-righting, and manipulation of up to 3.5 kg.
AI.motivation: Addresses the challenge of accessing 'Hidden Spaces'—environments inaccessible to aerial robots due to geometry, limited flight time, and comms issues—by extending UAV capabilities through a deployable ground robot.
AI.method: Hardware-and-system design including dual articulated arms, integrated LiDAR/depth sensing, modular electronics; a tether module with an electro-permanent magnetic head enabling safe deployment, retrieval, and optional detachment; experimental evaluation on terrain navigation, self-righting, and object manipulation.
AI.result: Demonstrates robust terrain navigation, self-righting, and manipulation of objects up to 3.5 kg; validates miniUGV2 as a versatile hybrid aerial-ground robotics platform.
AI.conclusion: miniUGV2 extends UAV capabilities into confined environments, enabling reliable hybrid aerial-ground operations and expanding autonomy for tasks requiring both aerial reach and grounded manipulation.

title: Improving Full Waveform Inversion in Large Model Era
summary: Full Waveform Inversion (FWI) is a highly nonlinear and ill-posed problem that aims to recover subsurface velocity maps from surface-recorded seismic waveforms data. Existing data-driven FWI typically uses small models, as available datasets have limited volume, geological diversity, and spatial extent, leading to substantial concerns about overfitting. Although they perform well on synthetic datasets, current methods fail to generalize to more realistic geological structures. In this work, we show that a model trained entirely on simulated and relatively simple data can generalize remarkably well to challenging and unseen geological benchmarks. We provide a working recipe that tames a billion-parameter model for FWI through coordinated scaling across three axes: model capacity, data diversity, and training strategy. Our model achieves state-of-the-art performance on OpenFWI and significantly narrows the generalization gap in data-driven FWI. Across six challenging geophysical benchmarks, including Marmousi, 2D SEG/EAGE Salt and Overthrust, 2004 BP, Sigsbee, and SEAM Phase I, it infers complex structures absent from the training set and delivers significant performance improvements (SSIM from 0.5844 to 0.7669). Overall, our results demonstrate that with an appropriate scaling strategy, large models trained on simple synthetic data can achieve substantial generalization to more complex and realistic geological structures.

AI.tldr: Large-scale FWI models trained on simple synthetic data can generalize to complex geological benchmarks by scaling model capacity, data diversity, and training strategy, achieving state-of-the-art results and narrowing the generalization gap.
AI.motivation: Data-driven FWI suffers from overfitting and poor generalization due to small model sizes and limited, homogeneous datasets. Demonstrating that synthetic data combined with scale can generalize to real, diverse geology addresses a key limitation in FWI research.
AI.method: Develop and train a billion-parameter FWI model using coordinated scaling across three axes: (1) model capacity (increase parameter count), (2) data diversity (include varied geological benchmarks and synthetic scenarios), and (3) training strategy (optimized training regimen). Evaluate on OpenFWI and six benchmarks (Marmousi, 2D SEG/EAGE Salt and Overthrust, 2004 BP, Sigsbee, SEAM Phase I).
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on OpenFWI; significantly narrows the generalization gap for data-driven FWI. On six challenging benchmarks, the model infers complex structures not present in training and improves SSIM from 0.5844 to 0.7669, demonstrating strong generalization from synthetic to realistic geology.
AI.conclusion: With proper scaling across capacity, data diversity, and training strategy, large FWI models trained on simple synthetic data can generalize to realistic geological structures, challenging the notion that large labeled real-data is mandatory for strong data-driven FWI performance.

title: Summer-22B: A Systematic Approach to Dataset Engineering and Training at Scale for Video Foundation Model
summary: We describe our experience training Summer-22B, a video foundation model developed from scratch. This report documents the engineering challenges, design decisions, and lessons learned while scaling from raw footage collection to a functional model trained on approximately 50 million clips. We outline our approach combining metadata-driven dataset curation, multi-stage filtering, $μ$P parameterization, and hypersphere-constrained optimization. We developed the Lavender Data system for dataset management and adopted inference-aware architectural choices. We share observations on what worked in our setting: dataset engineering consumed the majority of effort, architectural variants showed smaller differences than we expected, and $μ$P hyperparameter transfer appeared effective even under geometric constraints. We hope this account proves useful to others undertaking similar projects.

AI.tldr: Summarizes the authors' experience building Summer-22B, a video foundation model trained from 50M clips; emphasizes dataset engineering, metadata-driven curation, μP parameterization, and hypersphere optimization; introduces Lavender Data system and inference-aware design; finds data work dominates effort, architecture variations are minor, and μP transfer is robust.
AI.motivation: To document the practical engineering challenges, design decisions, and lessons learned when scaling from raw footage to a functional video foundation model, with the aim of guiding others undertaking similar projects.
AI.method: Pipeline includes metadata-driven dataset curation, multi-stage filtering, μP (micro-parameter) parameterization, and hypersphere-constrained optimization. Introduces Lavender Data system for dataset management and adopts inference-aware architectural choices.
AI.result: Successful training of Summer-22B on ~50 million clips. Key observations: dataset engineering dominated the effort, architectural variants produced smaller differences than expected, μP hyperparameter transfer remained effective under geometric constraints.
AI.conclusion: Provides actionable guidance for future practitioners, highlighting the primacy of dataset engineering and suggesting robustness of μP methods and inference-aware design in video foundation-model development.

title: Tracking Capabilities for Safer Agents
summary: AI agents that interact with the real world through tool calls pose fundamental safety challenges: agents might leak private information, cause unintended side effects, or be manipulated through prompt injection. To address these challenges, we propose to put the agent in a programming-language-based "safety harness": instead of calling tools directly, agents express their intentions as code in a capability-safe language: Scala 3 with capture checking. Capabilities are program variables that regulate access to effects and resources of interest. Scala's type system tracks capabilities statically, providing fine-grained control over what an agent can do. In particular, it enables local purity, the ability to enforce that sub-computations are side-effect-free, preventing information leakage when agents process classified data. We demonstrate that extensible agent safety harnesses can be built by leveraging a strong type system with tracked capabilities. Our experiments show that agents can generate capability-safe code with no significant loss in task performance, while the type system reliably prevents unsafe behaviors such as information leakage and malicious side effects.

AI.tldr: A safety framework for AI agents using a capability-tracked programming language (Scala 3 with capture checking) to constrain tool usage, enabling local purity and preventing information leakage and unsafe side effects; experiments show minimal performance impact and effective safety guarantees.
AI.motivation: AI agents interacting with real-world tools risk leaking private data, causing unintended side effects, or being manipulated via prompt injection. A rigorous, language-based safety harness offers formal _and_ practical guarantees by tracking capabilities.
AI.method: Propose a Scala 3-based safety harness where agents express intents as code in a capability-safe language. Capabilities are program variables that govern access to effects/resources. The type system statically tracks capabilities to enforce access control and enforce local purity (side-effect-free subcomputations) to prevent information leakage. Build extensible safety harnesses leveraging the strong type system with tracked capabilities.
AI.result: Agents can generate capability-safe code with no significant loss in task performance. The type system reliably prevents unsafe behaviors such as information leakage and malicious side effects.
AI.conclusion: A strong, capability-tracked type system enables safe, extensible agent-tool interactions with maintained performance, suggesting practical viability of language-based safety harnesses for AI agents.

title: An Open-Source Modular Benchmark for Diffusion-Based Motion Planning in Closed-Loop Autonomous Driving
summary: Diffusion-based motion planners have achieved state-of-the-art results on benchmarks such as nuPlan, yet their evaluation within closed-loop production autonomous driving stacks remains largely unexplored. Existing evaluations abstract away ROS 2 communication latency and real-time scheduling constraints, while monolithic ONNX deployment freezes all solver parameters at export time. We present an open-source modular benchmark that addresses both gaps: using ONNX GraphSurgeon, we decompose a monolithic 18,398 node diffusion planner into three independently executable modules and reimplement the DPM-Solver++ denoising loop in native C++. Integrated as a ROS 2 node within Autoware, the open-source AD stack deployed on real vehicles worldwide, the system enables runtime-configurable solver parameters without model recompilation and per-step observability of the denoising process, breaking the black box of monolithic deployment. Unlike evaluations in standalone simulators such as CARLA, our benchmark operates within a production-grade stack and is validated through AWSIM closed-loop simulation. Through systematic comparison of DPM-Solver++ (first- and second-order) and DDIM across six step-count configurations (N in {3, 5, 7, 10, 15, 20}), we show that encoder caching yields a 3.2x latency reduction, and that second-order solving reduces FDE by 41% at N=3 compared to first-order. The complete codebase will be released as open-source, providing a direct path from simulation benchmarks to real-vehicle deployment.

AI.tldr: Modular open-source benchmark for diffusion-based motion planning integrated in ROS 2 Autoware; decouples a large monolithic diffusion planner into three modules, reimplements DPM-Solver++ denoising in C++, enables runtime config and per-step observability; validated in AWSIM closed-loop; shows 3.2x latency with encoder caching and 41% FDE reduction for second-order at N=3; code will be open-sourced.
AI.motivation: Fill evaluative gaps: (1) evaluate diffusion planners within production stacks with real-time constraints/ROS 2 latency, (2) avoid monolithic ONNX that freezes solver parameters, enable runtime configurability and observability.
AI.method: Decompose 18,398-node diffusion planner using ONNX GraphSurgeon into three modules; rewrite DPM-Solver++ denoising loop in C++; integrate as ROS 2 node in Autoware; configure solver params at runtime; provide per-step observability; evaluate across DPM-Solver++ first/second order and DDIM across N in {3,5,7,10,15,20} using AWSIM closed-loop simulation.
AI.result: Encoder caching yields 3.2x latency reduction; second-order solving reduces FDE by 41% at N=3 vs first-order; observed performance across six N; open-source codebase planned.
AI.conclusion: Provides a practical bridge from simulation benchmarks to real-vehicle deployment; addresses realism and configurability gaps; enables production-grade diffusion motion planning benchmarks.

title: Hereditary Geometric Meta-RL: Nonlocal Generalization via Task Symmetries
summary: Meta-Reinforcement Learning (Meta-RL) commonly generalizes via smoothness in the task encoding. While this enables local generalization around each training task, it requires dense coverage of the task space and leaves richer task space structure untapped. In response, we develop a geometric perspective that endows the task space with a "hereditary geometry" induced by the inherent symmetries of the underlying system. Concretely, the agent reuses a policy learned at the train time by transforming states and actions through actions of a Lie group. This converts Meta-RL into symmetry discovery rather than smooth extrapolation, enabling the agent to generalize to wider regions of the task space. We show that when the task space is inherited from the symmetries of the underlying system, the task space embeds into a subgroup of those symmetries whose actions are linearizable, connected, and compact--properties that enable efficient learning and inference at the test time. To learn these structures, we develop a differential symmetry discovery method. This collapses functional invariance constraints and thereby improves numerical stability and sample efficiency over functional approaches. Empirically, on a two-dimensional navigation task, our method efficiently recovers the ground-truth symmetry and generalizes across the entire task space, while a common baseline generalizes only near training tasks.

AI.tldr: Symmetry-driven Meta-RL with hereditary geometry: reuse policies via Lie-group actions, learn linearizable symmetry subgroups with differential symmetry discovery, enabling broad generalization beyond densely covered task spaces.
AI.motivation: Standard Meta-RL relies on local task-space smoothness and dense task coverage, which limits generalization. Exploiting intrinsic symmetries (hereditary geometry) can expand the generalization region and improve efficiency.
AI.method: Reuse a train-time policy by transforming states and actions with Lie-group symmetries; embed the task space into a subgroup of symmetries that are linearizable, connected, and compact; develop differential symmetry discovery to collapse invariance constraints for numerical stability and sample efficiency.
AI.result: Empirically, the method recovers the ground-truth symmetry on a 2D navigation task and generalizes across the entire task space, whereas baselines generalize only near training tasks.
AI.conclusion: Leveraging hereditary geometry via system symmetries enables broader, more efficient generalization in Meta-RL; differential symmetry discovery enhances stability and sample efficiency, suggesting applicability to symmetry-rich tasks.

title: Infinite Self-Attention
summary: The quadratic cost of softmax attention limits Transformer scalability in high-resolution vision. We introduce Infinite Self-Attention (InfSA), a spectral reformulation that treats each attention layer as a diffusion step on a content-adaptive token graph, accumulating multi-hop interactions through a discounted Neumann series over attention matrices. This links self-attention to classical graph centrality (Katz, PageRank, eigenvector centrality) for interpretable token weighting. We also show the Neumann kernel equals the fundamental matrix of an absorbing Markov chain, so a token's centrality is its expected number of random-walk visits before absorption. We then propose Linear-InfSA, a linear-time variant that approximates the principal eigenvector of the implicit attention operator without forming the full attention matrix. It keeps an auxiliary state of fixed size proportional to per-head dimension dh (independent of sequence length N), is drop-in compatible with Vision Transformers, and supports stable training at 4096 by 4096 and inference at 9216 by 9216 (about 332k tokens). In a 4-layer ViT (53.5M parameters, 59 GFLOPs at 224 by 224), Linear-InfSA reaches 84.7% top-1 on ImageNet-1K, a +3.2 point architectural gain over an equal-depth softmax ViT trained with the same recipe. On ImageNet-V2, InfViT variants outperform all compared baselines (up to 79.8% vs 76.8%), indicating robustness under distribution shift. On an A100 40GB GPU, Linear-InfViT runs at 231 images/s and 0.87 J/image (13x better throughput and energy than equal-depth ViT) and is the only tested model to complete 9216 by 9216 inference without out-of-memory. The linear approximation closely matches the dominant eigenvector of the quadratic operator (cosine 0.985).

AI.tldr: InfSA reframes self-attention as diffusion on a content-adaptive token graph and introduces Linear-InfSA, a linear-time approximation of the dominant eigenvector of the attention operator. The approach yields scalable, accurate vision transformers and strong robustness, enabling large-scale token inputs with high efficiency.
AI.motivation: The quadratic cost of softmax attention limits Transformer scalability in high-resolution vision. Interpreting attention through graph centrality offers a principled, interpretable framework and connects attention to diffusion processes and Markov chains, enabling scalable and robust token weighting.
AI.method: InfSA treats each attention layer as a diffusion step on a content-adaptive token graph and accumulates multi-hop interactions via a discounted Neumann series over attention matrices. The Neumann kernel corresponds to the fundamental matrix of an absorbing Markov chain, linking token centrality to expected random-walk visits. Linear-InfSA provides a linear-time approximation that estimates the dominant eigenvector without forming the full attention matrix, using a fixed-size auxiliary state proportional to per-head dimension and remaining drop-in compatible with Vision Transformers.
AI.result: Empirical results show: (1) 4-layer ViT (53.5M params, 59 GFLOPs at 224x224) with Linear-InfSA achieves 84.7% top-1 on ImageNet-1K, a +3.2 point gain over an equal-depth softmax ViT; (2) on ImageNet-V2, InfViT variants outperform baselines (up to 79.8% vs 76.8%); (3) on A100 40GB, Linear-InfViT runs at 231 images/s and 0.87 J/image, ~13x energy/throughput improvement over equal-depth ViT, and handles 9216x9216 inference without OOM; (4) the linear approximation closely matches the dominant eigenvector of the quadratic operator (cosine similarity 0.985).
AI.conclusion: InfSA and Linear-InfSA deliver scalable, accurate Vision Transformers capable of processing very large token grids with strong robustness to distribution shifts, combining interpretable centrality-based token weighting with a practical linear-time approximation.

title: CollabEval: Enhancing LLM-as-a-Judge via Multi-Agent Collaboration
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) have revolutionized AI-generated content evaluation, with the LLM-as-a-Judge paradigm becoming increasingly popular. However, current single-LLM evaluation approaches face significant challenges, including inconsistent judgments and inherent biases from pre-training data. To address these limitations, we propose CollabEval, a novel multi-agent evaluation framework that implements a three-phase Collaborative Evaluation process: initial evaluation, multi-round discussion, and final judgment. Unlike existing approaches that rely on competitive debate or single-model evaluation, CollabEval emphasizes collaboration among multiple agents with strategic consensus checking for efficiency. Our extensive experiments demonstrate that CollabEval consistently outperforms single-LLM approaches across multiple dimensions while maintaining robust performance even when individual models struggle. The framework provides comprehensive support for various evaluation criteria while ensuring efficiency through its collaborative design.

AI.tldr: A multi-agent collaborative framework, CollabEval, for evaluating LLM-generated content using a three-phase process (initial evaluation, multi-round discussion, final judgment). It emphasizes collaboration over competition, uses consensus checks for efficiency, and outperforms single-LLM methods while remaining robust when individual models underperform.
AI.motivation: Single-LLM evaluation methods suffer from inconsistent judgments and biases from pre-training data. A collaborative, multi-agent approach aims to reduce bias, improve reliability, and provide scalable evaluation across diverse criteria.
AI.method: Three-phase Collaborative Evaluation: Phase 1 initial evaluation by multiple agents; Phase 2 multi-round discussion among agents; Phase 3 final judgment with strategic consensus checking. The framework emphasizes collaboration rather than competitive debate and seeks efficiency through distributed reasoning and consensus mechanisms, while supporting multiple evaluation criteria.
AI.result: Empirical results show CollabEval consistently outperforms single-LLM approaches across various dimensions and remains robust when some models underperform. The framework offers comprehensive support for diverse evaluation criteria and maintains efficiency through its collaborative design.
AI.conclusion: CollabEval provides a scalable, robust, and efficient multi-agent evaluation paradigm for LLMs, reducing reliance on any single model and offering improved reliability and adaptability for evaluating content across multiple criteria.

title: Compact Task-Aligned Imitation Learning for Laboratory Automation
summary: Robotic laboratory automation has traditionally relied on carefully engineered motion pipelines and task-specific hardware interfaces, resulting in high design cost and limited flexibility. While recent imitation learning techniques can generate general robot behaviors, their large model sizes often require high-performance computational resources, limiting applicability in practical laboratory environments. In this study, we propose a compact imitation learning framework for laboratory automation using small foundation models. The proposed method, TVF-DiT, aligns a self-supervised vision foundation model with a vision-language model through a compact adapter, and integrates them with a Diffusion Transformer-based action expert. The entire model consists of fewer than 500M parameters, enabling inference on low-VRAM GPUs. Experiments on three real-world laboratory tasks - test tube cleaning, test tube arrangement, and powder transfer - demonstrate an average success rate of 86.6%, significantly outperforming alternative lightweight baselines. Furthermore, detailed task prompts improve vision-language alignment and task performance. These results indicate that small foundation models, when properly aligned and integrated with diffusion-based policy learning, can effectively support practical laboratory automation with limited computational resources.

AI.tldr: Compact imitation learning for lab automation using sub-500M param TVF-DiT framework, aligning a self-supervised vision foundation model with a vision-language model via a compact adapter, integrated with a diffusion-transformer action expert. Demonstrates 86.6% average success on test tube cleaning/arrangement/powder transfer on low-VRAM GPUs.
AI.motivation: Reduce design cost and computational demands of imitation learning in laboratory automation by leveraging small foundation models and efficient alignment with language-conditioned policies, enabling practical deployment on accessible hardware.
AI.method: TVF-DiT architecture: a compact adapter aligns a self-supervised vision foundation model with a vision-language model; diffusion-transformer-based action policy; total parameters < 500M; real-world tasks: test tube cleaning, test tube arrangement, powder transfer; uses detailed task prompts to enhance vision-language alignment and task performance.
AI.result: Average success rate of 86.6%, outperforming lightweight baselines; inference feasible on low-VRAM GPUs; three real-world lab tasks validate the approach.
AI.conclusion: Small foundation models, when properly aligned and fused with diffusion-based policy learning, can effectively support practical laboratory automation under limited computational resources.

title: TENG-BC: Unified Time-Evolving Natural Gradient for Neural PDE Solvers with General Boundary Conditions
summary: Accurately solving time-dependent partial differential equations (PDEs) with neural networks remains challenging due to long-time error accumulation and the difficulty of enforcing general boundary conditions. We introduce TENG-BC, a high-precision neural PDE solver based on the Time-Evolving Natural Gradient, designed to perform under general boundary constraints. At each time step, TENG-BC performs a boundary-aware optimization that jointly enforces interior dynamics and boundary conditions, accommodating Dirichlet, Neumann, Robin, and mixed types within a unified framework. This formulation admits a natural-gradient interpretation, enabling stable time evolution without delicate penalty tuning. Across benchmarks over diffusion, transport, and nonlinear PDEs with various boundary conditions, TENG-BC achieves solver-level accuracy under comparable sampling budgets, outperforming conventional solvers and physics-informed neural network (PINN) baselines.

AI.tldr: Introduces TENG-BC, a time-evolving natural gradient neural PDE solver that enforces general boundary conditions at each time step, achieving high accuracy and stability compared to conventional solvers and PINNs across diffusion, transport, and nonlinear PDEs.
AI.motivation: Address persistent long-time error accumulation in neural PDE solvers and the challenge of enforcing diverse boundary conditions. Many existing neural approaches, including PINNs, struggle with general boundary types (Dirichlet, Neumann, Robin, mixed) and require delicate penalty tuning.
AI.method: At each time step, perform a boundary-aware optimization that jointly enforces interior PDE dynamics and boundary conditions within a unified framework. Leverage Time-Evolving Natural Gradient to provide a natural-gradient interpretation and stable time evolution without penalty tuning.
AI.result: Empirical benchmarks on diffusion, transport, and nonlinear PDEs with various boundary conditions show solver-level accuracy under comparable sampling budgets, outperforming conventional solvers and PINN baselines.
AI.conclusion: TENG-BC offers stable, high-precision neural PDE solving under general boundary constraints through a boundary-aware, natural-gradient-based time-stepping approach, with advantages over traditional solvers and PINNs.

title: Zero-Shot and Supervised Bird Image Segmentation Using Foundation Models: A Dual-Pipeline Approach with Grounding DINO~1.5, YOLOv11, and SAM~2.1
summary: Bird image segmentation remains a challenging task in computer vision due to extreme pose diversity, complex plumage patterns, and variable lighting conditions. This paper presents a dual-pipeline framework for binary bird image segmentation leveraging 2025 foundation models. We introduce two operating modes built upon Segment Anything Model 2.1 (SAM 2.1) as a shared frozen backbone: (1) a zero-shot pipeline using Grounding DINO 1.5 to detect birds via the text prompt "bird" before prompting SAM 2.1 with bounding boxes requiring no labelled bird data; and (2) a supervised pipeline that fine-tunes YOLOv11 on the CUB-200-2011 dataset for high-precision detection, again prompting SAM 2.1 for pixel-level masks. The segmentation model is never retrained for new species or domains. On CUB-200-2011 (11,788 images, 200 species), the supervised pipeline achieves IoU 0.912, Dice 0.954, and F1 0.953 outperforming all prior baselines including SegFormer-B2 (IoU 0.842) by +7.0 percentage points. The zero-shot pipeline achieves IoU 0.831 using only a text prompt, the first such result reported on this benchmark. We demonstrate that prompt-based foundation model pipelines outperform task specific end-to-end trained segmentation networks, while requiring only lightweight detector fine-tuning (~1 hour) for domain adaptation. Complete PyTorch implementation, dataset preparation scripts, and trained weights are publicly available.

AI.tldr: A dual-pipeline bird segmentation framework leveraging SAM 2.1 as a frozen backbone with (i) zero-shot using Grounding DINO for bird detection via text prompt, and (ii) supervised YOLOv11 fine-tuned on CUB-200-2011, both producing pixel-level masks through SAM 2.1. It achieves state-of-the-art segmentation on CUB-200-2011 with strong IoU and Dice scores and demonstrates that prompt-based foundation models can outperform end-to-end segmentation networks with minimal detector fine-tuning (~1 hour).
AI.motivation: Bird image segmentation is challenged by extreme pose diversity, intricate plumage, and illumination variability. Prompt-driven foundation models offer a way to leverage broad visual knowledge and generate accurate masks without large task-specific training, enabling zero-shot and lightweight domain adaptation.
AI.method: Two operating modes built on a frozen SAM 2.1 backbone: (1) zero-shot pipeline uses Grounding DINO 1.5 to detect birds from the text prompt 'bird', providing bounding boxes to SAM 2.1 to obtain masks without labeled bird data; (2) supervised pipeline fine-tunes YOLOv11 on CUB-200-2011 for high-precision bird detection, then feeds detected boxes to SAM 2.1 for pixel-level masks. The segmentation model itself is not retrained for new species or domains.
AI.result: On CUB-200-2011 (11,788 images, 200 species), supervised pipeline achieves IoU 0.912, Dice 0.954, F1 0.953, outperforming SegFormer-B2 (IoU 0.842) by 0.070 IoU points. Zero-shot pipeline attains IoU 0.831, representing the first such result on this benchmark.
AI.conclusion: Prompt-based, foundation-model pipelines can surpass task-specific end-to-end segmentation networks while enabling only lightweight detector fine-tuning (~1 hour) for domain adaptation; complete PyTorch implementation, data preparation scripts, and trained weights are publicly available.

title: MMCOMET: A Large-Scale Multimodal Commonsense Knowledge Graph for Contextual Reasoning
summary: We present MMCOMET, the first multimodal commonsense knowledge graph (MMKG) that integrates physical, social, and eventive knowledge. MMCOMET extends the ATOMIC2020 knowledge graph to include a visual dimension, through an efficient image retrieval process, resulting in over 900K multimodal triples. This new resource addresses a major limitation of existing MMKGs in supporting complex reasoning tasks like image captioning and storytelling. Through a standard visual storytelling experiment, we show that our holistic approach enables the generation of richer, coherent, and contextually grounded stories than those produced using text-only knowledge. This resource establishes a new foundation for multimodal commonsense reasoning and narrative generation.

AI.tldr: MMCOMET extends ATOMIC2020 with a visual dimension to form a multimodal commonsense knowledge graph (~900K triples), enabling richer storytelling beyond text-only knowledge.
AI.motivation: Addresses the gap in multimodal commonsense resources that integrate physical, social, and eventive knowledge with visual data to support complex reasoning tasks.
AI.method: Augments ATOMIC2020 with retrieved images to produce multimodal triples; uses an efficient image retrieval pipeline; evaluates on visual storytelling showing improved coherence and grounding.
AI.result: Produces over 900K multimodal triples; demonstrates richer, coherent, contextually grounded stories compared to text-only baselines.
AI.conclusion: MMCOMET establishes a new foundation for multimodal commonsense reasoning and narrative generation, enabling tasks requiring visual-grounded reasoning.

title: USE: Uncertainty Structure Estimation for Robust Semi-Supervised Learning
summary: In this study, a novel idea, Uncertainty Structure Estimation (USE), a lightweight, algorithm-agnostic procedure that emphasizes the often-overlooked role of unlabeled data quality is introduced for Semi-supervised learning (SSL). SSL has achieved impressive progress, but its reliability in deployment is limited by the quality of the unlabeled pool. In practice, unlabeled data are almost always contaminated by out-of-distribution (OOD) samples, where both near-OOD and far-OOD can negatively affect performance in different ways. We argue that the bottleneck does not lie in algorithmic design, but rather in the absence of principled mechanisms to assess and curate the quality of unlabeled data. The proposed USE trains a proxy model on the labeled set to compute entropy scores for unlabeled samples, and then derives a threshold, via statistical comparison against a reference distribution, that separates informative (structured) from uninformative (structureless) samples. This enables assessment as a preprocessing step, removing uninformative or harmful unlabeled data before SSL training begins. Through extensive experiments on imaging (CIFAR-100) and NLP (Yelp Review) data, it is evident that USE consistently improves accuracy and robustness under varying levels of OOD contamination. Thus, it can be concluded that the proposed approach reframes unlabeled data quality control as a structural assessment problem, and considers it as a necessary component for reliable and efficient SSL in realistic mixed-distribution environments.

AI.tldr: Uncertainty Structure Estimation (USE) is a lightweight SSL preprocessing method that filters unlabeled data by entropy-based scoring to separate informative from uninformative samples, improving SSL robustness to OOD contamination.
AI.motivation: SSL performance is hampered by unlabeled data quality; unlabeled pools are often contaminated with near-ODE and far-OOD samples; a principled data-quality control step is needed instead of relying solely on algorithmic design.
AI.method: Train a proxy model on the labeled data to compute entropy scores for unlabeled samples; derive a threshold via statistical comparison against a reference distribution to separate structured (informative) from uninformative (structureless) samples; use this preprocessing to remove unsuitable unlabeled data before SSL training.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on CIFAR-100 (image) and Yelp (text) datasets shows improved accuracy and robustness across varying levels of OOD contamination.
AI.conclusion: Reframes unlabeled data quality control as a structural assessment problem and argues it is essential for reliable and efficient SSL in realistic mixed-distribution environments.

title: Efficient Long-Horizon GUI Agents via Training-Free KV Cache Compression
summary: Large Vision-Language Models (VLMs) have emerged as powerful engines for autonomous GUI agents, yet their deployment is severely constrained by the substantial memory footprint and latency of the Key-Value (KV) cache during long-horizon interactions. While existing cache compression methods have proven effective for LLMs, we empirically demonstrate that they suffer from suboptimal performance in GUI scenarios due to a fundamental misalignment: unlike general visual tasks where attention sparsity varies across layers, GUI attention patterns exhibit uniform high-sparsity across all transformer layers. Motivated by this insight, we propose ST-Lite, a training-free KV cache compression framework tailored for efficient GUI agents that explicitly addresses the dynamic spatio-trajectory dependencies within GUI data streams. ST-Lite introduces a novel dual-branch scoring policy incorporating Component-centric Spatial Saliency (CSS) and Trajectory-aware Semantic Gating (TSG). Specifically, CSS preserves the structural integrity of interactive UI elements by evaluating local neighborhood saliency, while TSG mitigates historical redundancy by dynamically filtering visually repetitive KV pairs within the interaction trajectory. Extensive evaluations demonstrate that with only a 10-20% cache budget, ST-Lite achieves a 2.45x decoding acceleration while maintaining comparable or even superior performance compared to full-cache baselines, offering a scalable solution for resource-constrained GUI agents.

AI.tldr: ST-Lite is a training-free KV cache compression framework for GUI agents that uses Component-centric Spatial Saliency (CSS) and Trajectory-aware Semantic Gating (TSG) to maintain performance with a small cache budget (10-20%), achieving ~2.45x decoding acceleration while preserving or improving accuracy vs full-cache.
AI.motivation: GUI-powered autonomous agents incur high memory and latency from KV cache. Conventional cache compression methods underperform in GUI settings due to uniform high sparsity across transformer layers, unlike general visual tasks. A GUI-specific solution is needed to exploit spatio-trajectory characteristics of GUI data streams.
AI.method: Dual-branch scoring policy: Component-centric Spatial Saliency (CSS) preserves UI structure by evaluating local neighborhood saliency; Trajectory-aware Semantic Gating (TSG) reduces redundancy by filtering repetitive KV pairs along the interaction trajectory. The approach is training-free and tailored to GUI data streams.
AI.result: With only a 10-20% cache budget, ST-Lite achieves approximately 2.45x decoding acceleration while delivering comparable or superior performance to full-cache baselines, validated through extensive evaluations.
AI.conclusion: ST-Lite offers a scalable, training-free KV cache compression framework for GUI agents by addressing dynamic spatio-trajectory dependencies, enabling substantial speedups with modest cache budgets without sacrificing performance.

title: Alien Science: Sampling Coherent but Cognitively Unavailable Research Directions from Idea Atoms
comment: Published at the ICLR 2026 Post-AGI Science and Society Workshop
summary: Large language models are adept at synthesizing and recombining familiar material, yet they often fail at a specific kind of creativity that matters most in research: producing ideas that are both coherent and non-obvious to the current community. We formalize this gap through cognitive availability, the likelihood that a research direction would be naturally proposed by a typical researcher given what they have worked on. We introduce a pipeline that (i) decomposes papers into granular conceptual units, (ii) clusters recurring units into a shared vocabulary of idea atoms, and (iii) learns two complementary models: a coherence model that scores whether a set of atoms constitutes a viable direction, and an availability model that scores how likely that direction is to be generated by researchers drawn from the community. We then sample "alien" directions that score high on coherence but low on availability. On a corpus of $\sim$7,500 recent LLM papers from NeurIPS, ICLR and ICML, we validate that (a) conceptual units preserve paper content under reconstruction, (b) idea atoms generalize across papers rather than memorizing paper-specific phrasing, and (c) the Alien sampler produces research directions that are more diverse than LLM baselines while maintaining coherence.

AI.tldr: Formalizes cognitive availability, builds a pipeline to extract idea atoms from papers, trains coherence and availability models, and samples 'alien' directions that are coherent yet unlikely to be proposed by typical researchers; validated on ~7.5k LLM papers showing diverse, coherent novel directions.
AI.motivation: LLMs excel at recombining known ideas but struggle to generate ideas that are both coherent and non-obvious to the research community; define cognitive availability to quantify how likely a direction would be proposed by researchers.
AI.method: Decompose papers into granular conceptual units; cluster units into a shared vocabulary of idea atoms; learn a coherence model predicting viability of a set of atoms; learn an availability model predicting likelihood given a researcher community; sample alien directions with high coherence but low availability; validate on NeurIPS/ICML/ICLR corpora; test reconstruction, generalization of atoms, diversity and coherence of samples.
AI.result: Conceptual units reconstruct content; idea atoms generalize across papers; alien sampler yields more diverse directions while preserving coherence; demonstration on ~7,500 papers.
AI.conclusion: This framework enables discovery of more diverse and coherent research directions by balancing novelty (low availability) and feasibility (high coherence); potential tool for guiding AI-assisted research.

title: Fast Confidence-Aware Human Prediction via Hardware-accelerated Bayesian Inference for Safe Robot Navigation
summary: As robots increasingly integrate into everyday environments, ensuring their safe navigation around humans becomes imperative. Efficient and safe motion planning requires robots to account for human behavior, particularly in constrained spaces such as grocery stores or care homes, where interactions with multiple individuals are common. Prior research has employed Bayesian frameworks to model human rationality based on navigational intent, enabling the prediction of probabilistic trajectories for planning purposes. In this work, we present a simple yet novel approach for confidence-aware prediction that treats future predictions as particles. This framework is highly parallelized and accelerated on an graphics processing unit (GPU). As a result, this enables longer-term predictions at a frequency of 125 Hz and can be easily extended for multi-human predictions. Compared to existing methods, our implementation supports finer prediction time steps, yielding more granular trajectory forecasts. This enhanced resolution allows motion planners to respond effectively to subtle changes in human behavior. We validate our approach through real-world experiments, demonstrating a robot safely navigating among multiple humans with diverse navigational goals. Our results highlight the methods potential for robust and efficient human-robot coexistence in dynamic environments.

AI.tldr: GPU-accelerated particle-based, confidence-aware human-trajectory prediction for safe, real-time robotic navigation in multi-human environments.
AI.motivation: Need for safe, real-time motion planning around humans; prior Bayesian approaches model rationality but are computationally intensive; require faster predictions to handle dynamic, multi-human scenarios.
AI.method: Treats future predictions as particles; highly parallelizable and GPU-accelerated; allows longer-term predictions at 125 Hz; supports multi-human scenarios; uses finer time steps for granular forecasts.
AI.result: Real-world experiments show safe navigation among multiple humans; improved granularity and prediction frequency; demonstrated robustness and efficiency for human-robot coexistence.
AI.conclusion: The approach offers a scalable, efficient framework for confidence-aware trajectory forecasting, enabling robust motion planning in dynamic human-rich environments; easily extendable to multi-human scenarios.

title: Exact and Asymptotically Complete Robust Verifications of Neural Networks via Quantum Optimization
summary: Deep neural networks (DNNs) enable high performance across domains but remain vulnerable to adversarial perturbations, limiting their use in safety-critical settings. Here, we introduce two quantum-optimization-based models for robust verification that reduce the combinatorial burden of certification under bounded input perturbations. For piecewise-linear activations (e.g., ReLU and hardtanh), our first model yields an exact formulation that is sound and complete, enabling precise identification of adversarial examples. For general activations (including sigmoid and tanh), our second model constructs scalable over-approximations via piecewise-constant bounds and is asymptotically complete, with approximation error vanishing as the segmentation is refined. We further integrate Quantum Benders Decomposition with interval arithmetic to accelerate solving, and propose certificate-transfer bounds that relate robustness guarantees of pruned networks to those of the original model. Finally, a layerwise partitioning strategy supports a quantum--classical hybrid workflow by coupling subproblems across depth. Experiments on robustness benchmarks show high certification accuracy, indicating that quantum optimization can serve as a principled primitive for robustness guarantees in neural networks with complex activations.

AI.tldr: Quantum-optimization-based verification models for DNN robustness under bounded perturbations, offering exact results for piecewise-linear activations and scalable over-approximations for general activations, with hybrid quantum-classical workflow and acceleration via Quantum Benders Decomposition and interval arithmetic.
AI.motivation: Improve robustness certification of neural networks against adversarial perturbations by reducing combinatorial complexity through quantum optimization and decomposition techniques.
AI.method: 1) Exact, sound, and complete formulation for piecewise-linear activations (e.g., ReLU) using quantum optimization. 2) Scalable over-approximation for general activations via piecewise-constant bounds with asymptotic completeness as segmentation refines. 3) Integration of Quantum Benders Decomposition with interval arithmetic to accelerate solving. 4) Certificate-transfer bounds linking pruned networks to original models. 5) Layerwise partitioning for a quantum–classical hybrid workflow across network depth.
AI.result: Empirical robustness certification on benchmarks with high accuracy, demonstrating that quantum optimization can provide principled robustness guarantees for networks with diverse activations.
AI.conclusion: Quantum optimization is a viable primitive for robustness guarantees in neural networks, enabling exact verification for piecewise-linear activations and scalable, asymptotically complete over-approximations for general activations, especially when combined with decomposition, interval arithmetic, and layerwise partitioning.

title: SKeDA: A Generative Watermarking Framework for Text-to-video Diffusion Models
summary: The rise of text-to-video generation models has raised growing concerns over content authenticity, copyright protection, and malicious misuse. Watermarking serves as an effective mechanism for regulating such AI-generated content, where high fidelity and strong robustness are particularly critical. Recent generative image watermarking methods provide a promising foundation by leveraging watermark information and pseudo-random keys to control the initial sampling noise, enabling lossless embedding. However, directly extending these techniques to videos introduces two key limitations: Existing designs implicitly rely on strict alignment between video frames and frame-dependent pseudo-random binary sequences used for watermark encryption. Once this alignment is disrupted, subsequent watermark extraction becomes unreliable; and Video-specific distortions, such as inter-frame compression, significantly degrade watermark reliability. To address these issues, we propose SKeDA, a generative watermarking framework tailored for text-to-video diffusion models. SKeDA consists of two components: (1) Shuffle-Key-based Distribution-preserving Sampling (SKe) employs a single base pseudo-random binary sequence for watermark encryption and derives frame-level encryption sequences through permutation. This design transforms watermark extraction from synchronization-sensitive sequence decoding into permutation-tolerant set-level aggregation, substantially improving robustness against frame reordering and loss; and (2) Differential Attention (DA), which computes inter-frame differences and dynamically adjusts attention weights during extraction, enhancing robustness against temporal distortions. Extensive experiments demonstrate that SKeDA preserves high video generation quality and watermark robustness.

AI.tldr: SKeDA introduces Shuffle-Key-based Distribution-preserving Sampling and Differential Attention to watermark text-to-video generations, achieving permutation-tolerant, synchronization-insensitive watermark extraction and robustness to temporal distortions while preserving video quality.
AI.motivation: Extend image-based watermarking to video generation by addressing frame alignment sensitivity and vulnerability to inter-frame distortions such as reordering, loss, and compression.
AI.method: (1) SKe uses a single base PR binary sequence for watermark encryption and derives frame-level sequences via permutation, enabling permutation-tolerant set-level aggregation instead of sequence decoding. (2) Differential Attention (DA) computes inter-frame differences and dynamically adjusts attention weights during extraction to handle temporal distortions.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show SKeDA maintains high video generation quality while improving watermark robustness against frame reordering, frame loss, and temporal distortions.
AI.conclusion: SKeDA provides a robust, high-quality watermarking framework for text-to-video diffusion models, addressing synchronization and temporal distortion challenges in video watermarking.

title: DIVA-GRPO: Enhancing Multimodal Reasoning through Difficulty-Adaptive Variant Advantage
comment: Accepted to ICLR 2026. Code and models are available at https://github.com/Siaaaaaa1/DIVA-GRPO
summary: Reinforcement learning (RL) with group relative policy optimization (GRPO) has become a widely adopted approach for enhancing the reasoning capabilities of multimodal large language models (MLLMs). While GRPO enables long-chain reasoning without a critic, it often suffers from sparse rewards on difficult problems and advantage vanishing when group-level rewards are too consistent for overly easy or hard problems. Existing solutions (sample expansion, selective utilization, and indirect reward design) often fail to maintain enough variance in within-group reward distributions to yield clear optimization signals. To address this, we propose DIVA-GRPO, a difficulty-adaptive variant advantage method that adjusts variant difficulty distributions from a global perspective. DIVA-GRPO dynamically assesses problem difficulty, samples variants with appropriate difficulty levels, and calculates advantages across local and global groups using difficulty-weighted and normalized scaling. This alleviates reward sparsity and advantage vanishing while improving training stability. Extensive experiments on six reasoning benchmarks demonstrate that DIVA-GRPO outperforms existing approaches in training efficiency and reasoning performance. Code: https://github.com/Siaaaaaa1/DIVA-GRPO

AI.tldr: DIVA-GRPO introduces a difficulty-adaptive variant advantage method for GRPO in multimodal LLMs, mitigating reward sparsity and advantage vanishing, yielding improved training efficiency and reasoning performance across six benchmarks.
AI.motivation: GRPO without a critic suffers from sparse rewards and vanishing advantages when group rewards are too consistent; existing remedies fail to preserve within-group variance, hindering clear optimization signals.
AI.method: Propose DIVA-GRPO, which dynamically assesses problem difficulty, samples variants at appropriate difficulty levels, and computes advantages across local and global groups using difficulty-weighted and normalized scaling to maintain informative signals.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on six reasoning benchmarks shows superior training efficiency and reasoning performance compared to existing approaches; code provided.
AI.conclusion: Difficulty-adaptive variant sampling with normalized, difficulty-weighted advantages alleviates reward sparsity and advantage vanishing, improving stability and performance in GRPO for multimodal LLMs.

title: Pro-HOI: Perceptive Root-guided Humanoid-Object Interaction
summary: Executing reliable Humanoid-Object Interaction (HOI) tasks for humanoid robots is hindered by the lack of generalized control interfaces and robust closed-loop perception mechanisms. In this work, we introduce Perceptive Root-guided Humanoid-Object Interaction, Pro-HOI, a generalizable framework for robust humanoid loco-manipulation. First, we collect box-carrying motions that are suitable for real-world deployment and optimize penetration artifacts through a Signed Distance Field loss. Second, we propose a novel training framework that conditions the policy on a desired root-trajectory while utilizing reference motion exclusively as a reward. This design not only eliminates the need for intricate reward tuning but also establishes root trajectory as a universal interface for high-level planners, enabling simultaneous navigation and loco-manipulation. Furthermore, to ensure operational reliability, we incorporate a persistent object estimation module. By fusing real-time detection with Digital Twin, this module allows the robot to autonomously detect slippage and trigger re-grasping maneuvers. Empirical validation on a Unitree G1 robot demonstrates that Pro-HOI significantly outperforms baselines in generalization and robustness, achieving reliable long-horizon execution in complex real-world scenarios.

AI.tldr: Pro-HOI is a generalizable root-trajectory conditioned framework for robust humanoid loco-manipulation, using SDF loss to reduce penetration, reference-motion-based reward to avoid reward tuning, and a Digital Twin–assisted persistent object estimation module to detect slippage and trigger re-grasping; validated on Unitree G1 with improved generalization and long-horizon reliability.
AI.motivation: Addresses the lack of generalized control interfaces and reliable closed-loop perception in humanoid object interaction, and the need for a universal root-trajectory interface to coordinate navigation and loco-manipulation.
AI.method: 1) Collect real-world box-carrying motions and apply a Signed Distance Field loss to minimize penetration artifacts. 2) Train policy conditioned on a target root-trajectory while using reference motion solely as reward, removing reward-tuning requirements and enabling root-trajectory as a universal planner interface. 3) Add a persistent object estimation module by fusing real-time detections with a Digital Twin to autonomously detect slippage and trigger re-grasping maneuvers.
AI.result: Unitree G1 experiments show Pro-HOI substantially outperforms baselines in generalization and robustness, achieving reliable long-horizon execution in complex real-world scenarios.
AI.conclusion: Pro-HOI provides a generalizable, robust HOI framework that leverages root-trajectory conditioning and enhanced perception to enable reliable loco-manipulation in real-world settings; demonstrates strong transferability and resilience to disturbances.

title: Physics-Aware Learnability: From Set-Theoretic Independence to Operational Constraints
comment: 31 pages, 4 figures (Main Text + Supplementary Information) / Comment welcome
summary: Beyond binary classification, learnability can become a logically fragile notion: in EMX, even the class of all finite subsets of $[0,1]$ is learnable in some models of ZFC and not in others. We argue the paradox is operational. The standard definitions quantify over arbitrary set-theoretic learners that implicitly assume non-operational resources (infinite precision, unphysical data access, and non-representable outputs). We introduce physics-aware learnability (PL), which defines the learnability relative to an explicit access model -- a family of admissible physical protocols. Finite-precision coarse-graining reduces continuum EMX to a countable problem, via an exact pushforward/pullback reduction that preserves the EMX objective, making the independence example provably learnable with explicit $(ε,δ)$ sample complexity. For quantum data, admissible learners are exactly POVMs on $d$ copies, turning sample size into copy complexity and yielding Helstrom(-type) lower bounds. For finite no-signaling and quantum models, PL feasibility becomes linear or semidefinite and is therefore decidable.

AI.tldr: Physics-aware learnability (PL) makes learnability operational by tying it to explicit physical access models; finite-precision coarse-graining reduces continuum EMX to a countable problem and yields sample-complexity bounds; in quantum settings, learnability becomes copy complexity with Helstrom-type bounds; for finite no-signaling and quantum models, PL feasibility is decidable via linear or semidefinite programs.
AI.motivation: EMX can be logically fragile and vary across set-theoretic models (e.g., ZFC), since standard learnability assumes non-operational resources. PL seeks an operational notion by grounding learnability in concrete physical access constraints.
AI.method: Define physics-aware learnability (PL) with an explicit access model (family of admissible physical protocols); show that finite-precision coarse-graining yields a countable reduction of continuum EMX via exact pushforward/pullback reductions that preserve the EMX objective; extend to quantum data by restricting learners to POVMs on d copies; derive complexity and decidability results (linear/SDP) for finite no-signaling and quantum models.
AI.result: The paradox is resolved: the independence example becomes provably learnable under explicit access with (ε,δ) sample complexity; quantum data yield copy complexity and Helstrom-type lower bounds; PL feasibility is decidable in finite no-signaling and quantum settings via linear or SDP formulations.
AI.conclusion: PL provides an operational, decidable framework that attaches learnability to concrete physical access models, rendering EMX tasks tractable under finite-precision and quantum/no-signaling constraints through pushforward/pullback reductions and convex-optimization tools.

title: A Case Study on Concept Induction for Neuron-Level Interpretability in CNN
summary: Deep Neural Networks (DNNs) have advanced applications in domains such as healthcare, autonomous systems, and scene understanding, yet the internal semantics of their hidden neurons remain poorly understood. Prior work introduced a Concept Induction-based framework for hidden neuron analysis and demonstrated its effectiveness on the ADE20K dataset. In this case study, we investigate whether the approach generalizes by applying it to the SUN2012 dataset, a large-scale scene recognition benchmark. Using the same workflow, we assign interpretable semantic labels to neurons and validate them through web-sourced images and statistical testing. Our findings confirm that the method transfers to SUN2012, showing its broader applicability.

AI.tldr: Concept Induction-based hidden-neuron analysis generalizes from ADE20K to SUN2012, enabling semantic labeling of neurons and validation via web images and statistics.
AI.motivation: Test whether the hidden-neuron interpretability framework is dataset-agnostic and scalable to a larger, different scene dataset.
AI.method: Apply the same Concept Induction workflow on SUN2012: assign interpretable semantic labels to neurons and validate them using web-sourced images and statistical testing.
AI.result: The approach transfers to SUN2012, showing broader applicability and cross-dataset generalization of neuron semantics labeling.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates that Concept Induction for hidden neurons can generalize across scene datasets, supporting wider applicability of interpretable neuron analyses.

title: HVR-Met: A Hypothesis-Verification-Replaning Agentic System for Extreme Weather Diagnosis
summary: While deep learning-based weather forecasting paradigms have made significant strides, addressing extreme weather diagnostics remains a formidable challenge. This gap exists primarily because the diagnostic process demands sophisticated multi-step logical reasoning, dynamic tool invocation, and expert-level prior judgment. Although agents possess inherent advantages in task decomposition and autonomous execution, current architectures are still hampered by critical bottlenecks: inadequate expert knowledge integration, a lack of professional-grade iterative reasoning loops, and the absence of fine-grained validation and evaluation systems for complex workflows under extreme conditions. To this end, we propose HVR-Met, a multi-agent meteorological diagnostic system characterized by the deep integration of expert knowledge. Its central innovation is the ``Hypothesis-Verification-Replanning'' closed-loop mechanism, which facilitates sophisticated iterative reasoning for anomalous meteorological signals during extreme weather events. To bridge gaps within existing evaluation frameworks, we further introduce a novel benchmark focused on atomic-level subtasks. Experimental evidence demonstrates that the system excels in complex diagnostic scenarios.

AI.tldr: Introduces HVR-Met, a multi-agent meteorological diagnostic system with a Hypothesis–Verification–Replanning loop and a new atomic-level benchmark, achieving strong performance in extreme-weather diagnostics through expert-knowledge integration and iterative reasoning.
AI.motivation: Extreme-weather diagnostics demand multi-step reasoning, dynamic tool use, and high-quality prior judgment. Current DL-based approaches underperform due to inadequate expert knowledge integration, lack of iterative reasoning loops, and weak fine-grained evaluation under extreme conditions.
AI.method: Proposes HVR-Met, a multi-agent architecture with a closed-loop Hypothesis–Verification–Replanning mechanism that deeply integrates expert meteorological knowledge. Also introduces a benchmark focused on atomic-level subtasks to evaluate subcomponents and complex workflows in extreme-event scenarios.
AI.result: Experimental evidence shows the system excels in complex diagnostic scenarios involving anomalous meteorological signals during extreme weather events and performs well on the new atomic-level benchmark, indicating improved diagnostic capability and robustness.
AI.conclusion: HVR-Met advances automated extreme-weather diagnostics by tightly coupling expert knowledge with iterative hypothesis-driven reasoning; the atomic-level benchmark fills evaluation gaps and supports more reliable, interpretable meteorological AI systems.

title: A Deployable Bio-inspired Compliant Leg Design for Enhanced Leaping in Quadruped Robots
summary: Quadruped robots are becoming increasingly essential for various applications, including industrial inspection and catastrophe search and rescue. These scenarios require robots to possess enhanced agility and obstacle-navigation skills. Nonetheless, the performance of current platforms is often constrained by insufficient peak motor power, limiting their ability to perform explosive jumps. To address this challenge, this paper proposes a bio-inspired method that emulates the energy-storage mechanism found in froghopper legs. We designed a Deployable Compliant Leg (DCL) utilizing a specialized 3D-printed elastic material, Polyether block amide (PEBA), featuring a lightweight internal lattice structure. This structure functions analogously to biological tendons, storing elastic energy during the robot's squatting phase and rapidly releasing it to augment motor output during the leap. The proposed mechanical design significantly enhances the robot's vertical jumping capability. Through finite element analysis (FEA) and experimental validation, we demonstrate a relative performance improvement of 17.1% in vertical jumping height.

AI.tldr: A bio-inspired Deployable Compliant Leg (DCL) for quadrupeds uses a 3D-printed PEBA lattice to store elastic energy during squats and release it during leaps, boosting vertical jump height by 17.1% in FE and experiments.
AI.motivation: Address the limited peak motor power of quadruped platforms, which hampers explosive jumping required for inspection and search-and-rescue tasks.
AI.method: Design and integrate a DCL with a PEBA lattice that mimics tendons, employing energy storage during the squat phase and rapid energy release during the leap; validate via finite element analysis and physical experiments.
AI.result: Demonstrates a relative 17.1% improvement in vertical jumping height and enhanced jumping capability.
AI.conclusion: Elastic-energy storage via a bio-inspired compliant tendon mechanism can significantly augment dynamic performance in quadruped robots without solely increasing actuator power.

title: Weight Updates as Activation Shifts: A Principled Framework for Steering
summary: Activation steering promises to be an extremely parameter-efficient form of adaptation, but its effectiveness depends on critical design choices -- such as intervention location and parameterization -- that currently rely on empirical heuristics rather than a principled foundation. We establish a first-order equivalence between activation-space interventions and weight-space updates, deriving the conditions under which activation steering can replicate fine-tuning behavior. This equivalence yields a principled framework for steering design and identifies the post-block output as a theoretically-backed and highly expressive intervention site. We further explain why certain intervention locations outperform others and show that weight updates and activation updates play distinct, complementary functional roles. This analysis motivates a new approach -- joint adaptation -- that trains in both spaces simultaneously. Our post-block steering achieves accuracy within 0.2%-0.9%$ of full-parameter tuning, on average across tasks and models, while training only 0.04% of model parameters. It consistently outperforms prior activation steering methods such as ReFT and PEFT approaches including LoRA, while using significantly fewer parameters. Finally, we show that joint adaptation often surpasses the performance ceilings of weight and activation updates in isolation, introducing a new paradigm for efficient model adaptation.

AI.tldr: We show a first-order equivalence between activation-space interventions and weight-space updates, identify post-block outputs as a principled, expressive intervention site, and propose joint activation+weight adaptation. This yields highly parameter-efficient performance that rivals full fine-tuning and outperforms prior PEFT methods, suggesting joint adaptation as a new paradigm.
AI.motivation: To enable highly parameter-efficient model adaptation with principled design choices for activation steering, addressing the lack of a theoretical foundation for intervention location and parameterization.
