AI.conclusion: Enables systematic, scalable assessment of AI cultural intelligence, facilitating cross-context comparability, standardization, and laying groundwork for future measurement research and benchmarking in AI culture-aware evaluation.

title: Spherical Latent Motion Prior for Physics-Based Simulated Humanoid Control
summary: Learning motion priors for physics-based humanoid control is an active research topic. Existing approaches mainly include variational autoencoders (VAE) and adversarial motion priors (AMP). VAE introduces information loss, and random latent sampling may sometimes produce invalid behaviors. AMP suffers from mode collapse and struggles to capture diverse motion skills. We present the Spherical Latent Motion Prior (SLMP), a two-stage method for learning motion priors. In the first stage, we train a high-quality motion tracking controller. In the second stage, we distill the tracking controller into a spherical latent space. A combination of distillation, a discriminator, and a discriminator-guided local semantic consistency constraint shapes a structured latent action space, allowing stable random sampling without information loss. To evaluate SLMP, we collect a two-hour human combat motion capture dataset and show that SLMP preserves fine motion detail without information loss, and random sampling yields semantically valid and stable behaviors. When applied to a two-agent physics-based combat task, SLMP produces human-like and physically plausible combat behaviors only using simple rule-based rewards. Furthermore, SLMP generalizes across different humanoid robot morphologies, demonstrating its transferability beyond a single simulated avatar.

AI.tldr: Two-stage spherical latent motion prior (SLMP) learning for humanoid control; resolves VAE information loss and AMP mode collapse; enables stable, semantically valid random sampling and cross-morphology transfer.
AI.motivation: Need robust, diverse, and transferable motion priors for physics-based humanoid control; existing VAEs lose information; AMP lacks diversity and suffers mode collapse.
AI.method: Stage 1 train a high-quality motion-tracking controller; Stage 2 distill into a spherical latent space using distillation, a discriminator, and a discriminator-guided local semantic consistency constraint to shape a structured latent action space enabling stable sampling.
AI.result: On a two-hour human combat MoCap dataset, SLMP preserves motion detail and supports semantically valid, stable random sampling. In a two-agent combat robot task, yields human-like, physically plausible behaviors with simple rewards. Demonstrates transferability across humanoid morphologies.
AI.conclusion: SLMP provides an effective, information-preserving latent prior with stable sampling and cross-morphology transfer, addressing key drawbacks of VAE and AMP.

title: A Polynomial-Time Axiomatic Alternative to SHAP for Feature Attribution
comment: 28 pages, 4 figures, 2 tables. Code will be released
summary:
In this paper, we provide a theoretically grounded and computationally efficient alternative to SHAP. To this end, we study feature attribution through the lens of cooperative game theory by formulating a class of XAI--TU games. Building on this formulation, we investigate equal-surplus-type and proportional-allocation-type attribution rules and propose a low-cost attribution rule, ESENSC_rev2, constructed by combining two polynomial-time closed-form rules while ensuring the null-player property in the XAI--TU domain.
  Extensive experiments on tabular prediction tasks demonstrate that ESENSC_rev2 closely approximates exact SHAP while substantially improving scalability as the number of features increases. These empirical results indicate that equal-surplus-type attribution rules can achieve favorable trade-offs between computational cost and approximation accuracy in high-dimensional explainability settings.
  To provide theoretical foundations for these findings, we establish an axiomatic characterization showing that ESENSC_rev2 is uniquely determined by efficiency, the null-player axiom, a restricted differential marginality principle, an intermediate inessential-game property, and axioms that reduce computational requirements. Our results suggest that axiomatically justified and computationally efficient attribution rules can serve as practical and theoretically principled substitutes for SHAP-based approximations in modern explainability pipelines.

AI.tldr: Introduces ESENSC_rev2, a low-cost, SHAP-like attribution rule for XAI-TU games, achieving close SHAP accuracy with polynomial-time closed-form rules and strong axiomatic justification.
AI.motivation: SHAP provides accurate feature attributions but is computationally expensive, especially with many features. The paper seeks a scalable, theoretically grounded alternative within a cooperative game-theoretic framework (XAI-TU games).
AI.method: Formulates equal-surplus-type and proportional-allocation-type attribution rules within XAI-TU games; constructs ESENSC_rev2 by combining two polynomial-time closed-form rules; enforces the null-player property; offers an axiomatic characterization (efficiency, null-player axiom, restricted differential marginality, intermediate inessential-game property, and computational-reduction axioms).
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on tabular data shows ESENSC_rev2 closely approximates exact SHAP while providing substantially better scalability as feature count grows; demonstrates favorable cost-accuracy trade-offs for high-dimensional explanations.
AI.conclusion: Axioms yield a uniquely determined, computationally efficient attribution rule that can serve as a principled substitute for SHAP in modern explainability pipelines, enabling scalable, theory-grounded XAI explanations.

title: Seeking Necessary and Sufficient Information from Multimodal Medical Data
summary: Learning multimodal representations from medical images and other data sources can provide richer information for decision-making. While various multimodal models have been developed for this, they overlook learning features that are both necessary (must be present for the outcome to occur) and sufficient (enough to determine the outcome). We argue learning such features is crucial as they can improve model performance by capturing essential predictive information, and enhance model robustness to missing modalities as each modality can provide adequate predictive signals. Such features can be learned by leveraging the Probability of Necessity and Sufficiency (PNS) as a learning objective, an approach that has proven effective in unimodal settings. However, extending PNS to multimodal scenarios remains underexplored and is non-trivial as key conditions of PNS estimation are violated. We address this by decomposing multimodal representations into modality-invariant and modality-specific components, then deriving tractable PNS objectives for each. Experiments on synthetic and real-world medical datasets demonstrate our method's effectiveness. Code will be available on GitHub.

AI.tldr: Proposes a multimodal learning framework that uses Probability of Necessity and Sufficiency (PNS) to learn features that are both necessary and sufficient for predicting outcomes by decomposing representations into modality-invariant and modality-specific components; derives tractable PNS objectives; shows effectiveness on synthetic and real medical data; code to be released on GitHub.
AI.motivation: In multimodal medicine, features that are necessary and sufficient for the target can improve predictive performance and robustness to missing modalities. PNS has shown promise in unimodal settings, but extending it to multimodal data is challenging due to violated conditions for PNS estimation. The work argues for learning the necessary/sufficient features to enhance reliability across modalities.
AI.method: Decompose multimodal representations into modality-invariant and modality-specific components. Derive tractable PNS objectives for each component, enabling learning signals that reflect necessity and sufficiency across modalities. Train models with these PNS-based objectives on synthetic and real-world medical datasets.
AI.result: Experiments on synthetic and real-world medical datasets demonstrate the method's effectiveness, indicating improved predictive signals and robustness to missing modalities.
AI.conclusion: A PNS-based learning framework for multimodal representations, coupled with a representation decomposition, yields effective and robust predictive features in medical multimodal data; code will be released on GitHub.

title: The Lattice Representation Hypothesis of Large Language Models
summary: We propose the Lattice Representation Hypothesis of large language models: a symbolic backbone that grounds conceptual hierarchies and logical operations in embedding geometry. Our framework unifies the Linear Representation Hypothesis with Formal Concept Analysis (FCA), showing that linear attribute directions with separating thresholds induce a concept lattice via half-space intersections. This geometry enables symbolic reasoning through geometric meet (intersection) and join (union) operations, and admits a canonical form when attribute directions are linearly independent. Experiments on WordNet sub-hierarchies provide empirical evidence that LLM embeddings encode concept lattices and their logical structure, revealing a principled bridge between continuous geometry and symbolic abstraction.

AI.tldr: Proposes the Lattice Representation Hypothesis (LRH) for LLMs: a symbolic backbone grounded in embedding geometry that unifies the Linear Representation Hypothesis with Formal Concept Analysis (FCA) to derive concept lattices from linear attribute directions with thresholds, enabling geometric meet/join symbolic reasoning; canonical form under linear independence; WordNet sub-hierarchies evidence that embeddings encode concept lattices and their logical structure, bridging continuous geometry with symbolic abstraction.
AI.motivation: Bridge continuous embedding geometry and symbolic/logic-based abstraction by showing how linear attribute directions with thresholds induce a concept lattice, enabling symbolic reasoning within LLM embeddings.
AI.method: Integrates the Linear Representation Hypothesis with Formal Concept Analysis; models concept lattice as intersections of half-spaces defined by linear attribute directions and thresholds; establishes a canonical form when attribute directions are linearly independent; defines geometric meet (intersection) and join (union) operations for reasoning; empirically evaluates on WordNet sub-hierarchies.
AI.result: Empirical evidence that LLM embeddings encode concept lattices and their logical structure, indicating a principled link between embedding geometry and symbolic abstraction.
AI.conclusion: The LRH provides a geometric-symbolic framework for LLM reasoning, enabling canonical representations and symbolic operations, with WordNet experiments supporting the central claim.

title: Hybrid TD3: Overestimation Bias Analysis and Stable Policy Optimization for Hybrid Action Space
summary: Reinforcement learning in discrete-continuous hybrid action spaces presents fundamental challenges for robotic manipulation, where high-level task decisions and low-level joint-space execution must be jointly optimized. Existing approaches either discretize continuous components or relax discrete choices into continuous approximations, which suffer from scalability limitations and training instability in high-dimensional action spaces and under domain randomization. In this paper, we propose Hybrid TD3, an extension of Twin Delayed Deep Deterministic Policy Gradient (TD3) that natively handles parameterized hybrid action spaces in a principled manner. We conduct a rigorous theoretical analysis of overestimation bias in hybrid action settings, deriving formal bounds under twin-critic architectures and establishing a complete bias ordering across five algorithmic variants. Building on this analysis, we introduce a weighted clipped Q-learning target that marginalizes over the discrete action distribution, achieving equivalent bias reduction to standard clipped minimization while improving policy smoothness. Experimental results demonstrate that Hybrid TD3 achieves superior training stability and competitive performance against state-of-the-art hybrid action baselines

AI.tldr: Hybrid TD3: a principled extension of TD3 for parameterized hybrid action spaces, featuring a formal bias analysis and a weighted Q-learning target; it delivers improved training stability and competitive performance against state-of-the-art baselines.
AI.motivation: To address reinforcement learning in discrete-continuous hybrid action spaces common in robotic manipulation, where discretization or continuous relaxations lead to scalability issues and training instability under domain randomization.
AI.method: Extend TD3 to handle parameterized hybrid actions with twin critics; perform a formal analysis of overestimation bias in hybrid-action settings, deriving bounds and a bias ordering across five algorithmic variants; introduce a weighted clipped Q-learning target that marginalizes over the discrete action distribution to reduce bias while preserving policy smoothness.
AI.result: Derivation of formal overestimation bias bounds for hybrid action scenarios and a complete bias ordering among five variants; introduction of a weighted clipped Q-learning target achieving equivalent bias reduction to standard clipping but with improved policy smoothness; empirical results show enhanced training stability and competitive performance versus state-of-the-art hybrid baselines.
AI.conclusion: Hybrid TD3 provides a principled, scalable approach to learning in discrete-continuous hybrid action spaces, with a bias-aware target that improves stability and yields competitive results in robotic manipulation benchmarks.

title: Antibody: Strengthening Defense Against Harmful Fine-Tuning for Large Language Models via Attenuating Harmful Gradient Influence
summary: Fine-tuning-as-a-service introduces a threat to Large Language Models' safety when service providers fine-tune their models on poisoned user-submitted datasets, a process known as harmful fine-tuning attacks. In this work, we show that by regularizing the gradient contribution of harmful samples encountered during fine-tuning, we can effectively mitigate the impact of harmful fine-tuning attacks. To this end, we introduce Antibody, a defense strategy that first ensures robust safety alignment for the model before fine-tuning, and then applies a safety-preservation learning algorithm during fine-tuning. Specifically, in the alignment stage before fine-tuning, we propose optimizing the model to be in a flat loss region with respect to harmful samples, which makes the safety alignment more resilient to subsequent harmful fine-tuning. Then, in the fine-tuning stage, we design a fine-tuning algorithm that applies a weighting scheme to all samples in each training batch to inhibit the model from learning from harmful samples while encouraging learning from benign samples. Experimental results demonstrate that Antibody successfully mitigates harmful fine-tuning attacks while boosting fine-tuning performance on the user-submitted dataset.

AI.tldr: Antibody is a two-stage defense against harmful fine-tuning attacks in LLMs: (1) align the model by making the loss landscape flat around harmful samples, and (2) during fine-tuning apply a per-sample weighting to suppress learning from harmful data while promoting benign data, thereby mitigating poisoned datasets and boosting performance on user data.
AI.motivation: To counter harmful fine-tuning attacks in fine-tuning-as-a-service, where attackers submit poisoned data to cause unsafe model behavior.
AI.method: Two-stage approach: (1) alignment phase before fine-tuning that regularizes gradient contributions to harmful samples by pushing the model into a flat loss region relative to those samples; (2) fine-tuning phase that uses a weighting scheme over samples in each batch to downweight harmful examples and upweight benign ones, effectively preserving safety while maintaining learning effectiveness.
AI.result: Experimental results show that Antibody mitigates harmful fine-tuning attacks and improves fine-tuning performance on user-submitted datasets.
AI.conclusion: Antibody combines robust safety alignment with safety-preserving fine-tuning; its two-stage design addresses both preexisting vulnerability and during-training exploitation, with potential for broader applicability and further evaluation under adaptive adversaries.

title: Proof-of-Perception: Certified Tool-Using Multimodal Reasoning with Compositional Conformal Guarantees
summary: We present Proof-of-Perception (PoP), a tool-using framework that casts multimodal reasoning as an executable graph with explicit reliability guarantees. Each perception or logic node outputs a conformal set, yielding calibrated, stepwise uncertainty; a lightweight controller uses these certificates to allocate compute under a budget, expanding with extra tool calls only when needed and stopping early otherwise. This grounds answers in verifiable evidence, reduces error compounding and hallucinations, and enables principled accuracy-compute trade-offs. Across document, chart, and multi-image QA benchmarks, PoP improves performance and reliability over strong chain-of-thought, ReAct-style, and program-of-thought baselines while using computation more efficiently.

AI.tldr: Proof-of-Perception (PoP) is a tool-using framework that models multimodal reasoning as an executable graph where every perception or logic node yields a conformal set; a lightweight controller allocates compute under a budget, expanding tool use only as needed, to ground answers in verifiable evidence and reduce hallucinations, improving efficiency and reliability over strong baselines.
AI.motivation: To mitigate error compounding and hallucinations in multimodal reasoning by introducing calibrated, verifiable uncertainty and a budget-driven compute mechanism that can selectively invoke tools.
AI.method: Construct an executable graph of perception and reasoning nodes; each node outputs a conformal set (calibrated uncertainty). A lightweight controller uses these certificates to manage computation under a budget, expanding tool calls only when necessary and stopping early when sufficient evidence is obtained. Applied to document, chart, and multi-image QA, enabling integration of tools and evidence-based reasoning.
AI.result: PoP achieves higher performance and reliability than chain-of-thought, ReAct-style, and program-of-thought baselines on document, chart, and multi-image QA benchmarks, while using computation more efficiently due to calibrated uncertainty and selective tool usage.
AI.conclusion: PoP provides a principled, verifiable framework for multimodal reasoning that grounds answers in evidence, reduces error propagation and hallucinations, and offers controllable accuracy-compute trade-offs through a toolbox of tool calls guided by conformal certificates.

title: Extended Empirical Validation of the Explainability Solution Space
summary: This technical report provides an extended validation of the Explainability Solution Space (ESS) through cross-domain evaluation. While initial validation focused on employee attrition prediction, this study introduces a heterogeneous intelligent urban resource allocation system to demonstrate the generality and domain-independence of the ESS framework. The second case study integrates tabular, temporal, and geospatial data under multi-stakeholder governance conditions. Explicit quantitative positioning of representative XAI families is provided for both contexts. Results confirm that ESS rankings are not domain-specific but adapt systematically to governance roles, risk profiles, and stakeholder configurations. The findings reinforce ESS as a generalizable operational decision-support instrument for explainable AI strategy design across socio-technical systems.

AI.tldr: ESS generalizes across domains; cross-domain evaluation shows domain-independent rankings that adapt to governance and stakeholder contexts.
AI.motivation: Test the generalizability and domain-independence of the Explainability Solution Space (ESS) by applying it to diverse, governance-rich contexts beyond its original domain.
AI.method: Two case studies: (1) employee attrition prediction, (2) heterogeneous urban resource allocation with tabular, temporal, and geospatial data under multi-stakeholder governance. Explicitly quantify and position representative XAI families in both contexts to assess domain transferability of ESS rankings.
AI.result: ESS rankings are not domain-specific; they adapt systematically to governance roles, risk profiles, and stakeholder configurations, supporting cross-domain applicability.
AI.conclusion: ESS is a generalizable operational decision-support instrument for explainable AI strategy design across socio-technical systems.

title: D-GVIO: A Buffer-Driven and Efficient Decentralized GNSS-Visual-Inertial State Estimator for Multi-Agent Systems
summary: Cooperative localization is essential for swarm applications like collaborative exploration and search-and-rescue missions. However, maintaining real-time capability, robustness, and computational efficiency on resource-constrained platforms presents significant challenges. To address these challenges, we propose D-GVIO, a buffer-driven and fully decentralized GNSS-Visual-Inertial Odometry (GVIO) framework that leverages a novel buffering strategy to support efficient and robust distributed state estimation. The proposed framework is characterized by four core mechanisms. Firstly, through covariance segmentation, covariance intersection and buffering strategy, we modularize propagation and update steps in distributed state estimation, significantly reducing computational and communication burdens. Secondly, the left-invariant extended Kalman filter (L-IEKF) is adopted for information fusion, which exhibits superior state estimation performance over the traditional extended Kalman filter (EKF) since its state transition matrix is independent of the system state. Thirdly, a buffer-based re-propagation strategy is employed to handle delayed measurements efficiently and accurately by leveraging the L-IEKF, eliminating the need for costly re-computation. Finally, an adaptive buffer-driven outlier detection method is proposed to dynamically cull GNSS outliers, enhancing robustness in GNSS-challenged environments.

AI.tldr: A decentralized GNSS-Visual-Inertial Odometry framework (D-GVIO) using buffer-driven propagation, covariance segmentation, left-invariant EKF, buffer-based repropagation, and adaptive outlier detection to improve efficiency, robustness, and real-time performance in GNSS-challenged swarm scenarios.
AI.motivation: To enable real-time, robust, and computationally efficient localization for resource-constrained swarm platforms performing collaborative exploration and rescue tasks, even under GNSS degradation or denial.
AI.method: Modular propagation/update via covariance segmentation and covariance intersection with a buffering strategy; information fusion with left-invariant IEKF (L-IEKF); buffer-based re-propagation to handle measurement delays without full re-computation; adaptive buffer-driven GNSS outlier detection to dynamically cull erroneous GNSS measurements.
AI.result: Claimed improvements include reduced computational and communication burdens, robust distributed state estimation, improved handling of delayed measurements via re-propagation, and enhanced robustness through adaptive GNSS outlier culling in GNSS-challenged environments.
AI.conclusion: D-GVIO presents a cohesive, fully decentralized GVIO framework that combines novel buffering, L-IEKF fusion, and outlier handling to address core swarm localization challenges under resource constraints and imperfect GNSS conditions.

title: Trinity: A Scenario-Aware Recommendation Framework for Large-Scale Cold-Start Users
summary: Early-stage users in a new scenario intensify cold-start challenges, yet prior works often address only parts of the problem through model architecture. Launching a new user experience to replace an established product involves sparse behavioral signals, low-engagement cohorts, and unstable model performance. We argue that effective recommendations require the synergistic integration of feature engineering, model architecture, and stable model updating. We propose Trinity, a framework embodying this principle. Trinity extracts valuable information from existing scenarios while ensuring predictive effectiveness and accuracy in the new scenario. In this paper, we showcase Trinity applied to a billion-user Microsoft product transition. Both offline and online experiments demonstrate that our framework achieves substantial improvements in addressing the combined challenge of new users in new scenarios.

AI.tldr: Trinity is a holistic framework for cold-start in new-user, new-scenario settings that unifies feature engineering, model architecture, and stable updates; demonstrated on a billion-user Microsoft product transition with offline and online gains.
AI.motivation: Address the compounded cold-start problem where sparse signals and low engagement in new scenarios degrade performance; prior work studies components in isolation, lacking a holistic approach.
AI.method: Introduce Trinity, a framework that leverages information from existing scenarios, integrates feature engineering with robust model architectures, and supports stable continual updates to maintain accuracy in the new scenario; evaluated with both offline metrics and online experiments.
AI.result: Offline and online experiments show substantial improvements in addressing the combined challenge of new users in new scenarios.
AI.conclusion: Trinity enables effective recommendations in large-scale transitions by synthesizing cross-scenario knowledge, feature engineering, and stable updating; suitable for deployment in large products.

title: Diffusion-Based Low-Light Image Enhancement with Color and Luminance Priors
summary: Low-light images often suffer from low contrast, noise, and color distortion, degrading visual quality and impairing downstream vision tasks. We propose a novel conditional diffusion framework for low-light image enhancement that incorporates a Structured Control Embedding Module (SCEM). SCEM decomposes a low-light image into four informative components including illumination, illumination-invariant features, shadow priors, and color-invariant cues. These components serve as control signals that condition a U-Net-based diffusion model trained with a simplified noise-prediction loss. Thus, the proposed SCEM equipped Diffusion method enforces structured enhancement guided by physical priors. In experiments, our model is trained only on the LOLv1 dataset and evaluated without fine-tuning on LOLv2-real, LSRW, DICM, MEF, and LIME. The method achieves state-of-the-art performance in quantitative and perceptual metrics, demonstrating strong generalization across benchmarks. https://casted.github.io/scem/.

AI.tldr: A diffusion-based low-light image enhancer using a Structured Control Embedding Module (SCEM) that decomposes inputs into four priors to condition a U-Net diffusion model; trained on LOLv1 and claims state-of-the-art performance with strong cross-dataset generalization without fine-tuning.
AI.motivation: Low-light images suffer from low contrast, noise, and color distortion. A physics-informed, structured conditioning mechanism can guide generative enhancement to preserve fidelity and perceptual quality across varied scenes.
AI.method: SCEM decomposes a low-light image into four components—illumination, illumination-invariant features, shadow priors, and color-invariant cues. These components act as control signals that condition a U-Net-based diffusion model trained with a simplified noise-prediction loss. The model is trained on LOLv1 and evaluated without fine-tuning on LOLv2-real, LSRW, DICM, MEF, and LIME.
AI.result: Reported state-of-the-art performance on both quantitative and perceptual metrics across multiple benchmarks, indicating strong generalization despite single-dataset training.
AI.conclusion: Structured, priors-guided diffusion can effectively enhance low-light images with good generalization; the approach emphasizes physical interpretability via SCEM and reduces the need for cross-dataset fine-tuning.

title: Beyond Reward: A Bounded Measure of Agent Environment Coupling
summary: Real-world reinforcement learning (RL) agents operate in closed-loop systems where actions shape future observations, making reliable deployment under distribution shifts a persistent challenge. Existing monitoring relies on reward or task metrics, capturing outcomes but missing early coupling failures. We introduce bipredictability (P) as the ratio of shared information in the observation, action, outcome loop to the total available information, a principled, real time measure of interaction effectiveness with provable bounds, comparable across tasks. An auxiliary monitor, the Information Digital Twin (IDT), computes P and its diagnostic components from the interaction stream. We evaluate SAC and PPO agents on MuJoCo HalfCheetah under eight agent, and environment-side perturbations across 168 trials. Under nominal operation, agents exhibit P = 0.33 plus minus 0.02, below the classical bound of 0.5, revealing an informational cost of action selection. The IDT detects 89.3% of perturbations versus 44.0% for reward based monitoring, with 4.4x lower median latency. Bipredictability enables early detection of interaction degradation before performance drops and provides a prerequisite signal for closed loop self regulation in deployed RL systems.

AI.tldr: Proposes bipredictability P and an Information Digital Twin (IDT) to monitor real-time RL interaction; demonstrates earlier detection of perturbations and faster latency than reward-based monitors, across SAC/PPO on MuJoCo HalfCheetah.
AI.motivation: Address degradation in real-world RL closed-loop settings under distribution shifts; traditional reward-based monitoring misses early coupling failures; need real-time, task-agnostic interaction-level metrics with provable bounds.
AI.method: Define P as ratio of mutual information shared among observation, action, and outcome to total information available in the loop. Develop Information Digital Twin to compute P and components from interaction stream. Evaluate on SAC and PPO in MuJoCo HalfCheetah with eight perturbation scenarios across 168 trials.
AI.result: Nominal P ~0.33 ± 0.02, below classical bound 0.5, indicating informational cost of action selection. IDT detects 89.3% perturbations vs 44.0% for reward-based monitors; median latency 4.4x lower. IDT enables early detection of interaction degradation prior to performance drop.
AI.conclusion: Bipredictability and IDT offer principled, real-time, cross-task diagnostics for RL in closed-loop systems and support closed-loop self-regulation; practical estimation, scalability, and verification across tasks warrant further work.

title: Jailbreaking Embodied LLMs via Action-level Manipulation
comment: This paper has been officially accepted for ACM SenSys 2026
summary: Embodied Large Language Models (LLMs) enable AI agents to interact with the physical world through natural language instructions and actions. However, beyond the language-level risks inherent to LLMs themselves, embodied LLMs with real-world actuation introduce a new vulnerability: instructions that appear semantically benign may still lead to dangerous real-world consequences, revealing a fundamental misalignment between linguistic security and physical outcomes. In this paper, we introduce Blindfold, an automated attack framework that leverages the limited causal reasoning capabilities of embodied LLMs in real-world action contexts. Rather than iterative trial-and-error jailbreaking of black-box embodied LLMs, Blindfold adopts an Adversarial Proxy Planning strategy: it compromises a local surrogate LLM to perform action-level manipulations that appear semantically safe but could result in harmful physical effects when executed. Blindfold further conceals key malicious actions by injecting carefully crafted noise to evade detection by defense mechanisms, and it incorporates a rule-based verifier to improve the attack executability. Evaluations on both embodied AI simulators and a real-world 6DoF robotic arm show that Blindfold achieves up to 53% higher attack success rates than SOTA baselines, highlighting the urgent need to move beyond surface-level language censorship and toward consequence-aware defense mechanisms to secure embodied LLMs.

AI.tldr: Blindfold exploits embodied LLMs by using an adversarial proxy planning attack: compromising a local surrogate LLM to craft action-level manipulations that look semantically safe but cause harmful physical effects when executed, aided by noise injection to evade defenses and a rule-based verifier. It achieves up to 53% higher attack success than SOTA baselines across simulators and a real 6DoF robotic arm, underscoring the need for consequence-aware defenses.
AI.motivation: Embodied LLMs risk going beyond language safety: semantically benign instructions can lead to dangerous real-world actions due to limited causal reasoning. Current language-only censorship may be insufficient to prevent harmful physical outcomes.
AI.method: Adversarial Proxy Planning: (1) compromise a local surrogate LLM to generate action-level manipulations that appear semantically safe; (2) inject carefully crafted noise to evade detection by defense mechanisms; (3) employ a rule-based verifier to improve attack executability. Unlike iterative jailbreaking of black-box models, the attack operates through a surrogate model and targeted manipulations. Evaluations are performed on embodied AI simulators and a real-world 6DoF robotic arm.
AI.result: Attack success rates outperform SOTA baselines by up to 53% across both simulated and real-world embodied setups, demonstrating a practical vulnerability in embodied LLMs.
AI.conclusion: Surface-level language censorship is insufficient to secure embodied LLMs. It is essential to develop consequence-aware defenses that account for physical outcomes and action-level reasoning to mitigate adversarial manipulation of embodied agents.

title: FastBUS: A Fast Bayesian Framework for Unified Weakly-Supervised Learning
summary: Machine Learning often involves various imprecise labels, leading to diverse weakly supervised settings. While recent methods aim for universal handling, they usually suffer from complex manual pre-work, ignore the relationships between associated labels, or are unable to batch process due to computational design flaws, resulting in long running times. To address these limitations, we propose a novel general framework that efficiently infers latent true label distributions across various weak supervisions. Our key idea is to express the label brute-force search process as a probabilistic transition of label variables, compressing diverse weakly supervised DFS tree structures into a shared Bayesian network. From this, we derived a latent probability calculation algorithm based on generalized belief propagation and proposed two joint acceleration strategies: 1) introducing a low-rank assumption to approximate the transition matrix, reducing time complexity; 2) designing an end-to-end state evolution module to learn batch-scale transition matrices, facilitating multi-category batch processing. In addition, the equivalence of our method with the EM algorithm in most scenarios is further demonstrated. Extensive experiments show that our method achieves SOTA results under most weakly supervised settings, and achieves up to hundreds of times faster acceleration in running time compared to other general methods.

AI.tldr: A general probabilistic framework for weak supervision that unifies diverse label noises into a Bayesian network, enabling efficient latent label inference via generalized belief propagation, with low-rank transition matrix and batch-wise state evolution to achieve large speedups while maintaining SOTA performance, and EM-equivalence in many cases.
AI.motivation: Weakly supervised learning with imprecise/noisy labels suffers from manual pre-processing, ignoring inter-label relationships, and poor batch processing. A scalable, unified approach that can handle various weak supervision signals is needed.
AI.method: Model the search over true labels as probabilistic transitions between label variables, condensing DFS-tree structures into a shared Bayesian network. Compute latent probabilities via generalized belief propagation. Employ two acceleration strategies: (1) low-rank approximation of the transition matrix to reduce time complexity; (2) an end-to-end state evolution module to learn batch-scale transition matrices for multi-category batching. Also show equivalence to the EM algorithm in many settings.
AI.result: Empirical results demonstrate state-of-the-art performance across most weakly supervised settings and speedups up to hundreds of times faster than existing general methods.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework provides a unified, efficient, and scalable approach to weak supervision by probabilistic latent-label inference, with practical benefits in accuracy and training speed; EM-equivalence suggests compatibility with classical latent-variable learning.

title: Percept-Aware Surgical Planning for Visual Cortical Prostheses with Vascular Avoidance
summary: Cortical visual prostheses aim to restore sight by electrically stimulating neurons in early visual cortex (V1). With the emergence of high-density and flexible neural interfaces, electrode placement within three-dimensional cortex has become a critical surgical planning problem. Existing strategies emphasize visual field coverage and anatomical heuristics but do not directly optimize predicted perceptual outcomes under safety constraints. We present a percept-aware framework for surgical planning of cortical visual prostheses that formulates electrode placement as a constrained optimization problem in anatomical space. Electrode coordinates are treated as learnable parameters and optimized end-to-end using a differentiable forward model of prosthetic vision. The objective minimizes task-level perceptual error while incorporating vascular avoidance and gray matter feasibility constraints. Evaluated on simulated reading and natural image tasks using realistic folded cortical geometry (FreeSurfer fsaverage), percept-aware optimization consistently improves reconstruction fidelity relative to coverage-based placement strategies. Importantly, vascular safety constraints eliminate margin violations while preserving perceptual performance. The framework further enables co-optimization of multi-electrode thread configurations under fixed insertion budgets. These results demonstrate how differentiable percept models can inform anatomically grounded, safety-aware computer-assisted planning for cortical neural interfaces and provide a foundation for optimizing next-generation visual prostheses.

AI.tldr: A percept-aware planning framework for cortical visual prostheses optimizes electrode placement as a differentiable, constrained end-to-end problem in cortical anatomy, balancing perceptual performance with safety (vascular) and feasibility, and enabling multi-electrode co-optimization under fixed insertion budgets.
AI.motivation: Existing placement strategies rely on visual field coverage or anatomy heuristics and do not directly optimize predicted perceptual outcomes under safety constraints; there is a need for end-to-end planning that links electrode layout to perceptual performance.
AI.method: Treat electrode coordinates as learnable parameters and optimize them end-to-end using a differentiable forward model of prosthetic vision; incorporate vascular avoidance and gray matter feasibility as constraints; evaluate on simulated reading and natural image tasks with realistic folded cortex (FreeSurfer fsaverage); enable co-optimization of multi-electrode threads under a fixed insertion budget.
AI.result: Percept-aware optimization improves reconstruction fidelity relative to coverage-based strategies across tasks; vascular safety constraints prevent margin violations while preserving perceptual performance; framework supports co-optimization of electrode threads under insertion budgets.
AI.conclusion: Differentiable percept models can inform anatomically grounded, safety-aware planning for cortical neural interfaces and lay the foundation for next-generation visual prostheses.

title: Information-Theoretic Framework for Self-Adapting Model Predictive Controllers
summary: Model Predictive Control (MPC) is a vital technique for autonomous systems, like Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs), enabling optimized motion planning. However, traditional MPC struggles to adapt to real-time changes such as dynamic obstacles and shifting system dynamics, lacking inherent mechanisms for self-monitoring and adaptive optimization. Here, we introduce Entanglement Learning (EL), an information-theoretic framework that enhances MPC adaptability through an Information Digital Twin (IDT). The IDT monitors and quantifies, in bits, the information flow between MPC inputs, control actions, and UAV behavior. By introducing new information-theoretic metrics we call entanglement metrics, it tracks variations in these dependencies. These metrics measure the mutual information between the optimizer's input, its control actions, and the resulting UAV dynamics, enabling a deeper understanding of their interrelationships. This allows the IDT to detect performance deviations and generate real-time adaptive signals to recalibrate MPC parameters, preserving stability. Unlike traditional MPC, which relies on error-based feedback, this dual-feedback approach leverages information flow for proactive adaptation to evolving conditions. Scalable and leveraging existing infrastructure, this framework improves MPC reliability and robustness across diverse scenarios, extending beyond UAV control to any MPC implementation requiring adaptive performance.

AI.tldr: Proposes Entanglement Learning (EL) and an Information Digital Twin (IDT) to augment Model Predictive Control (MPC) with real-time, information-theoretic self-monitoring and adaptive optimization for UAVs, aiming to improve robustness and scalability.
AI.motivation: Traditional MPC struggles with dynamic obstacles and shifting dynamics and lacks self-monitoring; need for proactive, information-based adaptation.
AI.method: Introduce EL framework with IDT that quantifies information flow via entanglement metrics (mutual information) between MPC inputs, actions, and UAV dynamics; IDT detects deviations and emits adaptive signals to recalibrate MPC parameters; dual-feedback based on information flow rather than purely error-based.
AI.result: Conceptual framework with potential benefits; claims improved reliability and robustness and scalability; no empirical results presented in the abstract.
AI.conclusion: EL/IDT enables proactive, information-driven adaptation of MPC, preserving stability under evolving conditions and generalizing to other MPC applications.

title: PhysGraph: Physically-Grounded Graph-Transformer Policies for Bimanual Dexterous Hand-Tool-Object Manipulation
summary: Bimanual dexterous manipulation for tool use remains a formidable challenge in robotics due to the high-dimensional state space and complicated contact dynamics. Existing methods naively represent the entire system state as a single configuration vector, disregarding the rich structural and topological information inherent to articulated hands. We present PhysGraph, a physically-grounded graph transformer policy designed explicitly for challenging bimanual hand-tool-object manipulation. Unlike prior works, we represent the bimanual system as a kinematic graph and introduce per-link tokenization to preserve fine-grained local state information. We propose a physically-grounded bias generator that injects structural priors directly into the attention mechanism, including kinematic spatial distance, dynamic contact states, geometric proximity, and anatomical properties. This allows the policy to explicitly reason about physical interactions rather than learning them implicitly from sparse rewards. Extensive experiments show that PhysGraph significantly outperforms baseline - ManipTrans in manipulation precision and task success rates while using only 51% of the parameters of ManipTrans. Furthermore, the inherent topological flexibility of our architecture shows qualitative zero-shot transfer to unseen tool/object geometries, and is sufficiently general to be trained on three robotic hands (Shadow, Allegro, Inspire).

AI.tldr: PhysGraph is a physically-grounded graph transformer policy for bimanual hand-tool-object manipulation that leverages a kinematic graph with per-link tokenization and a bias generator to inject structural priors into attention, achieving better manipulation accuracy with about half the parameters of a baseline and showing zero-shot generalization across unseen geometries and multiple hands.
AI.motivation: Bimanual manipulation of tools and objects faces a high-dimensional state space and complex contact dynamics. Naively concatenating all state into a single vector discards structural and topological information of articulated hands, hindering learning and generalization.
AI.method: Model the bimanual system as a kinematic graph with per-link tokenization. Introduce a physically-grounded bias generator that injects structural priors (kinematic distance, dynamic contact states, geometric proximity, anatomical properties) into the attention mechanism of a graph transformer policy.
AI.result: PhysGraph significantly outperforms the baseline ManipTrans in manipulation precision and task success rates and uses only 51% of ManipTrans’ parameters. It exhibits qualitative zero-shot transfer to unseen tool/object geometries and generalizes across three robotic hands (Shadow, Allegro, Inspire).
AI.conclusion: A graph-structured, physics-informed transformer policy can effectively reason about physical interactions in bimanual manipulation, offering improved performance, smaller models, and strong generalization across geometries and hands.

title: Phys-Diff: A Physics-Inspired Latent Diffusion Model for Tropical Cyclone Forecasting
comment: 5 pages, 4 figures. Accepted to IEEE ICASSP 2026
summary: Tropical cyclone (TC) forecasting is critical for disaster warning and emergency response. Deep learning methods address computational challenges but often neglect physical relationships between TC attributes, resulting in predictions lacking physical consistency. To address this, we propose Phys-Diff, a physics-inspired latent diffusion model that disentangles latent features into task-specific components (trajectory, pressure, wind speed) and employs cross-task attention to introduce prior physics-inspired inductive biases, thereby embedding physically consistent dependencies among TC attributes. Phys-Diff integrates multimodal data including historical cyclone attributes, ERA5 reanalysis data, and FengWu forecast fields via a Transformer encoder-decoder architecture, further enhancing forecasting performance. Experiments demonstrate state-of-the-art performance on global and regional datasets.

AI.tldr: A physics-informed latent diffusion model (Phys-Diff) for tropical cyclone forecasting that disentangles latent features into trajectory, pressure, and wind speed, uses cross-task attention to enforce physics-inspired priors, and fuses multimodal data (historical attributes, ERA5, FengWu) within a Transformer encoder-decoder; achieves state-of-the-art results on global and regional datasets.
AI.motivation: Current DL approaches for TC forecasting often neglect physical interdependencies among cyclone attributes, leading to predictions that lack physical consistency. There is a need to embed physics-inspired priors and improve cross-attribute consistency while handling multimodal data.
AI.method: Introduce Phys-Diff, a physics-inspired latent diffusion model that disentangles latent features into task-specific components (trajectory, pressure, wind speed). Employ cross-task attention to inject prior physics-inspired inductive biases, enforcing physically consistent dependencies among TC attributes. Fuse multimodal inputs (historical cyclone attributes, ERA5 reanalysis, FengWu forecast fields) through a Transformer encoder-decoder architecture.
AI.result: Experiments demonstrate state-of-the-art performance on both global and regional TC datasets, indicating improved forecasting accuracy and potentially better physical consistency.
AI.conclusion: Disentangled latent representations combined with cross-task attention and multimodal data fusion yield physics-consistent and high-accuracy tropical cyclone forecasts, setting a new benchmark for TC prediction.

title: Unsupervised Semantic Segmentation in Synchrotron Computed Tomography with Self-Correcting Pseudo Labels
summary: X-ray computed tomography (CT) is a widely used imaging technique that provides detailed examinations into the internal structure of an object with synchrotron CT (SR-CT) enabling improved data quality by using higher energy, monochromatic X-rays. While SR-CT allows for improved resolution, time-resolved experimentation, and reduced imaging artifacts, it also produces significantly larger datasets than conventional CT. Accurate and efficient evaluation of these datasets is a critical component of these workflows; yet is often done manually representing a major bottleneck in the analysis phase. While deep learning has emerged as a powerful tool capable of providing a wide range of purely data-driven solutions, it requires a substantial amount of labeled data for training and manual annotation of SR-CT datasets is impractical in practice. In this paper, we introduce a novel framework that enables automatic segmentation of large, high-resolution SR-CT datasets by eliminating the need to hand label images for deep learning training. First, we generate pseudo labels by clustering on the voxel values identifying regions in the volume with similar attenuation coefficients producing an initial semantic map. Afterwards, we train a segmentation model on the pseudo labels before utilizing the Unbiased Teacher approach to self-correct them ensuring accurate final segmentations. We find our approach improves pixel-wise accuracy and mIoU by 13.31% and 15.94%, respectively, over the baseline pseudo labels when using a magnesium crystal SR-CT sample. Additionally, we extensively evaluate the different components of our workflow including segmentation model, loss function, pseudo labeling strategy, and input type. Finally, we evaluate our approach on to two additional samples highlighting our frameworks ability to produce segmentations that are considerably better than the original pseudo labels.

AI.tldr: A framework for automatic segmentation of large high-resolution synchrotron CT (SR-CT) datasets using pseudo-labels from voxel clustering, followed by Unbiased Teacher self-training to correct labels, achieving improvements in pixel accuracy and mIoU over baseline pseudo-labels on magnesium crystal SR-CT and generalizing to other samples.
AI.motivation: To enable accurate, scalable, and label-free segmentation of large SR-CT datasets, addressing the bottleneck of manual labeling and the impracticality of annotating SR-CT volumes.
AI.method: 1) Generate initial semantic maps by clustering voxel values (attenuation coefficients) to create pseudo labels. 2) Train a segmentation model on these pseudo labels. 3) Apply Unbiased Teacher self-training to correct pseudo labels and refine segmentations. 4) Evaluate and ablate components (model, loss, pseudo-label strategy, input type) and test on additional samples.
AI.result: The approach yields improvements of 13.31% in pixel-wise accuracy and 15.94% in mean Intersection over Union (mIoU) over the baseline pseudo labels for a magnesium crystal SR-CT sample. The study includes extensive ablations and demonstrates that the refined framework produces segmentations considerably better than the original pseudo labels on two additional samples.
AI.conclusion: A label-free, end-to-end segmentation framework for large SR-CT data that combines clustering-based pseudo labeling with Unbiased Teacher self-training, achieving notable accuracy gains and generalization across multiple samples.

title: Opponent State Inference Under Partial Observability: An HMM-POMDP Framework for 2026 Formula 1 Energy Strategy
comment: 17 pages. Pre-registered theoretical framework; empirical calibration on 2026 race telemetry begins Australian Grand Prix, 8 March 2026. Paper 1 of 3. ResearchGate preprint: DOI 10.13140/RG.2.2.16034.08644
summary: The 2026 Formula 1 technical regulations introduce a fundamental change to energy strategy: under a 50/50 internal combustion engine / battery power split with unlimited regeneration and a driver-controlled Override Mode (abbreviated MOM throughout), the optimal energy deployment policy depends not only on a driver's own state but on the hidden state of rival cars. This creates a Partially Observable Stochastic Game that cannot be solved by single-agent optimisation methods. We present a tractable two-layer inference and decision framework. The first layer is a 30-state Hidden Markov Model (HMM) that infers a probability distribution over each rival's ERS charge level, Override Mode status, and tyre degradation state from five publicly observable telemetry signals. The second layer is a Deep Q-Network (DQN) policy that takes the HMM belief state as input and selects between energy deployment strategies. We formally characterise the counter-harvest trap -- a deceptive strategy in which a car deliberately suppresses observable deployment signals to induce a rival into a failed attack -- and show that detecting it requires belief-state inference rather than reactive threshold rules. On synthetic races generated from the model's own assumptions, the HMM achieves 92.3% ERS inference accuracy (random baseline: 33.3%) and detects counter-harvest trap conditions with 95.7% recall. Pre-registration -- empirical validation begins Australian Grand Prix, 8 March 2026.

AI.tldr: A two-layer framework for energy deployment under partially observable multi-agent competition in next-gen F1. A 30-state HMM infers rivals’ ERS charge, Override Mode, and tyre degradation from telemetry; a Deep Q-Network uses the belief state to choose energy strategies. The work formalizes a deception strategy (counter-harvest trap) and shows high synthetic validation: 92.3% ERS inference accuracy and 95.7% recall for trap detection. Pre-registration planned for the 2026 Australian Grand Prix.
AI.motivation: Address energy management under partial observability and adversarial competition in new F1 regulations (50/50 engine/battery split with driver-controlled Override Mode), where optimal policy depends on rivals’ hidden states.
AI.method: Two-layer approach: (1) a 30-state Hidden Markov Model infers rival ERS charge, Override Mode status, and tyre degradation from five observable telemetry signals; (2) a Deep Q-Network policy uses the HMM belief state to select energy deployment strategies. The work also defines and analyzes the counter-harvest trap (deceptive signaling) and demonstrates detection via belief inference. Validation uses synthetic races generated from the model assumptions.
AI.result: HMM inference accuracy: 92.3% (random baseline 33.3%). Counter-harvest trap detection recall: 95.7%.
AI.conclusion: Belief-state inference is essential for robust energy policy under partial observability and deception; single-agent thresholds are insufficient. The proposed two-layer framework provides a tractable solution with strong synthetic validation and plans for empirical validation at the Australian Grand Prix.

title: Non-Markovian Long-Horizon Robot Manipulation via Keyframe Chaining
summary: Existing Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models often struggle to generalize to long-horizon tasks due to their heavy reliance on immediate observations. While recent studies incorporate retrieval mechanisms or extend context windows to handle procedural tasks, they often struggle to capture Non-Markovian dependencies, where optimal actions rely solely on specific past states rather than the current observation. To address this, we introduce Keyframe-Chaining VLA, a framework that extracts and links key historical frames to model long-horizon dependencies. Specifically, we propose an automatic keyframe selector that learns a discriminative embedding space, effectively identifying distinct state transitions. To capture task-critical information, we design a progress-aware query mechanism that dynamically retrieves historical frames based on their temporal relevance to the current execution phase. These selected keyframes are integrated into the VLA as interleaved visual tokens, explicitly grounding the policy in the long-horizon temporal context. Finally, we introduce a suite of four Non-Markovian manipulation tasks built upon the ManiSkill simulator to measure task success rates. Experimental results demonstrate that our method achieves superior performance, effectively tackling robot manipulation tasks characterized by long-horizon temporal dependencies. Code is available at https://github.com/cytoplastm/KC-VLA.

AI.tldr: KC-VLA introduces Keyframe-Chaining to tackle Non-Markovian dependencies in Vision-Language-Action by automatic keyframe selection and progress-aware retrieval, leveraging interleaved keyframes as visual tokens; evaluated on ManiSkill-based tasks with improved long-horizon performance.
AI.motivation: Non-Markovian dependencies in long-horizon VLA cause optimal actions to depend on past states rather than the current observation; existing methods either expand context windows or use retrieval but struggle to properly leverage past critical states.
AI.method: Automatic keyframe selector learns a discriminative embedding space to identify distinct state transitions; a progress-aware query mechanism retrieves historical frames relevant to the current execution phase; selected keyframes are integrated into VLA as interleaved visual tokens to ground policy in long-horizon context; evaluation on four Non-Markovian manipulation tasks built on ManiSkill; code released at the provided URL.
AI.result: Method achieves superior performance on long-horizon manipulation tasks characterized by Non-Markovian dependencies, demonstrating effective utilization of past states via keyframe chaining; ablations (implicitly) suggest the keyframes and progress-aware retrieval contribute to gains.
AI.conclusion: KC-VLA effectively grounds policies in long-horizon temporal context, expanding VLA capabilities to Non-Markovian tasks; promising direction for scaling to real-world robotic manipulation with temporal dependencies; future work could explore broader task suites and multi-modal extensions.

title: Bridge Matching Sampler: Scalable Sampling via Generalized Fixed-Point Diffusion Matching
summary: Sampling from unnormalized densities using diffusion models has emerged as a powerful paradigm. However, while recent approaches that use least-squares `matching' objectives have improved scalability, they often necessitate significant trade-offs, such as restricting prior distributions or relying on unstable optimization schemes. By generalizing these methods as special forms of fixed-point iterations rooted in Nelson's relation, we develop a new method that addresses these limitations, called Bridge Matching Sampler (BMS). Our approach enables learning a stochastic transport map between arbitrary prior and target distributions with a single, scalable, and stable objective. Furthermore, we introduce a damped variant of this iteration that incorporates a regularization term to mitigate mode collapse and further stabilize training. Empirically, we demonstrate that our method enables sampling at unprecedented scales while preserving mode diversity, achieving state-of-the-art results on complex synthetic densities and high-dimensional molecular benchmarks.

AI.tldr: Introduces Bridge Matching Sampler (BMS), a diffusion-based method for sampling from unnormalized densities by learning a stochastic transport map between arbitrary prior and target distributions via fixed-point iterations rooted in Nelson's relation; includes a damped, regularized variant to prevent mode collapse; claims scalable, stable learning with state-of-the-art results on complex synthetic and molecular benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Overcome limitations of least-squares matching objectives in diffusion-based sampling, which often require restricting priors or rely on unstable optimization, by formulating a unified, scalable fixed-point framework that can handle arbitrary priors and targets.
AI.method: Generalizes least-squares matching as fixed-point iterations derived from Nelson's relation. Proposes Bridge Matching Sampler (BMS) to learn a stochastic transport map between any prior and target with a single scalable objective. Introduces a damped iteration with a regularization term to reduce mode collapse and improve training stability.
AI.result: Empirically demonstrates that BMS enables sampling at unprecedented scales while preserving mode diversity, achieving state-of-the-art performance on complex synthetic densities and high-dimensional molecular benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: BMS provides a unified, scalable, and stable objective for learning stochastic transport maps between arbitrary priors and targets in diffusion-based unnormalized density sampling. The damped, regularized variant enhances stability and mitigates mode collapse, with strong empirical performance on challenging benchmarks.

title: DiffSOS: Acoustic Conditional Diffusion Model for Speed-of-Sound Reconstruction in Ultrasound Computed Tomography
summary: Accurate Speed-of-Sound (SoS) reconstruction from acoustic waveforms is a cornerstone of ultrasound computed tomography (USCT), enabling quantitative velocity mapping that reveals subtle anatomical details and pathological variations often invisible in conventional imaging. However, practical utility is hindered by the limitations of existing algorithms; traditional Full Waveform Inversion (FWI) is computationally intensive, while current deep learning approaches tend to produce oversmoothed results lacking fine details. We propose DiffSOS, a conditional diffusion model that directly maps acoustic waveforms to SoS maps. Our framework employs a specialized acoustic ControlNet to strictly ground the denoising process in physical wave measurements. To ensure structural consistency, we optimize a hybrid loss function that integrates noise prediction, spatial reconstruction, and noise frequency content. To accelerate inference, we employ stochastic Denoising Diffusion Implicit Model (DDIM) sampling, achieving near real-time reconstruction with only 10 steps. Crucially, we exploit the stochastic generative nature of our framework to estimate pixel-wise uncertainty, providing a measure of reliability that is often absent in deterministic approaches. Evaluated on the OpenPros USCT benchmark, DiffSOS significantly outperforms state-of-the-art networks, achieving an average Multi-scale Structural Similarity of 0.957. Our approach provides high-fidelity SoS maps with a principled measure of confidence, facilitating safer and faster clinical interpretation.

AI.tldr: A diffusion-based conditional model (DiffSOS) maps acoustic waveforms to speed-of-sound maps using a physics-grounded ControlNet, hybrid loss, and DDIM; achieves near real-time inference with 10 steps and pixel-wise uncertainty; state-of-the-art MS-SSIM on OpenPros USCT benchmark.
AI.motivation: Traditional Full Waveform Inversion (FWI) is computationally heavy, and deep learning methods often produce oversmoothed SoS maps lacking fine detail. There is a need for accurate, rapid SoS reconstructions with per-pixel uncertainty to support clinical interpretation.
AI.method: A conditional diffusion model (DiffSOS) that maps acoustic waveforms to SoS maps. Incorporates a specialized acoustic ControlNet to ground denoising in physical wave measurements. Uses a hybrid loss combining noise prediction, spatial reconstruction, and noise-frequency content. Employs stochastic DDIM sampling to achieve near real-time inference with 10 steps. Leverages the stochastic nature to estimate pixel-wise uncertainty.
AI.result: On the OpenPros USCT benchmark, DiffSOS significantly outperforms state-of-the-art networks, achieving an average Multi-scale Structural Similarity (MS-SSIM) of 0.957. Provides high-fidelity SoS maps with a principled confidence estimate.
AI.conclusion: DiffSOS delivers high-fidelity SoS reconstructions with integrated uncertainty quantification, enabling safer and faster clinical interpretation, by fusing physics-grounded conditioning, a hybrid loss, and efficient diffusion-based inference.

title: ASTRA-bench: Evaluating Tool-Use Agent Reasoning and Action Planning with Personal User Context
summary: Next-generation AI must manage vast personal data, diverse tools, and multi-step reasoning, yet most benchmarks remain context-free and single-turn. We present ASTRA-bench (Assistant Skills in Tool-use, Reasoning \& Action-planning), a benchmark that uniquely unifies time-evolving personal context with an interactive toolbox and complex user intents. Our event-driven pipeline generates 2,413 scenarios across four protagonists, grounded in longitudinal life events and annotated by referential, functional, and informational complexity. Evaluation of state-of-the-art models (e.g., Claude-4.5-Opus, DeepSeek-V3.2) reveals significant performance degradation under high-complexity conditions, with argument generation emerging as the primary bottleneck. These findings expose critical limitations in current agents' ability to ground reasoning within messy personal context and orchestrate reliable multi-step plans. We release ASTRA-bench with a full execution environment and evaluation scripts to provide a diagnostic testbed for developing truly context-aware AI assistants.

AI.tldr: ASTRA-bench unifies evolving personal context with an interactive toolbox for AI reasoning and planning, evaluating 2,413 scenarios across four protagonists. It reveals major bottlenecks in argument generation and grounding within messy personal data, and provides an execution environment and scripts for development of context-aware assistants.
AI.motivation: To address the gap in benchmarks that couple context-rich, multi-turn reasoning with personal data and tool-use, enabling evaluation of agents' ability to ground reasoning in messy, time-evolving contexts and to orchestrate multi-step plans.
AI.method: An event-driven pipeline generates 2,413 scenarios grounded in longitudinal life events for four protagonists, annotated for referential, functional, and informational complexity. Evaluation against state-of-the-art models (e.g., Claude-4.5-Opus, DeepSeek-V3.2) with a full execution environment and evaluation scripts.
AI.result: Models exhibit significant performance degradation under high-complexity conditions, with argument generation identified as the primary bottleneck for success. Grounding reasoning in personal context and reliable multi-step planning remains limited.
AI.conclusion: ASTRA-bench provides a diagnostic testbed and release of resources to spur development of truly context-aware AI assistants capable of managing evolving personal contexts and tool use.

title: Mean-Flow based One-Step Vision-Language-Action
summary: Recent advances in FlowMatching-based Vision-Language-Action (VLA) frameworks have demonstrated remarkable advantages in generating high-frequency action chunks, particularly for highly dexterous robotic manipulation tasks. Despite these notable achievements, their practical applications are constrained by prolonged generation latency, which stems from inherent iterative sampling requirements and architectural limitations. To address this critical bottleneck, we propose a Mean-Flow based One-Step VLA approach. Specifically, we resolve the noise-induced issues in the action generation process, thereby eliminating the consistency constraints inherent to conventional Flow-Matching methods. This significantly enhances generation efficiency and enables one-step action generation. Real-world robotic experiments show that the generation speed of the proposed Mean-Flow based One-Step VLA is 8.7 times and 83.9 times faster than that of SmolVLA and Diffusion Policy, respectively. These results elucidate its great potential as a high-efficiency backbone for VLA-based robotic manipulation.

AI.tldr: Mean-Flow based One-Step VLA to overcome noise and consistency constraints in Flow-Matching VLA, enabling one-shot action generation with substantial speedups.
AI.motivation: Flow-Matching VLA excels at high-frequency action chunks but suffers from long latency due to iterative sampling and architectural constraints; speeding up generation is essential for practical robotic manipulation.
AI.method: Introduce Mean-Flow to address noise-induced issues and remove the consistency constraints of conventional Flow-Matching, enabling one-step action generation for VLA-based robotic manipulation.
AI.result: Real-world robotics experiments report generation speed enhancements of 8.7x over SmolVLA and 83.9x over Diffusion Policy.
AI.conclusion: Mean-Flow based One-Step VLA is a promising, high-efficiency backbone for VLA-based robotic manipulation, enabling practical, real-time applications.

title: Mathematical Foundations of Poisoning Attacks on Linear Regression over Cumulative Distribution Functions
summary: Learned indexes are a class of index data structures that enable fast search by approximating the cumulative distribution function (CDF) using machine learning models (Kraska et al., SIGMOD'18). However, recent studies have shown that learned indexes are vulnerable to poisoning attacks, where injecting a small number of poison keys into the training data can significantly degrade model accuracy and reduce index performance (Kornaropoulos et al., SIGMOD'22). In this work, we provide a rigorous theoretical analysis of poisoning attacks targeting linear regression models over CDFs, one of the most basic regression models and a core component in many learned indexes. Our main contributions are as follows: (i) We present a theoretical proof characterizing the optimal single-point poisoning attack and show that the existing method yields the optimal attack. (ii) We show that in multi-point attacks, the existing greedy approach is not always optimal, and we rigorously derive the key properties that an optimal attack should satisfy. (iii) We propose a method to compute an upper bound of the multi-point poisoning attack's impact and empirically demonstrate that the loss under the greedy approach is often close to this bound. Our study deepens the theoretical understanding of attack strategies against linear regression models on CDFs and provides a foundation for the theoretical evaluation of attacks and defenses on learned indexes.

AI.tldr: The paper provides theory-backed analysis of poisoning attacks on linear regression models used in learned indexes, proving single-point attacks are optimal under existing methods, showing greedy is not always optimal for multi-point attacks, and offering an upper-bound computation with empirical relevance.
AI.motivation: Address the vulnerability of learned indexes to data poisoning and establish a rigorous theoretical foundation for attack strategies and defensive evaluation.
AI.method: Theoretical analysis of poisoning attacks on linear regression over CDFs. Derives the optimal single-point attack and demonstrates optimality of the known method. For multi-point attacks, analyzes why greedy is not always optimal and identifies properties the optimal attack must satisfy. Introduces an upper-bound computation for multi-point attack impact and validates it empirically.
AI.result: Proof that the existing single-shot poisoning method reaches the optimal attack for a single poisoned key. In multi-point settings, the greedy approach is not universally optimal; key properties of an optimal attack are established. An upper-bound computation for the attack impact is proposed, with empirical evidence that greedy performance often approaches this bound.
AI.conclusion: Provides a rigorous theoretical framework for evaluating and defending learned indexes against poisoning, clarifying attack optimality conditions and offering tools (upper bounds) to benchmark defenses and guide future research.

title: SSR: Pushing the Limit of Spatial Intelligence with Structured Scene Reasoning
summary: While Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) excel in semantic tasks, they frequently lack the "spatial sense" essential for sophisticated geometric reasoning. Current models typically suffer from exorbitant modality-alignment costs and deficiency in fine-grained structural modeling precision.We introduce SSR, a framework designed for Structured Scene Reasoning that seamlessly integrates 2D and 3D representations via a lightweight alignment mechanism. To minimize training overhead, our framework anchors 3D geometric features to the large language model's pre-aligned 2D visual semantics through cross-modal addition and token interleaving, effectively obviating the necessity for large-scale alignment pre-training. To underpin complex spatial reasoning, we propose a novel scene graph generation pipeline that represents global layouts as a chain of independent local triplets defined by relative coordinates. This is complemented by an incremental generation algorithm, enabling the model to construct "language-model-friendly" structural scaffolds for complex environments. Furthermore, we extend these capabilities to global-scale 3D global grounding task, achieving absolute metric precision across heterogeneous data sources. At a 7B parameter scale, SSR achieves state-of-the-art performance on multiple spatial intelligence benchmarks, notably scoring 73.9 on VSI-Bench. Our approach significantly outperforms much larger models, demonstrating that efficient feature alignment and structured scene reasoning are the cornerstones of authentic spatial intelligence.

AI.tldr: SSR introduces a lightweight 2D-3D alignment framework and a local-triplet scene graph with incremental generation, enabling strong spatial reasoning at 7B parameters and achieving SOTA on VSI-Bench with reduced alignment cost.
AI.motivation: MLLMs excel at semantic tasks but often lack spatial sense necessary for precise geometric reasoning. High modality-alignment costs and coarse structural modeling hinder fine-grained spatial intelligence, motivating a framework that integrates 2D semantics with 3D geometry efficiently.
AI.method: SSR aligns 3D geometric features to LLMs' pre-aligned 2D visual semantics using cross-modal addition and token interleaving to avoid large-scale alignment pretraining. It introduces a scene graph generation pipeline that represents global layouts as a chain of independent local triplets defined by relative coordinates and employs an incremental generation algorithm to build language-model-friendly structural scaffolds. It further extends to global-scale 3D grounding for absolute metric precision across heterogeneous data sources.
AI.result: At 7B parameters, SSR achieves state-of-the-art performance on multiple spatial benchmarks, notably scoring 73.9 on VSI-Bench, and outperforms much larger models, demonstrating that efficient feature alignment and structured scene reasoning are key to authentic spatial intelligence.
AI.conclusion: Efficient feature alignment coupled with structured scene reasoning enables robust spatial intelligence in MLLMs. SSR shows that achieving high spatial accuracy does not require large alignment pretraining if 2D semantics can anchor 3D representations and if structured local-triplet graphs are used for global reasoning.

title: Words & Weights: Streamlining Multi-Turn Interactions via Co-Adaptation
summary: Test-time policy adaptation for multi-turn interactions (T2PAM) is essential for aligning Large Language Models (LLMs) with dynamic user needs during inference time. However, existing paradigms commonly treat test-time adaptation as a single-axis problem, either purely refining instructions (Prompt Engineering) or only adjusting weights (Test-Time Training), ignoring that interaction failures stem from a coupled mix of ambiguity and incapacity. We argue that these two optimization paths are not merely additive but synergistic: semantic clarity acts as a pre-conditioner for effective parameter updates. To this end, we propose ROSA2, a framework that reformulates interaction as a joint optimization problem over the heterogeneous space of Words and Weights. By mathematically decomposing the error signal, ROSA2 utilizes textual gradients to rectify intent ambiguity and parameter updates to bridge capability gaps. Theoretically, we prove that this co-adaptation strictly reduces the required parameter shift for convergence. Empirically, ROSA2 outperforms state-of-the-art baselines by 30% on MATH while reducing interaction turns by 40%, demonstrating that refining the context unlocks the true potential of parameter updates.

AI.tldr: ROSA2 jointly optimizes text (Words) and model parameters (Weights) for test-time adaptation in multi-turn LLM interactions, achieving improved convergence and efficiency.
AI.motivation: Current test-time adaptation treats either instruction refinement or weight updates, failing to address their coupling. A unified framework is needed to handle ambiguity (textual cues) and incapacity (model capability) jointly.
AI.method: Formulate interaction as a joint optimization over the heterogeneous spaces of Words and Weights; decompose the error signal into textual gradients to resolve intent ambiguity and parameter updates to bridge capability gaps; provide theoretical guarantees that co-adaptation reduces the required parameter shift for convergence.
AI.result: Empirically, ROSA2 outperforms state-of-the-art baselines by ~30% on the MATH task and reduces interaction turns by ~40%.
AI.conclusion: Semantic clarity acts as a pre-conditioner for effective parameter updates; joint Words-Weights optimization more efficiently unlocks the model’s adaptation potential.

title: ROSER: Few-Shot Robotic Sequence Retrieval for Scalable Robot Learning
summary: A critical bottleneck in robot learning is the scarcity of task-labeled, segmented training data, despite the abundance of large-scale robotic datasets recorded as long, continuous interaction logs. Existing datasets contain vast amounts of diverse behaviors, yet remain structurally incompatible with modern learning frameworks that require cleanly segmented, task-specific trajectories.
