We address this data utilization crisis by formalizing robotic sequence retrieval: the task of extracting reusable, task-centric segments from unlabeled logs using only a few reference examples. We introduce ROSER, a lightweight few-shot retrieval framework that learns task-agnostic metric spaces over temporal windows, enabling accurate retrieval with as few as 3-5 demonstrations, without any task-specific training required. To validate our approach, we establish comprehensive evaluation protocols and benchmark ROSER against classical alignment methods, learned embeddings, and language model baselines across three large-scale datasets (e.g., LIBERO, DROID, and nuScenes). Our experiments demonstrate that ROSER consistently outperforms all prior methods in both accuracy and efficiency, achieving sub-millisecond per-match inference while maintaining superior distributional alignment. By reframing data curation as few-shot retrieval, ROSER provides a practical pathway to unlock underutilized robotic datasets, fundamentally improving data availability for robot learning.

AI.tldr: ROSER enables few-shot retrieval of task-centric segments from unlabeled robotic logs by learning task-agnostic metric spaces over temporal windows, enabling 3–5 demonstrations with sub-ms inference and no task-specific training.
AI.motivation: Large, heterogeneous robotic datasets are mostly unlabeled and continuous; existing labeled, segmented data is scarce, hindering task-specific learning. A retrieval framework can repurpose long logs into usable, task-focused trajectories.
AI.method: ROSER learns task-agnostic metric spaces over temporal windows to perform few-shot retrieval from unlabeled logs. It requires only 3–5 demonstrations and does not need task-specific training. The evaluation compares against classical alignment, learned embeddings, and language-model baselines across LIBERO, DROID, and nuScenes.
AI.result: ROSER consistently outperforms prior methods in accuracy and efficiency, achieving sub-millisecond per-match inference and better distributional alignment across three large-scale datasets.
AI.conclusion: Framing data curation as few-shot retrieval provides a practical path to unlock underutilized robotic datasets and improves data availability for robot learning.

title: Spectral Condition for $μ$P under Width-Depth Scaling
summary: Generative foundation models are increasingly scaled in both width and depth, posing significant challenges for stable feature learning and reliable hyperparameter (HP) transfer across model sizes. While maximal update parameterization ($μ$P) has provided a principled solution to both problems for width scaling, existing extensions to the joint width-depth scaling regime remain fragmented, architecture- and optimizer-specific, and often rely on technically involved theories. In this work, we develop a simple and unified spectral framework for $μ$P under joint width-depth scaling. Considering residual networks of varying block depths, we first introduce a spectral $μ$P condition that precisely characterizes how the norms of weights and their per-step updates should scale with width and depth, unifying previously disparate $μ$P formulations as special cases. Building on this condition, we then derive a general recipe for implementing $μ$P across a broad class of optimizers by mapping the spectral constraints to concrete HP parameterizations. This approach not only recovers existing $μ$P formulations (e.g., for SGD and AdamW) but also naturally extends to a wider range of optimizers. Finally, experiments on GPT-2 style language models demonstrate that the proposed spectral $μ$P condition preserves stable feature learning and enables robust HP transfer under width-depth scaling.

AI.tldr: A unified spectral maximal update parameterization (μP) for joint width-depth scaling in residual networks, linking weight norms and updates to width and depth; derives an optimizer-agnostic HP mapping; recovers existing μP formulations and extends to more optimizers; validated on GPT-2–style models.
AI.motivation: Stability of feature learning and reliable hyperparameter transfer when scaling both width and depth in large generative foundation models; existing μP extensions are fragmented and theory-heavy.
AI.method: Introduce a spectral μP condition for residual networks with varying block depths; derive a mapping from spectral constraints to concrete hyperparameter parameterizations for a broad class of optimizers; show that known μP setups are special cases and extend to additional optimizers.
AI.result: The spectral μP condition enables stable feature learning and robust hyperparameter transfer under width-depth scaling; empirical validation on GPT-2–style language models supports the approach.
AI.conclusion: A simple, unified spectral framework for μP under joint width-depth scaling; recovers existing formulations and broadens applicability across optimizers; provides practical hyperparameter transfer guidance for large-scale generative models.

title: PointAlign: Feature-Level Alignment Regularization for 3D Vision-Language Models
summary: The development of 3D Vision-Language Models (VLMs), crucial for applications in robotics, autonomous driving, and augmented reality, is severely constrained by the scarcity of paired 3D-text data. Existing methods rely solely on next-token prediction loss, using only language tokens for supervision. This results in inefficient utilization of limited 3D data and leads to a significant degradation and loss of valuable geometric information in intermediate representations. To address these limitations, we propose {\mname}, a novel feature-level alignment regularization method. {\mname} explicitly supervises intermediate point cloud tokens to preserve fine-grained 3D geometric-semantic information throughout the language modeling process. Specifically, we constrain the intermediate point cloud tokens within the LLM to align with visual input tokens via a consistency loss. By training only a lightweight alignment projector and LoRA adapters, {\mname} achieves explicit feature-level supervision with minimal computational overhead, effectively preventing geometric degradation. Extensive experiments on ModelNet40 and Objaverse datasets demonstrate that our method achieves \textbf{2.08} pp improvement on average for classification tasks, with a substantial \textbf{7.50} pp gain on the challenging open-vocabulary Objaverse classification task and \textbf{4.88} pp improvement on 3D object captioning evaluated by Qwen2-72B-Instruct, validating the effectiveness of {\mname}. Code is publicly available at \href{https://github.com/yharoldsu0627/PointAlign}{https://github.com/yharoldsu0627/PointAlign}.

AI.tldr: Proposes PointAlign, a feature-level alignment regularization for 3D Vision-Language Models that preserves fine-grained geometric-semantic information by aligning intermediate point-cloud tokens with visual tokens; uses lightweight adapters; yields notable gains on ModelNet40, Objaverse, and 3D captioning; code released.
AI.motivation: 3D-VLMs are severely data-constrained due to scarcity of paired 3D-text data. Relying solely on next-token (language) supervision underutilizes limited 3D data and causes degradation of geometric information in intermediate representations.
AI.method: Introduce explicit feature-level supervision by aligning intermediate 3D point cloud tokens within the LLM to visual input tokens via a consistency loss. Train only a lightweight alignment projector and LoRA adapters to enable this supervision with minimal computational overhead, preventing geometric degradation.
AI.result: Classification gains: 2.08 percentage points on average (ModelNet40/Objaverse). Open-vocabulary Objaverse classification improves by 7.50 pp. 3D object captioning (Qwen2-72B-Instruct) improves by 4.88 pp. Code available at the referenced repo.
AI.conclusion: The approach provides effective feature-level supervision that preserves geometric information during language modeling, yielding consistent performance gains with modest additional parameters and training cost.

title: HarmonyCell: Automating Single-Cell Perturbation Modeling under Semantic and Distribution Shifts
comment: 18 pages total (8 pages main text + appendix), 6 figures
summary: Single-cell perturbation studies face dual heterogeneity bottlenecks: (i) semantic heterogeneity--identical biological concepts encoded under incompatible metadata schemas across datasets; and (ii) statistical heterogeneity--distribution shifts from biological variation demanding dataset-specific inductive biases. We propose HarmonyCell, an end-to-end agent framework resolving each challenge through a dedicated mechanism: an LLM-driven Semantic Unifier autonomously maps disparate metadata into a canonical interface without manual intervention; and an adaptive Monte Carlo Tree Search engine operates over a hierarchical action space to synthesize architectures with optimal statistical inductive biases for distribution shifts. Evaluated across diverse perturbation tasks under both semantic and distribution shifts, HarmonyCell achieves a 95% valid execution rate on heterogeneous input datasets (versus 0% for general agents) while matching or even exceeding expert-designed baselines in rigorous out-of-distribution evaluations. This dual-track orchestration enables scalable automatic virtual cell modeling without dataset-specific engineering.

AI.tldr: HarmonyCell unifies semantic metadata and adapts architectures to distribution shifts via an LLM-driven unifier and adaptive Monte Carlo Tree Search, achieving high valid execution rates and strong OOD performance in single-cell perturbation tasks.
AI.motivation: Dual heterogeneity bottlenecks hinder cross-dataset integration and robust modeling; need an end-to-end framework to canonicalize metadata and tailor inductive biases.
AI.method: LLM-driven Semantic Unifier maps disparate metadata to a canonical schema; Adaptive Monte Carlo Tree Search over hierarchical action space designs architectures with optimal inductive biases for distribution shifts.
AI.result: On diverse perturbation tasks with semantic and distribution shifts, HarmonyCell achieves 95% valid execution on heterogeneous inputs (vs 0% for general agents) and matches or exceeds expert-designed baselines in out-of-distribution tests.
AI.conclusion: Enables scalable automatic virtual cell modeling without dataset-specific engineering through dual-track orchestration.

title: SFCo-Nav: Efficient Zero-Shot Visual Language Navigation via Collaboration of Slow LLM and Fast Attributed Graph Alignment
comment: Accepted by 2026 IEEE International Conference on Robotics and Automation (ICRA)
summary: Recent advances in large vision-language models (VLMs) and large language models (LLMs) have enabled zero-shot approaches to visual language navigation (VLN), where an agent follows natural language instructions using only ego perception and reasoning. However, existing zero-shot methods typically construct a naive observation graph and perform per-step VLM-LLM inference on it, resulting in high latency and computation costs that limit real-time deployment. To address this, we present SFCo-Nav, an efficient zero-shot VLN framework inspired by the principle of slow-fast cognitive collaboration. SFCo-Nav integrates three key modules: 1) a slow LLM-based planner that produces a strategic chain of subgoals, each linked to an imagined object graph; 2) a fast reactive navigator for real-time object graph construction and subgoal execution; and 3) a lightweight asynchronous slow-fast bridge aligns advanced structured, attributed imagined and perceived graphs to estimate navigation confidence, triggering the slow LLM planner only when necessary. To the best of our knowledge, SFCo-Nav is the first slow-fast collaboration zero-shot VLN system supporting asynchronous LLM triggering according to the internal confidence. Evaluated on the public R2R and REVERIE benchmarks, SFCo-Nav matches or exceeds prior state-of-the-art zero-shot VLN success rates while cutting total token consumption per trajectory by over 50% and running more than 3.5 times faster. Finally, we demonstrate SFCo-Nav on a legged robot in a hotel suite, showcasing its efficiency and practicality in indoor environments.

AI.tldr: SFCo-Nav introduces a slow-fast, asynchronous zero-shot VLN framework that couples a slow LLM planner with a fast reactive navigator via a lightweight bridge to reduce latency and compute while maintaining or surpassing SOTA performance on R2R/REVERIE.
AI.motivation: Current zero-shot VLN methods suffer from high latency and computational cost due to per-step VLM-LLM inference on a naive observation graph, hindering real-time deployment; a confidence-driven, slow-fast architecture can balance planning quality and runtime.
AI.method: Three modules: (1) slow LLM-based planner generating subgoals tied to imagined object graphs; (2) fast reactive navigator performing real-time object-graph construction and subgoal execution; (3) lightweight asynchronous slow-fast bridge aligning imagined and perceived graphs to estimate navigation confidence and trigger planner when needed.
AI.result: On R2R and REVERIE benchmarks, SFCo-Nav matches or exceeds prior SOTA zero-shot VLN success rates; reduces total token consumption per trajectory by over 50%; runs more than 3.5x faster.
AI.conclusion: First slow-fast collaboration zero-shot VLN system with asynchronous LLM triggering; demonstrates practicality on a legged robot in indoor environments; supports real-time deployment in indoor navigation scenarios.

title: Learning to Attack: A Bandit Approach to Adversarial Context Poisoning
summary: Neural contextual bandits are vulnerable to adversarial attacks, where subtle perturbations to rewards, actions, or contexts induce suboptimal decisions. We introduce AdvBandit, a black-box adaptive attack that formulates context poisoning as a continuous-armed bandit problem, enabling the attacker to jointly learn and exploit the victim's evolving policy. The attacker requires no access to the victim's internal parameters, reward function, or gradient information; instead, it constructs a surrogate model using a maximum-entropy inverse reinforcement learning module from observed context-action pairs and optimizes perturbations against this surrogate using projected gradient descent. An upper confidence bound-aware Gaussian process guides arm selection. An attack-budget control mechanism is also introduced to limit detection risk and overhead. We provide theoretical guarantees, including sublinear attacker regret and lower bounds on victim regret linear in the number of attacks. Experiments on three real-world datasets (Yelp, MovieLens, and Disin) against various victim contextual bandits demonstrate that our attack model achieves higher cumulative victim regret than state-of-the-art baselines.

AI.tldr: AdvBandit introduces a black-box, adaptive attack on neural contextual bandits by framing context poisoning as a continuous-armed bandit problem, learning a surrogate via maximum-entropy IRL, and optimizing perturbations with projected gradient descent guided by an UCB-GP, with an attack-budget mechanism. Empirically, it outperforms baselines on Yelp, MovieLens, and Disin in driving victim regret.
AI.motivation: Neural contextual bandits are vulnerable to subtle, hard-to-detect perturbations when the attacker lacks access to internal parameters, rewards, or gradients. There is a need for realistic black-box attacks that can adapt to evolving policies.
AI.method: Form a maximum-entropy inverse reinforcement learning surrogate from observed context-action pairs. Optimize context perturbations against this surrogate using projected gradient descent. Use an upper confidence bound-guided Gaussian process to select perturbation strategies (arms). Introduce an attack-budget control to limit detection and overhead.
AI.result: Theoretical guarantees include sublinear attacker regret and lower bounds on victim regret linear in the number of attacks. Experiments on Yelp, MovieLens, and Disin show higher cumulative victim regret compared with state-of-the-art baselines across various victim contextual bandits.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates a practical, strong black-box attack on neural contextual bandits, highlighting security vulnerabilities and motivating the development of defenses and robust policy designs.

title: DiffTrans: Differentiable Geometry-Materials Decomposition for Reconstructing Transparent Objects
summary: Reconstructing transparent objects from a set of multi-view images is a challenging task due to the complicated nature and indeterminate behavior of light propagation. Typical methods are primarily tailored to specific scenarios, such as objects following a uniform topology, exhibiting ideal transparency and surface specular reflections, or with only surface materials, which substantially constrains their practical applicability in real-world settings. In this work, we propose a differentiable rendering framework for transparent objects, dubbed DiffTrans, which allows for efficient decomposition and reconstruction of the geometry and materials of transparent objects, thereby reconstructing transparent objects accurately in intricate scenes with diverse topology and complex texture. Specifically, we first utilize FlexiCubes with dilation and smoothness regularization as the iso-surface representation to reconstruct an initial geometry efficiently from the multi-view object silhouette. Meanwhile, we employ the environment light radiance field to recover the environment of the scene. Then we devise a recursive differentiable ray tracer to further optimize the geometry, index of refraction and absorption rate simultaneously in a unified and end-to-end manner, leading to high-quality reconstruction of transparent objects in intricate scenes. A prominent advantage of the designed ray tracer is that it can be implemented in CUDA, enabling a significantly reduced computational cost. Extensive experiments on multiple benchmarks demonstrate the superior reconstruction performance of our DiffTrans compared with other methods, especially in intricate scenes involving transparent objects with diverse topology and complex texture. The code is available at https://github.com/lcp29/DiffTrans.

AI.tldr: A differentiable rendering pipeline (DiffTrans) for reconstructing geometry and materials of transparent objects from multi-view images, using FlexiCubes for iso-surface initialization, an environment radiance field, and a CUDA-accelerated differentiable ray tracer for end-to-end optimization.
AI.motivation: Existing methods are restricted to uniform topology, ideal transparency, or surface-only materials, limiting applicability in real-world scenes with diverse topology and textures.
AI.method: Three-stage approach: (1) initialize geometry with FlexiCubes (dilation + smoothness) from silhouettes; (2) estimate scene lighting via environment radiance field; (3) recursively differentiable ray tracing to jointly optimize geometry, refractive index, and absorption; end-to-end; CUDA implementation for speed.
AI.result: Demonstrates superior reconstruction performance on multiple benchmarks, especially in intricate scenes with diverse topology and complex textures; code released.
AI.conclusion: DiffTrans provides accurate, efficient reconstruction of translucent objects in complex scenes by integrating flexible iso-surface geometry, lighting, and differentiable ray tracing within a unified framework.

title: The Observer-Situation Lattice: A Unified Formal Basis for Perspective-Aware Cognition
comment: Extended version of the AAMAS 2026 paper with the same title
summary: Autonomous agents operating in complex, multi-agent environments must reason about what is true from multiple perspectives. Existing approaches often struggle to integrate the reasoning of different agents, at different times, and in different contexts, typically handling these dimensions in separate, specialized modules. This fragmentation leads to a brittle and incomplete reasoning process, particularly when agents must understand the beliefs of others (Theory of Mind). We introduce the Observer-Situation Lattice (OSL), a unified mathematical structure that provides a single, coherent semantic space for perspective-aware cognition. OSL is a finite complete lattice where each element represents a unique observer-situation pair, allowing for a principled and scalable approach to belief management. We present two key algorithms that operate on this lattice: (i) Relativized Belief Propagation, an incremental update algorithm that efficiently propagates new information, and (ii) Minimal Contradiction Decomposition, a graph-based procedure that identifies and isolates contradiction components. We prove the theoretical soundness of our framework and demonstrate its practical utility through a series of benchmarks, including classic Theory of Mind tasks and a comparison with established paradigms such as assumption-based truth maintenance systems. Our results show that OSL provides a computationally efficient and expressive foundation for building robust, perspective-aware autonomous agents.

AI.tldr: A finite, complete lattice called the Observer-Situation Lattice (OSL) unifies perspective-based reasoning for multi-agent systems, enabling efficient Relativized Belief Propagation and Minimal Contradiction Decomposition; validated on Theory of Mind tasks and comparisons to truth maintenance systems.
AI.motivation: To address fragmentation in perspective reasoning and Theory of Mind in autonomous agents that must integrate beliefs across different observers, times, and contexts.
AI.method: Define OSL as a finite complete lattice of observer-situation pairs. Develop Relativized Belief Propagation for incremental information propagation and Minimal Contradiction Decomposition to identify and isolate contradiction components. Prove soundness and compare to assumption-based truth maintenance systems. Evaluate on classic Theory of Mind benchmarks.
AI.result: OSL provides a computationally efficient and expressive foundation for perspective-aware cognition; achieves effective belief management and competitive or superior performance on ToM tasks; demonstrates scalable integration across perspectives.
AI.conclusion: OSL offers a unified semantic substrate for perspective-aware cognition in autonomous agents, enabling robust, scalable reasoning across observers and situations.

authors: Kunlin Xie Chenghao Li Haolan Zhang
title: Multimodal Adversarial Quality Policy for Safe Grasping
summary: Vision-guided robot grasping based on Deep Neural Networks (DNNs) generalizes well but poses safety risks in the Human-Robot Interaction (HRI). Recent works solved it by designing benign adversarial attacks and patches with RGB modality, yet depth-independent characteristics limit their effectiveness on RGBD modality. In this work, we propose the Multimodal Adversarial Quality Policy (MAQP) to realize multimodal safe grasping. Our framework introduces two key components. First, the Heterogeneous Dual-Patch Optimization Scheme (HDPOS) mitigates the distribution discrepancy between RGB and depth modalities in patch generation by adopting modality-specific initialization strategies, employing a Gaussian distribution for depth patches and a uniform distribution for RGB patches, while jointly optimizing both modalities under a unified objective function. Second, the Gradient-Level Modality Balancing Strategy (GLMBS) is designed to resolve the optimization imbalance from RGB and Depth patches in patch shape adaptation by reweighting gradient contributions based on per-channel sensitivity analysis and applying distance-adaptive perturbation bounds. We conduct extensive experiments on the benchmark datasets and a cobot, showing the effectiveness of MAQP.

AI.tldr: MAQP introduces a multimodalattentive adversarial framework for RGB-D grasping safety, using HDPOS for modality-specific patch initialization and joint optimization, and GLMBS to balance RGB/depth gradient contributions, validated on benchmarks and a cobot.
AI.motivation: Safety concerns in vision-guided grasping with deep neural networks, where RGB-only adversarial patches fail to transfer to RGB-D; need a multimodal adversarial defense/robustness approach to ensure safer HRI grasping.
AI.method: MAQP comprises two components: (1) Heterogeneous Dual-Patch Optimization Scheme (HDPOS) to reduce RGB-depth distribution mismatch by initializing depth patches with Gaussian distributions and RGB patches with Uniform distributions, jointly optimizing both under a unified objective; (2) Gradient-Level Modality Balancing Strategy (GLMBS) to mitigate optimization imbalance by reweighting gradient contributions via per-channel sensitivity analysis and applying distance-adaptive perturbation bounds.
AI.result: Extensive experiments conducted on benchmark datasets and a cobot demonstrate effectiveness of MAQP in achieving multimodal safe grasping, indicating improved robustness over RGB-only approaches.
AI.conclusion: MAQP effectively enables multimodal safe grasping by addressing distribution discrepancies between RGB and depth patches and by balancing optimization across modalities, with demonstrated practical validation on real hardware.

title: Enhancing Molecular Property Predictions by Learning from Bond Modelling and Interactions
summary: Molecule representation learning is crucial for understanding and predicting molecular properties. However, conventional atom-centric models, which treat chemical bonds merely as pairwise interactions, often overlook complex bond-level phenomena like resonance and stereoselectivity. This oversight limits their predictive accuracy for nuanced chemical behaviors. To address this limitation, we introduce \textbf{DeMol}, a dual-graph framework whose architecture is motivated by a rigorous information-theoretic analysis demonstrating the information gain from a bond-centric perspective. DeMol explicitly models molecules through parallel atom-centric and bond-centric channels. These are synergistically fused by multi-scale Double-Helix Blocks designed to learn intricate atom-atom, atom-bond, and bond-bond interactions. The framework's geometric consistency is further enhanced by a regularization term based on covalent radii to enforce chemically plausible structures. Comprehensive evaluations on diverse benchmarks, including PCQM4Mv2, OC20 IS2RE, QM9, and MoleculeNet, show that DeMol establishes a new state-of-the-art, outperforming existing methods. These results confirm the superiority of explicitly modelling bond information and interactions, paving the way for more robust and accurate molecular machine learning.

AI.tldr: DeMol introduces a dual-graph, bond-centric framework with parallel atom-centric and bond-centric channels fused by multi-scale Double-Helix Blocks, plus covalent radii regularization; achieves state-of-the-art results across PCQM4Mv2, OC20 IS2RE, QM9, MoleculeNet.
AI.motivation: Conventional atom-centric molecular representations overlook bond-level phenomena (e.g., resonance, stereoselectivity) that influence properties, leading to limited predictive performance; a bond-centric perspective can provide additional information gain and robustness.
AI.method: A dual-graph architecture with parallel atom-centric and bond-centric channels. Multi-scale Double-Helix Blocks enable learning of atom-atom, atom-bond, and bond-bond interactions. A covalent radii-based regularization enforces chemically plausible geometry. Evaluations on PCQM4Mv2, OC20 IS2RE, QM9, and MoleculeNet.
AI.result: Achieves new state-of-the-art performance on multiple benchmarks, outperforming prior atom-centric models and validating the effectiveness of explicit bond modeling.
AI.conclusion: Explicitly modeling bond information via a bond-centric dual-graph with specialized fusion blocks yields superior molecular representations and predictive accuracy; supports broader adoption of bond-aware architectures in molecular ML.

title: Station2Radar: query conditioned gaussian splatting for precipitation field
comment: This paper was accepted to ICLR 2026
summary: Precipitation forecasting relies on heterogeneous data. Weather radar is accurate, but coverage is geographically limited and costly to maintain. Weather stations provide accurate but sparse point measurements, while satellites offer dense, high-resolution coverage without direct rainfall retrieval. To overcome these limitations, we propose Query-Conditioned Gaussian Splatting (QCGS), the first framework to fuse automatic weather station (AWS) observations with satellite imagery for generating precipitation fields. Unlike conventional 2D Gaussian splatting, which renders the entire image plane, QCGS selectively renders only queried precipitation regions, avoiding unnecessary computation in non-precipitating areas while preserving sharp precipitation structures. The framework combines a radar point proposal network that identifies rainfall-support locations with an implicit neural representation (INR) network that predicts Gaussian parameters for each point. QCGS enables efficient, resolution-flexible precipitation field generation in real time. Through extensive evaluation with benchmark precipitation products, QCGS demonstrates over 50\% improvement in RMSE compared to conventional gridded precipitation products, and consistently maintains high performance across multiple spatiotemporal scales.

AI.tldr: A framework (QCGS) that fuses automatic weather station (AWS) data with satellite imagery to generate high-quality precipitation fields. It uses a radar point proposal network to select rainfall-support locations and an implicit neural representation to predict Gaussian parameters, rendering only queried regions for efficiency. It achieves >50% RMSE improvement over conventional gridded products and works across scales in real time.
AI.motivation: Address the limitations of radar-only (geographic coverage), AWS (sparse), and satellites (dense but indirect rainfall retrieval). Need a data-fusion method that provides accurate, scalable, real-time precipitation fields.
AI.method: Propose Query-Conditioned Gaussian Splatting (QCGS): a radar point proposal network identifies rainfall-support locations; an INR predicts Gaussian parameters for each point; selective rendering renders only precipitation regions; integrates AWS observations with satellite imagery; real-time, resolution-flexible.
AI.result: Extensive evaluation on benchmark precipitation products shows >50% RMSE reduction compared to conventional gridded precipitation products; robust performance across multiple spatiotemporal scales.
AI.conclusion: QCGS is the first framework to fuse AWS observations with satellite imagery to generate precipitation fields; selective rendering improves computational efficiency without sacrificing structure; supports real-time, high-resolution, scalable precipitation field generation.

title: MIST-RL: Mutation-based Incremental Suite Testing via Reinforcement Learning
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) often fail to generate correct code on the first attempt, which requires using generated unit tests as verifiers to validate the solutions. Despite the success of recent verification methods, they remain constrained by a "scaling-by-quantity" paradigm. This brute-force approach suffers from a critical limitation: it yields diminishing returns in fault detection while causing severe test redundancy. To address this, we propose MIST-RL (Mutation-based Incremental Suite Testing via Reinforcement Learning), a framework that shifts the focus to "scaling-by-utility". We formulate test generation as a sequential decision process optimized via Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO). Specifically, we introduce a novel incremental mutation reward combined with dynamic penalties, which incentivizes the model to discover new faults while it suppresses functionally equivalent assertions. Experiments on HumanEval+ and MBPP+ demonstrate that MIST-RL outperforms state-of-the-art baselines. It achieves a +28.5% higher mutation score while reducing the number of test cases by 19.3%. Furthermore, we show that these compact, high-utility tests serve as superior verifiers, which improves downstream code reranking accuracy on HumanEval+ by 3.05% over the SOTA baseline with 10 candidate samples. The source code and data are provided in the supplementary material.

AI.tldr: MIST-RL: a mutation-based, utility-focused test generation framework for LLMs, using GRPO to optimize incremental mutations and penalties, yielding higher fault detection with fewer tests and better code reranking.
AI.motivation: LLMs often produce incorrect code; existing verification relies on large numbers of tests, causing diminishing returns and redundancy. A utility-driven approach aims to maximize fault detection per test.
AI.method: Formulates test generation as a sequential decision process optimized with Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO). Introduces an incremental mutation reward and dynamic penalties to promote discovery of new faults while suppressing functionally equivalent assertions.
AI.result: Outperforms baselines: +28.5% higher mutation score and 19.3% fewer test cases. Compact high-utility tests also improve downstream code reranking accuracy by 3.05% on HumanEval+ with 10 candidates.
AI.conclusion: Shifts from scaling-by-quantity to scaling-by-utility, delivering more effective verifiers and better reranking with fewer tests; code and data are provided in supplementary material.

title: Towards Robot Skill Learning and Adaptation with Gaussian Processes
summary: General robot skill adaptation requires expressive representations robust to varying task configurations. While recent learning-based skill adaptation methods refined via Reinforcement Learning (RL), have shown success, existing skill models often lack sufficient representational capacity for anything beyond minor environmental changes. In contrast, Gaussian Process (GP)-based skill modelling provides an expressive representation with useful analytical properties; however, adaptation of GP-based skills remains underexplored. This paper proposes a novel, robust skill adaptation framework that utilises GPs with sparse via-points for compact and expressive modelling. The model considers the trajectory's poses and leverages its first and second analytical derivatives to preserve the skill's kinematic profile. We present three adaptation methods to cater for the variability between initial and observed configurations. Firstly, an optimisation agent that adjusts the path's via-points while preserving the demonstration velocity. Second, a behaviour cloning agent trained to replicate output trajectories from the optimisation agent. Lastly, an RL agent that has learnt to modify via-points whilst maintaining the kinematic profile and enabling online capabilities. Evaluated across three tasks (drawer opening, cube-pushing and bar manipulation) in both simulation and hardware, our proposed methods outperform every benchmark in success rates. Furthermore, the results demonstrate that the GP-based representation enables all three methods to attain high cosine similarity and low velocity magnitude errors, indicating strong preservation of the kinematic profile. Overall, our formulation provides a compact representation capable of adapting to large deviations from a single demonstrated skill.

AI.tldr: GP-based sparse via-point skill representation with three adaptation methods (optimization, BC, RL) preserves kinematic profile and achieves high success across manipulation tasks, outperforming benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Current skill representations struggle to generalize to large task/configuration changes. GP-based Skill modeling offers rich, analyzable representations, but GP-based skill adaptation remains underexplored, requiring compact, robust adaptation frameworks.
AI.method: Represent trajectories with Gaussian Processes using sparse via-points, incorporating poses and their first/second derivatives to preserve the kinematic profile. Three adaptation pipelines are proposed: (1) an optimization agent to adjust via-points while preserving demonstration velocity; (2) a behavior cloning agent trained to reproduce the optimized trajectories; (3) a reinforcement learning agent that modifies via-points online while maintaining the kinematic profile.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on three tasks—drawer opening, cube-pushing, and bar manipulation—performed in both simulation and hardware shows superior success rates compared to benchmarks. All methods achieve high cosine similarity and low velocity magnitude errors, indicating strong preservation of the kinematic profile. The GP-based representation proves compact and capable of adapting to large deviations from a single demonstrated skill.
AI.conclusion: GP-based sparse via-point representations offer robust, compact skill models for large deviations from a single demonstration and support multiple adaptation strategies (optimization, imitation, and RL) that preserve kinodynamics and enable online capabilities.

title: DeepAFL: Deep Analytic Federated Learning
comment: Accepted in the Fourteenth International Conference on Learning Representations (ICLR 2026)
summary: Federated Learning (FL) is a popular distributed learning paradigm to break down data silo. Traditional FL approaches largely rely on gradient-based updates, facing significant issues about heterogeneity, scalability, convergence, and overhead, etc. Recently, some analytic-learning-based work has attempted to handle these issues by eliminating gradient-based updates via analytical (i.e., closed-form) solutions. Despite achieving superior invariance to data heterogeneity, these approaches are fundamentally limited by their single-layer linear model with a frozen pre-trained backbone. As a result, they can only achieve suboptimal performance due to their lack of representation learning capabilities. In this paper, to enable representable analytic models while preserving the ideal invariance to data heterogeneity for FL, we propose our Deep Analytic Federated Learning approach, named DeepAFL. Drawing inspiration from the great success of ResNet in gradient-based learning, we design gradient-free residual blocks in our DeepAFL with analytical solutions. We introduce an efficient layer-wise protocol for training our deep analytic models layer by layer in FL through least squares. Both theoretical analyses and empirical evaluations validate our DeepAFL's superior performance with its dual advantages in heterogeneity invariance and representation learning, outperforming state-of-the-art baselines by up to 5.68%-8.42% across three benchmark datasets.

AI.tldr: DeepAFL introduces gradient-free deep analytic models for federated learning, using layer-wise least-squares to train analytic residual blocks, aiming to combine heterogeneity invariance with representation learning, outperforming baselines on three datasets.
AI.motivation: To overcome limitations of gradient-based FL in presence of data heterogeneity and to enable representation learning, addressing analytic-learning methods' lack of depth and representation capacity.
AI.method: Gradient-free deep analytic architecture with ResNet-like residual blocks; layer-wise, layer-by-layer training via least squares in a federated setting; analytical solutions for each block; FL protocol.
AI.result: Theoretical analyses plus empirical results show improved performance, achieving 5.68%-8.42% relative gains over state-of-the-art baselines across three datasets.
AI.conclusion: DeepAFL demonstrates that deep analytic models with layer-wise least squares can achieve both heterogeneity invariance and representation learning in FL, offering competitive performance and reduced gradient-based overhead; invites further exploration of analytic deep models in distributed learning.

title: An Interpretable Local Editing Model for Counterfactual Medical Image Generation
summary: Counterfactual medical image generation have emerged as a critical tool for enhancing AI-driven systems in medical domain by answering "what-if" questions. However, existing approaches face two fundamental limitations: First, they fail to prevent unintended modifications, resulting collateral changes in demographic attributes when only disease features should be affected. Second, they lack interpretability in their editing process, which significantly limits their utility in real-world medical applications. To address these limitations, we present InstructX2X, a novel interpretable local editing model for counterfactual medical image generation featuring Region-Specific Editing. This approach restricts modifications to specific regions, effectively preventing unintended changes while simultaneously providing a Guidance Map that offers inherently interpretable visual explanations of the editing process. Additionally, we introduce MIMIC-EDIT-INSTRUCTION, a dataset for counterfactual medical image generation derived from expert-verified medical VQA pairs. Through extensive experiments, InstructX2X achieve state-of-the-art performance across all major evaluation metrics. Our model successfully generates high-quality counterfactual chest X-ray images along with interpretable explanations.

AI.tldr: InstructX2X introduces region-specific editing for counterfactual medical image generation with an interpretable Guidance Map and an MIMIC-EDIT-INSTRUCTION dataset, achieving state-of-the-art results on chest X-ray counterfactuals.
AI.motivation: Limit unintended demographic/attribute changes when editing disease features; improve interpretability of the editing process for clinical applicability.
AI.method: An interpretable local editing model (InstructX2X) with Region-Specific Editing to constrain edits to certain regions, plus a Guidance Map for interpretable explanations; dataset MIMIC-EDIT-INSTRUCTION derived from expert-verified medical VQA pairs.
AI.result: State-of-the-art performance on major evaluation metrics; high-quality counterfactual chest X-ray images with interpretable explanations.
AI.conclusion: Proposes a robust, interpretable framework for counterfactual medical image generation with region-specific edits and explainability, backed by a specialized dataset.

title: GraphScout: Empowering Large Language Models with Intrinsic Exploration Ability for Agentic Graph Reasoning
summary: Knowledge graphs provide structured and reliable information for many real-world applications, motivating increasing interest in combining large language models (LLMs) with graph-based retrieval to improve factual grounding. Recent Graph-based Retrieval-Augmented Generation (GraphRAG) methods therefore introduce iterative interaction between LLMs and knowledge graphs to enhance reasoning capability. However, existing approaches typically depend on manually designed guidance and interact with knowledge graphs through a limited set of predefined tools, which substantially constrains graph exploration. To address these limitations, we propose GraphScout, a training-centric agentic graph reasoning framework equipped with more flexible graph exploration tools. GraphScout enables models to autonomously interact with knowledge graphs to synthesize structured training data which are then used to post-train LLMs, thereby internalizing agentic graph reasoning ability without laborious manual annotation or task curation. Extensive experiments across five knowledge-graph domains show that a small model (e.g., Qwen3-4B) augmented with GraphScout outperforms baseline methods built on leading LLMs (e.g., Qwen-Max) by an average of 16.7\% while requiring significantly fewer inference tokens. Moreover, GraphScout exhibits robust cross-domain transfer performance. Our code will be made publicly available~\footnote{https://github.com/Ying-Yuchen/_GraphScout_}.

AI.tldr: GraphScout is a training-centric agentic graph reasoning framework that autonomously interacts with knowledge graphs to generate structured training data and post-train LLMs, achieving stronger, more token-efficient performance and robust cross-domain transfer compared to baselines.
AI.motivation: To address the limitations of GraphRAG-like methods which rely on manual guidance and a limited set of predefined graph tools, by enabling flexible, autonomous graph exploration and training-data synthesis.
AI.method: Introduce GraphScout, a training-centric agent that uses flexible graph exploration tools to interact with knowledge graphs, synthesize structured training data, and post-train LLMs; evaluated with small models (e.g., Qwen3-4B) across five KG domains.
AI.result: GraphScout-augmented models outperform baselines (e.g., Qwen-Max) by an average of 16.7% while using fewer inference tokens; exhibits robust cross-domain transfer across five knowledge-graph domains.
AI.conclusion: GraphScout effectively internalizes agentic graph reasoning through autonomously generated training data and post-training, reducing manual annotation and task curation while delivering superior, scalable performance; code will be released.

title: FATE: Closed-Loop Feasibility-Aware Task Generation with Active Repair for Physically Grounded Robotic Curricula
summary: Recent breakthroughs in generative simulation have harnessed Large Language Models (LLMs) to generate diverse robotic task curricula, yet these open-loop paradigms frequently produce linguistically coherent but physically infeasible goals, stemming from ungrounded task specifications or misaligned objective formulations. To address this critical limitation, we propose FATE (Feasibility-Aware Task gEneration), a closed-loop, self-correcting framework that reimagines task generation as an iterative validation-and-refinement process. Unlike conventional methods that decouple generation and verification into discrete stages, FATE embeds a generalist embodied agent directly into the generation loop to proactively guarantee the physical groundedness of the resulting curriculum. FATE instantiates a sequential auditing pipeline: it first validates static scene attributes (e.g., object affordances, layout compatibility) and subsequently verifies execution feasibility via simulated embodied interaction. Critical to its performance, upon detecting an infeasible task, FATE deploys an active repair module that autonomously adapts scene configurations or policy specifications, converting unworkable proposals into physically valid task instances. Extensive experiments validate that FATE generates semantically diverse, physically grounded task curricula while achieving a substantial reduction in execution failure rates relative to state-of-the-art generative baselines.

AI.tldr: FATE offers a closed-loop, feasibility-aware task generation for robotics by integrating an embodied agent into generation, performing iterative validation and repair to ensure physically grounded, diverse curricula and lower failure rates.
AI.motivation: Address the gap in generative robotic curricula where open-loop generation yields coherent but physically infeasible goals due to ungrounded specifications and objective misalignment.
AI.method: Sequential auditing: validate static scene attributes (affordances, layouts) then verify execution feasibility via simulated embodied interaction; an active repair module adjusts scenes or policy specs when infeasible, enabling turnkey generation of feasible tasks.
AI.result: Significantly more semantically diverse and physically grounded curricula with substantially reduced execution failure rates compared to state-of-the-art generative baselines.
AI.conclusion: Closed-loop generation with embodied validation and adaptive repair improves task feasibility and diversity, highlighting the value of integrated generation-verification pipelines for robotic curriculum design.

title: Unlearning Evaluation through Subset Statistical Independence
comment: 21 pages, 6 figures, to appear at ICLR 2026
summary: Evaluating machine unlearning remains challenging, as existing methods typically require retraining reference models or performing membership inference attacks, both of which rely on prior access to training configuration or supervision labels, making them impractical in realistic scenarios. Motivated by the fact that most unlearning algorithms remove a small, random subset of the training data, we propose a subset-level evaluation framework based on statistical independence. Specifically, we design a tailored use of the Hilbert-Schmidt Independence Criterion to assess whether the model outputs on a given subset exhibit statistical dependence, without requiring model retraining or auxiliary classifiers. Our method provides a simple, standalone evaluation procedure that aligns with unlearning workflows. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our approach reliably distinguishes in-training from out-of-training subsets and clearly differentiates unlearning effectiveness, even when existing evaluations fall short.

AI.tldr: HSIC-based subset-level evaluation for unlearning that avoids retraining or extra classifiers.
AI.motivation: Existing unlearning evaluations rely on retraining reference models or membership-inference attacks, which require training configs or labels and are impractical. Since unlearning often removes a small random subset, a practical, subset-level, training-free metric is desirable.
AI.method: Apply a tailored use of the Hilbert-Schmidt Independence Criterion to test whether model outputs on a given subset exhibit statistical dependence, without retraining or auxiliary classifiers, yielding a standalone evaluation aligned with unlearning workflows.
AI.result: Empirically, the HSIC-based statistic reliably separates in-training versus out-of-training subsets and differentiates unlearning effectiveness, even where prior evaluations fail.
AI.conclusion: The approach provides a simple, standalone, retraining-free evaluation procedure that integrates with unlearning pipelines and offers reliable assessment of unlearning effectiveness.

title: Taxonomy-Aware Representation Alignment for Hierarchical Visual Recognition with Large Multimodal Models
comment: Published as a conference paper at CVPR 2026
summary: A high-performing, general-purpose visual understanding model should map visual inputs to a taxonomic tree of labels, identify novel categories beyond the training set for which few or no publicly available images exist. Large Multimodal Models (LMMs) have achieved remarkable progress in fine-grained visual recognition (FGVR) for known categories. However, they remain limited in hierarchical visual recognition (HVR) that aims at predicting consistent label paths from coarse to fine categories, especially for novel categories. To tackle these challenges, we propose Taxonomy-Aware Representation Alignment (TARA), a simple yet effective strategy to inject taxonomic knowledge into LMMs. TARA leverages representations from biology foundation models (BFMs) that encode rich biological relationships through hierarchical contrastive learning. By aligning the intermediate representations of visual features with those of BFMs, LMMs are encouraged to extract discriminative visual cues well structured in the taxonomy tree. Additionally, we align the representations of the first answer token with the ground-truth label, flexibly bridging the gap between contextualized visual features and categories of varying granularity according to user intent. Experiments demonstrate that TARA consistently enhances LMMs' hierarchical consistency and leaf node accuracy, enabling reliable recognition of both known and novel categories within complex biological taxonomies. Code is available at https://github.com/PKU-ICST-MIPL/TARA_CVPR2026.

AI.tldr: TARA injects taxonomic knowledge into large multimodal models by aligning visual representations with biology foundation models and aligning the first answer token to the ground-truth label, improving hierarchical visual recognition for known and novel categories.
AI.motivation: High-performing visual models must predict label paths across taxonomic trees and recognize novel categories with limited data. While large multimodal models excel at fine-grained recognition of known categories, they struggle with hierarchical visual recognition (HVR) and novel taxa, especially within complex taxonomies.
AI.method: TARA uses representations from biology foundation models (BFMs) and hierarchical contrastive learning to align intermediate visual features with BFMs. It also aligns the first answer token with the ground-truth label to bridge contextualized features to varying label granularity based on user intent.
AI.result: Empirically, TARA improves hierarchical consistency and leaf-node accuracy of LMMs, enabling reliable recognition of both known and novel categories within complex biological taxonomies.
AI.conclusion: TARA provides a simple and effective way to inject taxonomic knowledge into LMMs, enhancing hierarchical recognition; code is available at the provided GitHub link.

title: Securing the Floor and Raising the Ceiling: A Merging-based Paradigm for Multi-modal Search Agents
summary: Recent advances in Vision-Language Models (VLMs) have motivated the development of multi-modal search agents that can actively invoke external search tools and integrate retrieved evidence through multi-step reasoning. While promising, existing approaches typically rely on large-scale supervised trajectories or expensive reinforcement learning (RL), leading to high training cost, instability, and a severe cold-start problem for standard VLMs. We propose a training-free paradigm to empower VLMs with autonomous search capabilities via cross-modal model merging. By fusing a text-based search agent with a base VLM, we show that multi-modal search capabilities can be effectively composed without any additional multi-modal training data. To mitigate parameter interference during cross-modal integration, we introduce Optimal Brain Merging (OBM), a saliency-aware merging algorithm that identifies task-critical parameters based on their impact on model loss using only a small set of calibration samples. Extensive experiments on search-intensive benchmarks (e.g., InfoSeek, MMSearch) reveal that: (1) Model merging secures a reasonable performance floor as a zero-shot agent, with OBM achieving superior search rates; (2) OBM significantly raises the performance ceiling as a warm-start strategy, achieving faster convergence and higher peak accuracy than standard VLM initialization.

AI.tldr: A training-free cross-modal model-merge approach (Optimal Brain Merging) enables VLMs to perform autonomous multi-modal search by merging a text-based search agent with a base VLM, providing zero-shot floor and warm-start gains.
AI.motivation: Reduce training cost, instability, and cold-start issues in multi-modal search by composing existing components without additional multi-modal training data.
AI.method: Cross-modal model merging using Optimal Brain Merging (OBM): a saliency-aware parameter merging algorithm that identifies task-critical parameters based on their impact on model loss using calibration samples to minimize interference between the text search agent and the base VLM.
AI.result: On search-intensive benchmarks (InfoSeek, MMSearch), merging yields a reasonable zero-shot floor; OBM achieves superior search rates. As a warm-start, OBM speeds convergence and attains higher peak accuracy than standard VLM initialization.
AI.conclusion: A training-free paradigm can effectively compose multi-modal search capabilities; OBM mitigates parameter interference, enabling faster deployment and better performance without multi-modal training data.

title: (hu)Man vs. Machine: In the Future of Motorsport, can Autonomous Vehicles Compete?
summary: Motorsport has historically driven technological innovation in the automotive industry. Autonomous racing provides a proving ground to push the limits of performance of autonomous vehicle (AV) systems. In principle, AVs could be at least as fast, if not faster, than humans. However, human driven racing provides broader audience appeal thus far, and is more strategically challenging. Both provide opportunities to push each other even further technologically, yet competitions remain separate. This paper evaluates whether the future of motorsport could encompass joint competition between humans and AVs. Analysis of the current state of the art, as well as recent competition outcomes, shows that while technical performance has reached comparable levels, there are substantial challenges in racecraft, strategy and safety that need to be overcome. Outstanding issues involved in mixed human-AI racing, ranging from an initial assessment of critical factors such as system-level latencies, to effective planning and risk guarantees are explored. The crucial non-technical aspect of audience engagement and appeal regarding the changing character of motorsport is addressed. In the wider context of motorsport and AVs, this work outlines a proposed agenda for future research to 'keep pushing the possible', in the true spirit of motorsport.

AI.tldr: The abstract reviews the potential of joint human-AI racing, finding parity in performance but significant non-technical hurdles (racecraft, strategy, safety) and emphasizes audience appeal; it outlines a research agenda to advance mixed racing.
AI.motivation: To explore whether mixed human-AI motorsport is feasible and to identify the technical and non-technical barriers that must be overcome for joint competition.
AI.method: Literature and competition outcome analysis of current AV and human racing states, identifying critical factors (e.g., system latencies) and outlining planning and safety considerations.
AI.result: Technical performance between humans and AVs is comparable, but substantial challenges remain in racecraft, strategic planning, and safety in mixed racing scenarios; non-technical factors like audience engagement are crucial.
AI.conclusion: Proposes a research agenda to advance mixed racing and keep pushing the boundaries of motorsport, addressing both technical and audience-related aspects.

title: Energy-Efficient Information Representation in MNIST Classification Using Biologically Inspired Learning
comment: 14 pages, accepted for publication in proceedings of the 10th BWHPC Symposium
summary: Efficient representation learning is essential for optimal information storage and classification. However, it is frequently overlooked in artificial neural networks (ANNs). This neglect results in networks that can become overparameterized by factors of up to 13, increasing redundancy and energy consumption. As the demand for large language models (LLMs) and their scale increase, these issues are further highlighted, raising significant ethical and environmental concerns. We analyze our previously developed biologically inspired learning rule using information-theoretic concepts, evaluating its efficiency on the MNIST classification task. The proposed rule, which emulates the brain's structural plasticity, naturally prevents overparameterization by optimizing synaptic usage and retaining only the essential number of synapses. Furthermore, it outperforms backpropagation (BP) in terms of efficiency and storage capacity. It also eliminates the need for pre-optimization of network architecture, enhances adaptability, and reflects the brain's ability to reserve 'space' for new memories. This approach advances scalable and energy-efficient AI and provides a promising framework for developing brain-inspired models that optimize resource allocation and adaptability.

AI.tldr: A biologically inspired structural plasticity rule reduces overparameterization and improves efficiency versus backpropagation on MNIST, without architecture pre-optimization, suggesting scalable, energy-efficient brain-inspired AI for adaptive resource allocation.
AI.motivation: Address inefficiencies and overparameterization in artificial neural networks, especially as model sizes grow (e.g., LLMs), to reduce redundancy, energy consumption, and environmental impact; align learning with brain-like structural plasticity.
AI.method: Apply/investigate a pre-existing biologically inspired learning rule that implements structural plasticity, using information-theoretic metrics to assess efficiency, tested on MNIST classification to evaluate synaptic usage optimization and network sparsity.
AI.result: The rule naturally minimizes unnecessary synapses, preserves essential connections, and outperforms backpropagation in efficiency and storage capacity on MNIST. It also obviates the need for pre-optimization of network architecture and enhances adaptability.
AI.conclusion: The approach contributes to scalable, energy-efficient AI and offers a framework for brain-inspired models that optimize resource allocation and adaptability without extensive architectural pre-tuning.

title: TAP-SLF: Parameter-Efficient Adaptation of Vision Foundation Models for Multi-Task Ultrasound Image Analysis
comment: 4 pages, 2 figures, 4 tables; Submitted to ISBI FMC UIA 2026; Our code is publicly available at https://github.com/huiwanHW/Florence-2-adaptation
summary: Executing multiple tasks simultaneously in medical image analysis, including segmentation, classification, detection, and regression, often introduces significant challenges regarding model generalizability and the optimization of shared feature representations. While Vision Foundation Models (VFMs) provide powerful general representations, full fine-tuning on limited medical data is prone to overfitting and incurs high computational costs. Moreover, existing parameter-efficient fine-tuning approaches typically adopt task-agnostic adaptation protocols, overlooking both task-specific mechanisms and the varying sensitivity of model layers during fine-tuning. In this work, we propose Task-Aware Prompting and Selective Layer Fine-Tuning (TAP-SLF), a unified framework for multi-task ultrasound image analysis. TAP-SLF incorporates task-aware soft prompts to encode task-specific priors into the input token sequence and applies LoRA to selected specific top layers of the encoder. This strategy updates only a small fraction of the VFM parameters while keeping the pre-trained backbone frozen. By combining task-aware prompts with selective high-layer fine-tuning, TAP-SLF enables efficient VFM adaptation to diverse medical tasks within a shared backbone. Results on the FMC_UIA 2026 Challenge test set, where TAP-SLF wins fifth place, combined with evaluations on the officially released training dataset using an 8:2 train-test split, demonstrate that task-aware prompting and selective layer tuning are effective strategies for efficient VFM adaptation.

AI.tldr: TAP-SLF is a task-aware prompting and selective high-layer fine-tuning framework for multi-task ultrasound analysis with vision foundation models, achieving efficient adaptation by freezing the backbone and updating a small subset of parameters (LoRA on top encoder layers); it ranks fifth on FMC_UIA 2026 and shows strong performance on an 8:2 train-test split of the official data.
AI.motivation: Medical image analysis tasks (segmentation, classification, detection, regression) require generalizable shared representations. Vision foundation models risk overfitting and high compute when fully fine-tuned on limited medical data; existing adapters are mostly task-agnostic and ignore task-specific mechanisms and layer sensitivity.
AI.method: Introduce task-aware soft prompts to encode task priors into input tokens. Apply LoRA-based fine-tuning to selected top layers of the encoder while keeping the pretrained backbone frozen. This yields a parameter-efficient adaptation capable of handling multiple tasks within a shared backbone.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on the FMC_UIA 2026 Challenge test set shows TAP-SLF achieving fifth place. Additional evaluation on the officially released training data with an 8:2 train-test split supports the effectiveness of task-aware prompting and selective layer tuning for efficient VFM adaptation.
AI.conclusion: Task-aware prompts combined with selective high-layer fine-tuning provide an effective and efficient strategy for adapting vision foundation models to diverse, multi-task medical imaging tasks without full fine-tuning, reducing overfitting and computational burden while maintaining performance.

title: SciDER: Scientific Data-centric End-to-end Researcher
summary: Automated scientific discovery with large language models is transforming the research lifecycle from ideation to experimentation, yet existing agents struggle to autonomously process raw data collected from scientific experiments. We introduce SciDER, a data-centric end-to-end system that automates the research lifecycle. Unlike traditional frameworks, our specialized agents collaboratively parse and analyze raw scientific data, generate hypotheses and experimental designs grounded in specific data characteristics, and write and execute corresponding code. Evaluation on three benchmarks shows SciDER excels in specialized data-driven scientific discovery and outperforms general-purpose agents and state-of-the-art models through its self-evolving memory and critic-led feedback loop. Distributed as a modular Python package, we also provide easy-to-use PyPI packages with a lightweight web interface to accelerate autonomous, data-driven research and aim to be accessible to all researchers and developers.

AI.tldr: SciDER is a data-centric end-to-end system that uses collaborative specialized agents to parse raw experimental data, generate hypotheses and experimental designs grounded in data characteristics, and write/execute code, outperforming baselines on three benchmarks.
AI.motivation: To bridge the gap where autonomous agents struggle with raw scientific data and end-to-end experimentation, enabling scalable, data-aware autonomous discovery.
AI.method: Introduce SciDER as a modular Python package with specialized agents that parse raw data, generate data-grounded hypotheses and experiments, and automatically write and execute code, supported by a self-evolving memory and critic-led feedback loop; evaluated on three benchmarks; distributed as PyPI packages with a lightweight web interface.
AI.result: SciDER excels in data-driven discovery and outperforms general-purpose agents and state-of-the-art models on the evaluated benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: SciDER accelerates autonomous, data-driven scientific research and is accessible to researchers via a modular package and web interface.

title: KERV: Kinematic-Rectified Speculative Decoding for Embodied VLA Models
comment: This paper has been accepted by DAC 2026
summary: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models build a token-domain robot control paradigm, yet suffer from low speed. Speculative Decoding (SD) is an optimization strategy that can boost inference speed. Two key issues emerge when integrating VLA and SD: first, SD relies on re-inference to address token errors, which is computationally expensive; second, to mitigate token errors, the acceptance threshold in SD requires careful adjustment. Existing works fail to address the above two issues effectively. Meanwhile, as the bridge between AI and the physical world, existing embodied intelligence has overlooked the application of robotic kinematics. To address these issues, we innovatively combine token-domain VLA models with kinematic-domain prediction for SD, proposing a kinematic-rectified SD framework named KERV. We employ a kinematics-based Kalman Filter to predict actions and compensate for SD errors, avoiding costly re-inference. Moreover, we design a kinematics-based adjustment strategy to dynamically rectify the acceptance threshold, addressing the difficulty of threshold determination. Experimental results across diverse tasks and environments demonstrate that KERV achieves 27%~37% acceleration with nearly no Success Rate loss.

AI.tldr: KERV integrates token-domain VLA with kinematic-domain prediction to accelerate speculative decoding, using a Kalman Filter to predict actions and adjust the SD acceptance threshold, yielding 27–37% speedups with minimal success-rate loss.
AI.motivation: Address two limitations of speculative decoding in vision-language-action systems: (1) costly re-inference to correct token errors, and (2) the need for carefully tuned acceptance thresholds; and to bridge AI with physical robots by incorporating kinematic reasoning.
AI.method: Propose kinematic-rectified speculative decoding (KERV). Employ a Kalman Filter in the action/kinematics space to forecast actions and compensate SD errors, eliminating the need for re-inference. Develop a dynamic, kinematics-based threshold adjustment strategy to adapt the acceptance threshold during inference.
AI.result: Empirical results show 27%–37% acceleration with nearly no loss in success rate across diverse tasks and environments.
AI.conclusion: KERV effectively speeds up VLA inference without compromising performance and demonstrates the value of integrating robotic kinematics with speculative decoding for embodied AI.

title: Learning to Explore: Policy-Guided Outlier Synthesis for Graph Out-of-Distribution Detection
summary: Detecting out-of-distribution (OOD) graphs is crucial for ensuring the safety and reliability of Graph Neural Networks. In unsupervised graph-level OOD detection, models are typically trained using only in-distribution (ID) data, resulting in incomplete feature space characterization and weak decision boundaries. Although synthesizing outliers offers a promising solution, existing approaches rely on fixed, non-adaptive sampling heuristics (e.g., distance- or density-based), limiting their ability to explore informative OOD regions. We propose a Policy-Guided Outlier Synthesis (PGOS) framework that replaces static heuristics with a learned exploration strategy. Specifically, PGOS trains a reinforcement learning agent to navigate low-density regions in a structured latent space and sample representations that most effectively refine the OOD decision boundary. These representations are then decoded into high-quality pseudo-OOD graphs to improve detector robustness. Extensive experiments demonstrate that PGOS achieves state-of-the-art performance on multiple graph OOD and anomaly detection benchmarks.

AI.tldr: Reinforcement-learning-guided outlier synthesis for graph OOD detection; learns to explore latent space to generate high-quality pseudo-OOD graphs, achieving state-of-the-art performance.
AI.motivation: Unsupervised graph-level OOD detection suffers from incomplete feature characterization and weak decision boundaries; fixed sampling heuristics fail to cover informative OOD regions.
AI.method: Train an RL agent to navigate low-density regions in a structured latent space, sample informative representations, decode them into pseudo-OOD graphs to refine the OOD boundary.
AI.result: Experiments demonstrate state-of-the-art performance on multiple graph OOD and anomaly detection benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Policy-guided synthesis improves detector robustness by adaptive exploration, yielding better OOD discrimination across benchmarks.

title: Self-Correction Inside the Model: Leveraging Layer Attention to Mitigate Hallucinations in Large Vision Language Models
summary: Although Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) have made substantial progress, hallucination, where generated text is not grounded in the visual input, remains a challenge. As LVLMs become stronger, previously reported hallucination patterns, such as linguistic bias and overthinking phenomenon, become far less consistent, making the corresponding mitigation techniques substantially less effective. In this paper, we introduce an Internal self-Correction mechanism utilizing Layer Attention (ICLA) that operates directly on hidden states during generation. Each layer selectively retrieves information from all preceding layers through a diagonal cross-layer attention mechanism, enabling self-refinement without any external correction signals. With introducing and training only 0.2M and 0.1M additional parameters on LLaVA1.5-7B and Qwen2.5-VL-7B, \ours consistently improves visual grounding across multiple hallucination benchmarks, demonstrating its effectiveness for more advanced LVLMs.

AI.tldr: ICLA introduces a lightweight internal self-correction mechanism via diagonal cross-layer attention to refine LVLMs during generation, improving grounding with minimal parameter overhead.
AI.motivation: Hallucination in LVLMs persists and becomes harder to mitigate as models strengthen; external correction signals are limited, so a self-contained mechanism is desirable.
AI.method: Add Layer Attention-based diagonal cross-layer attention across hidden states, allowing each layer to selectively retrieve information from preceding layers to refine its outputs without external signals. Training adds 0.2M parameters on LLaVA1.5-7B and 0.1M on Qwen2.5-VL-7B.
AI.result: Consistent improvement in visual grounding across multiple hallucination benchmarks; demonstrates effectiveness for more advanced LVLMs.
AI.conclusion: Internal self-correction via layer attention is a lightweight, effective approach to reduce hallucinations in LVLMs, achieving grounding improvements with minimal parameter overhead.

title: Decoding Answers Before Chain-of-Thought: Evidence from Pre-CoT Probes and Activation Steering
summary: As chain-of-thought (CoT) has become central to scaling reasoning capabilities in large language models (LLMs), it has also emerged as a promising tool for interpretability, suggesting the opportunity to understand model decisions through verbalized reasoning. However, the utility of CoT toward interpretability depends upon its faithfulness -- whether the model's stated reasoning reflects the underlying decision process. We provide mechanistic evidence that instruction-tuned models often determine their answer before generating CoT. Training linear probes on residual stream activations at the last token before CoT, we can predict the model's final answer with 0.9 AUC on most tasks. We find that these directions are not only predictive, but also causal: steering activations along the probe direction flips model answers in over 50% of cases, significantly exceeding orthogonal baselines. When steering induces incorrect answers, we observe two distinct failure modes: non-entailment (stating correct premises but drawing unsupported conclusions) and confabulation (fabricating false premises). While post-hoc reasoning may be instrumentally useful when the model has a correct pre-CoT belief, these failure modes suggest it can result in undesirable behaviors when reasoning from a false belief.

AI.tldr: CoT faithfulness in instruction-tuned LLMs is questionable: models often decide the answer before generating CoT; residual-stream probes show predictive, causal signals; steering along these directions can flip answers (>50%); failure modes include non-entailment and confabulation; interpretability via CoT is thus fragile and context-dependent.
AI.motivation: Investigate whether chain-of-thought explanations faithfully reflect the model's decision process and whether CoT can be reliably used as an interpretability tool in instruction-tuned LLMs.
AI.method: Train linear probes on residual stream activations at the last token before CoT to predict the model's final answer (achieving ~0.9 AUC). Test causal influence by steering activations along the probe direction to flip answers. Compare against orthogonal baselines. Characterize failure modes when steering yields incorrect answers.
AI.result: The residual activations before CoT encode the final answer with high predictive power (AUC ~0.9). Steering along the probe direction can causally flip the model's answer in over half of cases. When steering produces incorrect answers, two failure modes emerge: non-entailment (correct premises with unsupported conclusions) and confabulation (fabricated premises).
AI.conclusion: Post-hoc reasoning can be instrumentally helpful if the model holds a correct pre-CoT belief, but it can lead to undesirable behavior when the model reasons from a false belief. CoT-based interpretability is not universally faithful and should be approached with caution.

title: Learning Thermal-Aware Locomotion Policies for an Electrically-Actuated Quadruped Robot
summary: Electrically-actuated quadrupedal robots possess high mobility on complex terrains, but their motors tend to accumulate heat under high-torque cyclic loads, potentially triggering overheat protection and limiting long-duration tasks. This work proposes a thermal-aware control method that incorporates motor temperatures into reinforcement learning locomotion policies and introduces thermal-constraint rewards to prevent temperature exceedance. Real-world experiments on the Unitree A1 demonstrate that, under a fixed 3 kg payload, the baseline policy triggers overheat protection and stops within approximately 7 minutes, whereas the proposed method can operate continuously for over 27 minutes without thermal interruptions while maintaining comparable command-tracking performance, thereby enhancing sustainable operational capability.

AI.tldr: Thermal-aware reinforcement learning controller extends continuous operation of quadrupedal robots by incorporating motor temperature constraints, achieving >27 min operation vs ~7 min baseline under 3 kg payload.
AI.motivation: High motor heating under high-torque cyclic locomotion causes overheating and overheat protections; longer endurance is needed for sustained tasks.
AI.method: Train RL locomotion policies with motor temperature as state and introduce thermal-constraint rewards to penalize overheating; validate on real Unitree A1 with a fixed 3 kg payload.
AI.result: Baseline policy triggers overheating and stops within ~7 minutes; proposed method runs >27 minutes without thermal interruptions and maintains comparable command-tracking performance.
AI.conclusion: Thermal-aware RL with temperature-constrained rewards mitigates overheating, enabling substantially longer sustainable operation without sacrificing control quality.

title: Multi-Domain Riemannian Graph Gluing for Building Graph Foundation Models
summary: Multi-domain graph pre-training integrates knowledge from diverse domains to enhance performance in the target domains, which is crucial for building graph foundation models. Despite initial success, existing solutions often fall short of answering a fundamental question: how is knowledge integrated or transferred across domains? This theoretical limitation motivates us to rethink the consistency and transferability between model pre-training and domain adaptation. In this paper, we propose a fresh Riemannian geometry perspective, whose core idea is to merge any graph dataset into a unified, smooth Riemannian manifold, enabling a systematic understanding of knowledge integration and transfer. To achieve this, our key contribution is the theoretical establishment of neural manifold gluing, which first characterizes local geometry using an adaptive orthogonal frame and then "glues" the local pieces together into a coherent whole.
