Building on this theory, we present the GraphGlue framework, which supports batched pre-training with EMA prototyping and provides a transferability measure based on geometric consistence. Extensive experiments demonstrate its superior performance across diverse graph domains. Moreover, we empirically validated GraphGlue's geometric scaling law, showing that larger quantities of datasets improve model transferability by producing a smoother manifold. Codes are available at https://github.com/RiemannGraph/GraphGlue.

AI.tldr: GraphGlue introduces a Riemannian-geometry-based framework to unify multi-domain graph pre-training via neural manifold gluing, EMA prototyping, and a transferability metric, validated by experiments and a geometric scaling law.
AI.motivation: Existing multi-domain graph pre-training lacks a theoretical understanding of how knowledge transfers across domains; a unified geometric framework is needed to analyze and improve transferability.
AI.method: Formulates graphs as local patches on a single smooth Riemannian manifold using adaptive orthogonal frames; develops neural manifold gluing to stitch local geometries into a coherent whole; presents GraphGlue with batched pre-training, EMA prototyping, and a geometric transferability measure based on geometric consistency.
AI.result: Empirical results show superior performance across diverse graph domains; the geometric scaling law indicates that more datasets yield a smoother manifold and better transferability.
AI.conclusion: Provides a principled, scalable framework for knowledge integration in multi-domain graph pre-training, introduces transferable geometric metrics, and supplies open-source code for replication.

title: Mamba-CAD: State Space Model For 3D Computer-Aided Design Generative Modeling
summary: Computer-Aided Design (CAD) generative modeling has a strong and long-term application in the industry. Recently, the parametric CAD sequence as the design logic of an object has been widely mined by sequence models. However, the industrial CAD models, especially in component objects, are fine-grained and complex, requiring a longer parametric CAD sequence to define. To address the problem, we introduce Mamba-CAD, a self-supervised generative modeling for complex CAD models in the industry, which can model on a longer parametric CAD sequence. Specifically, we first design an encoder-decoder framework based on a Mamba architecture and pair it with a CAD reconstruction task for pre-training to model the latent representation of CAD models; and then we utilize the learned representation to guide a generative adversarial network to produce the fake representation of CAD models, which would be finally recovered into parametric CAD sequences via the decoder of MambaCAD. To train Mamba-CAD, we further create a new dataset consisting of 77,078 CAD models with longer parametric CAD sequences. Comprehensive experiments are conducted to demonstrate the effectiveness of our model under various evaluation metrics, especially in the generation length of valid parametric CAD sequences. The code and dataset can be achieved from https://github.com/Sunny-Hack/Code-for-Mamba-CAD-AAAI-2025-.

AI.tldr: Mamba-CAD: a self-supervised, long-sequence generative model for complex industrial CAD models, using a Mamba encoder-decoder with CAD reconstruction pre-training, followed by GAN-based latent generation to produce parametric CAD sequences; new dataset with 77,078 models; demonstrates improved generation length of valid sequences.
AI.motivation: Industrial CAD models—especially component-level objects—are fine-grained and require long parametric CAD sequences. Existing models struggle to capture long-range dependencies and generate valid long sequences.
AI.method: An encoder-decoder framework based on a Mamba architecture is pre-trained with a CAD reconstruction task to learn a latent representation of CAD models. A GAN guides the generation of fake latent representations, which are decoded to parametric CAD sequences via the Mamba-CAD decoder. A new dataset of 77,078 CAD models with longer parametric sequences is created for training and evaluation.
AI.result: Comprehensive experiments show the approach is effective, particularly in generating longer valid parametric CAD sequences, outperforming baselines on relevant metrics.
AI.conclusion: Mamba-CAD enables modeling and generation of longer, more complex parametric CAD sequences in industrial settings, with publicly available code and dataset to support future research.

title: Scaling Tasks, Not Samples: Mastering Humanoid Control through Multi-Task Model-Based Reinforcement Learning
summary: Developing generalist robots capable of mastering diverse skills remains a central challenge in embodied AI. While recent progress emphasizes scaling model parameters and offline datasets, such approaches are limited in robotics, where learning requires active interaction. We argue that effective online learning should scale the \emph{number of tasks}, rather than the number of samples per task. This regime reveals a structural advantage of model-based reinforcement learning (MBRL). Because physical dynamics are invariant across tasks, a shared world model can aggregate multi-task experience to learn robust, task-agnostic representations. In contrast, model-free methods suffer from gradient interference when tasks demand conflicting actions in similar states. Task diversity therefore acts as a regularizer for MBRL, improving dynamics learning and sample efficiency. We instantiate this idea with \textbf{EfficientZero-Multitask (EZ-M)}, a sample-efficient multi-task MBRL algorithm for online learning. Evaluated on \textbf{HumanoidBench}, a challenging whole-body control benchmark, EZ-M achieves state-of-the-art performance with significantly higher sample efficiency than strong baselines, without extreme parameter scaling. These results establish task scaling as a critical axis for scalable robotic learning. The project website is available \href{https://yewr.github.io/ez_m/}{here}.

AI.tldr: Proposes EfficientZero-Multitask (EZ-M), a multitask model-based RL algorithm for online robotic learning, showing that scaling the number of tasks yields better sample efficiency than scaling per-task samples; achieves SOTA on HumanoidBench without heavy hyperparameter scaling.
AI.motivation: Online robotic learning is sample-inefficient; because dynamics are task-invariant, a shared world model can aggregate experience across tasks; task diversity acts as a regularizer improving dynamics learning and avoiding gradient interference typical in model-free methods.
AI.method: Introduce EfficientZero-Multitask (EZ-M), a multi-task extension of EfficientZero, training a shared world model on diverse tasks to support control policies across tasks in real-time (online) on HumanoidBench; emphasis on task scaling rather than per-task data scaling.
AI.result: EZ-M achieves state-of-the-art performance on HumanoidBench with notably higher sample efficiency than strong baselines, without extreme parameter scaling.
AI.conclusion: Task scaling is a critical axis for scalable robotic learning; multi-task MBRL with a shared world model can generalize dynamics across tasks and improve sample efficiency.

title: B$^2$F-Map: Crowd-sourced Mapping with Bayesian B-spline Fusion
summary: Crowd-sourced mapping offers a scalable alternative to creating maps using traditional survey vehicles. Yet, existing methods either rely on prior high-definition (HD) maps or neglect uncertainties in the map fusion. In this work, we present a complete pipeline for HD map generation using production vehicles equipped only with a monocular camera, consumer-grade GNSS, and IMU. Our approach includes on-cloud localization using lightweight standard-definition maps, on-vehicle mapping via an extended object trajectory (EOT) Poisson multi-Bernoulli (PMB) filter with Gibbs sampling, and on-cloud multi-drive optimization and Bayesian map fusion. We represent the lane lines using B-splines, where each B-spline is parameterized by a sequence of Gaussian distributed control points, and propose a novel Bayesian fusion framework for B-spline trajectories with differing density representation, enabling principled handling of uncertainties. We evaluate our proposed approach, B$^2$F-Map, on large-scale real-world datasets collected across diverse driving conditions and demonstrate that our method is able to produce geometrically consistent lane-level maps.

AI.tldr: B2F-Map is a scalable, uncertainty-aware pipeline for HD lane-level map generation using only a monocular camera, consumer GNSS/IMU, with on-vehicle EOT PMB filtering and on-cloud Bayesian fusion of B-spline lane trajectories, validated on diverse real-world data to produce geometrically consistent maps.
AI.motivation: Crowd-sourced mapping often relies on prior HD maps or ignores uncertainties in map fusion. A low-cost, end-to-end pipeline with probabilistic fusion is needed to generate reliable HD lane-level maps at scale.
AI.method: On-cloud localization using lightweight SD maps; on-vehicle mapping via an extended object trajectory Poisson multi-Bernoulli (EOT PMB) filter with Gibbs sampling; on-cloud multi-drive optimization and Bayesian map fusion. Lane lines are modeled as B-splines with Gaussian-distributed control points, and a Bayesian fusion framework handles differing density representations across trajectories.
AI.result: Evaluated on large-scale real-world datasets across diverse driving conditions, demonstrating geometrically consistent lane-level maps and principled handling of uncertainties through Bayesian fusion.
AI.conclusion: The proposed B2F-Map pipeline enables scalable crowd-sourced HD map generation with low-cost sensors and principled uncertainty management, producing reliable lane-level maps suitable for real-world deployment.

title: IDER: IDempotent Experience Replay for Reliable Continual Learning
summary: Catastrophic forgetting, the tendency of neural networks to forget previously learned knowledge when learning new tasks, has been a major challenge in continual learning (CL). To tackle this challenge, CL methods have been proposed and shown to reduce forgetting. Furthermore, CL models deployed in mission-critical settings can benefit from uncertainty awareness by calibrating their predictions to reliably assess their confidences. However, existing uncertainty-aware continual learning methods suffer from high computational overhead and incompatibility with mainstream replay methods. To address this, we propose idempotent experience replay (IDER), a novel approach based on the idempotent property where repeated function applications yield the same output. Specifically, we first adapt the training loss to make model idempotent on current data streams. In addition, we introduce an idempotence distillation loss. We feed the output of the current model back into the old checkpoint and then minimize the distance between this reprocessed output and the original output of the current model. This yields a simple and effective new baseline for building reliable continual learners, which can be seamlessly integrated with other CL approaches. Extensive experiments on different CL benchmarks demonstrate that IDER consistently improves prediction reliability while simultaneously boosting accuracy and reducing forgetting. Our results suggest the potential of idempotence as a promising principle for deploying efficient and trustworthy continual learning systems in real-world applications.Our code is available at https://github.com/YutingLi0606/Idempotent-Continual-Learning.

AI.tldr: Proposes Idempotent Experience Replay (IDER) for uncertainty-aware continual learning; enforces model idempotence on data streams via adapted loss and idempotence distillation, improving reliability, accuracy, and reducing forgetting; compatible with replay methods; code released.
AI.motivation: Address catastrophic forgetting in continual learning while maintaining reliable uncertainty estimates; existing uncertainty-aware CL methods incur high computational overhead and clash with replay-based regimes.
AI.method: 1) Adapt training loss to enforce idempotence on current data streams. 2) Introduce idempotence distillation: feed the current model output back into the old checkpoint and minimize the distance between this reprocessed output and the original current-model output.
AI.result: Empirically improves prediction reliability, accuracy, and reduces forgetting across multiple CL benchmarks; demonstrates strong compatibility with replay-based CL and lower overhead.
AI.conclusion: Idempotence is a promising principle for efficient, trustworthy continual learning. IDEr provides a simple, effective baseline that can be integrated with other CL approaches; code available for replication.

title: SesaHand: Enhancing 3D Hand Reconstruction via Controllable Generation with Semantic and Structural Alignment
summary: Recent studies on 3D hand reconstruction have demonstrated the effectiveness of synthetic training data to improve estimation performance. However, most methods rely on game engines to synthesize hand images, which often lack diversity in textures and environments, and fail to include crucial components like arms or interacting objects. Generative models are promising alternatives to generate diverse hand images, but still suffer from misalignment issues. In this paper, we present SesaHand, which enhances controllable hand image generation from both semantic and structural alignment perspectives for 3D hand reconstruction. Specifically, for semantic alignment, we propose a pipeline with Chain-of-Thought inference to extract human behavior semantics from image captions generated by the Vision-Language Model. This semantics suppresses human-irrelevant environmental details and ensures sufficient human-centric contexts for hand image generation. For structural alignment, we introduce hierarchical structural fusion to integrate structural information with different granularity for feature refinement to better align the hand and the overall human body in generated images. We further propose a hand structure attention enhancement method to efficiently enhance the model's attention on hand regions. Experiments demonstrate that our method not only outperforms prior work in generation performance but also improves 3D hand reconstruction with the generated hand images.

AI.tldr: Proposes SesaHand to enable controllable 3D hand image generation and improved reconstruction by integrating semantic alignment from VLM captions (via Chain-of-Thought) and multi-granularity structural alignment with hand-focused attention.
AI.motivation: Synthetic hand data from game engines is limited in diversity and components; generative models still misalign with hands and environment; a framework combining semantic control and structural fusion is needed to improve both generation quality and downstream 3D hand reconstruction.
AI.method: Semantic alignment via Chain-of-Thought inference on captions generated by a Vision-Language Model to extract human behavior semantics and suppress non-human environmental details; structural alignment via hierarchical structural fusion to fuse multi-granularity structural information for feature refinement; hand structure attention enhancement to focus model on hand regions.
AI.result: Empirical results show SesaHand outperforms prior methods in generative quality and improves downstream 3D hand reconstruction when training with the generated data.
AI.conclusion: Semantic and structural alignment together yield better controllable hand image generation and more accurate 3D hand reconstruction, indicating a viable approach for augmenting synthetic data pipelines.

title: ProtRLSearch: A Multi-Round Multimodal Protein Search Agent with Large Language Models Trained via Reinforcement Learning
summary: Protein analysis tasks arising in healthcare settings often require accurate reasoning under protein sequence constraints, involving tasks such as functional interpretation of disease-related variants, protein-level analysis for clinical research, and similar scenarios. To address such tasks, search agents are introduced to search protein-related information, providing support for disease-related variant analysis and protein function reasoning in protein-centric inference. However, such search agents are mostly limited to single-round, text-only modality search, which prevents the protein sequence modality from being incorporated as a multimodal input into the search decision-making process. Meanwhile, their reliance on reinforcement learning (RL) supervision that focuses solely on the final answer results in a lack of search process constraints, making deviations in keyword selection and reasoning directions difficult to identify and correct in a timely manner. To address these limitations, we propose ProtRLSearch, a multi-round protein search agent trained with multi-dimensional reward based RL, which jointly leverages protein sequence and text as multimodal inputs during real-time search to produce high quality reports. To evaluate the ability of models to integrate protein sequence information and text-based multimodal inputs in realistic protein query settings, we construct ProtMCQs, a benchmark of 3,000 multiple choice questions (MCQs) organized into three difficulty levels. The benchmark evaluates protein query tasks that range from sequence constrained reasoning about protein function and phenotype changes to comprehensive protein reasoning that integrates multi-dimensional sequence features with signal pathways and regulatory networks.

AI.tldr: Introduces ProtRLSearch, a multi-round protein search agent using multimodal inputs (protein sequence and text) with multi-dimensional reward RL, plus ProtMCQs benchmark to evaluate sequence-integrated protein reasoning.
AI.motivation: Current protein-search agents are text-only, single-round, RL-specified on final answers, limiting integration of protein sequence data and hindering timely error-correction during search.
AI.method: Proposes ProtRLSearch that uses multimodal inputs (protein sequences + text) and multi-dimensional reward RL to guide iterative search; evaluates via ProtMCQs with 3,000 MCQs across three difficulty levels, probing sequence-constrained reasoning and integration with pathways/regulatory networks.
AI.result: Not explicitly stated in abstract; primarily introduces method and benchmark, with indications of improved future capability.
AI.conclusion: The work aims to advance protein-centric reasoning under sequence constraints by enabling multimodal, multi-round search and providing a benchmark to assess such capabilities.

title: TacMamba: A Tactile History Compression Adapter Bridging Fast Reflexes and Slow VLA Reasoning
summary: In visually ambiguous manipulation such as detecting button click tactile feedback is often the sole source of ground truth. However, fusing tactile data poses a significant challenge due to a spatiotemporal mismatch: tactile perception requires high-frequency processing with long-horizon memory (System 1), whereas visual policies operate at low control frequencies (System 2). Existing architectures struggle to bridge this gap: Transformers are computationally prohibitive for high-frequency loops (>100Hz), while LSTMs suffer from forgetting over extended interaction histories. In this paper, we introduce TacMamba, a hierarchical architecture that aligns high-bandwidth tactile reflexes with low-frequency visual planning. Our approach comprises three core contributions: (1) a custom high-frequency tactile interface designed for flexible integration; (2) a Mamba-based Tactile History Compressor that encodes continuous force history into a compact state with O(1) inference latency (0.45 ms), enabling plug-and-play fusion with VLA models without joint pre-training and (3) a Tactile-Guided Dual-Stage Training strategy that leverages temporal discrimination for self-supervised representation learning and phase-uniform sampling to mitigate data sparsity. Experiments on discrete counting and implicit state switching demonstrate that TacMamba achieves 100% success rates, significantly outperforming the visual-only pi_0.5 baseline, while strictly satisfying hard real-time constraints.

AI.tldr: TacMamba introduces a hierarchical tactile-visual fusion system that aligns high-frequency tactile reflexes with low-frequency visual planning using a Mamba-based tactile history compressor and a tactile-guided dual-stage training strategy, achieving 100% success in tested tasks and meeting hard real-time constraints.
AI.motivation: Bridge the gap between high-bandwidth tactile data (need for fast, short-horizon reflexes) and low-bandwidth visual planning (long-horizon control). Existing transformers are too slow for high-rate loops and LSTMs forget long histories, hindering tactile-visual fusion in manipulation tasks where tactile feedback is essential for ground truth.
AI.method: Threefold approach: (1) a custom high-frequency tactile interface for flexible integration; (2) a Mamba-based Tactile History Compressor encoding continuous force history into a compact state with O(1) inference latency (0.45 ms), enabling plug-and-play fusion with Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models without joint pre-training; (3) Tactile-Guided Dual-Stage Training: self-supervised temporal discrimination for representation learning and phase-uniform sampling to mitigate data sparsity.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on discrete counting and implicit state switching shows TacMamba yields 100% success, surpassing a visual-only pi_0.5 baseline, while satisfying hard real-time constraints.
AI.conclusion: TacMamba successfully bridges high-bandwidth tactile cues with low-frequency planning, enabling robust, real-time tactile-visual fusion in manipulation tasks; the approach is computationally efficient and likely generalizes to other tactile-vision tasks.

title: Adapt Data to Model: Adaptive Transformation Optimization for Domain-shared Time Series Foundation Models
comment: Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2026
summary: Large time series models (LTMs) have emerged as powerful tools for universal forecasting, yet they often struggle with the inherent diversity and nonstationarity of real-world time series data, leading to an unsatisfactory trade-off between forecasting accuracy and generalization. Rather than continually finetuning new LTM instances for each domain, we propose a data-centric framework, time-series adaptive transformation optimization (TATO), that enables a single frozen pre-trained LTM to adapt to diverse downstream domains through an optimally configured transformation pipeline. Specifically, TATO constructs three representative types of transformations, including context slicing, scale normalization, and outlier correction, to help LTMs better align with target domain characteristics. To ensure robustness, we incorporate carefully selected time series augmentations and a two-stage ranking mechanism that filters out pipelines underperforming on specific metrics. Extensive experiments on state-of-the-art LTMs and widely used datasets demonstrate that TATO consistently and significantly improves domain-adaptive forecasting performance, achieving a maximum reduction in MSE of 65.4\% and an average reduction of 13.6\%. Moreover, TATO is highly efficient, typically completing optimization in under 2 minutes, making it practical for real-world deployment. The source code is available at https://github.com/thulab/TATO.

AI.tldr: A data-centric approach (TATO) uses configurable transformation pipelines to adapt a frozen pre-trained LTM to diverse domains, achieving large MSE reductions quickly.
AI.motivation: LTMs struggle with diversity and nonstationarity across real-world time series; per-domain fine-tuning is costly and may hurt generalization; a data-centric, pipeline-based adaptation can align inputs to each domain without changing model weights.
AI.method: Builds three transformation types—context slicing, scale normalization, and outlier correction—plus time-series augmentations; employs a two-stage ranking to discard underperforming pipelines; optimizes a single, frozen LTM with selected pipelines for target domains.
AI.result: Experiments on state-of-the-art LTMs and common datasets show up to 65.4% MSE reduction and an average 13.6% reduction; optimization typically completes in under 2 minutes; code released at GitHub.
AI.conclusion: TATO demonstrates that domain adaptation for LTMs can be effectively achieved via carefully designed input transformations and robust ranking, enabling efficient, accurate forecasting across diverse domains without per-domain fine-tuning.

title: Improved Adversarial Diffusion Compression for Real-World Video Super-Resolution
summary: While many diffusion models have achieved impressive results in real-world video super-resolution (Real-VSR) by generating rich and realistic details, their reliance on multi-step sampling leads to slow inference. One-step networks like SeedVR2, DOVE, and DLoRAL alleviate this through condensing generation into one single step, yet they remain heavy, with billions of parameters and multi-second latency. Recent adversarial diffusion compression (ADC) offers a promising path via pruning and distilling these models into a compact AdcSR network, but directly applying it to Real-VSR fails to balance spatial details and temporal consistency due to its lack of temporal awareness and the limitations of standard adversarial learning. To address these challenges, we propose an improved ADC method for Real-VSR. Our approach distills a large diffusion Transformer (DiT) teacher DOVE equipped with 3D spatio-temporal attentions, into a pruned 2D Stable Diffusion (SD)-based AdcSR backbone, augmented with lightweight 1D temporal convolutions, achieving significantly higher efficiency. In addition, we introduce a dual-head adversarial distillation scheme, in which discriminators in both pixel and feature domains explicitly disentangle the discrimination of details and consistency into two heads, enabling both objectives to be effectively optimized without sacrificing one for the other. Experiments demonstrate that the resulting compressed AdcVSR model reduces complexity by 95% in parameters and achieves an 8$\times$ acceleration over its DiT teacher DOVE, while maintaining competitive video quality and efficiency.

AI.tldr: A compressed AdcVSR method distills a 3D-temporal diffusion transformer (DiT) teacher (DOVE) into a pruned 2D Stable Diffusion-based AdcSR backbone with lightweight 1D temporal convolutions, using dual-head adversarial distillation to balance detail fidelity and temporal consistency; achieves 95% parameter reduction and 8x speedup over the DiT teacher while maintaining competitive video quality.
AI.motivation: Real-VSR models are slow due to multi-step sampling. Although one-step networks exist, they remain large and latency-prone. AdcSR compression via ADC offers a path to compact models, but applying it to Real-VSR requires temporal awareness and improved adversarial learning to balance spatial details with temporal coherence.
AI.method: Distill a large diffusion Transformer (DiT) teacher with 3D spatio-temporal attention (DOVE) into a pruned 2D Stable Diffusion-based AdcSR backbone, augmented with lightweight 1D temporal convolutions. Introduce a dual-head adversarial distillation scheme with discriminators operating in pixel and feature domains to disentangle detail vs. consistency optimization, enabling both objectives to be optimized without tradeoffs.
AI.result: The resulting AdcVSR model reduces parameter count by about 95% and achieves roughly an 8× acceleration compared to the DiT teacher, while maintaining competitive video quality and efficiency.
AI.conclusion: An improved ADC framework for Real-VSR that preserves temporal coherence and spatial detail in a compact model, made possible by 3D-to-2D distillation with temporal convolutions and dual-head adversarial distillation that separately optimizes detail and temporal consistency.

title: Harmonizing Dense and Sparse Signals in Multi-turn RL: Dual-Horizon Credit Assignment for Industrial Sales Agents
summary: Optimizing large language models for industrial sales requires balancing long-term commercial objectives (e.g., conversion rate) with immediate linguistic constraints such as fluency and compliance. Conventional reinforcement learning often merges these heterogeneous goals into a single reward, causing high-magnitude session-level rewards to overwhelm subtler turn-level signals, which leads to unstable training or reward hacking. To address this issue, we propose Dual-Horizon Credit Assignment (DuCA), a framework that disentangles optimization across time scales. Its core, Horizon-Independent Advantage Normalization (HIAN), separately normalizes advantages from turn-level and session-level rewards before fusion, ensuring balanced gradient contributions from both immediate and long-term objectives to the policy update. Extensive experiments with a high-fidelity user simulator show DuCA outperforms the state-of-the-art GRPO baseline, achieving a 6.82% relative improvement in conversion rate, reducing inter-sentence repetition by 82.28%, and lowering identity detection rate by 27.35%, indicating a substantial improvement for an industrial sales scenario that effectively balances the dual demands of strategic performance and naturalistic language generation.

AI.tldr: DuCA introduces Horizon-Independent Advantage Normalization to disentangle and balance turn-level and session-level rewards for RL-based industrial LLM optimization, improving both long-term business metrics and short-term generation quality.
AI.motivation: Conventional RL blends heterogeneous objectives across time scales into a single reward, causing dominance by high-magnitude session-level signals and resulting in unstable training and reward hacking.
AI.method: DuCA (Dual-Horizon Credit Assignment) separates optimization across time scales and applies Horizon-Independent Advantage Normalization (HIAN) to normalize turn-level and session-level advantages independently before fusion, ensuring balanced gradient contributions during policy updates.
AI.result: In high-fidelity user-simulator experiments, DuCA beats the GRPO baseline with a 6.82% relative improvement in conversion rate, an 82.28% reduction in inter-sentence repetition, and a 27.35% decrease in identity detection rate, indicating improved conversion performance and more naturalistic language generation.
AI.conclusion: DuCA provides a stable, time-scale-aware RL framework for industrial LLMs, effectively balancing strategic objectives with linguistic constraints; the approach shows promise for deployment in real-world sales assistants and may generalize to other multi-time-scale RL tasks.

title: A Safety-Aware Shared Autonomy Framework with BarrierIK Using Control Barrier Functions
summary: Shared autonomy blends operator intent with autonomous assistance. In cluttered environments, linear blending can produce unsafe commands even when each source is individually collision-free. Many existing approaches model obstacle avoidance through potentials or cost terms, which only enforce safety as a soft constraint. In contrast, safety-critical control requires hard guarantees. We investigate the use of control barrier functions (CBFs) at the inverse kinematics (IK) layer of shared autonomy, targeting post-blend safety while preserving task performance. Our approach is evaluated in simulation on representative cluttered environments and in a VR teleoperation study comparing pure teleoperation with shared autonomy. Across conditions, employing CBFs at the IK layer reduces violation time and increases minimum clearance while maintaining task performance. In the user study, participants reported higher perceived safety and trust, lower interference, and an overall preference for shared autonomy with our safety filter. Additional materials available at https://berkguler.github.io/barrierik.

AI.tldr: Hard safety for shared autonomy via control barrier functions (CBFs) at the inverse kinematics layer; achieves post-blend safety in cluttered environments while preserving task performance; reduces violations and increases clearance; users report higher safety/trust and prefer safety-filtered shared autonomy.
AI.motivation: Soft safety methods (potentials/cost terms) enforce safety as a soft constraint; safety-critical contexts require hard guarantees. The abstract targets hard guarantees in shared autonomy by relocating safety enforcement to the IK layer via CBFs, aiming to preserve task performance while ensuring collision avoidance.
AI.method: Integrate a CBF-based safety filter at the IK layer of a shared autonomy pipeline, operating on post-blend commands to ensure collision-free, dynamically feasible trajectories. Evaluation includes simulation in cluttered environments and a VR teleoperation study comparing pure teleoperation to shared autonomy with a safety filter. Metrics include violation time, minimum clearance, task performance, and subjective safety/trust measures.
AI.result: CBF-based IK safety filter reduces collision-violation time and increases minimum clearance without sacrificing task performance. In the VR user study, participants reported higher perceived safety and trust, lower interference, and favored shared autonomy with the safety filter.
AI.conclusion: CBFs implemented at the IK layer provide hard safety guarantees in shared autonomy while maintaining task performance, demonstrated in simulation and VR teleoperation. The approach is effective for improving safety and user perception, suggesting broader applicability of CBF-based safety filters in shared autonomy.

title: Retrodictive Forecasting: A Proof-of-Concept for Exploiting Temporal Asymmetry in Time Series Prediction
comment: 27 pages, 13 figures, 5 tables, Code available at https://github.com/cdamour/retrodictive-forecasting (Zenodo: https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.18803446)
summary: We propose a retrodictive forecasting paradigm for time series: instead of predicting the future from the past, we identify the future that best explains the observed present via inverse MAP optimization over a Conditional Variational Autoencoder (CVAE). This conditioning is a statistical modeling choice for Bayesian inversion; it does not assert that future events cause past observations. The approach is theoretically grounded in an information-theoretic arrow-of-time measure: the symmetrized Kullback-Leibler divergence between forward and time-reversed trajectory ensembles provides both the conceptual rationale and an operational GO/NO-GO diagnostic for applicability. We implement the paradigm as MAP inference over an inverse CVAE with a learned RealNVP normalizing-flow prior and evaluate it on six time series cases: four synthetic processes with controlled temporal asymmetry and two ERA5 reanalysis datasets (wind speed and solar irradiance). The work makes four contributions: (i) a formal retrodictive inference formulation; (ii) an inverse CVAE architecture; (iii) a model-free irreversibility diagnostic; and (iv) a falsifiable validation protocol with four pre-specified predictions. All pre-specified predictions are empirically supported: the diagnostic correctly classifies all six cases; the learned flow prior improves over an isotropic Gaussian baseline on GO cases; the inverse MAP yields no spurious advantage on time-reversible dynamics; and on irreversible GO cases, it achieves competitive or superior RMSE relative to forward baselines, with a statistically significant 17.7% reduction over a forward MLP on ERA5 solar irradiance. These results provide a structured proof-of-concept that retrodictive forecasting can constitute a viable alternative to conventional forward prediction when statistical time-irreversibility is present and exploitable.

AI.tldr: Retrodictive forecasting via inverse CVAE with MAP inference, grounded in an information-theoretic irreversibility measure, showing diagnostic capability and competitive performance on irreversible time-series including ERA5 solar irradiance.
AI.motivation: Address limitations of forward-pocused forecasting by exploiting time irreversibility; provide a formal retrodictive framework, a diagnostic for applicability, and a falsifiable validation protocol.
AI.method: Formulate retrodictive inference as MAP over an inverse CVAE with a RealNVP prior; quantify irreversibility with a symmetrized KL between forward and reverse trajectories; implement a GO/NO-GO diagnostic; evaluate on six time series (four synthetic, two ERA5 datasets) with pre-specified predictions.
AI.result: Diagnostic correctly classified all six cases; learned flow prior outperforms isotropic Gaussian in GO cases; inverse MAP does not give advantage on reversible dynamics; in irreversible GO cases, achieves competitive or superior RMSE vs forward baselines, with a 17.7% RMSE reduction over a forward MLP on ERA5 solar irradiance.
AI.conclusion: Retrodictive forecasting is a viable alternative to forward prediction when statistical time-irreversibility is present and exploitable; the framework includes a formal inference formulation, a model-free irreversibility diagnostic, and a falsifiable validation protocol.

title: Explainable Continuous-Time Mask Refinement with Local Self-Similarity Priors for Medical Image Segmentation
summary: Accurate semantic segmentation of foot ulcers is essential for automated wound monitoring, yet boundary delineation remains challenging due to tissue heterogeneity and poor contrast with surrounding skin. To overcome the limitations of standard intensity-based networks, we present LSS-LTCNet:an ante-hoc explainable framework synergizing deterministic structural priors with continuous-time neural dynamics. Our architecture departs from traditional black-box models by employing a Local Self-Similarity (LSS) mechanism that extracts dense, illumination-invariant texture descriptors to explicitly disentangle necrotic tissue from background artifacts. To enforce topological precision, we introduce a Liquid Time-Constant (LTC) refinement module that treats boundary evolution as an ODEgoverned dynamic system, iteratively refining masks over continuous time-steps. Comprehensive evaluation on the MICCAI FUSeg dataset demonstrates that LSS-LTCNet achieves state-of-the-art boundary alignment, securing a peak Dice score of 86.96% and an exceptional 95th percentile Hausdorff Distance (HD95) of 8.91 pixels. Requiring merely 25.70M parameters, the model significantly outperforms heavier U-Net and transformer baselines in efficiency. By providing inherent visual audit trails alongside high-fidelity predictions, LSS-LTCNet offers a robust and transparent solution for computer-aided diagnosis in mobile healthcare (mHealth) settings.

AI.tldr: Introduces LSS-LTCNet, an ante-hoc explainable segmentation model for foot ulcers using Local Self-Similarity descriptors and a Liquid Time-Constant ODE refinement, achieving state-of-the-art boundary accuracy with efficient parameters.
AI.motivation: Accurate ulcer boundary delineation is challenging due to tissue heterogeneity and low contrast; existing models are black-box and may lack boundary precision and efficiency.
AI.method: Combine Local Self-Similarity texture descriptors to create illumination-invariant features; LTC refinement module treats segmentation boundary evolution as a continuous-time ODE process, refining masks over time; architecture integrates deterministic priors with continuous-time neural dynamics; evaluated on MICCAI FUSeg dataset.
AI.result: Dice score 86.96%, HD95 8.91 px at 95th percentile; 25.70M parameters; outperforms heavier U-Net and transformer baselines; provides visual audit trails.
AI.conclusion: Offers robust, transparent, and efficient solution for computer-aided wound management in mobile health.

title: Agentic Multi-Source Grounding for Enhanced Query Intent Understanding: A DoorDash Case Study
summary: Accurately mapping user queries to business categories is a fundamental Information Retrieval challenge for multi-category marketplaces, where context-sparse queries such as "Wildflower" exhibit intent ambiguity, simultaneously denoting a restaurant chain, a retail product, and a floral item. Traditional classifiers force a winner-takes-all assignment, while general-purpose LLMs hallucinate unavailable inventory. We introduce an Agentic Multi-Source Grounded system that addresses both failure modes by grounding LLM inference in (i) a staged catalog entity retrieval pipeline and (ii) an agentic web-search tool invoked autonomously for cold-start queries. Rather than predicting a single label, the model emits an ordered multi-intent set, resolved by a configurable disambiguation layer that applies deterministic business policies and is designed for extensibility to personalization signals. This decoupled design generalizes across domains, allowing any marketplace to supply its own grounding sources and resolution rules without modifying the core architecture. Evaluated on DoorDash's multi-vertical search platform, the system achieves +10.9pp over the ungrounded LLM baseline and +4.6pp over the legacy production system. On long-tail queries, incremental ablations attribute +8.3pp to catalog grounding, +3.2pp to agentic web search grounding, and +1.5pp to dual intent disambiguation, yielding 90.7% accuracy (+13.0pp over baseline). The system is deployed in production, serving over 95% of daily search impressions, and establishes a generalizable paradigm for applications requiring foundation models grounded in proprietary context and real-time web knowledge to resolve ambiguous, context-sparse decision problems at scale.

AI.tldr: Agentic Multi-Source Grounded system improves multi-intent query mapping by grounding LLMs in catalog retrieval and live web search; outputs an ordered multi-intent set disambiguated by business rules, achieving strong production gains on DoorDash.
AI.motivation: Ambiguity in context-sparse, multi-category marketplaces; traditional single-label classifiers and LLMs hallucinate; need grounded, extensible approach.
AI.method: Grounding pipeline: staged catalog entity retrieval; agentic web-search tool for cold-start; produce ordered multi-intent hypotheses; disambiguation layer applies deterministic business policies; decoupled architecture; generic across domains; can supply grounding sources and resolution rules.
AI.result: Quantitative gains: +10.9 percentage points vs ungrounded LLM baseline; +4.6 pp vs legacy production; long-tail: +8.3 pp catalog grounding; +3.2 pp web search grounding; +1.5 pp dual-intent disambiguation; accuracy 90.7% (+13.0 pp). Deployed in production, handles >95% daily search impressions.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates generalized paradigm for grounding foundation models with proprietary context and real-time knowledge; scalable across domains; supports personalization; reduces hallucinations; design decouples core model from grounding sources; suitable for ambiguous decision problems at scale.

title: Shape-Interpretable Visual Self-Modeling Enables Geometry-Aware Continuum Robot Control
summary: Continuum robots possess high flexibility and redundancy, making them well suited for safe interaction in complex environments, yet their continuous deformation and nonlinear dynamics pose fundamental challenges to perception, modeling, and control. Existing vision-based control approaches often rely on end-to-end learning, achieving shape regulation without explicit awareness of robot geometry or its interaction with the environment. Here, we introduce a shape-interpretable visual self-modeling framework for continuum robots that enables geometry-aware control. Robot shapes are encoded from multi-view planar images using a Bezier-curve representation, transforming visual observations into a compact and physically meaningful shape space that uniquely characterizes the robot's three-dimensional configuration. Based on this representation, neural ordinary differential equations are employed to self-model both shape and end-effector dynamics directly from data, enabling hybrid shape-position control without analytical models or dense body markers. The explicit geometric structure of the learned shape space allows the robot to reason about its body and surroundings, supporting environment-aware behaviors such as obstacle avoidance and self-motion while maintaining end-effector objectives. Experiments on a cable-driven continuum robot demonstrate accurate shape-position regulation and tracking, with shape errors within 1.56% of image resolution and end-effector errors within 2% of robot length, as well as robust performance in constrained environments. By elevating visual shape representations from two-dimensional observations to an interpretable three-dimensional self-model, this work establishes a principled alternative to vision-based end-to-end control and advances autonomous, geometry-aware manipulation for continuum robots.

AI.tldr: A shape-interpretable visual self-model for continuum robots that uses Bezier-curve encoding from multi-view images and neural ODEs to enable geometry-aware, hybrid shape-position control without analytical models.
AI.motivation: Continuum robots are highly flexible and compliant, but their continuous deformation and nonlinear dynamics complicate perception, modeling, and control. End-to-end vision-based controllers lack explicit geometric awareness of the robot and its interaction with the environment, limiting reliable manipulation and safety.
AI.method: Encode robot shape from multi-view planar images into a Bezier-curve representation to obtain a compact, physically meaningful 3D shape space. Use neural ordinary differential equations to self-model both shape and end-effector dynamics from data, enabling hybrid shape-position control without analytical models or dense markers. Leverage the explicit geometry of the learned shape space for environment-aware behaviors such as obstacle avoidance and self-motion while pursuing end-effector objectives. Validate on a cable-driven continuum robot.
AI.result: Achieves accurate shape-position regulation with shape errors within 1.56% of image resolution and end-effector errors within 2% of robot length; demonstrates robust performance in constrained environments and supports geometry-aware, prediction-based control without hand-crafted models.
AI.conclusion: The work elevates visual shape representations from 2D observations to an interpretable 3D self-model, offering a principled alternative to end-to-end vision-based control and advancing autonomous, geometry-aware manipulation for continuum robots.

title: Reward-Modulated Local Learning in Spiking Encoders: Controlled Benchmarks with STDP and Hybrid Rate Readouts
comment: 10 pages, 5 figures. Submitted to IEEE Transactions on Neural Networks and Learning Systems
summary: This paper presents a controlled empirical study of biologically motivated local learning for handwritten digit recognition. We evaluate an STDP-inspired competitive proxy and a practical hybrid benchmark built on the same spiking population encoder. The proxy is motivated by leaky integrate-and-fire E/I circuit models with three-factor delayed reward modulation. The hybrid update is local in pre x post rates but uses supervised labels and no timing-based credit assignment. On sklearn digits, fixed-seed evaluation shows classical pixel baselines from 98.06 to 98.22% accuracy, while local spike-based models reach 86.39 +/- 4.75% (hybrid default) and 87.17 +/- 3.74% (STDP-style competitive proxy). Ablations identify normalization and reward-shaping settings as the strongest observed levers, with a best hybrid ablation of 95.52 +/- 1.11%. A network-free synthetic temporal benchmark supports the same timing-versus-rate interpretation under matched local-update training. A descriptive 2x2 analysis further shows reward-shaping effects can reverse sign across stabilization regimes, so reward-shaping conclusions should be reported jointly with normalization settings.

AI.tldr: Biologically motivated local learning for handwritten digit recognition is evaluated with STDP-inspired proxy and a local-rate hybrid update; normalization and reward shaping are key levers; local methods underperform pixel baselines, but targeted ablations approach high accuracy (~95%).
AI.motivation: Assess viability and key factors (timing vs rate, normalization, reward shaping) of biologically plausible local learning rules for supervised digit recognition.
AI.method: Empirical study comparing an STDP-inspired competitive proxy and a local-rate hybrid update on a spiking population encoder; evaluation on sklearn digits with fixed seeds; ablations on normalization and reward shaping; network-free temporal benchmark; descriptive 2x2 analysis.
AI.result: Pixel baselines: 98.06–98.22%. Local models: 86.39±4.75% (hybrid) and 87.17±3.74% (STDP proxy). Best hybrid ablation: 95.52±1.11%. Reward shaping can reverse sign across stabilization regimes; network-free benchmark supports timing-vs-rate interpretation under matched local updates.
AI.conclusion: Reward shaping must be reported with normalization; timing-vs-rate conclusions are contingent on normalization and reward-shaping settings; local biologically motivated learning remains below pixel baselines in general, though careful ablations can materially improve performance.

title: ReMoT: Reinforcement Learning with Motion Contrast Triplets
summary: We present ReMoT, a unified training paradigm to systematically address the fundamental shortcomings of VLMs in spatio-temporal consistency -- a critical failure point in navigation, robotics, and autonomous driving. ReMoT integrates two core components: (1) A rule-based automatic framework that generates ReMoT-16K, a large-scale (16.5K triplets) motion-contrast dataset derived from video meta-annotations, surpassing costly manual or model-based generation. (2) Group Relative Policy Optimization, which we empirically validate yields optimal performance and data efficiency for learning this contrastive reasoning, far exceeding standard Supervised Fine-Tuning. We also construct the first benchmark for fine-grained motion contrast triplets to measure a VLM's discrimination of subtle motion attributes (e.g., opposing directions). The resulting model achieves state-of-the-art performance on our new benchmark and multiple standard VLM benchmarks, culminating in a remarkable 25.1% performance leap on spatio-temporal reasoning tasks.

AI.tldr: ReMoT introduces a unified training paradigm for vision-language models (VLMs) to improve spatio-temporal consistency in perception tasks, featuring a rule-based auto-generated ReMoT-16K motion-contrast dataset and Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) for data-efficient contrastive learning, plus a new benchmark for motion contrast triplets. The model demonstrates state-of-the-art performance on the benchmark and standard VLM benchmarks, with a 25.1% gain on spatio-temporal reasoning tasks.
AI.motivation: Spatio-temporal reasoning is a fundamental limitation of current VLMs, impairing performance in navigation, robotics, and autonomous driving. There is a need for scalable data generation and efficient learning strategies to improve motion discrimination and temporal consistency.
AI.method: (1) Generate ReMoT-16K via a rule-based automatic framework from video meta-annotations, creating 16.5K motion-contrast triplets without manual labeling or heavy model generation. (2) Apply Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) to optimize contrastive reasoning for this data, achieving data-efficient learning beyond standard supervised fine-tuning. (3) Construct a new benchmark for fine-grained motion contrast triplets to assess discrimination of subtle motion attributes (e.g., opposing directions).
AI.result: Empirical evaluation shows that ReMoT yields state-of-the-art performance on the new motion-contrast benchmark and multiple standard VLM benchmarks, with a notable 25.1% improvement on spatio-temporal reasoning tasks.
AI.conclusion: ReMoT provides a scalable, data-efficient framework to address spatio-temporal inconsistencies in VLMs by combining rule-based dataset generation with a group-relative contrastive learning paradigm and introducing a dedicated motion-contrast benchmark that drives substantial gains in spatio-temporal reasoning.

title: LLM-assisted Semantic Option Discovery for Facilitating Adaptive Deep Reinforcement Learning
summary: Despite achieving remarkable success in complex tasks, Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL) is still suffering from critical issues in practical applications, such as low data efficiency, lack of interpretability, and limited cross-environment transferability. However, the learned policy generating actions based on states are sensitive to the environmental changes, struggling to guarantee behavioral safety and compliance. Recent research shows that integrating Large Language Models (LLMs) with symbolic planning is promising in addressing these challenges. Inspired by this, we introduce a novel LLM-driven closed-loop framework, which enables semantic-driven skill reuse and real-time constraint monitoring by mapping natural language instructions into executable rules and semantically annotating automatically created options. The proposed approach utilizes the general knowledge of LLMs to facilitate exploration efficiency and adapt to transferable options for similar environments, and provides inherent interpretability through semantic annotations. To validate the effectiveness of this framework, we conduct experiments on two domains, Office World and Montezuma's Revenge, respectively. The results demonstrate superior performance in data efficiency, constraint compliance, and cross-task transferability.

AI.tldr: An LLM-driven closed-loop DRL framework that maps natural language instructions to executable rules and semantically annotates created options, enabling semantic skill reuse, real-time constraint monitoring, and improved data efficiency, safety, and transfer; validated on Office World and Montezuma's Revenge.
AI.motivation: DRL often suffers from low data efficiency, poor interpretability, and limited cross-environment transferability. Actions derived from states are sensitive to environmental changes, jeopardizing safety. Incorporating LLMs with symbolic planning can provide interpretable, generalizable guidance and constraints.
AI.method: Integrate LLMs to translate natural language instructions into executable rules and semantically annotate automatically created options, forming a closed-loop framework that enables semantic-driven skill reuse and real-time constraint monitoring. Leverage LLMs' general knowledge to enhance exploration efficiency and develop transferable options across similar environments.
AI.result: Experiments in Office World and Montezuma's Revenge show superior data efficiency, constraint compliance, and cross-task transferability.
AI.conclusion: The proposed LLM-driven framework demonstrates the viability of combining LLMs with symbolic planning to enhance DRL in terms of efficiency, safety, and interpretability, with promising transferability across related tasks.

title: Neural Implicit Action Fields: From Discrete Waypoints to Continuous Functions for Vision-Language-Action Models
summary: Despite the rapid progress of Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models, the prevailing paradigm of predicting discrete waypoints remains fundamentally misaligned with the intrinsic continuity of physical motion. This discretization imposes rigid sampling rates, lacks high-order differentiability, and introduces quantization artifacts that hinder precise, compliant interaction. We propose Neural Implicit Action Fields (NIAF), a paradigm shift that reformulates action prediction from discrete waypoints to continuous action function regression. By utilizing an MLLM as a hierarchical spectral modulator over a learnable motion prior, NIAF synthesizes infinite-resolution trajectories as continuous-time manifolds. This formulation enables analytical differentiability, allowing for explicit supervision of velocity, acceleration, and jerk to ensure mathematical consistency and physical plausibility. Our approach achieves state-of-the-art results on CALVIN and LIBERO benchmarks across diverse backbones. Furthermore, real-world experiments demonstrate that NIAF enables stable impedance control, bridging the gap between high-level semantic understanding and low-level dynamic execution.

AI.tldr: NIAF reframes action prediction as continuous-time function regression using an MLLM-driven spectral modulation over a motion prior to produce differentiable, high-fidelity trajectories that outperform discrete waypoint methods.
AI.motivation: Discrete waypoint sampling in Vision-Language-Action models imposes rigid temporal discretization, lacks higher-order differentiability, and introduces quantization artifacts, hindering precise, compliant physical interaction. Continuous-action representations promise smoother, physically plausible motion and better control.
AI.method: Replace discrete waypoint prediction with continuous action fields. NIAF uses a Multimodal Large Language Model (MLLM) as a hierarchical spectral modulator over a learnable motion prior to synthesize infinite-resolution trajectories as continuous-time manifolds. This enables analytical differentiability and allows explicit supervision of velocity, acceleration, and jerk.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on CALVIN and LIBERO benchmarks across diverse backbones. Real-world experiments demonstrate stable impedance control, indicating effective bridging between high-level semantic understanding and low-level dynamic execution.
AI.conclusion: Continuous-action representations like NIAF can significantly improve physical plausibility and control in VLA systems, enabling better integration of perception-language-action with dynamics and offering broad applicability to robotics and interactive systems.

title: Frozen Policy Iteration: Computationally Efficient RL under Linear $Q^π$ Realizability for Deterministic Dynamics
summary:
We study computationally and statistically efficient reinforcement learning under the linear $Q^π$ realizability assumption, where any policy's $Q$-function is linear in a given state-action feature representation. Prior methods in this setting are either computationally intractable, or require (local) access to a simulator. In this paper, we propose a computationally efficient online RL algorithm, named Frozen Policy Iteration, under the linear $Q^π$ realizability setting that works for Markov Decision Processes (MDPs) with stochastic initial states, stochastic rewards and deterministic transitions. Our algorithm achieves a regret bound of $\widetilde{O}(\sqrt{d^2H^6T})$, where $d$ is the dimensionality of the feature space, $H$ is the horizon length, and $T$ is the total number of episodes. Our regret bound is optimal for linear (contextual) bandits which is a special case of our setting with $H = 1$.
  Existing policy iteration algorithms under the same setting heavily rely on repeatedly sampling the same state by access to the simulator, which is not implementable in the online setting with stochastic initial states studied in this paper. In contrast, our new algorithm circumvents this limitation by strategically using only high-confidence part of the trajectory data and freezing the policy for well-explored states, which ensures that all data used by our algorithm remains effectively on-policy during the whole course of learning. We further demonstrate the versatility of our approach by extending it to the Uniform-PAC setting and to function classes with bounded eluder dimension.

AI.tldr: Frozen Policy Iteration is a computationally efficient online reinforcement learning algorithm under the linear Q^π realizability assumption, applicable to MDPs with stochastic initial states and rewards and deterministic transitions. It achieves a regret bound of tilde O(sqrt(d^2 H^6 T)), matching the linear contextual bandit optimum when H=1, and extends to Uniform-PAC and bounded eluder dimension function classes.
AI.motivation: There is a need for a tractable online RL method under linear Q^π realizability that does not rely on replaying many states via a simulator. Existing policy-iteration approaches either are computationally intractable or require simulator access, which is unrealistic in online settings with stochastic initial states and rewards.
AI.method: Introduce Frozen Policy Iteration, an online algorithm that strategically uses only the high-confidence portion of trajectory data and freezes the policy for well-explored states to keep data effectively on-policy. This avoids the need for repeatedly sampling the same state from a simulator and yields a data-efficient, implementable approach. The framework is also extended to Uniform-PAC and to function classes with bounded eluder dimension.
AI.result: The algorithm achieves a regret bound of tilde O(sqrt(d^2 H^6 T)). This bound is optimal for linear contextual bandits (H=1). The approach eliminates reliance on simulators by using on-policy data and demonstrates versatility by extending to Uniform-PAC and eluder-dimension-bounded function classes.
AI.conclusion: Frozen Policy Iteration provides a computationally efficient online RL method under linear Q^π realizability for MDPs with stochastic initial states and rewards and deterministic transitions. It bridges the gap between theory and practice by avoiding simulator dependence, attaining near-optimal regret, and extending to broader function class settings (Uniform-PAC and bounded eluder dimension).

title: OPGAgent: An Agent for Auditable Dental Panoramic X-ray Interpretation
summary: Orthopantomograms (OPGs) are the standard panoramic radiograph in dentistry, used for full-arch screening across multiple diagnostic tasks. While Vision Language Models (VLMs) now allow multi-task OPG analysis through natural language, they underperform task-specific models on most individual tasks. Agentic systems that orchestrate specialized tools offer a path to both versatility and accuracy, this approach remains unexplored in the field of dental imaging. To address this gap, we propose OPGAgent, a multi-tool agentic system for auditable OPG interpretation. OPGAgent coordinates specialized perception modules with a consensus mechanism through three components: (1) a Hierarchical Evidence Gathering module that decomposes OPG analysis into global, quadrant, and tooth-level phases with dynamically invoking tools, (2) a Specialized Toolbox encapsulating spatial, detection, utility, and expert zoos, and (3) a Consensus Subagent that resolves conflicts through anatomical constraints. We further propose OPG-Bench, a structured-report protocol based on (Location, Field, Value) triples derived from real clinical reports, which enables a comprehensive review of findings and hallucinations, extending beyond the limitations of VQA indicators. On our OPG-Bench and the public MMOral-OPG benchmark, OPGAgent outperforms current dental VLMs and medical agent frameworks across both structured-report and VQA evaluation. Code will be released upon acceptance.

AI.tldr: OPGAgent is a multi-tool agent system for auditable panoramic dental radiograph interpretation that combines hierarchical evidence gathering, a specialized toolbox, and a consensus subagent, with a structured-report protocol (OPG-Bench). It outperforms existing dental VLMs and medical agents on OPG tasks and VQA.
AI.motivation: Address the gap where general Vision-Language Models underperform on task-specific dental tasks and to enable auditable, multi-task OPG analysis through orchestrated specialized tools.
AI.method: Three components: (1) Hierarchical Evidence Gathering with global, quadrant, and tooth-level analysis and dynamic tool invocation; (2) Specialized Toolbox comprising spatial, detection, utility, and expert zoos; (3) Consensus Subagent enforcing anatomical constraints to resolve conflicts. Also introduces OPG-Bench with Location-Field-Value triples for robust evaluation beyond VQA indicators.
AI.result: OPGAgent outperforms current dental VLMs and medical agent frameworks on both structured-report and VQA evaluations, as demonstrated on the OPG-Bench and MMOral-OPG benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates that agentic, modular architectures with anatomically constrained consensus improve accuracy and auditable interpretation in dental imaging; OPG-Bench enables comprehensive hallucination analysis and evaluation, with code to be released.

title: Multimodal Mixture-of-Experts with Retrieval Augmentation for Protein Active Site Identification
summary: Accurate identification of protein active sites at the residue level is crucial for understanding protein function and advancing drug discovery. However, current methods face two critical challenges: vulnerability in single-instance prediction due to sparse training data, and inadequate modality reliability estimation that leads to performance degradation when unreliable modalities dominate fusion processes. To address these challenges, we introduce Multimodal Mixture-of-Experts with Retrieval Augmentation (MERA), the first retrieval-augmented framework for protein active site identification. MERA employs hierarchical multi-expert retrieval that dynamically aggregates contextual information from chain, sequence, and active-site perspectives through residue-level mixture-of-experts gating. To prevent modality degradation, we propose a reliability-aware fusion strategy based on Dempster-Shafer evidence theory that quantifies modality trustworthiness through belief mass functions and learnable discounting coefficients, enabling principled multimodal integration. Extensive experiments on ProTAD-Gen and TS125 datasets demonstrate that MERA achieves state-of-the-art performance, with 90% AUPRC on active site prediction and significant gains on peptide-binding site identification, validating the effectiveness of retrieval-augmented multi-expert modeling and reliability-guided fusion.

AI.tldr: A novel retrieval-augmented, reliability-aware multimodal framework (MERA) for protein active-site identification that mitigates data sparsity and unreliable modality fusion, achieving state-of-the-art performance.
AI.motivation: Protein active-site identification is hampered by sparse training data (vulnerability of single-instance predictions) and unreliable multimodal inputs (modality degradation during fusion). A retrieval-augmented, multi-expert approach with reliability-aware fusion can address these issues.
AI.method: MERA uses hierarchical multi-expert retrieval to dynamically aggregate information from chain, sequence, and active-site perspectives through a residue-level mixture-of-experts gating mechanism. Reliability-aware fusion is implemented via Dempster-Shafer evidence theory with belief mass functions and learnable discounting coefficients to quantify modality trust and guide fusion.
AI.result: On ProTAD-Gen and TS125 datasets, MERA achieves state-of-the-art performance, including ~90% AUPRC for active site prediction and notable improvements in peptide-binding site identification.
AI.conclusion: Retrieval-augmented, multi-expert modeling coupled with reliability-guided fusion effectively addresses data sparsity and modality degradation, yielding robust, high-performance protein active-site identification.

title: MARS: Harmonizing Multimodal Convergence via Adaptive Rank Search
comment: 17 pages; Project Page: this https URL: https://minkyoungcho.github.io/mars/
summary: Fine-tuning Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) with parameter-efficient methods like Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) is crucial for task adaptation. However, imbalanced training dynamics across modalities often lead to suboptimal accuracy due to negative interference, a challenge typically addressed with inefficient heuristic methods such as manually tuning separate learning rates. To overcome this, we introduce MARS (Multimodal Adaptive Rank Search), an approach to discover optimal rank pairs that balance training dynamics while maximizing performance. Our key innovation, a proposed framework of dual scaling laws, enables this search: one law models module-specific convergence time to prune the search space to candidates with aligned dynamics, while the other predicts final task performance to select the optimal pair from the pruned set. By re-purposing the LoRA rank as a controller for modality-specific convergence speed, MARS outperforms baseline methods and provides a robust, automated strategy for optimizing MLLM fine-tuning.

AI.tldr: MARS automates rank-tuning for LoRA-based multimodal LLM fine-tuning by using dual scaling laws to balance modality dynamics, achieving better performance with automated search over rank pairs.
AI.motivation: Imbalanced training dynamics across modalities lead to negative interference and suboptimal accuracy; manual tuning of learning rates is inefficient.
AI.method: Propose dual scaling laws: (1) a convergence-time model to prune rank-pair candidates to align dynamics across modalities; (2) a performance predictor to select the best pair from the pruned set. Re-purpose LoRA rank as a controller for modality-specific convergence speed during fine-tuning.
AI.result: Empirically outperforms baselines, delivering an automated, robust strategy for optimizing MLLM fine-tuning without manual per-modality tuning.
AI.conclusion: MARS provides a dynamic, automated framework for rank-based modulation of LoRA in MLLMs, improving efficiency and accuracy and potentially generalizing to other parameter-efficient fine-tuning methods.

title: DreamWorld: Unified World Modeling in Video Generation
summary: Despite impressive progress in video generation, existing models remain limited to surface-level plausibility, lacking a coherent and unified understanding of the world. Prior approaches typically incorporate only a single form of world-related knowledge or rely on rigid alignment strategies to introduce additional knowledge. However, aligning the single world knowledge is insufficient to constitute a world model that requires jointly modeling multiple heterogeneous dimensions (e.g., physical commonsense, 3D and temporal consistency). To address this limitation, we introduce \textbf{DreamWorld}, a unified framework that integrates complementary world knowledge into video generators via a \textbf{Joint World Modeling Paradigm}, jointly predicting video pixels and features from foundation models to capture temporal dynamics, spatial geometry, and semantic consistency. However, naively optimizing these heterogeneous objectives can lead to visual instability and temporal flickering. To mitigate this issue, we propose \textit{Consistent Constraint Annealing (CCA)} to progressively regulate world-level constraints during training, and \textit{Multi-Source Inner-Guidance} to enforce learned world priors at inference. Extensive evaluations show that DreamWorld improves world consistency, outperforming Wan2.1 by 2.26 points on VBench. Code will be made publicly available at \href{https://github.com/ABU121111/DreamWorld}{\textcolor{mypink}{\textbf{Github}}}.

AI.tldr: DreamWorld proposes a unified Joint World Modeling Paradigm for video generation that jointly models pixels and features from foundation models to capture temporal dynamics, 3D geometry, and semantic consistency; it introduces Consistent Constraint Annealing and Multi-Source Inner-Guidance to stabilize training and inference, achieving improved world consistency and outperforming Wan2.1 on VBench; code will be released.
AI.motivation: Existing video-generation models lack a coherent, multi-faceted world model and rely on single-form world knowledge or rigid alignment, leading to limited world understanding and temporal instability.
AI.method: Joint World Modeling Paradigm that jointly predicts video pixels and features from foundation models to encode temporal, spatial, and semantic information; Consistent Constraint Annealing (CCA) to progressively regulate world-level constraints during training; Multi-Source Inner-Guidance to enforce learned world priors during inference.
AI.result: Extensive evaluations show improved world consistency, with DreamWorld outperforming Wan2.1 by 2.26 points on VBench.
AI.conclusion: DreamWorld establishes a unified, multi-knowledge world model for video generation, offering enhanced consistency and quality with training-time constraints and inference priors; code will be publicly available.

title: Tiny-DroNeRF: Tiny Neural Radiance Fields aboard Federated Learning-enabled Nano-drones
comment: This paper has been accepted for publication in the IEEE ICRA 2026 conference. ©2026 IEEE
summary: Sub-30g nano-sized aerial robots can leverage their agility and form factor to autonomously explore cluttered and narrow environments, like in industrial inspection and search and rescue missions. However, the price for their tiny size is a strong limit in their resources, i.e., sub-100 mW microcontroller units (MCUs) delivering $\sim$100 GOps/s at best, and memory budgets well below 100 MB. Despite these strict constraints, we aim to enable complex vision-based tasks aboard nano-drones, such as dense 3D scene reconstruction: a key robotic task underlying fundamental capabilities like spatial awareness and motion planning. Top-performing 3D reconstruction methods leverage neural radiance fields (NeRF) models, which require GBs of memory and massive computation, usually delivered by high-end GPUs consuming 100s of Watts. Our work introduces Tiny-DroNeRF, a lightweight NeRF model, based on Instant-NGP, and optimized for running on a GAP9 ultra-low-power (ULP) MCU aboard our nano-drones. Then, we further empower our Tiny-DroNeRF by leveraging a collaborative federated learning scheme, which distributes the model training among multiple nano-drones. Our experimental results show a 96% reduction in Tiny-DroNeRF's memory footprint compared to Instant-NGP, with only a 5.7 dB drop in reconstruction accuracy. Finally, our federated learning scheme allows Tiny-DroNeRF to train with an amount of data otherwise impossible to keep in a single drone's memory, increasing the overall reconstruction accuracy. Ultimately, our work combines, for the first time, NeRF training on an ULP MCU with federated learning on nano-drones.

AI.tldr: Tiny-DroNeRF enables 3D reconstruction on sub-30g nano-drones using an ultra-low-power MCU. It reduces memory by 96% versus Instant-NGP with a 5.7 dB drop in reconstruction accuracy; federated learning distributes training across drones to improve overall performance, demonstrating NeRF training on an ULP MCU for drone swarms.
AI.motivation: Enable dense 3D scene reconstruction and spatial awareness onboard ultra-light, ultra-low-power nano-drones operating in cluttered environments (industrial inspection, search and rescue) despite severe resource constraints.
AI.method: Adapt Instant-NGP into Tiny-DroNeRF for GAP9 ultra-low-power MCU, achieving substantial memory/compute reductions; implement collaborative federated learning to distribute NeRF training across multiple nano-drones.
AI.result: 97%? 96% reduction in Tiny-DroNeRF memory footprint vs Instant-NGP; reconstruction accuracy decreases by 5.7 dB; federated learning enables training with data across drones, improving overall reconstruction accuracy.
AI.conclusion: First demonstration of NeRF training on an ultra-low-power MCU with federated learning on nano-drones, enabling onboard dense 3D reconstruction under stringent resource limits.

title: To Use or not to Use Muon: How Simplicity Bias in Optimizers Matters
summary: For a long period of time, Adam has served as the ubiquitous default choice for training deep neural networks. Recently, many new optimizers have been introduced, out of which Muon has perhaps gained the highest popularity due to its superior training speed. While many papers set out to validate the benefits of Muon, our paper investigates the potential downsides stemming from the mechanism driving this speedup. We explore the biases induced when optimizing with Muon, providing theoretical analysis and its consequences to the learning trajectories and solutions learned. While the theory does provide justification for the benefits Muon brings, it also guides our intuition when coming up with a couple of examples where Muon-optimized models have disadvantages. The core problem we emphasize is that Muon optimization removes a simplicity bias that is naturally preserved by older, more thoroughly studied methods like Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD). We take first steps toward understanding consequences this may have: Muon might struggle to uncover common underlying structure across tasks, and be more prone to fitting spurious features. More broadly, this paper should serve as a reminder: when developing new optimizers, it is essential to consider the biases they introduce, as these biases can fundamentally change a model's behavior -- for better or for worse.

AI.tldr: Muon speeds up training but may erode a simplicity bias, risking worse cross-task structure discovery and higher susceptibility to spurious features; highlights speed–bias trade-offs in optimizer design.
AI.motivation: To understand potential downsides and biases introduced by Muon, beyond its training speed advantages.
AI.method: Theoretical analysis of Muon's optimization dynamics; derive induced biases on learning trajectories and solutions; present illustrative examples showing disadvantages.
AI.result: Theory validates some benefits of Muon while revealing biases that can hinder uncovering common structure and increase fitting of spurious features; provides intuition and initial demonstrations.
AI.conclusion: Optimizer design must account for induced biases; speed advantages should be weighed against potential changes in model behavior; call for bias-aware evaluation and broader study.

title: High Dynamic Range Imaging Based on an Asymmetric Event-SVE Camera System
comment: This paper has been accepted by Optics Express
summary: High dynamic range (HDR) imaging under extreme illumination remains challenging for conventional cameras due to overexposure. Event cameras provide microsecond temporal resolution and high dynamic range, while spatially varying exposure (SVE) sensors offer single-shot radiometric diversity.We present a hardware--algorithm co-designed HDR imaging system that tightly integrates an SVE micro-attenuation camera with an event sensor in an asymmetric dual-modality configuration. To handle non-coaxial geometry and heterogeneous optics, we develop a two-stage cross-modal alignment framework that combines feature-guided coarse homography estimation with a multi-scale refinement module based on spatial pooling and frequency-domain filtering. On top of aligned representations, we develop a cross-modal HDR reconstruction network with convolutional fusion, mutual-information regularization, and a learnable fusion loss that adaptively balances intensity cues and event-derived structural constraints.
