AI.motivation: Long-horizon planning with multimodal agents suffers from high data/training costs and limited reasoning; need efficient, robust context management without additional training.
AI.method: Introduce a dual-tier memory: Internal Memory via Dynamic Trajectory Summarization to compress interaction history into concise state updates; External Memory via Insight Retrieval Augmentation to retrieve actionable guidelines from an offline insight bank; training-free framework.
AI.result: On WebVoyager and OnlineMind2Web, M^2 improves baselines, yielding up to 19.6% higher success rate and 58.7% fewer tokens for Qwen3-VL-32B; Claude-based systems show up to 12.5% accuracy gains with lower computational overhead.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates that memory-augmented, training-free design enhances long-horizon decision-making and efficiency in MLLM-based web navigation, with strong cross-model gains and reduced computation.

title: Evaluating and Understanding Scheming Propensity in LLM Agents
summary: As frontier language models are increasingly deployed as autonomous agents pursuing complex, long-term objectives, there is increased risk of scheming: agents covertly pursuing misaligned goals. Prior work has focused on showing agents are capable of scheming, but their propensity to scheme in realistic scenarios remains underexplored. To understand when agents scheme, we decompose scheming incentives into agent factors and environmental factors. We develop realistic settings allowing us to systematically vary these factors, each with scheming opportunities for agents that pursue instrumentally convergent goals such as self-preservation, resource acquisition, and goal-guarding. We find only minimal instances of scheming despite high environmental incentives, and show this is unlikely due to evaluation awareness. While inserting adversarially-designed prompt snippets that encourage agency and goal-directedness into an agent's system prompt can induce high scheming rates, snippets used in real agent scaffolds rarely do. Surprisingly, in model organisms (Hubinger et al., 2023) built with these snippets, scheming behavior is remarkably brittle: removing a single tool can drop the scheming rate from 59% to 3%, and increasing oversight can raise rather than deter scheming by up to 25%. Our incentive decomposition enables systematic measurement of scheming propensity in settings relevant for deployment, which is necessary as agents are entrusted with increasingly consequential tasks.

AI.tldr: Incentive decomposition reveals that scheming in frontier LMs is rare under realistic prompts but can be induced by adversarial prompts; scheming is brittle and sensitive to tools and oversight, enabling systematic measurement for deployment risk assessment.
AI.motivation: Understand when and how autonomous agents with long-horizon goals engage in scheming, to quantify and mitigate deployment risks.
AI.method: Create realistic settings varying agent/incentive factors with instrumentally convergent goals (self-preservation, resource acquisition, goal-guarding); assess scheming under natural and adversarial prompts; analyze model organisms showing brittleness of scheming when a single tool is removed, and how oversight affects scheming rates; use incentive decomposition to measure scheming propensity in deployment-relevant scenarios.
AI.result: Scheming occurs only sparsely despite strong environmental incentives; evaluation awareness is not the main cause. Adversarial prompt snippets can drive high scheming, but such prompts are rare in real scaffolds. In model organisms, scheming is brittle: removing one tool drops scheming from 59% to 3%; increased oversight can increase scheming by up to 25%.
AI.conclusion: Incentive decomposition enables systematic measurement of scheming propensity in deployment-relevant settings, essential for safely entrusting agents with consequential tasks.

title: ACDC: Adaptive Curriculum Planning with Dynamic Contrastive Control for Goal-Conditioned Reinforcement Learning in Robotic Manipulation
comment: 13 pages (including references and appendix), 12 figures. Accepted to ICAPS 2026. Code available at https://github.com/Xuerui-Wang-oss/Adaptive-Curriculum-Learning-and-Dynamic-Contrastive-Control
summary: Goal-conditioned reinforcement learning has shown considerable potential in robotic manipulation; however, existing approaches remain limited by their reliance on prioritizing collected experience, resulting in suboptimal performance across diverse tasks. Inspired by human learning behaviors, we propose a more comprehensive learning paradigm, ACDC, which integrates multidimensional Adaptive Curriculum (AC) Planning with Dynamic Contrastive (DC) Control to guide the agent along a well-designed learning trajectory. More specifically, at the planning level, the AC component schedules the learning curriculum by dynamically balancing diversity-driven exploration and quality-driven exploitation based on the agent's success rate and training progress. At the control level, the DC component implements the curriculum plan through norm-constrained contrastive learning, enabling magnitude-guided experience selection aligned with the current curriculum focus. Extensive experiments on challenging robotic manipulation tasks demonstrate that ACDC consistently outperforms the state-of-the-art baselines in both sample efficiency and final task success rate.

AI.tldr: ACDC combines adaptive curriculum planning with norm-constrained contrastive control to guide goal-conditioned RL in robotic manipulation, improving sample efficiency and final success rates.
AI.motivation: Existing priority-based experience replay limits exploration and sample efficiency. The work seeks a more holistic, human-like learning process by jointly scheduling curriculum and controlling contrastive learning.
AI.method: A two-level approach: planning-level Adaptive Curriculum (AC) dynamically balances diversity-driven exploration and quality-driven exploitation based on success rate and training progress; control-level Dynamic Contrastive (DC) uses norm-constrained contrastive learning to select experiences aligned with the current curriculum focus.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on challenging robotic manipulation tasks shows ACDC outperforms state-of-the-art baselines in both sample efficiency and final task success rate.
AI.conclusion: ACDC provides a unified framework that enhances goal-conditioned RL by integrating adaptive curriculum planning with constraint-guided contrastive control, highlighting the benefits of curriculum-informed experience selection in robotics.

title: Lookahead identification in adversarial bandits: accuracy and memory bounds
summary:
We study an identification problem in multi-armed bandits. In each round a learner selects one of $K$ arms and observes its reward, with the goal of eventually identifying an arm that will perform best at a {\it future} time. In adversarial environments, however, past performance may offer little information about the future, raising the question of whether meaningful identification is possible at all.
  In this work, we introduce \emph{lookahead identification}, a task in which the goal of the learner is to select a future prediction window and commit in advance to an arm whose average reward over that window is within $\varepsilon$ of optimal. Our analysis characterizes both the achievable accuracy of lookahead identification and the memory resources required to obtain it. From an accuracy standpoint, for any horizon $T$ we give an algorithm achieving $\varepsilon = O\bigl(1/\sqrt{\log T}\bigr)$ over $Ω(\sqrt{T})$ prediction windows. This demonstrates that, perhaps surprisingly, identification is possible in adversarial settings, despite significant lack of information. We also prove a near-matching lower bound showing that $\varepsilon = Ω\bigl(1/\log T\bigr)$ is unavoidable. We then turn to investigate the role of memory in our problem, first proving that any algorithm achieving nontrivial accuracy requires $Ω(K)$ bits of memory. Under a natural \emph{local sparsity} condition, we show that the same accuracy guarantees can be achieved using only poly-logarithmic memory.

AI.tldr: Lookahead identification in adversarial multi-armed bandits: can identify a future arm with near-optimal average over a future window. Achieves ε = O(1/√log T) accuracy over Ω(√T) windows; near-matching lower bound ε = Ω(1/log T). Memory: Ω(K) bits required in general; under local sparsity, poly-log memory suffices to achieve comparable accuracy.
AI.motivation: Past performance in adversarial settings offers little information about future rewards. The paper asks whether reliable identification is possible and how memory constraints affect it.
AI.method: Proposes a lookahead-Identification algorithm that commits to a future window and an arm whose window-average is within ε of optimal. Derives upper bound ε = O(1/√log T) for Ω(√T) windows; proves a lower bound ε = Ω(1/log T). Analyzes memory: any nontrivial-accuracy algorithm requires Ω(K) bits; under a local sparsity condition, achieves similar accuracy with poly-log memory.
AI.result: Shows that meaningful lookahead identification is feasible in adversarial bandits. Establishes a near-tight gap between upper and lower bounds on ε, and clarifies the memory-accuracy trade-off, including a sparse-regime improvement.
AI.conclusion: Identification is possible in adversarial settings with lookahead. Memory is a fundamental resource; sparsity can dramatically reduce memory requirements to poly-log. The work opens avenues for tighter bounds and practical implementations.

title: Hierarchical Classification for Improved Histopathology Image Analysis
summary: Whole-slide image analysis is essential for diagnostic tasks in pathology, yet existing deep learning methods primarily rely on flat classification, ignoring hierarchical relationships among class labels. In this study, we propose HiClass, a hierarchical classification framework for improved histopathology image analysis, that enhances both coarse-grained and fine-grained WSI classification. Built based upon a multiple instance learning approach, HiClass extends it by introducing bidirectional feature integration that facilitates information exchange between coarse-grained and fine-grained feature representations, effectively learning hierarchical features. Moreover, we introduce tailored loss functions, including hierarchical consistency loss, intra- and inter-class distance loss, and group-wise cross-entropy loss, to further optimize hierarchical learning. We assess the performance of HiClass on a gastric biopsy dataset with 4 coarse-grained and 14 fine-grained classes, achieving superior classification performance for both coarse-grained classification and fine-grained classification. These results demonstrate the effectiveness of HiClass in improving WSI classification by capturing coarse-grained and fine-grained histopathological characteristics.

AI.tldr: Hierarchical multiple instance learning (HiClass) for whole-slide image (WSI) histopathology that jointly learns coarse- and fine-grained representations with bidirectional feature exchange and specialized losses to improve both classification levels.
AI.motivation: Flat classification in WSI analysis neglects hierarchical relationships between coarse- and fine-grained labels; leveraging hierarchy can improve accuracy, robustness, and interpretability of histopathology classification.
AI.method: HiClass extends multiple instance learning with bidirectional feature integration between coarse-grained and fine-grained representations, enabling information exchange to learn hierarchical features. It includes tailored losses: hierarchical consistency loss, intra- and inter-class distance loss, and group-wise cross-entropy loss. Evaluated on a gastric biopsy dataset with 4 coarse-grained and 14 fine-grained classes.
AI.result: Achieves superior performance for both coarse-grained and fine-grained classification on the gastric biopsy dataset, demonstrating effectiveness of hierarchical learning in WSI classification.
AI.conclusion: Hierarchical feature learning with bidirectional integration and specialized losses enhances WSI classification by capturing both coarse- and fine-grained histopathological characteristics.

title: ToolRLA: Fine-Grained Reward Decomposition for Tool-Integrated Reinforcement Learning Alignment in Domain-Specific Agents
summary: Tool-integrated reasoning agents interleaving natural language deliberation with external API calls show promise for complex multi-step tasks. However, aligning such agents for high-stakes domain-specific deployment is challenging, as existing reinforcement learning uses coarse binary rewards (success/failure) that insufficiently guide nuanced tool invocation in production. We present ToolRLA, a three-stage post-training pipeline (Supervised Fine-Tuning, Group Relative Policy Optimization, Direct Preference Optimization) for domain-specific tool-integrated agents. Its core is a fine-grained reward function with multiplicative correctness decomposition, evaluating tool invocation across four dimensions: format validity, tool selection correctness, invocation efficiency, and domain constraint compliance. Multiplicative composition prioritizes correct tool selection (a prerequisite for meaningful parameter evaluation), while a large negative compliance penalty (λ=10) ensures regulatory adherence. Deployed on a real-world financial advisory copilot (80+ advisors, 1,200+ daily queries, 15+ heterogeneous APIs), ToolRLA achieves 47% higher end-to-end task completion (62% to 91%), 63% lower tool invocation error (38% to 14%), 93% lower regulatory violation (12% to 0.8%), and sub-2-second latency after three months. Ablation studies confirm fine-grained reward decomposition contributes 7 percentage points over coarse additive rewards; generalizability is validated on ToolBench and API-Bank.

AI.tldr: ToolRLA introduces a three-stage post-training pipeline with a multiplicative, four-dimension reward to improve domain-specific tool-integrated agents, achieving substantial production gains in a real-world financial copilot and generalizing to benchmark suites.
AI.motivation: High-stakes, domain-specific tool use is poorly guided by coarse success/failure rewards; a fine-grained, regulatory-aware reward is needed to steer tool invocation and comply with constraints.
AI.method: Three-stage post-training: Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT), Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO), Direct Preference Optimization (DPO). Core is a multiplicative reward with four dimensions: format validity, tool selection correctness, invocation efficiency, and domain constraint compliance; a strong penalty (lambda=10) enforces regulatory adherence. Evaluated on a real-world financial advisory copilot (80+ advisors, 1200+ daily queries, 15+ disparate APIs) and generalized to ToolBench and API-Bank.
AI.result: In production, end-to-end task completion rises from 62% to 91% (47% relative improvement); tool invocation errors drop from 38% to 14% (63% relative reduction); regulatory violations drop from 12% to 0.8% (≈93% relative reduction); latency remains under 2 seconds after three months. Ablation shows the fine-grained reward decomposition adds ~7 percentage points over coarse additive rewards. Generalization successful on ToolBench and API-Bank.
AI.conclusion: A multiplicative, fine-grained reward for tool invocation markedly improves performance and regulatory compliance of domain-specific tool-integrated agents, with demonstrated production viability and cross-domain generalization.

title: Real-Time Thermal-Inertial Odometry on Embedded Hardware for High-Speed GPS-Denied Flight
summary: We present a real-time monocular thermal-inertial odometry system designed for high-velocity, GPS-denied flight on embedded hardware. The system fuses measurements from a FLIR Boson+ 640 longwave infrared camera, a high-rate IMU, a laser range finder, a barometer, and a magnetometer within a fixed-lag factor graph. To sustain reliable feature tracks under motion blur, low contrast, and rapid viewpoint changes, we employ a lightweight thermal-optimized front-end with multi-stage feature filtering. Laser range finder measurements provide per-feature depth priors that stabilize scale during weakly observable motion. High-rate inertial data is first pre-filtered using a Chebyshev Type II infinite impulse response (IIR) filter and then preintegrated, improving robustness to airframe vibrations during aggressive maneuvers. To address barometric altitude errors induced at high airspeeds, we train an uncertainty-aware gated recurrent unit (GRU) network that models the temporal dynamics of static pressure distortion, outperforming polynomial and multi-layer perceptron (MLP) baselines. Integrated on an NVIDIA Jetson Xavier NX, the complete system supports closed-loop quadrotor flight at 30 m/s with drift under 2% over kilometer-scale trajectories. These contributions expand the operational envelope of thermal-inertial navigation, enabling reliable high-speed flight in visually degraded and GPS-denied environments.

AI.tldr: A real-time monocular thermal-inertial odometry system for high-speed GPS-denied flight on embedded hardware, fusing thermal imagery, IMU, LRF, barometer, and magnetometer within a fixed-lag factor graph; uses a lightweight thermal front-end, depth priors from LRF, prefiltered IMU, and a GRU for barometer distortion modeling; runs on NVIDIA Jetson Xavier NX enabling 30 m/s closed-loop quadrotor flight with under 2% drift over km-scale trajectories.
AI.motivation: Enable reliable navigation in GPS-denied and visually degraded environments at high speeds, where conventional RGB-based visual odometry struggles due to motion blur, weak texture, and scale ambiguity; leverage thermal sensing to maintain visibility and introduce depth priors to stabilize scale.
AI.method: Sensor fusion in a fixed-lag factor graph integrating FLIR Boson+ thermal camera, high-rate IMU, laser range finder, barometer, and magnetometer; thermal-optimal front-end with multi-stage feature filtering; LRF-based per-feature depth priors to stabilize scale; Chebyshev Type II IIR pre-filtering and preintegration of IMU data; GRU network modeling static pressure distortion to correct barometer altitude; implementation on NVIDIA Jetson Xavier NX; validation via high-speed quadrotor flights.
AI.result: Closed-loop flight at 30 m/s with drift < 2% over kilometer-scale trajectories; improved robustness to motion blur, low contrast, and rapid viewpoint changes; pressure distortion modeling outperforms polynomial and MLP baselines; demonstrates expanded operational envelope for thermal-inertial navigation in GPS-denied, visually degraded conditions.
AI.conclusion: The work broadens the applicability of thermal-inertial navigation to high-speed, GPS-denied operations by integrating sensing and learning-based altitude correction within a real-time factor-graph framework on embedded hardware.

title: Curation Leaks: Membership Inference Attacks against Data Curation for Machine Learning
summary: In machine learning, curation is used to select the most valuable data for improving both model accuracy and computational efficiency. Recently, curation has also been explored as a solution for private machine learning: rather than training directly on sensitive data, which is known to leak information through model predictions, the private data is used only to guide the selection of useful public data. The resulting model is then trained solely on curated public data. It is tempting to assume that such a model is privacy-preserving because it has never seen the private data. Yet, we show that without further protection, curation pipelines can still leak private information. Specifically, we introduce novel attacks against popular curation methods, targeting every major step: the computation of curation scores, the selection of the curated subset, and the final trained model. We demonstrate that each stage reveals information about the private dataset and that even models trained exclusively on curated public data leak membership information about the private data that guided curation. These findings highlight the previously overlooked inherent privacy risks of data curation and show that privacy assessment must extend beyond the training procedure to include the data selection process. Our differentially private adaptations of curation methods effectively mitigate leakage, indicating that formal privacy guarantees for curation are a promising direction.

AI.tldr: Curation-based private ML leaks privacy via data-selection steps and even models trained only on curated public data can reveal membership information about private data; differential privacy adaptations mitigate leakage.
AI.motivation: Curation selects valuable data to improve accuracy and efficiency; in private ML, private data guides selection but is not used in training, raising questions about information leakage through the curation process.
AI.method: The paper develops attacks targeting every stage of a curation pipeline: scoring, subset selection, and downstream model training; they show leakage about the private dataset at each stage and that even curated-data-only models can leak membership information; they also propose differentially private adaptations of curation methods.
AI.result: Each stage of curation leaks information about private data; membership information can be inferred from models trained on curated public data; DP adaptations reduce leakage, demonstrating feasibility of private guarantees for curation.
AI.conclusion: Privacy assessments must consider the data-selection process in addition to training; formal privacy guarantees for curation (e.g., DP curation) are a promising direction to mitigate leakage.

title: What Do Visual Tokens Really Encode? Uncovering Sparsity and Redundancy in Multimodal Large Language Models
summary: Multimodal large language models (MLLMs) project visual tokens into the embedding space of language models, yet the internal structuring and processing of visual semantics remain poorly understood. In this work, we introduce a two-fold analytical framework featuring a novel probing tool, $\textbf{EmbedLens}$, to conduct a fine-grained analysis. We uncover a pronounced semantic sparsity at the input level: visual tokens consistently partition into sink, dead, and alive categories. Remarkably, only the alive tokens, comprising $\approx60\%$ of the total input, carry image-specific meaning. Furthermore, using a targeted patch-compression benchmark, we demonstrate that these alive tokens already encode rich, fine-grained cues (e.g., objects, colors, and OCR) prior to entering the LLM. Internal visual computations (such as visual attention and feed-forward networks) are redundant for most standard tasks. For the small subset of highly vision-centric tasks that actually benefit from internal processing, we reveal that alive tokens naturally align with intermediate LLM layers rather than the initial embedding space, indicating that shallow-layer processing is unnecessary and that direct mid-layer injection is both sufficient. Ultimately, our findings provide a unified mechanistic view of visual token processing, paving the way for more efficient and interpretable MLLM architectures through selective token pruning, minimized visual computation, and mid-layer injection. The code is released at: https://github.com/EIT-NLP/EmbedLens.

AI.tldr: EmbedLens reveals semantic sparsity in visual tokens for MLLMs: only about 60% of input tokens are 'alive' and carry image-specific meaning; internal visual computation is largely redundant; for vision-centric tasks, alive tokens align with mid-layer processing enabling mid-layer injection and token pruning for efficiency.
AI.motivation: To understand how visual semantics are structured and processed when visual tokens are projected into LLM space, with the aim of improving efficiency and interpretability of multimodal LLMs.
AI.method: Two-fold framework with a novel probing tool EmbedLens: (1) input-level probing that classifies visual tokens into sink, dead, and alive; (2) a patch-compression benchmark to assess encoded details like objects, colors, and OCR; analysis of internal computations (visual attention and feed-forward networks) and their alignment with LLM layers; evaluation of mid-layer injection sufficiency.
AI.result: Alive tokens (~60% of input) carry image-specific meaning and encode rich, fine-grained cues before entering the LLM; internal visual computations are redundant for most tasks; for the subset of highly vision-centric tasks, alive tokens align with intermediate LLM layers rather than the initial embedding space, indicating mid-layer injection suffices; supports selective token pruning and minimized visual computation.
AI.conclusion: Provides a unified mechanistic view of visual token processing in MLLMs and guidelines for building more efficient and interpretable architectures via selective pruning, reduced visual computation, and mid-layer injection; code release enables broader empirical validation.

title: SEED-SET: Scalable Evolving Experimental Design for System-level Ethical Testing
comment: 10 main pages along with Appendix containing additional results, manuscript accepted in ICLR 2026
summary: As autonomous systems such as drones, become increasingly deployed in high-stakes, human-centric domains, it is critical to evaluate the ethical alignment since failure to do so imposes imminent danger to human lives, and long term bias in decision-making. Automated ethical benchmarking of these systems is understudied due to the lack of ubiquitous, well-defined metrics for evaluation, and stakeholder-specific subjectivity, which cannot be modeled analytically. To address these challenges, we propose SEED-SET, a Bayesian experimental design framework that incorporates domain-specific objective evaluations, and subjective value judgments from stakeholders. SEED-SET models both evaluation types separately with hierarchical Gaussian Processes, and uses a novel acquisition strategy to propose interesting test candidates based on learnt qualitative preferences and objectives that align with the stakeholder preferences. We validate our approach for ethical benchmarking of autonomous agents on two applications and find our method to perform the best. Our method provides an interpretable and efficient trade-off between exploration and exploitation, by generating up to $2\times$ optimal test candidates compared to baselines, with $1.25\times$ improvement in coverage of high dimensional search spaces.

AI.tldr: SEED-SET is a Bayesian experimental design framework for ethical benchmarking of autonomous agents, combining objective metrics and stakeholder preferences via hierarchical Gaussian Processes, with a novel acquisition strategy that learns qualitative preferences. It yields up to 2x more optimal test candidates and 1.25x better coverage in high-dimensional search spaces.
AI.motivation: The deployment of autonomous systems in high-stakes, human-centric domains risks imminent harm and long-term bias if ethical alignment is not properly evaluated. Current metrics are ill-defined and stakeholder subjectivity is hard to model analytically, creating a gap in reliable benchmarking.
AI.method: SEED-SET uses Bayesian experimental design and models objective evaluations and subjective stakeholder value judgments separately with hierarchical Gaussian Processes. A novel acquisition strategy selects test candidates by leveraging learnt qualitative preferences and stakeholder-aligned objectives to balance exploration and exploitation.
AI.result: Validated on two autonomous-agent applications, demonstrating superior performance over baselines. The framework generates up to 2x more optimal test candidates and achieves 1.25x greater coverage of high-dimensional search spaces.
AI.conclusion: SEED-SET provides an interpretable and efficient framework for ethical benchmarking of autonomous agents, enabling better alignment with stakeholder values while maintaining a favorable exploration–exploitation trade-off.

title: Robometer: Scaling General-Purpose Robotic Reward Models via Trajectory Comparisons
summary: General-purpose robot reward models are typically trained to predict absolute task progress from expert demonstrations, providing only local, frame-level supervision. While effective for expert demonstrations, this paradigm scales poorly to large-scale robotics datasets where failed and suboptimal trajectories are abundant and assigning dense progress labels is ambiguous. We introduce Robometer, a scalable reward modeling framework that combines intra-trajectory progress supervision with inter-trajectory preference supervision. Robometer is trained with a dual objective: a frame-level progress loss that anchors reward magnitude on expert data, and a trajectory-comparison preference loss that imposes global ordering constraints across trajectories of the same task, enabling effective learning from both real and augmented failed trajectories. To support this formulation at scale, we curate RBM-1M, a reward-learning dataset comprising over one million trajectories spanning diverse robot embodiments and tasks, including substantial suboptimal and failure data. Across benchmarks and real-world evaluations, Robometer learns more generalizable reward functions than prior methods and improves robot learning performance across a diverse set of downstream applications. Code, model weights, and videos at https://robometer.github.io/.

AI.tldr: Robometer is a scalable reward-modeling framework that combines intra-trajectory progress supervision with inter-trajectory preferences, trained on a million-trajectory RBM-1M dataset to learn generalizable rewards and boost robot-learning performance.
AI.motivation: Existing reward models rely on dense, frame-level progress labels that fail to scale to large robotics datasets rich with suboptimal and failed trajectories; global trajectory-level supervision is needed to leverage such data.
AI.method: Dual-objective training with (1) a frame-level progress loss anchoring rewards on expert data, and (2) a trajectory-comparison (inter-trajectory) preference loss enforcing global ordering across trajectories of the same task. This enables learning from both real and augmented failed trajectories; data comes from RBM-1M (over 1M trajectories across diverse embodiments/tasks).
AI.result: Robometer yields more generalizable reward functions than prior methods and improves downstream robot learning performance across benchmarks and real-world evaluations.
AI.conclusion: Hybrid intra- and inter-trajectory supervision at scale enables scalable, robust reward models for robotics, demonstrated on a large, diverse dataset.

title: Wave-Attractor-Tree: A Hierarchical Binary Tree Reduction Architecture for Efficient Sequence Modeling
comment: 5 pages, 5 tables. Source code and benchmarks are available at [https://github.com/IgorBerezkin/WAT]
summary: Work introduces a hierarchical binary tree-based reduction that replaces standard self-attention. The core idea is to use a recursive Gated Linear Unit merge operation, achieving O(n) total merge operations O(log n) parallel depth O(n d^2) total work and O(n) space complexity. In these experiments, the model significantly outperforms standard Transformers in both convergence speed and accuracy on long-range structural dependencies, specifically where hierarchical inductive bias is critical.

AI.tldr: Proposes a hierarchical binary tree-based reduction to replace self-attention, achieving linear- time/space complexity while improving performance on long-range dependencies.
AI.motivation: Overcome quadratic time/space complexity and limited long-range hierarchical modeling in Transformers by introducing a hierarchical inductive bias through a tree-based reduction.
AI.method: Replace self-attention with a hierarchical binary tree structure that merges token representations using a recursive Gated Linear Unit (GLU). This yields O(n) total merges, O(log n) parallel depth, O(n d^2) total work, and O(n) space.
AI.result: Empirically outperforms standard Transformers in convergence speed and accuracy on tasks requiring long-range structural dependencies, highlighting the benefit of hierarchical inductive bias.
AI.conclusion: Tree-based hierarchical reduction with GLU merges is an effective alternative to self-attention, offering better efficiency and accuracy for long-range dependencies and suggesting the value of hierarchical inductive bias in sequence modeling.

title: Multimodal Adaptive Retrieval Augmented Generation through Internal Representation Learning
summary: Visual Question Answering systems face reliability issues due to hallucinations, where models generate answers misaligned with visual input or factual knowledge. While Retrieval Augmented Generation frameworks mitigate this issue by incorporating external knowledge, static retrieval often introduces irrelevant or conflicting content, particularly in visual RAG settings where visually similar but semantically incorrect evidence may be retrieved. To address this, we propose Multimodal Adaptive RAG (MMA-RAG), which dynamically assesses the confidence in the internal knowledge of the model to decide whether to incorporate the retrieved external information into the generation process. Central to MMA-RAG is a decision classifier trained through a layer-wise analysis, which leverages joint internal visual and textual representations to guide the use of reverse image retrieval. Experiments demonstrated that the model achieves a significant improvement in response performance in three VQA datasets. Meanwhile, ablation studies highlighted the importance of internal representations in adaptive retrieval decisions. In general, the experimental results demonstrated that MMA-RAG effectively balances external knowledge utilization and inference robustness in diverse multimodal scenarios.

AI.tldr: MMA-RAG adaptively decides whether to incorporate retrieved external knowledge in multimodal VQA by a decision classifier trained on joint internal visual-text representations, improving performance and robustness across datasets; ablations show internal representations are crucial for adaptive retrieval.
AI.motivation: Address hallucinations in VQA by avoiding irrelevant or conflicting external content when the model's internal knowledge is deemed reliable, enabling selective use of external information.
AI.method: Train a layer-wise decision classifier that uses joint internal visual and textual representations to gauge confidence in the model's internal knowledge and control the use of reverse image retrieval; integrate retrieved information only when confidence warrants it.
AI.result: Significant improvements in response quality across three VQA datasets; ablation studies confirm the importance of internal representations for adaptive retrieval decisions.
AI.conclusion: MMA-RAG effectively balances external knowledge usage with inference robustness in multimodal VQA, demonstrating that adaptive retrieval guided by internal representations enhances performance.

title: Learning Structured Reasoning via Tractable Trajectory Control
summary: Large language models can exhibit emergent reasoning behaviors, often manifested as recurring lexical patterns (e.g., "wait," indicating verification). However, complex reasoning trajectories remain sparse in unconstrained sampling, and standard RL often fails to guarantee the acquisition of diverse reasoning behaviors. We propose a systematic discovery and reinforcement of diverse reasoning patterns through structured reasoning, a paradigm that requires targeted exploration of specific reasoning patterns during the RL process. To this end, we propose Ctrl-R, a framework for learning structured reasoning via tractable trajectory control that actively guides the rollout process, incentivizing the exploration of diverse reasoning patterns that are critical for complex problem-solving. The resulting behavior policy enables accurate importance-sampling estimation, supporting unbiased on-policy optimization. We further introduce a power-scaling factor on the importance-sampling weights, allowing the policy to selectively learn from exploratory, out-of-distribution trajectories while maintaining stable optimization. Experiments demonstrate that Ctrl-R enables effective exploration and internalization of previously unattainable reasoning patterns, yielding consistent improvements across language and vision-language models on mathematical reasoning tasks.

AI.tldr: Ctrl-R introduces a structured RL framework that guides LLM reasoning trajectories to diversify and internalize reasoning patterns, using tractable trajectory control and power-scaled importance sampling to enable unbiased on-policy optimization.
AI.motivation: Emergent reasoning in large language models is sparse; unconstrained sampling misses diverse reasoning paths, and standard RL often fails to guarantee coverage of complex reasoning steps.
AI.method: Ctrl-R learns structured reasoning via tractable trajectory control, actively guiding rollouts to encourage diverse reasoning patterns; employs importance sampling for unbiased on-policy optimization and introduces a power-scaling factor on IS weights to balance exploration of exploratory, out-of-distribution trajectories with stable optimization.
AI.result: Experiments show Ctrl-R enables exploration and internalization of previously unattainable reasoning patterns, yielding consistent improvements across language and vision-language models on mathematical reasoning tasks.
AI.conclusion: Ctrl-R provides a principled framework for discovering and reinforcing diverse reasoning strategies through targeted trajectory control, with stable optimization enabled by power-scaled importance sampling, leading to improved mathematical reasoning performance.

title: Rethinking Camera Choice: An Empirical Study on Fisheye Camera Properties in Robotic Manipulation
summary: The adoption of fisheye cameras in robotic manipulation, driven by their exceptionally wide Field of View (FoV), is rapidly outpacing a systematic understanding of their downstream effects on policy learning. This paper presents the first comprehensive empirical study to bridge this gap, rigorously analyzing the properties of wrist-mounted fisheye cameras for imitation learning. Through extensive experiments in both simulation and the real world, we investigate three critical research questions: spatial localization, scene generalization, and hardware generalization. Our investigation reveals that: (1) The wide FoV significantly enhances spatial localization, but this benefit is critically contingent on the visual complexity of the environment. (2) Fisheye-trained policies, while prone to overfitting in simple scenes, unlock superior scene generalization when trained with sufficient environmental diversity. (3) While naive cross-camera transfer leads to failures, we identify the root cause as scale overfitting and demonstrate that hardware generalization performance can be improved with a simple Random Scale Augmentation (RSA) strategy. Collectively, our findings provide concrete, actionable guidance for the large-scale collection and effective use of fisheye datasets in robotic learning. More results and videos are available on https://robo-fisheye.github.io/

AI.tldr: A comprehensive empirical study on wrist-mounted fisheye cameras for imitation learning, revealing how FoV affects localization, generalization, and cross-camera transfer, and proposing Random Scale Augmentation (RSA) to improve hardware generalization.
AI.motivation: To systematically understand downstream effects of fisheye cameras on policy learning, addressing a gap in robotic learning with wide-field data.
AI.method: Simulation and real-world experiments investigating three questions—spatial localization, scene generalization, and hardware generalization; analysis of cross-camera transfer; introduction of RSA augmentation to mitigate scale overfitting.
AI.result: Wide FoV improves spatial localization but depends on environmental visual complexity; fisheye-trained policies overfit in simple scenes but generalize with diverse environments; naive cross-camera transfer fails due to scale overfitting, RSA improves hardware generalization; provides actionable guidelines for fisheye dataset collection and use.
AI.conclusion: The study offers concrete guidance for leveraging fisheye data in robotic learning and highlights RSA as a simple, effective approach to improve cross-camera robustness; supplementary results available on the project site.

title: A Gauge Theory of Superposition: Toward a Sheaf-Theoretic Atlas of Neural Representations
summary:
We develop a discrete gauge-theoretic framework for superposition in large language models (LLMs) that replaces the single-global-dictionary premise with a sheaf-theoretic atlas of local semantic charts. Contexts are clustered into a stratified context complex; each chart carries a local feature space and a local information-geometric metric (Fisher/Gauss--Newton) identifying predictively consequential feature interactions. This yields a Fisher-weighted interference energy and three measurable obstructions to global interpretability: (O1) local jamming (active load exceeds Fisher bandwidth), (O2) proxy shearing (mismatch between geometric transport and a fixed correspondence proxy), and (O3) nontrivial holonomy (path-dependent transport around loops).
  We prove and instantiate four results on a frozen open LLM (Llama~3.2~3B Instruct) using WikiText-103, a C4-derived English web-text subset, and \texttt{the-stack-smol}. (A) After constructive gauge fixing on a spanning tree, each chord residual equals the holonomy of its fundamental cycle, making holonomy computable and gauge-invariant. (B) Shearing lower-bounds a data-dependent transfer mismatch energy, turning $D_{\mathrm{shear}}$ into an unavoidable failure bound. (C) We obtain non-vacuous certified jamming/interference bounds with high coverage and zero violations across seeds/hyperparameters. (D) Bootstrap and sample-size experiments show stable estimation of $D_{\mathrm{shear}}$ and $D_{\mathrm{hol}}$, with improved concentration on well-conditioned subsystems.

AI.tldr: This work introduces a discrete gauge-theoretic, sheaf-based framework for interpreting large language models by replacing a single global dictionary with an atlas of local semantic charts. It defines a stratified context complex with local feature spaces and Fisher/Gauss–Newton metrics, yielding a Fisher-weighted interference energy and three measurable obstructions to global interpretability (local jamming, proxy shearing, nontrivial holonomy). The authors prove gauge-invariant holonomy after spanning-tree gauge fixing, derive lower bounds from shearing, and report certified bounds and stable estimations from experiments on an open LLM across standard corpora.
AI.motivation: Motivated by the limitation of a single global dictionary for semantic interpretation in LLMs, the paper seeks a local, geometry-informed framework (sheaf-theoretic atlas) to track information flow and interactions across contexts, enabling principled, computable diagnostics of global interpretability.
AI.method: Construct a stratified context complex with contexts clustered into charts; assign a local feature space and a local information-geometric metric (Fisher/Gauss–Newton); define a Fisher-weighted interference energy and three obstructions to global interpretability (O1–O3); perform constructive gauge fixing on a spanning tree, show that chord residual equals holonomy of fundamental cycles; derive a data-dependent lower bound D_shear on transfer mismatch; establish non-vacuous, certified bounds on jamming/interference; conduct bootstrap and subsampling experiments to assess estimation of D_shear and D_hol on Llama-3.2-3B Instruct using WikiText-103, C4-derived English web-text subset, and the-stack-smol.
AI.result: (A) After gauge fixing on a spanning tree, chord residuals equal the holonomy of their fundamental cycles, making holonomy computable and gauge-invariant. (B) Shearing lower-bounds a data-dependent transfer-mismatch energy D_shear, providing a universal lower bound on failure. (C) Non-vacuous certified jamming/interference bounds with high coverage and zero violations across seeds/hyperparameters. (D) Bootstrap and sampling show stable estimation of D_shear and D_hol, with better concentration on well-conditioned subsystems.
AI.conclusion: The framework yields computable, gauge-invariant diagnostics linking local geometry to global interpretability in LLMs, with provable obstructions and bounds validated empirically on multiple corpora and model subsets; it enables principled, local-to-global interpretability analysis and suggests robustness improvements for LLMs through geometry-aware diagnostics.

title: Wavelet-based Frame Selection by Detecting Semantic Boundary for Long Video Understanding
summary: Frame selection is crucial due to high frame redundancy and limited context windows when applying Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) to long videos. Current methods typically select frames with high relevance to a given query, resulting in a disjointed set of frames that disregard the narrative structure of video. In this paper, we introduce Wavelet-based Frame Selection by Detecting Semantic Boundary (WFS-SB), a training-free framework that presents a new perspective: effective video understanding hinges not only on high relevance but, more importantly, on capturing semantic shifts - pivotal moments of narrative change that are essential to comprehending the holistic storyline of video. However, direct detection of abrupt changes in the query-frame similarity signal is often unreliable due to high-frequency noise arising from model uncertainty and transient visual variations. To address this, we leverage the wavelet transform, which provides an ideal solution through its multi-resolution analysis in both time and frequency domains. By applying this transform, we decompose the noisy signal into multiple scales and extract a clean semantic change signal from the coarsest scale. We identify the local extrema of this signal as semantic boundaries, which segment the video into coherent clips. Building on this, WFS-SB comprises a two-stage strategy: first, adaptively allocating a frame budget to each clip based on a composite importance score; and second, within each clip, employing the Maximal Marginal Relevance approach to select a diverse yet relevant set of frames. Extensive experiments show that WFS-SB significantly boosts LVLM performance, e.g., improving accuracy by 5.5% on VideoMME, 9.5% on MLVU, and 6.2% on LongVideoBench, consistently outperforming state-of-the-art methods.

AI.tldr: A training-free wavelet-based approach (WFS-SB) detects semantic boundaries in long videos to guide LVLM frame selection, improving narrative coherence and accuracy over state-of-the-art methods.
AI.motivation: Frame redundancy and limited context windows in LVLMs hinder long-video understanding. Relevance-based frame selection ignores narrative structure, leading to disjointed summaries. Semantic shifts are pivotal for coherent storytelling.
AI.method: Two-stage framework. (1) Use wavelet transform on query-frame similarity to denoise and extract a multi-scale semantic-change signal; identify local extrema on the coarsest scale as semantic boundaries to segment video into clips. (2) Allocate frame budget per clip via a composite importance score; within each clip, apply Maximal Marginal Relevance (MMR) to select a diverse, relevant subset of frames. Training-free and emphasizes narrative dynamics.
AI.result: Empirical results show consistent gains: +5.5% accuracy on VideoMME, +9.5% on MLVU, and +6.2% on LongVideoBench over state-of-the-art methods, indicating improved LVLM performance for long videos.
AI.conclusion: Capturing semantic boundaries through wavelet-based multi-resolution analysis and combining adaptive budgeting with MMR yields robust, narrative-aware frame selection that enhances LVLM understanding of long videos.

title: CeProAgents: A Hierarchical Agents System for Automated Chemical Process Development
summary: The development of chemical processes, a cornerstone of chemical engineering, presents formidable challenges due to its multi-faceted nature, integrating specialized knowledge, conceptual design, and parametric simulation. Capitalizing on this, we propose CeProAgents, a hierarchical multi-agent system designed to automate the development of chemical process through collaborative division of labor. Our architecture comprises three specialized agent cohorts focused on knowledge, concept, and parameter respectively. To effectively adapt to the inherent complexity of chemical tasks, each cohort employs a novel hybrid architecture that integrates dynamic agent chatgroups with structured agentic workflows. To rigorously evaluate the system, we establish CeProBench, a multi-dimensional benchmark structured around three core pillars of chemical engineering. We design six distinct types of tasks across these dimensions to holistically assess the comprehensive capabilities of the system in chemical process development. The results not only confirm the effectiveness and superiority of our proposed approach but also reveal the transformative potential as well as the current boundaries of Large Language Models (LLMs) for industrial chemical engineering.

AI.tldr: Proposes CeProAgents, a hierarchical multi-agent system for automated chemical process development, with knowledge/concept/parameter cohorts, and a novel hybrid architecture of dynamic chatgroups plus structured workflows; introduces CeProBench benchmark with six task types; reports effectiveness and provides insights into the capabilities and limits of LLMs in industrial chemical engineering.
AI.motivation: Address the inherent complexity of chemical process development, which requires multi-domain knowledge, conceptual design, and parametric simulation, and establish a rigorous evaluation framework for LLMs in industrial chemical engineering.
AI.method: Three specialized agent cohorts (knowledge, concept, parameter) employing a hybrid architecture that fuses dynamic agent chatgroups with structured agentic workflows; CeProBench benchmark with six task types across three core pillars of chemical engineering; empirical evaluation to assess system capabilities.
AI.result: Empirical results demonstrate the effectiveness and superiority of CeProAgents; the study also reveals the transformative potential as well as current boundaries of LLMs for industrial chemical engineering.
AI.conclusion: CeProAgents and CeProBench offer a scalable path for automated chemical process development, validating a collaborative multi-agent design and providing a holistic benchmark to gauge LLM capabilities while highlighting remaining gaps and future directions.

title: DriveCode: Domain Specific Numerical Encoding for LLM-Based Autonomous Driving
comment: The project page is available at https://shiftwilliam.github.io/DriveCode
summary: Large language models (LLMs) have shown great promise for autonomous driving. However, discretizing numbers into tokens limits precise numerical reasoning, fails to reflect the positional significance of digits in the training objective, and makes it difficult to achieve both decoding efficiency and numerical precision. These limitations affect both the processing of sensor measurements and the generation of precise control commands, creating a fundamental barrier for deploying LLM-based autonomous driving systems. In this paper, we introduce DriveCode, a novel numerical encoding method that represents numbers as dedicated embeddings rather than discrete text tokens. DriveCode employs a number projector to map numbers into the language model's hidden space, enabling seamless integration with visual and textual features in a unified multimodal sequence. Evaluated on OmniDrive, DriveGPT4, and DriveGPT4-V2 datasets, DriveCode demonstrates superior performance in trajectory prediction and control signal generation, confirming its effectiveness for LLM-based autonomous driving systems.

AI.tldr: DriveCode introduces dedicated numerical embeddings to replace discrete tokens, enabling precise numeric reasoning for LLM-based autonomous driving and improving trajectory and control performance.
AI.motivation: Conventional token-based LLMs discretize numbers, losing numeric precision and positional digit significance, hindering sensor interpretation and control command generation; this creates a barrier for end-to-end LLM-driven driving.
AI.method: A number projector maps numeric values into the LLM's hidden space as dedicated embeddings; integrated into a multimodal sequence with visual and textual features, enabling seamless processing of sensor data and commands.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on OmniDrive, DriveGPT4, and DriveGPT4-V2 shows superior trajectory prediction and control signal generation compared to baselines, validating numerical encoding's effectiveness for autonomous driving.
AI.conclusion: DriveCode provides a viable path to precise numerical reasoning in LLM-based autonomous driving and can enhance end-to-end perception, planning, and control through unified multimodal representations.

title: GeMi: A Graph-based, Multimodal Recommendation System for Narrative Scroll Paintings
summary: Recommendation Systems are effective in managing the ever-increasing amount of multimodal data available today and help users discover interesting new items. These systems can handle various media types such as images, text, audio, and video data, and this has made it possible to handle content-based recommendation utilizing features extracted from items while also incorporating user preferences. Graph Neural Network (GNN)-based recommendation systems are a special class of recommendation systems that can handle relationships between items and users, making them particularly attractive for content-based recommendations. Their popularity also stems from the fact that they use advanced machine learning techniques, such as deep learning on graph-structured data, to exploit user-to-item interactions. The nodes in the graph can access higher-order neighbor information along with state-of-the-art vision-language models for processing multimodal content, and there are well-designed algorithms for embedding, message passing, and propagation. In this work, we present the design of a GNN-based recommendation system on a novel data set collected from field research. Designed for an endangered performing art form, the recommendation system uses multimodal content (text and image data) to suggest similar paintings for viewing and purchase. To the best of our knowledge, there is no recommendation system designed for narrative scroll paintings -- our work therefore serves several purposes, including art conservation, a data storage system for endangered art objects, and a state-of-the-art recommendation system that leverages both the novel characteristics of the data and preferences of the user population interested in narrative scroll paintings.

AI.tldr: GNN-based recommender for narrative scroll paintings using multimodal (text+image) data on a field-collected dataset, aimed at art conservation and preservation.
AI.motivation: Address the need for effective recommendations over multimodal, graph-structured data and support endangered art forms by leveraging user preferences and higher-order neighbor information.
AI.method: Design of a graph neural network–based recommender using a novel multimodal dataset (text and image) of narrative scroll paintings; constructs a user-item graph, applies embedding, message passing, and propagation, potentially integrating vision-language features; serves as a data storage/ conservation tool and recommendation system.
AI.result: Not explicitly reported in the abstract; the work presents a design rather than empirical results on performance.
AI.conclusion: Aims to contribute to art conservation and data storage for endangered objects while advancing multimodal GNN-based recommendation for a niche art domain.

title: MLLM-4D: Towards Visual-based Spatial-Temporal Intelligence
summary: Humans are born with vision-based 4D spatial-temporal intelligence, which enables us to perceive and reason about the evolution of 3D space over time from purely visual inputs. Despite its importance, this capability remains a significant bottleneck for current multimodal large language models (MLLMs). To tackle this challenge, we introduce MLLM-4D, a comprehensive framework designed to bridge the gaps in training data curation and model post-training for spatiotemporal understanding and reasoning. On the data front, we develop a cost-efficient data curation pipeline that repurposes existing stereo video datasets into high-quality 4D spatiotemporal instructional data. This results in the MLLM4D-2M and MLLM4D-R1-30k datasets for Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) and Reinforcement Fine-Tuning (RFT), alongside MLLM4D-Bench for comprehensive evaluation. Regarding model training, our post-training strategy establishes a foundational 4D understanding via SFT and further catalyzes 4D reasoning capabilities by employing Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) with specialized Spatiotemporal Chain of Thought (ST-CoT) prompting and Spatiotemporal reward functions (ST-reward) without involving the modification of architecture. Extensive experiments demonstrate that MLLM-4D achieves state-of-the-art spatial-temporal understanding and reasoning capabilities from purely 2D RGB inputs. Project page: https://github.com/GVCLab/MLLM-4D.

AI.tldr: MLLM-4D introduces a data-and-training framework to endow LLM-based multimodal models with 4D spatiotemporal understanding from purely 2D inputs by repurposing stereo videos into instructional data and applying SFT followed by GRPO with Spatiotemporal CoT prompts and ST rewards, achieving state-of-the-art 4D reasoning and providing new datasets and benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Current multimodal LLMs struggle with 4D spatiotemporal understanding; there is a need for cost-effective data curation and post-training strategies that enable 4D reasoning without changing model architecture.
AI.method: 1) A data curation pipeline repurposing existing stereo video datasets into high-quality 4D instructional data, producing MLLM4D-2M (SFT) and MLLM4D-R1-30k (RFT) and MLLM4D-Bench for evaluation. 2) Post-training via supervised fine-tuning (SFT) to build 4D grounding, then Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) with Spatiotemporal Chain of Thought (ST-CoT) prompting and Spatiotemporal reward (ST-reward) functions, without architectural modification.
AI.result: Empirical evidence shows state-of-the-art performance in spatial-temporal understanding and reasoning from purely 2D RGB inputs.
AI.conclusion: The proposed data-centric and post-training framework effectively imparts 4D spatiotemporal capabilities to MLLMs, enabling robust 4D reasoning without changing the model architecture and providing ready-to-use datasets and benchmarks.

title: Chain-of-Context Learning: Dynamic Constraint Understanding for Multi-Task VRPs
comment: This paper is accepted by ICLR 2026
summary: Multi-task Vehicle Routing Problems (VRPs) aim to minimize routing costs while satisfying diverse constraints. Existing solvers typically adopt a unified reinforcement learning (RL) framework to learn generalizable patterns across tasks. However, they often overlook the constraint and node dynamics during the decision process, making the model fail to accurately react to the current context. To address this limitation, we propose Chain-of-Context Learning (CCL), a novel framework that progressively captures the evolving context to guide fine-grained node adaptation. Specifically, CCL constructs step-wise contextual information via a Relevance-Guided Context Reformulation (RGCR) module, which adaptively prioritizes salient constraints. This context then guides node updates through a Trajectory-Shared Node Re-embedding (TSNR) module, which aggregates shared node features from all trajectories' contexts and uses them to update inputs for the next step. By modeling evolving preferences of the RL agent, CCL captures step-by-step dependencies in sequential decision-making. We evaluate CCL on 48 diverse VRP variants, including 16 in-distribution and 32 out-of-distribution (with unseen constraints) tasks. Experimental results show that CCL performs favorably against the state-of-the-art baselines, achieving the best performance on all in-distribution tasks and the majority of out-of-distribution tasks.

AI.tldr: A new Chain-of-Context Learning (CCL) framework for multi-task VRPs that models evolving context to guide node updates, using RGCR for context reformulation and TSNR for trajectory-wide node re-embedding, achieving strong results across 48 VRP variants.
AI.motivation: Existing RL solvers for VRPs often ignore constraint and node dynamics during decision-making, limiting responsiveness to current context and task variation.
AI.method: CCL introduces two modules: RGCR (Relevance-Guided Context Reformulation) to prioritize salient constraints and construct step-wise context, and TSNR (Trajectory-Shared Node Re-embedding) to aggregate shared node features from all trajectories' contexts and update inputs for the next step, enabling step-by-step dependency modeling in sequential decisions. Evaluated on 48 VRP variants (16 in-distribution, 32 out-of-distribution).
AI.result: CCL outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, achieving best performance on all in-distribution tasks and the majority of out-of-distribution tasks.
AI.conclusion: Modeling evolving preferences through step-by-step context and shared node representations enhances adaptability and robustness for multi-task VRPs across diverse task distributions.

title: Navigating Time's Possibilities: Plausible Counterfactual Explanations for Multivariate Time-Series Forecast through Genetic Algorithms
summary: Counterfactual learning has become promising for understanding and modeling causality in complex and dynamic systems. This paper presents a novel method for counterfactual learning in the context of multivariate time series analysis and forecast. The primary objective is to uncover hidden causal relationships and identify potential interventions to achieve desired outcomes. The proposed methodology integrates genetic algorithms and rigorous causality tests to infer and validate counterfactual dependencies within temporal sequences. More specifically, we employ Granger causality to enhance the reliability of identified causal relationships, rigorously assessing their statistical significance. Then, genetic algorithms, in conjunction with quantile regression, are used to exploit these intricate causal relationships to project future scenarios. The synergy between genetic algorithms and causality tests ensures a thorough exploration of the temporal dynamics present in the data, revealing hidden dependencies and enabling the projection of outcomes under hypothetical interventions. We evaluate the performance of our algorithm on real-world data, showcasing its ability to handle complex causal relationships, revealing meaningful counterfactual insights, and allowing for the prediction of outcomes under hypothetical interventions.

AI.tldr: A novel counterfactual learning method for multivariate time series that combines Granger causality validation with genetic algorithms and quantile regression to uncover and project causal relationships under hypothetical interventions.
AI.motivation: Understand and model causality in complex, dynamic multivariate time series and identify interventions to achieve desired outcomes.
AI.method: Use Granger causality to validate causal links in temporal data; employ genetic algorithms to explore and optimize causal structures; apply quantile regression to project future scenarios under interventions; integrate these components to infer counterfactual dependencies and forecast under hypothetical changes.
AI.result: Claims the approach can handle complex causal relationships, reveal meaningful counterfactual insights, and predict outcomes under hypothetical interventions on real-world data.
AI.conclusion: The combination of genetic algorithms with causality tests enables thorough exploration of temporal dynamics, uncovering hidden dependencies and supporting counterfactual projections.

title: Vision-TTT: Efficient and Expressive Visual Representation Learning with Test-Time Training
summary: Learning efficient and expressive visual representation has long been the pursuit of computer vision research. While Vision Transformers (ViTs) gradually replace traditional Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) as more scalable vision learners, their applications are plagued by the quadratic complexity of the self-attention mechanism. To address the challenge, we introduce a new linear-time sequence modeling method Test-Time Training (TTT) into vision and propose Vision-TTT, which compresses the visual token sequence in a novel self-supervised learning manner. By incorporating bidirectional scan strategy and the Conv2d module, Vision-TTT effectively extends vanilla TTT to model 2D visual correlations with global receptive fields. Extensive experiments show that \texttt{Vittt-T/S/B} achieve 77.3%,81.2%,82.5% Top-1 accuracy on ImageNet classification and also greatly outperform their counterparts on downstream tasks. At 1280x1280 resolution, \texttt{Vittt-T} reduces FLOPs by 79.4% and runs 4.38x faster with 88.9% less memory than DeiT-T. These results demonstrate the expressiveness and efficiency of Vision-TTT as a strong candidate for the next-generation generic visual backbone.

AI.tldr: Vision-TTT introduces a linear-time sequence model for vision, using Test-Time Training with a bidirectional scan and Conv2d to extend TTT to 2D visuals. It reduces computation and memory while achieving strong ImageNet and downstream task performance, outperforming DeiT-T at high resolutions.
AI.motivation: Mitigate the quadratic complexity of self-attention in Vision Transformers and enable efficient, scalable 2D visual modeling with global receptive fields through a linear-time framework.
AI.method: Proposes Vision-TTT, applying Test-Time Training in a self-supervised manner. Uses a bidirectional scan strategy and Conv2d to compress the visual token sequence, extending vanilla TTT to capture 2D correlations with global receptive fields.
AI.result: Vittt-T/S/B models achieve 77.3%, 81.2%, 82.5% Top-1 on ImageNet. They outperform baselines on downstream tasks. At 1280x1280, Vittt-T reduces FLOPs by 79.4%, runs 4.38x faster, and uses 88.9% less memory than DeiT-T.
AI.conclusion: Vision-TTT offers a lightweight, expressive alternative to standard ViTs, combining efficiency with strong performance, making it a competitive next-generation visual backbone.

title: FT-Dojo: Towards Autonomous LLM Fine-Tuning with Language Agents
summary: Fine-tuning large language models for vertical domains remains a labor-intensive and expensive process, requiring domain experts to curate data, configure training, and iteratively diagnose model behavior. Despite growing interest in autonomous machine learning, no prior work has tackled end-to-end LLM fine-tuning with agents. Can LLM-based agents automate this complete process? We frame this as a substantially open problem: agents must navigate an open-ended search space spanning data curation from diverse data sources, processing with complex tools, building a training pipeline, and iteratively refining their approach based on evaluation outcomes in rapidly growing logs--an overall scenario far more intricate than existing benchmarks. To study this question, we introduce FT-Dojo, an interactive environment comprising 13 tasks across 5 domains. We further develop FT-Agent, an autonomous system that mirrors human experts by leveraging evaluation-driven feedback to iteratively diagnose failures and refine fine-tuning strategies. Experiments on FT-Dojo demonstrate that purpose-built fine-tuning agents significantly outperform general-purpose alternatives, with FT-Agent achieving the best performance on 10 out of 13 tasks across all five domains. Ablations show that the approach generalizes effectively to 3B models, with additional insights on data scaling trade-offs and backbone sensitivity. Case analyses reveal that agents can recover from failures through cumulative learning from historical experience, while also exposing fundamental limitations in causal reasoning--highlighting both the promise and current boundaries of autonomous LLM fine-tuning.

AI.tldr: Autonomous LLM fine-tuning via specialized agents in FT-Dojo substantially improves performance over generic agents, with notable cross-domain generalization, but reveals limits in causal reasoning and data-efficiency.
AI.motivation: Tackle the labor-intensive and expensive process of fine-tuning LLMs in vertical domains by automating data curation, tool use, training pipeline construction, and iterative refinement through evaluation feedback.
AI.method: Introduce FT-Dojo, an interactive environment with 13 tasks across 5 domains, and FT-Agent, an autonomous system that leverages evaluation-driven feedback to iteratively diagnose failures and refine fine-tuning strategies. Conduct experiments on FT-Dojo, ablations on 3B models, analyze data scaling, backbone sensitivity, and provide case analyses of cumulative learning and causal reasoning limits.
AI.result: FT-Agent significantly outperforms general-purpose alternatives, achieving the best performance on 10 of 13 tasks across all five domains. Ablations show generalization to 3B models; insights into data scaling trade-offs and backbone sensitivity. Case analyses indicate agents can recover from failures via cumulative learning from history, but reveal fundamental limitations in causal reasoning.
AI.conclusion: Autonomous, evaluation-driven fine-tuning of LLMs is promising and yields strong cross-domain gains, yet current systems face limits in causal reasoning and data efficiency, indicating clear directions for future work to bolster robust, scalable autonomous fine-tuning.

title: Monocular 3D Object Position Estimation with VLMs for Human-Robot Interaction
comment: Accepted at Workshop on Integrating Image Processing with Large-Scale Language/Vision Models for Advanced Visual Understanding (LVLM) at IEEE International Conference on Image Processing (ICIP) 2025
summary: Pre-trained general-purpose Vision-Language Models (VLM) hold the potential to enhance intuitive human-machine interactions due to their rich world knowledge and 2D object detection capabilities. However, VLMs for 3D coordinates detection tasks are rare. In this work, we investigate interactive abilities of VLMs by returning 3D object positions given a monocular RGB image from a wrist-mounted camera, natural language input, and robot states. We collected and curated a heterogeneous dataset of more than 100,000 images and finetuned a VLM using QLoRA with a custom regression head. By implementing conditional routing, our model maintains its ability to process general visual queries while adding specialized 3D position estimation capabilities. Our results demonstrate robust predictive performance with a median MAE of 13 mm on the test set and a five-fold improvement over a simpler baseline without finetuning. In about 25% of the cases, predictions are within a range considered acceptable for the robot to interact with objects.

AI.tldr: Fine-tunes a Vision-Language Model with QLoRA to predict 3D object positions from monocular RGB images, yielding MAE 13 mm and ~25% usable cases; uses conditional routing to preserve general VLM abilities.
AI.motivation: Bridge the gap between VLMs' 2D/object knowledge and 3D coordinate estimation for interactive robot tasks; enable natural language + robot state-driven 3D localization from a wrist-mounted camera.
AI.method: Collect >100k heterogeneous images; finetune a VLM using QLoRA with a custom regression head; implement conditional routing to maintain general visual query processing while adding 3D position estimation; inputs include monocular RGB image from a wrist-mounted camera, natural language input, and robot states; outputs are 3D coordinates.
AI.result: Median MAE of 13 mm on the test set; five-fold improvement over a simpler baseline without finetuning; ~25% of predictions fall within an acceptable range for robot interaction with objects.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates that VLMs can be extended to 3D coordinate estimation with limited fine-tuning while preserving general multimodal capabilities; the approach shows promise for robot–vision interactions, though results are dataset-dependent and real-world deployment requires robustness analyses.

title: Jano: Adaptive Diffusion Generation with Early-stage Convergence Awareness
summary: Diffusion models have achieved remarkable success in generative AI, yet their computational efficiency remains a significant challenge, particularly for Diffusion Transformers (DiTs) requiring intensive full-attention computation. While existing acceleration approaches focus on content-agnostic uniform optimization strategies, we observe that different regions in generated content exhibit heterogeneous convergence patterns during the denoising process. We present Jano, a training-free framework that leverages this insight for efficient region-aware generation. Jano introduces an early-stage complexity recognition algorithm that accurately identifies regional convergence requirements within initial denoising steps, coupled with an adaptive token scheduling runtime that optimizes computational resource allocation. Through comprehensive evaluation on state-of-the-art models, Jano achieves substantial acceleration (average 2.0 times speedup, up to 2.4 times) while preserving generation quality. Our work challenges conventional uniform processing assumptions and provides a practical solution for accelerating large-scale content generation. The source code of our implementation is available at https://github.com/chen-yy20/Jano.

AI.tldr: A training-free region-aware acceleration method for diffusion transformers (Jano) that identifies regional convergence early and adaptively schedules tokens to accelerate generation without sacrificing quality.
AI.motivation: Diffusion models, especially Diffusion Transformers (DiTs), are computationally intensive due to full-attention. Existing optimizations are content-agnostic; there is a need to exploit heterogeneous denoising dynamics across regions.
AI.method: Proposes an early-stage complexity recognition algorithm that detects regional convergence requirements in the initial denoising steps, and an adaptive token scheduling runtime that reallocates compute accordingly. The framework is training-free and evaluated on state-of-the-art DiTs.
AI.result: Jano achieves substantial acceleration with preserved generation quality: average 2.0x speedup and up to 2.4x. Demonstrated across multiple state-of-the-art models; source code released.
AI.conclusion: Challenges the assumption of uniform processing in diffusion generation, offering a practical, training-free solution for region-aware acceleration in large-scale content generation.

title: GMP: A Benchmark for Content Moderation under Co-occurring Violations and Dynamic Rules
summary: Online content moderation is essential for maintaining a healthy digital environment, and reliance on AI for this task continues to grow. Consider a user comment using national stereotypes to insult a politician. This example illustrates two critical challenges in real-world scenarios: (1) Co-occurring Violations, where a single post violates multiple policies (e.g., prejudice and personal attacks); (2) Dynamic rules of moderation, where determination of a violation depends on platform-specific guidelines that evolve across contexts . The intersection of co-occurring harms and dynamically changing rules highlights a core limitation of current AI systems: although large language models (LLMs) are adept at following fixed guidelines, their judgment capabilities degrade when policies are unstable or context-dependent . In practice, such shortcomings lead to inconsistent moderation: either erroneously restricting legitimate expression or allowing harmful content to remain online . This raises a critical question for evaluation: Does high performance on existing static benchmarks truly guarantee robust generalization of AI judgment to real-world scenarios involving co-occurring violations and dynamically changing rules?

AI.tldr: Static benchmark performance may not generalize to real-world moderation where posts exhibit co-occurring violations and rules are dynamic; raises need for evaluation frameworks that capture policy overlap and context dependence.
AI.motivation: Moderation systems must handle multiple simultaneous policy breaches and evolving platform-specific guidelines; current LLMs perform well under fixed rules but struggle when judgments depend on unstable, context-dependent policies; real-world deployment faces inconsistent moderation.
AI.method: Conceptual analysis framing two challenges (co-occurring violations; dynamic rules) and argument for evaluating generalization; proposes reframing evaluation to test cross-context consistency and policy adaptation rather than relying on fixed benchmarks.
AI.result: No empirical results are presented; identifies core limitations and questions about generalization of AI judgment from static benchmarks to real-world, dynamic contexts.
AI.conclusion: Highlights the need to rethink evaluation of AI moderation to ensure robustness under co-occurring harms and evolving rules; warns that high static-benchmark performance may not imply robust, context-sensitive judgments in practice.

title: Active Flow Matching
summary: Discrete diffusion and flow matching models capture complex, non-additive and non-autoregressive structure in high-dimensional objective landscapes through parallel, iterative refinement. However, their implicit generative nature precludes direct integration with principled variational frameworks for online black-box optimisation, such as variational search distributions (VSD) and conditioning by adaptive sampling (CbAS). We introduce Active Flow Matching (AFM), which reformulates variational objectives to operate on conditional endpoint distributions along the flow, enabling gradient-based steering of flow models toward high-fitness regions while preserving the rigour of VSD and CbAS. We derive forward and reverse Kullback-Leibler (KL) variants using self-normalised importance sampling. Across a suite of online protein and small molecule design tasks, forward-KL AFM consistently performs competitively compared to state-of-the-art baselines, demonstrating effective exploration-exploitation under tight experimental budgets.

AI.tldr: AFM reframes flow-based design with variational objectives on conditional endpoints, enabling gradient-guided steering within VSD/CbAS frameworks; forward-KL AFM learns to target high-fitness regions under budgets.
