AI.motivation: Bridge discrete diffusion/flow matching models with principled variational online black-box optimization (VSD and CbAS) by enabling variational objectives on the flow endpoints to guide search.
AI.method: Formulate variational objectives on conditional endpoint distributions along the flow; derive forward and reverse KL variants via self-normalised importance sampling; enable gradient-based steering of flow models toward high-fitness regions while preserving VSD/CbAS rigor.
AI.result: Across online protein and small-molecule design tasks, forward-KL AFM yields competitive performance against state-of-the-art baselines, demonstrating effective exploration-exploitation under tight experimental budgets.
AI.conclusion: AFM provides a principled variational framework for flow-based models in online design, enabling controlled exploration without sacrificing the theoretical guarantees of VSD and CbAS.

title: Mesh-Pro: Asynchronous Advantage-guided Ranking Preference Optimization for Artist-style Quadrilateral Mesh Generation
summary: Reinforcement learning (RL) has demonstrated remarkable success in text and image generation, yet its potential in 3D generation remains largely unexplored. Existing attempts typically rely on offline direct preference optimization (DPO) method, which suffers from low training efficiency and limited generalization. In this work, we aim to enhance both the training efficiency and generation quality of RL in 3D mesh generation. Specifically, (1) we design the first asynchronous online RL framework tailored for 3D mesh generation post-training efficiency improvement, which is 3.75$\times$ faster than synchronous RL. (2) We propose Advantage-guided Ranking Preference Optimization (ARPO), a novel RL algorithm that achieves a better trade-off between training efficiency and generalization than current RL algorithms designed for 3D mesh generation, such as DPO and group relative policy optimization (GRPO). (3) Based on asynchronous ARPO, we propose Mesh-Pro, which additionally introduces a novel diagonal-aware mixed triangular-quadrilateral tokenization for mesh representation and a ray-based reward for geometric integrity. Mesh-Pro achieves state-of-the-art performance on artistic and dense meshes.

AI.tldr: Mesh-Pro: asynchronous online RL for 3D mesh generation with ARPO; faster training (3.75x) and state-of-the-art results via diagonal-aware tokenization and ray-based rewards.
AI.motivation: Current RL approaches for 3D generation rely on offline direct preference optimization (DPO), suffering from poor training efficiency and limited generalization; there is a need for online, efficient RL with geometry-aware rewards.
AI.method: Introduce an asynchronous online RL framework for 3D meshes; propose Advantage-guided Ranking Preference Optimization (ARPO); build Mesh-Pro with diagonal-aware mixed triangular-quadrilateral tokenization and a ray-based reward to ensure geometric integrity.
AI.result: Training speed improved by 3.75x over synchronous RL; ARPO provides a better efficiency-generalization trade-off than DPO and GRPO; Mesh-Pro achieves state-of-the-art performance on artistic and dense meshes.
AI.conclusion: Asynchronous ARPO-based RL with geometry-aware tokenization and ray-based rewards substantially improves efficiency and quality in 3D mesh generation, setting a strong practical baseline.

title: GAM-RAG: Gain-Adaptive Memory for Evolving Retrieval in Retrieval-Augmented Generation
summary: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) grounds large language models with external evidence, but many implementations rely on pre-built indices that remain static after construction. Related queries therefore repeat similar multi-hop traversal, increasing latency and compute. Motivated by schema-based learning in cognitive neuroscience, we propose GAM-RAG, a training-free framework that accumulates retrieval experience from recurring or related queries and updates retrieval memory over time. GAM-RAG builds a lightweight, relation-free hierarchical index whose links capture potential co-occurrence rather than fixed semantic relations. During inference, successful retrieval episodes provide sentence-level feedback, updating sentence memories so evidence useful for similar reasoning types becomes easier to activate later. To balance stability and adaptability under noisy feedback, we introduce an uncertainty-aware, Kalman-inspired gain rule that jointly updates memory states and perplexity-based uncertainty estimates. It applies fast updates for reliable novel signals and conservative refinement for stable or noisy memories. We provide a theoretical analysis of the update dynamics, and empirically show that GAM-RAG improves average performance by 3.95% over the strongest baseline and by 8.19% with 5-turn memory, while reducing inference cost by 61%. Our code and datasets are available at: https://anonymous.4open.science/r/GAM_RAG-2EF6.

AI.tldr: GAM-RAG is a training-free, memory-augmented RAG system that learns from recurring queries using a relation-free hierarchical index and Kalman-inspired uncertainty updates to memory; it improves accuracy and cuts inference cost.
AI.motivation: RAG systems typically rely on static indices that cause repetitive multi-hop traversals and high latency; there is a need for evolving retrieval memory that adapts with recurring queries inspired by schema-based learning in neuroscience.
AI.method: Construct a lightweight, relation-free hierarchical index whose links reflect co-occurrence patterns rather than fixed semantic relations; during inference, use successful retrieval episodes to update sentence-level memories; apply an uncertainty-aware, Kalman-inspired gain rule to jointly update memory states and perplexity-based uncertainty estimates, enabling fast updates for reliable signals and conservative updates for noisy memories.
AI.result: Average performance gains of 3.95% over the strongest baseline, 8.19% improvement with 5-turn memory; inference cost reduced by 61%.
AI.conclusion: GAM-RAG offers a training-free mechanism to accumulate retrieval experience and adapt retrieval memory, with theoretical analysis of update dynamics and empirical gains; code and data are provided.

title: Integrating LTL Constraints into PPO for Safe Reinforcement Learning
summary: This paper proposes Proximal Policy Optimization with Linear Temporal Logic Constraints (PPO-LTL), a framework that integrates safety constraints written in LTL into PPO for safe reinforcement learning. LTL constraints offer rigorous representations of complex safety requirements, such as regulations that broadly exist in robotics, enabling systematic monitoring of safety requirements. Violations against LTL constraints are monitored by limit-deterministic Büchi automata, and then translated by a logic-to-cost mechanism into penalty signals. The signals are further employed for guiding the policy optimization via the Lagrangian scheme. Extensive experiments on the Zones and CARLA environments show that our PPO-LTL can consistently reduce safety violations, while maintaining competitive performance, against the state-of-the-art methods. The code is at https://github.com/EVIEHub/PPO-LTL.

AI.tldr: PPO-LTL introduces a framework that embeds linear temporal logic (LTL) safety constraints into Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) for safe reinforcement learning, using limit-deterministic Büchi automata to monitor violations and a logic-to-cost translation into penalties guided by a Lagrangian; validated in Zones and CARLA with reduced safety violations and competitive performance; code released.
AI.motivation: There is a need to enforce expressive, verifiable safety requirements in reinforcement learning for robotics and autonomous driving. LTL offers rigorous safety specifications, but integrating them into sample-efficient RL training with formal monitoring is challenging; the work aims to bridge this gap.
AI.method: Safety constraints are written in LTL and monitored via limit-deterministic Büchi automata. Violations are translated by a logic-to-cost mechanism into penalty signals, which are incorporated into a Lagrangian-based PPO optimization to steer policy updates away from unsafe behaviors.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation in Zone and CARLA environments shows PPO-LTL reduces safety violations while maintaining competitive task performance compared to state-of-the-art methods.
AI.conclusion: The framework demonstrates that formal LTL safety constraints can be effectively integrated into PPO through automata-based monitoring and a logic-to-cost penalty, yielding safer policies without substantial performance loss; code is available at the linked repository.

title: Knowledge without Wisdom: Measuring Misalignment between LLMs and Intended Impact
summary: LLMs increasingly excel on AI benchmarks, but doing so does not guarantee validity for downstream tasks. This study evaluates the performance of leading foundation models (FMs, i.e., generative pre-trained base LLMs) with out-of-distribution (OOD) tasks of the teaching and learning of schoolchildren. Across all FMs, inter-model behaviors on disparate tasks correlate higher than they do with expert human behaviors on target tasks. These biases shared across LLMs are poorly aligned with downstream measures of teaching quality and often \textit{negatively aligned with learning outcomes}. Further, we find multi-model ensembles, both unanimous model voting and expert-weighting by benchmark performance, further exacerbate misalignment with learning. We measure that 50\% of the variation in misalignment error is shared across foundation models, suggesting that common pretraining accounts for much of the misalignment in these tasks. We demonstrate methods for robustly measuring alignment of complex tasks and provide unique insights into both educational applications of foundation models and to understanding limitations of models.

AI.tldr: Cross-model biases in foundation models align poorly with downstream educational outcomes; ensemble methods worsen misalignment. Shared pretraining explains much of this misalignment; robust alignment measurement is feasible and reveals educational limitations of LLMs.
AI.motivation: To test whether state-of-the-art foundation models, tuned on benchmarks, produce valid support for downstream, out-of-distribution educational tasks involving teaching and learning of schoolchildren.
AI.method: Evaluate leading foundation models on OOD educational tasks; assess alignment with teaching quality and learning outcomes; analyze inter-model correlations across tasks; compare unanimous voting and expert-weighted ensembles; quantify shared variance across models.
AI.result: Inter-model correlations exceed correlations with human target-task performance; shared biases across LMs poorly align and often negatively affect learning outcomes; ensembles exacerbate misalignment; ~50% of misalignment variance is shared across models, implicating common pretraining; introduces methods for robust alignment measurement and provides educational insights and model limitations.
AI.conclusion: There is a systematic misalignment between benchmark-driven LLM capabilities and real-world educational effectiveness; common pretraining biases underlie this misalignment; careful, robust alignment assessment is necessary before applying LMs to education, and ensembles may be counterproductive.

title: TP-Spikformer: Token Pruned Spiking Transformer
summary: Spiking neural networks (SNNs) offer an energy-efficient alternative to traditional neural networks due to their event-driven computing paradigm. However, recent advancements in spiking transformers have focused on improving accuracy with large-scale architectures, which require significant computational resources and limit deployment on resource-constrained devices. In this paper, we propose a simple yet effective token pruning method for spiking transformers, termed TP-Spikformer, that reduces storage and computational overhead while maintaining competitive performance. Specifically, we first introduce a heuristic spatiotemporal information-retaining criterion that comprehensively evaluates tokens' importance, assigning higher scores to informative tokens for retention and lower scores to uninformative ones for pruning. Based on this criterion, we propose an information-retaining token pruning framework that employs a block-level early stopping strategy for uninformative tokens, instead of removing them outright. This also helps preserve more information during token pruning. We demonstrate the effectiveness, efficiency and scalability of TP-Spikformer through extensive experiments across diverse architectures, including Spikformer, QKFormer and Spike-driven Transformer V1 and V3, and a range of tasks such as image classification, object detection, semantic segmentation and event-based object tracking. Particularly, TP-Spikformer performs well in a training-free manner. These results reveal its potential as an efficient and practical solution for deploying SNNs in real-world applications with limited computational resources.

AI.tldr: TP-Spikformer introduces a token pruning method for spiking transformers using a spatiotemporal information-retaining criterion and block-level early stopping to reduce compute/storage while maintaining performance, with training-free capability across multiple architectures and tasks.
AI.motivation: Deploying spiking transformer models on resource-constrained devices is hindered by high storage and computation demands. An effective pruning strategy that preserves crucial information without extensive retraining is highly desirable.
AI.method: Propose a heuristic spatiotemporal information-retaining criterion to score tokens by importance. Implement an information-retaining token pruning framework that applies block-level early stopping to uninformative tokens rather than removing them outright, aiming to retain more information. Validate across multiple architectures (Spikformer, QKFormer, Spike-driven Transformer V1/V3) and tasks (image classification, object detection, semantic segmentation, event-based object tracking) and highlight training-free performance.
AI.result: Extensive experiments demonstrate effectiveness, efficiency, and scalability of TP-Spikformer across diverse architectures and tasks, achieving competitive performance with reduced storage/computational overhead. The method can operate in a training-free mode, highlighting practicality for real-world deployment on constrained devices.
AI.conclusion: TP-Spikformer offers a simple yet effective pruning paradigm for spiking transformers, enabling energy-efficient, scalable deployment by conserving information through a block-level, information-retaining pruning mechanism that does not necessarily rely on retraining.

title: Incremental, inconsistency-resilient reasoning over Description Logic Abox streams
summary: More and more, data is being produced in a streaming fashion. This has led to increased interest into how actionable insights can be extracted in real time from data streams through Stream Reasoning. Reasoning over data streams raises multiple challenges, notably the high velocity of data, the real time requirement of the reasoning, and the noisy and volatile nature of streams. This paper proposes novel semantics for incremental reasoning over streams of Description Logic ABoxes, in order to tackle these challenges. To address the first two challenges, our semantics for reasoning over sliding windows on streams allow for incrementally computing the materialization of the window based on the materialization of the previous window. Furthermore, to deal with the volatile nature of streams, we present novel semantics for inconsistency repair on such windows, based on preferred repair semantics. We then detail our proposed semi-naive algorithms for incremental materialization maintenance in the case of OWL2 RL, both in the presence of inconsistencies and without.

AI.tldr: Introduces incremental, window-based stream reasoning over Description Logic ABoxes with inconsistency repair; provides semi-naive incremental materialization algorithms for OWL2 RL.
AI.motivation: To enable real-time, actionable insights from high-velocity data streams. Stream reasoning faces challenges of velocity, real-time constraints, and noisy/volatile data, requiring incremental and robust methods for ABox materialization and inconsistency handling.
AI.method: Proposes novel semantics for incremental reasoning over sliding windows on streams, enabling materialization of a window to be computed incrementally from the previous window's materialization. Introduces inconsistency repair semantics based on preferred repairs to address volatility. Details semi-naive algorithms for incremental materialization maintenance in OWL2 RL, both with and without inconsistencies.
AI.result: Formalizes incremental sliding-window semantics and repair strategies; presents semi-naive incremental materialization algorithms for OWL2 RL, applicable under both consistent and inconsistent settings (no empirical results reported in the abstract).
AI.conclusion: Advances are made in real-time, robust stream reasoning by providing incremental semantics, inconsistency repair, and practical semi-naive algorithms for maintaining DL ABox materialization under streaming windows.

title: Align and Filter: Improving Performance in Asynchronous On-Policy RL
summary: Distributed training and increasing the gradient update frequency are practical strategies to accelerate learning and improve performance, but both exacerbate a central challenge: \textit{policy lag}, which is the mismatch between the behavior policy generating data and the learning policy being updated. Policy lag can hinder the scaling of on-policy learning algorithms to larger problems. In this paper, we identify the sources of policy lag caused by distributed learning and high update frequency. We use the findings to propose \textit{total Variation-based Advantage aligned Constrained policy Optimization (\methodacronym)} as a practical approach to mitigate policy lag. We empirically validate our method and show that it offers better robustness to policy lag in classic RL tasks and a modern RL for LLM math reasoning task.

AI.tldr: A novel policy optimization method TVAAC using a Total Variation-based Advantage alignment and constraints to reduce policy lag in distributed/high-frequency RL; empirically robust on standard benchmarks and an LLM math reasoning task.
AI.motivation: Policy lag from data-policy mismatch in distributed training and high update frequency hinders scaling of on-policy RL.
AI.method: Introduce Total Variation-based Advantage aligned Constrained policy Optimization (TVAAC).
AI.result: Empirical validation shows improved robustness to policy lag across classic RL tasks and a modern RL setup for LLM math reasoning.
AI.conclusion: TVAAC mitigates policy lag under distributed/high-frequency updates, enabling better scalability of on-policy RL, including tasks involving LLM reasoning.

title: CaptionFool: Universal Image Captioning Model Attacks
summary: Image captioning models are encoder-decoder architectures trained on large-scale image-text datasets, making them susceptible to adversarial attacks. We present CaptionFool, a novel universal (input-agnostic) adversarial attack against state-of-the-art transformer-based captioning models. By modifying only 7 out of 577 image patches (approximately 1.2% of the image), our attack achieves 94-96% success rate in generating arbitrary target captions, including offensive content. We further demonstrate that CaptionFool can generate "slang" terms specifically designed to evade existing content moderation filters. Our findings expose critical vulnerabilities in deployed vision-language models and underscore the urgent need for robust defenses against such attacks. Warning: This paper contains model outputs which are offensive in nature.

AI.tldr: Universal, input-agnostic patch-based attack (CaptionFool) on transformer-based image captioning models; perturbs 7 of 577 image patches (~1.2%) to achieve 94–96% targeted caption success, and can craft slang to evade content moderation.
AI.motivation: Expose vulnerabilities of state-of-the-art vision-language/captioning models and safety filters to universal adversarial attacks; assess robustness and safety implications.
AI.method: Develop a universal (input-agnostic) patch perturbation affecting only 7 image patches to steer captions toward a chosen target caption; evaluated on transformer-based captioning models; demonstrates generation of content including offensive/offensive-sounding slang to bypass moderation.
AI.result: High targeted attack success (94–96%); capable of generating arbitrary target captions, including offensive content; demonstrated ability to evade existing content moderation filters.
AI.conclusion: Captioning models are vulnerable to universal patch attacks; underlines need for robust defenses (adversarial training, detection mechanisms, moderation-aware training) and further study on defense and safety implications.

title: What Papers Don't Tell You: Recovering Tacit Knowledge for Automated Paper Reproduction
comment: 32 pages (+ appendix), 8 figures. Lehui Li and Ruining Wang contributed equally. Yongshun Gong is the corresponding author
summary: Automated paper reproduction -- generating executable code from academic papers -- is bottlenecked not by information retrieval but by the tacit knowledge that papers inevitably leave implicit. We formalize this challenge as the progressive recovery of three types of tacit knowledge -- relational, somatic, and collective -- and propose \method, a graph-based agent framework with a dedicated mechanism for each: node-level relation-aware aggregation recovers relational knowledge by analyzing implementation-unit-level reuse and adaptation relationships between the target paper and its citation neighbors; execution-feedback refinement recovers somatic knowledge through iterative debugging driven by runtime signals; and graph-level knowledge induction distills collective knowledge from clusters of papers sharing similar implementations. On an extended ReproduceBench spanning 3 domains, 10 tasks, and 40 recent papers, \method{} achieves an average performance gap of 10.04\% against official implementations, improving over the strongest baseline by 24.68\%. The code will be publicly released upon acceptance; the repository link will be provided in the final version.

AI.tldr: A graph-based agent framework (\method) to recover tacit knowledge for automated paper reproduction, addressing relational, somatic, and collective knowledge; shows strong gains on ReproduceBench.
AI.motivation: Automated paper reproduction is blocked by tacit knowledge left implicit in papers. The work formalizes this as progressive recovery of relational, somatic, and collective knowledge and aims to enable executable code generation from papers.
AI.method: A graph-based agent framework with three mechanisms: (1) node-level relation-aware aggregation to recover relational knowledge by analyzing implementation-unit-level reuse/adaptation between a target paper and citation neighbors; (2) execution-feedback refinement to recover somatic knowledge via iterative debugging driven by runtime signals; (3) graph-level knowledge induction to distill collective knowledge from clusters of papers with similar implementations.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on an extended ReproduceBench (3 domains, 10 tasks, 40 papers) shows an average performance gap of 10.04% to official implementations, and a 24.68% improvement over the strongest baseline; code will be released upon acceptance.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates the effectiveness of tri-partite tacit-knowledge recovery for automated paper reproduction; provides a concrete framework and a scalable evaluation setup, with code release planned.

title: Unifying Language-Action Understanding and Generation for Autonomous Driving
summary: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models are emerging as a promising paradigm for end-to-end autonomous driving, valued for their potential to leverage world knowledge and reason about complex driving scenes. However, existing methods suffer from two critical limitations: a persistent misalignment between language instructions and action outputs, and the inherent inefficiency of typical auto-regressive action generation. In this paper, we introduce LinkVLA, a novel architecture that directly addresses these challenges to enhance both alignment and efficiency. First, we establish a structural link by unifying language and action tokens into a shared discrete codebook, processed within a single multi-modal model. This structurally enforces cross-modal consistency from the ground up. Second, to create a deep semantic link, we introduce an auxiliary action understanding objective that trains the model to generate descriptive captions from trajectories, fostering a bidirectional language-action mapping. Finally, we replace the slow, step-by-step generation with a two-step coarse-to-fine generation method C2F that efficiently decodes the action sequence, saving 86% inference time. Experiments on closed-loop driving benchmarks show consistent gains in instruction following accuracy and driving performance, alongside reduced inference latency.

AI.tldr: LinkVLA unifies language and action into a shared discrete codebook, adds an auxiliary caption-from-trajectory objective for bidirectional mapping, and uses a two-step coarse-to-fine decoder (C2F) to accelerate action generation, yielding improved instruction-following and driving performance with substantial latency reductions on closed-loop benchmarks.
AI.motivation: To tackle two core issues in Vision-Language-Action driving: (1) persistent misalignment between natural language instructions and resultant actions, and (2) inefficiency of autoregressive action generation that limits real-time performance.
AI.method: 1) Create a shared discrete codebook for language and action tokens and process them in a single multimodal model to enforce cross-modal consistency; 2) introduce an auxiliary action understanding objective by training the model to generate captions from trajectories, enabling bidirectional language-action mapping; 3) implement a two-step coarse-to-fine (C2F) generation strategy to replace slow sequential decoding for action sequences, achieving substantial speedups.
AI.result: The approach yields consistent improvements in instruction-following accuracy and driving performance, while significantly reducing inference latency (notably ~86% faster decoding) in closed-loop driving benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Structural alignment via a shared codebook, enriched semantics through bidirectional mappings, and efficient two-stage decoding collectively advance the performance and practicality of VLA driving models, moving toward more reliable and real-time end-to-end autonomous systems.

title: Evaluating AI Grading on Real-World Handwritten College Mathematics: A Large-Scale Study Toward a Benchmark
summary:
Grading in large undergraduate STEM courses often yields minimal feedback due to heavy instructional workloads. We present a large-scale empirical study of AI grading on real, handwritten single-variable calculus work from UC Irvine. Using OCR-conditioned large language models with structured, rubric-guided prompting, our system produces scores and formative feedback for thousands of free-response quiz submissions from nearly 800 students. In a setting with no single ground-truth label, we evaluate performance against official teaching-assistant grades, student surveys, and independent human review, finding strong alignment with TA scoring and a large majority of AI-generated feedback rated as correct or acceptable across quizzes.
  Beyond calculus, this setting highlights core challenges in OCR-conditioned mathematical reasoning and partial-credit assessment. We analyze key failure modes, propose practical rubric- and prompt-design principles, and introduce a multi-perspective evaluation protocol for reliable, real-course deployment. Building on the dataset and evaluation framework developed here, we outline a standardized benchmark for AI grading of handwritten mathematics to support reproducible comparison and future research.

AI.tldr: OCR-conditioned LLMs with rubric-guided prompting can grade handwritten calculus at scale, aligning with TA grades and yielding acceptable feedback; the work also provides a dataset and benchmark for reproducible AI grading of handwritten mathematics.
AI.motivation: Address grading bottlenecks and feedback gaps in large undergraduate STEM courses; enable scalable, formative feedback while mitigating lack of ground-truth labels by relying on multiple human-aligned evaluation signals; establish a reproducible evaluation framework and dataset for AI grading of handwritten math.
AI.method: Apply OCR to handwritten single-variable calculus submissions; condition a large language model on OCR outputs using structured rubric-guided prompts to produce scores and formative feedback; evaluate on thousands of free-response quizzes from ~800 students; compare AI outputs with TA grades, student surveys, and independent human reviews; analyze failure modes; propose rubric/prompt-design principles; present a multi-perspective evaluation protocol and dataset to support reproducible benchmarking.
AI.result: AI grading showed strong alignment with TA scoring; most AI-generated feedback was rated as correct or acceptable across quizzes; identified key failure modes in OCR-conditioned mathematical reasoning and partial-credit assessment; proposed practical rubric and prompt-design guidelines; introduced a multi-perspective evaluation protocol and released dataset and evaluation framework to support reliable deployment and future research.
AI.conclusion: Rubric-guided OCR-conditioned LLM grading is a viable approach for scalable, formative feedback in large courses when paired with robust evaluation, protocol development, and openly available datasets to enable reproducible comparisons and safe deployment.

title: RAFM: Retrieval-Augmented Flow Matching for Unpaired CBCT-to-CT Translation
summary: Cone-beam CT (CBCT) is routinely acquired in radiotherapy but suffers from severe artifacts and unreliable Hounsfield Unit (HU) values, limiting its direct use for dose calculation. Synthetic CT (sCT) generation from CBCT is therefore an important task, yet paired CBCT--CT data are often unavailable or unreliable due to temporal gaps, anatomical variation, and registration errors. In this work, we introduce rectified flow (RF) into unpaired CBCT-to-CT translation in medical imaging. Although RF is theoretically compatible with unpaired learning through distribution-level coupling and deterministic transport, its practical effectiveness under small medical datasets and limited batch sizes remains underexplored. Direct application with random or batch-local pseudo pairing can produce unstable supervision due to semantically mismatched endpoint samples. To address this challenge, we propose Retrieval-Augmented Flow Matching (RAFM), which adapts RF to the medical setting by constructing retrieval-guided pseudo pairs using a frozen DINOv3 encoder and a global CT memory bank. This strategy improves empirical coupling quality and stabilizes unpaired flow-based training. Experiments on SynthRAD2023 under a strict subject-level true-unpaired protocol show that RAFM outperforms existing methods across FID, MAE, SSIM, PSNR, and SegScore. The code is available at https://github.com/HiLab-git/RAFM.git.

AI.tldr: Introduces Retrieval-Augmented Flow Matching (RAFM) to stabilize unpaired CBCT-to-CT translation using a retrieval-guided pseudo-pair scheme with a frozen DINOv3 encoder and a CT memory bank, improving several image-quality and segmentation metrics on SynthRAD2023.
AI.motivation: Unpaired CBCT-to-CT translation is essential for synthetic CT generation in radiotherapy due to unavailable or unreliable paired CBCT--CT data. Existing flow-based approaches struggle with small medical datasets and semantically mismatched supervision, leading to unstable training.
AI.method: Adapt Rectified Flow (RF) to the medical unpaired setting by constructing retrieval-guided pseudo pairs. Use a frozen DINOv3 encoder to extract features and a global CT memory bank to guide pairing, forming more reliable supervision for flow-based CBCT→CT translation (RAFM).
AI.result: On SynthRAD2023 with a strict subject-level true-unpaired protocol, RAFM outperforms existing methods across FID, MAE, SSIM, PSNR, and SegScore; code released at the provided GitHub link.
AI.conclusion: RAFM provides a more stable and effective unpaired CBCT→CT translation framework by coupling global retrieval with flow-based learning, improving quantitative and semantic metrics for sCT generation in radiotherapy. Future work could explore ablations, generalization to other datasets, and computational efficiency.

title: Emerging Human-like Strategies for Semantic Memory Foraging in Large Language Models
summary: Both humans and Large Language Models (LLMs) store a vast repository of semantic memories. In humans, efficient and strategic access to this memory store is a critical foundation for a variety of cognitive functions. Such access has long been a focus of psychology and the computational mechanisms behind it are now well characterized. Much of this understanding has been gleaned from a widely-used neuropsychological and cognitive science assessment called the Semantic Fluency Task (SFT), which requires the generation of as many semantically constrained concepts as possible. Our goal is to apply mechanistic interpretability techniques to bring greater rigor to the study of semantic memory foraging in LLMs. To this end, we present preliminary results examining SFT as a case study. A central focus is on convergent and divergent patterns of generative memory search, which in humans play complementary strategic roles in efficient memory foraging. We show that these same behavioral signatures, critical to human performance on the SFT, also emerge as identifiable patterns in LLMs across distinct layers. Potentially, this analysis provides new insights into how LLMs may be adapted into closer cognitive alignment with humans, or alternatively, guided toward productive cognitive \emph{disalignment} to enhance complementary strengths in human-AI interaction.

AI.tldr: LLMs exhibit convergent/divergent memory-search signatures similar to human semantic memory foraging (SFT); preliminary mechanistic interpretability analysis across layers; implications for alignment or productive disalignment in human–AI interaction.
AI.motivation: Understand whether LLM internal memory search mirrors human semantic foraging and leverage mechanistic interpretability to analyze it; inform alignment strategies and potential disalignment to enhance human–AI collaboration.
AI.method: Apply mechanistic interpretability techniques to a Semantic Fluency Task (SFT) case study in LLMs; examine generative memory search patterns across model layers and compare with human patterns of convergent/divergent search.
AI.result: Preliminary results show that the same behavioral signatures (convergent/divergent search patterns) observed in humans emerge as identifiable patterns in LLMs across distinct layers.
AI.conclusion: This approach provides a framework to align LLM cognition with human strategies or to engineer deliberate cognitive disalignment to harness complementary strengths in human–AI interaction; SFT serves as a tractable testbed for memory-foraging mechanisms in LLMs.

title: Principled Fast and Meta Knowledge Learners for Continual Reinforcement Learning
summary: Inspired by the human learning and memory system, particularly the interplay between the hippocampus and cerebral cortex, this study proposes a dual-learner framework comprising a fast learner and a meta learner to address continual Reinforcement Learning~(RL) problems. These two learners are coupled to perform distinct yet complementary roles: the fast learner focuses on knowledge transfer, while the meta learner ensures knowledge integration. In contrast to traditional multi-task RL approaches that share knowledge through average return maximization, our meta learner incrementally integrates new experiences by explicitly minimizing catastrophic forgetting, thereby supporting efficient cumulative knowledge transfer for the fast learner. To facilitate rapid adaptation in new environments, we introduce an adaptive meta warm-up mechanism that selectively harnesses past knowledge. We conduct experiments in various pixel-based and continuous control benchmarks, revealing the superior performance of continual learning for our proposed dual-learner approach relative to baseline methods. The code is released in https://github.com/datake/FAME.

AI.tldr: A dual-learner continual RL framework (fast learner + meta learner) inspired by hippocampus-cortex, with an adaptive meta-warm-up to minimize forgetting and enable rapid adaptation; achieves superior performance over baselines with released code.
AI.motivation: To address continual reinforcement learning and catastrophic forgetting by emulating the brain's memory hierarchy. Traditional multi-task RL often relies on average return sharing, which fails to preserve accumulated knowledge across tasks; the work seeks cumulative knowledge transfer through a meta-learning component.
AI.method: A coupled fast learner (for knowledge transfer) and meta learner (for knowledge integration). The meta learner explicitly minimizes catastrophic forgetting to retain prior knowledge while integrating new experiences. An adaptive meta warm-up mechanism selectively leverages past knowledge to accelerate adaptation. Evaluations on pixel-based and continuous control benchmarks. Code released at GitHub.
AI.result: The proposed dual-learner approach yields superior continual RL performance compared with baseline methods, showing effective knowledge transfer while mitigating forgetting and enabling rapid adaptation to new environments.
AI.conclusion: The dual-learner framework with an adaptive meta-warm-up effectively combines fast transfer and robust knowledge integration to advance continual RL, offering improved performance and rapid adaptation in diverse task settings.

title: Adaptive Dynamic Dehazing via Instruction-Driven and Task-Feedback Closed-Loop Optimization for Diverse Downstream Task Adaptation
summary: In real-world vision systems,haze removal is required not only to enhance image visibility but also to meet the specific needs of diverse downstream tasks.To address this challenge,we propose a novel adaptive dynamic dehazing framework that incorporates a closed-loop optimization mechanism.It enables feedback-driven refinement based on downstream task performance and user instruction-guided adjustment during inference,allowing the model to satisfy the specific requirements of multiple downstream tasks without retraining.Technically,our framework integrates two complementary and innovative mechanisms: (1)a task feedback loop that dynamically modulates dehazing outputs based on performance across multiple downstream tasks,and (2) a text instruction interface that allows users to specify high-level task preferences.This dual-guidance strategy enables the model to adapt its dehazing behavior after training,tailoring outputs in real time to the evolving needs of multiple tasks.Extensive experiments across various vision tasks demonstrate the strong effectiveness,robustness,and generalizability of our approach.These results establish a new paradigm for interactive,task-adaptive dehazing that actively collaborates with downstream applications.

AI.tldr: Adaptive dynamic dehazing with closed-loop optimization: task feedback and user-guided text instructions enable real-time, post-training, task-adaptive dehazing without retraining.
AI.motivation: Haze removal must support diverse downstream tasks and user preferences in real-world vision systems; standard dehazing is often task-agnostic and inflexible.
AI.method: Two mechanisms: (1) a task feedback loop that modulates dehazing outputs based on performance across multiple downstream tasks; (2) a text instruction interface allowing users to specify high-level task preferences; combined in a closed-loop during inference.
AI.result: Extensive experiments across various vision tasks demonstrate strong effectiveness, robustness, and generalizability; the approach adapts outputs to evolving task needs without retraining.
AI.conclusion: Proposes a new paradigm for interactive, task-adaptive dehazing that actively collaborates with downstream applications by incorporating feedback and user guidance at inference time.

title: CoVe: Training Interactive Tool-Use Agents via Constraint-Guided Verification
summary: Developing multi-turn interactive tool-use agents is challenging because real-world user needs are often complex and ambiguous, yet agents must execute deterministic actions to satisfy them. To address this gap, we introduce \textbf{CoVe} (\textbf{Co}nstraint-\textbf{Ve}rification), a post-training data synthesis framework designed for training interactive tool-use agents while ensuring both data complexity and correctness. CoVe begins by defining explicit task constraints, which serve a dual role: they guide the generation of complex trajectories and act as deterministic verifiers for assessing trajectory quality. This enables the creation of high-quality training trajectories for supervised fine-tuning (SFT) and the derivation of accurate reward signals for reinforcement learning (RL). Our evaluation on the challenging $τ^2$-bench benchmark demonstrates the effectiveness of the framework. Notably, our compact \textbf{CoVe-4B} model achieves success rates of 43.0\% and 59.4\% in the Airline and Retail domains, respectively; its overall performance significantly outperforms strong baselines of similar scale and remains competitive with models up to $17\times$ its size. These results indicate that CoVe provides an effective and efficient pathway for synthesizing training data for state-of-the-art interactive tool-use agents. To support future research, we open-source our code, trained model, and the full set of 12K high-quality trajectories used for training.

AI.tldr: CoVe is a post-training data synthesis framework that uses explicit task constraints as both generative guides and deterministic verifiers to produce high-quality training data for supervised fine-tuning and reinforcement learning of interactive tool-use agents; it achieves strong results on τ^2-bench with a compact model and open-sources data and code.
AI.motivation: Bridge the gap between complex, ambiguous real-world user needs and the requirement for deterministic agent actions by enabling controllable, verifiable data synthesis for training interactive agents.
AI.method: Define explicit task constraints to guide generation of complex trajectories and serve as deterministic trajectory quality verifiers; synthesize training data for SFT and derive reward signals for RL; evaluate on τ^2-bench; release code, model, and 12K trajectories.
AI.result: CoVe-4B attains 43.0% and 59.4% success rates in Airline and Retail domains respectively; overall performance surpasses strong baselines of similar scale and is competitive with models up to 17× larger.
AI.conclusion: CoVe provides an effective, efficient pathway for synthesizing high-quality training data for state-of-the-art interactive tool-use agents, with open-source resources to facilitate future research.

title: Pri4R: Learning World Dynamics for Vision-Language-Action Models with Privileged 4D Representation
summary: Humans learn not only how their bodies move, but also how the surrounding world responds to their actions. In contrast, while recent Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models exhibit impressive semantic understanding, they often fail to capture the spatiotemporal dynamics governing physical interaction. In this paper, we introduce Pri4R, a simple yet effective approach that endows VLA models with an implicit understanding of world dynamics by leveraging privileged 4D information during training. Specifically, Pri4R augments VLAs with a lightweight point track head that predicts 3D point tracks. By injecting VLA features into this head to jointly predict future 3D trajectories, the model learns to incorporate evolving scene geometry within its shared representation space, enabling more physically aware context for precise control. Due to its architectural simplicity, Pri4R is compatible with dominant VLA design patterns with minimal changes. During inference, we run the model using the original VLA architecture unchanged; Pri4R adds no extra inputs, outputs, or computational overhead. Across simulation and real-world evaluations, Pri4R significantly improves performance on challenging manipulation tasks, including a +10% gain on LIBERO-Long and a +40% gain on RoboCasa. We further show that 3D point track prediction is an effective supervision target for learning action-world dynamics, and validate our design choices through extensive ablations.

AI.tldr: Pri4R adds a lightweight 3D point-track head to Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models to learn world dynamics from privileged 4D information, improving manipulation performance with no inference overhead.
AI.motivation: Current VLA models capture semantic meaning but struggle with spatiotemporal, physically grounded dynamics. Privileged 4D data during training can teach models to reason about action-world interactions.
AI.method: Introduce a light 3D point-track head that predicts point trajectories and jointly predicts future trajectories by injecting VLA features into this head. Train with privileged 4D information to learn evolving scene geometry; during inference, use the unchanged VLA architecture with no extra inputs/outputs/compute.
AI.result: Empirical results show notable gains in manipulation tasks: +10% on LIBERO-Long and +40% on RoboCasa in both simulation and real-world settings. 3D point-track prediction serves as effective supervision for action-world dynamics; ablations validate design choices.
AI.conclusion: Pri4R offers a simple, architecture-compatible approach to imbue VLA models with physical awareness by leveraging 4D supervision, improving performance without additional inference cost.

title: When Does Margin Clamping Affect Training Variance? Dataset-Dependent Effects in Contrastive Forward-Forward Learning
summary: Contrastive Forward-Forward (CFF) learning trains Vision Transformers layer by layer against supervised contrastive objectives. CFF training can be sensitive to random seed, but the sources of this instability are poorly understood. We focus on one implementation detail: the positive-pair margin in the contrastive loss is applied through saturating similarity clamping, $\min(s + m,\, 1)$. We prove that an alternative formulation, subtracting the margin after the log-probability, is gradient-neutral under the mean-over-positives reduction. On CIFAR-10 ($2 \times 2$ factorial, $n{=}7$ seeds per cell), clamping produces $5.90\times$ higher pooled test-accuracy variance ($p{=}0.003$) with no difference in mean accuracy. Analyses of clamp activation rates, layerwise gradient norms, and a reduced-margin probe point to saturation-driven gradient truncation at early layers. The effect does not transfer cleanly to other datasets: on CIFAR-100, SVHN, and Fashion-MNIST, clamping produces equal or lower variance. Two factors account for the discrepancy. First, positive-pair density per batch controls how often saturation occurs. Second, task difficulty compresses seed-to-seed spread when accuracy is high. An SVHN difficulty sweep confirms the interaction on a single dataset, with the variance ratio moving from $0.25\times$ at high accuracy to $16.73\times$ under aggressive augmentation. In moderate-accuracy regimes with many same-class pairs per batch, switching to the gradient-neutral subtraction reference removes this variance inflation at no cost to mean accuracy. Measuring the layer-0 clamp activation rate serves as a simple check for whether the problem applies.

AI.tldr: Clamping-based margin in CFF introduces seed-dependent variance on CIFAR-10 due to saturation-driven gradient truncation; switching to a gradient-neutral subtraction after log-probability mitigates variance in moderate-accuracy regimes, though the effect is dataset-dependent.
AI.motivation: Identify sources of instability in Contrastive Forward-Forward training and understand why positive-pair margin clamping affects gradient flow and seed-to-seed variance across datasets.
AI.method: Compare two margin formulations (clamped margin vs log-probability subtraction) within CFF training. Evaluate on CIFAR-10 with a 2x2 factorial design and seven seeds per cell. Analyze clamp activation, layerwise gradient norms, and perform a reduced-margin probe. Extend analysis to CIFAR-100, SVHN, and Fashion-MNIST, plus an SVHN difficulty sweep. Measure test-accuracy variance and mean; assess how positive-pair density and task difficulty modulate effects.
AI.result: On CIFAR-10, clamping yields ~5.90x higher pooled test-accuracy variance (p=0.003) with no mean accuracy change. Layer-0 clamp activation and gradient norms indicate saturation-driven gradient truncation in early layers. On CIFAR-100, SVHN, and Fashion-MNIST, clamping does not inflate variance and can lower it in some cases. Two factors explain dataset differences: positive-pair density per batch and task difficulty compressing seed-to-seed spread when accuracy is high. An SVHN difficulty sweep shows variance ratio moving from 0.25x at high accuracy to 16.73x under aggressive augmentation. In moderate-accuracy regimes with many same-class pairs, using the gradient-neutral subtraction reference removes variance inflation without harming mean accuracy. Layer-0 clamp activation rate serves as a practical diagnostic.
AI.conclusion: Saturation via clamp is not universally harmful but can destabilize training on datasets with high positive-pair density and high baseline accuracy. A gradient-neutral margin subtraction offers a robust alternative for variance control in many practical regimes; monitoring layer-0 clamp activation provides a quick diagnostic for applicability.

title: Cross-Scale Pansharpening via ScaleFormer and the PanScale Benchmark
summary: Pansharpening aims to generate high-resolution multi-spectral images by fusing the spatial detail of panchromatic images with the spectral richness of low-resolution MS data. However, most existing methods are evaluated under limited, low-resolution settings, limiting their generalization to real-world, high-resolution scenarios. To bridge this gap, we systematically investigate the data, algorithmic, and computational challenges of cross-scale pansharpening. We first introduce PanScale, the first large-scale, cross-scale pansharpening dataset, accompanied by PanScale-Bench, a comprehensive benchmark for evaluating generalization across varying resolutions and scales. To realize scale generalization, we propose ScaleFormer, a novel architecture designed for multi-scale pansharpening. ScaleFormer reframes generalization across image resolutions as generalization across sequence lengths: it tokenizes images into patch sequences of the same resolution but variable length proportional to image scale. A Scale-Aware Patchify module enables training for such variations from fixed-size crops. ScaleFormer then decouples intra-patch spatial feature learning from inter-patch sequential dependency modeling, incorporating Rotary Positional Encoding to enhance extrapolation to unseen scales. Extensive experiments show that our approach outperforms SOTA methods in fusion quality and cross-scale generalization. The datasets and source code are available upon acceptance.

AI.tldr: Introduces ScaleFormer for cross-scale pansharpening, backed by PanScale dataset and PanScale-Bench benchmark, achieving better generalization across resolutions. Uses scale-aware patchification and Rotary Positional Encoding to model intra-patch and inter-patch information, reframing scale generalization as length generalization.
AI.motivation: Current pansharpening methods are evaluated mainly on low-resolution data, hindering real-world applicability. There is a need for large-scale cross-scale data, standardized benchmarks, and models that generalize across image resolutions/scales.
AI.method: Propose PanScale: a large-scale cross-scale pansharpening dataset and PanScale-Bench benchmark. Propose ScaleFormer: a multi-scale pansharpening architecture that tokenizes images into patch sequences with fixed resolution but varying length corresponding to scale. Introduce Scale-Aware Patchify to train on fixed-size crops. Decouple intra-patch spatial feature learning from inter-patch sequential dependencies. Use Rotary Positional Encoding to improve extrapolation to unseen scales.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show ScaleFormer outperforms state-of-the-art fusion methods in both fusion quality and cross-scale generalization. Datasets and source code are to be released upon acceptance.
AI.conclusion: The work provides a practical framework for cross-scale pansharpening, including a large-scale dataset and benchmark, and a novel architecture that generalizes across scales, addressing real-world applicability; it establishes a baseline and resources for future research.

title: LiveCultureBench: a Multi-Agent, Multi-Cultural Benchmark for Large Language Models in Dynamic Social Simulations
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed as autonomous agents, yet evaluations focus primarily on task success rather than cultural appropriateness or evaluator reliability. We introduce LiveCultureBench, a multi-cultural, dynamic benchmark that embeds LLMs as agents in a simulated town and evaluates them on both task completion and adherence to socio-cultural norms. The simulation models a small city as a location graph with synthetic residents having diverse demographic and cultural profiles. Each episode assigns one resident a daily goal while others provide social context. An LLM-based verifier generates structured judgments on norm violations and task progress, which we aggregate into metrics capturing task-norm trade-offs and verifier uncertainty. Using LiveCultureBench across models and cultural profiles, we study (i) cross-cultural robustness of LLM agents, (ii) how they balance effectiveness against norm sensitivity, and (iii) when LLM-as-a-judge evaluation is reliable for automated benchmarking versus when human oversight is needed.

AI.tldr: Introduces LiveCultureBench, a dynamic multi-cultural benchmark where LLM agents operate in a simulated town and are evaluated on task completion, normative adherence, and verifier reliability.
AI.motivation: Addresses a gap in LLM evaluation by moving beyond task success to include socio-cultural appropriateness and the reliability of automated judgments.
AI.method: Models a small city as a location graph with synthetic residents of diverse cultural profiles; episodes assign a resident a daily goal while others provide social context; an LLM-based verifier produces structured judgments on norm violations and task progress, aggregated into metrics for task-norm trade-offs and verifier uncertainty; analyses across models and cultural profiles.
AI.result: The study aims to reveal cross-cultural robustness of agents, how they balance effectiveness against norm sensitivity, and when LLM-based evaluation is reliable versus when human oversight is needed.
AI.conclusion: LiveCultureBench offers a dynamic, ethnoculturally aware benchmarking framework that couples task performance with normative alignment and evaluation reliability, guiding future benchmark design and interpretation.

title: Stabilizing Policy Optimization via Logits Convexity
summary: While reinforcement learning (RL) has been central to the recent success of large language models (LLMs), RL optimization is notoriously unstable, especially when compared to supervised fine-tuning (SFT). In this work, we investigate the stability gap between SFT and RL from a gradient-based perspective, and show that the convexity of the SFT loss with respect to model logits plays a key role in enabling stable training. Our theoretical analysis demonstrates that this property induces favorable gradient directionality during optimization. In contrast, Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO), a widely adopted policy gradient algorithm utilizing a clipped surrogate objective, lacks this stabilizing property. Motivated by this observation, we propose Logits Convex Optimization (LCO), a simple yet effective policy optimization framework that aligns the learned policy with an optimal target derived from the original RL objective, thereby emulating the stabilizing effects of logits-level convexity. Extensive experiments across multiple model families show that our LCO framework consistently improves training stability and outperforms conventional RL methods on a broad range of benchmarks.

AI.tldr: Convexity of the supervised fine-tuning (SFT) loss in model logits stabilizes RL optimization for LLMs; the authors propose Logits Convex Optimization (LCO) to emulate this stabilizing effect and show improved stability and performance over PPO-based methods across multiple models and benchmarks.
AI.motivation: RL optimization for large language models is inherently unstable, and the paper seeks to identify a structural reason for the stability gap between SFT and RL, aiming to close it with a convexity-informed method.
AI.method: The authors provide a gradient-based theoretical analysis showing that logit-wise convexity of the SFT loss guides gradient directions, contrast this with PPO's lack of such convexity, and introduce LCO, a policy optimization framework that aligns the learned policy with an optimal target derived from the original RL objective to mimic the stabilizing effects of logits convexity.
AI.result: Empirical evaluations across multiple model families indicate that LCO improves training stability and yields better performance than conventional RL methods on a broad set of benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Logits-level convexity is a key factor in stabilizing RL versus SFT; LCO leverages this insight by aligning the policy toward an optimal RL-derived target, achieving enhanced stability and stronger performance across diverse models and tasks.

title: Multiple Inputs and Mixwd data for Alzheimer's Disease Classification Based on 3D Vision Transformer
summary: The current methods for diagnosing Alzheimer Disease using Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI) have significant limitations. Many previous studies used 2D Transformers to analyze individual brain slices independently, potentially losing critical 3D contextual information. Region of interest-based models often focus on only a few brain regions despite Alzheimer's affecting multiple areas. Additionally, most classification models rely on a single test, whereas diagnosing Alzheimer's requires a multifaceted approach integrating diverse data sources for a more accurate assessment. This study introduces a novel methodology called the Multiple Inputs and Mixed Data 3D Vision Transformer (MIMD-3DVT). This method processes consecutive slices together to capture the feature dimensions and spatial information, fuses multiple 3D ROI imaging data inputs, and integrates mixed data from demographic factors, cognitive assessments, and brain imaging. The proposed methodology was experimentally evaluated using a combined dataset that included the Alzheimer's Disease Neuroimaging Initiative (ADNI), the Australian Imaging, Biomarker, and Lifestyle Flagship Study of Ageing (AIBL), and the Open Access Series of Imaging Studies (OASIS). Our MIMD-3DVT, utilizing single or multiple ROIs, achieved an accuracy of 97.14%, outperforming the state-of-the-art methods in distinguishing between Normal Cognition and Alzheimer's Disease.

AI.tldr: Introduces MIMD-3DVT, a 3D vision transformer that processes consecutive MRI slices, fuses multi-ROI 3D imaging data, and integrates mixed demographic, cognitive, and imaging data to diagnose Alzheimer's Disease, achieving 97.14% accuracy on a combined ADNI/AIBL/OASIS dataset and surpassing state-of-the-art methods.
AI.motivation: Overcomes limitations of 2D slice analysis, ROI-restricted models, and single-test diagnosis by capturing 3D context, leveraging multiple ROIs, and incorporating heterogeneous data sources.
AI.method: Proposes MIMD-3DVT that (1) processes consecutive slices to preserve 3D context, (2) fuses multiple 3D ROI inputs, (3) integrates mixed data (demographics, cognitive assessments, imaging). Trained and evaluated on a combined ADNI/AIBL/OASIS dataset.
AI.result: Achieves 97.14% accuracy in distinguishing Normal Cognition from Alzheimer's Disease, outperforming state-of-the-art methods.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates the effectiveness of integrating 3D contextual imaging, multi-ROI fusion, and mixed data for robust AD diagnosis with higher accuracy, suggesting potential for more reliable clinical decision support.

title: According to Me: Long-Term Personalized Referential Memory QA
summary: Personalized AI assistants must recall and reason over long-term user memory, which naturally spans multiple modalities and sources such as images, videos, and emails. However, existing Long-term Memory benchmarks focus primarily on dialogue history, failing to capture realistic personalized references grounded in lived experience. We introduce ATM-Bench, the first benchmark for multimodal, multi-source personalized referential Memory QA. ATM-Bench contains approximately four years of privacy-preserving personal memory data and human-annotated question-answer pairs with ground-truth memory evidence, including queries that require resolving personal references, multi-evidence reasoning from multi-source and handling conflicting evidence. We propose Schema-Guided Memory (SGM) to structurally represent memory items originated from different sources. In experiments, we implement 5 state-of-the-art memory systems along with a standard RAG baseline and evaluate variants with different memory ingestion, retrieval, and answer generation techniques. We find poor performance (under 20\% accuracy) on the ATM-Bench-Hard set, and that SGM improves performance over Descriptive Memory commonly adopted in prior works. Code available at: https://github.com/JingbiaoMei/ATM-Bench

AI.tldr: ATM-Bench introduces the first multimodal, multi-source personalized memory QA benchmark (ATM-Bench) with four years of privacy-preserving personal memory data, adds Schema-Guided Memory (SGM) for structured memory items, and presents evaluation of multiple memory systems showing SGMs outperform descriptive memory on challenging data; code available.
AI.motivation: Current long-term memory benchmarks largely focus on dialogue history and single-source text, failing to capture realistic, multi-modal, multi-source personal memories and their grounded references required for personalized assistants.
AI.method: Create ATM-Bench: a multimodal, multi-source memory QA dataset with ~4 years of privacy-preserving personal memory data and human-annotated QA with ground-truth memory evidence. Propose Schema-Guided Memory (SGM) to structurally represent memory items from diverse sources. Evaluate 5 state-of-the-art memory systems plus a standard RAG baseline, experimenting with different memory ingestion, retrieval, and answer-generation strategies.
AI.result: ATM-Bench-Hard set achieves very low accuracy (<20%). SGM yields improvements over Descriptive Memory in prior approaches, indicating value of structured representations for cross-source memory reasoning.
AI.conclusion: The work highlights the difficulty of multimodal, multi-source personal memory QA and demonstrates that schema-guided, structured memory representations can meaningfully improve performance over descriptive, unstructured memory assumptions. It also provides a public code release for replication (GitHub link).

title: LEAR: Learning Edge-Aware Representations for Event-to-LiDAR Localization
summary: Event cameras offer high-temporal-resolution sensing that remains reliable under high-speed motion and challenging lighting, making them promising for localization from LiDAR point clouds in GPS-denied and visually degraded environments. However, aligning sparse, asynchronous events with dense LiDAR maps is fundamentally ill-posed, as direct correspondence estimation suffers from modality gaps. We propose LEAR, a dual-task learning framework that jointly estimates edge structures and dense event-depth flow fields to bridge the sensing-modality divide. Instead of treating edges as a post-hoc aid, LEAR couples them with flow estimation through a cross-modal fusion mechanism that injects modality-invariant geometric cues into the motion representation, and an iterative refinement strategy that enforces mutual consistency between the two tasks over multiple update steps. This synergy produces edge-aware, depth-aligned flow fields that enable more robust and accurate pose recovery via Perspective-n-Point (PnP) solvers. On several popular and challenging datasets, LEAR achieves superior performance over the best prior method. The source code, trained models, and demo videos are made publicly available online.

AI.tldr: LEAR is a dual-task learning framework that jointly estimates edge structures and dense event-depth flow fields to bridge the gap between sparse, asynchronous event data and dense LiDAR maps for robust pose estimation, using cross-modal fusion and iterative mutual refinement; achieves state-of-the-art performance on challenging datasets and releases code and models.
AI.motivation: To address the ill-posed problem of aligning event-camera data with dense LiDAR maps in GPS-denied or visually degraded environments, by combining edge estimation with flow in a joint framework to provide modality-invariant geometric cues.
AI.method: A dual-task network jointly predicts edge maps and dense event-depth flow; employs cross-modal fusion to inject edge-based geometric cues into the flow representation; uses an iterative refinement strategy enforcing mutual consistency between edge and flow estimates across multiple update steps.
AI.result: Led to edge-aware, depth-aligned flow fields that improve pose recovery via PnP; outperforms prior methods on multiple challenging datasets; source code, trained models, and demos are publicly available.
AI.conclusion: LEAR demonstrates that coupling edge detection with depth-aware flow in an iterative framework yields more robust localization from event data aligned to LiDAR maps, enabling improved PnP-based pose estimation and facilitating practical deployment through public release of resources.

title: Intent-Context Synergy Reinforcement Learning for Autonomous UAV Decision-Making in Air Combat
summary: Autonomous UAV infiltration in dynamic contested environments remains a significant challenge due to the partially observable nature of threats and the conflicting objectives of mission efficiency versus survivability. Traditional Reinforcement Learning (RL) approaches often suffer from myopic decision-making and struggle to balance these trade-offs in real-time. To address these limitations, this paper proposes an Intent-Context Synergy Reinforcement Learning (ICS-RL) framework. The framework introduces two core innovations: (1) An LSTM-based Intent Prediction Module that forecasts the future trajectories of hostile units, transforming the decision paradigm from reactive avoidance to proactive planning via state augmentation; (2) A Context-Analysis Synergy Mechanism that decomposes the mission into hierarchical sub-tasks (safe cruise, stealth planning, and hostile breakthrough). We design a heterogeneous ensemble of Dueling DQN agents, each specialized in a specific tactical context. A dynamic switching controller based on Max-Advantage values seamlessly integrates these agents, allowing the UAV to adaptively select the optimal policy without hard-coded rules. Extensive simulations demonstrate that ICS-RL significantly outperforms baselines (Standard DDQN) and traditional methods (PSO, Game Theory). The proposed method achieves a mission success rate of 88\% and reduces the average exposure frequency to 0.24 per episode, validating its superiority in ensuring robust and stealthy penetration in high-dynamic scenarios.

AI.tldr: ICS-RL combines LSTM-based intent prediction with a context-aware hierarchical DDQN ensemble and a dynamic switching controller to enhance autonomous UAV infiltration under dynamic threats.
AI.motivation: Address partial observability and the trade-off between mission efficiency and survivability in dynamic contested environments where myopic RL struggles to balance objectives.
AI.method: Propose Intent-Context Synergy RL (ICS-RL) with (1) an LSTM Intent Prediction Module that forecasts hostile trajectories for state augmentation and proactive planning; (2) a Context-Analysis Synergy Mechanism that decomposes the mission into safe cruise, stealth planning, and hostile breakthrough; a heterogeneous ensemble of specialized Dueling DQN agents; and a dynamic Max-Advantage switching controller that selects the optimal policy without hard-coded rules.
AI.result: Simulation results show ICS-RL outperforms Standard DDQN and baselines PSO and Game Theory, achieving a mission success rate of 88% and reducing average exposure frequency to 0.24 per episode.
AI.conclusion: ICS-RL demonstrates robust, stealthy penetration in high-dynamic scenarios by combining proactive intent modeling with context-driven policy selection; the framework advances real-time decision-making under partial observability and complex mission structures.

title: Weakly Supervised Video Anomaly Detection with Anomaly-Connected Components and Intention Reasoning
summary: Weakly supervised video anomaly detection (WS-VAD) involves identifying the temporal intervals that contain anomalous events in untrimmed videos, where only video-level annotations are provided as supervisory signals. However, a key limitation persists in WS-VAD, as dense frame-level annotations are absent, which often leaves existing methods struggling to learn anomaly semantics effectively. To address this issue, we propose a novel framework named LAS-VAD, short for Learning Anomaly Semantics for WS-VAD, which integrates anomaly-connected component mechanism and intention awareness mechanism. The former is designed to assign video frames into distinct semantic groups within a video, and frame segments within the same group are deemed to share identical semantic information. The latter leverages an intention-aware strategy to distinguish between similar normal and abnormal behaviors (e.g., taking items and stealing). To further model the semantic information of anomalies, as anomaly occurrence is accompanied by distinct characteristic attributes (i.e., explosions are characterized by flames and thick smoke), we additionally incorporate anomaly attribute information to guide accurate detection. Extensive experiments on two benchmark datasets, XD-Violence and UCF-Crime, demonstrate that our LAS-VAD outperforms current state-of-the-art methods with remarkable gains.

AI.tldr: LAS-VAD: a weakly supervised video anomaly detection framework using anomaly-connected components and intention awareness, augmented by anomaly attribute guidance, achieving state-of-the-art results on XD-Violence and UCF-Crime.
AI.motivation: WS-VAD lacks dense frame-level annotations, making it hard to learn fine-grained anomaly semantics. The paper argues for semantic grouping of frames and discrimination of similar normal/abnormal actions, and introduces anomaly attribute cues to better model anomalies.
AI.method: LAS-VAD integrates two mechanisms: (1) anomaly-connected component mechanism to partition a video into semantic groups where frames in the same group share semantic meaning; (2) intention awareness mechanism to separate similar normal vs. abnormal behaviors. It also incorporates anomaly attribute information (e.g., flames, smoke for explosions) to guide detection.
AI.result: Experimental results on XD-Violence and UCF-Crime show that LAS-VAD outperforms current state-of-the-art methods with remarkable gains.
AI.conclusion: LAS-VAD effectively learns anomaly semantics under weak supervision, with anomaly-connected components, intention awareness, and attribute-guided modeling contributing to improved detection performance and potential generalization to other WS-VAD settings.

title: Rich Insights from Cheap Signals: Efficient Evaluations via Tensor Factorization
summary: Moving beyond evaluations that collapse performance across heterogeneous prompts toward fine-grained evaluation at the prompt level, or within relatively homogeneous subsets, is necessary to diagnose generative models' strengths and weaknesses. Such fine-grained evaluations, however, suffer from a data bottleneck: human gold-standard labels are too costly at this scale, while automated ratings are often misaligned with human judgment. To resolve this challenge, we propose a novel statistical model based on tensor factorization that merges cheap autorater data with a limited set of human gold-standard labels. Specifically, our approach uses autorater scores to pretrain latent representations of prompts and generative models, and then aligns those pretrained representations to human preferences using a small calibration set. This sample-efficient methodology is robust to autorater quality, more accurately predicts human preferences on a per-prompt basis than standard baselines, and provides tight confidence intervals for key statistical parameters of interest. We also showcase the practical utility of our method by constructing granular leaderboards based on prompt qualities and by estimating model performance solely from autorater scores, eliminating the need for additional human annotations.

AI.tldr: Proposes a tensor-factorization model that fuses cheap autorater scores with a small set of human labels to enable fine-grained, per-prompt evaluation of generative models; yields robust, calibrated predictions and practical leaderboards without heavy human annotation.
AI.motivation: Fine-grained prompt-level evaluation is needed to diagnose model strengths and weaknesses, but human gold-standard labels are costly and automated ratings can be misaligned; a sample-efficient method that leverages autoraters and limited human data is desirable.
AI.method: A tensor-factorization-based framework that pretrains latent representations of prompts and generative models from autorater data, then aligns these representations to human preferences using a small calibration set; the approach is robust to autorater quality and yields calibrated per-prompt predictions with confidence intervals.
AI.result: More accurate predictions of human preferences on a per-prompt basis than standard baselines; robust to autorater quality; provides tight confidence intervals for key statistics; enables granular leaderboards and estimates model performance from autorater scores alone.
AI.conclusion: The method offers a data-efficient, robust solution for fine-grained prompt-level evaluation, enabling detailed diagnostics and scalable benchmarking with limited human labeling.

title: Streaming Real-Time Trajectory Prediction Using Endpoint-Aware Modeling
comment: WACV 2026 Oral. Project Page at https://a-pru.github.io/seam/
summary: Future trajectories of neighboring traffic agents have a significant influence on the path planning and decision-making of autonomous vehicles. While trajectory forecasting is a well-studied field, research mainly focuses on snapshot-based prediction, where each scenario is treated independently of its global temporal context. However, real-world autonomous driving systems need to operate in a continuous setting, requiring real-time processing of data streams with low latency and consistent predictions over successive timesteps. We leverage this continuous setting to propose a lightweight yet highly accurate streaming-based trajectory forecasting approach. We integrate valuable information from previous predictions with a novel endpoint-aware modeling scheme. Our temporal context propagation uses the trajectory endpoints of the previous forecasts as anchors to extract targeted scenario context encodings. Our approach efficiently guides its scene encoder to extract highly relevant context information without needing refinement iterations or segment-wise decoding. Our experiments highlight that our approach effectively relays information across consecutive timesteps. Unlike methods using multi-stage refinement processing, our approach significantly reduces inference latency, making it well-suited for real-world deployment. We achieve state-of-the-art streaming trajectory prediction results on the Argoverse~2 multi-agent and single-agent benchmarks, while requiring substantially fewer resources.

AI.tldr: A lightweight streaming trajectory forecasting method that propagates temporal context through endpoint-aware anchoring, enabling real-time, consistent predictions with state-of-the-art accuracy and lower resource usage on Argoverse 2.
AI.motivation: Snapshot-based trajectory forecasting treats each scenario independently and fails to exploit temporal continuity in streaming, real-time autonomous driving where low latency and temporal consistency are essential.
AI.method: Endpoint-aware modeling that uses endpoints from previous forecasts as anchors to propagate targeted scene context encodings. The temporal context guides a single-pass scene encoder, avoiding refinement iterations and segment-wise decoding, enabling efficient streaming prediction.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art streaming trajectory prediction on Argoverse 2 for both multi-agent and single-agent benchmarks, while requiring substantially fewer computational resources and lower latency.
AI.conclusion: The approach effectively relays information across successive timesteps, enabling real-time deployment in real-world autonomous systems without sacrificing predictive accuracy.

title: Forgetting is Competition: Rethinking Unlearning as Representation Interference in Diffusion Models
summary: Unlearning in text-to-image diffusion models often leads to uneven concept removal and unintended forgetting of unrelated capabilities. This complicates tasks such as copyright compliance, protected data mitigation, artist opt-outs, and policy-driven content updates. As models grow larger and adopt more diverse architectures, achieving precise and selective unlearning while preserving generative quality becomes increasingly challenging. We introduce SurgUn (pronounced as Surgeon), a surgical unlearning method that applies targeted weight-space updates to remove specific visual concepts in text-conditioned diffusion models. Our approach is motivated by retroactive interference theory, which holds that newly acquired memories can overwrite, suppress, or impede access to prior ones by competing for shared representational pathways.
