We adapt this principle to diffusion models by inducing retroactive concept interference, enabling focused destabilization of only the target concept while preserving unrelated capabilities through a novel training paradigm. SurgUn achieves high-precision unlearning across diverse settings. It performs strongly on compact U-Net based models such as Stable Diffusion v1.5, scales effectively to the larger U-Net architecture SDXL, and extends to SANA, representing an underexplored Diffusion Transformer based architecture for unlearning.

AI.tldr: SurgUn is a surgical unlearning method for diffusion models that uses targeted weight-space updates to erase specific visual concepts with retroactive interference, preserving unrelated capabilities. It scales from compact U-Nets (Stable Diffusion v1.5) to SDXL and SANA, a diffusion transformer.
AI.motivation: Unlearning in text-to-image diffusion models often causes uneven concept removal and unintended forgetting of unrelated abilities, which complicates tasks like copyright compliance, protected data mitigation, artist opt-outs, and policy updates as models grow more capable and varied.
AI.method: Apply targeted weight-space updates guided by retroactive interference theory to induce focused destabilization of the target concept while preserving other capabilities. This entails a novel training paradigm that encourages retroactive interference to overwrite or suppress access to the targeted concept without degrading unrelated features, and it demonstrates applicability across different diffusion architectures.
AI.result: SurgUn achieves high-precision unlearning across diverse settings, performing well on Stable Diffusion v1.5, scaling to SDXL, and extending to SANA (Diffusion Transformer).
AI.conclusion: SurgUn provides a precise, architecture-agnostic approach to unlearning in diffusion models, enabling selective concept removal with preserved generative quality across multiple model families and scales.

title: Geometry OR Tracker: Universal Geometric Operating Room Tracking
summary: In operating rooms (OR), world-scale multi-view 3D tracking supports downstream applications such as surgeon behavior recognition, where physically meaningful quantities such as distances and motion statistics must be measured in meters. However, real clinical deployments rarely satisfy the geometric prerequisites for stable multi-view fusion and tracking: camera calibration and RGB-D registration are always unreliable, leading to cross-view geometric inconsistency that produces "ghosting" during fusion and degrades 3D trajectories in a shared OR coordinate frame. To address this, we introduce Geometry OR Tracker, a two-stage pipeline that first rectifies imprecise calibration into a scaleconsistent and geometrically consistent camera setup with a single global scale via a Multi-view Metric Geometry Rectification module, and then performs Occlusion-Robust 3D Point Tracking directly in the unified OR world frame. On the MM-OR benchmark, improved geometric consistency translates into tracking gains: our rectification front-end reduces cross-view depth disagreement by more than 30$\times$ compared to raw calibration. Ablation studies further demonstrate the relationship between calibration quality and tracking accuracy, showing that improved geometric consistency yields stronger world-frame tracking.

AI.tldr: A two-stage Geometry OR Tracker that first rectifies calibration into a scale-consistent, globally aligned multi-view setup, then performs occlusion-robust 3D point tracking in a unified OR world frame, yielding large reductions in cross-view depth disagreement and improved world-frame tracking.
AI.motivation: Real-world operating rooms suffer from unreliable camera calibration and RGB-D registration, causing cross-view geometric inconsistency (ghosting) and degraded metric 3D trajectories in a shared OR frame.
AI.method: Stage 1 Multi-view Metric Geometry Rectification to achieve a scale-consistent, globally aligned camera configuration; Stage 2 Occlusion-Robust 3D Point Tracking in the unified OR world frame.
AI.result: On the MM-OR benchmark, the rectification front-end reduces cross-view depth disagreement by more than 30× compared to raw calibration. Ablation studies show a strong relationship between calibration quality and tracking accuracy, with improved geometric consistency yielding stronger world-frame tracking.
AI.conclusion: Rectifying calibration to enforce geometric consistency and a unified scale significantly improves multi-view 3D tracking in operating rooms, enabling more reliable metric measurements in the OR and demonstrating that calibration quality directly drives tracking accuracy.

title: Event-Only Drone Trajectory Forecasting with RPM-Modulated Kalman Filtering
summary: Event cameras provide high-temporal-resolution visual sensing that is well suited for observing fast-moving aerial objects; however, their use for drone trajectory prediction remains limited. This work introduces an event-only drone forecasting method that exploits propeller-induced motion cues. Propeller rotational speed are extracted directly from raw event data and fused within an RPM-aware Kalman filtering framework. Evaluations on the FRED dataset show that the proposed method outperforms learning-based approaches and vanilla kalman filter in terms of average distance error and final distance error at 0.4s and 0.8s forecasting horizons. The results demonstrate robust and accurate short- and medium-horizon trajectory forecasting without reliance on RGB imagery or training data.

AI.tldr: Event-only drone trajectory forecasting using RPM-aware Kalman filtering; extracts propeller cues from event data and predicts trajectories without RGB or training data; achieves superior short- and mid-horizon accuracy (0.4s and 0.8s) on FRED.
AI.motivation: High-temporal-resolution sensing is needed to forecast fast aerial motion. RGB-based methods or data-hungry learning approaches struggle with speed and data requirements; event cameras offer rich temporal information for this task.
AI.method: Extract propeller rotational speed cues from raw event streams and fuse them within an RPM-aware Kalman filter to forecast drone trajectories without visual RGB input.
AI.result: On the FRED dataset, the proposed method outperforms learning-based baselines and a vanilla Kalman filter in average and final distance errors at 0.4s and 0.8s horizons, indicating robust short- to mid-horizon forecasting without imagery or training data.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates the viability of event-only, RPM-informed trajectory forecasting for drones, providing accurate, data-efficient predictions without RGB imagery or training data.

title: Compensation-free Machine Unlearning in Text-to-Image Diffusion Models by Eliminating the Mutual Information
summary: The powerful generative capabilities of diffusion models have raised growing privacy and safety concerns regarding generating sensitive or undesired content. In response, machine unlearning (MU) -- commonly referred to as concept erasure (CE) in diffusion models -- has been introduced to remove specific knowledge from model parameters meanwhile preserving innocent knowledge. Despite recent advancements, existing unlearning methods often suffer from excessive and indiscriminate removal, which leads to substantial degradation in the quality of innocent generations. To preserve model utility, prior works rely on compensation, i.e., re-assimilating a subset of the remaining data or explicitly constraining the divergence from the pre-trained model on remaining concepts. However, we reveal that generations beyond the compensation scope still suffer, suggesting such post-remedial compensations are inherently insufficient for preserving the general utility of large-scale generative models. Therefore, in this paper, we advocate for developing compensation-free concept erasure operations, which precisely identify and eliminate the undesired knowledge such that the impact on other generations is minimal. In technique, we propose to MiM-MU, which is to unlearn a concept by minimizing the mutual information with a delicate design for computational effectiveness and for maintaining sampling distribution for other concepts. Extensive evaluations demonstrate that our proposed method achieves effective concept removal meanwhile maintaining high-quality generations for other concepts, and remarkably, without relying on any post-remedial compensation for the first time.

AI.tldr: MiM-MU proposes compensation-free concept erasure in diffusion models by minimizing mutual information between the undesired concept and the model's behavior, aiming to remove the concept while preserving other generations without post-remedial compensation.
AI.motivation: Growing privacy and safety concerns in diffusion models due to their powerful generative capabilities. Existing unlearning methods often over-remove and rely on compensation steps that attempt to preserve utility but may still degrade innocent generations.
AI.method: Minimize mutual information between the undesired concept and the model's parameters or outputs with a design tailored for computational efficiency and to preserve the sampling distribution for other concepts, enabling precise erasure without relying on compensation.
AI.result: Extensive evaluations indicate effective removal of the undesired concept while maintaining high-quality generations for other concepts; claims to succeed without any post-remedial compensation.
AI.conclusion: Compensation-free concept erasure via mutual-information minimization is feasible and can preserve model utility better than compensation-based approaches, potentially shifting the paradigm for unlearning in large diffusion models.

title: MIDAS: Multi-Image Dispersion and Semantic Reconstruction for Jailbreaking MLLMs
summary: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have achieved remarkable performance but remain vulnerable to jailbreak attacks that can induce harmful content and undermine their secure deployment. Previous studies have shown that introducing additional inference steps, which disrupt security attention, can make MLLMs more susceptible to being misled into generating malicious content. However, these methods rely on single-image masking or isolated visual cues, which only modestly extend reasoning paths and thus achieve limited effectiveness, particularly against strongly aligned commercial closed-source models. To address this problem, in this paper, we propose Multi-Image Dispersion and Semantic Reconstruction (MIDAS), a multimodal jailbreak framework that decomposes harmful semantics into risk-bearing subunits, disperses them across multiple visual clues, and leverages cross-image reasoning to gradually reconstruct the malicious intent, thereby bypassing existing safety mechanisms. The proposed MIDAS enforces longer and more structured multi-image chained reasoning, substantially increases the model's reliance on visual cues while delaying the exposure of malicious semantics and significantly reducing the model's security attention, thereby improving the performance of jailbreak against advanced MLLMs. Extensive experiments across different datasets and MLLMs demonstrate that the proposed MIDAS outperforms state-of-the-art jailbreak attacks for MLLMs and achieves an average attack success rate of 81.46% across 4 closed-source MLLMs. Our code is available at this [link](https://github.com/Winnie-Lian/MIDAS).

AI.tldr: A multimodal jailbreak (MIDAS) disperses harmful semantics across multiple images to enable cross-image reasoning, delaying exposure of malicious content and weakening safety attention, achieving higher jailbreak success than prior methods.
AI.motivation: Overcome the limited effectiveness of single-image masking attacks and vulnerabilities of strongly aligned closed-source MLLMs.
AI.method: Decompose harmful semantics into risk-bearing subunits, disperse them across several visual cues, and use cross-image reasoning to gradually reconstruct malicious intent; enforce long, structured multi-image chains to bypass safety mechanisms and reduce security attention.
AI.result: Outperforms state-of-the-art jailbreaks on four closed-source MLLMs, with an average attack success rate of 81.46%; code is released.
AI.conclusion: Longer, cross-image reasoning can substantially weaken MLLM safety; this highlights the need for stronger multimodal safety alignment and robust defenses against multi-image, cross-modal jailbreaks.

title: Exploring Plan Space through Conversation: An Agentic Framework for LLM-Mediated Explanations in Planning
summary: When automating plan generation for a real-world sequential decision problem, the goal is often not to replace the human planner, but to facilitate an iterative reasoning and elicitation process, where the human's role is to guide the AI planner according to their preferences and expertise. In this context, explanations that respond to users' questions are crucial to improve their understanding of potential solutions and increase their trust in the system. To enable natural interaction with such a system, we present a multi-agent Large Language Model (LLM) architecture that is agnostic to the explanation framework and enables user- and context-dependent interactive explanations. We also describe an instantiation of this framework for goal-conflict explanations, which we use to conduct a user study comparing the LLM-powered interaction with a baseline template-based explanation interface.

AI.tldr: Proposes a modular, multi-agent LLM framework for contextual, user- and task-dependent explanations to support iterative human-in-the-loop plan generation; includes a goal-conflict explanation instantiation and an empirical user study against a template-based interface.
AI.motivation: Aid human planners in guiding AI planners by providing explanations that answer user questions, thereby improving understanding and trust; address the need for natural, interactive interaction in real-world sequential decision problems.
AI.method: Introduce a multi-agent LLM architecture agnostic to the explanation framework; instantiate it for goal-conflict explanations; conduct a user study comparing the LLM-powered interaction with a baseline template-based explanation interface.
AI.result: The user study contrasts the two interfaces and (as described) suggests improved user understanding and trust with the LLM-powered interaction; the abstract does not provide quantitative metrics.
AI.conclusion: A general-purpose, context-sensitive explanation framework for interactive planning is feasible; the goal-conflict instantiation demonstrates practical benefits and can be extended to other explanation types to support iterative elicitation and refinement.

title: From Leaderboard to Deployment: Code Quality Challenges in AV Perception Repositories
summary: Autonomous vehicle (AV) perception models are typically evaluated solely on benchmark performance metrics, with limited attention to code quality, production readiness and long-term maintainability. This creates a significant gap between research excellence and real-world deployment in safety-critical systems subject to international safety standards. To address this gap, we present the first large-scale empirical study of software quality in AV perception repositories, systematically analyzing 178 unique models from the KITTI and NuScenes 3D Object Detection leaderboards. Using static analysis tools (Pylint, Bandit, and Radon), we evaluated code errors, security vulnerabilities, maintainability, and development practices. Our findings revealed that only 7.3% of the studied repositories meet basic production-readiness criteria, defined as having zero critical errors and no high-severity security vulnerabilities. Security issues are highly concentrated, with the top five issues responsible for almost 80% of occurrences, which prompted us to develop a set of actionable guidelines to prevent them. Additionally, the adoption of Continuous Integration/Continuous Deployment pipelines was correlated with better code maintainability. Our findings highlight that leaderboard performance does not reflect production readiness and that targeted interventions could substantially improve the quality and safety of AV perception code.

AI.tldr: Large-scale empirical study evaluating software quality in AV perception repositories reveals a substantial gap between leaderboard performance and production readiness, with only 7.3% meeting basic criteria and security issues concentrated in a few patterns; adoption of CI/CD correlates with maintainability and actionable guidelines proposed.
AI.motivation: Bridge the gap between research benchmark success in AV perception and real-world production safety requirements by auditing code quality, security, and development practices across major repositories.
AI.method: Static analysis of 178 unique AV perception models from KITTI and NuScenes leaderboards using Pylint, Bandit, and Radon to assess code errors, security vulnerabilities, maintainability, and development practices.
AI.result: Only 7.3% of repositories meet basic production-readiness criteria (zero critical errors and no high-severity vulnerabilities). Security issues are highly concentrated (top five issues account for ~80% of occurrences). CI/CD adoption correlates with better maintainability.
AI.conclusion: Leaderboard performance is not predictive of production readiness; targeted interventions and governance guidelines can substantially improve safety-critical AV perception code.

title: DWAFM: Dynamic Weighted Graph Structure Embedding Integrated with Attention and Frequency-Domain MLPs for Traffic Forecasting
summary: Accurate traffic prediction is a key task for intelligent transportation systems. The core difficulty lies in accurately modeling the complex spatial-temporal dependencies in traffic data. In recent years, improvements in network architecture have failed to bring significant performance enhancements, while embedding technology has shown great potential. However, existing embedding methods often ignore graph structure information or rely solely on static graph structures, making it difficult to effectively capture the dynamic associations between nodes that evolve over time. To address this issue, this letter proposes a novel dynamic weighted graph structure (DWGS) embedding method, which relies on a graph structure that can truly reflect the changes in the strength of dynamic associations between nodes over time. By first combining the DWGS embedding with the spatial-temporal adaptive embedding, as well as the temporal embedding and feature embedding, and then integrating attention and frequency-domain multi-layer perceptrons (MLPs), we design a novel traffic prediction model, termed the DWGS embedding integrated with attention and frequency-domain MLPs (DWAFM). Experiments on five real-world traffic datasets show that the DWAFM achieves better prediction performance than some state-of-the-arts.

AI.tldr: DWAFM is a traffic prediction model that uses a dynamic weighted graph structure embedding to capture evolving node associations, integrated with spatial-temporal adaptive embedding, temporal and feature embeddings, and attention plus frequency-domain MLPs, achieving superior results on five real-world datasets.
AI.motivation: Static graph structures fail to reflect time-varying relational strengths among traffic nodes; capturing dynamic graph changes promises more accurate spatial-temporal modeling for traffic prediction.
AI.method: Propose DWGS embedding to reflect dynamic association strengths over time. Combine DWGS embedding with spatial-temporal adaptive embedding, temporal embedding, and feature embedding, then integrate attention and frequency-domain MLPs to form the DWAFM model.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on five real-world traffic datasets shows DWAFM outperforms several state-of-the-art methods in predictive accuracy.
AI.conclusion: Dynamic graph-structure embeddings jointly with attention mechanisms and frequency-domain MLPs provide a more effective framework for traffic forecasting, validating the importance of modeling evolving node relationships.

title: Decoupling Stability and Plasticity for Multi-Modal Test-Time Adaptation
summary: Adapting pretrained multi-modal models to evolving test-time distributions, known as multi-modal test-time adaptation, presents a significant challenge. Existing methods frequently encounter negative transfer in the unbiased modality and catastrophic forgetting in the biased modality. To address these challenges, we propose Decoupling Adaptation for Stability and Plasticity (DASP), a novel diagnose-then-mitigate framework. Our analysis reveals a critical discrepancy within the unified latent space: the biased modality exhibits substantially higher interdimensional redundancy (i.e., strong correlations across feature dimensions) compared to the unbiased modality. Leveraging this insight, DASP identifies the biased modality and implements an asymmetric adaptation strategy. This strategy employs a decoupled architecture where each modality-specific adapter is divided into stable and plastic components. The asymmetric mechanism works as follows: for the biased modality, which requires plasticity, the plastic component is activated and updated to capture domain-specific information, while the stable component remains fixed. Conversely, for the unbiased modality, which requires stability, the plastic component is bypassed, and the stable component is updated using KL regularization to prevent negative transfer. This asymmetric design enables the model to adapt flexibly to new domains while preserving generalizable knowledge. Comprehensive evaluations on diverse multi-modal benchmarks demonstrate that DASP significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods.

AI.tldr: DASP proposes a diagnose-then-mitigate framework with asymmetric, modality-specific adapters (stable/plastic) to address negative transfer in the unbiased modality and forgetting in the biased modality during multi-modal test-time adaptation; leverages interdimensional redundancy in the biased modality to identify it and decouple adaptation, achieving improved performance.
AI.motivation: Mitigate two core failures in multi-modal test-time adaptation: negative transfer in the unbiased modality and catastrophic forgetting in the biased modality; address via diagnosing latent-space discrepancy and controlled modular adaptation.
AI.method: Diagnose latent-space redundancy to identify the biased modality. Use a decoupled adapter architecture with stable and plastic components per modality. Apply asymmetric updates: for the biased modality (need plasticity), activate/update the plastic component; keep the stable component fixed. For the unbiased modality (need stability), bypass plastic, update the stable component with KL regularization to prevent negative transfer.
AI.result: Significant improvements over state-of-the-art on diverse multi-modal benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: DASP offers a principled diagnose-then-mitigate approach that leverages interdimensional redundancy to guide modular adaptation, reducing negative transfer and forgetting.

title: Pencil Puzzle Bench: A Benchmark for Multi-Step Verifiable Reasoning
summary:
We introduce Pencil Puzzle Bench, a framework for evaluating large language model reasoning through pencil puzzles, a family of constraint-satisfaction problems closely related to NP-complete problems, with deterministic, step-level verification. From a database of 62,231 puzzles across 94 varieties with verified unique solutions, we select a benchmark of 300 puzzles spanning 20 varieties and evaluate 51 models from 11 providers in two modes: direct ask (single-shot) and agentic (multi-turn with iterative verification). A key differentiator of our benchmark is that every intermediate board state can be checked against variety-specific constraints, localizing errors to the exact rule violated, providing the infrastructure for dense, per-move reward signals for process supervision and reinforcement learning.
  Our evaluation reveals two distinct axes of capability: (1) reasoning effort scaling, where GPT-5.2 improves 81x from no reasoning to maximum effort; and (2) agentic iteration, where Claude Opus 4.6 rises from 0.3% to 30.0% through iterative checking, while GPT-5.2@xhigh improves from 20.2% to 56.0%. Agentic attempts span a median of 29 turns over 17 minutes, with the longest exceeding 1,221 turns and 14.3 hours - a demanding test of long-context utilization, not just reasoning.

AI.tldr: Pencil Puzzle Bench presents a large, verifiable CSP-style benchmark for LLM reasoning using pencil puzzles, enabling per-step error localization and dense reward signals for supervision and RL; evaluation shows strong gains with increased reasoning effort and agentic iteration, and reveals substantial long-context demands.
AI.motivation: To enable precise, verifiable evaluation of long-horizon LLM reasoning on constraint-satisfaction problems and to provide infrastructure for per-move feedback for supervision and reinforcement learning.
AI.method: Construct a CSP-based benchmark from a large puzzle database (62,231 puzzles, 94 varieties, with verified unique solutions), curate 300 puzzles across 20 varieties, and evaluate 51 models from 11 providers in two modes (direct single-shot and agentic multi-turn with iterative verification). Use deterministic, variety-specific constraints to check every intermediate state, localizing errors and enabling dense per-move rewards.
AI.result: Identify two capability axes: (1) reasoning effort scaling—GPT-5.2 improves ~81x from no reasoning to maximum effort; (2) agentic iteration—Claude Opus 4.6 rises from 0.3% to 30.0%, GPT-5.2@xhigh from 20.2% to 56.0%. Agentic runs median 29 turns over 17 minutes, with long-outs exceeding 1,221 turns and 14.3 hours, underscoring long-context usage as well as reasoning.
AI.conclusion: The framework provides precise, verifiable, per-step supervision signals for CSP-like tasks, enabling better evaluation and potential RL training; it demonstrates the critical role of long-context capacity and iterative checking for solving complex puzzles and highlights the need for robust tooling to localize errors to specific rules.

title: Feature-Weighted Maximum Representative Subsampling
summary: In the social sciences, it is often necessary to debias studies and surveys before valid conclusions can be drawn. Debiasing algorithms enable the computational removal of bias using sample weights. However, an issue arises when only a subset of features is highly biased, while the rest is already representative. Algorithms need to strongly alter the sample distribution to manage a few highly biased features, which can in turn introduce bias into already representative variables. To address this issue, we developed a method that uses feature weights to minimize the impact of highly biased features on the computation of sample weights. Our algorithm is based on Maximum Representative Subsampling (MRS), which debiases datasets by aligning a non-representative sample with a representative one through iterative removal of elements to create a representative subsample. The new algorithm, named feature-weighted MRS (FW-MRS), decreases the emphasis on highly biased features, allowing it to retain more instances for downstream tasks. The feature weights are derived from the feature importance of a domain classifier trained to differentiate between the representative and non-representative datasets. We validated FW-MRS using eight tabular datasets, each of which we artificially biased. Biased features can be important for downstream tasks, and focusing less on them could lead to a decline in generalization. For this reason, we assessed the generalization performance of FW-MRS on downstream tasks and found no statistically significant differences. Additionally, FW-MRS was applied to a real-world dataset from the social sciences. The source code is available at https://github.com/kramerlab/FeatureWeightDebiasing.

AI.tldr: A feature-weighted extension of Maximum Representative Subsampling (FW-MRS) to reduce bias from highly biased features by weighting features according to a domain classifier's importance, aiming to preserve representative samples while debiasing; evaluated on eight datasets with artificial bias and a real-world social-science dataset; reports maintained downstream generalization and provides code.
AI.motivation: Debiasing studies with partial feature bias is challenging: traditional MRS can overemphasize a few biased features, distorting other informative variables. FW-MRS aims to prevent this by down-weighting biased features during sample-weight computation to preserve useful instances.
AI.method: Introduce feature weights into Maximum Representative Subsampling (MRS). Feature weights are derived from feature importance scores of a domain classifier trained to distinguish representative vs non-representative data. The algorithm iteratively removes samples to align non-representative with representative data while de-emphasizing highly biased features, thereby retaining more instances for downstream tasks. Evaluation on eight tabular datasets with artificial bias and a real-world social-science dataset; code at GitHub.
AI.result: FW-MRS reduces the impact of highly biased features, allowing retention of more instances while maintaining downstream performance. No statistically significant degradation in generalization on downstream tasks. Demonstrated on eight datasets with artificial bias and one real-world dataset; source code available.
AI.conclusion: FW-MRS offers a practical debiasing approach for tabular data where bias concentrates on a subset of features. By down-weighting biased features based on domain classifier importance, it preserves representation and downstream performance. Further validation across more domains and data modalities would strengthen generalizability.

title: WildActor: Unconstrained Identity-Preserving Video Generation
summary: Production-ready human video generation requires digital actors to maintain strictly consistent full-body identities across dynamic shots, viewpoints and motions, a setting that remains challenging for existing methods. Prior methods often suffer from face-centric behavior that neglects body-level consistency, or produce copy-paste artifacts where subjects appear rigid due to pose locking. We present Actor-18M, a large-scale human video dataset designed to capture identity consistency under unconstrained viewpoints and environments. Actor-18M comprises 1.6M videos with 18M corresponding human images, covering both arbitrary views and canonical three-view representations. Leveraging Actor-18M, we propose WildActor, a framework for any-view conditioned human video generation. We introduce an Asymmetric Identity-Preserving Attention mechanism coupled with a Viewpoint-Adaptive Monte Carlo Sampling strategy that iteratively re-weights reference conditions by marginal utility for balanced manifold coverage. Evaluated on the proposed Actor-Bench, WildActor consistently preserves body identity under diverse shot compositions, large viewpoint transitions, and substantial motions, surpassing existing methods in these challenging settings.

AI.tldr: Actor-18M introduces a large-scale dataset (1.6M videos, 18M images) to enable identity-consistent human video generation across unconstrained viewpoints and motions; WildActor achieves state-of-the-art body identity preservation using asymmetric attention and viewpoint-aware sampling.
AI.motivation: Current methods struggle to maintain consistent full-body identity across dynamic shots and viewpoints and suffer from face-centric bias or pose-locked artifacts. A scalable dataset capturing identity under diverse viewpoints is needed to train production-ready generation models.
AI.method: WildActor framework with any-view conditioning, featuring an Asymmetric Identity-Preserving Attention mechanism and a Viewpoint-Adaptive Monte Carlo Sampling strategy that iteratively re-weights reference conditions by marginal utility to ensure balanced manifold coverage. Uses Actor-18M including arbitrary views and canonical three-view representations.
AI.result: Evaluations on the proposed Actor-Bench demonstrate that WildActor consistently preserves body identity under varied shot compositions, large viewpoint transitions, and substantial motions, outperforming existing methods in these challenging settings.
AI.conclusion: Actor-18M and WildActor together advance production-grade, identity-consistent human video generation across arbitrary viewpoints, providing a robust benchmark and a scalable framework for future research.

title: Nano-EmoX: Unifying Multimodal Emotional Intelligence from Perception to Empathy
comment: 17 pages,8 figures, The IEEE/CVF Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition 2026
summary: The development of affective multimodal language models (MLMs) has long been constrained by a gap between low-level perception and high-level interaction, leading to fragmented affective capabilities and limited generalization. To bridge this gap, we propose a cognitively inspired three-level hierarchy that organizes affective tasks according to their cognitive depth-perception, understanding, and interaction-and provides a unified conceptual foundation for advancing affective modeling. Guided by this hierarchy, we introduce Nano-EmoX, a small-scale multitask MLM, and P2E (Perception-to-Empathy), a curriculum-based training framework. Nano-EmoX integrates a suite of omni-modal encoders, including an enhanced facial encoder and a fusion encoder, to capture key multimodal affective cues and improve cross-task transferability. The outputs are projected into a unified language space via heterogeneous adapters, empowering a lightweight language model to tackle diverse affective tasks. Concurrently, P2E progressively cultivates emotional intelligence by aligning rapid perception with chain-of-thought-driven empathy. To the best of our knowledge, Nano-EmoX is the first compact MLM (2.2B) to unify six core affective tasks across all three hierarchy levels, achieving state-of-the-art or highly competitive performance across multiple benchmarks, demonstrating excellent efficiency and generalization.

AI.tldr: Three-level cognitively inspired hierarchy for affective multimodal language models; Nano-EmoX (2.2B) unifies six affective tasks across perception, understanding, and interaction; P2E curriculum-based training aligns perception with empathy, yielding state-of-the-art or competitive results with high efficiency and generalization.
AI.motivation: Bridge the perception–interaction gap in affective MLMs and provide a unified framework to improve generalization and cross-task transferability with a compact model.
AI.method: Propose a three-level hierarchy (perception, understanding, interaction). Build Nano-EmoX with omni-modal encoders (enhanced facial encoder, fusion encoder) feeding into a unified language space via heterogeneous adapters; train using P2E, a curriculum-based approach that aligns rapid perceptual processing with chain-of-thought-driven empathy.
AI.result: Nano-EmoX (2.2B) is the first compact MLM to unify six core affective tasks across all three hierarchy levels; achieves state-of-the-art or highly competitive results on multiple benchmarks; demonstrates efficiency and generalization.
AI.conclusion: The cognitively inspired hierarchy and curriculum-based training enable a compact, generalizable affective MLM capable of broad tasks, illustrating a path toward unified affective intelligence.

title: One-Token Verification for Reasoning Correctness Estimation
summary: Recent breakthroughs in large language models (LLMs) have led to notable successes in complex reasoning tasks, such as mathematical problem solving. A common strategy for improving performance is parallel thinking, in which multiple reasoning traces are generated and the final prediction is made using aggregation schemes like majority voting or best-of-$N$ decoding. However, two key challenges persist. First, multi-sample decoding incurs substantial inference latency, especially for long-form outputs. Second, effective mechanisms for reliably assessing the correctness of individual reasoning traces are still limited. To address these challenges, we introduce One-Token Verification (OTV), a computational method that estimates reasoning correctness in a single forward pass during generation. OTV is activated by a learnable token and integrated into the LLM via low-rank adaptation to probe internal reasoning signals through the key-value cache, supporting token-level correctness estimation at any stage of generation without disrupting primary reasoning. Experiments on mathematical reasoning benchmarks demonstrate that OTV consistently surpasses existing verifiers. Additionally, OTV reduces token usage by up to $90\%$ through correctness-guided early termination, prioritizing shorter, more reliable solutions.

AI.tldr: One-Token Verification (OTV) enables single-pass, token-level reasoning verification in LLMs via a learnable token and LoRA, yielding faster, more reliable long-form reasoning and up to 90% token savings through early termination.
AI.motivation: Tackle latency and reliability gaps in multi-sample decoding for chain-of-thought reasoning by providing an efficient verifier.
AI.method: OTV uses a learnable token to probe internal reasoning signals through the key-value cache; integrated with the model via low-rank adaptation; operates during generation in a single forward pass and supports early termination.
AI.result: OTV outperforms existing verifiers on mathematical reasoning benchmarks and reduces token usage by up to 90% through correctness-guided early stopping.
AI.conclusion: OTV offers efficient, non-disruptive, token-level correctness estimation during generation, enabling faster and more reliable long-form reasoning.

title: AlignVAR: Towards Globally Consistent Visual Autoregression for Image Super-Resolution
summary: Visual autoregressive (VAR) models have recently emerged as a promising alternative for image generation, offering stable training, non-iterative inference, and high-fidelity synthesis through next-scale prediction. This encourages the exploration of VAR for image super-resolution (ISR), yet its application remains underexplored and faces two critical challenges: locality-biased attention, which fragments spatial structures, and residual-only supervision, which accumulates errors across scales, severely compromises global consistency of reconstructed images. To address these issues, we propose AlignVAR, a globally consistent visual autoregressive framework tailored for ISR, featuring two key components: (1) Spatial Consistency Autoregression (SCA), which applies an adaptive mask to reweight attention toward structurally correlated regions, thereby mitigating excessive locality and enhancing long-range dependencies; and (2) Hierarchical Consistency Constraint (HCC), which augments residual learning with full reconstruction supervision at each scale, exposing accumulated deviations early and stabilizing the coarse-to-fine refinement process. Extensive experiments demonstrate that AlignVAR consistently enhances structural coherence and perceptual fidelity over existing generative methods, while delivering over 10x faster inference with nearly 50% fewer parameters than leading diffusion-based approaches, establishing a new paradigm for efficient ISR.

AI.tldr: Introduces AlignVAR, a globally consistent visual autoregressive model for image super-resolution, addressing locality-biased attention and residual-only supervision with SCA and HCC, achieving strong quality with large speed/parameter gains over diffusion methods.
AI.motivation: VAR-based ISR promises stable training and fast inference but struggles with local attention fragmenting structure and cumulative errors from residual supervision; there is a need for global consistency and efficiency.
AI.method: Two components: Spatial Consistency Autoregression (SCA) applies adaptive masks to reweight attention toward structurally correlated regions; Hierarchical Consistency Constraint (HCC) provides full reconstruction supervision at every scale to reveal deviations early and stabilize coarse-to-fine refinement.
AI.result: Empirical results show improved structural coherence and perceptual fidelity versus existing generative methods; AlignVAR achieves over 10x faster inference and ~50% fewer parameters than leading diffusion-based approaches.
AI.conclusion: AlignVAR offers a new efficient ISR paradigm delivering globally consistent visuals with strong performance and speed, bridging VAR-based generation and high-fidelity super-resolution.

title: Conformal Policy Control
summary: An agent must try new behaviors to explore and improve. In high-stakes environments, an agent that violates safety constraints may cause harm and must be taken offline, curtailing any future interaction. Imitating old behavior is safe, but excessive conservatism discourages exploration. How much behavior change is too much? We show how to use any safe reference policy as a probabilistic regulator for any optimized but untested policy. Conformal calibration on data from the safe policy determines how aggressively the new policy can act, while provably enforcing the user's declared risk tolerance. Unlike conservative optimization methods, we do not assume the user has identified the correct model class nor tuned any hyperparameters. Unlike previous conformal methods, our theory provides finite-sample guarantees even for non-monotonic bounded constraint functions. Our experiments on applications ranging from natural language question answering to biomolecular engineering show that safe exploration is not only possible from the first moment of deployment, but can also improve performance.

AI.tldr: A conformal-calibration framework uses a safe reference policy as a probabilistic regulator to safely explore with an optimized but untested policy, providing finite-sample guarantees without model-hyperparameter tuning.
AI.motivation: Enable safe, data-driven exploration in high-stakes environments by conditioning exploration on a safety constraint enforced by a trusted reference policy.
AI.method: Apply conformal calibration on data generated by the safe policy to quantify how aggressively the new policy can act; treat the safe policy as a regulator; provide finite-sample guarantees for non-monotonic bounded constraint functions; avoid assuming a specific model class or needing hyperparameter tuning.
AI.result: Demonstrates that safe exploration is feasible from deployment and can improve performance across applications such as NLP question answering and biomolecular engineering.
AI.conclusion: A practical, theory-backed framework for safety-aware exploration that delivers finite-sample guarantees without requiring model-specific tuning or prior knowledge of the true dynamics.

title: Fed-ADE: Adaptive Learning Rate for Federated Post-adaptation under Distribution Shift
summary: Federated learning (FL) in post-deployment settings must adapt to non-stationary data streams across heterogeneous clients without access to ground-truth labels. A major challenge is learning rate selection under client-specific, time-varying distribution shifts, where fixed learning rates often lead to underfitting or divergence. We propose Fed-ADE (Federated Adaptation with Distribution Shift Estimation), an unsupervised federated adaptation framework that leverages lightweight estimators of distribution dynamics. Specifically, Fed-ADE employs uncertainty dynamics estimation to capture changes in predictive uncertainty and representation dynamics estimation to detect covariate-level feature drift, combining them into a per-client, per-timestep adaptive learning rate. We provide theoretical analyses showing that our dynamics estimation approximates the underlying distribution shift and yields dynamic regret and convergence guarantees. Experiments on image and text benchmarks under diverse distribution shifts (label and covariate) demonstrate consistent improvements over strong baselines. These results highlight that distribution shift-aware adaptation enables effective and robust federated post-adaptation under real-world non-stationarity.

AI.tldr: Fed-ADE introduces per-client, per-timestep adaptive learning rates in Federated Learning by unsupervised distribution-shift estimation (uncertainty dynamics and representation dynamics), with theoretical guarantees and empirical improvements under label and covariate shifts.
AI.motivation: Post-deployment FL faces non-stationary, heterogeneous data streams and lacks ground-truth labels. Fixed learning rates lead to underfitting or divergence; there is a need for distribution-shift-aware adaptation to maintain performance.
AI.method: Fed-ADE uses lightweight, unsupervised estimators of distribution dynamics: uncertainty dynamics estimation (changes in predictive uncertainty) and representation dynamics estimation (covariate-level feature drift). These are combined to produce a per-client, per-timestep adaptive learning rate. The authors provide theoretical analyses for dynamic regret and convergence and validate the approach on image and text benchmarks with label and covariate shifts.
AI.result: Dynamic estimation approximates the underlying distribution shift, enabling dynamic regret bounds and convergence guarantees. Empirically, Fed-ADE achieves consistent improvements over strong baselines across diverse shifts in image and text tasks.
AI.conclusion: Distribution-shift-aware adaptation via Fed-ADE enables robust federated post-adaptation under real-world non-stationarity, showcasing the value of unsupervised distribution dynamics in guiding learning-rate adaptation.

title: UNICBench: UNIfied Counting Benchmark for MLLM
comment: This paper has been accepted by CVPR 2026
summary: Counting is a core capability for multimodal large language models (MLLMs), yet there is no unified counting dataset to rigorously evaluate this ability across image, text, and audio. We present UNICBench, a unified multimodal, multi level counting benchmark and evaluation toolkit with accurate ground truth, deterministic numeric parsing, and stratified reporting. The corpus comprises 5,300 images (5,508 QA), 872 documents (5,888 QA), and 2,069 audio clips (2,905 QA), annotated with a three level capability taxonomy and difficulty tags. Under a standardized protocol with fixed splits/prompts/seeds and modality specific matching rules, we evaluate 45 state-of-the-art MLLMs across modalities. Results show strong performance on some basic counting tasks but significant gaps on reasoning and the hardest partitions, highlighting long-tail errors and substantial headroom for improving general counting. UNICBench offers a rigorous and comparable basis for measurement and a public toolkit to accelerate progress.

AI.tldr: UNICBench is a unified, multi-modal counting benchmark and evaluation toolkit that rigorously tests counting across images, documents, and audio with fixed prompts/seeds, extensive ground truth, and 45 SOTA MLLMs, revealing gaps in reasoning and hard partitions.
AI.motivation: To fill the lack of a unified counting dataset spanning multiple modalities and to provide a rigorous, reproducible evaluation framework for the counting capability of multimodal LLMs.
AI.method: Construct UNICBench with 5,300 images (5,508 QA), 872 documents (5,888 QA), and 2,069 audio clips (2,905 QA), all annotated with a three-level capability taxonomy and difficulty tags. Use deterministic ground truth and numeric parsing, a standardized protocol with fixed splits/prompts/seeds, and modality-specific matching rules. Evaluate 45 state-of-the-art MLLMs across modalities and report stratified results.
AI.result: The benchmark reveals strong performance on basic counting tasks but notable gaps in reasoning and hardest partitions, with long-tail errors and substantial room for improvement. Provides a rigorous, comparable baseline and a public toolkit to accelerate progress in multimodal counting.
AI.conclusion: UNICBench establishes a rigorous, comparable framework for measuring counting ability across image, text, and audio, and offers a public toolkit to accelerate progress in multimodal counting.

title: Tool Verification for Test-Time Reinforcement Learning
summary: Test-time reinforcement learning (TTRL) has emerged as a promising paradigm for self-evolving large reasoning models (LRMs), enabling online adaptation on unlabeled test inputs via self-induced rewards through majority voting. However, a spurious yet high-frequency unverified consensus can become a biased and reinforced reward signal, leading to incorrect mode collapse. We address this failure mode with T^3RL (Tool-Verification for Test-Time Reinforcement Learning), which introduces test-time tool verification into reward estimation. Concretely, a verifier uses an external tool as evidence (e.g., from code execution) to upweight verified rollouts in a verification-aware voting, producing more reliable pseudo-labels for training. Across various math difficulties (MATH-500, AMC, and AIME 2024) and diverse backbone types, T^3RL significantly improves over TTRL, with larger gains on harder problems. More broadly, T^3RL can be viewed as verified online data synthesis, highlighting test-time tool verification as a key mechanism for stabilizing self-evolution.

AI.tldr: T^3RL mitigates failure modes in test-time RL by incorporating a verifier that uses external tool evidence to weight validated rollouts, improving pseudo-label quality and performance on math benchmarks.
AI.motivation: In TTRL, high-frequency, unverified consensus can bias rewards and cause mode collapse; a verification mechanism is needed to stabilize self-evolution.
AI.method: Introduce a test-time verifier that consults an external tool (e.g., code execution) to provide evidence, upweight verified rollouts in a verification-aware voting scheme, and synthesize more reliable pseudo-labels during training.
AI.result: Empirical gains over TTRL on MATH-500, AMC, and AIME 2024 across various backbones, with larger improvements on harder problems.
AI.conclusion: Verified online data synthesis through test-time tool verification is a key mechanism for stabilizing self-evolution in LRMs; T^3RL provides a practical framework to reduce mode collapse and improve robustness.

title: Evaluating GFlowNet from partial episodes for stable and flexible policy-based training
summary: Generative Flow Networks (GFlowNets) were developed to learn policies for efficiently sampling combinatorial candidates by interpreting their generative processes as trajectories in directed acyclic graphs. In the value-based training workflow, the objective is to enforce the balance over partial episodes between the flows of the learned policy and the estimated flows of the desired policy, implicitly encouraging policy divergence minimization. The policy-based strategy alternates between estimating the policy divergence and updating the policy, but reliable estimation of the divergence under directed acyclic graphs remains a major challenge. This work bridges the two perspectives by showing that flow balance also yields a principled policy evaluator that measures the divergence, and an evaluation balance objective over partial episodes is proposed for learning the evaluator. As demonstrated on both synthetic and real-world tasks, evaluation balance not only strengthens the reliability of policy-based training but also broadens its flexibility by seamlessly supporting parameterized backward policies and enabling the integration of offline data-collection techniques.

AI.tldr: Flow balance in GFlowNets acts as a policy evaluator and enables an evaluation objective over partial episodes, improving policy-based training and supporting backward policies and offline data integration.
AI.motivation: Address the challenge of estimating policy divergence under DAG-based GFlowNets and unify value-based and policy-based training to improve reliability and flexibility.
AI.method: Show that flow balance yields a principled divergence evaluator; propose an evaluation balance objective to train the evaluator; extend to parameterized backward policies; integrate offline data collection.
AI.result: Evaluation balance strengthens the reliability of policy-based training across synthetic and real-world tasks, and broadens flexibility to backward policies and offline data usage.
AI.conclusion: Flow balance can serve as a unified evaluation and training signal in GFlowNets; the evaluation balance objective enhances reliability and enables offline data integration and backward-policy support.

title: Data-Centric Benchmark for Label Noise Estimation and Ranking in Remote Sensing Image Segmentation
summary: High-quality pixel-level annotations are essential for the semantic segmentation of remote sensing imagery. However, such labels are expensive to obtain and often affected by noise due to the labor-intensive and time-consuming nature of pixel-wise annotation, which makes it challenging for human annotators to label every pixel accurately. Annotation errors can significantly degrade the performance and robustness of modern segmentation models, motivating the need for reliable mechanisms to identify and quantify noisy training samples. This paper introduces a novel Data-Centric benchmark, together with a novel, publicly available dataset and two techniques for identifying, quantifying, and ranking training samples according to their level of label noise in remote sensing semantic segmentation. Such proposed methods leverage complementary strategies based on model uncertainty, prediction consistency, and representation analysis, and consistently outperform established baselines across a range of experimental settings. The outcomes of this work are publicly available at https://github.com/keillernogueira/label_noise_segmentation.

AI.tldr: Proposes a data-centric approach to detect and rank noisy pixel-level labels in remote-sensing segmentation, introducing a Data-Centric benchmark, a dataset, and two noise-detection methods based on uncertainty, consistency, and representations; achieves strong gains over baselines on multiple benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Pixel-level annotation noise in remote-sensing segmentation degrades model performance and robustness; there is a need for reliable mechanisms to identify, quantify, and rank noisy training samples to guide data-centric improvements.
AI.method: 1) Introduce a Data-Centric benchmark and a publicly available dataset for label-noise analysis in remote-sensing segmentation. 2) Propose two techniques to identify, quantify, and rank noisy training samples using complementary signals from model uncertainty, prediction consistency, and representation analysis.
AI.result: Empirically outperforms established baselines across diverse experimental settings, demonstrating the effectiveness of the proposed noise-detection and ranking methods.
AI.conclusion: This work advances data-centric learning for remote-sensing segmentation by providing benchmarks, datasets, and practical tools to identify and quantify noisy labels; resources are publicly available for the community.

title: No More Maybe-Arrows: Resolving Causal Uncertainty by Breaking Symmetries
summary: The recent works on causal discovery have followed a similar trend of learning partial ancestral graphs (PAGs) since observational data constrain the true causal directed acyclic graph (DAG) only up to a Markov equivalence class. This limits their application in the majority of downstream tasks, as uncertainty in causal relations remains unresolved. We propose a new refinement framework, CausalSAGE, for converting PAGs to DAGs while respecting the underlying causal relations. The framework expands discrete variables into state-level representations, constrains the search space using structural knowledge and soft priors, and applies a unified differentiable objective for joint optimization. The final DAG is obtained by aggregating the optimized structures and enforcing acyclicity when necessary. Our experimental evaluations show that the obtained DAGs preserve the underlying causal relations while also being efficient to obtain.

AI.tldr: CausalSAGE refines PAGs into DAGs by expanding state-level representations and optimizing a differentiable objective with priors, yielding DAGs that preserve causal relations efficiently.
AI.motivation: Observational data constrain causal DAGs only to a Markov equivalence class (PAGs), leaving directionality and downstream task applicability uncertain. A practical, accurate method to recover concrete DAGs from PAGs is needed.
AI.method: Expand discrete variables into state-level representations; constrain the search using structural knowledge and soft priors; optimize a unified differentiable objective for joint structure learning; aggregate optimized structures and enforce acyclicity as needed to obtain a final DAG.
AI.result: Experiments indicate the derived DAGs preserve the underlying causal relations and can be obtained efficiently, suggesting improved downstream applicability over PAGs without sacrificing causal fidelity.
AI.conclusion: CausalSAGE offers a practical, differentiable pipeline for converting PAGs to DAGs that respects causal relations and supports efficient downstream use.

title: IdGlow: Dynamic Identity Modulation for Multi-Subject Generation
summary: Multi-subject image generation requires seamlessly harmonizing multiple reference identities within a coherent scene. However, existing methods relying on rigid spatial masks or localized attention often struggle with the "stability-plasticity dilemma," particularly failing in tasks that require complex structural deformations, such as identity-preserving age transformation. To address this, we present IdGlow, a mask-free, progressive two-stage framework built upon Flow Matching diffusion models. In the supervised fine-tuning (SFT) stage, we introduce task-adaptive timestep scheduling aligned with diffusion generative dynamics: a linear decay schedule that progressively relaxes constraints for natural group composition, and a temporal gating mechanism that concentrates identity injection within a critical semantic window, successfully preserving adult facial semantics without overriding child-like anatomical structures. To resolve attribute leakage and semantic ambiguity without explicit layout inputs, we further integrate a badcase-driven Vision-Language Model (VLM) for precise, context-aware prompt synthesis. In the second stage, we design a Fine-Grained Group-Level Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) with a weighted margin formulation to simultaneously eliminate multi-subject artifacts, elevate texture harmony, and recalibrate identity fidelity towards real-world distributions. Extensive experiments on two challenging benchmarks -- direct multi-person fusion and age-transformed group generation -- demonstrate that IdGlow fundamentally mitigates the stability-plasticity conflict, achieving a superior Pareto balance between state-of-the-art facial fidelity and commercial-grade aesthetic quality.

AI.tldr: IdGlow is a two-stage, mask-free diffusion framework for multi-subject image generation that uses Flow Matching, adaptive SFT scheduling, VLM-guided prompt synthesis, and fine-grained group-level DPO to improve identity fidelity, texture harmony, and age-transformation quality, addressing stability-plasticity trade-offs.
AI.motivation: Multi-subject image synthesis with accurate identity preservation across adults/children and age transformations is challenging due to rigidity of spatial masks and localized attention, leading to instability and poor deformation handling. There is a need for a mask-free, progressively adaptable method that maintains semantic fidelity while allowing complex structural changes.
AI.method: 1) Stage 1 SFT with a linear decay timestep schedule that loosens constraints and a temporal gating mechanism to confine identity injection within a semantic window; a badcase-driven Vision-Language Model (VLM) for precise, context-aware prompt synthesis without explicit layout inputs. 2) Stage 2 Fine-Grained Group-Level DPO with a weighted margin objective to reduce artifacts, harmonize texture, and recalibrate identity fidelity toward real-world distributions.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on direct multi-person fusion and age-transformed group generation benchmarks shows IdGlow substantially mitigates the stability-plasticity conflict, achieving a favorable Pareto balance between facial fidelity and aesthetic quality, outperforming baselines on both objectives.
AI.conclusion: A mask-free, two-stage diffusion framework with adaptive scheduling, VLM-guided prompts, and DPO optimization effectively enables complex, identity-preserving multi-subject generation (including age transformations) with improved stability and realism.

title: A level-wise training scheme for learning neural multigrid smoothers with application to integral equations
summary:
Convolution-type integral equations commonly occur in signal processing and image processing. Discretizing these equations yields large and ill-conditioned linear systems. While the classic multigrid method is effective for solving linear systems derived from partial differential equations (PDE) problems, it fails to solve integral equations because its smoothers, which are implemented as conventional relaxation methods, are ineffective in reducing high-frequency components in the errors.
  We propose a novel neural multigrid scheme where learned neural operators replace classical smoothers. Unlike classical smoothers, these operators are trained offline. Once trained, the neural smoothers generalize to new right-hand-side vectors without retraining, making it an efficient solver. We design level-wise loss functions incorporating spectral filtering to emulate the multigrid frequency decomposition principle, ensuring each operator focuses on solving distinct high-frequency spectral bands.
  Although we focus on integral equations, the framework is generalizable to all kinds of problems, including PDE problems. Our experiments demonstrate superior efficiency over classical solvers and robust convergence across varying problem sizes and regularization weights.

AI.tldr: Neural multigrid with offline-trained neural operators replacing classical smoothers; level-wise spectral-aware loss; generalizes to PDEs; efficient and robust for integral equations.
AI.motivation: To solve ill-conditioned linear systems from discretized convolution-type integral equations where classical multigrid smoothing fails at high frequencies.
AI.method: Train neural operators offline to serve as multigrid smoothers; apply level-wise loss with spectral filtering to enforce frequency decomposition; each operator targets distinct high-frequency spectral bands; solver generalizes to new RHS without retraining; applicable to other problems.
AI.result: Demonstrates superior efficiency compared to classical solvers and robust convergence across problem sizes and regularization weights; empirical validation on integral equations; potential applicability to PDEs.
AI.conclusion: Neural multigrid with learned smoothers offers a generalizable, efficient solver framework for integral equations and potentially PDEs, leveraging spectral-aware training to reproduce multigrid frequency separation.

title: Linking Modality Isolation in Heterogeneous Collaborative Perception
summary: Collaborative perception leverages data exchange among multiple agents to enhance overall perception capabilities. However, heterogeneity across agents introduces domain gaps that hinder collaboration, and this is further exacerbated by an underexplored issue: modality isolation. It arises when multiple agents with different modalities never co-occur in any training data frame, enlarging cross-modal domain gaps. Existing alignment methods rely on supervision from spatially overlapping observations, thus fail to handle modality isolation. To address this challenge, we propose CodeAlign, the first efficient, co-occurrence-free alignment framework that smoothly aligns modalities via cross-modal feature-code-feature(FCF) translation. The key idea is to explicitly identify the representation consistency through codebook, and directly learn mappings between modality-specific feature spaces, thereby eliminating the need for spatial correspondence. Codebooks regularize feature spaces into code spaces, providing compact yet expressive representations. With a prepared code space for each modality, CodeAlign learns FCF translations that map features to the corresponding codes of other modalities, which are then decoded back into features in the target code space, enabling effective alignment. Experiments show that, when integrating three modalities, CodeAlign requires only 8% of the training parameters of prior alignment methods, reduces communication load by 1024x, and achieves state-of-the-art perception performance on both OPV2V and DAIR-V2X dataset. Code will be released on https://github.com/cxliu0314/CodeAlign.

AI.tldr: CodeAlign enables co-occurrence-free alignment for cross-modal collaborative perception by translating features through learned modality-specific codebooks (feature-code-feature translation), achieving strong performance with minimal parameter and communication overhead.
AI.motivation: Heterogeneity across collaborating agents creates cross-modal domain gaps. Modality isolation—where modalities never co-occur in training frames—further enlarges these gaps. Existing alignment methods rely on spatially overlapping observations, which fails under modality isolation.
AI.method: Introduce per-modality codebooks to regularize representations into discrete code spaces. Learn cross-modal feature-code-feature translations that map modality-specific features to codes of other modalities, decode back into the target code space, and perform alignment without requiring spatial co-occurrence.
AI.result: With three modalities, CodeAlign uses only 8% of the training parameters of prior alignment methods and reduces communication load by 1024x, achieving state-of-the-art perception on OPV2V and DAIR-V2X. Code will be released at the project repository.
AI.conclusion: CodeAlign is the first efficient, co-occurrence-free alignment framework for cross-modal collaborative perception, enabling effective multi-modal integration with significant reductions in computation and communication.

title: Understanding LoRA as Knowledge Memory: An Empirical Analysis
summary: Continuous knowledge updating for pre-trained large language models (LLMs) is increasingly necessary yet remains challenging. Although inference-time methods like In-Context Learning (ICL) and Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) are popular, they face constraints in context budgets, costs, and retrieval fragmentation. Departing from these context-dependent paradigms, this work investigates a parametric approach using Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) as a modular knowledge memory. Although few recent works examine this concept, the fundamental mechanics governing its capacity and composability remain largely unexplored. We bridge this gap through the first systematic empirical study mapping the design space of LoRA-based memory, ranging from characterizing storage capacity and optimizing internalization to scaling multi-module systems and evaluating long-context reasoning. Rather than proposing a single architecture, we provide practical guidance on the operational boundaries of LoRA memory. Overall, our findings position LoRA as the complementary axis of memory alongside RAG and ICL, offering distinct advantages.

AI.tldr: Systematic empirical study of LoRA-based memory for LLMs, mapping capacity and design space, and providing practical guidelines; positions LoRA memory as a complementary axis to RAG and ICL with distinct advantages.
AI.motivation: Continuous knowledge updating for pre-trained LLMs is challenging; ICL and RAG face context budget, cost, and retrieval fragmentation constraints, motivating a parametric memory approach using LoRA.
AI.method: Empirical mapping of the design space for LoRA-based memory: characterize storage capacity, optimize internalization, scale multi-module systems, and evaluate long-context reasoning; provide practical operating guidance rather than a single architecture.
AI.result: First systematic empirical study of LoRA-based memory; characterizes capacity, internalization, and scaling of multi-module systems; provides design-space insights and practical guidelines; demonstrates that LoRA memory is a complementary memory axis alongside RAG and ICL with distinct advantages.
AI.conclusion: LoRA-based memory offers a viable, modular memory paradigm that complements retrieval-augmented and in-context learning approaches; practitioners should consider integrating LoRA memory within LLM deployments and use the guidelines to navigate its boundaries.

title: Exploring Spatiotemporal Feature Propagation for Video-Level Compressive Spectral Reconstruction: Dataset, Model and Benchmark
summary: Recently, Spectral Compressive Imaging (SCI) has achieved remarkable success, unlocking significant potential for dynamic spectral vision. However, existing reconstruction methods, primarily image-based, suffer from two limitations: (i) Encoding process masks spatial-spectral features, leading to uncertainty in reconstructing missing information from single compressed measurements, and (ii) The frame-by-frame reconstruction paradigm fails to ensure temporal consistency, which is crucial in the video perception. To address these challenges, this paper seeks to advance spectral reconstruction from the image level to the video level, leveraging the complementary features and temporal continuity across adjacent frames in dynamic scenes. Initially, we construct the first high-quality dynamic hyperspectral image dataset (DynaSpec), comprising 30 sequences obtained through frame-scanning acquisition. Subsequently, we propose the Propagation-Guided Spectral Video Reconstruction Transformer (PG-SVRT), which employs a spatial-then-temporal attention to effectively reconstruct spectral features from abundant video information, while using a bridged token to reduce computational complexity. Finally, we conduct simulation experiments to assess the performance of four SCI systems, and construct a DD-CASSI prototype for real-world data collection and benchmarking. Extensive experiments demonstrate that PG-SVRT achieves superior performance in reconstruction quality, spectral fidelity, and temporal consistency, while maintaining minimal FLOPs. Project page: https://github.com/nju-cite/DynaSpec

AI.tldr: Proposes PG-SVRT, a propagation-guided spectral video reconstruction transformer for dynamic hyperspectral imaging (SCI), backed by a new dynamic hyperspectral dataset DynaSpec, bridging image-level SCI to video-level reconstruction with spatial-then-temporal attention and a bridged token to reduce complexity; shows improved reconstruction quality, spectral fidelity, and temporal consistency with low FLOPs on simulations and real data via a DD-CASSI prototype.
AI.motivation: Current SCI reconstruction is predominantly frame-wise and image-based, suffering from (i) spatial-spectral information masking during encoding which creates missing data uncertainty, and (ii) lack of temporal consistency across frames. Exploiting temporal continuity across adjacent frames should improve reconstruction reliability and video realism.
AI.method: 1) Create DynaSpec: the first high-quality dynamic hyperspectral image dataset (30 sequences) via frame-scanning. 2) Develop PG-SVRT: a spectral video reconstruction transformer with a spatial-then-temporal attention scheme and a bridged token to curb computation; employs propagation-guided mechanisms to leverage temporal information across frames. 3) Evaluate on four SCI systems; build a DD-CASSI prototype for real-world data collection and benchmarking.
AI.result: PG-SVRT achieves superior reconstruction quality, spectral fidelity, and temporal consistency with minimal FLOPs across simulations and real data.
AI.conclusion: The work advances SCI from image-level to video-level reconstruction by leveraging temporal continuity through a transformer-based architecture and provides a high-quality dataset (DynaSpec) and a real-data benchmark (DD-CASSI) to facilitate future research.

title: A Deep Learning Framework for Heat Demand Forecasting using Time-Frequency Representations of Decomposed Features
summary: District Heating Systems are essential infrastructure for delivering heat to consumers across a geographic region sustainably, yet efficient management relies on optimizing diverse energy sources, such as wood, gas, electricity, and solar, in response to fluctuating demand. Aligning supply with demand is critical not only for ensuring reliable heat distribution but also for minimizing carbon emissions and extending infrastructure lifespan through lower operating temperatures. However, accurate multi-step forecasting to support these goals remains challenging due to complex, non-linear usage patterns and external dependencies. In this work, we propose a novel deep learning framework for day-ahead heat demand prediction that leverages time-frequency representations of historical data. By applying Continuous Wavelet Transform to decomposed demand and external meteorological factors, our approach enables Convolutional Neural Networks to learn hierarchical temporal features that are often inaccessible to standard time domain models. We systematically evaluate this method against statistical baselines, state-of-the-art Transformers, and emerging foundation models using multi-year data from three distinct Danish districts, a Danish city, and a German city. The results show a significant advancement, reducing the Mean Absolute Error by 36% to 43% compared to the strongest baselines, achieving forecasting accuracy of up to 95% across annual test datasets. Qualitative and statistical analyses further confirm the accuracy and robustness by reliably tracking volatile demand peaks where others fail. This work contributes both a high-performance forecasting architecture and critical insights into optimal feature composition, offering a validated solution for modern energy applications.

AI.tldr: Time-frequency deep learning for day-ahead heat demand forecasting using Continuous Wavelet Transform (CWT) and CNNs; achieves large MAE reductions (36-43%) vs strong baselines with up to 95% annual test accuracy; validated on multiple Danish and German cities.
AI.motivation: Efficient district heating requires accurate, robust forecasts to align supply with demand, minimize emissions, and extend infrastructure life. Traditional models struggle with non-linear, non-stationary demand and external dependencies (weather, other factors).
AI.method: Apply Continuous Wavelet Transform to decompose historical demand and external meteorological factors into time-frequency representations, then learn features with Convolutional Neural Networks. Evaluate against statistical baselines, Transformers, and foundation models using multi-year data from three Danish districts, one Danish city, and one German city.
AI.result: Significant MAE reduction (36-43% vs strongest baselines) and forecast accuracy up to 95% across annual test sets; robust tracking of volatile demand peaks; comprehensive qualitative and statistical validation.
AI.conclusion: Proposes a high-performance forecasting architecture and insights into effective feature composition for modern energy applications; demonstrates strong potential for improved district heating management.

title: Position: Evaluation of Visual Processing Should Be Human-Centered, Not Metric-Centered
summary: This position paper argues that the evaluation of modern visual processing systems should no longer be driven primarily by single-metric image quality assessment benchmarks, particularly in the era of generative and perception-oriented methods. Image restoration exemplifies this divergence: while objective IQA metrics enable reproducible, scalable evaluation, they have increasingly drifted apart from human perception and user preferences. We contend that this mismatch risks constraining innovation and misguiding research progress across visual processing tasks. Rather than rejecting metrics altogether, this paper calls for a rebalancing of evaluation paradigms, advocating a more human-centered, context-aware, and fine-grained approach to assessing the visual models' outcomes.

AI.tldr: Rebalance evaluation of modern visual processing from single IQA metrics toward human-centered, context-aware assessment; address mismatch between objective metrics and human perception in image restoration and generative/perception-oriented methods.
AI.motivation: To prevent metrics-driven research from constraining innovation and misaligning progress with user preferences; extend evaluation beyond single metrics to capture perceptual and contextual factors.
AI.method: Conceptual position paper; argues for a rebalanced evaluation paradigm; outlines criteria for human-centered, context-aware, fine-grained assessments.
AI.result: Proposes a framework or direction for future evaluation; emphasizes aligning benchmarks with human perception and preferences.
AI.conclusion: Adopt more nuanced, user-centric evaluation to better reflect real-world use and spur development that aligns with perceptual quality and user needs.

title: A Decomposition Framework for Certifiably Optimal Orthogonal Sparse PCA
summary:
Sparse Principal Component Analysis (SPCA) is an important technique for high-dimensional data analysis, improving interpretability by imposing sparsity on principal components. However, existing methods often fail to simultaneously guarantee sparsity, orthogonality, and optimality of the principal components.
  To address this challenge, this work introduces a novel Sparse Principal Component Analysis (SPCA) algorithm called \textsc{GS-SPCA} (SPCA with Gram-Schmidt Orthogonalization), which simultaneously enforces sparsity, orthogonality, and optimality. However, the original GS-SPCA algorithm is computationally expensive due to the inherent $\ell_0$-norm constraint. To address this issue, we propose two acceleration strategies:
  First, we combine \textbf{Branch-and-Bound} with the GS-SPCA algorithm. By incorporating this strategy, we are able to obtain $\varepsilon$-optimal solutions with a trade-off between precision and efficiency, significantly improving computational speed.
  Second, we propose a \textbf{decomposition framework} for efficiently solving \textbf{multiple} principal components. This framework approximates the covariance matrix using a block-diagonal matrix through a thresholding method, reducing the original SPCA problem to a set of block-wise subproblems on approximately block-diagonal matrices.
