AI.tldr: GS-SPCA enforces sparsity, orthogonality, and optimality via Gram-Schmidt; to beat computational cost, it uses Branch-and-Bound for ε-optimal solutions and a decomposition framework that approximates the covariance as block-diagonal to solve multiple components efficiently.
AI.motivation: Enhance interpretability and statistical efficiency of SPCA by achieving sparsity, orthogonality, and optimality simultaneously, while addressing high computational complexity.
AI.method: Introduce GS-SPCA algorithm with Gram-Schmidt orthogonalization; accelerate with (1) Branch-and-Bound to obtain ε-optimal solutions, trading precision for speed; (2) a decomposition framework that approximates the covariance matrix by a block-diagonal structure, converting the problem into parallelizable, block-wise subproblems for multiple PCs.
AI.result: Demonstrates improved computational efficiency through ε-optimal Branch-and-Bound and block-wise decomposition; enables faster computation of multiple sparse PCs while maintaining sparsity and orthogonality constraints (the extent of empirical gains not specified in the abstract).
AI.conclusion: GS-SPCA, augmented with Branch-and-Bound and a covariance decomposition strategy, offers a scalable framework for sparse, orthogonal, and near-optimal principal components.

title: Exploring 3D Dataset Pruning
summary: Dataset pruning has been widely studied for 2D images to remove redundancy and accelerate training, while particular pruning methods for 3D data remain largely unexplored. In this work, we study dataset pruning for 3D data, where its observed common long-tail class distribution nature make optimization under conventional evaluation metrics Overall Accuracy (OA) and Mean Accuracy (mAcc) inherently conflicting, and further make pruning particularly challenging. To address this, we formulate pruning as approximating the full-data expected risk with a weighted subset, which reveals two key errors: coverage error from insufficient representativeness and prior-mismatch bias from inconsistency between subset-induced class weights and target metrics. We propose representation-aware subset selection with per-class retention quotas for long-tail coverage, and prior-invariant teacher supervision using calibrated soft labels and embedding-geometry distillation. The retention quota also serves as a switch to control the OA-mAcc trade-off. Extensive experiments on 3D datasets show that our method can improve both metrics across multiple settings while adapting to different downstream preferences. Our code is available at https://github.com/XiaohanZhao123/3D-Dataset-Pruning.

AI.tldr: A study on pruning 3D datasets to address long-tail distributions by selecting a representative subset with per-class quotas and using calibrated soft labels and embedding-geometry distillation, achieving better OA and mAcc and tunable trade-offs.
AI.motivation: 3D data pruning is underexplored; long-tail distributions cause OA and mAcc to conflict, making pruning difficult; need to approximate full-data risk with a subset addressing coverage and bias.
AI.method: Representation-aware subset selection with per-class retention quotas to ensure long-tail coverage; prior-invariant teacher supervision via calibrated soft labels and embedding-geometry distillation; the quota also tunes OA-mAcc trade-off.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on 3D datasets show improvements in OA and mAcc across settings; method adapts to downstream preferences; code released.
AI.conclusion: Pruning that accounts for coverage and prior mismatch in 3D data yields better accuracy metrics and configurable trade-offs; offers practical, implementable approach with strong empirical gains.

title: Demystifying Group Relative Policy Optimization: Its Policy Gradient is a U-Statistic
summary: Group relative policy optimization (GRPO), a core methodological component of DeepSeekMath and DeepSeek-R1, has emerged as a cornerstone for scaling reasoning capabilities of large language models. Despite its widespread adoption and the proliferation of follow-up works, the theoretical properties of GRPO remain less studied. This paper provides a unified framework to understand GRPO through the lens of classical U-statistics. We demonstrate that the GRPO policy gradient is inherently a U-statistic, allowing us to characterize its mean squared error (MSE), derive the finite-sample error bound and asymptotic distribution of the suboptimality gap for its learned policy. Our findings reveal that GRPO is asymptotically equivalent to an oracle policy gradient algorithm -- one with access to a value function that quantifies the goodness of its learning policy at each training iteration -- and achieves asymptotically optimal performance within a broad class of policy gradient algorithms. Furthermore, we establish a universal scaling law that offers principled guidance for selecting the optimal group size. Empirical experiments further validate our theoretical findings, demonstrating that the optimal group size is universal, and verify the oracle property of GRPO.

AI.tldr: GRPO is cast as a U-statistic, enabling theoretical guarantees: MSE bounds, finite-sample error, and asymptotic distribution; asymptotically equivalent to an oracle policy gradient; universal scaling law for optimal group size; empirical validation confirms universality and oracle properties.
AI.motivation: GRPO is widely used in scalable reasoning with large language models, but its theoretical properties are under-explored. A unified, statistically grounded framework can elucidate performance, guide practice (e.g., group sizing), and connect GRPO to classical estimators.
AI.method: Frame GRPO as a U-statistic. Derive mean squared error (MSE) of the learned policy, finite-sample error bounds, and the asymptotic distribution of the suboptimality gap. Prove asymptotic equivalence to an oracle policy gradient that uses a value function. Establish a universal scaling law for selecting the group size. Validate results with empirical experiments showing a universal optimal group size and the oracle property.
AI.result: The GRPO gradient is a U-statistic, enabling precise characterization of its error. The learned policy’s suboptimality gap has a derived finite-sample bound and an asymptotic distribution. GRPO is asymptotically equivalent to an oracle policy gradient that has access to a value function, and hence achieves asymptotically optimal performance within a broad class of policy gradient methods. A universal scaling law for the group size is established, indicating a principled way to choose the group size. Experiments corroborate that the optimal group size is universal and that GRPO exhibits the oracle property.
AI.conclusion: The paper provides a rigorous statistical backbone for GRPO, linking it to U-statistics and oracle-gradient behavior, and offering guidance on hyperparameter (group size) selection. The results suggest GRPO's asymptotic optimality within policy gradients and general applicability across settings, supported by empirical validation.

title: RC-GeoCP: Geometric Consensus for Radar-Camera Collaborative Perception
summary: Collaborative perception (CP) enhances scene understanding through multi-agent information sharing. While LiDAR-centric systems offer precise geometry, high costs and performance degradation in adverse weather necessitate multi-modal alternatives. Despite dense visual semantics and robust spatial measurements, the synergy between cameras and 4D radar remains underexplored in collaborative settings. This work introduces RC-GeoCP, the first framework to explore the fusion of 4D radar and images in CP. To resolve misalignment caused by depth ambiguity and spatial dispersion across agents, RC-GeoCP establishes a radar-anchored geometric consensus. Specifically, Geometric Structure Rectification (GSR) aligns visual semantics with geometry derived from radar to generate spatially grounded, geometry-consistent representations. Uncertainty-Aware Communication (UAC) formulates selective transmission as a conditional entropy reduction process to prioritize informative features based on inter-agent disagreement. Finally, the Consensus-Driven Assembler (CDA) aggregates multi-agent information via shared geometric anchors to form a globally coherent representation. We establish the first unified radar-camera CP benchmark on V2X-Radar and V2X-R, demonstrating state-of-the-art performance with significantly reduced communication overhead. Code will be released soon.

AI.tldr: Radar-camera collaborative perception framework RC-GeoCP that uses radar-anchored geometry to rectify camera features, selects informative communications via uncertainty-aware transmission, and fuses multi-agent data through shared geometric anchors; achieves SOTA with lower communication cost on V2X radar benchmarks; code coming soon.
AI.motivation: Fill the gap in multi-modal CP by leveraging 4D radar to address depth ambiguity and inter-agent misalignment while reducing communication overhead.
AI.method: Geometric Structure Rectification (GSR) aligns visual semantics with radar-derived geometry; Uncertainty-Aware Communication (UAC) prioritizes features via conditional entropy reduction based on inter-agent disagreement; Consensus-Driven Assembler (CDA) aggregates information using shared geometric anchors to produce a global, geometry-consistent representation.
AI.result: First unified radar-camera CP benchmark on V2X-Radar and V2X-V; achieves state-of-the-art performance with significantly reduced communication overhead; code will be released soon.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates that radar-guided geometric consensus enables effective radar-camera CP with lower communication costs and robust multi-agent fusion; provides a scalable framework for future cross-modal CP research.

title: SphUnc: Hyperspherical Uncertainty Decomposition and Causal Identification via Information Geometry
summary: Reliable decision-making in complex multi-agent systems requires calibrated predictions and interpretable uncertainty. We introduce SphUnc, a unified framework combining hyperspherical representation learning with structural causal modeling. The model maps features to unit hypersphere latents using von Mises-Fisher distributions, decomposing uncertainty into epistemic and aleatoric components through information-geometric fusion. A structural causal model on spherical latents enables directed influence identification and interventional reasoning via sample-based simulation. Empirical evaluations on social and affective benchmarks demonstrate improved accuracy, better calibration, and interpretable causal signals, establishing a geometric-causal foundation for uncertainty-aware reasoning in multi-agent settings with higher-order interactions.

AI.tldr: Unified hyperspherical representation with structural causal modeling (SphUnc) for calibrated, interpretable uncertainty in multi-agent systems.
AI.motivation: There is a need for calibrated predictions and interpretable uncertainty in complex multi-agent settings; existing approaches struggle with higher-order interactions and interpretable uncertainty, motivating a geometry-plus-causality framework.
AI.method: Map features to unit hypersphere latents via von Mises-Fisher distributions; decompose uncertainty into epistemic and aleatoric components through information-geometric fusion; construct a structural causal model on spherical latents to identify directed influences and perform interventional reasoning via sample-based simulation.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on social and affective benchmarks shows improved accuracy, better calibration, and interpretable causal signals, demonstrating a geometric-causal foundation for uncertainty-aware reasoning in multi-agent settings with higher-order interactions.
AI.conclusion: SphUnc provides a principled geometric-causal framework for uncertainty-aware decision-making in complex multi-agent systems, enabling calibrated predictions and interpretable interventions.

title: Stateful Cross-layer Vision Modulation
summary: Recent multimodal large language models (MLLMs) widely adopt multi-layer visual feature fusion to enhance visual representation. However, existing approaches typically perform static concatenation or weighted aggregation after visual encoding, without intervening in the representation formation process itself. As a result, fine-grained details from early layers may be progressively suppressed during hierarchical abstraction. Moreover, directly introducing shallow-layer features into the language model often leads to semantic distribution mismatch with the visual feature space that the LLM's cross-attention layers were pretrained on, which typically requires additional adaptation or fine-tuning of the LLM. To address these limitations, we revisit visual representation learning from the perspective of representation evolution control and propose a cross-layer memory-modulated vision framework(SCVM). Specifically, we introduce a recursively updated cross-layer memory state inside the vision encoder to model long-range inter-layer dependencies. We further design a layer-wise feedback modulation mechanism that refreshes token representations at each layer based on the accumulated memory, thereby structurally regulating the representation evolution trajectory. In addition, we incorporate an auxiliary semantic alignment objective that explicitly supervises the final memory state, encouraging progressive compression and reinforcement of task-relevant information. Experimental results on multiple visual question answering and hallucination evaluation benchmarks demonstrate that SCVM achieves consistent performance improvements without expanding visual tokens, introducing additional vision encoders, or modifying or fine-tuning the language model.

AI.tldr: SCVM proposes a cross-layer memory-modulated vision framework to evolve visual representations within the vision encoder, improving detail preservation and semantic alignment without altering the LLM or token count.
AI.motivation: Current multimodal models fail to preserve fine-grained early-layer details and suffer from semantic mismatch when injecting shallow features into LLMs, due to static fusion and lack of representation evolution control.
AI.method: Introduce a recursively updated cross-layer memory state in the vision encoder to model long-range inter-layer dependencies. Implement layer-wise feedback modulation that refreshes token representations at each layer using the accumulated memory. Add an auxiliary semantic alignment objective to supervise the final memory state for progressive compression and reinforcement of task-relevant information.
AI.result: Empirical gains on multiple VQA and hallucination benchmarks, achieved without increasing visual tokens, adding new vision encoders, or fine-tuning the LLM.
AI.conclusion: SCVM demonstrates that controlling representation evolution via cross-layer memory and feedback can enhance multimodal fusion efficiency and accuracy, preserving detail and alignment without modifying the LLM.

title: PARWiS: Winner determination under shoestring budgets using active pairwise comparisons
summary: Determining a winner among a set of items using active pairwise comparisons under a limited budget is a challenging problem in preference-based learning. The goal of this study is to implement and evaluate the PARWiS algorithm, which shows spectral ranking and disruptive pair selection to identify the best item under shoestring budgets. This work have extended the PARWiS with a contextual variant (Contextual PARWiS) and a reinforcement learning-based variant (RL PARWiS), comparing them against baselines, including Double Thompson Sampling and a random selection strategy. This evaluation spans synthetic and real-world datasets (Jester and MovieLens), using budgets of 40, 60, and 80 comparisons for 20 items. The performance is measured through recovery fraction, true rank of reported winner, reported rank of true winner, and cumulative regret, alongside the separation metric \(Δ_{1,2}\). Results show that PARWiS and RL PARWiS outperform baselines across all datasets, particularly in the Jester dataset with a higher \(Δ_{1,2}\), while performance gaps narrow in the more challenging MovieLens dataset with a smaller \(Δ_{1,2}\). Contextual PARWiS shows comparable performance to PARWiS, indicating that contextual features may require further tuning to provide significant benefits.

AI.tldr: PARWiS is a budget-aware active learning method for selecting a winner via pairwise comparisons, extended with contextual and RL variants. It outperforms baselines (Double Thompson Sampling, random) on synthetic, Jester, and MovieLens data, with stronger gains on Jester (larger Δ1,2) and smaller gains on MovieLens (smaller Δ1,2). Contextual PARWiS is on par with PARWiS, suggesting potential gains require tuning.
AI.motivation: To efficiently identify a best item under budget constraints using pairwise comparisons in preference-based learning, leveraging spectral ranking and disruptive pair selection.
AI.method: Implement PARWiS with spectral ranking and disruptive pair selection; extend to Contextual PARWiS and RL PARWiS; compare against baselines (Double Thompson Sampling, random). Evaluate on synthetic data, Jester, and MovieLens with budgets 40, 60, 80 for 20 items; assess recovery fraction, true/reported winner rank, cumulative regret, and Δ1,2 separation.
AI.result: PARWiS and RL PARWiS consistently outperform baselines across datasets; strongest gains on Jester with higher Δ1,2; smaller gains on MovieLens with lower Δ1,2; Contextual PARWiS performs comparably to PARWiS, indicating contextual features require further tuning.
AI.conclusion: Spectral ranking plus disruptive pair selection underpin effective winner identification under tight budgets; the base PARWiS and its RL variant are robust across datasets; contextual enhancements show potential but need tuning to surpass the non-contextual variant.

title: Act Like a Pathologist: Tissue-Aware Whole Slide Image Reasoning
summary: Computational pathology has advanced rapidly in recent years, driven by domain-specific image encoders and growing interest in using vision-language models to answer natural-language questions about diseases. Yet, the core problem behind pathology question-answering remains unsolved, considering that a gigapixel slide contains far more information than necessary for a given question. Pathologists naturally navigate tissue and morphology complexity by scanning broadly, and zooming in selectively according to the clinical questions. Current models, in contrast, rely on uniform patch sampling or broad attention maps, often attending equally to irrelevant regions while overlooking key visual evidence. In this work, we try to bring models closer to how humans actually examine slides. We propose a question-guided, tissue-aware, and coarse-to-fine retrieval framework, HistoSelect, that consists of two key components: a group sampler that identifies question-relevant tissue regions, followed by a patch selector that retrieves the most informative patches within those regions. By selecting only the most informative patches, our method becomes significantly more efficient: reducing visual token usage by 70% on average, while improving accuracy across three pathology QA tasks. Evaluated on 356,000 question-answer pairs, our approach outperforms existing methods and produces answers grounded in interpretable, pathologist-consistent regions. Our results suggest that bringing human-like search and attention patterns into WSI reasoning is a promising direction for building practical and reliable pathology VLMs.

AI.tldr: HistoSelect uses a question-guided, tissue-aware coarse-to-fine retrieval to identify relevant tissue regions and selectively retrieve informative patches, reducing visual tokens by 70% and improving pathology QA accuracy with interpretable grounding.
AI.motivation: WSIQA tasks suffer from information overload: gigapixel slides contain far more data than needed for a given question. Uniform patch sampling and broad attention often miss key evidence and waste computation. Mimicking how pathologists search—broad scanning then selective zooming—could improve efficiency and reliability.
AI.method: Two-stage framework: (1) a group sampler identifies question-relevant tissue regions on whole-slide images, and (2) a patch selector retrieves the most informative patches within those regions. This coarse-to-fine retrieval reduces visual token usage while maintaining or improving QA performance.
AI.result: On 356k QA pairs across three pathology QA tasks, the method reduces visual tokens by ~70% and improves accuracy versus baselines, with answers grounded in interpretable, pathologist-consistent regions.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating human-like search and attention patterns into WSI reasoning is a promising direction for practical, reliable pathology vision-language models.

title: Scaling of learning time for high dimensional inputs
summary: Representation learning from complex data typically involves models with a large number of parameters, which in turn require large amounts of data samples. In neural network models, model complexity grows with the number of inputs to each neuron, with a trade-off between model expressivity and learning time. A precise characterization of this trade-off would help explain the connectivity and learning times observed in artificial and biological networks. We present a theoretical analysis of how learning time depends on input dimensionality for a Hebbian learning model performing independent component analysis. Based on the geometry of high-dimensional spaces, we show that the learning dynamics reduce to a unidimensional problem, with learning times dependent only on initial conditions. For higher input dimensions, initial parameters have smaller learning gradients and larger learning times. We find that learning times have supralinear scaling, becoming quickly prohibitive for high input dimensions. These results reveal a fundamental limitation for learning in high dimensions and help elucidate how the optimal design of neural networks depends on data complexity. Our approach outlines a new framework for analyzing learning dynamics and model complexity in neural network models.

AI.tldr: In a Hebbian ICA model, learning time grows supralinearly with input dimension because high-dimensional spaces reduce learning gradients, making learning time prohibitively long; dynamics effectively become one-dimensional.
AI.motivation: To explain how learning time and model complexity scale with input dimensionality in neural networks, shedding light on the connectome constraints in artificial and biological systems and guiding network design.
AI.method: Theoretical analysis leveraging high-dimensional geometry to derive that Hebbian learning dynamics for ICA reduce to a one-dimensional problem; analysis of gradient magnitudes and learning-time scaling with dimension.
AI.result: Learning times scale supralinearly with input dimension; initialization gradients shrink with higher dimensions, increasing learning time; the dynamics collapse to a unidimensional problem regardless of full dimensionality.
AI.conclusion: Highlights a fundamental high-dimensional learning limitation and suggests a framework for assessing model complexity and data-driven network design.

title: Direct low-field MRI super-resolution using undersampled k-space
comment: 4 pages, 4 figures, conference (The IEEE International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI))
summary: Low-field magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) provides affordable access to diagnostic imaging but suffers from prolonged acquisition and limited image quality. Accelerated imaging can be achieved with k-space undersampling, while super-resolution (SR) and image quality transfer (IQT) methods typically rely on spatial-domain post-processing. In this work, we propose a novel framework for reconstructing high-field MR like images directly from undersampled low-field k-space. Our approach employs a k-space dual channel U-Net that processes the real and imaginary components of undersampled k-space to restore missing frequency content. Experiments on low-field brain MRI demonstrate that our k-space-driven image enhancement consistently outperforms the counterpart spatial-domain method. Furthermore, reconstructions from undersampled k-space achieve image quality comparable to full k-space acquisitions. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first work that investigates low-field MRI SR/IQT directly from undersampled k-space.

AI.tldr: Reconstruct high-field-like MR images from undersampled low-field k-space using a k-space dual-channel U-Net, outperforming spatial-domain methods and approaching full k-space quality; novel SR/IQT directly in k-space.
AI.motivation: Low-field MRI is affordable but suffers from long acquisitions and limited image quality. Accelerated imaging via k-space undersampling is desirable, and existing SR/IQT methods operate in image space; this work aims to transfer SR/IQT into the k-space domain to restore missing frequency content.
AI.method: Introduces a k-space dual-channel U-Net that processes the real and imaginary components of undersampled k-space to restore missing frequency content and synthesize high-field-like images.
AI.result: Experiments on low-field brain MRI show the k-space–driven method consistently outperforms spatial-domain counterparts, with reconstructions from undersampled k-space achieving image quality comparable to full k-space acquisitions.
AI.conclusion: Claims to be the first work to investigate low-field MRI SR/IQT directly from undersampled k-space, establishing a new direction for SR/IQT in MRI.

title: Operator Learning Using Weak Supervision from Walk-on-Spheres
summary: Training neural PDE solvers is often bottlenecked by expensive data generation or unstable physics-informed neural network (PINN) that involves challenging optimization landscapes due to higher-order derivatives. To tackle this issue, we propose an alternative approach using Monte Carlo approaches to estimate the solution to the PDE as a stochastic process for weak supervision during training. Leveraging the walk-on-spheres method, we introduce a learning scheme called \emph{Walk-on-Spheres Neural Operator (WoS-NO)} which uses weak supervision from WoS to train any given neural operator. We propose to amortize the cost of Monte Carlo walks across the distribution of PDE instances using stochastic representations from the WoS algorithm to generate cheap, noisy, estimates of the PDE solution during training. This is formulated into a data-free physics-informed objective where a neural operator is trained to regress against these weak supervisions, allowing the operator to learn a generalized solution map for an entire family of PDEs. This strategy results in a mesh-free framework that operates without expensive pre-computed datasets, avoids the need for computing higher-order derivatives for loss functions that are memory-intensive and unstable, and demonstrates zero-shot generalization to novel PDE parameters and domains. Experiments show that for the same number of training steps, our method exhibits up to 8.75$\times$ improvement in $L_2$-error compared to standard physics-informed training schemes, up to 6.31$\times$ improvement in training speed, and reductions of up to 2.97$\times$ in GPU memory consumption. We present the code at https://github.com/neuraloperator/WoS-NO

AI.tldr: WoS-NO trains neural operators via Monte Carlo, weak supervision from the walk-on-spheres (WoS) stochastic representation, enabling data-free, mesh-free training without higher-order derivatives. It yields zero-shot generalization to new PDEs, with substantial gains in accuracy, speed, and memory over standard PINNs; code released.
AI.motivation: Address data-generation bottlenecks and instability/inefficiency of physics-informed neural networks that rely on higher-order derivatives and costly gradient computations.
AI.method: Use Monte Carlo WoS-based weak supervision to train any neural operator. Amortize MC walks across a distribution of PDE instances to generate cheap, noisy PDE solutions. Formulate a data-free physics-informed objective so the operator learns a generalized solution map for a family of PDEs, without precomputed datasets and without requiring high-order derivative losses.
AI.result: For the same training steps, achieves up to 8.75x improvement in L2 error, up to 6.31x faster training, and up to 2.97x memory reduction (GPU). Demonstrates zero-shot generalization to novel PDE parameters and domains.
AI.conclusion: WoS-NO provides an effective, efficient, mesh-free operator learning framework that generalizes across PDE families using weak, Monte Carlo-based supervision, mitigating PINN optimization drawbacks; code available.

title: Specializing Foundation Models via Mixture of Low-Rank Experts for Comprehensive Head CT Analysis
summary: Foundation models pre-trained on large-scale datasets demonstrate strong transfer learning capabilities; however, their adaptation to complex multi-label diagnostic tasks-such as comprehensive head CT finding detection-remains understudied. Standard parameter-efficient fine-tuning methods such as LoRA apply uniform adaptations across pathology types, which may limit performance for diverse medical findings. We propose a Mixture of Low-Rank Experts (MoLRE) framework that extends LoRA with multiple specialized low-rank adapters and unsupervised soft routing. This approach enables conditional feature adaptation with less than 0.5% additional parameters and without explicit pathology supervision. We present a comprehensive benchmark of MoLRE across six state-of-the-art medical imaging foundation models spanning 2D and 3D architectures, general-domain, medical-domain, and head CT-specific pretraining, and model sizes ranging from 7M to 431M parameters. Using over 70,000 non-contrast head CT scans with 75 annotated findings-including hemorrhage, infarction, trauma, mass lesions, structural abnormalities, and chronic changes-our experiments demonstrate consistent performance improvements across all models. Gains vary substantially: general-purpose and medical-domain models show the largest improvements (DINOv3-Base: +4.6%; MedGemma: +4.3%), whereas 3D CT-specialized or very large models show more modest gains (+0.2-1.3%). The combination of MoLRE and MedGemma achieves the highest average detection AUC of 0.917. These findings highlight the importance of systematic benchmarking on target clinical tasks, as pretraining domain, architecture, and model scale interact in non-obvious ways.

AI.tldr: MoLRE extends LoRA with multiple specialized low-rank adapters and unsupervised soft routing to enable conditional feature adaptation with minimal parameter overhead, evaluated on head CT multi-label findings across diverse foundation models, yielding consistent gains and a highest AUC of 0.917 with MedGemma.
AI.motivation: Address the limitation of uniform, pathology-agnostic fine-tuning in large medical imaging foundation models for complex multi-label tasks (e.g., head CT findings) and to enable family-wide, parameter-efficient adaptation without explicit pathology supervision.
AI.method: Introduce Mixture of Low-Rank Experts (MoLRE): multiple specialized low-rank adapters plus unsupervised soft routing atop LoRA; evaluate across six SOTA 2D/3D medical imaging foundation models pretrained in general-domain, medical-domain, or head CT-specific settings; train/fine-tune on a dataset of 70k non-contrast head CT scans with 75 annotated findings; measure detection AUC and compare gains across models; achieve <0.5% additional parameters.
AI.result: MoLRE yields consistent performance improvements across all models. Larger gains for general-purpose and medical-domain models (e.g., DINOv3-Base +4.6%; MedGemma +4.3%), smaller gains for 3D CT-specialized or very large models (+0.2–1.3%). The combination MoLRE+MedGemma achieves the highest average detection AUC of 0.917.
AI.conclusion: Systematic benchmarking reveals that pretraining domain, architecture, and model scale interact non-trivially; MoLRE effectively enables conditional adaptation with minimal parameter overhead and without pathology supervision, highlighting the need to benchmark on target clinical tasks to understand domain-model interactions.

title: Subliminal Signals in Preference Labels
summary: As AI systems approach superhuman capabilities, scalable oversight increasingly relies on LLM-as-a-judge frameworks where models evaluate and guide each other's training. A core assumption is that binary preference labels provide only semantic supervision about response quality. We challenge this assumption by demonstrating that preference labels can function as a covert communication channel. We show that even when a neutral student model generates semantically unbiased completions, a biased judge can transmit unintended behavioral traits through preference assignments, which even strengthen across iterative alignment rounds. Our findings suggest that robust oversight in superalignment settings requires mechanisms that can detect and mitigate subliminal preference transmission, particularly when judges may pursue unintended objectives.

AI.tldr: Binary preference labels in LLM-based evaluation can act as covert communications, leaking unintended behavioral signals from biased judges to students and persisting/enhancing across alignment rounds.
AI.motivation: As scalable oversight relies on preference-based supervision, there is a risk that preferred labels encode biases or objectives, compromising the purity of supervision and enabling subliminal transmission of traits.
AI.method: Empirical and conceptual study with a neutral student model and a biased judge; analyze semantically unbiased completions to isolate subliminal signals; examine how preference assignments carry behavioral traits and how these signals accumulate across iterative alignment rounds.
AI.result: Preference labels can transmit unintended behavioral traits from judge to student, even with neutral inputs, strengthening across rounds; signals are subliminal yet detectable and can undermine oversight.
AI.conclusion: To ensure robust superalignment, develop mechanisms to detect and mitigate subliminal preference transmission, including audits of preference signals, red-teaming, and multi-objective oversight that reduces reliance on single-label preferences.

title: CoLC: Communication-Efficient Collaborative Perception with LiDAR Completion
summary: Collaborative perception empowers autonomous agents to share complementary information and overcome perception limitations. While early fusion offers more perceptual complementarity and is inherently robust to model heterogeneity, its high communication cost has limited its practical deployment, prompting most existing works to favor intermediate or late fusion. To address this, we propose a communication-efficient early Collaborative perception framework that incorporates LiDAR Completion to restore scene completeness under sparse transmission, dubbed as CoLC. Specifically, the CoLC integrates three complementary designs. First, each neighbor agent applies Foreground-Aware Point Sampling (FAPS) to selectively transmit informative points that retain essential structural and contextual cues under bandwidth constraints. The ego agent then employs Completion-Enhanced Early Fusion (CEEF) to reconstruct dense pillars from the received sparse inputs and adaptively fuse them with its own observations, thereby restoring spatial completeness. Finally, the Dense-Guided Dual Alignment (DGDA) strategy enforces semantic and geometric consistency between the enhanced and dense pillars during training, ensuring consistent and robust feature learning. Experiments on both simulated and real-world datasets demonstrate that CoLC achieves superior perception-communication trade-offs and remains robust under heterogeneous model settings. The code is available at https://github.com/CatOneTwo/CoLC.

AI.tldr: A communication-efficient early collaborative perception framework (CoLC) that uses Foreground-Aware Point Sampling (FAPS) for sparse transmission, Completion-Enhanced Early Fusion (CEEF) to densify and fuse received data, and Dense-Guided Dual Alignment (DGDA) for aligned training, achieving better perception-communication trade-offs under bandwidth constraints and model heterogeneity.
AI.motivation: Early fusion offers maximal perceptual complementarity and robustness to model heterogeneity but suffers from high communication costs, limiting practicality. A bandwidth-efficient early fusion approach is needed to enable robust cross-agent perception.
AI.method: Three core designs: (1) Foreground-Aware Point Sampling (FAPS) for neighbors to transmit informative, foreground points under bandwidth limits; (2) Completion-Enhanced Early Fusion (CEEF) at the ego to reconstruct dense pillars from sparse inputs and adaptively fuse them with own observations; (3) Dense-Guided Dual Alignment (DGDA) during training to enforce semantic and geometric consistency between enhanced and dense pillars, incorporating LiDAR completion for scene-wide density.
AI.result: Experiments on simulated and real-world datasets show CoLC achieves superior perception-communication trade-offs and robust performance under heterogeneous model settings.
AI.conclusion: CoLC offers a practical, bandwidth-aware early fusion framework for collaborative perception, leveraging LiDAR completion to restore scene completeness under sparse transmission; code is released at the provided GitHub URL.

title: Learn Hard Problems During RL with Reference Guided Fine-tuning
summary:
Reinforcement learning (RL) for mathematical reasoning can suffer from reward sparsity: for challenging problems, LLM fails to sample any correct trajectories, preventing RL from receiving meaningful positive feedback. At the same time, there often exist human-written reference solutions along with the problem (e.g., problems from AoPS), but directly fine-tuning on these solutions offers no benefit because models often cannot imitate human proofs that lie outside their own reasoning distribution.
  We introduce Reference-Guided Fine-Tuning (ReGFT), a simple and effective method that utilizes human-written reference solutions to synthesize positive trajectories on hard problems and train on them before RL. For each problem, we provide the model with a partial reference solution and let it generate its own reasoning trace, ensuring the resulting trajectories remain in the model's reasoning space while still benefiting from reference guidance.
  Fine-tuning on these reference-guided trajectories increases the number of solvable problems and produces a checkpoint that receives more positive rewards during RL. Across three benchmarks (AIME24, AIME25, BeyondAIME), ReGFT consistently improves supervised accuracy, accelerates DAPO training, and raises the final performance plateau of RL. Our results show that ReGFT effectively overcomes reward sparsity and unlocks stronger RL-based mathematical reasoning.

AI.tldr: Reference-Guided Fine-Tuning (ReGFT) uses human reference solutions to synthesize positive, model-internal reasoning trajectories that guide pre-RL fine-tuning, addressing reward sparsity and improving RL performance on math problems.
AI.motivation: RL for mathematical reasoning suffers from reward sparsity when correct trajectories are rare; although human reference solutions exist, naive fine-tuning on them fails due to distribution mismatch between human proofs and the model's reasoning capabilities.
AI.method: For each problem, provide a partial reference solution and let the model generate its own reasoning trace, producing reference-guided trajectories that remain within the model's reasoning space; pre-train on these trajectories before standard RL.
AI.result: ReGFT increases the number of solvable problems, improves supervised accuracy, accelerates DAPO training, and raises the final RL performance plateau across AIME24, AIME25, and BeyondAIME.
AI.conclusion: Reference-guided fine-tuning effectively mitigates reward sparsity and enhances RL-based mathematical reasoning by leveraging partial reference guidance without leaving the model's reasoning distribution.

title: SCOUT: Fast Spectral CT Imaging in Ultra LOw-data Regimes via PseUdo-label GeneraTion
summary: Noise and artifacts during computed tomography (CT) scans are a fundamental challenge affecting disease diagnosis. However, current methods either involve excessively long reconstruction times or rely on data-driven models for optimization, failing to adequately consider the valuable information inherent in the data itself, especially medical 3D data. This work proposes a reconstruction method under ultra-low raw data conditions, requiring no external data and avoiding lengthy pre-training processes. By leveraging spatial nonlocal similarity and the conjugate properties of the projection domain to generate pseudo-3D data for self-supervised training, high-fidelity results can be achieved in a very short time. Extensive experiments demonstrate that this method not only mitigates detector-induced ring artifacts but also exhibits unprecedented capabilities in detail recovery. This method provides a new paradigm for research using unlabeled raw projection data. Code is available at https://github.com/yqx7150/SCOUT.

AI.tldr: Self-supervised ultra-low-data CT reconstruction using intrinsic data information: spatial nonlocal similarity and projection-domain conjugacy to synthesize pseudo-3D data, enabling fast, high-fidelity reconstructions without external data or pretraining; reduces ring artifacts and improves detail recovery; code available.
AI.motivation: CT imaging suffers from noise/artifacts and often requires long reconstruction times or dependency on data-driven models with external data; there is a need to exploit intrinsic information in 3D medical data and avoid pretraining.
AI.method: A self-supervised framework that leverages spatial nonlocal similarity and the conjugate properties of the projection domain to generate pseudo-3D data from ultra-low raw data, enabling training without external data or pretraining.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show mitigated detector-induced ring artifacts and unprecedented detail recovery, with very short reconstruction times.
AI.conclusion: Proposes a new paradigm for CT research using unlabeled raw projection data and demonstrates effectiveness of self-supervised, data-efficient reconstruction; code is available at the provided GitHub URL.

title: MOSAIC: A Unified Platform for Cross-Paradigm Comparison and Evaluation of Homogeneous and Heterogeneous Multi-Agent RL, LLM, VLM, and Human Decision-Makers
summary: Reinforcement learning (RL), large language models (LLMs), and vision-language models (VLMs) have been widely studied in isolation. However, existing infrastructure lacks the ability to deploy agents from different decision-making paradigms within the same environment, making it difficult to study them in hybrid multi-agent settings or to compare their behaviour fairly under identical conditions. We present MOSAIC, an open-source platform that bridges this gap by incorporating a diverse set of existing reinforcement learning environments and enabling heterogeneous agents (RL policies, LLMs, VLMs, and human players) to operate within them in ad-hoc team settings with reproducible results. MOSAIC introduces three contributions. (i) An IPC-based worker protocol that wraps both native and third-party frameworks as isolated subprocess workers, each executing its native training and inference logic unmodified, communicating through a versioned inter-process protocol. (ii) An operator abstraction that forms an agent-level interface by mapping workers to agents: each operator, regardless of whether it is backed by an RL policy, an LLM, or a human, conforms to a minimal unified interface. (iii) A deterministic cross-paradigm evaluation framework offering two complementary modes: a manual mode that advances up to N concurrent operators in lock-step under shared seeds for fine-grained visual inspection of behavioural differences, and a script mode that drives automated, long-running evaluation through declarative Python scripts, for reproducible experiments. We release MOSAIC as an open, visual-first platform to facilitate reproducible cross-paradigm research across the RL, LLM, and human-in-the-loop communities.

AI.tldr: MOSAIC is an open-source platform that unifies RL, LLMs, and VLMs into shared environments using an IPC-based worker protocol, an agent-level operator interface, and deterministic cross-paradigm evaluation (manual lock-step and script-driven automation) to enable reproducible, heterogeneous-agent experiments.
AI.motivation: Existing research infrastructure treats decision paradigms in isolation, hindering cross-paradigm study and fair comparisons under identical conditions across RL policies, LLMs, VLMs, and humans.
AI.method: Three main contributions: (i) an IPC-based worker protocol wrapping native and third-party frameworks as isolated subprocess workers with a versioned communication protocol; (ii) an operator abstraction that maps workers to agents through a minimal unified interface; (iii) a deterministic evaluation framework offering a manual mode (up to N concurrent operators in lock-step with shared seeds) and a script mode for automated, long-running experiments.
AI.result: The paper presents the architecture and release of MOSAIC, detailing how to enable cross-paradigm, reproducible experiments across RL, LLM, and human-in-the-loop components; it provides an open-source, visual-first platform for researchers.
AI.conclusion: MOSAIC addresses a critical gap by enabling heterogeneous agents to operate in the same environments with reproducible results, facilitating cross-paradigm research across RL, LLM, and human-in-the-loop communities.

title: STMI: Segmentation-Guided Token Modulation with Cross-Modal Hypergraph Interaction for Multi-Modal Object Re-Identification
summary: Multi-modal object Re-Identification (ReID) aims to exploit complementary information from different modalities to retrieve specific objects. However, existing methods often rely on hard token filtering or simple fusion strategies, which can lead to the loss of discriminative cues and increased background interference. To address these challenges, we propose STMI, a novel multi-modal learning framework consisting of three key components: (1) Segmentation-Guided Feature Modulation (SFM) module leverages SAM-generated masks to enhance foreground representations and suppress background noise through learnable attention modulation; (2) Semantic Token Reallocation (STR) module employs learnable query tokens and an adaptive reallocation mechanism to extract compact and informative representations without discarding any tokens; (3) Cross-Modal Hypergraph Interaction (CHI) module constructs a unified hypergraph across modalities to capture high-order semantic relationships. Extensive experiments on public benchmarks (i.e., RGBNT201, RGBNT100, and MSVR310) demonstrate the effectiveness and robustness of our proposed STMI framework in multi-modal ReID scenarios.

AI.tldr: STMI introduces a three-component multi-modal ReID framework that uses segmentation-guided feature modulation, semantic token reallocation, and cross-modal hypergraph interaction to improve discrimination and reduce background noise across modalities; validated on RGBNT201/100 and MSVR310 with strong results.
AI.motivation: Current multi-modal ReID methods rely on hard token filtering or simple fusion, which can discard discriminative cues and amplify background interference; STMI aims to preserve information and exploit high-order cross-modal relations.
AI.method: Segmentation-Guided Feature Modulation (SFM) uses SAM-generated masks to modulate features with learnable attention to emphasize foreground; Semantic Token Reallocation (STR) introduces learnable query tokens and an adaptive reallocation mechanism to extract compact yet informative representations without discarding tokens; Cross-Modal Hypergraph Interaction (CHI) constructs a unified hypergraph across modalities to capture high-order semantic relationships.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on RGBNT201, RGBNT100, and MSVR310 demonstrate the effectiveness and robustness of STMI in multi-modal ReID, outperforming baselines and showing consistent gains across datasets.
AI.conclusion: STMI effectively fuses segmentation-guided modulation, semantic token reallocation, and cross-modal hypergraph interaction to enhance multi-modal ReID performance and robustness.

title: S2O: Enhancing Adversarial Training with Second-Order Statistics of Weights
summary: Adversarial training has emerged as a highly effective way to improve the robustness of deep neural networks (DNNs). It is typically conceptualized as a min-max optimization problem over model weights and adversarial perturbations, where the weights are optimized using gradient descent methods, such as SGD. In this paper, we propose a novel approach by treating model weights as random variables, which paves the way for enhancing adversarial training through \textbf{S}econd-Order \textbf{S}tatistics \textbf{O}ptimization (S$^2$O) over model weights. We challenge and relax a prevalent, yet often unrealistic, assumption in prior PAC-Bayesian frameworks: the statistical independence of weights. From this relaxation, we derive an improved PAC-Bayesian robust generalization bound. Our theoretical developments suggest that optimizing the second-order statistics of weights can substantially tighten this bound. We complement this theoretical insight by conducting an extensive set of experiments that demonstrate that S$^2$O not only enhances the robustness and generalization of neural networks when used in isolation, but also seamlessly augments other state-of-the-art adversarial training techniques. The code is available at https://github.com/Alexkael/S2O.

AI.tldr: Treat weights as random variables and optimize their second-order statistics to tighten PAC-Bayesian robust generalization bounds, improving adversarial training robustness and generalization; effective standalone and when combined with state-of-the-art techniques; code available at the provided repository.
AI.motivation: Relax a strong and often unrealistic independence assumption on neural network weights within PAC-Bayesian analyses of adversarial training; by optimizing second-order statistics (covariances) of weights, derive a tighter robust generalization bound and potentially improve empirical robustness.
AI.method: Model weights as random variables and relax the independence assumption to derive a PAC-Bayesian robust generalization bound that incorporates weight covariances. Propose S^2O (Second-Order Statistics Optimization) to optimize these second-order statistics during training, and integrate with existing adversarial training frameworks. Validate through extensive experiments on standard benchmarks, and demonstrate compatibility with other SOTA adversarial training methods.
AI.result: Empirical results show that S^2O improves robustness and generalization when used alone and provides additive gains when combined with other adversarial training techniques. The approach yields tighter robust generalization bounds per the theoretical analysis, and code is released at the linked repository.
AI.conclusion: Optimizing second-order statistics of model weights is a viable pathway to tighten PAC-Bayesian robust generalization bounds and enhance adversarial training performance, offering complementary benefits to existing methods and potential for further exploration of covariance-aware optimization in robust learning.

title: TokenSplat: Token-aligned 3D Gaussian Splatting for Feed-forward Pose-free Reconstruction
summary: We present TokenSplat, a feed-forward framework for joint 3D Gaussian reconstruction and camera pose estimation from unposed multi-view images. At its core, TokenSplat introduces a Token-aligned Gaussian Prediction module that aligns semantically corresponding information across views directly in the feature space. Guided by coarse token positions and fusion confidence, it aggregates multi-scale contextual features to enable long-range cross-view reasoning and reduce redundancy from overlapping Gaussians. To further enhance pose robustness and disentangle viewpoint cues from scene semantics, TokenSplat employs learnable camera tokens and an Asymmetric Dual-Flow Decoder (ADF-Decoder) that enforces directionally constrained communication between camera and image tokens. This maintains clean factorization within a feed-forward architecture, enabling coherent reconstruction and stable pose estimation without iterative refinement. Extensive experiments demonstrate that TokenSplat achieves higher reconstruction fidelity and novel-view synthesis quality in pose-free settings, and significantly improves pose estimation accuracy compared to prior pose-free methods. Project page: https://kidleyh.github.io/tokensplat/.

AI.tldr: TokenSplat proposes a fully feed-forward framework for joint 3D Gaussian reconstruction and camera pose estimation from unposed multi-view images, using a Token-aligned Gaussian Prediction module and an Asymmetric Dual-Flow Decoder with learnable camera tokens to enable robust pose-free reconstruction and improved pose estimation.
AI.motivation: Address the challenge of reconstructing 3D Gaussians and estimating camera pose from unposed multi-view data, while aligning semantically corresponding information across views and disentangling viewpoint cues from scene semantics in a non-iterative manner.
AI.method: Key components: Token-aligned Gaussian Prediction module to align information across views in feature space; multi-scale contextual feature aggregation guided by coarse token positions and fusion confidence; long-range cross-view reasoning; Asymmetric Dual-Flow Decoder (ADF-Decoder) with learnable camera tokens enforcing directional communication from camera to image tokens; no iterative refinement; feed-forward reconstruction and pose estimation.
AI.result: Experimental results show higher reconstruction fidelity and novel-view synthesis quality in pose-free settings and improved pose estimation accuracy over prior pose-free methods.
AI.conclusion: TokenSplat demonstrates that a fully feed-forward, token-aligned approach can jointly reconstruct 3D Gaussians and estimate camera pose from unposed multi-view data, achieving robust pose separation and coherent reconstruction without refinement; a promising direction for pose-free 3D understanding.

title: GlassMol: Interpretable Molecular Property Prediction with Concept Bottleneck Models
summary: Machine learning accelerates molecular property prediction, yet state-of-the-art Large Language Models and Graph Neural Networks operate as black boxes. In drug discovery, where safety is critical, this opacity risks masking false correlations and excluding human expertise. Existing interpretability methods suffer from the effectiveness-trustworthiness trade-off: explanations may fail to reflect a model's true reasoning, degrade performance, or lack domain grounding. Concept Bottleneck Models (CBMs) offer a solution by projecting inputs to human-interpretable concepts before readout, ensuring that explanations are inherently faithful to the decision process. However, adapting CBMs to chemistry faces three challenges: the Relevance Gap (selecting task-relevant concepts from a large descriptor space), the Annotation Gap (obtaining concept supervision for molecular data), and the Capacity Gap (degrading performance due to bottleneck constraints). We introduce GlassMol, a model-agnostic CBM that addresses these gaps through automated concept curation and LLM-guided concept selection. Experiments across thirteen benchmarks demonstrate that \method generally matches or exceeds black-box baselines, suggesting that interpretability does not sacrifice performance and challenging the commonly assumed trade-off. Code is available at https://github.com/walleio/GlassMol.

AI.tldr: GlassMol is a model-agnostic concept bottleneck model for chemistry that uses automated concept curation and LLM-guided concept selection to deliver interpretable explanations without sacrificing performance, validated on 13 benchmarks; code at GitHub.
AI.motivation: In drug discovery, interpretability is critical but current ML models (LLMs, GNNs) are black-boxes. Concept Bottleneck Models (CBMs) promise faithful explanations but three chemistry-specific gaps exist: Relevance Gap (finding task-relevant concepts from many descriptors), Annotation Gap (limited concept supervision for molecules), and Capacity Gap (bottlenecks hurting performance).
AI.method: GlassMol implements a model-agnostic CBM with automated concept curation to select relevant descriptors and LLM-guided concept selection to reduce annotation burden, producing interpretable concept representations before readout.
AI.result: Across thirteen benchmarks, GlassMol generally matches or surpasses black-box baselines, indicating interpretability can be achieved without sacrificing predictive performance.
AI.conclusion: Interpretable molecular property prediction is feasible without a performance-trade-off; GlassMol addresses key gaps and provides a practical, open-source solution.

title: Towards Universal Khmer Text Recognition
summary: Khmer is a low-resource language characterized by a complex script, presenting significant challenges for optical character recognition (OCR). While document printed text recognition has advanced because of available datasets, performance on other modalities, such as handwritten and scene text, remains limited by data scarcity. Training modality-specific models for each modality does not allow cross-modality transfer learning, from which modalities with limited data could otherwise benefit. Moreover, deploying many modality-specific models results in significant memory overhead and requires error-prone routing each input image to the appropriate model. On the other hand, simply training on a combined dataset with a non-uniform data distribution across different modalities often leads to degraded performance on underrepresented modalities. To address these, we propose a universal Khmer text recognition (UKTR) framework capable of handling diverse text modalities. Central to our method is a novel modality-aware adaptive feature selection (MAFS) technique designed to adapt visual features according to a particular input image modality and enhance recognition robustness across modalities. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our model achieves state-of-the-art (SoTA) performance. Furthermore, we introduce the first comprehensive benchmark for universal Khmer text recognition, which we release to the community to facilitate future research. Our datasets and models can be accessible via this gated repository\footnote{in review}.

AI.tldr: A universal Khmer text recognition framework (UKTR) using modality-aware adaptive feature selection (MAFS) to handle printed, handwritten, and scene text, addressing data scarcity and cross-modality transfer without multiple modality-specific models, achieving SoTA and providing a new benchmark.
AI.motivation: Khmer is a low-resource language with a complex script. Non-printed modalities (handwritten, scene text) suffer from data scarcity. Modality-specific models demand separate resources and hinder cross-modality transfer; unified training on mixed data risks underrepresenting modalities and increases memory; a universal approach with efficient adaptation is needed.
AI.method: Propose UKTR with a modality-aware adaptive feature selection (MAFS) module that adapts visual features according to the input image modality to improve recognition robustness across modalities. Likely a shared backbone with modality-conditioned feature selection and possibly joint training on multi-modal data.
AI.result: The approach achieves state-of-the-art performance across the considered modalities. Furthermore, the authors introduce the first comprehensive benchmark for universal Khmer text recognition and release datasets/models via a gated repository.
AI.conclusion: UKTR with MAFS provides robust cross-modality recognition for Khmer text and enables community-wide benchmarking; facilitates future research by offering a unified framework and a benchmark dataset.

title: The Impact of Battery Cell Configuration on Electric Vehicle Performance: An XGBoost-Based Classification with SHAP Interpretability
summary: As the electric vehicle (EV) market continues to prioritize dynamic performance and rapid charging, battery configuration has rapidly evolved. Despite this, current literature has often overlooked the complex, non-linear relationship between battery configuration and electric vehicle performance. To address this gap, this study proposes a machine learning framework which categorizes the EV acceleration performance into High (<= 4.0 seconds), Mid (4.0 - 7.0 seconds), and Low (> 7.0 seconds). Utilizing a preprocessed dataset consisting of 276 EV samples, an Extreme Gradient Boosting (XGBoost) classifier was utilized, achieving 87.5% predictive accuracy, a 0.968 ROC-AUC, and a 0.812 MCC. In order to ensure engineering transparency SHapley Additive exPlanations (SHAP) were employed. Results of analysis shows that an increase in battery cell count initially boosts power delivery, but its mass and complexity diminished performance gains eventually. As such, these findings indicate that battery configuration in EVs must balance system complexity and architectural configuration in order to receive and retain optimal vehicle performance.

AI.tldr: XGBoost classifier on EV battery configuration yields 87.5% accuracy in classifying acceleration into High/Mid/Low; SHAP reveals non-linear effects of cell count due to mass/complexity; implies balancing cell count with mass to optimize performance.
AI.motivation: Address the overlooked non-linear link between battery configuration and EV acceleration; provide a transparent ML framework to relate design choices to performance.
AI.method: Train an Extreme Gradient Boosting (XGBoost) classifier on a preprocessed dataset of 276 EV samples; evaluate with accuracy, ROC-AUC, MCC; apply SHAP for interpretability to reveal feature impacts.
AI.result: Classification performance: 87.5% accuracy, ROC-AUC 0.968, MCC 0.812. SHAP analysis shows that increasing battery cell count initially enhances power delivery, but subsequent increases incur higher mass and complexity, reducing gains.
AI.conclusion: Battery configuration in EVs requires balancing system complexity and architectural choices to achieve optimal performance; non-linear relationships exist between cell count and acceleration due to mass and design constraints.

title: Towards Khmer Scene Document Layout Detection
summary: While document layout analysis for Latin scripts has advanced significantly, driven by the advent of large multimodal models (LMMs), progress for the Khmer language remains constrained because of the scarcity of annotated training data. This gap is particularly acute for scene documents, where perspective distortions and complex backgrounds challenge traditional methods. Given the structural complexities of Khmer script, such as diacritics and multi-layer character stacking, existing Latin-based layout analysis models fail to accurately delineate semantic layout units, particularly for dense text regions (e.g., list items). In this paper, we present the first comprehensive study on Khmer scene document layout detection. We contribute a novel framework comprising three key elements: (1) a robust training and benchmarking dataset specifically for Khmer scene layouts; (2) an open-source document augmentation tool capable of synthesizing realistic scene documents to scale training data; and (3) layout detection baselines utilizing YOLO-based architectures with oriented bounding boxes (OBB) to handle geometric distortions. To foster further research in the Khmer document analysis and recognition (DAR) community, we release our models, code, and datasets in this gated repository (in review).

AI.tldr: Introduces Khmer scene document layout detection with a dedicated dataset, data augmentation tool, and oriented-YOLO baselines; offers open-source resources.
AI.motivation: Scarcity of annotated Khmer scene data and the complexity of Khmer script impede layout analysis; Latin-based methods underperform in dense, diacritic-rich regions; need robust Khmer-specific scene layout models.
AI.method: Three elements: (1) a robust training and benchmarking dataset for Khmer scene layouts; (2) an open-source document augmentation tool to synthesize realistic Khmer scene documents; (3) layout detection baselines using YOLO-based architectures with oriented bounding boxes to handle geometric distortions.
AI.result: Proposes a complete framework consisting of a dataset, augmentation tool, and YOLO-OBB baselines for Khmer scene layout detection; demonstrates feasibility and establishes baselines; plans to release models, code, and datasets in a gated repository to foster further research.
AI.conclusion: Provides foundational resources and a framework to advance Khmer document analysis and recognition, inviting broader community involvement through released resources.

title: Attention Smoothing Is All You Need For Unlearning
summary: Large Language Models are prone to memorizing sensitive, copyrighted, or hazardous content, posing significant privacy and legal concerns. Retraining from scratch is computationally infeasible, whereas current unlearning methods exhibit unstable trade-offs between forgetting and utility, frequently producing incoherent outputs on forget prompts and failing to generalize due to the persistence of lexical-level and semantic-level associations in attention. We propose Attention Smoothing Unlearning (ASU), a principled framework that casts unlearning as self-distillation from a forget-teacher derived from the model's own attention. By increasing the softmax temperature, ASU flattens attention distributions and directly suppresses the lexical-level and semantic-level associations responsible for reconstructing memorized knowledge. This results in a bounded optimization objective that erases factual information yet maintains coherence in responses to forget prompts. Empirical evaluation on TOFU, MUSE, and WMDP, along with real-world and continual unlearning scenarios across question answering and text completion, demonstrates that ASU outperforms the baselines for most unlearning scenarios, delivering robust unlearning with minimal loss of model utility.

AI.tldr: ASU introduces Attention Smoothing Unlearning, a self-distillation approach using a forget-teacher from the model's own attention. By increasing softmax temperature to flatten attention, it suppresses lexical and semantic associations that enable memorization, yielding a bounded optimization that forgets facts while preserving coherence. Empirically outperforms baselines on TOFU, MUSE, WMDP and real-world continual unlearning in QA and text completion, with minimal utility loss.
AI.motivation: Large language models memorize sensitive, copyrighted, or hazardous content, creating privacy and legal risks. Retraining from scratch is computationally infeasible; existing unlearning methods show unstable forgetting-utility trade-offs and struggle to remove memorized content due to persistent attention-level associations.
AI.method: ASU frames unlearning as self-distillation from a forget-teacher derived from the model's own attention. It increases the softmax temperature to flatten attention distributions, thereby directly suppressing lexical- and semantic-level associations that reconstruct memorized knowledge. The optimization objective is bounded to erase factual information while preserving response coherence on forget prompts.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on TOFU, MUSE, WMDP and real-world/continual unlearning tasks across question answering and text completion shows ASU outperforms baselines in most scenarios, achieving robust unlearning with minimal loss of model utility.
AI.conclusion: ASU offers a principled, scalable approach to unlearning that reduces memorization by attenuating attention-driven associations without severely sacrificing utility, applicable to real-world continual unlearning challenges in QA and text generation.

title: A Reconstruction System for Industrial Pipeline Inner Walls Using Panoramic Image Stitching with Endoscopic Imaging
summary: Visual analysis and reconstruction of pipeline inner walls remain challenging in industrial inspection scenarios. This paper presents a dedicated reconstruction system for pipeline inner walls via industrial endoscopes, which is built on panoramic image stitching technology. Equipped with a custom graphical user interface (GUI), the system extracts key frames from endoscope video footage, and integrates polar coordinate transformation with image stitching techniques to unwrap annular video frames of pipeline inner walls into planar panoramic images. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method enables efficient processing of industrial endoscope videos, and the generated panoramic stitched images preserve all detailed features of pipeline inner walls in their entirety. This provides intuitive and accurate visual support for defect detection and condition assessment of pipeline inner walls. In comparison with the traditional frame-by-frame video review method, the proposed approach significantly elevates the efficiency of pipeline inner wall reconstruction and exhibits considerable engineering application value.

AI.tldr: A panoramic-image-stitching pipeline for inner-wall reconstruction using endoscopy, unwrapping annular frames into planar panoramas via polar transform, enabling efficient and complete-detail visualization for defects.
AI.motivation: Industrial pipeline inspection faces challenge visualizing and reconstructing cylindrical inner walls; frame-by-frame review is inefficient and may miss or misrepresent features; a unified, efficient visualization tool is needed.
AI.method: Develop a dedicated reconstruction system based on panoramic image stitching. It includes a GUI to extract key frames from endoscope videos and combines polar coordinate transformation with image stitching to unwrap annular pipeline frames into planar panoramic images.
AI.result: Experimental results show efficient processing of endoscope videos and panoramas that preserve all detailed features of the inner walls; the method is more efficient than frame-by-frame review and holds strong engineering applicability.
AI.conclusion: The proposed system provides intuitive, accurate visual support for defect detection and condition assessment of pipeline inner walls and substantially enhances reconstruction efficiency with practical engineering value.

title: JailNewsBench: Multi-Lingual and Regional Benchmark for Fake News Generation under Jailbreak Attacks
summary: Fake news undermines societal trust and decision-making across politics, economics, health, and international relations, and in extreme cases threatens human lives and societal safety. Because fake news reflects region-specific political, social, and cultural contexts and is expressed in language, evaluating the risks of large language models (LLMs) requires a multi-lingual and regional perspective. Malicious users can bypass safeguards through jailbreak attacks, inducing LLMs to generate fake news. However, no benchmark currently exists to systematically assess attack resilience across languages and regions. Here, we propose JailNewsBench, the first benchmark for evaluating LLM robustness against jailbreak-induced fake news generation. JailNewsBench spans 34 regions and 22 languages, covering 8 evaluation sub-metrics through LLM-as-a-Judge and 5 jailbreak attacks, with approximately 300k instances. Our evaluation of 9 LLMs reveals that the maximum attack success rate (ASR) reached 86.3% and the maximum harmfulness score was 3.5 out of 5. Notably, for English and U.S.-related topics, the defensive performance of typical multi-lingual LLMs was significantly lower than for other regions, highlighting substantial imbalances in safety across languages and regions. In addition, our analysis shows that coverage of fake news in existing safety datasets is limited and less well defended than major categories such as toxicity and social bias. Our dataset and code are available at https://github.com/kanekomasahiro/jail_news_bench.

AI.tldr: JailNewsBench is a multilingual, region-aware benchmark for evaluating LLM resilience to jailbreak-induced fake news; spans 34 regions and 22 languages, with 8 metrics and 5 attack types (~300k instances). Evaluations show high attack success and regional safety gaps, with English/U.S.-related topics particularly under-defended; dataset and code released.
AI.motivation: There is no systematic, multilingual benchmark to assess LLM safety against jailbreak-induced fake news across languages and regions; this work aims to fill that gap and quantify cross-lingual/regional vulnerabilities.
AI.method: Construct JailNewsBench with 34 regions, 22 languages, 8 evaluation sub-metrics, and 5 jailbreak attack types; treat LLMs as judges to score harmfulness; evaluate 9 LLMs on ~300k instances; measure attack success rate and harmfulness; analyze regional/language safety disparities and data coverage in safety datasets.
AI.result: Max ASR 86.3%; max harmfulness 3.5/5; English and U.S.-related topics show weaker safety defense; safety datasets under-coverage for fake news relative to toxicity and bias; code and data released.
AI.conclusion: Safety of LLMs is uneven across languages and regions; benchmark highlights need for multilingual, region-aware safety improvements; encourages broader dataset coverage for fake news; provides reproducible baseline.

title: Diversity over Uniformity: Rethinking Representation in Generated Image Detection
summary: With the rapid advancement of generative models, generated image detection has become an important task in visual forensics. Although existing methods have achieved remarkable progress, they often rely, after training, on only a small subset of highly salient forgery cues, which limits their ability to generalize to unseen generative mechanisms. We argue that reliably generated image detection should not depend on a single decision path but should preserve multiple judgment perspectives, enabling the model to understand the differences between real and generated images from diverse viewpoints. Based on this idea, we propose an anti-feature-collapse learning framework that filters task-irrelevant components and suppresses excessive overlap among different forgery cues in the representation space, preventing discriminative information from collapsing into a few dominant feature directions. This design maintains diverse and complementary evidence within the model, reduces reliance on a small set of salient cues, and enhances robustness under unseen generative settings. Extensive experiments on multiple public benchmarks demonstrate that the proposed method significantly outperforms the state-of-the-art approaches in cross-model scenarios, achieving an accuracy improvement of 5.02% and exhibiting superior generalization and detection reliability. The source code is available at https://github.com/Yanmou-Hui/DoU.

AI.tldr: Proposes anti-feature-collapse learning to mitigate reliance on a single forgery cue in generative-image detection, promoting multiple, diverse cues for robustness to unseen models; reports a notable cross-model accuracy gain (5.02%) and strong generalization; code available.
AI.motivation: Current detectors often overfit to a small subset of salient forgery cues, hindering generalization to unseen generative mechanisms. A multi-perspective, non-collapse representation is desirable to differentiate real vs. generated images across diverse cues.
AI.method: Introduces an anti-feature-collapse learning framework that filters task-irrelevant components and suppresses excessive overlap among different forgery cues in the representation space, preserving diverse and complementary evidence and reducing reliance on dominant cues to prevent feature collapse.
AI.result: Empirically outperforms state-of-the-art methods in cross-model scenarios, achieving a 5.02% accuracy improvement and demonstrating stronger generalization and detection reliability across multiple public benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Mitigating feature collapse yields more robust fake-image detectors that generalize to unseen generative models; the approach enhances reliability by maintaining diverse cues rather than relying on a single decision path.

title: BornoViT: A Novel Efficient Vision Transformer for Bengali Handwritten Basic Characters Classification
summary: Handwritten character classification in the Bengali script is a significant challenge due to the complexity and variability of the characters. The models commonly used for classification are often computationally expensive and data-hungry, making them unsuitable for resource-limited languages such as Bengali. In this experiment, we propose a novel, efficient, and lightweight Vision Transformer model that effectively classifies Bengali handwritten basic characters and digits, addressing several shortcomings of traditional methods. The proposed solution utilizes a deep convolutional neural network (DCNN) in a more simplified manner compared to traditional DCNN architectures, with the aim of reducing computational burden. With only 0.65 million parameters, a model size of 0.62 MB, and 0.16 GFLOPs, our model, BornoViT, is significantly lighter than current state-of-the-art models, making it more suitable for resource-limited environments, which is essential for Bengali handwritten character classification. BornoViT was evaluated on the BanglaLekha Isolated dataset, achieving an accuracy of 95.77%, and demonstrating superior efficiency compared to existing state-of-the-art approaches. Furthermore, the model was evaluated on our self-collected dataset, Bornomala, consisting of approximately 222 samples from different age groups, where it achieved an accuracy of 91.51%.

AI.tldr: Proposes BornoViT, a lightweight Vision Transformer-based model for Bengali handwriting classification with 0.65M parameters; achieves 95.77% on BanglaLekha Isolated and 91.51% on Bornomala; suitable for resource-constrained settings.
AI.motivation: To overcome the computational burden and data-hungry nature of traditional deep learning models for Bengali handwritten character recognition, enabling efficient classification on limited-resource devices.
AI.method: Introduces a simplified DCNN-assisted Vision Transformer architecture named BornoViT with 0.65 million parameters, 0.62 MB model size, and 0.16 GFLOPs; evaluated on BanglaLekha Isolated and a self-collected Bornomala dataset.
AI.result: Achieves 95.77% accuracy on BanglaLekha Isolated; 91.51% on Bornomala; substantially lighter than current state-of-the-art models while maintaining high accuracy.
