AI.conclusion: Demonstrates that a compact Vision Transformer-based approach can effectively and efficiently classify Bengali handwritten characters, making it suitable for deployment in resource-limited settings and for languages with limited data.

title: Theoretical Perspectives on Data Quality and Synergistic Effects in Pre- and Post-Training Reasoning Models
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) are pretrained on massive datasets and later instruction-tuned via supervised fine-tuning (SFT) or reinforcement learning (RL). Best practices emphasize large, diverse pretraining data, whereas post-training operates differently: SFT relies on smaller, high-quality datasets, while RL benefits more from scale, with larger amounts of feedback often outweighing label quality. Yet it remains unclear why pretraining and RL require large datasets, why SFT excels on smaller ones, and what defines high-quality SFT data. In this work, we theoretically analyze transformers trained on an in-context weight prediction task for linear regression. Our analysis reveals several key findings: $(i)$ balanced pretraining data can induce latent capabilities later activated during post-training, and $(ii)$ SFT learns best from a small set of examples challenging for the pretrained model, while excessively large SFT datasets may dilute informative pretraining signals. In contrast, RL is most effective on large-scale data that is not overly difficult for the pretrained model. We validate these theoretical insights with experiments on large nonlinear transformer architectures.

AI.tldr: Balanced pretraining can seed latent post-training capabilities; SFT performs best with a small, hard-to-predict set of examples, while too-large SFT data may drown pretrained signals; RL scales with data but requires tasks not too difficult for the pretrained model; theory on in-context weight prediction for linear regression, validated on large nonlinear transformers.
AI.motivation: Explain the data-quantity and data-quality requirements across pretraining, SFT, and RL in modern LLMs; reconcile empirical best practices with theory by analyzing a controlled in-context learning task.
AI.method: Theoretical analysis of transformers trained on an in-context weight prediction task for linear regression; derive conditions under which pretraining signals are latent and activated during post-training; assess SFT vs RL data-size effects; validate with experiments on large nonlinear transformer architectures.
AI.result: (i) Balanced pretraining data can engender latent capabilities later activated during post-training. (ii) SFT yields best performance from a small set of examples that are challenging for the pretrained model; excessively large SFT datasets may dilute informative pretraining signals. In contrast, RL is most effective on large-scale data that is not overly difficult for the pretrained model. These insights are corroborated by experiments on large nonlinear transformer architectures.
AI.conclusion: The findings clarify how data balance, size, and quality across pretraining, SFT, and RL shape post-training capabilities, suggesting practical guidance: use balanced pretraining to seed latent abilities, choose small, hard SFT datasets to maximize signal from pretraining, avoid overly large SFT corpora, and rely on substantial, yet tractable, data for RL; results generalize across large transformer models.

title: Stroke outcome and evolution prediction from CT brain using a spatiotemporal diffusion autoencoder
comment: Accepted in The 6th International Workshop on Machine Learning in Clinical Neuroimaging (MLCN 2023)
summary: Stroke is a major cause of death and disability worldwide. Accurate outcome and evolution prediction has the potential to revolutionize stroke care by individualizing clinical decision-making leading to better outcomes. However, despite a plethora of attempts and the rich data provided by neuroimaging, modelling the ultimate fate of brain tissue remains a challenging task. In this work, we apply recent ideas in the field of diffusion probabilistic models to generate a self-supervised semantically meaningful stroke representation from Computed Tomography (CT) images. We then improve this representation by extending the method to accommodate longitudinal images and the time from stroke onset. The effectiveness of our approach is evaluated on a dataset consisting of 5,824 CT images from 3,573 patients across two medical centers with minimal labels. Comparative experiments show that our method achieves the best performance for predicting next-day severity and functional outcome at discharge.

AI.tldr: Diffusion probabilistic-model–based self-supervised representation from CT strokes, extended to longitudinal data and onset time, achieves state-of-the-art predictions for next-day severity and discharge outcome using a large, minimally labeled multi-center dataset.
AI.motivation: Stroke outcomes are hard to predict due to tissue fate complexity; a semantically meaningful, data-efficient representation from neuroimaging could improve clinical decision support, especially when labels are scarce and longitudinal data are available.
AI.method: Train a diffusion probabilistic model to learn a self-supervised representation from CT images of stroke patients. Extend the framework to incorporate longitudinal scans and time since onset. Dataset: 5,824 CT images from 3,573 patients across two centers with minimal labels. Evaluate via comparative experiments on next-day severity and discharge functional outcome.
AI.result: The method achieves the best performance among tested approaches for predicting next-day severity and functional outcome at discharge.
AI.conclusion: A diffusion-based, self-supervised, longitudinally aware representation improves outcome prediction in stroke on CT data with limited labels, offering a pathway toward more personalized, data-efficient prognostics.

title: I Can't Believe It's Not Robust: Catastrophic Collapse of Safety Classifiers under Embedding Drift
comment: Accepted at the ICBINB: Where LLMs Need to Improve workshop at ICLR 2026. 12 pages and 3 Figures
summary: Instruction tuned reasoning models are increasingly deployed with safety classifiers trained on frozen embeddings, assuming representation stability across model updates. We systematically investigate this assumption and find it fails: normalized perturbations of magnitude $σ=0.02$ (corresponding to $\approx 1^\circ$ angular drift on the embedding sphere) reduce classifier performance from $85\%$ to $50\%$ ROC-AUC. Critically, mean confidence only drops $14\%$, producing dangerous silent failures where $72\%$ of misclassifications occur with high confidence, defeating standard monitoring. We further show that instruction-tuned models exhibit 20$\%$ worse class separability than base models, making aligned systems paradoxically harder to safeguard. Our findings expose a fundamental fragility in production AI safety architectures and challenge the assumption that safety mechanisms transfer across model versions.

AI.tldr: Embedding-based safety classifiers are not robust to small changes in representations, undermining the common practice of freezing embeddings across model updates. Instruction-tuning worsens class separability, and the safety mechanism transfer across versions is fragile, with dangerous silent failures due to high-confidence misclassifications.
AI.motivation: To test the assumption that safety classifiers anchored to frozen embeddings remain effective as models are updated, and to quantify the robustness of safety mechanisms in production AI systems.
AI.method: Systematically apply normalized perturbations of embeddings with magnitude σ=0.02 (≈1° on the embedding sphere), measure ROC-AUC degradation from 85% to 50%; analyze mean confidence drop vs misclassification rate; compare instruction-tuned models to base models in class separability; assess transferability of safety across model versions.
AI.result: Robust perturbations substantially degrade safety classifier performance (ROC-AUC drop from 85% to 50%). Mean confidence only falls by 14%, yet 72% of misclassifications occur with high confidence. Instruction-tuned models exhibit ~20% worse class separability than base models, indicating safer systems are harder to safeguard post-alignment.
AI.conclusion: Production AI safety architectures are fragile to representation drift and alignment, challenging the assumption that safety mechanisms transfer across model versions; new strategies are required to ensure reliable monitoring and safeguarding during model updates.

title: Analyzing and Improving Fast Sampling of Text-to-Image Diffusion Models
summary: Text-to-image diffusion models have achieved unprecedented success but still struggle to produce high-quality results under limited sampling budgets. Existing training-free sampling acceleration methods are typically developed independently, leaving the overall performance and compatibility among these methods unexplored. In this paper, we bridge this gap by systematically elucidating the design space, and our comprehensive experiments identify the sampling time schedule as the most pivotal factor. Inspired by the geometric properties of diffusion models revealed through the Frenet-Serret formulas, we propose constant total rotation schedule (TORS), a scheduling strategy that ensures uniform geometric variation along the sampling trajectory. TORS outperforms previous training-free acceleration methods and produces high-quality images with 10 sampling steps on Flux.1-Dev and Stable Diffusion 3.5. Extensive experiments underscore the adaptability of our method to unseen models, hyperparameters, and downstream applications.

AI.tldr: A training-free sampling acceleration method (TORS) for diffusion models improves image quality under very limited sampling steps by enforcing a constant total rotation along the sampling trajectory using Frenet-Serret geometry; shows strong empirical gains and broad adaptability.
AI.motivation: Training-free sampling accelerations exist but are evaluated in isolation, with unclear design space and compatibility. This work identifies sampling time schedule as the key factor and seeks a unified, principled approach to accelerate sampling under budget constraints.
AI.method: Introduce constant total rotation schedule (TORS) grounded in Frenet-Serret geometry of the diffusion process. Treats the sampling trajectory as a geometric curve and enforces uniform geometric variation by keeping the total rotation constant across steps, enabling effective quality with very few steps (e.g., 10 steps) and compatibility across models.
AI.result: TORS outperforms previous training-free accelerations, achieving high-quality image synthesis with 10 sampling steps on Flux.1-Dev and Stable Diffusion 3.5. Extensive experiments show robustness to unseen models, hyperparameters, and downstream tasks.
AI.conclusion: Sampling time schedule is the pivotal factor in training-free diffusion acceleration; a principled geometric approach (TORS) yields practical gains and strong generalization across models and applications, suggesting broad applicability for efficient sampling.

title: Nonconvex Latent Optimally Partitioned Block-Sparse Recovery via Log-Sum and Minimax Concave Penalties
summary: We propose two nonconvex regularization methods, LogLOP-l2/l1 and AdaLOP-l2/l1, for recovering block-sparse signals with unknown block partitions. These methods address the underestimation bias of existing convex approaches by extending log-sum penalty and the Minimax Concave Penalty (MCP) to the block-sparse domain via novel variational formulations. Unlike Generalized Moreau Enhancement (GME) and Bayesian methods dependent on the squared-error data fidelity term, our proposed methods are compatible with a broad range of data fidelity terms. We develop efficient Alternating Direction Method of Multipliers (ADMM)-based algorithms for these formulations that exhibit stable empirical convergence. Numerical experiments on synthetic data, angular power spectrum estimation, and denoising of nanopore currents demonstrate that our methods outperform state-of-the-art baselines in estimation accuracy.

AI.tldr: Two nonconvex block-sparse regularizers (LogLOP and AdaLOP) extend log-sum and MCP to block-sparse recovery with unknown partitions, offering compatibility with diverse data fidelities and ADMM-based algorithms with stable convergence; they outperform baselines in synthetic tests, angular power spectrum estimation, and nanopore-denoising.
AI.motivation: Address underestimation bias of convex block-sparse methods and the challenge of unknown block partitions. Extend nonconvex penalties (log-sum, MCP) to blocks to improve recovery while remaining flexible to various data fidelity terms.
AI.method: Introduce LogLOP-l2/l1 and AdaLOP-l2/l1 via novel variational formulations of log-sum penalty and MCP for block-sparse signals. Develop ADMM-based optimization algorithms that work with a broad class of data-fidelity terms and demonstrate stable empirical convergence.
AI.result: Numerical experiments on synthetic data, angular power spectrum estimation, and nanopore current denoising show superior estimation accuracy compared with state-of-the-art baselines.
AI.conclusion: Nonconvex block-sparse regularizers with unknown partition handling offer lower bias and greater flexibility than convex methods, and the proposed ADMM algorithms provide stable convergence and practical effectiveness across diverse applications.

title: DUCX: Decomposing Unfairness in Tool-Using Chest X-ray Agents
summary: Tool-using medical agents can improve chest X-ray question answering by orchestrating specialized vision and language modules, but this added pipeline complexity also creates new pathways for demographic bias beyond standalone models. We present ours (Decomposing Unfairness in Chest X-ray agents), a systematic audit of chest X-ray agents instantiated with MedRAX. To localize where disparities arise, we introduce a stage-wise fairness decomposition that separates end-to-end bias from three agent-specific sources: tool exposure bias (utility gaps conditioned on tool presence), tool transition bias (subgroup differences in tool-routing patterns), and model reasoning bias (subgroup differences in synthesis behaviors). Extensive experiments on tool-used based agentic frameworks across five driver backbones reveal that (i) demographic gaps persist in end-to-end performance, with equalized odds up to 20.79%, and the lowest fairness-utility tradeoff down to 28.65%, and (ii) intermediate behaviors, tool usage, transition patterns, and reasoning traces exhibit distinct subgroup disparities that are not predictable from end-to-end evaluation alone (e.g., conditioned on segmentation-tool availability, the subgroup utility gap reaches as high as 50%). Our findings underscore the need for process-level fairness auditing and debiasing to ensure the equitable deployment of clinical agentic systems. Code is available here: https://anonymous.4open.science/r/DUCK-E5FE/README.md

AI.tldr: Introduces a stage-wise fairness decomposition to localize and audit bias sources in tool-using chest X-ray agents built on MedRAX, revealing end-to-end bias and distinct subgroup disparities in tool exposure, tool routing, and reasoning.
AI.motivation: Address the lack of process-level fairness auditing in pipeline-based medical AI agents by disentangling end-to-end performance bias from three agent-specific bias sources.
AI.method: Propose a stage-wise fairness framework that decomposes end-to-end bias into: (i) tool exposure bias (utility gaps conditional on tool presence), (ii) tool transition bias (differences in tool-routing patterns across subgroups), and (iii) model reasoning bias (subgroup differences in synthesis behaviors). Evaluate across five driver backbones and tool-based agentic frameworks using Demographic subgroups and tools, measuring end-to-end fairness (e.g., equalized odds) and a fairness-utility tradeoff; perform deep-dive analyses of intermediate behaviors conditioned on tool availability.
AI.result: End-to-end demographic gaps persist with equalized odds improvements up to 20.79% and fairness-utility tradeoff as low as 28.65%. Intermediate behaviors show distinct subgroup disparities that are not predictable from end-to-end metrics; for example, conditioned on segmentation-tool availability, the subgroup utility gap can reach ~50%. Across five backbones, tool exposure, transition, and reasoning biases manifest separately, underscoring distinct debiasing needs beyond end-to-end evaluation.
AI.conclusion: Process-level fairness auditing and debiasing are essential for safe deployment of clinical agentic systems; the work provides a structured framework to identify and address bias sources along the tool-using inference pipeline. Code is available at the provided repository.

title: PAC Guarantees for Reinforcement Learning: Sample Complexity, Coverage, and Structure
summary: When data is scarce or mistakes are costly, average-case metrics fall short. What a practitioner needs is a guarantee: with probability at least $1-δ$, the learned policy is $\varepsilon$-close to optimal after $N$ episodes. This is the PAC promise, and between 2018 and 2025 the RL theory community made striking progress on when such promises can be kept. We survey that progress. Our organizing tool is the Coverage-Structure-Objective (CSO) framework, proposed here, which decomposes nearly every PAC sample complexity result into three factors: coverage (how data were obtained), structure (intrinsic MDP or function-class complexity), and objective (what the learner must deliver). CSO is not a theorem but an interpretive template that identifies bottlenecks and makes cross-setting comparison immediate. The technical core covers tight tabular baselines and the uniform-PAC bridge to regret; structural complexity measures (Bellman rank, witness rank, Bellman-Eluder dimension) governing learnability with function approximation; results for linear, kernel/NTK, and low-rank models; reward-free exploration as upfront coverage investment; and pessimistic offline RL where inherited coverage is the binding constraint. We provide practitioner tools: rate lookup tables indexed by CSO coordinates, Bellman residual diagnostics, coverage estimation with deployment gates, and per-episode policy certificates. A final section catalogs open problems, separating near-term targets from frontier questions where coverage, structure, and computation tangle in ways current theory cannot resolve.

AI.tldr: A survey and interpretive framework (CSO) that decomposes PAC RL results into Coverage, Structure, and Objective, enabling cross-setting comparison and practical tooling; covers tabular and function-approx RL, reward-free exploration, offline RL, with open problems.
AI.motivation: To provide PAC-style guarantees in reinforcement learning under data scarcity or costly mistakes, where average-case metrics are insufficient, by delivering probabilistic guarantees with provable confidence.
AI.method: Propose the Coverage-Structure-Objective (CSO) framework that interprets nearly all PAC sample-complexity results through three coordinates: data coverage, intrinsic problem/model complexity, and the learning objective. The paper provides tight tabular baselines and a uniform-PAC bridge to regret; introduces structural complexity measures (Bellman rank, witness rank, Bellman-Eluder dimension) for function approximation; analyzes linear, kernel/NTK, and low-rank models; discusses reward-free exploration as upfront coverage investment and pessimistic offline RL where coverage is binding; furnishes practitioner tools such as rate lookup tables, Bellman residual diagnostics, and deployment gates for coverage estimation and per-episode certificates; concludes with open problems separating near-term targets from frontier questions.
AI.result: CSO acts as an interpretive framework that enables cross-setting comparison of PAC RL results, offers diagnostic tools and rate tables, and covers a range of settings (tabular, function approximation, reward-free, offline) while highlighting bottlenecks tied to coverage, structure, and computation.
AI.conclusion: The CSO framework clarifies how coverage, structure, and computation limit learnability, enabling targeted improvements and comparisons across settings, and outlining open problems where current theory cannot yet resolve the interactions among the three coordinates.

title: Neural Functional Alignment Space: Brain-Referenced Representation of Artificial Neural Networks
summary: We propose the Neural Functional Alignment Space (NFAS), a brain-referenced representational framework for characterizing artificial neural networks on equal functional grounds. NFAS departs from conventional alignment approaches that rely on layer-wise features or task-specific activations by modeling the intrinsic dynamical evolution of stimulus representations across network depth. Specifically, we model layer-wise embeddings as a depth-wise dynamical trajectory and apply Dynamic Mode Decomposition (DMD) to extract the stable mode. This representation is then projected into a biologically anchored coordinate system defined by distributed neural responses. We also introduce the Signal-to-Noise Consistency Index (SNCI) to quantify cross-model consistency at the modality level. Across 45 pretrained models spanning vision, audio, and language, NFAS reveals structured organization within this brain-referenced space, including modality-specific clustering and cross-modal convergence in integrative cortical systems. Our findings suggest that representation dynamics provide a principled basis for

AI.tldr: A brain-referenced dynamical framework (NFAS) for evaluating artificial networks via depth-wise representation trajectories and Dynamic Mode Decomposition, with SNCI for cross-model modality consistency; finds modality-specific clustering and cross-modal integration across 45 pretrained models.
AI.motivation: To provide a common, brain-referenced framework for evaluating artificial neural networks beyond layer-wise features or task-specific activations by leveraging dynamic representation evolution and a biologically anchored coordinate system.
AI.method: Model layer-wise embeddings as depth-wise dynamical trajectories; apply Dynamic Mode Decomposition (DMD) to extract stable modes; project into a brain-referenced coordinate system based on distributed neural responses; introduce Signal-to-Noise Consistency Index (SNCI) to quantify cross-model modality consistency; evaluate 45 pretrained models across vision, audio, and language.
AI.result: NFAS reveals structured organization in the brain-referenced space, with modality-specific clustering and cross-modal convergence in integrative cortical systems across the tested models.
AI.conclusion: Representation dynamics provide a principled basis for comparing artificial networks across modalities, enabling brain-referenced analysis that highlights shared representations and cross-modal integration.

title: Provable and Practical In-Context Policy Optimization for Self-Improvement
comment: 34 pages, 8 tables, 4 figures, Accepted by ICLR 2026
summary: We study test-time scaling, where a model improves its answer through multi-round self-reflection at inference. We introduce In-Context Policy Optimization (ICPO), in which an agent optimizes its response in context using self-assessed or externally observed rewards without modifying its parameters. To explain this ICPO process, we theoretically show that with sufficient pretraining under a novel Fisher-weighted logit-matching objective, a single-layer linear self-attention model can provably imitate policy-optimization algorithm for linear bandits. Building on this theory, we propose Minimum-Entropy ICPO (ME-ICPO), a practical algorithm that iteratively uses its response and self-assessed reward to refine its response in-context at inference time. By selecting the responses and their rewards with minimum entropy, ME-ICPO ensures the robustness of the self-assessed rewards via majority voting. Across standard mathematical reasoning tasks, ME-ICPO attains competitive, top-tier performance while keeping inference costs affordable compared with other inference-time algorithms. Overall, ICPO provides a principled understanding of self-reflection in LLMs and yields practical benefits for test-time scaling for mathematical reasoning.

AI.tldr: Proposes In-Context Policy Optimization (ICPO) for test-time self-reflection in LLMs and Minimum-Entropy ICPO (ME-ICPO) to robustly refine responses; presents theory showing a shallow self-attention model can imitate policy optimization under Fisher-weighted logit matching; achieves competitive math reasoning performance with affordable inference costs.
AI.motivation: Tackle test-time scaling of LLM reasoning by enabling self-reflection without weight updates; provide theoretical grounding and practical algorithm.
AI.method: Introduce ICPO; Fisher-weighted logit-matching pretraining; theory: single-layer linear self-attention can imitate policy optimization for linear bandits; develop ME-ICPO with iterative in-context refinement and minimum-entropy selection for robustness via majority voting.
AI.result: ME-ICPO attains competitive top-tier performance on standard mathematical reasoning tasks with cheaper inference than other inference-time methods.
AI.conclusion: ICPO offers a principled framework for self-reflection in LLMs and practical benefits for scaling test-time reasoning.

title: NERFIFY: A Multi-Agent Framework for Turning NeRF Papers into Code
comment: Accepted to CVPR 2026. Project page: https://seemandhar.github.io/NERFIFY/
summary: The proliferation of neural radiance field (NeRF) research requires significant efforts to reimplement papers before building upon them. We introduce NERFIFY, a multi-agent framework that reliably converts NeRF research papers into trainable Nerfstudio plugins, in contrast to generic paper-to-code methods and frontier models like GPT-5 that usually fail to produce runnable code. NERFIFY achieves domain-specific executability through six key innovations: (1) Context-free grammar (CFG): LLM synthesis is constrained by Nerfstudio formalized as a CFG, ensuring generated code satisfies architectural invariants. (2) Graph-of-Thought code synthesis: Specialized multi-file-agents generate repositories in topological dependency order, validating contracts and errors at each node. (3) Compositional citation recovery: Agents automatically retrieve and integrate components (samplers, encoders, proposal networks) from citation graphs of references. (4) Visual feedback: Artifacts are diagnosed through PSNR-minima ROI analysis, cross-view geometric validation, and VLM-guided patching to iteratively improve quality. (5) Knowledge enhancement: Beyond reproduction, methods can be improved with novel optimizations. (6) Benchmarking: An evaluation framework is designed for NeRF paper-to-code synthesis across 30 diverse papers. On papers without public implementations, NERFIFY achieves visual quality matching expert human code (+/-0.5 dB PSNR, +/-0.2 SSIM) while reducing implementation time from weeks to minutes. NERFIFY demonstrates that a domain-aware design enables code translation for complex vision papers, potentiating accelerated and democratized reproducible research. Code, data and implementations will be publicly released.

AI.tldr: NERFIFY is a domain-aware, multi-agent framework that converts NeRF research papers into runnable Nerfstudio plugins, using CFG-based synthesis, Graph-of-Thought collaboration, citation-driven component integration, visual quality feedback, knowledge enhancement, and benchmarking across 30 papers; it enables near-expert-quality reproduction with large time savings.
AI.motivation: Reproducibility and rapid prototyping in NeRF literature. Generic paper-to-code methods and large models often fail to produce runnable code; a domain-specific, verifiable workflow is needed to accelerate reproducible NeRF research.
AI.method: Six innovations: (1) Context-free grammar (CFG) constrains LLM output to Nerfstudio architecture, preserving invariants; (2) Graph-of-Thought code synthesis with multi-file agents generating repositories in dependency order and validating contracts/errors at each node; (3) Compositional citation recovery to fetch and integrate components (samplers, encoders, proposal networks) from reference citation graphs; (4) Visual feedback including PSNR-minima ROI analysis, cross-view geometric validation, and VLM-guided patching to iteratively improve quality; (5) Knowledge enhancement enabling not only reproduction but novel optimizations; (6) Benchmarking framework evaluating NeRF paper-to-code synthesis across 30 papers;
AI.result: For papers without public implementations, NERFIFY achieves visual quality on par with expert human code (within ±0.5 dB PSNR and ±0.2 SSIM) while reducing implementation time from weeks to minutes; a 30-paper evaluation demonstrates broad applicability; code, data, and implementations will be publicly released.
AI.conclusion: Domain-aware design enables reliable translation of complex vision papers into runnable NeRF code, accelerating reproducible research and democratizing access to NeRF advancements.

title: Relatively Smart: A New Approach for Instance-Optimal Learning
summary:
We revisit the framework of Smart PAC learning, which seeks supervised learners which compete with semi-supervised learners that are provided full knowledge of the marginal distribution on unlabeled data. Prior work has shown that such marginal-by-marginal guarantees are possible for "most" marginals, with respect to an arbitrary fixed and known measure, but not more generally. We discover that this failure can be attributed to an "indistinguishability" phenomenon: There are marginals which cannot be statistically distinguished from other marginals that require different learning approaches. In such settings, semi-supervised learning cannot certify its guarantees from unlabeled data, rendering them arguably non-actionable.
  We propose relatively smart learning, a new framework which demands that a supervised learner compete only with the best "certifiable" semi-supervised guarantee. We show that such modest relaxation suffices to bypass the impossibility results from prior work. In the distribution-free setting, we show that the OIG learner is relatively smart up to squaring the sample complexity, and show that no supervised learning algorithm can do better. For distribution-family settings, we show that relatively smart learning can be impossible or can require idiosyncratic learning approaches, and its difficulty can be non-monotone in the inclusion order on distribution families.

AI.tldr: Relatively smart learning reframes Smart PAC: it seeks supervised learners that compete with the best certifiable semi-supervised guarantees, addressing indistinguishability among marginals. It introduces the OIG learner, showing distribution-free gains up to squaring the sample complexity and optimality, while in distribution-family settings the feasibility varies and can be non-monotone in distribution inclusion.
AI.motivation: Traditional marginal-by-marginal guarantees fail due to indistinguishability between marginals that require different learning approaches, making semi-supervised guarantees non-actionable.
AI.method: Introduce relatively smart learning; define the OIG (Optimally Impartial Guarantee) learner; analyze in distribution-free and distribution-family settings; compare against standard supervised learning.
AI.result: In the distribution-free setting, the OIG learner is relatively smart up to squaring the sample complexity, and no supervised learner can do better. In distribution-family settings, relatively smart learning can be impossible or require idiosyncratic methods, with difficulty that can be non-monotone in the inclusion order of distribution families.
AI.conclusion: Relaxing to certifiable semi-supervised guarantees bypasses prior impossibility results; substantial gains in distribution-free settings, but limitations persist and depend on the chosen distribution family.

title: COMBAT: Conditional World Models for Behavioral Agent Training
summary:
Recent advances in video generation have spurred the development of world models capable of simulating 3D-consistent environments and interactions with static objects. However, a significant limitation remains in their ability to model dynamic, reactive agents that can intelligently influence and interact with the world. To address this gap, we introduce COMBAT, a real-time, action-controlled world model trained on the complex 1v1 fighting game Tekken 3. Our work demonstrates that diffusion models can successfully simulate a dynamic opponent that reacts to player actions, learning its behavior implicitly.
  Our approach utilizes a 1.2 billion parameter Diffusion Transformer, conditioned on latent representations from a deep compression autoencoder. We employ state-of-the-art techniques, including causal distillation and diffusion forcing, to achieve real-time inference. Crucially, we observe the emergence of sophisticated agent behavior by training the model solely on single-player inputs, without any explicit supervision for the opponent's policy. Unlike traditional imitation learning methods, which require complete action labels, COMBAT learns effectively from partially observed data to generate responsive behaviors for a controllable Player 1. We present an extensive study and introduce novel evaluation methods to benchmark this emergent agent behavior, establishing a strong foundation for training interactive agents within diffusion-based world models.

AI.tldr: COMBAT presents a real-time, action-controlled diffusion-based world model trained on Tekken 3 to generate a reactive opponent. It learns sophisticated opponent behavior from single-player data without explicit opponent supervision, using a 1.2B-parameter Diffusion Transformer conditioned on latent encodings from a compression autoencoder, and employs causal distillation and diffusion forcing for real-time inference.
AI.motivation: Address the limitation of diffusion-based world models in handling dynamic, interactive agents. Current models often simulate static objects or passive dynamics; there is a need for intelligent, responsive agents that can interact with and influence the environment, learned with minimal supervision.
AI.method: Train a 1.2B parameter Diffusion Transformer conditioned on latent representations from a deep compression autoencoder. Use causal distillation and diffusion forcing to enable real-time inference. Train exclusively on single-player Tekken 3 data, enabling emergent opponent behavior without explicit opponent policy supervision, and evaluate emergent responsiveness under partial observation.
AI.result: The model exhibits emergent, sophisticated opponent behavior that responds to Player 1 actions. It learns to generate reactive dynamics without explicit opponent policy labels and demonstrates effective real-time inference. The work also introduces novel evaluation methods for benchmarking emergent behavior in diffusion-based world models.
AI.conclusion: Diffusion-based world models can yield reactive, controllable agents learned from partial supervision. COMBAT provides a foundation for training interactive agents within diffusion frameworks and establishes evaluation protocols for emergent opponent behavior in dynamic environments.

title: UTICA: Multi-Objective Self-Distllation Foundation Model Pretraining for Time Series Classification
summary: Self-supervised foundation models have achieved remarkable success across domains, including time series. However, the potential of non-contrastive methods, a paradigm that has driven significant advances in computer vision, remains underexplored for time series. In this work, we adapt DINOv2-style self-distillation to pretrain a time series foundation model, building on the Mantis tokenizer and transformer encoder architecture as our backbone. Through a student-teacher framework, our method Utica learns representations that capture both temporal invariance via augmented crops and fine-grained local structure via patch masking. Our approach achieves state-of-the-art classification performance on both UCR and UEA benchmarks. These results suggest that non-contrastive methods are a promising and complementary pretraining strategy for time series foundation models.

AI.tldr: Non-contrastive self-distillation (DINOv2-style) for time-series foundation models (Utica) using Mantis tokenizer and Transformer encoder, achieving SOTA on UCR/UEA; demonstrates temporal invariance via augmented crops and local structure via patch masking.
AI.motivation: Investigate non-contrastive pretraining for time series foundation models, an underexplored area despite successes in computer vision; leverage self-distillation to learn robust representations.
AI.method: Adapt DINOv2-style student-teacher self-distillation; backbone: Mantis tokenizer + Transformer encoder; training signals from augmented crops for temporal invariance and patch masking for local structure; Utica performs pretraining and downstream classification.
AI.result: State-of-the-art classification on UCR and UEA benchmarks; results suggest non-contrastive pretraining is a promising and complementary strategy for time-series foundation models.
AI.conclusion: Non-contrastive pretraining is a viable and beneficial paradigm for time-series foundation models, complementing existing approaches and expanding the repertoire of self-supervised methods in this domain.

title: MME: Mixture of Mesh Experts with Random Walk Transformer Gating
summary: In recent years, various methods have been proposed for mesh analysis, each offering distinct advantages and often excelling on different object classes. We present a novel Mixture of Experts (MoE) framework designed to harness the complementary strengths of these diverse approaches. We propose a new gate architecture that encourages each expert to specialise in the classes it excels in. Our design is guided by two key ideas: (1) random walks over the mesh surface effectively capture the regions that individual experts attend to, and (2) an attention mechanism that enables the gate to focus on the areas most informative for each expert's decision-making. To further enhance performance, we introduce a dynamic loss balancing scheme that adjusts a trade-off between diversity and similarity losses throughout the training, where diversity prompts expert specialization, and similarity enables knowledge sharing among the experts. Our framework achieves state-of-the-art results in mesh classification, retrieval, and semantic segmentation tasks. Our code is available at: https://github.com/amirbelder/MME-Mixture-of-Mesh-Experts.

AI.tldr: A Mixture of Mesh Experts (MoE) framework uses random-walk–based region cues and attention-guided gating to specialize experts for class-specific mesh analysis, with a dynamic loss that balances diversity and similarity to enable specialization and knowledge sharing, achieving state-of-the-art results in mesh classification, retrieval, and semantic segmentation.
AI.motivation: No single mesh analysis method excels across all object classes; combining diverse approaches through a Mixture of Experts can leverage their complementary strengths and improve performance across tasks.
AI.method: Proposes a MoE framework with a new gate architecture that encourages per-expert specialization, a random-walk-based encoding of mesh regions to reflect where each expert attends, an attention mechanism to focus the gate on informative regions for each expert’s decisions, and a dynamic loss-balancing scheme that alternates between diversity (to promote specialization) and similarity (to enable knowledge sharing).
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art results on mesh classification, retrieval, and semantic segmentation; code released at the provided GitHub link.
AI.conclusion: A region-aware, attention-guided Mixture of Mesh Experts with a dynamic diversity–similarity loss jointly improves performance by enabling expert specialization and cross-expert knowledge sharing across multiple mesh tasks.

title: Constructing Synthetic Instruction Datasets for Improving Reasoning in Domain-Specific LLMs: A Case Study in the Japanese Financial Domain
comment: 8 pages, 2 figures. Japanese version published in NLP2026
summary: In adapting LLMs to specific domains, achieving both domain expertise and reasoning ability remains an urgent challenge. This study proposes a general method for constructing high-quality synthetic instruction data for any domain, starting from domain-specific vocabulary. As a demonstration, we applied this method to the financial domain and constructed a large-scale instruction dataset totaling approximately 9.5 billion tokens with Chain-of-Thought reasoning traces. Evaluation results confirmed performance improvements over baseline models on financial benchmarks, demonstrating the effectiveness of our approach. We also report findings on the impact of reasoning trace length on performance and its limitations. Lastly, we open-source our models and datasets on https://huggingface.co/nri-ai .

AI.tldr: A general framework to generate high-quality synthetic instruction data from domain vocabularies, enabling domain-specific LLMs with reasoning; demonstrated in finance with 9.5B tokens and Chain-of-Thought traces, yielding improvements over baselines and insights on reasoning trace length; models and data open-sourced.
AI.motivation: Domain-specific LLMs require both expertise and reasoning; existing instruction data is scarce for many domains; a scalable method to bootstrap data from domain vocabulary can unlock domain adaptation with reasoning capabilities.
AI.method: Construct synthetic instruction data starting from domain-specific vocabulary to create high-quality instruction datasets. Demonstrated in the financial domain by assembling a large-scale dataset (~9.5B tokens) with Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning traces. Train/finetune LLMs on this data and evaluate on financial benchmarks; analyze the effect of reasoning trace length and its limitations; release models and datasets open-source.
AI.result: The approach yields performance gains over baseline models on financial benchmarks, validating the effectiveness of synthetic data with CoT traces. The study also reveals how reasoning trace length impacts performance and highlights its limitations. Open-source release of models and data on HuggingFace (nri-ai).
AI.conclusion: A scalable, domain-agnostic pipeline for synthetic instruction data generation can effectively imbue LLMs with domain expertise and reasoning. The financial-domain demonstration shows practical improvements, and the open-source release facilitates broader adoption and extension to other domains.

title: Neural Discrimination-Prompted Transformers for Efficient UHD Image Restoration and Enhancement
comment: Accepted by IJCV'26; code is available at https://github.com/supersupercong/uhdpromer
summary: We propose a simple yet effective UHDPromer, a neural discrimination-prompted Transformer, for Ultra-High-Definition (UHD) image restoration and enhancement. Our UHDPromer is inspired by an interesting observation that there implicitly exist neural differences between high-resolution and low-resolution features, and exploring such differences can facilitate low-resolution feature representation. To this end, we first introduce Neural Discrimination Priors (NDP) to measure the differences and then integrate NDP into the proposed Neural Discrimination-Prompted Attention (NDPA) and Neural Discrimination-Prompted Network (NDPN). The proposed NDPA re-formulates the attention by incorporating NDP to globally perceive useful discrimination information, while the NDPN explores a continuous gating mechanism guided by NDP to selectively permit the passage of beneficial content. To enhance the quality of restored images, we propose a super-resolution-guided reconstruction approach, which is guided by super-resolving low-resolution features to facilitate final UHD image restoration. Experiments show that UHDPromer achieves the best computational efficiency while still maintaining state-of-the-art performance on $3$ UHD image restoration and enhancement tasks, including low-light image enhancement, image dehazing, and image deblurring. The source codes and pre-trained models will be made available at https://github.com/supersupercong/uhdpromer.

AI.tldr: UHDPromer: a neural discrimination-promoted transformer for UHD image restoration; introduces Neural Discrimination Priors to guide attention and gating, plus SR-guided reconstruction; claims state-of-the-art efficiency with competitive performance on UHD tasks.
AI.motivation: There are implicit neural differences between high- and low-resolution features; exploiting these differences via neural discrimination priors can improve low-resolution feature representations for UHD restoration.
AI.method: Define Neural Discrimination Priors (NDP); incorporate into Neural Discrimination-Prompted Attention (NDPA) and Neural Discrimination-Prompted Network (NDPN); add super-resolution-guided reconstruction; report efficiency and SOTA results on UHD restoration tasks.
AI.result: Experiments show UHDPromer achieves best computational efficiency while maintaining state-of-the-art performance on 3 UHD restoration tasks: low-light enhancement, dehazing, deblurring.
AI.conclusion: UHDPromer provides an effective, efficient architecture for UHD image restoration by leveraging neural discrimination priors to guide attention and gating, with SR-guided reconstruction; code to be released.

title: Fed-GAME: Personalized Federated Learning with Graph Attention Mixture-of-Experts For Time-Series Forecasting
summary: Federated learning (FL) on graphs shows promise for distributed time-series forecasting. Yet, existing methods rely on static topologies and struggle with client heterogeneity. We propose Fed-GAME, a framework that models personalized aggregation as message passing over a learnable dynamic implicit graph. The core is a decoupled parameter difference-based update protocol, where clients transmit parameter differences between their fine-tuned private model and a shared global model. On the server, these differences are decomposed into two streams: (1) averaged difference used to updating the global model for consensus (2) the selective difference fed into a novel Graph Attention Mixture-of-Experts (GAME) aggregator for fine-grained personalization. In this aggregator, shared experts provide scoring signals while personalized gates adaptively weight selective updates to support personalized aggregation. Experiments on two real-world electric vehicle charging datasets demonstrate that Fed-GAME outperforms state-of-the-art personalized FL baselines.

AI.tldr: Fed-GAME introduces a dynamic implicit graph for personalized federated learning on graphs, using a decoupled parameter-difference update and a Graph Attention Mixture-of-Experts (GAME) aggregator to improve personalization and global performance in time-series forecasting.
AI.motivation: Address static topologies and client heterogeneity in federated learning on graph-structured time-series data, aiming for effective personalized aggregation.
AI.method: Clients send parameter differences between their fine-tuned private model and the global model. Server decomposes these differences into an averaged part to update the global model and a selective part fed into a Graph Attention Mixture-of-Experts (GAME) aggregator. GAME uses shared experts for scoring signals and personalized gates to weight updates, with a learnable dynamic implicit graph guiding message passing.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on two real-world electric vehicle charging datasets shows Fed-GAME outperforms state-of-the-art personalized FL baselines.
AI.conclusion: A dynamic implicit graph and the GAME aggregator enable improved personalized aggregation and forecasting performance in graph-based federated learning, addressing heterogeneity and topology staticity.

title: PPC-MT: Parallel Point Cloud Completion with Mamba-Transformer Hybrid Architecture
summary: Existing point cloud completion methods struggle to balance high-quality reconstruction with computational efficiency. To address this, we propose PPC-MT, a novel parallel framework for point cloud completion leveraging a hybrid Mamba-Transformer architecture. Our approach introduces an innovative parallel completion strategy guided by Principal Component Analysis (PCA), which imposes a geometrically meaningful structure on unordered point clouds, transforming them into ordered sets and decomposing them into multiple subsets. These subsets are reconstructed in parallel using a multi-head reconstructor. This structured parallel synthesis paradigm significantly enhances the uniformity of point distribution and detail fidelity, while preserving computational efficiency. By integrating Mamba's linear complexity for efficient feature extraction during encoding with the Transformer's capability to model fine-grained multi-sequence relationships during decoding, PPC-MT effectively balances efficiency and reconstruction accuracy. Extensive quantitative and qualitative experiments on benchmark datasets, including PCN, ShapeNet-55/34, and KITTI, demonstrate that PPC-MT outperforms state-of-the-art methods across multiple metrics, validating the efficacy of our proposed framework.

AI.tldr: PPC-MT introduces a PCA-guided, parallelized point cloud completion framework that combines Mamba-based encoding and Transformer-based decoding to achieve efficient, high-quality reconstruction by partitioning unordered clouds into subsets and reconstructing them in parallel.
AI.motivation: Address the trade-off between reconstruction quality and computational efficiency in point cloud completion by imposing geometric structure on unordered points and enabling parallel synthesis.
AI.method: A PCA-driven process orders unordered point clouds, decomposing them into multiple subsets. These subsets are reconstructed in parallel by a multi-head reconstructor. The encoding uses Mamba for linear-time feature extraction, while decoding leverages Transformer capabilities to model multi-sequence relationships. This yields a structured parallel synthesis framework.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on PCN, ShapeNet-55/34, and KITTI show PPC-MT outperforms state-of-the-art methods across several metrics, validating the approach.
AI.conclusion: PPC-MT effectively balances efficiency and reconstruction accuracy through a PCA-guided parallel framework that integrates Mamba-based encoding and Transformer-based decoding for robust point cloud completion.

title: MMTA: Multi Membership Temporal Attention for Fine-Grained Stroke Rehabilitation Assessment
summary: To empower the iterative assessments involved during a person's rehabilitation, automated assessment of a person's abilities during daily activities requires temporally precise segmentation of fine-grained actions in therapy videos. Existing temporal action segmentation (TAS) models struggle to capture sub-second micro-movements while retaining exercise context, blurring rapid phase transitions and limiting reliable downstream assessment of motor recovery. We introduce Multi-Membership Temporal Attention (MMTA), a high-resolution temporal transformer for fine-grained rehabilitation assessment. Unlike standard temporal attention, which assigns each frame a single attention context per layer, MMTA lets each frame attend to multiple locally normalized temporal attention windows within the same layer. We fuse these concurrent temporal views via feature-space overlap resolution, preserving competing local contexts near transitions while enabling longer-range reasoning through layer-wise propagation. This increases boundary sensitivity without additional depth or multi-stage refinement. MMTA supports both video and wearable IMU inputs within a unified single-stage architecture, making it applicable to both clinical and home settings. MMTA consistently improves over the Global Attention transformer, boosting Edit Score by +1.3 (Video) and +1.6 (IMU) on StrokeRehab while further improving 50Salads by +3.3. Ablations confirm that performance gains stem from multi-membership temporal views rather than architectural complexity, offering a practical solution for resource-constrained rehabilitation assessment.

AI.tldr: MMTA provides high-resolution, multi-membership temporal attention for fine-grained TAS in rehabilitation, achieving better boundary sensitivity in a single-stage architecture across video and IMU inputs; improves over Global Attention on StrokeRehab and 50Salads.
AI.motivation: Sub-second micro-movements and rapid phase transitions in rehab videos require precise temporal segmentation; standard TAS with single-frame attention blurs transitions and hampers motor-recovery assessment, especially in resource-constrained settings.
AI.method: Introduce Multi-Membership Temporal Attention (MMTA): within a single layer, each frame attends to multiple locally normalized temporal windows; fuse views via feature-space overlap resolution; single-stage transformer architecture; modality-unified for video and IMU inputs.
AI.result: MMTA yields higher boundary sensitivity and overall segmentation quality: +1.3 Edit Score (Video) and +1.6 (IMU) on StrokeRehab; +3.3 on 50Salads; ablative evidence that gains originate from multi-membership views rather than architectural depth/complexity.
AI.conclusion: MMTA achieves improved fine-grained TAS without increasing model depth or requiring multi-stage refinement, enabling practical rehabilitation assessment in clinical and home settings, across modalities.

title: DUEL: Exact Likelihood for Masked Diffusion via Deterministic Unmasking
summary: Masked diffusion models (MDMs) generate text by iteratively selecting positions to unmask and then predicting tokens at those positions. Yet MDMs lack proper perplexity evaluation: the ELBO is a loose bound on likelihood under the training distribution, not the test-time distribution, while generative perplexity requires a biased external model and ignores diversity. To address this, we introduce the \textsc{DUEL} framework, which formalizes \emph{deterministic} position selection, unifying leading MDM sampling strategies. We prove \textbf{\textsc{DUEL} admits \emph{exact} likelihood computation} via a simple algorithm, evaluated under the same position selection used at test time. This \textbf{gives MDMs proper perplexity for the first time} -- the natural analogue of autoregressive perplexity. With proper perplexity in hand, we revisit key questions about MDMs. \textbf{MDMs are substantially better than previously thought}: the MDM-autoregressive perplexity gap shrinks by up to 32\% on in-domain data and 82\% on zero-shot benchmarks. \textsc{DUEL} enables the first principled comparison of fast, parallel samplers across compute budgets -- an analysis impossible with the ELBO and unreliable with generative perplexity -- identifying probability margin \citep{kim2025train} as a strong default. Finally, oracle search over position orderings reveals MDMs can far surpass autoregressive models -- achieving 36.47 vs.\ 52.11 perplexity on AG News -- demonstrating the ceiling of MDM performance has not yet been reached.

AI.tldr: DUEL provides exact likelihood for masked diffusion models under deterministic position selection, enabling proper perplexity and revealing that MDMs are closer to autoregressive models than previously thought; oracle ordering suggests further gains.
AI.motivation: Address the mismatch between ELBO-based evaluation and test-time distribution; provide a principled perplexity for MDMs; enable fair comparisons of samplers across compute budgets.
AI.method: Impose deterministic position selection and derive an algorithm to compute exact likelihood; apply DUEL to standard MDM sampling strategies; evaluate perplexity using same test-time orderings.
AI.result: MDM perplexity gap to autoregressive models shrinks significantly (up to 32% in-domain, 82% zero-shot); enables principled comparison of samplers; probability margin is a strong default; oracle ordering can surpass AR models (AG News: 36.47 vs 52.11 perplexity).
AI.conclusion: DUEL provides a principled framework to evaluate MDMs; the ceiling of MDM performance appears higher than previously thought; future work exploring more orderings and sampling strategies; better perplexity metrics will drive progress.

title: Causal Neural Probabilistic Circuits
summary: Concept Bottleneck Models (CBMs) enhance the interpretability of end-to-end neural networks by introducing a layer of concepts and predicting the class label from the concept predictions. A key property of CBMs is that they support interventions, i.e., domain experts can correct mispredicted concept values at test time to improve the final accuracy. However, typical CBMs apply interventions by overwriting only the corrected concept while leaving other concept predictions unchanged, which ignores causal dependencies among concepts. To address this, we propose the Causal Neural Probabilistic Circuit (CNPC), which combines a neural attribute predictor with a causal probabilistic circuit compiled from a causal graph. This circuit supports exact, tractable causal inference that inherently respects causal dependencies. Under interventions, CNPC models the class distribution based on a Product of Experts (PoE) that fuses the attribute predictor's predictive distribution with the interventional marginals computed by the circuit. We theoretically characterize the compositional interventional error of CNPC w.r.t. its modules and identify conditions under which CNPC closely matches the ground-truth interventional class distribution. Experiments on five benchmark datasets in both in-distribution and out-of-distribution settings show that, compared with five baseline models, CNPC achieves higher task accuracy across different numbers of intervened attributes.

AI.tldr: CNPC is a causal extension of concept bottleneck models that uses a neural attribute predictor and a causal probabilistic circuit to perform exact, tractable inference under interventions; it fuses predictor and interventional marginals via a Product of Experts, yielding higher accuracy than baselines on five benchmarks in ID and OOD settings.
AI.motivation: To fix CBMs' limitation of ignoring causal dependencies among concepts during test-time interventions and to provide exact, tractable causal inference that respects the causal graph structure.
AI.method: Integrate a neural attribute predictor with a causal probabilistic circuit derived from a causal graph. Under interventions, compute interventional marginals with the circuit and fuse them with the predictor's distribution through a Product of Experts. Theoretically analyze compositional interventional error and identify conditions for close alignment with ground-truth interventional distributions.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on five benchmark datasets shows CNPC achieves higher task accuracy than five baselines across varying numbers of intervened attributes, in both in-distribution and out-of-distribution settings.
AI.conclusion: CNPC effectively enforces causal dependencies during interventions, enables exact inference via the circuit, and, under certain conditions, closely matches the ground-truth interventional distribution, demonstrating robustness and improved accuracy in both ID and OOD regimes.

title: 3BASiL: An Algorithmic Framework for Sparse plus Low-Rank Compression of LLMs
comment: The Thirty-ninth Annual Conference on Neural Information Processing Systems
summary: Sparse plus Low-Rank $(\mathbf{S} + \mathbf{LR})$ decomposition of Large Language Models (LLMs) has emerged as a promising direction in model compression, aiming to decompose pre-trained model weights into a sum of sparse and low-rank matrices $(\mathbf{W} \approx \mathbf{S} + \mathbf{LR})$. Despite recent progress, existing methods often suffer from substantial performance degradation compared to dense models. In this work, we introduce 3BASiL-TM, an efficient one-shot post-training method for $(\mathbf{S} + \mathbf{LR})$ decomposition of LLMs that addresses this gap. Our approach first introduces a novel 3-Block Alternating Direction Method of Multipliers (ADMM) method, termed 3BASiL, to minimize the layer-wise reconstruction error with convergence guarantees. We then design an efficient transformer-matching (TM) refinement step that jointly optimizes the sparse and low-rank components across transformer layers. This step minimizes a novel memory-efficient loss that aligns outputs at the transformer level. Notably, the TM procedure is universal as it can enhance any $(\mathbf{S} + \mathbf{LR})$ decomposition, including pure sparsity. Our numerical experiments show that 3BASiL-TM reduces the WikiText2 perplexity gap relative to dense LLaMA-8B model by over 30% under a (2:4 Sparse + 64 LR) configuration, compared to prior methods. Moreover, our method achieves over 2.5x faster compression runtime on an A100 GPU compared to SOTA $(\mathbf{S} + \mathbf{LR})$ method. Our code is available at https://github.com/mazumder-lab/3BASiL.

AI.tldr: Efficient S+LR decomposition for LLMs via 3-block ADMM and a transformer-matching refinement (TM) that improves accuracy and speed in one shot.
AI.motivation: Close the performance gap between sparse+low-rank decompositions and dense models while preserving speed, enabling practical compression of large language models.
AI.method: (1) 3BASiL: a 3-block Alternating Direction Method of Multipliers to minimize layer-wise reconstruction error with convergence guarantees for S+LR decomposition. (2) TM refinement: a memory-efficient, transformer-wide loss that jointly optimizes sparse and low-rank components across layers to align transformer outputs; TM is universal and compatible with various S+LR decompositions.
AI.result: On WikiText2 with 2:4 sparsity and 64 low-rank components, achieves >30% perplexity gap reduction relative to dense LLaMA-8B; delivers over 2.5x faster compression runtime on A100 vs SOTA S+LR methods; code released.
AI.conclusion: 3BASiL-TM provides a scalable, one-shot post-training approach for effective S+LR decomposition in LLMs, with a universal TM refinement that enhances performance across decompositions and speeds up compression.

title: pySpatial: Generating 3D Visual Programs for Zero-Shot Spatial Reasoning
comment: Accepted at ICLR 2026, Project Page: Our project: https://pySpatial.github.io
summary: Multi-modal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have demonstrated strong capabilities in general-purpose perception and reasoning, but they still struggle with tasks that require spatial understanding of the 3D world. To address this, we introduce pySpatial, a visual programming framework that equips MLLMs with the ability to interface with spatial tools via Python code generation. Given an image sequence and a natural-language query, the model composes function calls to spatial tools including 3D reconstruction, camera-pose recovery, novel-view rendering, etc. These operations convert raw 2D inputs into an explorable 3D scene, enabling MLLMs to reason explicitly over structured spatial representations. Notably, pySpatial requires no gradient-based fine-tuning and operates in a fully zero-shot setting. Experimental evaluations on the challenging MindCube and Omni3D-Bench benchmarks demonstrate that our framework pySpatial consistently surpasses strong MLLM baselines; for instance, it outperforms GPT-4.1-mini by 12.94% on MindCube. Furthermore, we conduct real-world indoor navigation experiments where the robot can successfully traverse complex environments using route plans generated by pySpatial, highlighting the practical effectiveness of our approach.

AI.tldr: pySpatial enables zero-shot assembly of spatial tools via Python code generation to convert image sequences and natural-language queries into explorable 3D scenes, improving MLLM performance on 3D spatial tasks without fine-tuning.
AI.motivation: MLLMs perform well in perception and reasoning but struggle with explicit 3D spatial understanding. Bridging 2D inputs to structured 3D representations through programmable spatial tools can improve task performance and interpretability.
AI.method: A visual programming framework that maps an image sequence and NL query to Python-generated calls to spatial tools (3D reconstruction, camera-pose recovery, novel-view rendering) to construct explorable 3D scenes; operates entirely in zero-shot mode with no gradient fine-tuning.
AI.result: Empirical results on MindCube and Omni3D-Bench show pySpatial consistently surpassing strong MLLM baselines, including surpassing GPT-4.1-mini by 12.94% on MindCube; real-world indoor navigation experiments demonstrate successful traversal using route plans generated by pySpatial.
AI.conclusion: pySpatial demonstrates a practical zero-shot pathway to augment MLLMs with explicit 3D spatial reasoning via programmable tool use, enabling improved benchmark performance and real-world navigational capabilities without fine-tuning.

title: Invariant-Stratified Propagation for Expressive Graph Neural Networks
summary: Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) face fundamental limitations in expressivity and capturing structural heterogeneity. Standard message-passing architectures are constrained by the 1-dimensional Weisfeiler-Leman (1-WL) test, unable to distinguish graphs beyond degree sequences, and aggregate information uniformly from neighbors, failing to capture how nodes occupy different structural positions within higher-order patterns. While methods exist to achieve higher expressivity, they incur prohibitive computational costs and lack unified frameworks for flexibly encoding diverse structural properties. To address these limitations, we introduce Invariant-Stratified Propagation (ISP), a framework comprising both a novel WL variant (ISP-WL) and its efficient neural network implementation (ISPGNN). ISP stratifies nodes according to graph invariants, processing them in hierarchical strata that reveal structural distinctions invisible to 1-WL. Through hierarchical structural heterogeneity encoding, ISP quantifies differences in nodes' structural positions within higher-order patterns, distinguishing interactions where participants occupy different roles from those with uniform participation. We provide formal theoretical analysis establishing enhanced expressivity beyond 1-WL, convergence guarantees, and inherent resistance to oversmoothing. Extensive experiments across graph classification, node classification, and influence estimation demonstrate consistent improvements over standard architectures and state-of-the-art expressive baselines.

AI.tldr: Introduces Invariant-Stratified Propagation (ISP) for graph representation learning, combining a new WL variant (ISP-WL) with a scalable neural implementation (ISPGNN) that stratifies nodes by graph invariants into hierarchical layers to capture higher-order structural roles beyond 1-WL, with theoretical guarantees and strong empirical results.
AI.motivation: 1-WL-based GNNs cannot distinguish graphs beyond degree sequences and fail to capture nodes' positions within higher-order motifs; need a unified, scalable framework to encode diverse structural properties without prohibitive cost.
AI.method: Define ISP-WL to stratify nodes into strata according to graph invariants; propagate messages within and across strata; implement an efficient neural network (ISPGNN) that realizes ISP-WL; provide theoretical analysis for enhanced expressivity, convergence, and resistance to oversmoothing; validate on graph classification, node classification, and influence estimation.
AI.result: The framework achieves expressivity beyond 1-WL with convergence guarantees and reduced oversmoothing; empirical results show improvements over standard GNNs and state-of-the-art expressive baselines across multiple tasks.
AI.conclusion: ISP offers a unified, scalable approach to capture structural heterogeneity and higher-order roles in graphs, yielding both theoretical and practical performance gains.

title: ShiftLUT: Spatial Shift Enhanced Look-Up Tables for Efficient Image Restoration
summary: Look-Up Table based methods have emerged as a promising direction for efficient image restoration tasks. Recent LUT-based methods focus on improving their performance by expanding the receptive field. However, they inevitably introduce extra computational and storage overhead, which hinders their deployment in edge devices. To address this issue, we propose ShiftLUT, a novel framework that attains the largest receptive field among all LUT-based methods while maintaining high efficiency. Our key insight lies in three complementary components. First, Learnable Spatial Shift module (LSS) is introduced to expand the receptive field by applying learnable, channel-wise spatial offsets on feature maps. Second, we propose an asymmetric dual-branch architecture that allocates more computation to the information-dense branch, substantially reducing inference latency without compromising restoration quality. Finally, we incorporate a feature-level LUT compression strategy called Error-bounded Adaptive Sampling (EAS) to minimize the storage overhead. Compared to the previous state-of-the-art method TinyLUT, ShiftLUT achieves a 3.8$\times$ larger receptive field and improves an average PSNR by over 0.21 dB across multiple standard benchmarks, while maintaining a small storage size and inference time.

AI.tldr: ShiftLUT delivers the largest receptive field among LUT-based image restoration methods with high efficiency, by integrating learnable spatial shifts, an asymmetric dual-branch design, and an error-bounded adaptive sampling LUT compression; it surpasses TinyLUT by ~3.8x receptive field and ~0.21 dB PSNR on standard benchmarks while maintaining small storage and fast inference.
AI.motivation: LUT-based restoration is efficient but struggles with receptive-field expansion and growing storage/computation. To enable edge deployment, larger receptive fields are needed without prohibitive cost.
AI.method: Three components: (1) Learnable Spatial Shift (LSS) to expand receptive field via channel-wise spatial offsets; (2) asymmetric dual-branch architecture to allocate more computation to the information-dense branch and reduce latency; (3) Error-bounded Adaptive Sampling (EAS) to compress LUTs with controlled error and reduce storage.
AI.result: ShiftLUT achieves ~3.8× larger receptive field than prior LUT-based methods; average PSNR gain >0.21 dB across multiple standard benchmarks; maintains small storage size and fast inference time, outperforming the previous state-of-the-art TinyLUT.
AI.conclusion: ShiftLUT offers the best efficiency-receptive-field trade-off among LUT-based restoration methods and is well-suited for edge devices, demonstrating that large receptive-field LUT-based restoration can be achieved with controlled storage and latency.

title: One Operator to Rule Them All? On Boundary-Indexed Operator Families in Neural PDE Solvers
comment: Accepted to the ICLR 2026 Workshop on AI & PDEs. 10 pages, 5 figures
summary: Neural PDE solvers are often described as learning solution operators that map problem data to PDE solutions. In this work, we argue that this interpretation is generally incorrect when boundary conditions vary. We show that standard neural operator training implicitly learns a boundary-indexed family of operators, rather than a single boundary-agnostic operator, with the learned mapping fundamentally conditioned on the boundary-condition distribution seen during training. We formalize this perspective by framing operator learning as conditional risk minimization over boundary conditions, which leads to a non-identifiability result outside the support of the training boundary distribution. As a consequence, generalization in forcing terms or resolution does not imply generalization across boundary conditions. We support our theoretical analysis with controlled experiments on the Poisson equation, demonstrating sharp degradation under boundary-condition shifts, cross-distribution failures between distinct boundary ensembles, and convergence to conditional expectations when boundary information is removed. Our results clarify a core limitation of current neural PDE solvers and highlight the need for explicit boundary-aware modeling in the pursuit of foundation models for PDEs.

AI.tldr: Neural PDE solvers implicitly learn a boundary-indexed family of operators conditioned on the boundary-condition distribution, not a single boundary-agnostic operator; generalization across boundary conditions is not guaranteed.
AI.motivation: Broadly used neural PDE solvers often assume a universal operator, but varying boundary conditions can fundamentally alter the operator mapping; this work formalizes and tests that limitation.
AI.method: Theoretically frames operator learning as conditional risk minimization over boundary conditions, proving non-identifiability outside the training boundary support; empirically validates via Poisson equation experiments—boundary shifts degrade performance, cross-distribution failures occur between boundary ensembles, and removing boundary information collapses to conditional expectations.
AI.result: Experiments show sharp degradation with boundary-condition shifts, fails to generalize across different boundary ensembles, and converges to the conditional expectation when boundary data is removed; confirms boundary-aware modeling is essential.
AI.conclusion: A core limitation of current neural PDE solvers is revealed; explicit boundary-aware modeling (boundary-conditioned foundation models) is necessary for robust PDE solving and generalization across boundary scenarios.

title: UD-SfPNet: An Underwater Descattering Shape-from-Polarization Network for 3D Normal Reconstruction
summary: Underwater optical imaging is severely hindered by scattering, but polarization imaging offers the unique dual advantages of descattering and shape-from-polarization (SfP) 3D reconstruction. To exploit these advantages, this paper proposes UD-SfPNet, an underwater descattering shape-from-polarization network that leverages polarization cues for improved 3D surface normal prediction. The framework jointly models polarization-based image descattering and SfP normal estimation in a unified pipeline, avoiding error accumulation from sequential processing and enabling global optimization across both tasks. UD-SfPNet further incorporates a novel color embedding module to enhance geometric consistency by exploiting the relationship between color encodings and surface orientation. A detail enhancement convolution module is also included to better preserve high-frequency geometric details that are lost under scattering. Experiments on the MuS-Polar3D dataset show that the proposed method significantly improves reconstruction accuracy, achieving a mean surface normal angular error of 15.12$^\circ$ (the lowest among compared methods). These results confirm the efficacy of combining descattering with polarization-based shape inference, and highlight the practical significance and potential applications of UD-SfPNet for optical 3D imaging in challenging underwater environments. The code is available at https://github.com/WangPuyun/UD-SfPNet.

AI.tldr: A unified UD-SfPNet jointly performs polarization-based descattering and shape-from-polarization for accurate underwater surface normals, with color embedding and detail enhancement, achieving 15.12° MA on MuS-Polar3D.
AI.motivation: Underwater imaging is severely degraded by scattering; polarization offers dual benefits of descattering and 3D inference (shape-from-polarization). A unified framework can reduce error accumulation and enable global optimization across tasks.
AI.method: Proposes UD-SfPNet that jointly models polarization-based descattering and SfP normal estimation in a single pipeline. Introduces a color embedding module to tie color encodings to surface orientation and a detail enhancement convolution module to preserve high-frequency geometry. Trained/evaluated on the MuS-Polar3D dataset; outputs include surface normals.
AI.result: The method achieves improved reconstruction accuracy, with a mean surface normal angular error of 15.12 degrees, the lowest among compared methods on MuS-Polar3D, demonstrating the benefit of jointly descattering with polarization-based shape inference. Code is available at the provided GitHub link.
AI.conclusion: Jointly performing polarization-based descattering and SfP yields more accurate underwater 3D geometry than sequential or separate processing, validating the practical significance of UD-SfPNet for challenging underwater optical imaging and enabling robust 3D reconstruction in scattering environments.

title: Tackling multiphysics problems via finite element-guided physics-informed operator learning
summary: This work presents a finite element-guided physics-informed operator learning framework for multiphysics problems with coupled partial differential equations (PDEs) on arbitrary domains. Implemented with Folax, a JAX-based operator-learning platform, the proposed framework learns a mapping from the input parameter space to the solution space with a weighted residual formulation based on the finite element method, enabling discretization-independent prediction beyond the training resolution without relying on labaled simulation data. The present framework for multiphysics problems is verified on nonlinear thermo-mechanical problems. Two- and three-dimensional representative volume elements with varying heterogeneous microstructures, and a close-to-reality industrial casting example under varying boundary conditions are investigated as the example problems. We investigate the potential of several neural operator backbones, including Fourier neural operators (FNOs), deep operator networks (DeepONets), and a newly proposed implicit finite operator learning (iFOL) approach based on conditional neural fields. The results demonstrate that FNOs yield highly accurate solution operators on regular domains, where the global topology can be efficiently learned in the spectral domain, and iFOL offers efficient parametric operator learning capabilities for complex and irregular geometries. Furthermore, studies on training strategies, network decomposition, and training sample quality reveal that a monolithic training strategy using a single network is sufficient for accurate predictions, while training sample quality strongly influences performance. Overall, the present approach highlights the potential of physics-informed operator learning with a finite element-based loss as a unified and scalable approach for coupled multiphysics simulations.
