AI.tldr: A finite element–guided physics-informed operator learning framework enables discretization-independent predictions for multiphysics PDEs on arbitrary domains, using FE-based loss with neural operator backbones (FNO, DeepONet, iFOL); verified on thermo-mechanical problems with heterogeneous microstructures and complex geometries; monolithic training with high data quality yields strong performance.
AI.motivation: Address the challenge of solving coupled multiphysics PDEs on irregular geometries with discretization-independent predictions and limited labeled data, by integrating finite element residuals into a neural operator learning framework.
AI.method: Propose an FE-guided physics-informed operator learning framework implemented in Folax (JAX). Learn mappings from input-parameter space to solution space via a weighted FE residual loss. Evaluate multiple neural operator backbones (FNO, DeepONet, iFOL) and compare training strategies; test on 2D/3D RVEs with heterogeneous microstructures and a casting example.
AI.result: FNOs excel on regular domains by leveraging spectral learning of global topology; iFOL efficiently handles complex/irregular geometries; monolithic single-network training suffices; training data quality strongly affects performance; FE-based loss provides a unified approach.
AI.conclusion: Physics-informed operator learning with FE-based loss is a scalable, discretization-independent framework for coupled multiphysics simulations, capable of handling irregular geometries and heterogeneous microstructures with appropriate backbone choices.

title: On the Exact Algorithmic Extraction of Finite Tesselations Through Prime Extraction of Minimal Representative Forms
summary: The identification of repeating patterns in discrete grids is rudimentary within symbolic reasoning, algorithm synthesis and structural optimization across diverse computational domains. Although statistical approaches targeting noisy data can approximately recognize patterns, symbolic analysis utilizing deterministic extraction of periodic structures is underdeveloped. This paper aims to fill this gap by employing a hierarchical algorithm that discovers exact tessellations in finite planar grids, addressing the problem where multiple independent patterns may coexist within a hierarchical structure. The proposed method utilizes composite discovery (dual inspection and breadth-first pruning) for identifying rectangular regions with internal repetition, normalization to a minimal representative form, and prime extraction (selective duplication and hierarchical memoization) to account for irregular dimensions and to achieve efficient computation time. We evaluate scalability on grid sizes from 2x2 to 32x32, showing overlap detection on simple repeating tiles exhibits processing time under 1ms, while complex patterns which require exhaustive search and systematic exploration shows exponential growth. This algorithm provides deterministic behavior for exact, axis-aligned, rectangular tessellations, addressing a critical gap in symbolic grid analysis techniques, applicable to puzzle solving reasoning tasks and identification of exact repeating structures in discrete symbolic domains.

AI.tldr: Deterministic hierarchical algorithm to detect exact axis-aligned rectangular tessellations in finite grids, handling coexisting patterns via composite discovery, normalization, and prime extraction; scalable for small to moderate grids; exponential growth for complex patterns.
AI.motivation: Address the gap where symbolic grid analysis lacks deterministic extraction of exact periodic structures, balancing exactness with computational efficiency in the presence of multiple overlapping patterns.
AI.method: Composite discovery: dual inspection and breadth-first pruning to identify rectangular regions with internal repetition; normalization to a minimal canonical form; prime extraction: selective duplication and hierarchical memoization to handle irregular dimensions and aid computation time.
AI.result: Empirical scalability demonstrated on grids from 2x2 to 32x32; simple overlapping tiles detected in under 1 ms; complex patterns require exhaustive search with exponential time; deterministic results for exact tessellations.
AI.conclusion: Fills a critical gap in symbolic grid analysis, enabling exact, axis-aligned tessellations; applicable to puzzle solving and detection of exact repeating structures in discrete symbolic domains.

title: VGGT-Det: Mining VGGT Internal Priors for Sensor-Geometry-Free Multi-View Indoor 3D Object Detection
summary: Current multi-view indoor 3D object detectors rely on sensor geometry that is costly to obtain (i.e., precisely calibrated multi-view camera poses) to fuse multi-view information into a global scene representation, limiting deployment in real-world scenes. We target a more practical setting: Sensor-Geometry-Free (SG-Free) multi-view indoor 3D object detection, where there are no sensor-provided geometric inputs (multi-view poses or depth). Recent Visual Geometry Grounded Transformer (VGGT) shows that strong 3D cues can be inferred directly from images. Building on this insight, we present VGGT-Det, the first framework tailored for SG-Free multi-view indoor 3D object detection. Rather than merely consuming VGGT predictions, our method integrates VGGT encoder into a transformer-based pipeline. To effectively leverage both the semantic and geometric priors from inside VGGT, we introduce two novel key components: (i) Attention-Guided Query Generation (AG): exploits VGGT attention maps as semantic priors to initialize object queries, improving localization by focusing on object regions while preserving global spatial structure; (ii) Query-Driven Feature Aggregation (QD): a learnable See-Query interacts with object queries to 'see' what they need, and then dynamically aggregates multi-level geometric features across VGGT layers that progressively lift 2D features into 3D. Experiments show that VGGT-Det significantly surpasses the best-performing method in the SG-Free setting by 4.4 and 8.6 mAP@0.25 on ScanNet and ARKitScenes, respectively. Ablation study shows that VGGT's internally learned semantic and geometric priors can be effectively leveraged by our AG and QD.

AI.tldr: SG-Free multi-view indoor 3D detection via VGGT-Det, integrating VGGT encoder with a transformer, using AG and QD to exploit semantic and geometric priors, achieving strong gains over prior SG-Free methods.
AI.motivation: Eliminate reliance on sensor geometry (poses/depth) for multi-view camera fusion; leverage VGGT's image-only 3D cues to enable practical indoor 3D detection.
AI.method: Incorporate VGGT encoder into a transformer-based detector; design two components: Attention-Guided Query Generation (AG) to initialize queries using VGGT attention maps; Query-Driven Feature Aggregation (QD) where a See-Query interacts with queries to select and aggregate multi-level geometric features across VGGT layers to lift 2D to 3D.
AI.result: Outperforms state-of-the-art SG-Free baseline by 4.4 and 8.6 mAP@0.25 on ScanNet and ARKitScenes; ablations show that AG and QD effectively leverage semantic and geometric priors from VGGT.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates effective fusion of VGGT priors within a SG-Free transformer detector for indoor 3D object detection; suggests that semantic and geometric priors can be embedded in query initialization and feature aggregation to enable 3D understanding from single-view cues.

title: Randomized Kiring Believer for Parallel Bayesian Optimization with Regret Bounds
summary: We consider an optimization problem of an expensive-to-evaluate black-box function, in which we can obtain noisy function values in parallel. For this problem, parallel Bayesian optimization (PBO) is a promising approach, which aims to optimize with fewer function evaluations by selecting a diverse input set for parallel evaluation. However, existing PBO methods suffer from poor practical performance or lack theoretical guarantees. In this study, we propose a PBO method, called randomized kriging believer (KB), based on a well-known KB heuristic and inheriting the advantages of the original KB: low computational complexity, a simple implementation, versatility across various BO methods, and applicability to asynchronous parallelization. Furthermore, we show that our randomized KB achieves Bayesian expected regret guarantees. We demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed method through experiments on synthetic and benchmark functions and emulators of real-world data.

AI.tldr: A novel parallel Bayesian optimization method, randomized Kriging Believer (KB), is proposed for expensive black-box functions with noisy, parallel evaluations. It builds on the Kriging Believer heuristic to enable low-complexity, simple implementation and asynchronous parallelism, while providing Bayesian expected regret guarantees. Empirical validation is shown on synthetic, benchmark, and real-world data emulators.
AI.motivation: Tackle expensive, black-box optimization with parallel, noisy evaluations. While parallel BO (PBO) is promising, many methods suffer from poor practical performance or lack theoretical guarantees. The work aims to deliver a practical, theoretically grounded PBO method.
AI.method: Introduce randomized KB, a KB-based heuristic that preserves low computational complexity and simplicity, while enabling applicability to various BO backends and asynchronous parallelization. The approach is analyzed to yield Bayesian expected regret guarantees.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on synthetic and benchmark functions and emulators of real-world data demonstrates the effectiveness of the proposed method.
AI.conclusion: Randomized KB provides a scalable, theoretically supported PBO approach with practical advantages, bridging robust performance with theoretical regret guarantees; it is versatile across BO methods and suitable for asynchronous parallelization.

title: Improving Text-to-Image Generation with Intrinsic Self-Confidence Rewards
comment: 19 pages, accepted to CVPR 2026. Project page https://wookiekim.github.io/ARC/
summary: Text-to-image generation powers content creation across design, media, and data augmentation. Post-training of text-to-image generative models is a promising path to better match human preferences, factuality, and improved aesthetics. We introduce ARC (Adaptive Rewarding by self-Confidence), a post-training framework that replaces external reward supervision with an internal self-confidence signal, obtained by evaluating how accurately the model recovers injected noise under self-denoising probes. ARC converts this intrinsic signal into scalar rewards, enabling fully unsupervised optimization without additional datasets, annotators, or reward models. Empirically, by reinforcing high-confidence generations, ARC delivers consistent gains in compositional generation, text rendering and text-image alignment over the baseline. We also find that integrating ARC with external rewards results in a complementary improvement, with alleviated reward hacking.

AI.tldr: ARC is an unsupervised post-training framework for text-to-image models that uses a self-denoising recovery accuracy signal as an intrinsic reward, enabling optimization without external datasets or reward models; it improves compositional generation, text rendering, and text-image alignment, and complements external rewards to mitigate reward hacking.
AI.motivation: Reduce reliance on labeled rewards or external evaluators while improving T2I alignment and image quality; leverage model-internal confidence signals.
AI.method: Inject noise into inputs and assess the model's ability to denoise via self-denoising probes; translate recovery accuracy into scalar rewards for optimization; can be combined with external reward signals.
AI.result: Empirical gains over baseline in compositional generation, text rendering, and text–image alignment; integrating ARC with external rewards yields complementary improvements and reduces reward hacking.
AI.conclusion: ARC provides a fully unsupervised post-training signal for T2I models and can synergize with external rewards to boost performance and robustness.

title: GAC: Stabilizing Asynchronous RL Training for LLMs via Gradient Alignment Control
summary: Asynchronous execution is essential for scaling reinforcement learning (RL) to modern large model workloads, including large language models and AI agents, but it can fundamentally alter RL optimization behavior. While prior work on asynchronous RL focuses on training throughput and distributional correction, we show that naively applying asynchrony to policy-gradient updates can induce qualitatively different training dynamics and lead to severe training instability. Through systematic empirical and theoretical analysis, we identify a key signature of this instability: asynchronous training exhibits persistently high cosine similarity between consecutive policy gradients, in contrast to the near-orthogonal updates observed under synchronized training. This stale-aligned gradient effect amplifies correlated updates and increases the risk of overshooting and divergence. Motivated by this observation, we propose GRADIENT ALIGNMENT CONTROL(GAC), a simple dynamics-aware stabilization method that regulates asynchronous RL progress along stale-aligned directions via gradient projection. We establish convergence guarantees under bounded staleness and demonstrate empirically that GAC recovers stable, on-policy training dynamics and matches synchronized baselines even at high staleness.

AI.tldr: Asynchronous policy-gradient RL can destabilize training due to stale-aligned gradients; Gradient Alignment Control (GAC) stabilizes via gradient projection and achieves convergence under bounded staleness, matching synchronized baselines at high latency.
AI.motivation: As RL workloads grow (e.g., large language models, AI agents), asynchronous execution improves throughput but can alter optimization dynamics. Identifying and mitigating the resulting instability is critical for reliable training.
AI.method: Empirical and theoretical analysis of asynchronous policy-gradient updates; quantify gradient similarity (cosine similarity) across steps to identify instability; propose GRADIENT ALIGNMENT CONTROL (GAC) which uses gradient projection to regulate progress along stale-aligned directions; provide convergence guarantees under bounded staleness.
AI.result: GAC stabilizes on-policy training under high staleness, restoring dynamics to resemble synchronized baselines; converges with formal guarantees when staleness is bounded; empirically, performance matches or approaches synchronized baselines.
AI.conclusion: Naive asynchronous RL can induce persistent gradient alignment and instability; a simple dynamics-aware stabilization via gradient projection (GAC) can restore stable training and offer convergence guarantees, enabling scalable asynchronous RL.

title: Training Dynamics of Softmax Self-Attention: Fast Global Convergence via Preconditioning
summary: We study the training dynamics of gradient descent in a softmax self-attention layer trained to perform linear regression and show that a simple first-order optimization algorithm can converge to the globally optimal self-attention parameters at a geometric rate. Our analysis proceeds in two steps. First, we show that in the infinite-data limit the regression problem solved by the self-attention layer is equivalent to a nonconvex matrix factorization problem. Second, we exploit this connection to design a novel "structure-aware" variant of gradient descent which efficiently optimizes the original finite-data regression objective. Our optimization algorithm features several innovations over standard gradient descent, including a preconditioner and regularizer which help avoid spurious stationary points, and a data-dependent spectral initialization of parameters which lie near the manifold of global minima with high probability.

AI.tldr: Shows that gradient descent can reach globally optimal softmax self-attention parameters for linear regression at a geometric rate; analyzes training dynamics via two steps: infinite-data limit yields a nonconvex matrix factorization, then introduces a structure-aware gradient descent with a preconditioner, regularizer, and data-dependent spectral initialization to achieve efficient optimization on finite data.
AI.motivation: Understand the optimization landscape and dynamics of training softmax self-attention layers on a simple regression task, and to design algorithms with provable convergence and practical efficiency.
AI.method: Step 1: In the infinite-data limit, map the regression problem solved by the self-attention layer to a nonconvex matrix factorization problem. Step 2: Develop a structure-aware variant of gradient descent that incorporates a preconditioner, regularizer, and data-dependent spectral initialization to robustly optimize the original finite-data regression objective, aiming to avoid spurious stationary points and converge to a global minimum.
AI.result: The proposed algorithm achieves geometric-rate convergence to globally optimal self-attention parameters. The analysis suggests the optimization landscape has favorable structure under the proposed scheme, with a data-dependent initialization placing parameters near the global minima manifold with high probability and a preconditioner/regularizer mitigating spurious stationary points.
AI.conclusion: Linking attention-based regression to nonconvex matrix factorization provides a principled path to design optimization methods with strong convergence guarantees. The structure-aware GD leverages data geometry to navigate the nonconvex landscape, achieving efficient training and global optimality under the stated assumptions.

title: Learning to Weigh Waste: A Physics-Informed Multimodal Fusion Framework and Large-Scale Dataset for Commercial and Industrial Applications
summary: Accurate weight estimation of commercial and industrial waste is important for efficient operations, yet image-based estimation remains difficult because similar-looking objects may have different densities, and the visible size changes with camera distance. Addressing this problem, we propose Multimodal Weight Predictor (MWP) framework that estimates waste weight by combining RGB images with physics-informed metadata, including object dimensions, camera distance, and camera height. We also introduce Waste-Weight-10K, a real-world dataset containing 10,421 synchronized image-metadata collected from logistics and recycling sites. The dataset covers 11 waste categories and a wide weight range from 3.5 to 3,450 kg. Our model uses a Vision Transformer for visual features and a dedicated metadata encoder for geometric and category information, combining them with Stacked Mutual Attention Fusion that allows visual and physical cues guide each other. This helps the model manage perspective effects and link objects to material properties. To ensure stable performance across the wide weight range, we train the model using Mean Squared Logarithmic Error. On the test set, the proposed method achieves 88.06 kg Mean Absolute Error (MAE), 6.39% Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE), and an R2 coefficient of 0.9548. The model shows strong accuracy for light objects in the 0-100 kg range with 2.38 kg MAE and 3.1% MAPE, maintaining reliable performance for heavy waste in the 1000-2000 kg range with 11.1% MAPE. Finally, we incorporate a physically grounded explanation module using Shapley Additive Explanations (SHAP) and a large language model to provide clear, human-readable explanations for each prediction.

AI.tldr: A multimodal framework (MWP) estimates waste weight using RGB images plus physics-informed metadata, with a ViT visual backbone and a metadata encoder, fused via stacked mutual attention; validated on Waste-Weight-10K with strong accuracy and explanations.
AI.motivation: Weight estimation of commercial/industrial waste from images is difficult due to similar visuals and perspective effects; a data-driven model limited by image cues alone necessitates incorporating geometric and camera information.
AI.method: A Vision Transformer processes RGB images; a dedicated metadata encoder handles object dimensions, camera distance, height, and category; stacked mutual attention fusion combines visual and metadata cues; trained with Mean Squared Logarithmic Error; SHAP-based explanations and a large language model provide human-readable reasons for predictions.
AI.result: On Waste-Weight-10K (11 classes, 3.5–3,450 kg, 10,421 samples): MAE 88.06 kg, MAPE 6.39%, R^2 0.9548. Light objects (0–100 kg): MAE 2.38 kg, MAPE 3.1%; heavy objects (1000–2000 kg): MAPE 11.1%. Dataset covers diverse real-world scenarios.
AI.conclusion: The approach effectively mitigates perspective and density ambiguities through physics-informed multimodal fusion and offers interpretable predictions via SHAP and LLM explanations; promising for scalable waste-weight estimation in industrial settings.

title: Scalable Multi-Task Low-Rank Model Adaptation
comment: Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2026. 21 pages, 4 figures, 11 tables. Code is available
summary: Scaling multi-task low-rank adaptation (LoRA) to a large number of tasks induces catastrophic performance degradation, such as an accuracy drop from 88.2% to 2.0% on DOTA when scaling from 5 to 15 tasks. This failure is due to parameter and representation misalignment. We find that existing solutions, like regularization and dynamic routing, fail at scale because they are constrained by a fundamental trade-off: strengthening regularization to reduce inter-task conflict inadvertently suppresses the essential feature discrimination required for effective routing. In this work, we identify two root causes for this trade-off. First, uniform regularization disrupts inter-task knowledge sharing: shared underlying knowledge concentrates in high-SV components (89% alignment on Flanv2->BBH). Uniform regularization forces high-SV components to update in orthogonal directions, directly disrupting the shared knowledge. Second, Conflict Amplification: Applying LoRA at the component-level (e.g., W_q, W_v) amplifies gradient conflicts; we show block-level adaptation reduces this conflict by 76% with only 50% parameters. Based on these insights, we propose mtLoRA, a scalable solution with three novel designs: 1) Spectral-Aware Regularization to selectively orthogonalize low-SV components while preserving high-SV shared knowledge, 2) Block-Level Adaptation to mitigate conflict amplification and largely improve parameter efficiency, and 3) Fine-Grained Routing using dimension-specific weights for superior expressive power. On four large-scale (15-25 tasks) vision (DOTA and iNat2018) and NLP (Dolly-15k and BBH) benchmarks, mtLoRA achieves 91.7%, 81.5%, 44.5% and 38.5% accuracy on DOTA, iNat2018, Dolly-15k and BBH respectively, outperforming the state-of-the-art by 2.3% on average while using 47% fewer parameters and 24% less training time.

AI.tldr: Scaling multi-task LoRA causes catastrophic degradation; mtLoRA mitigates via spectral-aware regularization, block-level adaptation, and fine-grained routing, achieving strong accuracy with fewer parameters and less training time.
AI.motivation: Address performance collapse when scaling LoRA to many tasks; identify root causes: uniform regularization suppresses high-SV shared knowledge; conflict amplification when applying LoRA component-wise.
AI.method: Propose mtLoRA with three components: spectral-aware regularization (selectively orthogonalizes low-SV components while preserving high-SV knowledge), block-level adaptation (mitigates gradient conflicts with fewer parameters), fine-grained routing (dimension-specific weights). Evaluate on four large-scale benchmarks across vision and NLP.
AI.result: On DOTA (15-25 tasks) 91.7%; iNat2018 81.5%; Dolly-15k 44.5%; BBH 38.5%; outperforms SOTA by 2.3% average; uses 47% fewer parameters and 24% less training time.
AI.conclusion: mtLoRA provides scalable, efficient multi-task adaptation by solving the regularization-routing trade-off and reducing gradient conflicts; demonstrates cross-domain efficacy.

title: Seeing Beyond 8bits: Subjective and Objective Quality Assessment of HDR-UGC Videos
summary: High Dynamic Range (HDR) user-generated (UGC) videos are rapidly proliferating across social platforms, yet most perceptual video quality assessment (VQA) systems remain tailored to Standard Dynamic Range (SDR). HDR has a higher bit depth, wide color gamut, and elevated luminance range, exposing distortions such as near-black crushing, highlight clipping, banding, and exposure flicker that amplify UGC artifacts and challenge SDR models. To catalyze progress, we curate Beyond8Bits, a large-scale subjective dataset of 44K videos from 6.5K sources with over 1.5M crowd ratings, spanning diverse scenes, capture conditions, and compression settings. We further introduce HDR-Q, the first Multimodal Large Language Model (MLLM) for HDR-UGC VQA. We propose (i) a novel HDR-aware vision encoder to produce HDR-sensitive embeddings, and (ii) HDR-Aware Policy Optimization (HAPO), an RL finetuning framework that anchors reasoning to HDR cues. HAPO augments GRPO via an HDR-SDR contrastive KL that encourages token reliance on HDR inputs and a Gaussian weighted regression reward for fine-grained MOS calibration. Across Beyond8Bits and public HDR-VQA benchmarks, HDR-Q delivers state-of-the-art performance.

AI.tldr: HDR-Q, a Multimodal Large Language Model for HDR-UGC VQA, achieves state-of-the-art on HDR content by combining an HDR-aware vision encoder with HDR-Aware Policy Optimization (HAPO) that aligns reasoning to HDR cues via HDR-SDR contrastive KL and Gaussian-regression rewards, and performing well on Beyond8Bits and public HDR-VQA benchmarks.
AI.motivation: HDR user-generated videos have higher bit depth, wider gamut, and higher luminance, creating distortions (e.g., near-black crushing, highlight clipping, banding, exposure flicker) that existing SDR-focused VQA models struggle to assess accurately.
AI.method: Introduce an HDR-aware vision encoder to produce HDR-sensitive embeddings. Develop HDR-Aware Policy Optimization (HAPO), an RL finetuning framework that augments an existing model (GRPO) with an HDR-SDR contrastive KL loss to bias token reliance toward HDR inputs and a Gaussian-weighted regression reward for fine-grained MOS calibration.
AI.result: HDR-Q delivers state-of-the-art performance across the Beyond8Bits dataset and public HDR-VQA benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: HDR-aware multimodal VQA with RL fine-tuning improves reliance on HDR cues and MOS calibration, establishing a strong baseline for HDR-UGC VQA and suggesting further exploration of HDR-specific objectives and datasets.

title: LFPO: Likelihood-Free Policy Optimization for Masked Diffusion Models
summary: Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) has achieved remarkable success in improving autoregressive models, especially in domains requiring correctness like mathematical reasoning and code generation. However, directly applying such paradigms to Diffusion Large Language Models (dLLMs) is fundamentally hindered by the intractability of exact likelihood computation, which forces existing methods to rely on high-variance approximations. To bridge this gap, we propose Likelihood-Free Policy Optimization (LFPO), a native framework that maps the concept of vector field flow matching to the discrete token space. Specifically, LFPO formulates alignment as geometric velocity rectification, which directly optimizes denoising logits via contrastive updates. This design effectively bypasses the errors inherent in likelihood approximation, yielding the precise gradient estimation. Furthermore, LFPO enforce consistency by predicting final solutions from intermediate steps, effectively straightening the probability flow to enable high-quality generation with significantly fewer iterations. Extensive experiments demonstrate that LFPO not only outperforms state-of-the-art baselines on code and reasoning benchmarks but also accelerates inference by approximately 20% through reduced diffusion steps.

AI.tldr: Introduces Likelihood-Free Policy Optimization (LFPO) for diffusion LLMs to avoid likelihood computation, using vector-field flow matching in discrete token space; achieves precise gradient estimation and accelerates inference.
AI.motivation: Address intractable exact likelihood in diffusion-based LLMs and the high variance of likelihood-approximation methods; enable stable, sample-efficient training for discrete token generation.
AI.method: Reformulates alignment as geometric velocity rectification in token space; directly optimizes denoising logits via contrastive updates; enforces consistency by predicting final solutions from intermediate steps to straighten probability flow; reduces diffusion steps to speed up generation.
AI.result: LFPO outperforms state-of-the-art baselines on code and reasoning benchmarks and reduces inference steps by about 20%, achieving faster generation with high-quality outputs.
AI.conclusion: LFPO provides a principled, likelihood-free optimization framework for dLLMs, delivering accurate gradient estimation and faster inference, with potential applicability to other discrete diffusion models.

title: Mobile-VTON: High-Fidelity On-Device Virtual Try-On
comment: The project page is available at: https://zhenchenwan.github.io/Mobile-VTON/
summary: Virtual try-on (VTON) has recently achieved impressive visual fidelity, but most existing systems require uploading personal photos to cloud-based GPUs, raising privacy concerns and limiting on-device deployment. To address this, we present Mobile-VTON, a high-quality, privacy-preserving framework that enables fully offline virtual try-on on commodity mobile devices using only a single user image and a garment image. Mobile-VTON introduces a modular TeacherNet-GarmentNet-TryonNet (TGT) architecture that integrates knowledge distillation, garment-conditioned generation, and garment alignment into a unified pipeline optimized for on-device efficiency. Within this framework, we propose a Feature-Guided Adversarial (FGA) Distillation strategy that combines teacher supervision with adversarial learning to better match real-world image distributions. GarmentNet is trained with a trajectory-consistency loss to preserve garment semantics across diffusion steps, while TryonNet uses latent concatenation and lightweight cross-modal conditioning to enable robust garment-to-person alignment without large-scale pretraining. By combining these components, Mobile-VTON achieves high-fidelity generation with low computational overhead. Experiments on VITON-HD and DressCode at 1024 x 768 show that it matches or outperforms strong server-based baselines while running entirely offline. These results demonstrate that high-quality VTON is not only feasible but also practical on-device, offering a secure solution for real-world applications.

AI.tldr: Mobile-VTON enables fully offline, privacy-preserving virtual try-on on mobile devices using a modular TGT architecture (TeacherNet-GarmentNet-TryonNet) with Feature-Guided Adversarial Distillation, garment-conditioned generation, and clever garment alignment. It achieves server-quality VTON on VITON-HD and DressCode at 1024x768 with low compute.
AI.motivation: To enable high-fidelity virtual try-on without uploading user photos to cloud GPUs, addressing privacy concerns and enabling on-device deployment on commodity mobile devices.
AI.method: Proposes a modular TGT framework: TeacherNet, GarmentNet, and TryonNet integrated with knowledge distillation, garment-conditioned generation, and garment alignment. Introduces Feature-Guided Adversarial (FGA) Distillation to combine teacher supervision with adversarial learning. GarmentNet uses trajectory-consistency loss to preserve garment semantics across diffusion steps; TryonNet uses latent concatenation and lightweight cross-modal conditioning for robust garment-to-person alignment without large-scale pretraining.
AI.result: Mobile-VTON achieves high-fidelity VTON with low computational overhead and matches/outscores strong server-based baselines while operating entirely offline, demonstrated on VITON-HD and DressCode at 1024×768.
AI.conclusion: High-quality VTON is feasible and practical on-device with strong privacy guarantees, enabling real-world offline applications.

title: Rate-Distortion Signatures of Generalization and Information Trade-offs
summary: Generalization to novel visual conditions remains a central challenge for both human and machine vision, yet standard robustness metrics offer limited insight into how systems trade accuracy for robustness. We introduce a rate-distortion-theoretic framework that treats stimulus-response behavior as an effective communication channel, derives rate-distortion (RD) frontiers from confusion matrices, and summarizes each system with two interpretable geometric signatures - slope ($β$) and curvature ($κ$) - which capture the marginal cost and abruptness of accuracy-robustness trade-offs. Applying this framework to human psychophysics and 18 deep vision models under controlled image perturbations, we compare generalization geometry across model architectures and training regimes. We find that both biological and artificial systems follow a common lossy-compression principle but occupy systematically different regions of RD space. In particular, humans exhibit smoother, more flexible trade-offs, whereas modern deep networks operate in steeper and more brittle regimes even at matched accuracy. Across training regimes, robustness training induces systematic but dissociable shifts in beta/kappa, revealing cases where improved robustness or accuracy does not translate into more human-like generalization geometry. These results demonstrate that RD geometry provides a compact, model-agnostic lens for comparing generalization behavior across systems beyond standard accuracy-based metrics.

AI.tldr: A rate-distortion (RD) framework maps stimulus-response as a communication channel, deriving RD frontiers from confusion matrices to summarize generalization with two geometry metrics, beta (slope) and kappa (curvature).
AI.motivation: Standard robustness metrics miss the trade-off structure between accuracy and robustness across conditions; a geometry of generalization can reveal fundamental differences between humans and deep networks.
AI.method: Compute RD frontiers from confusion matrices under controlled perturbations; extract beta and kappa as signatures; compare humans and 18 deep vision models; analyze diverse training regimes.
AI.result: Humans and models obey a lossy-compression principle but occupy different RD regions; humans show smoother, flexible trade-offs; modern networks show steeper, more brittle regimes even at similar accuracy; robustness training shifts beta/kappa in systematic but dissociable ways; improved robustness or accuracy does not always align with human-like RD geometry.
AI.conclusion: RD geometry provides a compact, model-agnostic lens to compare generalization beyond accuracy; it can reveal qualitative differences in how systems handle perturbations, guiding robustness research.

title: StegoNGP: 3D Cryptographic Steganography using Instant-NGP
summary: Recently, Instant Neural Graphics Primitives (Instant-NGP) has achieved significant success in rapid 3D scene reconstruction, but securely embedding high-capacity hidden data, such as an entire 3D scene, remains a challenge. Existing methods rely on external decoders, require architectural modifications, and suffer from limited capacity, which makes them easily detectable. We propose a novel parameter-free 3D Cryptographic Steganography using Instant-NGP (StegoNGP), which leverages the Instant-NGP hash encoding function as a key-controlled scene switcher. By associating a default key with a cover scene and a secret key with a hidden scene, our method trains a single model to interweave both representations within the same network weights. The resulting model is indistinguishable from a standard Instant-NGP in architecture and parameter count. We also introduce an enhanced Multi-Key scheme, which assigns multiple independent keys across hash levels, dramatically expanding the key space and providing high robustness against partial key disclosure attacks. Experimental results demonstrated that StegoNGP can hide a complete high-quality 3D scene with strong imperceptibility and security, providing a new paradigm for high-capacity, undetectable information hiding in neural fields. The code can be found at https://github.com/jiang-wenxiang/StegoNGP.

AI.tldr: A parameter-free steganography method StegoNGP embeds a hidden 3D scene within a single Instant-NGP model by using hash encoding as a key-controlled scene switcher; introduces a Multi-Key scheme to expand the key space; claims high imperceptibility and security.
AI.motivation: Overcome low-capacity and detectable steganography in neural fields; enable high-capacity hidden data embedding without external decoders or architectural changes.
AI.method: Train one Instant-NGP model that interweaves a cover scene and a hidden scene in the same network weights by associating a default key with the cover and a secret key with the hidden scene; uses the Instant-NGP hash encoding as a switch controlled by keys; introduces a Multi-Key scheme distributing independent keys across hash levels to enlarge the key space and resist partial key disclosure.
AI.result: Demonstrates that StegoNGP can hide a complete high-quality 3D scene within a standard Instant-NGP model with strong imperceptibility and security; enhanced robustness against partial key disclosure thanks to the Multi-Key scheme; code available online.
AI.conclusion: Presents a new paradigm for high-capacity, undetectable information hiding in neural fields, achieving a parameter-free integration that preserves architectural parity with standard Instant-NGP and expands key-based security for neural implicit representations.

title: Jump Like A Squirrel: Optimized Execution Step Order for Anytime Random Forest Inference
summary:
Due to their efficiency and small size, decision trees and random forests are popular machine learning models used for classification on resource-constrained systems. In such systems, the available execution time for inference in a random forest might not be sufficient for a complete model execution. Ideally, the already gained prediction confidence should be retained. An anytime algorithm is designed to be able to be aborted anytime, while giving a result with an increasing quality over time. Previous approaches have realized random forests as anytime algorithms on the granularity of trees, stopping after some but not all trees of a forest have been executed. However, due to the way decision trees subdivide the sample space in every step, an increase in prediction quality is achieved with every additional step in one tree. In this paper, we realize decision trees and random forest as anytime algorithms on the granularity of single steps in trees. This approach opens a design space to define the step order in a forest, which has the potential to optimize the mean accuracy. We propose the Optimal Order, which finds a step order with a maximal mean accuracy in exponential runtime and the polynomial runtime heuristics Forward Squirrel Order and Backward Squirrel Order, which greedily maximize the accuracy for each additional step taken down and up the trees, respectively.
  Our evaluation shows, that the Backward Squirrel Order performs $\sim94\%$ as well as the Optimal Order and $\sim99\%$ as well as all other step orders.

AI.tldr: Proposes per-step (inside-tree) anytime inference for decision trees and random forests, introducing Optimal Order (exponential) and greedy heuristics Forward/Backward Squirrel Order. Backward Squirrel Order achieves about 94% of Optimal and 99% of all step orders in mean accuracy, enabling near-optimal incremental predictions under time constraints.
AI.motivation: Enable accurate anytime inference on resource-constrained systems where partial forest evaluation should yield the best possible accuracy given a fixed budget.
AI.method: Model decision trees and random forests as step-level anytime algorithms, define step orderings within the tree structure, and introduce three ordering strategies: Optimal Order (exponential runtime), Forward Squirrel Order (greedy, down-the-tree steps), and Backward Squirrel Order (greedy, up-the-tree steps).
AI.result: The Backward Squirrel Order achieves ~94% of the Optimal Order's mean accuracy and ~99% of all step-order performances, indicating the greedy heuristic is a strong practical substitute for the exponential-optimal ordering.
AI.conclusion: Step-ordering at the intra-tree level expands the design space for anytime ML in constrained environments; the proposed Backward Squirrel Order offers near-optimal performance with polynomial runtime, suggesting practical applicability and room for further optimization.

title: Decoupling Motion and Geometry in 4D Gaussian Splatting
summary: High-fidelity reconstruction of dynamic scenes is an important yet challenging problem. While recent 4D Gaussian Splatting (4DGS) has demonstrated the ability to model temporal dynamics, it couples Gaussian motion and geometric attributes within a single covariance formulation, which limits its expressiveness for complex motions and often leads to visual artifacts. To address this, we propose VeGaS, a novel velocity-based 4D Gaussian Splatting framework that decouples Gaussian motion and geometry. Specifically, we introduce a Galilean shearing matrix that explicitly incorporates time-varying velocity to flexibly model complex non-linear motions, while strictly isolating the effects of Gaussian motion from the geometry-related conditional Gaussian covariance. Furthermore, a Geometric Deformation Network is introduced to refine Gaussian shapes and orientations using spatio-temporal context and velocity cues, enhancing temporal geometric modeling. Extensive experiments on public datasets demonstrate that VeGaS achieves state-of-the-art performance.

AI.tldr: Proposes VeGaS: decoupling motion and geometry in 4D Gaussian Splatting via velocity-based modeling and a Geometric Deformation Network, achieving state-of-the-art results.
AI.motivation: Address the expressiveness limitations of 4DGS where a single covariance conflates motion and geometry, causing artifacts in dynamic scenes with complex non-linear motion.
AI.method: Introduce Galilean shearing matrix to encode time-varying velocity into motion covariance; enforce separation between motion and geometry via conditional Gaussian covariance; add Geometric Deformation Network to refine Gaussian shapes/orientations using spatio-temporal context and velocity cues.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on public datasets show VeGaS achieves state-of-the-art performance, with improved handling of complex motions and reduced artifacts.
AI.conclusion: Decoupled, velocity-aware 4D Gaussian Splatting with geometric refinement yields superior dynamic scene reconstruction and modeling of non-linear motions; potential broad applicability to dynamic radiance fields.

title: SafeSci: Safety Evaluation of Large Language Models in Science Domains and Beyond
summary: The success of large language models (LLMs) in scientific domains has heightened safety concerns, prompting numerous benchmarks to evaluate their scientific safety. Existing benchmarks often suffer from limited risk coverage and a reliance on subjective evaluation. To address these problems, we introduce SafeSci, a comprehensive framework for safety evaluation and enhancement in scientific contexts. SafeSci comprises SafeSciBench, a multi-disciplinary benchmark with 0.25M samples, and SafeSciTrain, a large-scale dataset containing 1.5M samples for safety enhancement. SafeSciBench distinguishes between safety knowledge and risk to cover extensive scopes and employs objective metrics such as deterministically answerable questions to mitigate evaluation bias. We evaluate 24 advanced LLMs, revealing critical vulnerabilities in current models. We also observe that LLMs exhibit varying degrees of excessive refusal behaviors on safety-related issues. For safety enhancement, we demonstrate that fine-tuning on SafeSciTrain significantly enhances the safety alignment of models. Finally, we argue that knowledge is a double-edged sword, and determining the safety of a scientific question should depend on specific context, rather than universally categorizing it as safe or unsafe. Our work provides both a diagnostic tool and a practical resource for building safer scientific AI systems.

AI.tldr: SafeSci: a comprehensive safety evaluation and enhancement framework for scientific LLMs, with SafeSciBench (0.25M samples) and SafeSciTrain (1.5M samples); shows safety vulnerabilities across 24 LLMs, variable refusal behavior, and that fine-tuning improves safety alignment; argues safety is context-dependent and not universally safe/unsafe.
AI.motivation: Addresses gaps in existing scientific safety benchmarks: limited risk coverage, and reliance on subjective evaluation. Seeks broad, objective risk knowledge and actionable improvement data for safer scientific AI systems.
AI.method: SafeSciBench: a multi-disciplinary benchmark with 0.25M samples that separates safety knowledge from risk, using objective metrics like deterministically answerable questions to reduce evaluation bias. SafeSciTrain: a 1.5M-sample dataset for safety enhancement. Evaluation of 24 advanced LLMs to identify vulnerabilities and excessive refusal behaviors. Safety enhancement through fine-tuning on SafeSciTrain.
AI.result: Reveals critical vulnerabilities in current models and varying degrees of excessive refusal behavior on safety issues. Fine-tuning on SafeSciTrain significantly improves safety alignment of models.
AI.conclusion: Knowledge is a double-edged sword; safety of a scientific question should depend on context rather than universal labeling. SafeSci provides a diagnostic tool and practical resource for building safer scientific AI systems.

title: PreciseCache: Precise Feature Caching for Efficient and High-fidelity Video Generation
summary: High computational costs and slow inference hinder the practical application of video generation models. While prior works accelerate the generation process through feature caching, they often suffer from notable quality degradation. In this work, we reveal that this issue arises from their inability to distinguish truly redundant features, which leads to the unintended skipping of computations on important features. To address this, we propose \textbf{PreciseCache}, a plug-and-play framework that precisely detects and skips truly redundant computations, thereby accelerating inference without sacrificing quality. Specifically, PreciseCache contains two components: LFCache for step-wise caching and BlockCache for block-wise caching. For LFCache, we compute the Low-Frequency Difference (LFD) between the prediction features of the current step and those from the previous cached step. Empirically, we observe that LFD serves as an effective measure of step-wise redundancy, accurately detecting highly redundant steps whose computation can be skipped through reusing cached features. To further accelerate generation within each non-skipped step, we propose BlockCache, which precisely detects and skips redundant computations at the block level within the network. Extensive experiments on various backbones demonstrate the effectiveness of our PreciseCache, such as achieving an average of $2.6\times$ speedup on Wan2.1-14B without noticeable quality loss.

AI.tldr: PreciseCache accelerates video generation by precisely identifying redundancy: LFCache uses Low-Frequency Difference to skip whole steps, while BlockCache detects and skips redundant computations at the block level within non-skipped steps, achieving around 2.6x speedups on Wan2.1-14B with negligible quality loss.
AI.motivation: Video generation models incur high computational costs and slow inference. Prior feature caching methods often degrade quality due to misclassification of redundant features, failing to preserve important computations.
AI.method: A two-tier, plug-and-play caching framework: LFCache performs step-wise caching by measuring Low-Frequency Difference (LFD) between current step predictions and the previous cached step to decide if a step is redundant; BlockCache further accelerates within each non-skipped step by detecting and skipping redundant computations at the block level inside the network.
AI.result: Extensive experiments across various backbones demonstrate the approach’s effectiveness, including an average 2.6× speedup on Wan2.1-14B without noticeable quality loss.
AI.conclusion: PreciseCache provides accurate redundancy detection enabling fast, quality-preserving video generation. The LFCache-BlockCache combination offers a practical, reusable caching framework for speeding up video generation models.

title: FAST-DIPS: Adjoint-Free Analytic Steps and Hard-Constrained Likelihood Correction for Diffusion-Prior Inverse Problems
summary: Training-free diffusion priors enable inverse-problem solvers without retraining, but for nonlinear forward operators data consistency often relies on repeated derivatives or inner optimization/MCMC loops with conservative step sizes, incurring many iterations and denoiser/score evaluations. We propose a training-free solver that replaces these inner loops with a hard measurement-space feasibility constraint (closed-form projection) and an analytic, model-optimal step size, enabling a small, fixed compute budget per noise level. Anchored at the denoiser prediction, the correction is approximated via an adjoint-free, ADMM-style splitting with projection and a few steepest-descent updates, using one VJP and either one JVP or a forward-difference probe, followed by backtracking and decoupled re-annealing. We prove local model optimality and descent under backtracking for the step-size rule, and derive an explicit KL bound for mode-substitution re-annealing under a local Gaussian conditional surrogate. We also develop a latent variant and a one-parameter pixel$\rightarrow$latent hybrid schedule. Experiments achieve competitive PSNR/SSIM/LPIPS with up to 19.5$\times$ speedup, without hand-coded adjoints or inner MCMC.

AI.tldr: Training-free diffusion priors for inverse problems with nonlinear forward operators using a closed-form projection and ADMM-style splitting to avoid inner optimization, enabling fast, competitive results.
AI.motivation: Eliminate costly inner loops and derivatives in training-free diffusion-prior solvers when enforcing data consistency for nonlinear forward operators; reduce compute while preserving accuracy.
AI.method: Hard measurement-space feasibility projection; analytic, model-optimal step size; adjoint-free ADMM-style splitting with projection and few steepest-descent updates; one VJP and one JVP or finite-difference probe; backtracking with decoupled re-annealing; latent variant and pixel-to-latent hybrid schedule.
AI.result: Competitive PSNR/SSIM/LPIPS with up to 19.5x speedup; no hand-coded adjoints or inner MCMC.
AI.conclusion: Claims local model optimality and descent guarantees under backtracking; KL bound for mode-substitution re-annealing under a local Gaussian surrogate; applicable to latent variant and hybrid schedule; demonstrates efficiency and effectiveness of a training-free, fast solver for nonlinear inverse problems.

title: EraseAnything++: Enabling Concept Erasure in Rectified Flow Transformers Leveraging Multi-Object Optimization
summary: Removing undesired concepts from large-scale text-to-image (T2I) and text-to-video (T2V) diffusion models while preserving overall generative quality remains a major challenge, particularly as modern models such as Stable Diffusion v3, Flux, and OpenSora employ flow-matching and transformer-based architectures and extend to long-horizon video generation. Existing concept erasure methods, designed for earlier T2I/T2V models, often fail to generalize to these paradigms. To address this issue, we propose EraseAnything++, a unified framework for concept erasure in both image and video diffusion models with flow-matching objectives. Central to our approach is formulating concept erasure as a constrained multi-objective optimization problem that explicitly balances concept removal with preservation of generative utility. To solve the resulting conflicting objectives, we introduce an efficient utility-preserving unlearning strategy based on implicit gradient surgery. Furthermore, by integrating LoRA-based parameter tuning with attention-level regularization, our method anchors erasure on key visual representations and propagates it consistently across spatial and temporal dimensions. In the video setting, we further enhance consistency through an anchor-and-propagate mechanism that initializes erasure on reference frames and enforces it throughout subsequent transformer layers, thereby mitigating temporal drift. Extensive experiments on both image and video benchmarks demonstrate that EraseAnything++ substantially outperforms prior methods in erasure effectiveness, generative fidelity, and temporal consistency, establishing a new state of the art for concept erasure in next-generation diffusion models.

AI.tldr: Unified framework EraseAnything++ for concept erasure in image/video diffusion models using flow-matching; casts erasure as constrained multi-objective optimization with implicit gradient surgery, LoRA tuning, and anchor-propagate for temporal consistency.
AI.motivation: Address the poor generalization of prior concept erasure methods to modern flow-based and transformer-based diffusion models, including long-horizon video generation, while preserving generative quality.
AI.method: Formulates erasure as a constrained multi-objective optimization balancing removal of undesired concepts with utility preservation. Introduces implicit gradient surgery for efficient trade-off, uses LoRA-based parameter tuning with attention-level regularization to anchor erasure in key representations, and applies an anchor-and-propagate mechanism in video to maintain temporal consistency across transformer layers.
AI.result: Empirical results indicate EraseAnything++ outperforms previous methods in erasure effectiveness, image/video generative fidelity, and temporal consistency, establishing new state-of-the-art for concept erasure in next-gen diffusion models.
AI.conclusion: A unified, scalable, and effective framework for concept erasure across image and video diffusion models, leveraging flow-matching, LoRA, and anchor-based propagation to achieve robust erasure without sacrificing fidelity or temporal coherence.

title: Boosting Entropy with Bell Box Quantization
comment: Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2026
summary: Quantization-Aware Pre-Training (QAPT) is an effective technique to reduce the compute and memory overhead of Deep Neural Networks while improving their energy efficiency on edge devices. Existing QAPT methods produce models stored in compute-efficient data types (e.g. integers) that are not information theoretically optimal (ITO). On the other hand, existing ITO data types (e.g. Quantile/NormalFloat Quantization) are not compute-efficient. We propose BBQ, the first ITO quantization method that is also compute-efficient. BBQ builds on our key insight that since learning is domain-agnostic, the output of a quantizer does not need to reside in the same domain as its input. BBQ performs ITO quantization in its input domain, and returns its output in a compute-efficient domain where ITO data types are mapped to compute-efficient data types. Without sacrificing compute efficiency, BBQ outperforms prior SOTA QAPT methods by a perplexity reduction of up to 2 points for 4-bit models, up to 4 points for 3-bit models, up to 5 points for 2-bit models, and up to 18 points for 1-bit models. Code is available at https://github.com/1733116199/bbq.

AI.tldr: BBQ introduces an information-theoretically optimal (ITO) quantization that remains compute-efficient by quantizing in the input domain and mapping outputs to compute-efficient data types, outperforming prior QAPT methods across bit-widths, especially at very low bit-widths.
AI.motivation: To reduce compute and memory overhead of DNNs on edge devices while preserving information-theoretic optimality; existing QAPT uses compute-efficient data types that are not ITO, while ITO data types are not compute-efficient, creating a gap that BBQ aims to bridge.
AI.method: Perform ITO quantization in the input domain and map the output to a compute-efficient domain where ITO types are represented by compute-friendly data types, thereby preserving ITO advantages while maintaining compute efficiency.
AI.result: BBQ yields substantial perplexity improvements over state-of-the-art QAPT: up to 2 points at 4-bit, up to 4 points at 3-bit, up to 5 points at 2-bit, and up to 18 points at 1-bit models; code available at the provided GitHub repository.
AI.conclusion: BBQ is the first method to achieve both information-theoretically optimal quantization and compute efficiency, addressing a key limitation in QAPT and enabling better low-bit quantized models for edge deployments.

title: Towards OOD Generalization in Dynamic Graphs via Causal Invariant Learning
summary: Although dynamic graph neural networks (DyGNNs) have demonstrated promising capabilities, most existing methods ignore out-of-distribution (OOD) shifts that commonly exist in dynamic graphs. Dynamic graph OOD generalization is non-trivial due to the following challenges: 1) Identifying invariant and variant patterns amid complex graph evolution, 2) Capturing the intrinsic evolution rationale from these patterns, and 3) Ensuring model generalization across diverse OOD shifts despite limited data distribution observations. Although several attempts have been made to tackle these challenges, none has successfully addressed all three simultaneously, and they face various limitations in complex OOD scenarios. To solve these issues, we propose a Dynamic graph Causal Invariant Learning (DyCIL) model for OOD generalization via exploiting invariant spatio-temporal patterns from a causal view. Specifically, we first develop a dynamic causal subgraph generator to identify causal dynamic subgraphs explicitly. Next, we design a causal-aware spatio-temporal attention module to extract the intrinsic evolution rationale behind invariant patterns. Finally, we further introduce an adaptive environment generator to capture the underlying dynamics of distributional shifts. Extensive experiments on both real-world and synthetic dynamic graph datasets demonstrate the superiority of our model over state-of-the-art baselines in handling OOD shifts.

AI.tldr: DyCIL: a dynamic graph causal invariant learning framework for OOD generalization in dynamic graphs. It identifies invariant causal subgraphs, uses a causal-aware spatio-temporal attention to extract evolution rationale, and employs an adaptive environment generator to model distribution shifts. Demonstrates superior OOD performance on real/synthetic datasets.
AI.motivation: Dynamic graphs and DyGNNs struggle with out-of-distribution shifts. There is a need to identify invariant vs. variant patterns, understand the evolution rationale, and generalize across diverse OOD shifts with limited data. Existing methods do not address all three simultaneously.
AI.method: 1) Dynamic causal subgraph generator to explicitly identify causal dynamic subgraphs. 2) Causal-aware spatio-temporal attention to capture intrinsic evolution rationale behind invariant patterns. 3) Adaptive environment generator to model the dynamics of distributional shifts.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on real-world and synthetic dynamic graph datasets show DyCIL outperforms state-of-the-art baselines in handling OOD shifts.
AI.conclusion: DyCIL effectively enables OOD generalization for dynamic graphs by leveraging invariant causal patterns through subgraph discovery, causally-aware attention, and adaptive environment modeling, leading to improved robustness across diverse shifts.

title: Event-Anchored Frame Selection for Effective Long-Video Understanding
summary: Massive frame redundancy and limited context window make efficient frame selection crucial for long-video understanding with large vision-language models (LVLMs). Prevailing approaches, however, adopt a flat sampling paradigm which treats the video as an unstructured collection of frames. In this paper, we introduce Event-Anchored Frame Selection (EFS), a hierarchical, event-aware pipeline. Leveraging self-supervised DINO embeddings, EFS first partitions the video stream into visually homogeneous temporal segments, which serve as proxies for semantic events. Within each event, it then selects the most query-relevant frame as an anchor. These anchors act as structural priors that guide a global refinement stage using an adaptive Maximal Marginal Relevance (MMR) scheme. This pipeline ensures the final keyframe set jointly optimizes for event coverage, query relevance, and visual diversity. As a training-free, plug-and-play module, EFS can be seamlessly integrated into off-the-shelf LVLMs, yielding substantial gains on challenging video understanding benchmarks. Specifically, when applied to LLaVA-Video-7B, EFS improves accuracy by 4.7%, 4.9%, and 8.8% on VideoMME, LongVideoBench, and MLVU, respectively.

AI.tldr: Event-Anchored Frame Selection (EFS) introduces a hierarchical, event-aware frame sampling pipeline for long videos, using self-supervised DINO embeddings to segment visually homogeneous events, selecting one query-relevant anchor frame per event, and refining globally with adaptive Maximal Marginal Relevance (MMR). It is training-free and plug-and-play for LVLMs, achieving notable accuracy gains on VideoMME, LongVideoBench, and MLVU when integrated with LLaVA-Video-7B.
AI.motivation: Long videos have massive frame redundancy and limited context windows in LVLMs. Flat sampling treats videos as unordered frames, leading to suboptimal event coverage and query relevance.
AI.method: 1) Use self-supervised DINO embeddings to partition the video into visually homogeneous temporal segments (events). 2) Within each event, select the most query-relevant frame as an anchor. 3) Use anchors as priors to guide a global refinement stage with an adaptive MMR scheme, jointly optimizing event coverage, query relevance, and visual diversity. 4) Training-free, plug-and-play with existing LVLMs.
AI.result: Integration with LLaVA-Video-7B yields accuracy gains on challenging benchmarks: VideoMME +4.7%, LongVideoBench +4.9%, MLVU +8.8%.
AI.conclusion: EFS provides a scalable, training-free frame-selection module that improves long-video understanding for LVLMs by balancing event coverage, query relevance, and diversity, and can be seamlessly inserted into off-the-shelf LVLMs.

title: DeLo: Dual Decomposed Low-Rank Experts Collaboration for Continual Missing Modality Learning
summary: Adapting Large Multimodal Models (LMMs) to real-world scenarios poses the dual challenges of learning from sequential data streams while handling frequent modality incompleteness, a task known as Continual Missing Modality Learning (CMML). However, existing works on CMML have predominantly relied on prompt tuning, a technique that struggles with this task due to cross-task interference between its learnable prompts in their shared embedding space. A naive application of Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) with modality-shared module will also suffer modality interference from competing gradients. To this end, we propose DeLo, the first framework to leverage a novel dual-decomposed low-rank expert architecture for CMML. Specifically, this architecture resolves modality interference through decomposed LoRA expert, dynamically composing LoRA update matrix with rank-one factors from disentangled modality-specific factor pools. Embedded within a task-partitioned framework that structurally prevents catastrophic forgetting, this expert system is supported by two key mechanisms: a Cross-Modal Guided Routing strategy to handle incomplete data and a Task-Key Memory for efficient, task-agnostic inference. Extensive experiments on established CMML benchmarks demonstrate that our method significantly outperforms state-of-the-art approaches. This highlights the value of a principled, architecturally-aware LoRA design for real-world multimodal challenges.

AI.tldr: Proposes DeLo, a dual-decomposed low-rank expert architecture for continual missing modality learning (CMML) that uses disentangled modality-specific factor pools to compose LoRA updates, within a task-partitioned framework with Cross-Modal Guided Routing and Task-Key Memory; achieves state-of-the-art on CMML benchmarks.
AI.motivation: CMML requires learning from sequential data streams with frequent modality incompleteness. Prompt-tuning-based CMML methods suffer cross-task interference in a shared embedding space, and naive LoRA with a modality-shared module incurs gradient interference; hence a principled, architecture-aware solution is needed.
AI.method: Introduce a dual-decomposed low-rank expert (DeLo) architecture. It uses decomposed LoRA experts that dynamically compose the update matrix from rank-one factors drawn from disentangled modality-specific factor pools. The system is embedded in a task-partitioned framework to mitigate catastrophic forgetting, and is supported by Cross-Modal Guided Routing to handle incomplete data and a Task-Key Memory for efficient, task-agnostic inference.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on established CMML benchmarks show that DeLo significantly outperforms state-of-the-art approaches.
AI.conclusion: Architectural design of a dual-decomposed LoRA expert with modality-specific factor pools and supporting mechanisms effectively mitigates modality interference in CMML, underscoring the value of principled, architecture-aware LoRA designs for real-world multimodal continual learning.

title: Transform-Invariant Generative Ray Path Sampling for Efficient Radio Propagation Modeling
comment: submitted to npj Wireless Technology, 26 pages, 14 figures
summary: Ray tracing has become a standard for accurate radio propagation modeling, but suffers from exponential computational complexity, as the number of candidate paths scales with the number of objects raised to the power of the interaction order. This bottleneck limits its use in large-scale or real-time applications, forcing traditional tools to rely on heuristics to reduce the number of path candidates at the cost of potentially reduced accuracy. To overcome this limitation, we propose a comprehensive machine-learning-assisted framework that replaces exhaustive path searching with intelligent sampling via Generative Flow Networks. Applying such generative models to this domain presents significant challenges, particularly sparse rewards due to the rarity of valid paths, which can lead to convergence failures and trivial solutions when evaluating high-order interactions in complex environments. To ensure robust learning and efficient exploration, our framework incorporates three key architectural components. First, we implement an \emph{experience replay buffer} to capture and retain rare valid paths. Second, we adopt a uniform exploratory policy to improve generalization and prevent the model from overfitting to simple geometries. Third, we apply a physics-based action masking strategy that filters out physically impossible paths before the model even considers them. As demonstrated in our experimental validation, the proposed model achieves substantial speedups over exhaustive search -- up to $10\times$ faster on GPU and $1000\times$ faster on CPU -- while maintaining high coverage accuracy and successfully uncovering complex propagation paths. The complete source code, tests, and tutorial are available at https://github.com/jeertmans/sampling-paths.

AI.tldr: ML-assisted ray tracing framework replaces exhaustive path search with generative sampling (GFlowNets) for propagation paths, aided by experience replay, uniform exploration, and physics-based action masking; achieves large speedups with maintained accuracy.
AI.motivation: Ray tracing yields accurate radio propagation modeling but has exponential computational complexity due to the combinatorial growth of candidate paths with interaction order. This bottleneck hinders large-scale and real-time applications, prompting reliance on heuristics that may reduce accuracy.
AI.method: Use Generative Flow Networks to sample propagation paths instead of exhaustive search. Incorporate three components: (1) experience replay buffer to preserve rare valid paths, (2) uniform exploratory policy to improve generalization and avoid overfitting to simple geometries, (3) physics-based action masking to filter out physically impossible paths before evaluation.
AI.result: Empirical validation shows substantial speedups: up to 10× on GPU and 1000× on CPU compared to exhaustive search, while maintaining high coverage accuracy and discovering complex propagation paths. Source code and tests available on GitHub.
AI.conclusion: The framework provides a robust, sample-efficient approach to path sampling in ray tracing for propagation modeling, enabling faster-than-exhaustive searches without sacrificing accuracy. It lays groundwork for scalable, real-time propagation modeling but may warrant further investigation into sparse-reward learning stability and high-order interaction handling.

title: Foundation Models in Remote Sensing: Evolving from Unimodality to Multimodality
summary: Remote sensing (RS) techniques are increasingly crucial for deepening our understanding of the planet. As the volume and diversity of RS data continue to grow exponentially, there is an urgent need for advanced data modeling and understanding capabilities to manage and interpret these vast datasets effectively. Foundation models present significant new growth opportunities and immense potential to revolutionize the RS field. In this paper, we conduct a comprehensive technical survey on foundation models in RS, offering a brand-new perspective by exploring their evolution from unimodality to multimodality. We hope this work serves as a valuable entry point for researchers interested in both foundation models and RS and helps them launch new projects or explore new research topics in this rapidly evolving area. This survey addresses the following three key questions: What are foundation models in RS? Why are foundation models needed in RS? How can we effectively guide junior researchers in gaining a comprehensive and practical understanding of foundation models in RS applications? More specifically, we begin by outlining the background and motivation, emphasizing the importance of foundation models in RS. We then review existing foundation models in RS, systematically categorizing them into unimodal and multimodal approaches. Additionally, we provide a tutorial-like section to guide researchers, especially beginners, on how to train foundation models in RS and apply them to real-world tasks. The survey aims to equip researchers in RS with a deeper and more efficient understanding of foundation models, enabling them to get started easily and effectively apply these models across various RS applications.

AI.tldr: A comprehensive survey of foundation models in remote sensing (RS), tracing their evolution from unimodal to multimodal models, and including a tutorial section to train and apply these models; designed to guide researchers, especially beginners, in RS foundation-model research and applications.
AI.motivation: RS data volume and diversity are exploding, creating a need for advanced modeling capabilities. Foundation models offer scalable, generalizable solutions that can be adapted to RS tasks and modalities.
AI.method: Systematic literature review that categorizes RS foundation models into unimodal and multimodal approaches, followed by a tutorial-like section guiding researchers on training foundation models in RS and applying them to real tasks.
AI.result: A structured taxonomy and analysis of existing RS foundation models, plus practical guidance and workflows to train and deploy such models in RS contexts; a didactic entry point for junior researchers.
AI.conclusion: Foundation models hold significant potential to advance RS by enabling versatile, scalable modeling across modalities; the survey provides a practical entry point and framework for researchers to start and expand RS foundation-model projects.

title: FreeGNN: Continual Source-Free Graph Neural Network Adaptation for Renewable Energy Forecasting
summary: Accurate forecasting of renewable energy generation is essential for efficient grid management and sustainable power planning. However, traditional supervised models often require access to labeled data from the target site, which may be unavailable due to privacy, cost, or logistical constraints. In this work, we propose FreeGNN, a Continual Source-Free Graph Domain Adaptation framework that enables adaptive forecasting on unseen renewable energy sites without requiring source data or target labels. Our approach integrates a spatio-temporal Graph Neural Network (GNN) backbone with a teacher--student strategy, a memory replay mechanism to mitigate catastrophic forgetting, graph-based regularization to preserve spatial correlations, and a drift-aware weighting scheme to dynamically adjust adaptation strength during streaming updates. This combination allows the model to continuously adapt to non-stationary environmental conditions while maintaining robustness and stability. We conduct extensive experiments on three real-world datasets: GEFCom2012, Solar PV, and Wind SCADA, encompassing multiple sites, temporal resolutions, and meteorological features. The ablation study confirms that each component memory, graph regularization, drift-aware adaptation, and teacher--student strategy contributes significantly to overall performance. The experiments show that FreeGNN achieves an MAE of 5.237 and an RMSE of 7.123 on the GEFCom dataset, an MAE of 1.107 and an RMSE of 1.512 on the Solar PV dataset, and an MAE of 0.382 and an RMSE of 0.523 on the Wind SCADA dataset. These results demonstrate its ability to achieve accurate and robust forecasts in a source-free, continual learning setting, highlighting its potential for real-world deployment in adaptive renewable energy systems. For reproducibility, implementation details are available at: https://github.com/AraoufBh/FreeGNN.

AI.tldr: FreeGNN is a source-free continual graph domain adaptation framework for adaptive renewable energy forecasting that combines a spatio-temporal GNN backbone with a teacher–student setup, memory replay, graph regularization, and drift-aware weighting to adapt to unseen sites under non-stationary conditions. It outperforms baselines across three real-world datasets and is supported by ablation studies and reproducibility materials.
AI.motivation: Target-site labeled data and even source data may be unavailable due to privacy, cost, or logistics. There is a need for models that can adapt to unseen sites in a continual, nonstationary environment without requiring labeled data from the target domain (source-free continual learning).
AI.method: A spatio-temporal Graph Neural Network backbone is integrated with a teacher–student framework. A memory replay mechanism mitigates catastrophic forgetting in continual learning. Graph-based regularization preserves spatial correlations. A drift-aware weighting scheme dynamically adjusts adaptation strength during streaming updates. The approach operates in a source-free setting and is evaluated on multiple sites and resolutions, with an ablation study validating each component.
AI.result: On GEFCom2012: MAE 5.237, RMSE 7.123. On Solar PV: MAE 1.107, RMSE 1.512. On Wind SCADA: MAE 0.382, RMSE 0.523. Ablation confirms memory, graph regularization, drift-aware adaptation, and teacher–student components each contribute to performance. Reproducibility: code available at the provided GitHub link.
AI.conclusion: FreeGNN demonstrates accurate and robust forecasting in a source-free continual learning setting, suitable for real-world adaptive renewable energy systems. It shows competitive performance across diverse datasets and highlights the value of integrating memory, regularization, and drift-aware adaptation with a teacher–student framework.

title: MLRecon: Robust Markerless Freehand 3D Ultrasound Reconstruction via Coarse-to-Fine Pose Estimation
summary: Freehand 3D ultrasound (US) reconstruction promises volumetric imaging with the flexibility of standard 2D probes, yet existing tracking paradigms face a restrictive trilemma: marker-based systems demand prohibitive costs, inside-out methods require intrusive sensor attachment, and sensorless approaches suffer from severe cumulative drift. To overcome these limitations, we present MLRecon, a robust markerless 3D US reconstruction framework delivering drift-resilient 6D probe pose tracking using a single commodity RGB-D camera. Leveraging the generalization power of vision foundation models, our pipeline enables continuous markerless tracking of the probe, augmented by a vision-guided divergence detector that autonomously monitors tracking integrity and triggers failure recovery to ensure uninterrupted scanning. Crucially, we further propose a dual-stage pose refinement network that explicitly disentangles high-frequency jitter from low-frequency bias, effectively denoising the trajectory while maintaining the kinematic fidelity of operator maneuvers. Experiments demonstrate that MLRecon significantly outperforms competing sensorless and sensor-aided methods, achieving average position errors as low as 0.88 mm on complex trajectories and yielding high-quality 3D reconstructions with sub-millimeter mean surface accuracy. This establishes a new benchmark for low-cost, accessible volumetric US imaging in resource-limited clinical settings.

AI.tldr: Markerless 6D pose tracking for freehand 3D ultrasound using a single RGB-D camera, with a vision-guided divergence detector and a dual-stage pose refinement network; achieves sub-millimeter surface accuracy and ~0.88 mm average position error on complex trajectories, surpassing sensorless and sensor-aided baselines.
AI.motivation: Overcome the prohibitive cost of marker-based tracking, intrusive requirements of inside-out approaches, and severe drift in sensorless methods by delivering a low-cost, markerless 6D tracking solution for volumetric US.
AI.method: A pipeline that uses vision foundation models for continuous markerless 6D pose tracking of the US probe with an RGB-D camera, augmented by a divergence detector to monitor tracking integrity and trigger recovery. A dual-stage pose refinement network disentangles high-frequency jitter from low-frequency bias to denoise the trajectory while preserving operator kinematics.
