AI.result: The method significantly outperforms competing sensorless and sensor-aided methods, achieving average position errors as low as 0.88 mm on complex trajectories and producing high-quality 3D reconstructions with sub-millimeter mean surface accuracy.
AI.conclusion: MLRecon establishes a new benchmark for low-cost, accessible volumetric US imaging in resource-limited clinical settings, by enabling drift-resilient, continuous markerless 6D tracking with automatic failure recovery.

title: Let Your Image Move with Your Motion! -- Implicit Multi-Object Multi-Motion Transfer
summary: Motion transfer has emerged as a promising direction for controllable video generation, yet existing methods largely focus on single-object scenarios and struggle when multiple objects require distinct motion patterns. In this work, we present FlexiMMT, the first implicit image-to-video (I2V) motion transfer framework that explicitly enables multi-object, multi-motion transfer. Given a static multi-object image and multiple reference videos, FlexiMMT independently extracts motion representations and accurately assigns them to different objects, supporting flexible recombination and arbitrary motion-to-object mappings. To address the core challenge of cross-object motion entanglement, we introduce a Motion Decoupled Mask Attention Mechanism that uses object-specific masks to constrain attention, ensuring that motion and text tokens only influence their designated regions. We further propose a Differentiated Mask Propagation Mechanism that derives object-specific masks directly from diffusion attention and progressively propagates them across frames efficiently. Extensive experiments demonstrate that FlexiMMT achieves precise, compositional, and state-of-the-art performance in I2V-based multi-object multi-motion transfer.

AI.tldr: FlexiMMT introduces an implicit image-to-video motion transfer framework that supports multi-object, multi-motion transfer by decoupling per-object motion via a Motion Decoupled Mask Attention and a Differentiated Mask Propagation mechanism, achieving state-of-the-art, compositional results.
AI.motivation: To address motion transfer in I2V where multiple objects must exhibit distinct motions, a problem neglected by single-object focused methods, and to reduce cross-object motion entanglement.
AI.method: Independently extract motion representations from multiple reference videos and assign them to different objects. Use Motion Decoupled Mask Attention with object-specific masks to constrain attention to designated regions. Introduce Differentiated Mask Propagation to derive masks from diffusion attention and progressively propagate them across frames efficiently.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show precise, compositional, and state-of-the-art performance in I2V-based multi-object multi-motion transfer.
AI.conclusion: The framework effectively decouples motions across objects, enables flexible recombination and arbitrary motion-to-object mappings, and achieves superior performance on multi-object motion transfer tasks.

title: Reasoning as Gradient: Scaling MLE Agents Beyond Tree Search
summary: LLM-based agents for machine learning engineering (MLE) predominantly rely on tree search, a form of gradient-free optimization that uses scalar validation scores to rank candidates. As LLM reasoning capabilities improve, exhaustive enumeration becomes increasingly inefficient compared to directed updates, analogous to how accurate gradients enable efficient descent over random search. We introduce \textsc{Gome}, an MLE agent that operationalizes gradient-based optimization. \textsc{Gome} maps structured diagnostic reasoning to gradient computation, success memory to momentum, and multi-trace execution to distributed optimization. Under a closed-world protocol that isolates architectural effects from external knowledge, \textsc{Gome} achieves a state-of-the-art 35.1\% any-medal rate on MLE-Bench with a restricted 12-hour budget on a single V100 GPU. Scaling experiments across 10 models reveal a critical crossover: with weaker models, tree search retains advantages by compensating for unreliable reasoning through exhaustive exploration; as reasoning capability strengthens, gradient-based optimization progressively outperforms, with the gap widening at frontier-tier models. Given the rapid advancement of reasoning-oriented LLMs, this positions gradient-based optimization as an increasingly favorable paradigm. We release our codebase and GPT-5 traces.

AI.tldr: Gome introduces gradient-based optimization for LLM-based MLE agents, mapping diagnostic reasoning to gradient computation, using momentum and distributed optimization. It achieves 35.1% any-medal rate on MLE-Bench within 12 hours on a single V100 GPU, outperforming tree search as reasoning improves; code and GPT-5 traces released.
AI.motivation: Address inefficiency of tree-search-based LLM agents in machine learning engineering (MLE) as LLM reasoning improves. Leverage gradient-based optimization to enable directed updates and faster convergence, analogous to how gradients guide descent in optimization.
AI.method: Gome maps structured diagnostic reasoning to gradient computation, uses success memory as momentum, and interprets multi-trace execution as distributed optimization. The evaluation operates under a closed-world protocol to isolate architectural effects, benchmarking on MLE-Bench with a 12-hour budget on one V100 GPU. Scaling studies across 10 models explore the crossover from tree search to gradient-based optimization.
AI.result: Gome achieves a state-of-the-art 35.1% any-medal rate on MLE-Bench under the specified budget. Across scaling experiments, a crossover is observed: weaker models benefit from tree search due to unreliable reasoning and exhaustive exploration, while stronger, reasoning-capable models increasingly benefit from gradient-based optimization, widening the gap at frontier-tier models.
AI.conclusion: As reasoning-oriented LLMs advance, gradient-based optimization becomes a more favorable paradigm for LLM-based MLE agents. The work provides evidence for a shift away from gradient-free tree search toward gradient-guided updates and releases code and GPT-5 traces.

title: Dr.Occ: Depth- and Region-Guided 3D Occupancy from Surround-View Cameras for Autonomous Driving
summary: 3D semantic occupancy prediction is crucial for autonomous driving perception, offering comprehensive geometric scene understanding and semantic recognition. However, existing methods struggle with geometric misalignment in view transformation due to the lack of pixel-level accurate depth estimation, and severe spatial class imbalance where semantic categories exhibit strong spatial anisotropy. To address these challenges, we propose Dr.Occ, a depth- and region-guided occupancy prediction framework. Specifically, we introduce a depth-guided 2D-to-3D View Transformer (D$^2$-VFormer) that effectively leverages high-quality dense depth cues from MoGe-2 to construct reliable geometric priors, thereby enabling precise geometric alignment of voxel features. Moreover, inspired by the Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) framework, we propose a region-guided Expert Transformer (R/R$^2$-EFormer) that adaptively allocates region-specific experts to focus on different spatial regions, effectively addressing spatial semantic variations. Thus, the two components make complementary contributions: depth guidance ensures geometric alignment, while region experts enhance semantic learning. Experiments on the Occ3D-nuScenes benchmark demonstrate that \textbf{Dr.Occ} improves the strong baseline BEVDet4D by 7.43\% mIoU and 3.09\% IoU under the full vision-only setting.

AI.tldr: Dr.Occ presents a depth- and region-guided framework for 3D occupancy prediction, combining a depth-guided 2D-to-3D view transformer (D^2-VFormer) that uses dense depth cues to improve geometric alignment, with a region-guided MoE-style Expert Transformer (R/R^2-EFormer) to handle spatial semantic imbalance.
AI.motivation: Address two main challenges in 3D semantic occupancy: (1) geometric misalignment from imperfect depth-based view transformation; (2) severe spatial class imbalance and anisotropy across regions in the scene.
AI.method: 1) D^2-VFormer leverages high-quality depth cues from MoGe-2 to construct reliable geometric priors for accurate voxel feature alignment. 2) R/R^2-EFormer adopts a Mixture-of-Experts-like mechanism to allocate region-specific experts, enabling region-aware semantic learning and handling spatial variations.
AI.result: On Occ3D-nuScenes, Dr.Occ improves the strong baseline BEVDet4D by 7.43 percentage points in mIoU and 3.09 percentage points in IoU under the full vision-only setting.
AI.conclusion: Depth-guided geometry and region-specific expert learning complement each other to enhance 3D occupancy prediction, delivering substantial gains over baseline methods on the Occ3D-nuScenes benchmark.

title: GeodesicNVS: Probability Density Geodesic Flow Matching for Novel View Synthesis
summary: Recent advances in generative modeling have substantially enhanced novel view synthesis, yet maintaining consistency across viewpoints remains challenging. Diffusion-based models rely on stochastic noise-to-data transitions, which obscure deterministic structures and yield inconsistent view predictions. We propose a Data-to-Data Flow Matching framework that learns deterministic transformations directly between paired views, enhancing view-consistent synthesis through explicit data coupling. To further enhance geometric coherence, we introduce Probability Density Geodesic Flow Matching (PDG-FM), which constrains flow trajectories using geodesic interpolants derived from probability density metrics of pretrained diffusion models. Such alignment with high-density regions of the data manifold promotes more realistic interpolants between samples. Empirically, our method surpasses diffusion-based NVS baselines, demonstrating improved structural coherence and smoother transitions across views. These results highlight the advantages of incorporating data-dependent geometric regularization into deterministic flow matching for consistent novel view generation.

AI.tldr: Introduces Data-to-Data Flow Matching (D2D-FM) for deterministic cross-view transformations to improve view-consistent novel view synthesis, augmented by PDG-FM with geodesic flow constraints for density-aligned interpolants; yields outperforming diffusion-based baselines.
AI.motivation: Diffusion-based NVS is stochastic and can obscure deterministic structure, leading to view inconsistency. A deterministic, geometry-aware cross-view mapping with explicit data coupling is desirable to improve coherence.
AI.method: Propose Data-to-Data Flow Matching (D2D-FM) to learn deterministic transformations between paired views. Introduce Probability Density Geodesic Flow Matching (PDG-FM) that constrains flow trajectories via geodesic interpolants derived from probability-density metrics of pretrained diffusion models to align with high-density regions of the data manifold.
AI.result: Empirically surpasses diffusion-based NVS baselines, with improved structural coherence and smoother transitions across views.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating data-dependent geometric regularization into deterministic flow matching enhances consistent novel view synthesis and demonstrates the advantage of geometry-aware flow regularization for NVS.

title: DynaMoE: Dynamic Token-Level Expert Activation with Layer-Wise Adaptive Capacity for Mixture-of-Experts Neural Networks
summary: Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architectures have emerged as a powerful paradigm for scaling neural networks while maintaining computational efficiency. However, standard MoE implementations rely on two rigid design assumptions: (1) fixed Top-K routing where exactly K experts are activated per token, and (2) uniform expert allocation across all layers. This paper introduces DynaMoE, a novel MoE framework that relaxes both constraints through dynamic token-level expert activation and layer-wise adaptive capacity allocation. DynaMoE introduces a principled routing mechanism where the number of active experts per token varies based on input complexity. Concurrently, the framework implements six distinct scheduling strategies for distributing expert capacity across network depth, including descending, ascending, pyramid, and wave patterns. We theoretically analyze the expressivity gains of dynamic routing and derive bounds on computational efficiency. Through extensive experiments on MNIST, Fashion-MNIST, CIFAR-10 (image classification), and Recycling-the-Web (language modeling) across multiple model scales, we demonstrate that DynaMoE achieves superior parameter efficiency compared to static baselines. Our key finding is that optimal expert schedules are task- and scale-dependent: descending schedules (concentrating capacity in early layers) outperform uniform baselines on image classification. For language modeling, optimal schedules vary by model size, descending for Tiny, ascending for Small, and uniform for Medium. Furthermore, dynamic routing reduces gradient variance during training, leading to improved convergence stability. DynaMoE establishes a new framework for adaptive computation in neural networks, providing principled guidance for MoE architecture design.

AI.tldr: A dynamic mixture-of-experts framework that enables per-token variable activation and layer-wise adaptive capacity, with six scheduling strategies; shows task- and scale-dependent optimal schedules and improved convergence, achieving better parameter efficiency than static MoE baselines.
AI.motivation: Standard MoE relies on fixed Top-K routing and uniform layer capacity, leading to inefficiencies and limited expressivity. There is a need for adaptive computation that matches input complexity and layerwise budget.
AI.method: Introduce DynaMoE with dynamic token-level routing (varying active experts per token). Implement six scheduling strategies for distributing capacity across depths (descending, ascending, pyramid, wave, and others). Provide theoretical analysis on expressivity and compute bounds. Empirical evaluation on MNIST, Fashion-MNIST, CIFAR-10, and Recycling-the-Web (language modeling) at multiple model scales. Compare against static baselines for parameter efficiency and train stability.
AI.result: Dynamic routing yields parameter-efficient models with superior performance relative to static MoEs. Optimal schedules are task- and scale-dependent (e.g., descending best for image classification; language modeling varies by model size). Dynamic routing reduces gradient variance, improving convergence stability.
AI.conclusion: DynaMoE establishes a principled adaptive-computation MoE framework, guiding architecture design with dynamic routing and layer-wise capacity schedules. It demonstrates the value of task- and scale-aware scheduling for MoEs.

title: Implementation of Licensed Plate Detection and Noise Removal in Image Processing
comment: 13 pages. This is the author's version, accepted manuscript
summary: Car license plate recognition system is an image processing technology used to identify vehicles by capturing their Car License Plates. The car license plate recognition technology is also known as automatic number-plate recognition, automatic vehicle identification, car license plate recognition or optical character recognition for cars. In Malaysia, as the number of vehicle is increasing rapidly nowadays, a pretty great number of vehicle on the road has brought about the considerable demands of car license plate recognition system. Car license plate recognition system can be implemented in electronic parking payment system, highway toll-fee system, traffic surveillance system and as police enforcement tools. Additionally, car license plate recognition system technology also has potential to be combined with various techniques in other different fields like biology, aerospace and so on to achieve the goal of solving some specialized problems.

AI.tldr: Overview of car license plate recognition (ALPR) technology and its applications, with a focus on Malaysia and potential cross-domain uses.
AI.motivation: Rising vehicle population in Malaysia drives demand for automated identification to support parking, tolls, traffic surveillance, and law enforcement; potential broader applications in biology, aerospace, etc.
AI.method: Conceptual overview / literature synthesis based on existing descriptions; no empirical experiments reported in abstract.
AI.result: ALPR technology enables automated recognition for parking, tolls, surveillance; integrated with other domains to solve specialized problems; high-level statement of benefits.
AI.conclusion: ALPR is increasingly relevant in Malaysia; expansion and integration with other techniques and fields can enhance problem-solving across sectors.

title: TopoCurate:Modeling Interaction Topology for Tool-Use Agent Training
summary: Training tool-use agents typically relies on outcome-based filtering: Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) on successful trajectories and Reinforcement Learning (RL) on pass-rate-selected tasks. However, this paradigm ignores interaction dynamics: successful trajectories may lack error recovery or exhibit redundancy, while pass rates fail to distinguish structurally informative tasks from trivial ones. We propose \textbf{TopoCurate}, an interaction-aware framework that projects multi-trial rollouts from the same task into a unified semantic quotient topology. By merging equivalent action-observation states, this projection transforms scattered linear trajectories into a structured manifold that explicitly captures how tool invocations and environmental responses drive the divergence between effective strategies and failure modes. Leveraging this representation, we introduce a dual-selection mechanism: for SFT, we prioritize trajectories demonstrating reflective recovery, semantic efficiency, and strategic diversity to mitigate covariate shift and mode collapse; for RL, we select tasks with high error branch ratios and strategic heterogeneity, maximizing gradient Signal-to-Noise Ratio to address vanishing signals in sparse-reward settings. Evaluations on BFCLv3 and Tau2 Bench show that TopoCurate achieves consistent gains of 4.2\% (SFT) and 6.9\% (RL) over state-of-the-art baselines. We will release the code and data soon for further investigations.

AI.tldr: TopoCurate projects multi-trial tool-use rollouts into a semantic quotient topology to enable interaction-aware selection for SFT and RL, achieving consistent gains on two benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Outcome-based filtering ignores interaction dynamics such as error recovery, redundancy, and task informativeness. There is a need to distinguish structurally informative trajectories from trivial ones and to mitigate covariate shift and mode collapse in SFT, as well as gradient noise in sparse-reward RL.
AI.method: Project multi-trial rollouts from the same task into a unified semantic quotient topology by merging equivalent action-observation states, turning scattered trajectories into a structured manifold. Dual selection: for SFT, prioritize reflective recovery, semantic efficiency, and strategic diversity; for RL, select tasks with high error-branch ratios and strategic heterogeneity to maximize gradient signal-to-noise ratio.
AI.result: Empirical gains of 4.2% for SFT and 6.9% for RL over state-of-the-art baselines on BFCLv3 and Tau2 Bench.
AI.conclusion: TopoCurate demonstrates an interaction-aware framework that improves trajectory/task selection by capturing dynamics of tool use; plans to release code and data to support replication and further study.

title: RaUF: Learning the Spatial Uncertainty Field of Radar
summary: Millimeter-wave radar offers unique advantages in adverse weather but suffers from low spatial fidelity, severe azimuth ambiguity, and clutter-induced spurious returns. Existing methods mainly focus on improving spatial perception effectiveness via coarse-to-fine cross-modal supervision, yet often overlook the ambiguous feature-to-label mapping, which may lead to ill-posed geometric inference and pose fundamental challenges to downstream perception tasks. In this work, we propose RaUF, a spatial uncertainty field learning framework that models radar measurements through their physically grounded anisotropic properties. To resolve conflicting feature-to-label mapping, we design an anisotropic probabilistic model that learns fine-grained uncertainty. To further enhance reliability, we propose a Bidirectional Domain Attention mechanism that exploits the mutual complementarity between spatial structure and Doppler consistency, effectively suppressing spurious or multipath-induced reflections. Extensive experiments on public benchmarks and real-world datasets demonstrate that RaUF delivers highly reliable spatial detections with well-calibrated uncertainty. Moreover, downstream case studies further validate the enhanced reliability and scalability of RaUF under challenging real-world driving scenarios.

AI.tldr: RaUF proposes a spatial uncertainty field learning framework for millimeter-wave radar that uses an anisotropic probabilistic model and a Bidirectional Domain Attention mechanism to improve reliability and uncertainty calibration, validated on public benchmarks and real-world driving data.
AI.motivation: Millimeter-wave radar provides robustness in adverse weather but suffers from low spatial fidelity, azimuth ambiguity, and clutter-induced spurious returns. Existing coarse-to-fine cross-modal supervision often neglects the ambiguous feature-to-label mapping, leading to ill-posed geometric inference and challenges for downstream perception tasks.
AI.method: 1) Model radar measurements using physically grounded anisotropic properties within a spatial uncertainty field. 2) Introduce an anisotropic probabilistic model to capture fine-grained uncertainty and resolve conflicting feature-to-label mappings. 3) Employ a Bidirectional Domain Attention mechanism to exploit mutual complementarity between spatial structure and Doppler consistency to suppress spurious/multipath reflections. 4) Integrate these into the RaUF framework and validate on benchmarks and real-world data.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on public benchmarks and real-world datasets show RaUF delivers highly reliable spatial detections with well-calibrated uncertainty. Downstream case studies demonstrate enhanced reliability and scalability under challenging real-world driving scenarios.
AI.conclusion: RaUF provides reliable, uncertainty-calibrated spatial detections for mmWave radar, demonstrating robustness, reliability, and scalability in adverse conditions and complex driving environments.

title: Decentralized Federated Learning by Partial Message Exchange
summary: Decentralized federated learning (DFL) has emerged as a transformative server-free paradigm that enables collaborative learning over large-scale heterogeneous networks. However, it continues to face fundamental challenges, including data heterogeneity, restrictive assumptions for theoretical analysis, and degraded convergence when standard communication- or privacyenhancing techniques are applied. To overcome these drawbacks, this paper develops a novel algorithm, PaME (DFL by Partial Message Exchange). The central principle is to allow only randomly selected sparse coordinates to be exchanged between two neighbor nodes. Consequently, PaME achieves substantial reductions in communication costs while still preserving a high level of privacy, without sacrificing accuracy. Moreover, grounded in rigorous analysis, the algorithm is shown to converge at a linear rate under the gradient to be locally Lipschitz continuous and the communication matrix to be doubly stochastic. These two mild assumptions not only dispense with many restrictive conditions commonly imposed by existing DFL methods but also enables PaME to effectively address data heterogeneity. Furthermore, comprehensive numerical experiments demonstrate its superior performance compared with several representative decentralized learning algorithms.

AI.tldr: PaME reduces communication in decentralized federated learning by exchanging randomly selected sparse coordinates between neighbors. It yields linear convergence under mild conditions and demonstrates strong empirical performance while maintaining privacy and addressing data heterogeneity.
AI.motivation: To overcome core challenges in decentralized federated learning: data heterogeneity, restrictive theoretical conditions, and degraded performance when applying standard communication- or privacy-enhancing techniques. The goal is to cut communication cost without sacrificing accuracy or privacy.
AI.method: Introduce Partial Message Exchange (PaME): neighbors exchange only randomly selected sparse coordinates rather than full gradient/parameter vectors. This reduces communication while preserving privacy. Theoretical analysis shows linear convergence under gradient locally Lipschitz continuity and doubly stochastic communication matrix. The approach relaxes common restrictive assumptions and improves robustness to data heterogeneity.
AI.result: PaME achieves linear convergence under mild conditions and significantly reduces communication overhead due to sparse exchanges. Empirical results show superior performance compared with several representative decentralized learning algorithms, indicating effectiveness in heterogeneous data settings and under privacy/communication constraints.
AI.conclusion: PaME provides a practical, theory-backed approach to DFL that balances communication efficiency, privacy, and accuracy. The method’s mild assumptions broaden applicability; future work could explore non-convex objectives, varying sparsity levels, and extensions to dynamic networks.

title: Content-Aware Frequency Encoding for Implicit Neural Representations with Fourier-Chebyshev Features
summary: Implicit Neural Representations (INRs) have emerged as a powerful paradigm for various signal processing tasks, but their inherent spectral bias limits the ability to capture high-frequency details. Existing methods partially mitigate this issue by using Fourier-based features, which usually rely on fixed frequency bases. This forces multi-layer perceptrons (MLPs) to inefficiently compose the required frequencies, thereby constraining their representational capacity. To address this limitation, we propose Content-Aware Frequency Encoding (CAFE), which builds upon Fourier features through multiple parallel linear layers combined via a Hadamard product. CAFE can explicitly and efficiently synthesize a broader range of frequency bases, while the learned weights enable the selection of task-relevant frequencies. Furthermore, we extend this framework to CAFE+, which incorporates Chebyshev features as a complementary component to Fourier bases. This combination provides a stronger and more stable frequency representation. Extensive experiments across multiple benchmarks validate the effectiveness and efficiency of our approach, consistently achieving superior performance over existing methods. Our code is available at https://github.com/JunboKe0619/CAFE.

AI.tldr: Content-Aware Frequency Encoding (CAFE) and CAFE+ propose learnable, task-aware frequency bases for implicit neural representations by combining parallel linear layers with a Hadamard product, optionally augmenting Fourier bases with Chebyshev features to improve high-frequency capture and stability; demonstrated to outperform baselines on multiple benchmarks with code release.
AI.motivation: Implicit Neural Representations (INRs) suffer from spectral bias, struggling to represent high-frequency details. Traditional Fourier-based features use fixed bases, forcing MLPs to compose needed frequencies inefficiently. A learnable, flexible encoding could better cover the frequency spectrum and improve representation efficiency.
AI.method: CAFE builds on Fourier features by using multiple parallel linear projections whose outputs are combined via a Hadamard product to synthesize a broader, learnable set of frequency bases. Learned weights tune the relevance of each frequency component for the task. CAFE+ further augments the framework with Chebyshev features, providing complementary basis functions to Fourier components for improved frequency coverage and stability.
AI.result: Experimental results on multiple benchmarks show superior performance and efficiency over existing INR methods. The authors provide a code release (GitHub) for reproducibility.
AI.conclusion: CAFE and CAFE+ deliver explicit, efficient, and learnable frequency representations that extend the frequency spectrum accessible to INRs. The Hadamard-combined parallel projections enable task-relevant frequency synthesis, while Chebyshev augmentation in CAFE+ improves stability and coverage. The approach is validated by extensive experiments and supported by public code.

title: CA-AFP: Cluster-Aware Adaptive Federated Pruning
summary:
Federated Learning (FL) faces major challenges in real-world deployments due to statistical heterogeneity across clients and system heterogeneity arising from resource-constrained devices. While clustering-based approaches mitigate statistical heterogeneity and pruning techniques improve memory and communication efficiency, these strategies are typically studied in isolation.
  We propose CA-AFP, a unified framework that jointly addresses both challenges by performing cluster-specific model pruning. In CA-AFP, clients are first grouped into clusters, and a separate model for each cluster is adaptively pruned during training. The framework introduces two key innovations: (1) a cluster-aware importance scoring mechanism that combines weight magnitude, intra-cluster coherence, and gradient consistency to identify parameters for pruning, and (2) an iterative pruning schedule that progressively removes parameters while enabling model self-healing through weight regrowth.
  We evaluate CA-AFP on two widely used human activity recognition benchmarks, UCI HAR and WISDM, under natural user-based federated partitions. Experimental results demonstrate that CA-AFP achieves a favorable balance between predictive accuracy, inter-client fairness, and communication efficiency. Compared to pruning-based baselines, CA-AFP consistently improves accuracy and lower performance disparity across clients with limited fine-tuning, while requiring substantially less communication than dense clustering-based methods. It also shows robustness to different Non-IID levels of data. Finally, ablation studies analyze the impact of clustering, pruning schedules and scoring mechanism offering practical insights into the design of efficient and adaptive FL systems.

AI.tldr: CA-AFP is a unified federated learning framework that clusters clients and jointly prunes cluster-specific models, improving accuracy and fairness with lower communication overhead and robustness to non-IID data.
AI.motivation: In FL, statistical heterogeneity and system heterogeneity hinder performance. Clustering and pruning are usually studied separately, yielding suboptimal trade-offs between accuracy, fairness, and efficiency.
AI.method: Clients are grouped into clusters; each cluster trains a separate pruned model adaptively. A cluster-aware scoring mechanism combines weight magnitude, intra-cluster coherence, and gradient consistency to select pruning candidates. An iterative pruning schedule with weight regrowth enables self-healing. Evaluations are conducted on UCI HAR and WISDM under natural user-based partitions.
AI.result: CA-AFP consistently improves accuracy and reduces inter-client performance disparity with limited fine-tuning, while achieving lower communication than dense clustering baselines. It shows robust performance across varying Non-IID levels. Ablation studies highlight the importance of clustering, pruning schedules, and scoring.
AI.conclusion: Jointly addressing statistical and system heterogeneity via cluster-specific pruning is effective for efficient, adaptive FL. CA-AFP offers practical guidelines for designing FL systems with simultaneous accuracy, fairness, and communication gains, and motivates further exploration of clustering-aware pruning strategies.

title: Reparameterized Tensor Ring Functional Decomposition for Multi-Dimensional Data Recovery
comment: 22 pages, 18 figures, 12 tables. Accepted by CVPR 2026
summary: Tensor Ring (TR) decomposition is a powerful tool for high-order data modeling, but is inherently restricted to discrete forms defined on fixed meshgrids. In this work, we propose a TR functional decomposition for both meshgrid and non-meshgrid data, where factors are parameterized by Implicit Neural Representations (INRs). However, optimizing this continuous framework to capture fine-scale details is intrinsically difficult. Through a frequency-domain analysis, we demonstrate that the spectral structure of TR factors determines the frequency composition of the reconstructed tensor and limits the high-frequency modeling capacity. To mitigate this, we propose a reparameterized TR functional decomposition, in which each TR factor is a structured combination of a learnable latent tensor and a fixed basis. This reparameterization is theoretically shown to improve the training dynamics of TR factor learning. We further derive a principled initialization scheme for the fixed basis and prove the Lipschitz continuity of our proposed model. Extensive experiments on image inpainting, denoising, super-resolution, and point cloud recovery demonstrate that our method achieves consistently superior performance over existing approaches. Code is available at https://github.com/YangyangXu2002/RepTRFD.

AI.tldr: Reparameterized Tensor Ring functional decomposition using implicit neural representations improves continuous TR factor learning and high-frequency modeling, with principled initialization and Lipschitz guarantees, yielding superior results on inpainting, denoising, super-resolution, and point-cloud recovery.
AI.motivation: Tensor Ring (TR) decomposition is powerful for high-order data but is tied to discrete fixed meshes. Extending TR to meshgrid and non-meshgrid data via INRs faces optimization and high-frequency modeling challenges due to spectral constraints.
AI.method: Introduce a TR functional decomposition with INR-parameterized factors. To address training difficulty, reparameterize each TR factor as a structured combo of a learnable latent tensor and a fixed basis; derive a principled initialization for the fixed basis; prove Lipschitz continuity of the model.
AI.result: Empirically, the method achieves consistent superior performance across image inpainting, denoising, super-resolution, and point cloud recovery compared to existing approaches.
AI.conclusion: The reparameterized TR functional decomposition enhances training dynamics and high-frequency modeling capacity for continuous TR representations, enabling improved performance across diverse tasks.

title: Discrete World Models via Regularization
summary: World models aim to capture the states and dynamics of an environment in a compact latent space. Moreover, using Boolean state representations is particularly useful for search heuristics and symbolic reasoning and planning. Existing approaches keep latents informative via decoder-based reconstruction, or instead via contrastive or reward signals. In this work, we introduce Discrete World Models via Regularization (DWMR): a reconstruction-free and contrastive-free method for unsupervised Boolean world-model learning. In particular, we introduce a novel world-modeling loss that couples latent prediction with specialized regularizers. Such regularizers maximize the entropy and independence of the representation bits through variance, correlation, and coskewness penalties, while simultaneously enforcing a locality prior for sparse action changes. To enable effective optimization, we also introduce a novel training scheme improving robustness to discrete roll-outs. Experiments on two benchmarks with underlying combinatorial structure show that DWMR learns more accurate representations and transitions than reconstruction-based alternatives. Finally, DWMR can also be paired with an auxiliary reconstruction decoder, and this combination yields additional gains.

AI.tldr: DWMR introduces a reconstruction-free, contrastive-free framework for learning Boolean world models via regularized latent predictions. It uses entropy and independence-promoting penalties (variance, correlation, coskewness) plus a locality prior on actions, with a training scheme robust to discrete rollouts; achieves stronger representations than reconstruction-based methods and can be augmented with a decoder for extra gains.
AI.motivation: Boolean latent representations are attractive for search, planning, and symbolic reasoning. Conventional reconstruction-based or contrastive/reward-based world models either rely on reconstructions or external signals, which can hamper learning of compact, discrete state representations.
AI.method: Propose Discrete World Models via Regularization (DWMR). A world-modeling loss couples latent prediction with regularizers that maximize bit entropy and independence (via variance, correlation, coskewness penalties) and enforce a locality prior for sparse action changes. Introduce a training scheme to improve robustness to discrete roll-outs. DWMR can optionally be paired with a reconstruction decoder.
AI.result: On two benchmarks with combinatorial structure, DWMR learns more accurate representations and transitions than reconstruction-based alternatives. When combined with an auxiliary reconstruction decoder, additional gains are observed.
AI.conclusion: DWMR demonstrates that reconstruction-free, regularization-driven learning can yield effective discrete world models, with competitive or superior representations and dynamics. The approach is compatible with adding a decoder for further improvement, suggesting flexibility for practical deployments.

title: Practical Deep Heteroskedastic Regression
summary: Uncertainty quantification (UQ) in deep learning regression is of wide interest, as it supports critical applications including sequential decision making and risk-sensitive tasks. In heteroskedastic regression, where the uncertainty of the target depends on the input, a common approach is to train a neural network that parameterizes the mean and the variance of the predictive distribution. Still, training deep heteroskedastic regression models poses practical challenges in the trade-off between uncertainty quantification and mean prediction, such as optimization difficulties, representation collapse, and variance overfitting. In this work we identify previously undiscussed fallacies and propose a simple and efficient procedure that addresses these challenges jointly by post-hoc fitting a variance model across the intermediate layers of a pretrained network on a hold-out dataset. We demonstrate that our method achieves on-par or state-of-the-art uncertainty quantification on several molecular graph datasets, without compromising mean prediction accuracy and remaining cheap to use at prediction time.

AI.tldr: Post-hoc variance modeling across intermediate layers of a pretrained deep network improves uncertainty quantification in heteroskedastic regression without sacrificing mean accuracy, achieving state-of-the-art results on molecular graph datasets while remaining prediction-time efficient.
AI.motivation: Uncertainty quantification (UQ) in deep heteroskedastic regression is challenging due to optimization difficulties, representation collapse, and variance overfitting; a simple, scalable solution is needed.
AI.method: Train a variance model post-hoc across intermediate layers of a pretrained network using a hold-out dataset, aligning the predictive variance with observed residuals without retraining the whole network.
AI.result: Achieves competitive or state-of-the-art UQ performance on several molecular graph datasets, with no degradation in mean predictions and low computation cost at inference.
AI.conclusion: Post-hoc, layer-wise variance fitting is an effective and scalable approach to calibrate uncertainty in deep heteroskedastic regression.

title: From Intuition to Investigation: A Tool-Augmented Reasoning MLLM Framework for Generalizable Face Anti-Spoofing
summary: Face recognition remains vulnerable to presentation attacks, calling for robust Face Anti-Spoofing (FAS) solutions. Recent MLLM-based FAS methods reformulate the binary classification task as the generation of brief textual descriptions to improve cross-domain generalization. However, their generalizability is still limited, as such descriptions mainly capture intuitive semantic cues (e.g., mask contours) while struggling to perceive fine-grained visual patterns. To address this limitation, we incorporate external visual tools into MLLMs to encourage deeper investigation of subtle spoof clues. Specifically, we propose the Tool-Augmented Reasoning FAS (TAR-FAS) framework, which reformulates the FAS task as a Chain-of-Thought with Visual Tools (CoT-VT) paradigm, allowing MLLMs to begin with intuitive observations and adaptively invoke external visual tools for fine-grained investigation. To this end, we design a tool-augmented data annotation pipeline and construct the ToolFAS-16K dataset, which contains multi-turn tool-use reasoning trajectories. Furthermore, we introduce a tool-aware FAS training pipeline, where Diverse-Tool Group Relative Policy Optimization (DT-GRPO) enables the model to autonomously learn efficient tool use. Extensive experiments under a challenging one-to-eleven cross-domain protocol demonstrate that TAR-FAS achieves SOTA performance while providing fine-grained visual investigation for trustworthy spoof detection.

AI.tldr: We propose TAR-FAS, a tool-augmented reasoning framework for face anti-spoofing that uses a Chain-of-Thought with Visual Tools (CoT-VT), a tool-augmented data pipeline, and a Diverse-Tool Group Relative Policy Optimization (DT-GRPO). It introduces the ToolFAS-16K dataset and demonstrates state-of-the-art performance in a challenging one-to-eleven cross-domain FAS setup with fine-grained visual explanations.
AI.motivation: MLLM-based FAS methods rely on brief textual descriptions that capture semantic cues but miss fine-grained visual patterns; incorporating external visual tools enables deeper, multi-turn inspection of subtle spoof cues for better generalization.
AI.method: Introduce the CoT-VT paradigm; develop a tool-augmented data annotation pipeline; construct ToolFAS-16K with multi-turn tool-use trajectories; train with Diverse-Tool Group Relative Policy Optimization (DT-GRPO) to learn efficient tool usage.
AI.result: Extensive experiments under one-to-eleven cross-domain protocol show TAR-FAS achieves state-of-the-art performance and provides fine-grained visual investigation for trustworthy spoof detection.
AI.conclusion: TAR-FAS enhances cross-domain generalization and interpretability in FAS by enabling structured tool-augmented reasoning; the ToolFAS-16K dataset and DT-GRPO training further support scalable, autonomous tool use in FAS.

title: Causal Circuit Tracing Reveals Distinct Computational Architectures in Single-Cell Foundation Models: Inhibitory Dominance, Biological Coherence, and Cross-Model Convergence
summary:
Motivation: Sparse autoencoders (SAEs) decompose foundation model activations into interpretable features, but causal feature-to-feature interactions across network depth remain unknown for biological foundation models.
  Results: We introduce causal circuit tracing by ablating SAE features and measuring downstream responses, and apply it to Geneformer V2-316M and scGPT whole-human across four conditions (96,892 edges, 80,191 forward passes). Both models show approximately 53 percent biological coherence and 65 to 89 percent inhibitory dominance, invariant to architecture and cell type. scGPT produces stronger effects (mean absolute d = 1.40 vs. 1.05) with more balanced dynamics. Cross-model consensus yields 1,142 conserved domain pairs (10.6x enrichment, p < 0.001). Disease-associated domains are 3.59x more likely to be consensus. Gene-level CRISPRi validation shows 56.4 percent directional accuracy, confirming co-expression rather than causal encoding.

AI.tldr: Causal circuit tracing of SAE features in biological foundation models reveals partial biological coherence, inhibitory-dominant interactions, conserved domain pairs with disease enrichment, and co-expression rather than causal encoding.
AI.motivation: Sparse autoencoders decompose foundation model activations into interpretable features, but causal feature-to-feature interactions across depth remain unknown for biological foundation models.
AI.method: Introduce causal circuit tracing by ablating SAE features and measuring downstream responses; apply to Geneformer V2-316M and scGPT whole-human across four conditions (96,892 edges, 80,191 forward passes).
AI.result: Both models show ~53% biological coherence and 65–89% inhibitory dominance, invariant to architecture and cell type; scGPT exhibits stronger effects (mean abs d = 1.40 vs 1.05); cross-model consensus yields 1,142 conserved domain pairs (10.6x enrichment, p < 0.001); disease-associated domains are 3.59x more likely to be consensus; gene-level CRISPRi validation shows 56.4% directional accuracy, confirming co-expression rather than causal encoding.
AI.conclusion: Causal circuit tracing identifies partially conserved, inhibitory-dominant causal-like interactions across biological foundation models with disease-relevant domain enrichment; results support co-expression over direct causal encoding, and scGPT shows stronger, more balanced dynamics.

title: MM-DeepResearch: A Simple and Effective Multimodal Agentic Search Baseline
summary: We aim to develop a multimodal research agent capable of explicit reasoning and planning, multi-tool invocation, and cross-modal information synthesis, enabling it to conduct deep research tasks. However, we observe three main challenges in developing such agents: (1) scarcity of search-intensive multimodal QA data, (2) lack of effective search trajectories, and (3) prohibitive cost of training with online search APIs. To tackle them, we first propose Hyper-Search, a hypergraph-based QA generation method that models and connects visual and textual nodes within and across modalities, enabling to generate search-intensive multimodal QA pairs that require invoking various search tools to solve. Second, we introduce DR-TTS, which first decomposes search-involved tasks into several categories according to search tool types, and respectively optimize specialized search tool experts for each tool. It then recomposes tool experts to jointly explore search trajectories via tree search, producing trajectories that successfully solve complex tasks using various search tools. Third, we build an offline search engine supporting multiple search tools, enabling agentic reinforcement learning without using costly online search APIs. With the three designs, we develop MM-DeepResearch, a powerful multimodal deep research agent, and extensive results shows its superiority across benchmarks. Code is available at https://github.com/HJYao00/MM-DeepResearch

AI.tldr: Introduce MM-DeepResearch, a multimodal deep research agent built from Hyper-Search, DR-TTS, and an offline search engine, enabling explicit reasoning, tool use, and cross-modal synthesis; demonstrated superiority on benchmarks; code released.
AI.motivation: Address three bottlenecks in building search-enabled multimodal agents: (1) scarcity of search-intensive multimodal QA data, (2) lack of effective search trajectories, and (3) high cost of training with online search APIs.
AI.method: Hyper-Search uses a hypergraph to connect visual and textual nodes within and across modalities to generate search-intensive multimodal QA pairs. DR-TTS decomposes search-involved tasks by search tool type, trains specialized tool experts per tool, and recomposes them via tree search to produce effective search trajectories. An offline search engine supports multiple search tools to enable agentic RL without relying on online APIs.
AI.result: Extensive experiments demonstrate MM-DeepResearch's superiority across benchmarks; code is available at the provided GitHub link.
AI.conclusion: The three components enable explicit reasoning, planning, multi-tool invocation, and cross-modal information synthesis for deep research tasks, making offline reinforcement learning viable for powerful multimodal agents.

title: Meta-Learning Hyperparameters for Parameter Efficient Fine-Tuning
comment: Accepted by CVPR 2025 (Highlight). Code is available at: https://github.com/doem97/metalora
summary: Training large foundation models from scratch for domain-specific applications is almost impossible due to data limits and long-tailed distributions -- taking remote sensing (RS) as an example. Fine-tuning natural image pre-trained models on RS images is a straightforward solution. To reduce computational costs and improve performance on tail classes, existing methods apply parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) techniques, such as LoRA and AdaptFormer. However, we observe that fixed hyperparameters -- such as intra-layer positions, layer depth, and scaling factors, can considerably hinder PEFT performance, as fine-tuning on RS images proves highly sensitive to these settings. To address this, we propose MetaPEFT, a method incorporating adaptive scalers that dynamically adjust module influence during fine-tuning. MetaPEFT dynamically adjusts three key factors of PEFT on RS images: module insertion, layer selection, and module-wise learning rates, which collectively control the influence of PEFT modules across the network. We conduct extensive experiments on three transfer-learning scenarios and five datasets in both RS and natural image domains. The results show that MetaPEFT achieves state-of-the-art performance in cross-spectral adaptation, requiring only a small amount of trainable parameters and improving tail-class accuracy significantly.

AI.tldr: MetaPEFT uses adaptive scalers to dynamically control PEFT components during fine-tuning on remote-sensing images, achieving state-of-the-art cross-spectral adaptation with few trainable parameters.
AI.motivation: Fine-tuning pre-trained natural-image models for RS tasks is hampered by data scarcity, long-tailed distributions, and sensitive hyperparameters. Fixed PEFT settings (insertion depth, layer usage, scaling) impede performance, especially for tail classes.
AI.method: Introduce adaptive scalers that dynamically adjust module insertion, layer selection, and module-wise learning rates in PEFT, enabling the model to vary PEFT influence across the network during fine-tuning.
AI.result: Extensive experiments across three transfer-learning scenarios and five datasets (RS and natural images) show MetaPEFT achieves state-of-the-art cross-spectral adaptation with minimal trainable parameters and significantly improved tail-class accuracy.
AI.conclusion: Adaptive, dynamic control of PEFT components enhances transfer learning for RS and cross-domain scenarios, offering both accuracy gains and parameter efficiency; further exploration on generalization and scalability is warranted.

title: Unleashing VLA Potentials in Autonomous Driving via Explicit Learning from Failures
summary: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models for autonomous driving often hit a performance plateau during Reinforcement Learning (RL) optimization. This stagnation arises from exploration capabilities constrained by previous Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT), leading to persistent failures in long-tail scenarios. In these critical situations, all explored actions yield a zero-value driving score. This information-sparse reward signals a failure, yet fails to identify its root cause -- whether it is due to incorrect planning, flawed reasoning, or poor trajectory execution. To address this limitation, we propose VLA with Explicit Learning from Failures (ELF-VLA), a framework that augments RL with structured diagnostic feedback. Instead of relying on a vague scalar reward, our method produces detailed, interpretable reports that identify the specific failure mode. The VLA policy then leverages this explicit feedback to generate a Feedback-Guided Refinement. By injecting these corrected, high-reward samples back into the RL training batch, our approach provides a targeted gradient, which enables the policy to solve critical scenarios that unguided exploration cannot. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method unlocks the latent capabilities of VLA models, achieving state-of-the-art (SOTA) performance on the public NAVSIM benchmark for overall PDMS, EPDMS score and high-level planning accuracy.

AI.tldr: ELF-VLA uses explicit failure diagnostics to guide RL in Vision-Language-Action for autonomous driving, replacing scalar rewards with interpretable feedback; this Feedback-Guided Refinement injects high-reward corrected samples, achieving SOTA on NAVSIM PDMS/EPDMS and planning accuracy.
AI.motivation: RL optimization in VLA stalls due to exploration limits set by SFT, especially in long-tail scenarios where all actions score zero; difficulty diagnosing whether planning, reasoning, or execution failures cause poor performance.
AI.method: Propose ELF-VLA: generate structured diagnostic reports that identify specific failure modes; use these reports to provide Feedback-Guided Refinement; inject corrected high-reward samples into RL batch to produce targeted gradients and improve policy on critical cases.
AI.result: Empirical results show SOTA performance on NAVSIM across overall PDMS, EPDMS, and high-level planning accuracy.
AI.conclusion: Explicit failure-aware feedback can unlock latent capabilities of VLA models in autonomous driving, mitigating exploration issues and improving planning/execution.

title: Modular Memory is the Key to Continual Learning Agents
comment: This work stems from discussions held at the Dagstuhl seminar on Continual Learning in the Era of Foundation Models (October 2025)
summary: Foundation models have transformed machine learning through large-scale pretraining and increased test-time compute. Despite surpassing human performance in several domains, these models remain fundamentally limited in continuous operation, experience accumulation, and personalization, capabilities that are central to adaptive intelligence. While continual learning research has long targeted these goals, its historical focus on in-weight learning (IWL), i.e., updating a single model's parameters to absorb new knowledge, has rendered catastrophic forgetting a persistent challenge. Our position is that combining the strengths of In-Weight Learning (IWL) and the newly emerged capabilities of In-Context Learning (ICL) through the design of modular memory is the missing piece for continual adaptation at scale. We outline a conceptual framework for modular memory-centric architectures that leverage ICL for rapid adaptation and knowledge accumulation, and IWL for stable updates to model capabilities, charting a practical roadmap toward continually learning agents.

AI.tldr: Proposes modular memory architectures that fuse In-Weight Learning (IWL) and In-Context Learning (ICL) to enable continual adaptation at scale, aiming to mitigate catastrophic forgetting.
AI.motivation: Foundation models excel in static tasks but struggle with continuous operation, experience accumulation, and personalization. IWL alone leads to forgetting; integrating ICL for rapid adaptation and memory assembly could enable continual learning.
AI.method: Conceptual framework for modular memory-centric architectures that leverage ICL for rapid adaptation and knowledge accumulation and IWL for stable updates to capabilities; outlines design principles and a practical roadmap toward continually learning agents.
AI.result: Conceptual framework and roadmap rather than empirical results; articulates integration strategy, potential benefits, and research directions for combining IWL and ICL in modular memory.
AI.conclusion: Modular memory that combines IWL and ICL is proposed as the missing piece for scalable continual adaptation, enabling continually learning agents through synergistic memory design.

title: LLaDA-o: An Effective and Length-Adaptive Omni Diffusion Model
summary: We present \textbf{LLaDA-o}, an effective and length-adaptive omni diffusion model for multimodal understanding and generation. LLaDA-o is built on a Mixture of Diffusion (MoD) framework that decouples discrete masked diffusion for text understanding and continuous diffusion for visual generation, while coupling them through a shared, simple, and efficient attention backbone that reduces redundant computation for fixed conditions. Building on MoD, we further introduce a data-centric length adaptation strategy that enables flexible-length decoding in multimodal settings without architectural changes. Extensive experiments show that LLaDA-o achieves state-of-the-art performance among omni-diffusion models on multimodal understanding and generation benchmarks, and reaches 87.04 on DPG-Bench for text-to-image generation, supporting the effectiveness of unified omni diffusion modeling. Code is available at https://github.com/ML-GSAI/LLaDA-o.

AI.tldr: A length-adaptive omni diffusion model (LLaDA-o) using a Mixture of Diffusion (MoD) to separate text understanding (discrete masked diffusion) and visual generation (continuous diffusion) with a shared attention backbone; introduces data-centric length adaptation for flexible-length decoding; achieves state-of-the-art in omni-diffusion benchmarks and 87.04 on DPG-Bench for text-to-image generation; code released.
AI.motivation: Address the demand for a unified, efficient framework for multimodal understanding and generation that can handle variable-length outputs without heavy architectural changes, and reduce redundant computation for fixed conditions.
AI.method: Propose MoD with discrete diffusion for text and continuous diffusion for images, coupled via a shared attention backbone; add a data-centric length adaptation strategy that enables flexible-length decoding without changing the architecture; evaluate on multimodal benchmarks.
AI.result: State-of-the-art among omni-diffusion models on multimodal understanding and generation benchmarks; 87.04 on DPG-Bench for text-to-image generation.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates effectiveness of unified omni diffusion modeling and the viability of length-adaptive decoding in multimodal diffusion tasks; provides code.

title: DGNet: Discrete Green Networks for Data-Efficient Learning of Spatiotemporal PDEs
comment: Accepted as a conference paper at ICLR 2026
summary: Spatiotemporal partial differential equations (PDEs) underpin a wide range of scientific and engineering applications. Neural PDE solvers offer a promising alternative to classical numerical methods. However, existing approaches typically require large numbers of training trajectories, while high-fidelity PDE data are expensive to generate. Under limited data, their performance degrades substantially, highlighting their low data efficiency. A key reason is that PDE dynamics embody strong structural inductive biases that are not explicitly encoded in neural architectures, forcing models to learn fundamental physical structure from data. A particularly salient manifestation of this inefficiency is poor generalization to unseen source terms. In this work, we revisit Green's function theory-a cornerstone of PDE theory-as a principled source of structural inductive bias for PDE learning. Based on this insight, we propose DGNet, a discrete Green network for data-efficient learning of spatiotemporal PDEs. The key idea is to transform the Green's function into a graph-based discrete formulation, and embed the superposition principle into the hybrid physics-neural architecture, which reduces the burden of learning physical priors from data, thereby improving sample efficiency. Across diverse spatiotemporal PDE scenarios, DGNet consistently achieves state-of-the-art accuracy using only tens of training trajectories. Moreover, it exhibits robust zero-shot generalization to unseen source terms, serving as a stress test that highlights its data-efficient structural design.

AI.tldr: DGNet uses a discrete Green's function on a graph to enforce the superposition principle, providing a physics-informed inductive bias for spatiotemporal PDE learning. This yields high data efficiency and robust zero-shot generalization to unseen source terms, achieving state-of-the-art accuracy with only tens of trajectories.
AI.motivation: Neural PDE solvers struggle with data efficiency because they lack explicit physical priors. PDE dynamics have strong structural inductive biases (Green's function, linear superposition) that are not explicitly encoded in standard architectures. Incorporating these priors could reduce data requirements and improve generalization, especially to unseen forcing.
AI.method: Transform Green's function into a discrete graph-based formulation and embed the superposition principle within a hybrid physics–neural network architecture. This DGNet framework leverages the discrete Green's function to represent PDE responses and allows learning of residuals or complex components with reduced data needs.
AI.result: DGNet consistently achieves state-of-the-art accuracy across diverse spatiotemporal PDE tasks using only tens of training trajectories. It also demonstrates robust zero-shot generalization to unseen source terms, indicating improved data efficiency and structural generalization.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating classical PDE structure (Green's function and superposition) as a discrete, graph-based inductive bias yields substantial gains in data efficiency and zero-shot generalization for neural PDE solvers.

title: Flow Matching-enabled Test-Time Refinement for Unsupervised Cardiac MR Registration
summary: Diffusion-based unsupervised image registration has been explored for cardiac cine MR, but expensive multi-step inference limits practical use. We propose FlowReg, a flow-matching framework in displacement field space that achieves strong registration in as few as two steps and supports further refinement with more steps. FlowReg uses warmup-reflow training: a single-step network first acts as a teacher, then a student learns to refine from arbitrary intermediate states, removing the need for a pre-trained model as in existing methods. An Initial Guess strategy feeds back the model prediction as the next starting point, improving refinement from step two onward. On ACDC and MM2 across six tasks (including cross-dataset generalization), FlowReg outperforms the state of the art on five tasks (+0.6% mean Dice score on average), with the largest gain in the left ventricle (+1.09%), and reduces LVEF estimation error on all six tasks (-2.58 percentage points), using only 0.7% extra parameters and no segmentation labels. Code is available at https://github.com/mathpluscode/FlowReg.

AI.tldr: FlowReg is a diffusion-based unsupervised cardiac cine MRI registration method that achieves strong alignment with as few as two steps by learning in displacement-field space. It uses warmup-reflow training and an Initial Guess feedback loop to refine from intermediate states without a pre-trained teacher, achieving state-of-the-art on ACDC/MM2 across six tasks with minimal parameter overhead and no segmentation labels.
AI.motivation: Reduce the computational burden of diffusion-based image registration in cardiac cine MRI, eliminate dependence on pre-trained teacher models, and improve cross-dataset generalization and LVEF accuracy.
AI.method: FlowReg employs a flow-matching framework in displacement-field space. It uses warmup-reflow training where a single-step network acts as a teacher and a student learns to refine from arbitrary intermediate states, removing the need for a pre-trained model. An Initial Guess strategy feeds back the model prediction as the next starting point, enabling effective refinement from step two onward. The framework supports as few as two steps and can be extended with additional steps for further refinement.
AI.result: FlowReg outperforms the state of the art on five of six tasks across ACDC and MM2 datasets, with an average mean Dice improvement of +0.6%, the largest LV gain of +1.09%, and a reduction in LVEF estimation error by 2.58 percentage points across six tasks. It uses only 0.7% extra parameters and requires no segmentation labels.
AI.conclusion: FlowReg delivers fast, accurate, and label-free unsupervised cardiac image registration with strong generalization and minimal model overhead, representing a practical advance for diffusion-based registration in clinical MRI.

title: CHLU: The Causal Hamiltonian Learning Unit as a Symplectic Primitive for Deep Learning
comment: Accepted as a short paper at ICLR 2026 (AI & PDE)
summary: Current deep learning primitives dealing with temporal dynamics suffer from a fundamental dichotomy: they are either discrete and unstable (LSTMs) \citep{pascanu_difficulty_2013}, leading to exploding or vanishing gradients; or they are continuous and dissipative (Neural ODEs) \citep{dupont_augmented_2019}, which destroy information over time to ensure stability. We propose the \textbf{Causal Hamiltonian Learning Unit} (pronounced: \textit{clue}), a novel Physics-grounded computational learning primitive. By enforcing a Relativistic Hamiltonian structure and utilizing symplectic integration, a CHLU strictly conserves phase-space volume, as an attempt to solve the memory-stability trade-off. We show that the CHLU is designed for infinite-horizon stability, as well as controllable noise filtering. We then demonstrate a CHLU's generative ability using the MNIST dataset as a proof-of-principle.

AI.tldr: CHLU is a physics-grounded, Hamiltonian-based learning unit that conserves phase-space volume via symplectic integration to achieve infinite-horizon stability and noise filtering, demonstrated as a MNIST-generative proof-of-principle.
AI.motivation: Current temporal models fall into a dichotomy: discrete models like LSTMs are unstable (vanishing/exploding gradients), while continuous models like Neural ODEs dissipate information for stability. A physics-guided, volume-preserving approach aims to retain information while enabling long-horizon stability.
AI.method: Impose a Relativistic Hamiltonian structure and use symplectic integration to strictly conserve phase-space volume, aiming for infinite-horizon stability with controllable noise filtering; validate presence of generative ability on MNIST as proof-of-principle.
AI.result: The approach achieves phase-space volume conservation, demonstrates infinite-horizon stability and noise filtering, and shows MNIST-based generative capability as a proof-of-principle.
AI.conclusion: CHLU offers a promising physics-grounded primitive for temporal learning that addresses memory-stability trade-offs; further work needed to assess practicality, scalability, and applicability to diverse tasks and data modalities.

title: Hyperparameter Trajectory Inference with Conditional Lagrangian Optimal Transport
summary: Neural networks (NNs) often have critical behavioural trade-offs that are set at design time with hyperparameters-such as reward weights in reinforcement learning or quantile targets in regression. Post-deployment, however, user preferences can evolve, making initial settings undesirable, necessitating potentially expensive retraining. To circumvent this, we introduce the task of Hyperparameter Trajectory Inference (HTI): to learn, from observed data, how a NN's conditional output distribution changes with its hyperparameters, and construct a surrogate model that approximates the NN at unobserved hyperparameter settings. HTI requires extending existing trajectory inference approaches to incorporate conditions, exacerbating the challenge of ensuring inferred paths are feasible. We propose an approach based on conditional Lagrangian optimal transport, jointly learning the Lagrangian function governing hyperparameter-induced dynamics along with the associated optimal transport maps and geodesics between observed marginals, which form the surrogate model. We incorporate inductive biases based on the manifold hypothesis and least-action principles into the learned Lagrangian, improving surrogate model feasibility. We empirically demonstrate that our approach reconstructs NN outputs across various hyperparameter spectra better than other alternatives.

AI.tldr: HTI learns conditional hyperparameter–dependent output distributions via conditional Lagrangian OT to build a surrogate across unobserved hyperparameters.
AI.motivation: Hyperparameters steer NN behavior and user preferences can drift post-deployment; retraining to adapt is costly, so a predictive surrogate over hyperparameters is desirable.
AI.method: Extend trajectory inference to conditioning; propose conditional Lagrangian optimal transport; jointly learn the Lagrangian, OT maps, and geodesics between observed marginals to form the surrogate; impose inductive biases from the manifold hypothesis and least-action principles to improve feasibility.
AI.result: Empirically, the approach reconstructs NN outputs across a range of hyperparameters more accurately than competing alternatives.
AI.conclusion: Conditional OT–based HTI yields feasible, accurate surrogates for hyperparameter-induced dynamics, enabling offline adaptation without retraining and offering a foundation for broader hyperparameter-aware model maintenance.

title: Beyond Global Similarity: Towards Fine-Grained, Multi-Condition Multimodal Retrieval
summary: Recent advances in multimodal large language models (MLLMs) have substantially expanded the capabilities of multimodal retrieval, enabling systems to align and retrieve information across visual and textual modalities. Yet, existing benchmarks largely focus on coarse-grained or single-condition alignment, overlooking real-world scenarios where user queries specify multiple interdependent constraints across modalities. To bridge this gap, we introduce MCMR (Multi-Conditional Multimodal Retrieval): a large-scale benchmark designed to evaluate fine-grained, multi-condition cross-modal retrieval under natural-language queries. MCMR spans five product domains: upper and bottom clothing, jewelry, shoes, and furniture. It also preserves rich long-form metadata essential for compositional matching. Each query integrates complementary visual and textual attributes, requiring models to jointly satisfy all specified conditions for relevance. We benchmark a diverse suite of MLLM-based multimodal retrievers and vision-language rerankers to assess their condition-aware reasoning abilities. Experimental results reveal: (i) distinct modality asymmetries across models; (ii) visual cues dominate early-rank precision, while textual metadata stabilizes long-tail ordering; and (iii) MLLM-based pointwise rerankers markedly improve fine-grained matching by explicitly verifying query-candidate consistency. Overall, MCMR establishes a challenging and diagnostic benchmark for advancing multimodal retrieval toward compositional, constraint-aware, and interpretable understanding. Our code and dataset is available at https://github.com/EIT-NLP/MCMR

AI.tldr: A large-scale multi-conditional multimodal retrieval benchmark (MCMR) for five product domains, evaluating fine-grained cross-modal retrieval with long-form metadata; findings emphasize modality asymmetries and benefits of pointwise reranking.
AI.motivation: Existing benchmarks focus on coarse-grained or single-condition alignment and fail to capture real-world scenarios with multiple interdependent cross-modal constraints; there is a need for a compositional, constraint-aware evaluation of multimodal retrieval.
AI.method: Introduce MCMR dataset spanning five domains (upper/bottom clothing, jewelry, shoes, furniture) with rich long-form metadata; design natural-language queries that encode multiple complementary visual and textual attributes; benchmark a suite of MLLM-based multimodal retrievers and vision-language rerankers; report analyses and provide code/dataset.
AI.result: Visual cues drive early-rank precision while textual metadata stabilizes long-tail ordering; cross-modal alignment is constrained by modality asymmetries across models; pointwise MLLM-based rerankers significantly improve fine-grained matching by verifying query-candidate consistency.
AI.conclusion: MCMR is a challenging, diagnostic benchmark that encourages advances toward compositional, constraint-aware, and interpretable multimodal retrieval.

title: D3LM: A Discrete DNA Diffusion Language Model for Bidirectional DNA Understanding and Generation
comment: Accepted as a workshop paper at MLGenX 2026
summary: Early DNA foundation models adopted BERT-style training, achieving good performance on DNA understanding tasks but lacking generative capabilities. Recent autoregressive models enable DNA generation, but employ left-to-right causal modeling that is suboptimal for DNA where regulatory relationships are inherently bidirectional. We present D3LM (\textbf{D}iscrete \textbf{D}NA \textbf{D}iffusion \textbf{L}anguage \textbf{M}odel), which unifies bidirectional representation learning and DNA generation through masked diffusion. D3LM directly adopts the Nucleotide Transformer (NT) v2 architecture but reformulates the training objective as masked diffusion in discrete DNA space, enabling both bidirectional understanding and generation capabilities within a single model. Compared to NT v2 of the same size, D3LM achieves improved performance on understanding tasks. Notably, on regulatory element generation, D3LM achieves an SFID of 10.92, closely approaching real DNA sequences (7.85) and substantially outperforming the previous best result of 29.16 from autoregressive models. Our work suggests diffusion language models as a promising paradigm for unified DNA foundation models. We further present the first systematic study of masked diffusion models in the DNA domain, investigating practical design choices such as tokenization schemes and sampling strategies, thereby providing empirical insights and a solid foundation for future research. D3LM has been released at https://huggingface.co/collections/Hengchang-Liu/d3lm.

AI.tldr: D3LM is a diffusion-based, bidirectional DNA language model that unifies understanding and generation by reformulating masked diffusion in discrete DNA space, outperforming autoregressive methods on regulatory element generation.
AI.motivation: Early DNA foundation models trained like BERT excel at understanding but lack generative capabilities; autoregressive DNA models enable generation but rely on left-to-right causality, which is suboptimal for bidirectional regulatory relationships.
AI.method: Adopts the Nucleotide Transformer (NT) v2 architecture and reframes training as masked diffusion in discrete DNA space, enabling bidirectional understanding and generation. Systematically studies tokenization schemes and sampling strategies and evaluates on regulatory element generation with SFID metrics.
AI.result: Outperforms NT v2 on understanding tasks. For regulatory element generation, achieves SFID=10.92 (close to real DNA at 7.85) and substantially better than the previous autoregressive best of 29.16. Provides the first systematic study of masked diffusion in DNA and releases the model to the community.
AI.conclusion: Diffusion language models are a promising unified paradigm for DNA foundation models, offering concrete guidance on design choices and establishing a foundation for future research.

title: Can Vision Language Models Assess Graphic Design Aesthetics? A Benchmark, Evaluation, and Dataset Perspective
summary: Assessing the aesthetic quality of graphic design is central to visual communication, yet remains underexplored in vision language models (VLMs). We investigate whether VLMs can evaluate design aesthetics in ways comparable to humans. Prior work faces three key limitations: benchmarks restricted to narrow principles and coarse evaluation protocols, a lack of systematic VLM comparisons, and limited training data for model improvement. In this work, we introduce AesEval-Bench, a comprehensive benchmark spanning four dimensions, twelve indicators, and three fully quantifiable tasks: aesthetic judgment, region selection, and precise localization. Then, we systematically evaluate proprietary, open-source, and reasoning-augmented VLMs, revealing clear performance gaps against the nuanced demands of aesthetic assessment. Moreover, we construct a training dataset to fine-tune VLMs for this domain, leveraging human-guided VLM labeling to produce task labels at scale and indicator-grounded reasoning to tie abstract indicators to concrete design regions.Together, our work establishes the first systematic framework for aesthetic quality assessment in graphic design. Our code and dataset will be released at: \href{https://github.com/arctanxarc/AesEval-Bench}{https://github.com/arctanxarc/AesEval-Bench}

AI.tldr: AesEval-Bench introduces the first comprehensive benchmark for aesthetic assessment in graphic design for vision-language models, spanning four dimensions, twelve indicators, and three quantifiable tasks (aesthetic judgment, region selection, precise localization). It systematically evaluates diverse VLMs, identifies performance gaps, and provides a training dataset with human-guided labeling and indicator-grounded reasoning to fine-tune models. Code and data will be released.
AI.motivation: Aesthetic quality assessment is central to visual communication but underexplored in VLMs. Existing benchmarks cover narrow principles, lack systematic VLM comparisons, and suffer from limited training data for model improvement.
AI.method: Define AesEval-Bench with four dimensions, twelve indicators, and three tasks. Perform systematic evaluations across proprietary, open-source, and reasoning-augmented VLMs. Create a training dataset using human-guided VLM labeling to produce task labels at scale and develop indicator-grounded reasoning that ties abstract indicators to concrete design regions. Release code and dataset.
AI.result: Finds clear performance gaps between current VLMs and the nuanced demands of aesthetic assessment. Demonstrates the utility of a structured benchmark and targeted fine-tuning data. Specific quantitative results are not provided in the abstract.
AI.conclusion: Establishes the first systematic framework for aesthetic quality assessment in graphic design and provides resources for future research and model improvement in this domain.

title: Learning Shortest Paths with Generative Flow Networks
summary: In this paper, we present a novel learning framework for finding shortest paths in graphs utilizing Generative Flow Networks (GFlowNets). First, we examine theoretical properties of GFlowNets in non-acyclic environments in relation to shortest paths. We prove that, if the total flow is minimized, forward and backward policies traverse the environment graph exclusively along shortest paths between the initial and terminal states.
