Building on this result, we show that the pathfinding problem in an arbitrary graph can be solved by training a non-acyclic GFlowNet with flow regularization. We experimentally demonstrate the performance of our method in pathfinding in permutation environments and in solving Rubik's Cubes. For the latter problem, our approach shows competitive results with state-of-the-art machine learning approaches designed specifically for this task in terms of the solution length, while requiring smaller search budget at test-time.

AI.tldr: Non-acyclic GFlowNets with flow regularization provably enforce shortest-path behavior when total flow is minimized, enabling effective graph pathfinding and competitive Rubik's Cube solutions with reduced test-time search.
AI.motivation: Address shortest-path discovery in graphs using GFlowNets, including non-acyclic environments, to obtain optimal or near-optimal paths while leveraging generative sampling and potential efficiency gains over traditional methods.
AI.method: Theoretically show that minimizing total flow causes forward/backward policies to traverse only shortest paths between initial and terminal states in the environment graph. Propose training a non-acyclic GFlowNet with flow regularization to solve pathfinding in arbitrary graphs. Empirically evaluate on pathfinding in permutation environments and on solving Rubik's Cubes.
AI.result: Proved property that minimized total flow aligns policies with shortest paths. Empirical results show competitive performance with state-of-the-art ML approaches on Rubik's Cube in terms of solution length, with a smaller search budget at test time; successful pathfinding in permutation environments.
AI.conclusion: The framework provides a principled, general approach to graph pathfinding via non-acyclic GFlowNets with flow regularization, achieving efficient search and competitive solution quality in combinatorial tasks; invites further exploration of regularization and non-acyclic dynamics.

title: Unified Vision-Language Modeling via Concept Space Alignment
summary:
We introduce V-SONAR, a vision-language embedding space extended from the text-only embedding space SONAR (Omnilingual Embeddings Team et al., 2026), which supports 1500 text languages and 177 speech languages. To construct V-SONAR, we propose a post-hoc alignment pipeline that maps the representations of an existing vision encoder into the SONAR space. We thoroughly evaluate V-SONAR and show that its embeddings achieve competitive performance on text-to-video retrieval. Equipped with the OMNISONAR text decoder, V-SONAR further surpasses state-of-the-art vision-language models on video captioning tasks, including DREAM-1K (BLEU 23.9 vs. 19.6) and PE-VIDEO (BLEU 39.0 vs. 30.0).
  Leveraging V-SONAR, we first demonstrate that the Large Concept Model (LCM; LCM team et al. 2024) operating in SONAR and trained with English text only, can perform both single- and multi-visual concept understanding in a zero-shot manner. Finally, we introduce V-LCM, which extends the LCM with vision-language instruction tuning. V-LCM encodes vision and language inputs into an unified sequence of latent embeddings via V-SONAR and SONAR, and it is trained with the same latent diffusion objective for next-embedding prediction as in LCM's text-only pre-training. Experiments on a large-scale multilingual and -modal instruction-tuning data mixture highlight the potential of V-LCM: V-LCM matches state-of-the-art vision-language models on tasks covering image/video captioning and question answering, while significantly outperforming them across 61 rich- to low-resource languages out of all 62 tested languages.

AI.tldr: We present V-SONAR, a vision-language embedding space derived by post-hoc aligning a vision encoder into the SONAR (Omnilingual Embeddings) space, enabling 1500 text languages and 177 speech languages. It achieves competitive text-to-video retrieval and, with the OMNISONAR text decoder, surpasses SOTA on video captioning (DREAM-1K: BLEU 23.9 vs. 19.6; PE-VIDEO: BLEU 39.0 vs. 30.0). Building on this, the Large Concept Model (LCM) operating in SONAR can perform zero-shot single- and multi-visual concept understanding. The authors introduce V-LCM, extending LCM with vision-language instruction tuning. V-LCM encodes vision and language inputs into a unified sequence of latent embeddings via V-SONAR and SONAR, trained with the same latent-diffusion objective for next-embedding prediction as LCM. On a large multilingual/multimodal instruction-tuning dataset, V-LCM matches SOTA on image/video captioning and question answering, while significantly outperforming them across 61 of 62 tested languages (from rich to low-resource).
AI.motivation: Extend multilingual, multi-modal AI capabilities by leveraging a shared, text-focused embedding space (SONAR) for vision-language tasks, enabling extensive language coverage via post-hoc alignment to reduce training from scratch and improve cross-lingual performance.
AI.method: Post-hoc alignment maps vision encoder representations into the SONAR space to form V-SONAR; use OMNISONAR text decoder for decoding; introduce V-LCM that encodes vision and language into a unified latent embedding sequence via V-SONAR and SONAR, trained with latent diffusion for next-embedding prediction; train on a large multilingual/multimodal instruction dataset.
AI.result: V-SONAR yields competitive text-to-video retrieval; with OMNISONAR, achieves state-of-the-art video captioning (DREAM-1K BLEU 23.9 vs. 19.6; PE-VIDEO BLEU 39.0 vs. 30.0); LCM in SONAR demonstrates zero-shot multi-visual concept understanding; V-LCM matches SOTA on captioning and QA and substantially outperforms other models across 61 of 62 languages.
AI.conclusion: A unified, multilingual vision-language framework built on post-hoc alignment to SONAR can achieve strong cross-modal, cross-language performance without full retraining, validating the effectiveness of V-SONAR and V-LCM for broad multilingual multimodal tasks.

title: Phase-Type Variational Autoencoders for Heavy-Tailed Data
summary:
Heavy-tailed distributions are ubiquitous in real-world data, where rare but extreme events dominate risk and variability. However, standard Variational Autoencoders (VAEs) employ simple decoder distributions (e.g., Gaussian) that fail to capture heavy-tailed behavior, while existing heavy-tail-aware extensions remain restricted to predefined parametric families whose tail behavior is fixed a priori.
  We propose the Phase-Type Variational Autoencoder (PH-VAE), whose decoder distribution is a latent-conditioned Phase-Type (PH) distribution defined as the absorption time of a continuous-time Markov chain (CTMC). This formulation composes multiple exponential time scales, yielding a flexible and analytically tractable decoder that adapts its tail behavior directly from the observed data. Experiments on synthetic and real-world benchmarks demonstrate that PH-VAE accurately recovers diverse heavy-tailed distributions, significantly outperforming Gaussian, Student-t, and extreme-value-based VAE decoders in modeling tail behavior and extreme quantiles. In multivariate settings, PH-VAE captures realistic cross-dimensional tail dependence through its shared latent representation. To our knowledge, this is the first work to integrate Phase-Type distributions into deep generative modeling, bridging applied probability and representation learning.

AI.tldr: PH-VAE uses latent-conditioned Phase-Type distribution (absorption time of CTMC) as decoder to flexibly model heavy tails in VAEs, outperforming Gaussian/Student-t/EVT decoders and capturing cross-dimensional tail dependence; first integration of Phase-Type in deep generative models.
AI.motivation: Heavy-tailed data are common; Gaussian decoders miss tail behavior; existing heavy-tail VAEs rely on pre-specified tail families; need flexible, tractable tails learned from data.
AI.method: Decoder is latent-conditioned Phase-Type distribution, defined as CTMC absorption time; combines multiple exponential timescales; tractable analytic form; latent conditioning enables tail adaptation; enables cross-dimensional tail dependence via shared latent; training within VAE framework.
AI.result: Empirical results on synthetic and real data show accurate recovery of diverse heavy-tail distributions; outperforms Gaussian, Student-t, EVT-based decoders in tails and extreme quantiles; in multivariate data, captures tail dependence.
AI.conclusion: First integration of Phase-Type distributions in deep generative modeling; bridges applied probability and representation learning; flexible, interpretable approach to heavy-tailed modeling; potential for broader impact.

title: Differential privacy representation geometry for medical image analysis
summary: Differential privacy (DP)'s effect in medical imaging is typically evaluated only through end-to-end performance, leaving the mechanism of privacy-induced utility loss unclear. We introduce Differential Privacy Representation Geometry for Medical Imaging (DP-RGMI), a framework that interprets DP as a structured transformation of representation space and decomposes performance degradation into encoder geometry and task-head utilization. Geometry is quantified by representation displacement from initialization and spectral effective dimension, while utilization is measured as the gap between linear-probe and end-to-end utility. Across over 594,000 images from four chest X-ray datasets and multiple pretrained initializations, we show that DP is consistently associated with a utilization gap even when linear separability is largely preserved. At the same time, displacement and spectral dimension exhibit non-monotonic, initialization- and dataset-dependent reshaping, indicating that DP alters representation anisotropy rather than uniformly collapsing features. Correlation analysis reveals that the association between end-to-end performance and utilization is robust across datasets but can vary by initialization, while geometric quantities capture additional prior- and dataset-conditioned variation. These findings position DP-RGMI as a reproducible framework for diagnosing privacy-induced failure modes and informing privacy model selection.

AI.tldr: DP-RGMI is a framework that analyzes privacy-induced utility loss in medical imaging by decomposing it into representation geometry and task-head utilization, revealing non-monotonic geometry changes and a persistent utilization gap across large chest X-ray datasets.
AI.motivation: To illuminate the mechanism of differential privacy–induced utility degradation in medical imaging beyond end-to-end metrics by interpreting DP as a structured transformation of representation space.
AI.method: Introduce DP-RGMI, quantify geometry via representation displacement from initialization and spectral effective dimension, and quantify utilization via the gap between linear-probe and end-to-end performance. Validate across 594k images from four chest X-ray datasets with multiple pretrained initializations; analyze correlations across datasets and initializations.
AI.result: DP shows a consistent utilization gap even when linear separability is largely preserved. Displacement and spectral dimension reshaping are non-monotonic and depend on initialization/dataset, indicating DP alters representation anisotropy rather than collapsing features. End-to-end performance–utilization correlation is robust across datasets but varies with initialization; geometric quantities capture extra prior- and dataset-conditioned variation.
AI.conclusion: DP-RGMI provides a reproducible diagnostic framework for privacy-induced failure modes in medical imaging and can inform privacy model selection.

title: Uncertainty Quantification of Click and Conversion Estimates for the Autobidding
comment: 17 pages (10 main text + 7 appendix), 5 figures, 2 tables
summary: Modern e-commerce platforms employ various auction mechanisms to allocate paid slots for a given item. To scale this approach to the millions of auctions, the platforms suggest promotion tools based on the autobidding algorithms. These algorithms typically depend on the Click-Through-Rate (CTR) and Conversion-Rate (CVR) estimates provided by a pre-trained machine learning model. However, the predictions of such models are uncertain and can significantly affect the performance of the autobidding algorithm. To address this issue, we propose the DenoiseBid method, which corrects the generated CTRs and CVRs to make the resulting bids more efficient in auctions. The underlying idea of our method is to employ a Bayesian approach and replace noisy CTR or CVR estimates with those from recovered distributions. To demonstrate the performance of the proposed approach, we perform extensive experiments on the synthetic, iPinYou, and BAT datasets. To evaluate the robustness of our approach to the noise scale, we use synthetic noise and noise estimated from the predictions of the pre-trained machine learning model.

AI.tldr: A Bayesian denoising framework (DenoiseBid) improves autobid efficiency by replacing uncertain CTR/CVR with samples from recovered distributions, showing gains across synthetic, iPinYou, and BAT datasets under varying noise.
AI.motivation: Prediction uncertainty in CTR/CVR from ML models can significantly degrade autobidding performance; a denoising approach aims to stabilize bids and improve auction outcomes.
AI.method: DenoiseBid employs a Bayesian approach to substitute noisy CTR/CVR estimates with samples from recovered posterior distributions; denoised estimates feed the autobidding strategy; robustness assessed with synthetic noise and noise derived from model predictions across synthetic, iPinYou, and BAT datasets.
AI.result: The method yields improved bidding efficiency and robustness to noise scales, demonstrated consistently across the three datasets.
AI.conclusion: Bayesian denoising effectively mitigates adverse effects of CTR/CVR prediction uncertainty on autobidding; results suggest general applicability and potential extensions to broader forecast-based bidding systems.

title: HeroGS: Hierarchical Guidance for Robust 3D Gaussian Splatting under Sparse Views
summary: 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) has recently emerged as a promising approach in novel view synthesis, combining photorealistic rendering with real-time efficiency. However, its success heavily relies on dense camera coverage; under sparse-view conditions, insufficient supervision leads to irregular Gaussian distributions, characterized by globally sparse coverage, blurred background, and distorted high-frequency areas. To address this, we propose HeroGS, Hierarchical Guidance for Robust 3D Gaussian Splatting, a unified framework that establishes hierarchical guidance across the image, feature, and parameter levels. At the image level, sparse supervision is converted into pseudo-dense guidance, globally regularizing the Gaussian distributions and forming a consistent foundation for subsequent optimization. Building upon this, Feature-Adaptive Densification and Pruning (FADP) at the feature level leverages low-level features to refine high-frequency details and adaptively densifies Gaussians in background regions. The optimized distributions then support Co-Pruned Geometry Consistency (CPG) at parameter level, which guides geometric consistency through parameter freezing and co-pruning, effectively removing inconsistent splats. The hierarchical guidance strategy effectively constrains and optimizes the overall Gaussian distributions, thereby enhancing both structural fidelity and rendering quality. Extensive experiments demonstrate that HeroGS achieves high-fidelity reconstructions and consistently surpasses state-of-the-art baselines under sparse-view conditions.

AI.tldr: HeroGS uses hierarchical guidance across image, feature, and parameter levels to robustify 3D Gaussian Splatting under sparse views, via pseudo-dense supervision, feature-adaptive densification/pruning, and co-pruned geometry consistency.
AI.motivation: 3D Gaussian Splatting performs well with dense camera coverage but struggles under sparse views, leading to irregular, globally sparse Gaussians, blurred backgrounds, and distorted high-frequency areas.
AI.method: A three-level framework: (1) image-level pseudo-dense supervision to globally regularize Gaussian distributions; (2) feature-level FADP (Feature-Adaptive Densification and Pruning) using low-level features to refine high-frequency details and densify Gaussians in background regions; (3) parameter-level Co-Pruned Geometry Consistency (CPG) with parameter freezing and co-pruning to enforce geometric consistency by removing inconsistent splats.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show HeroGS achieves high-fidelity reconstructions and consistently surpasses state-of-the-art baselines under sparse-view conditions.
AI.conclusion: Hierarchical guidance effectively constrains and optimizes Gaussian distributions, improving structural fidelity and rendering quality in sparse-view 3D Gaussian Splatting.

title: Constrained Particle Seeking: Solving Diffusion Inverse Problems with Just Forward Passes
summary: Diffusion models have gained prominence as powerful generative tools for solving inverse problems due to their ability to model complex data distributions. However, existing methods typically rely on complete knowledge of the forward observation process to compute gradients for guided sampling, limiting their applicability in scenarios where such information is unavailable. In this work, we introduce \textbf{\emph{Constrained Particle Seeking (CPS)}}, a novel gradient-free approach that leverages all candidate particle information to actively search for the optimal particle while incorporating constraints aligned with high-density regions of the unconditional prior. Unlike previous methods that passively select promising candidates, CPS reformulates the inverse problem as a constrained optimization task, enabling more flexible and efficient particle seeking. We demonstrate that CPS can effectively solve both image and scientific inverse problems, achieving results comparable to gradient-based methods while significantly outperforming gradient-free alternatives. Code is available at https://github.com/deng-ai-lab/CPS.

AI.tldr: Constrained Particle Seeking (CPS) is a gradient-free method for diffusion-model–based inverse problems that actively searches for the optimal particle under prior-density constraints, matching gradient-based methods and outperforming other gradient-free approaches.
AI.motivation: Remove the need for forward-model gradients by leveraging diffusion priors, enabling inverse problems when the forward observation process is unknown or unavailable.
AI.method: CPS formulates inversion as a constrained optimization over candidate particles, uses all candidates, and enforces constraints aligned with high-density regions of the unconditional prior, enabling gradient-free, active particle seeking.
AI.result: Demonstrates effectiveness on image and scientific inverse problems, achieving performance comparable to gradient-based methods and substantially better than gradient-free baselines; code available.
AI.conclusion: CPS provides a flexible, efficient gradient-free alternative for diffusion-based inverses, broadening applicability to diverse domains.

title: Data-Efficient Brushstroke Generation with Diffusion Models for Oil Painting
summary: Many creative multimedia systems are built upon visual primitives such as strokes or textures, which are difficult to collect at scale and fundamentally different from natural image data. This data scarcity makes it challenging for modern generative models to learn expressive and controllable primitives, limiting their use in process-aware content creation. In this work, we study the problem of learning human-like brushstroke generation from a small set of hand-drawn samples (n=470) and propose StrokeDiff, a diffusion-based framework with Smooth Regularization (SmR). SmR injects stochastic visual priors during training, providing a simple mechanism to stabilize diffusion models under sparse supervision without altering the inference process. We further show how the learned primitives can be made controllable through a Bézier-based conditioning module and integrated into a complete stroke-based painting pipeline, including prediction, generation, ordering, and compositing. This demonstrates how data-efficient primitive modeling can support expressive and structured multimedia content creation. Experiments indicate that the proposed approach produces diverse and structurally coherent brushstrokes and enables paintings with richer texture and layering, validated by both automatic metrics and human evaluation.

AI.tldr: StrokeDiff enables data-efficient brushstroke learning from a small sample set using diffusion with Smooth Regularization, plus Bézier-conditioned controllability, integrated into a stroke-based painting pipeline.
AI.motivation: Visual primitives like strokes/textures are scarce and hard to collect at scale; existing models struggle to learn expressive, controllable primitives from limited data, hindering process-aware content creation.
AI.method: StrokeDiff is a diffusion-based framework with Smooth Regularization (SmR) that injects stochastic visual priors during training to stabilize diffusion under sparse supervision. Inference remains unchanged. A Bézier-based conditioning module provides controllability, and the approach is integrated into a full stroke-based painting pipeline (prediction, generation, ordering, compositing).
AI.result: The method yields diverse, structurally coherent brushstrokes and enables paintings with richer texture and layering. Evaluations include automatic metrics and human judgments.
AI.conclusion: Data-efficient primitive modeling via StrokeDiff with SmR demonstrates potential for expressive, structured multimedia content creation, with training stability under sparse supervision and controllable brushstroke generation.

title: Trivial Graph Features and Classical Learning are Enough to Detect Random Anomalies
summary: Detecting anomalies in link streams that represent various kinds of interactions is an important research topic with crucial applications. Because of the lack of ground truth data, proposed methods are mostly evaluated through their ability to detect randomly injected links. In contrast with most proposed methods, that rely on complex approaches raising computational and/or interpretability issues, we show here that trivial graph features and classical learning techniques are sufficient to detect such anomalies extremely well. This basic approach has very low computational costs and it leads to easily interpretable results. It also has many other desirable properties that we study through an extensive set of experiments. We conclude that detection methods should now target more complex kinds of anomalies.

AI.tldr: Simple graph features with standard ML can detect anomalies in link streams as effectively as complex methods, due to synthetic ground-truth injections; this approach is fast and interpretable.
AI.motivation: Ground truth for link-stream anomaly detection is scarce; many methods rely on evaluations using randomly injected links, which may favor simple baselines.
AI.method: Apply trivial graph features (e.g., basic structural metrics) and classical learning algorithms to detect injected anomalies; compare performance and interpretability against more complex approaches; conduct extensive experiments to assess generality and costs.
AI.result: Demonstrates that basic features with standard ML detect anomalies extremely well; benefits include low computation and high interpretability; performance appears robust across experiments; challenges the necessity of complex models for this anomaly class.
AI.conclusion: Suggests shifting focus toward detecting more complex, less obvious anomalies; calls for re-evaluating anomaly detection benchmarks and methodologies.

title: GroundedSurg: A Multi-Procedure Benchmark for Language-Conditioned Surgical Tool Segmentation
summary: Clinically reliable perception of surgical scenes is essential for advancing intelligent, context-aware intraoperative assistance such as instrument handoff guidance, collision avoidance, and workflow-aware robotic support. Existing surgical tool benchmarks primarily evaluate category-level segmentation, requiring models to detect all instances of predefined instrument classes. However, real-world clinical decisions often require resolving references to a specific instrument instance based on its functional role, spatial relation, or anatomical interaction capabilities not captured by current evaluation paradigms. We introduce GroundedSurg, the first language-conditioned, instance-level surgical grounding benchmark. Each instance pairs a surgical image with a natural-language description targeting a single instrument, accompanied by structured spatial grounding annotations including bounding boxes and point-level anchors. The dataset spans ophthalmic, laparoscopic, robotic, and open procedures, encompassing diverse instrument types, imaging conditions, and operative complexities. By jointly evaluating linguistic reference resolution and pixel-level localization, GroundedSurg enables a systematic and realistic evaluation of vision-language models in clinically realistic multi-instrument scenes. Extensive experiments demonstrate substantial performance gaps across modern segmentation and VLMs, highlighting the urgent need for clinically grounded vision-language reasoning in surgical AI systems. Code and data are publicly available at https://github.com/gaash-lab/GroundedSurg

AI.tldr: GroundedSurg is a language-conditioned, instance-level surgical grounding benchmark pairing images with instrument-specific natural language descriptions and precise spatial annotations (boxes and points), spanning multiple surgical domains; it reveals large gaps in current segmentation and vision-language models and provides a realistic benchmark for clinically grounded VLR.
AI.motivation: Real clinical decisions require resolving references to specific instrument instances by function, spatial relations, or anatomical context, which category-level segmentation benchmarks do not address.
AI.method: Assemble GroundedSurg dataset: image–text pairs where text targets a single instrument; provides bounding-box and point-level grounding; includes ophthalmic, laparoscopic, robotic, and open procedures; jointly evaluates linguistic reference resolution and pixel-level localization; public release of code and data.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show substantial performance gaps for modern segmentation and vision-language models, underscoring the need for clinically grounded vision-language reasoning in surgical AI systems.
AI.conclusion: GroundedSurg enables systematic, realistic evaluation of vision-language grounding in multi-instrument surgical scenes and should drive development of clinically aligned VLR; code/data are publicly available.

title: Tide: A Customisable Dataset Generator for Anti-Money Laundering Research
comment: Synthetic AML transaction datasets (Tide, HI and LI variants) are available at https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.18804069
summary:
The lack of accessible transactional data significantly hinders machine learning research for Anti-Money Laundering (AML). Privacy and legal concerns prevent the sharing of real financial data, while existing synthetic generators focus on simplistic structural patterns and neglect the temporal dynamics (timing and frequency) that characterise sophisticated laundering schemes.
  We present Tide, an open-source synthetic dataset generator that produces graph-based financial networks incorporating money laundering patterns defined by both structural and temporal characteristics. Tide enables reproducible, customisable dataset generation tailored to specific research needs. We release two reference datasets with varying illicit ratios (LI: 0.10\%, HI: 0.19\%), alongside the implementation of state-of-the-art detection models.
  Evaluation across these datasets reveals condition-dependent model rankings: LightGBM achieves the highest PR-AUC (78.05) in the low illicit ratio condition, while XGBoost performs best (85.12) at higher fraud prevalence. These divergent rankings demonstrate that the reference datasets can meaningfully differentiate model capabilities across operational conditions.
  Tide provides the research community with a configurable benchmark that exposes meaningful performance variation across model architectures, advancing the development of robust AML detection methods.

AI.tldr: Tide: an open-source synthetic AML dataset generator producing graph-based financial networks with structural and temporal laundering patterns; provides two reference datasets (LI=0.10%, 0.19%); shows condition-dependent model rankings (LightGBM PR-AUC 78.05 in low illicit ratio; XGBoost 85.12 in higher ratio); enables reproducible benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Address the lack of accessible transactional data for AML research due to privacy/legal constraints and the limitations of existing synthetic generators that overlook temporal dynamics; provide a configurable, reproducible benchmark to evaluate AML detectors across operational conditions.
AI.method: Generate graph-based financial networks encoding money-laundering patterns via both structural and temporal features; release two reference datasets with different illicit ratios; implement and compare state-of-the-art detection models (e.g., LightGBM, XGBoost).
AI.result: Through evaluation on the two datasets, model performance rankings vary by illicit ratio: LightGBM attains the highest PR-AUC (78.05) in the low illicit-ratio condition, while XGBoost performs best (85.12) at higher fraud prevalence, demonstrating condition-dependent model capabilities.
AI.conclusion: Tide provides a configurable benchmark that reveals meaningful performance variation across model architectures, supporting the development of robust AML detection methods and enabling reproducible research.

title: DeAR: Fine-Grained VLM Adaptation by Decomposing Attention Head Roles
summary: Prompt learning is a dominant paradigm for adapting pre-trained Vision-Language Models (VLMs) to downstream tasks. However, existing methods often rely on a simplistic, layer-centric view, assuming shallow layers capture general features while deep layers handle task-specific knowledge. This assumption results in uncontrolled interactions between learnable tokens and original tokens. Task-specific knowledge could degrades the model's core generalization and creates a trade-off between task adaptation and the preservation of zero-shot generalization. To address this, we challenge the layer-centric view and propose \textbf{DeAR}, a framework that achieves fine-grained VLM adaptation by \textbf{De}composing \textbf{A}ttention head \textbf{R}oles. We posit that the functional specialization within VLMs occurs not between layers, but at the finer-grained level of individual attention heads in the deeper layers. Based on this insight, we introduce a novel metric, Concept Entropy, to systematically classify attention heads into distinct functional roles: \textit{Attribute}, \textit{Generalization}, and \textit{Mixed}. Guided by these roles, we introduce specialized attribute tokens and a Role-Based Attention Mask mechanism to precisely control information flow, ensuring generalization heads remain isolated from task-specific knowledge. We further incorporate a Task-Adaptive Fusion Strategy for inference. Extensive experiments on fifteen datasets show that DeAR achieves a strong balance between task adaptation and generalization, outperforming previous methods across various tasks.

AI.tldr: DeAR defies a layer-centric adaptation paradigm by decomposing attention head roles for fine-grained VLM adaptation. It introduces Concept Entropy to classify heads into Attribute, Generalization, and Mixed, and uses attribute tokens, Role-Based Attention Masks, and Task-Adaptive Fusion to isolate generalization heads from task-specific knowledge, achieving strong results across 15 datasets.
AI.motivation: Layer-centric prompts and fixed layer-wise assumptions cause uncontrolled interactions between learnable tokens and original tokens, risking degradation of zero-shot generalization; a finer-grained head-level mechanism is proposed to preserve generalization while enabling task adaptation.
AI.method: Propose DeAR framework that analyzes individual attention heads in deeper layers. Introduce Concept Entropy to classify heads into Attribute, Generalization, and Mixed. Use attribute tokens and Role-Based Attention Mask to control information flow, keeping generalization heads isolated from task-specific knowledge. Include a Task-Adaptive Fusion Strategy for inference.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on fifteen datasets shows DeAR achieves a favorable balance between task adaptation and zero-shot generalization, outperforming prior methods across various tasks.
AI.conclusion: Head-level functional specialization rather than layer-level differences drives VLM adaptation. DeAR’s head-role decomposition, plus Concept Entropy-driven classification and mask-based control, yields improved generalization preservation while enabling task-specific improvements.

title: Diagnosing Generalization Failures from Representational Geometry Markers
comment: Published in the International Conference on Learning Representations (ICLR), 2026
summary: Generalization, the ability to perform well beyond the training context, is a hallmark of biological and artificial intelligence, yet anticipating unseen failures remains a central challenge. Conventional approaches often take a ``bottom-up'' mechanistic route by reverse-engineering interpretable features or circuits to build explanatory models. While insightful, these methods often struggle to provide the high-level, predictive signals for anticipating failure in real-world deployment. Here, we propose using a ``top-down'' approach to studying generalization failures inspired by medical biomarkers: identifying system-level measurements that serve as robust indicators of a model's future performance. Rather than mapping out detailed internal mechanisms, we systematically design and test network markers to probe structure, function links, identify prognostic indicators, and validate predictions in real-world settings. In image classification, we find that task-relevant geometric properties of in-distribution (ID) object manifolds consistently forecast poor out-of-distribution (OOD) generalization. In particular, reductions in two geometric measures, effective manifold dimensionality and utility, predict weaker OOD performance across diverse architectures, optimizers, and datasets. We apply this finding to transfer learning with ImageNet-pretrained models. We consistently find that the same geometric patterns predict OOD transfer performance more reliably than ID accuracy. This work demonstrates that representational geometry can expose hidden vulnerabilities, offering more robust guidance for model selection and AI interpretability.

AI.tldr: Top-down, biomarker-inspired indicators of generalization: geometric properties of in-distribution manifolds (effective dimensionality and utility) predict out-of-distribution and transfer performance better than ID accuracy.
AI.motivation: Generalization beyond the training context remains challenging; bottom-up, mechanistic explanations often fail to provide robust, high-level predictive signals for real-world failure. A top-down, biomarker-like approach seeks robust indicators of future performance.
AI.method: Systematically design and test network-level markers based on representational geometry. Measure ID object-manifold geometry (effective dimensionality and utility) and evaluate their ability to predict OOD generalization across architectures, optimizers, and datasets; apply findings to transfer learning with ImageNet-pretrained models.
AI.result: Reductions in effective manifold dimensionality and utility correlate with weaker OOD generalization; these metrics predict OOD transfer performance more reliably than ID accuracy, consistently across architectures, optimizers, and datasets.
AI.conclusion: Representational geometry can reveal hidden vulnerabilities and guide model selection and interpretability. A top-down biomarker approach provides robust signals to anticipate failures in deployment and to inform transfer decisions.

title: GuiDINO: Rethinking Vision Foundation Model in Medical Image Segmentation
summary: Foundation vision models are increasingly adopted in medical image analysis. Due to domain shift, these pretrained models misalign with medical image segmentation needs without being fully fine-tuned or lightly adapted. We introduce GuiDINO, a framework that repositions native foundation model to acting as a visual guidance generator for downstream segmentation. GuiDINO extracts visual feature representation from DINOv3 and converts them into a spatial guide mask via a lightweight TokenBook mechanism, which aggregates token-prototype similarities. This guide mask gates feature activations in multiple segmentation backbones, thereby injecting foundation-model priors while preserving the inductive biases and efficiency of medical dedicated architectures. Training relies on a guide supervision objective loss that aligns the guide mask to ground-truth regions, optionally augmented by a boundary-focused hinge loss to sharpen fine structures. GuiDINO also supports parameter-efficient adaptation through LoRA on the DINOv3 guide backbone. Across diverse medical datasets and nnUNet-style inference, GuiDINO consistently improves segmentation quality and boundary robustness, suggesting a practical alternative to fine-tuning and offering a new perspective on how foundation models can best serve medical vision. Code is available at https://github.com/Hi-FishU/GuiDINO

AI.tldr: GuiDINO repurposes a foundation model (DINOv3) as a visual guidance generator for medical image segmentation, using a lightweight TokenBook to create a spatial guide mask that gates backbone features, enabling parameter-efficient adaptation and improved segmentation and boundary robustness without full fine-tuning.
AI.motivation: Foundation vision models suffer from domain shift when applied to medical segmentation; full fine-tuning is costly and may erode domain-specific inductive biases. There is a need for a lightweight, effective way to inject foundation-model priors into medical segmentation backbones.
AI.method: Extract DINOv3 visual features and convert them into a spatial guide mask via TokenBook, which aggregates token-prototype similarities. Gate these guides into segmentation backbones to inject priors while preserving medical architectures. Train with a guide-supervision loss aligning guides to ground-truth regions, optionally add a boundary-focused hinge loss. Enable LoRA-based parameter-efficient adaptation on the DINOv3 backbone.
AI.result: GuiDINO consistently improves segmentation quality and boundary robustness across diverse medical datasets and nnUNet-style inference, suggesting effective integration of foundation priors without full fine-tuning.
AI.conclusion: GuiDINO offers a practical alternative to fine-tuning, demonstrating how foundation models can be leveraged as priors for medical vision while maintaining efficiency; code is available for reproduction.

title: SEAR: Sample Efficient Action Chunking Reinforcement Learning
summary: Action chunking can improve exploration and value estimation in long horizon reinforcement learning, but makes learning substantially harder since the critic must evaluate action sequences rather than single actions, greatly increasing approximation and data efficiency challenges. As a result, existing action chunking methods, primarily designed for the offline and offline-to-online settings, have not achieved strong performance in purely online reinforcement learning. We introduce SEAR, an off policy online reinforcement learning algorithm for action chunking. It exploits the temporal structure of action chunks and operates with a receding horizon, effectively combining the benefits of small and large chunk sizes. SEAR outperforms state of the art online reinforcement learning methods on Metaworld, training with chunk sizes up to 20.

AI.tldr: SEAR is an off-policy online RL algorithm for action chunking that uses a receding horizon to combine benefits of small and large chunk sizes, achieving state-of-the-art online performance on Metaworld with chunk sizes up to 20.
AI.motivation: Action chunking can improve exploration and value estimation in long-horizon RL but increases the critic's difficulty; offline-focused chunking methods underperform in online settings; there is a need for an online method that leverages chunking effectively.
AI.method: SEAR is an off-policy online RL algorithm that learns with action chunks by exploiting their temporal structure and employing a receding-horizon approach; it supports variable chunk sizes up to a large horizon (e.g., 20) and integrates chunk-level policy and critic updates to enable online learning.
AI.result: SEAR outperforms state-of-the-art online RL baselines on Metaworld, enabling training with chunk sizes up to 20 and achieving superior performance.
AI.conclusion: SEAR demonstrates the viability of online action chunking by combining temporal structure and receding horizon; it bridges small- and large-chunk benefits and opens avenues for more robust long-horizon online RL.

title: Improved MambdaBDA Framework for Robust Building Damage Assessment Across Disaster Domains
summary: Reliable post-disaster building damage assessment (BDA) from satellite imagery is hindered by severe class imbalance, background clutter, and domain shift across disaster types and geographies. In this work, we address these problems and explore ways to improve the MambaBDA, the BDA network of ChangeMamba architecture, one of the most successful BDA models. The approach enhances the MambaBDA with three modular components: (i) Focal Loss to mitigate class imbalance damage classification, (ii) lightweight Attention Gates to suppress irrelevant context, and (iii) a compact Alignment Module to spatially warp pre-event features toward post-event content before decoding. We experiment on multiple satellite imagery datasets, including xBD, Pakistan Flooding, Turkey Earthquake, and Ida Hurricane, and conduct in-domain and crossdataset tests. The proposed modular enhancements yield consistent improvements over the baseline model, with 0.8% to 5% performance gains in-domain, and up to 27% on unseen disasters. This indicates that the proposed enhancements are especially beneficial for the generalization capability of the system.

AI.tldr: Modular enhancements to ChangeMamba's MambaBDA improve post-disaster building damage assessment via Focal Loss, Attention Gates, and a compact Alignment Module; results show in-domain gains and substantial cross-disaster generalization gains.
AI.motivation: Post-disaster BDA suffers from severe class imbalance, background clutter, and domain shift across disaster types/geographies.
AI.method: Integrate three modules into MambaBDA: (i) Focal Loss for class imbalance; (ii) lightweight Attention Gates to suppress irrelevant context; (iii) a compact Alignment Module to warp pre-event features toward post-event content before decoding. Evaluated on xBD, Pakistan Flooding, Turkey Earthquake, Ida Hurricane with in-domain and cross-dataset tests.
AI.result: Consistent improvements over baseline; 0.8%–5% in-domain gains; up to 27% gains on unseen disasters; enhancements improve generalization.
AI.conclusion: The modular components effectively address class imbalance and domain shift, boosting BDA performance and cross-disaster generalization; promising for robust real-world deployment.

title: Efficient RLVR Training via Weighted Mutual Information Data Selection
summary: Reinforcement learning (RL) plays a central role in improving the reasoning and alignment of large language models, yet its efficiency critically depends on how training data are selected. Existing online selection strategies predominantly rely on difficulty-based heuristics, favouring datapoints with intermediate success rates, implicitly equating difficulty with informativeness and neglecting epistemic uncertainty arising from limited evidence. We introduce InSight, an INformation-guided data SamplInG metHod for RL Training, grounded in a weighted mutual information objective. By modeling data outcomes with Bayesian latent success rates, we show that expected uncertainty reduction decomposes into complementary difficulty- and evidence-dependent components, revealing a fundamental limitation of difficulty-only selection. Leveraging this observation, InSight constructs a stable acquisition score based on the mean belief of datapoints' success rather than noisy sampled outcomes, and naturally extends to multi-rollout settings common in reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR). Extensive experiments demonstrate that InSight consistently achieves state-of-the-art performance and improves training efficiency, including a +1.41 average gain on Planning & Mathmatics benchmarks, +1.01 improvement on general reasoning, and up to ~2.2x acceleration, with negligible additional computational overhead.

AI.tldr: An information-guided data sampling method, InSight, uses Bayesian latent success rates to optimize data selection for RL training via a weighted mutual information objective, balancing difficulty and evidence; yields state-of-the-art gains and faster training with minimal overhead.
AI.motivation: To overcome the limitations of difficulty-only, online selection in RL data curation that conflates difficulty with informativeness and ignores epistemic uncertainty from limited evidence.
AI.method: Model datapoint outcomes with Bayesian latent success rates; decompose expected uncertainty reduction into difficulty- and evidence-driven components; compute a stable acquisition score using mean beliefs; optimize a weighted mutual information objective; extend to multi-rollout RL with verifiable rewards (RLVR).
AI.result: Empirical studies show consistent state-of-the-art performance, e.g., +1.41 average gain on Planning & Mathematics benchmarks, +1.01 on general reasoning, up to ~2.2x training acceleration, with negligible additional computational overhead.
AI.conclusion: InSight addresses the fundamental limitation of difficulty-only data selection by incorporating epistemic uncertainty via Bayesian success-rate modeling, providing a principled, efficient sampling method for RL training and RLVR.

title: ClinCoT: Clinical-Aware Visual Chain-of-Thought for Medical Vision Language Models
summary: Medical Vision-Language Models have shown promising potential in clinical decision support, yet they remain prone to factual hallucinations due to insufficient grounding in localized pathological evidence. Existing medical alignment methods primarily operate at the response level through preference optimization, improving output correctness but leaving intermediate reasoning weakly connected to visual regions. Although chain-of-thought (CoT) enhances multimodal reasoning, it remains largely text-centric, limiting effective integration of clinical visual cues. To address this gap, we propose ClinCoT, a clinical-aware visual chain-of-thought framework that transforms preference optimization from response-level correction to visual-driven reasoning. We introduce an automatic data generation pipeline that constructs clinically grounded preference pairs through reasoning with hypotheses-driven region proposals. Multiple Med-LLMs evaluators rank and assign scores to each response, and these rankings serve as supervision to train the target model. We further introduce a scoring-based margin-aware optimization strategy that incorporates both preference ranking and score difference to refine region-level reasoning trajectories. To maintain alignment as the model's policy evolves during training, we adopt an iterative learning scheme that dynamically regenerates preference data. Extensive experiments on three medical VQA and report generation benchmarks demonstrate that ClinCoT consistently improves factual grounding and achieves superior performance compared with existing preference-based alignment methods.

AI.tldr: ClinCoT introduces a visual-grounded chain-of-thought framework for medical vision-language models, shifting reasoning from response-level corrections to region-level, clinically grounded visual reasoning with automatic data generation, ranking-based supervision, margin-aware optimization, and iterative data regeneration; it improves factual grounding and overall performance on medical VQA and report generation benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Medical VLMs often produce factual hallucinations due to weak grounding in localized pathological evidence. Existing alignment methods optimize at the response level and rarely integrate visual cues into intermediate reasoning. A visual-aware CoT can better leverage clinical images and regions to support decision-making.
AI.method: ClinCoT constructs hypothesis-driven region proposals to generate clinically grounded preference pairs. Multiple medical LLM evaluators rank and score responses, providing supervision to train the target model. A scoring-based margin-aware optimization uses both ranking and score differences to refine region-level reasoning trajectories. An iterative learning scheme regenerates preference data as the model policy evolves, ensuring alignment remains current.
AI.result: Experimental results on three medical VQA and report generation benchmarks show ClinCoT consistently improves factual grounding and outperforms existing preference-based alignment methods.
AI.conclusion: Transforming preference optimization from response-level to visually grounded region-level reasoning yields better factual grounding and overall performance for medical vision-language models.

title: Dream2Learn: Structured Generative Dreaming for Continual Learning
summary: Continual learning requires balancing plasticity and stability while mitigating catastrophic forgetting. Inspired by human dreaming as a mechanism for internal simulation and knowledge restructuring, we introduce Dream2Learn (D2L), a framework in which a model autonomously generates structured synthetic experiences from its own internal representations and uses them for self-improvement. Rather than reconstructing past data as in generative replay, D2L enables a classifier to create novel, semantically distinct dreamed classes that are coherent with its learned knowledge yet do not correspond to previously observed data. These dreamed samples are produced by conditioning a frozen diffusion model through soft prompt optimization driven by the classifier itself. The generated data are not used to replace memory, but to expand and reorganize the representation space, effectively allowing the network to self-train on internally synthesized concepts. By integrating dreamed classes into continual training, D2L proactively structures latent features to support forward knowledge transfer and adaptation to future tasks. This prospective self-training mechanism mirrors the role of sleep in consolidating and reorganizing memory, turning internal simulations into a tool for improved generalization. Experiments on Mini-ImageNet, FG-ImageNet, and ImageNet-R demonstrate that D2L consistently outperforms strong rehearsal-based baselines and achieves positive forward transfer, confirming its ability to enhance adaptability through internally generated training signals.

AI.tldr: Dream2Learn (D2L) proposes a continual learning framework where a classifier guides a frozen diffusion model to generate novel, semantically distinct “dreamed” classes. These internally simulated concepts are used for self-training to expand and reorganize the representation space, achieving forward transfer and outperforming rehearsal baselines on standard benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Addresses the plasticity-stability trade-off and catastrophic forgetting in continual learning. Inspired by human dreaming as an internal simulation mechanism to consolidate and reorganize memory. Aims to move beyond reconstructing past data (generative replay) by creating semantically distinct dreamed classes that expand knowledge without revisiting exact past samples.
AI.method: Freeze a diffusion model and optimize soft prompts conditioned on the classifier to generate dreamed samples that align with learned knowledge but do not correspond to observed data. Use these dreamed samples for self-training in addition to real data, expanding latent representations and enabling forward knowledge transfer. Integrate dreamed classes into continual training without replacing memory.
AI.result: Empirically, D2L outperforms strong rehearsal-based baselines on Mini-ImageNet, FG-ImageNet, and ImageNet-R and achieves positive forward transfer, indicating enhanced adaptability from internally generated training signals.
AI.conclusion: Internal dream-like simulations can restructure latent representations to improve continual learning. D2L provides a viable alternative to replay-based approaches by leveraging self-generated concepts to promote forward transfer and generalization. Further work could explore semantic control, scalability, and robustness across tasks.

title: Predictive Reasoning with Augmented Anomaly Contrastive Learning for Compositional Visual Relations
comment: Accepted by IEEE Transactions on Multimedia
summary: While visual reasoning for simple analogies has received significant attention, compositional visual relations (CVR) remain relatively unexplored due to their greater complexity. To solve CVR tasks, we propose Predictive Reasoning with Augmented Anomaly Contrastive Learning (PR-A$^2$CL), \ie, to identify an outlier image given three other images that follow the same compositional rules. To address the challenge of modelling abundant compositional rules, an Augmented Anomaly Contrastive Learning is designed to distil discriminative and generalizable features by maximizing similarity among normal instances while minimizing similarity between normal and anomalous outliers. More importantly, a predict-and-verify paradigm is introduced for rule-based reasoning, in which a series of Predictive Anomaly Reasoning Blocks (PARBs) iteratively leverage features from three out of the four images to predict those of the remaining one. Throughout the subsequent verification stage, the PARBs progressively pinpoint the specific discrepancies attributable to the underlying rules. Experimental results on SVRT, CVR and MC$^2$R datasets show that PR-A$^2$CL significantly outperforms state-of-the-art reasoning models.

AI.tldr: A framework (PR-A^2CL) for compositional visual reasoning that uses predictive anomaly reasoning blocks and augmented anomaly contrastive learning to identify an outlier image among three that share compositional rules, achieving strong results on standard CVR datasets.
AI.motivation: CVR tasks involve complex, rule-based relationships that are not well captured by simple analogies; there is a need for discriminative, generalizable representations and a transparent, stepwise reasoning process to identify rule violations.
AI.method: Proposes Augmented Anomaly Contrastive Learning (AAC-L) to learn discriminative, generalizable features by pulling together normal instances and pushing away normal–anomalous pairs; introduces Predictive Anomaly Reasoning Blocks (PARBs) in a predict-and-verify loop that uses three images to predict the fourth, with a verification stage to locate discrepancies linked to underlying rules.
AI.result: PR-A^2CL substantially outperforms state-of-the-art reasoning models on SVRT, CVR, and MC^2R benchmarks, indicating improved generalization and interpretability for CVR tasks.
AI.conclusion: The combination of AAC-L and the iterative PARB-based reasoning framework provides effective, interpretable, and scalable solutions for compositional visual reasoning tasks.

title: Explanation-Guided Adversarial Training for Robust and Interpretable Models
comment: Accepted by IEEE Transactions On Circuits and Systems For Video Technology (TCSVT 2026)
summary: Deep neural networks (DNNs) have achieved remarkable performance in many tasks, yet they often behave as opaque black boxes. Explanation-guided learning (EGL) methods steer DNNs using human-provided explanations or supervision on model attributions. These approaches improve interpretability but typically assume benign inputs and incur heavy annotation costs. In contrast, both predictions and saliency maps of DNNs could dramatically alter facing imperceptible perturbations or unseen patterns. Adversarial training (AT) can substantially improve robustness, but it does not guarantee that model decisions rely on semantically meaningful features. In response, we propose Explanation-Guided Adversarial Training (EGAT), a unified framework that integrates the strength of AT and EGL to simultaneously improve prediction performance, robustness, and explanation quality. EGAT generates adversarial examples on the fly while imposing explanation-based constraints on the model. By jointly optimizing classification performance, adversarial robustness, and attributional stability, EGAT is not only more resistant to unexpected cases, including adversarial attacks and out-of-distribution (OOD) scenarios, but also offer human-interpretable justifications for the decisions. We further formalize EGAT within the Probably Approximately Correct learning framework, demonstrating theoretically that it yields more stable predictions under unexpected situations compared to standard AT. Empirical evaluations on OOD benchmark datasets show that EGAT consistently outperforms competitive baselines in both clean accuracy and adversarial accuracy +37% while producing more semantically meaningful explanations, and requiring only a limited increase +16% in training time.

AI.tldr: EGAT combines adversarial training and explanation-guided learning to improve accuracy, robustness, and explanation quality, with PAC guarantees; demonstrates substantial gains on OOD benchmarks (e.g., ~+37% adversarial accuracy) and modest training-time overhead (~+16%).
AI.motivation: DNNs are opaque and brittle to perturbations; explanations can guide learning but require annotations; existing AT improves robustness without ensuring semantically meaningful decisions; EGL requires expensive supervision. A unified framework is needed to improve accuracy, robustness, and interpretable explanations, including under OOD and adversarial settings.
AI.method: Explanation-Guided Adversarial Training (EGAT) that generates adversarial examples on the fly while enforcing explanation-based constraints on attribution maps. It jointly optimizes classification performance, adversarial robustness, and attributional stability. The work also provides a Probably Approximately Correct (PAC) formalization showing stability benefits over standard AT.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on Out-Of-Distribution (OOD) benchmarks shows EGAT consistently outperforms competitive baselines in clean accuracy and adversarial accuracy (reported ~+37% improvement). It also yields more semantically meaningful explanations and incurs only a modest training-time increase (~+16%).
AI.conclusion: EGAT delivers more stable, robust, and interpretable predictions under unexpected inputs, bridging robustness and interpretability. The framework offers theoretical stability guarantees and is practical with modest computational overhead.

title: Teacher-Guided Causal Interventions for Image Denoising: Orthogonal Content-Noise Disentanglement in Vision Transformers
summary: Conventional image denoising models often inadvertently learn spurious correlations between environmental factors and noise patterns. Moreover, due to high-frequency ambiguity, they struggle to reliably distinguish subtle textures from stochastic noise, resulting in over-removed details or residual noise artifacts. We therefore revisit denoising via causal intervention, arguing that purely correlational fitting entangles intrinsic content with extrinsic noise, which directly degrades robustness under distribution shifts. Motivated by this, we propose the Teacher-Guided Causal Disentanglement Network (TCD-Net), which explicitly decomposes the generative mechanism via structured interventions on feature spaces within a Vision Transformer framework. Specifically, our method integrates three key components: (1) An Environmental Bias Adjustment (EBA) module projects features into a stable, de-centered subspace to suppress global environmental bias (de-confounding). (2) A dual-branch disentanglement head employs an orthogonality constraint to force a strict separation between content and noise representations, preventing information leakage. (3) To resolve structural ambiguity, we leverage Nano Banana Pro, Google's reasoning-guided AI image generation model, to guide a causal prior, effectively pulling content representations back onto the natural-image manifold. Extensive experiments demonstrate that TCD-Net outperforms mainstream methods across multiple benchmarks in both fidelity and efficiency, achieving a real-time speed of 104.2 FPS on a single RTX 5090 GPU.

AI.tldr: Proposes TCD-Net for denoising via causal disentanglement in a Vision Transformer; introduces Environmental Bias Adjustment, dual-branch content-noise disentanglement with an orthogonality constraint, and a causal prior guided by a reasoning AI to align representations with the natural image manifold; reports superior fidelity and real-time speed (104.2 FPS) on RTX 5090 across multiple benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Conventional denoising models learn spurious correlations between environmental factors and noise, leading to brittle performance under distribution shifts and difficulty distinguishing textures from stochastic noise.
AI.method: A three-component framework: (1) Environmental Bias Adjustment (EBA) to project features into a stable, de-centered subspace; (2) a dual-branch disentanglement head with an orthogonality constraint enforcing strict separation between content and noise; (3) a causal prior via Nano Banana Pro to pull content representations toward the natural-image manifold.
AI.result: Empirical results show TCD-Net outperforming mainstream denoising methods across several benchmarks in fidelity; achieves real-time inference at 104.2 FPS on a single RTX 5090 GPU.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating causal interventions and structured feature-space disentanglement enhances robustness to distribution shifts and preserves details, offering a practical, fast denoising solution.

title: BAED: a New Paradigm for Few-shot Graph Learning with Explanation in the Loop
summary: The challenges of training and inference in few-shot environments persist in the area of graph representation learning. The quality and quantity of labels are often insufficient due to the extensive expert knowledge required to annotate graph data. In this context, Few-Shot Graph Learning (FSGL) approaches have been developed over the years. Through sophisticated neural architectures and customized training pipelines, these approaches enhance model adaptability to new label distributions. However, compromises in \textcolor{black}{the model's} robustness and interpretability can result in overfitting to noise in labeled data and degraded performance. This paper introduces the first explanation-in-the-loop framework for the FSGL problem, called BAED. We novelly employ the belief propagation algorithm to facilitate label augmentation on graphs. Then, leveraging an auxiliary graph neural network and the gradient backpropagation method, our framework effectively extracts explanatory subgraphs surrounding target nodes. The final predictions are based on these informative subgraphs while mitigating the influence of redundant information from neighboring nodes. Extensive experiments on seven benchmark datasets demonstrate superior prediction accuracy, training efficiency, and explanation quality of BAED. As a pioneer, this work highlights the potential of the explanation-based research paradigm in FSGL.

AI.tldr: BAED introduces an explanation-in-the-loop framework for Few-Shot Graph Learning (FSGL). It uses belief propagation for label augmentation, an auxiliary GNN, and backpropagated explanations to identify informative subgraphs around target nodes. Predictions are based on these subgraphs, improving accuracy, efficiency, and explanation quality on seven benchmarks.
AI.motivation: FSGL suffers from label scarcity and limited robustness/interpretability, which can lead to overfitting and poor generalization. There is a need to integrate explanations into FSGL to guide learning and improve trustworthiness.
AI.method: Proposes BAED: an explanation-in-the-loop framework that (1) augments labels on graphs via belief propagation, (2) employs an auxiliary graph neural network to model explanatory subgraphs, and (3) uses gradient backpropagation to extract subgraphs surrounding target nodes. Final predictions are made using information from these subgraphs while suppressing redundant neighboring information.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on seven benchmark datasets show BAED achieves superior prediction accuracy, training efficiency, and explanation quality compared to baselines.
AI.conclusion: This work pioneers the explanation-based research paradigm in FSGL, highlighting the potential of explanation-driven frameworks to enhance performance and interpretability in few-shot graph learning.

title: ArtLLM: Generating Articulated Assets via 3D LLM
summary: Creating interactive digital environments for gaming, robotics, and simulation relies on articulated 3D objects whose functionality emerges from their part geometry and kinematic structure. However, existing approaches remain fundamentally limited: optimization-based reconstruction methods require slow, per-object joint fitting and typically handle only simple, single-joint objects, while retrieval-based methods assemble parts from a fixed library, leading to repetitive geometry and poor generalization. To address these challenges, we introduce ArtLLM, a novel framework for generating high-quality articulated assets directly from complete 3D meshes. At its core is a 3D multimodal large language model trained on a large-scale articulation dataset curated from both existing articulation datasets and procedurally generated objects. Unlike prior work, ArtLLM autoregressively predicts a variable number of parts and joints, inferring their kinematic structure in a unified manner from the object's point cloud. This articulation-aware layout then conditions a 3D generative model to synthesize high-fidelity part geometries. Experiments on the PartNet-Mobility dataset show that ArtLLM significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods in both part layout accuracy and joint prediction, while generalizing robustly to real-world objects. Finally, we demonstrate its utility in constructing digital twins, highlighting its potential for scalable robot learning.

AI.tldr: ArtLLM jointly discovers articulation structure and generates high-fidelity geometries for 3D objects from a complete mesh by autoregressively predicting parts/joints and conditioning a 3D generator, improving over optimization- and library-based methods.
AI.motivation: Current methods either require slow per-object joint fitting and handle only simple, single joints, or rely on fixed part libraries, causing repetitive geometry and poor generalization; a unified, data-driven articulation model is needed.
AI.method: Train a 3D multimodal LLM on a large articulation dataset to autoregressively infer a variable number of parts and joints from an object's point cloud, producing an articulation-aware layout that conditions a 3D generative model to synthesize detailed part geometries.
AI.result: On PartNet-Mobility, ArtLLM achieves superior part layout accuracy and joint prediction compared to state-of-the-art, generalizes to real-world objects, and enables applications like digital twins and scalable robot learning.
AI.conclusion: ArtLLM provides a unified framework for articulate asset generation from full meshes, offering robust generalization and streamlined creation of articulated assets for interactive environments.

title: Probabilistic Retrofitting of Learned Simulators
summary: Dominant approaches for modelling Partial Differential Equations (PDEs) rely on deterministic predictions, yet many physical systems of interest are inherently chaotic and uncertain. While training probabilistic models from scratch is possible, it is computationally expensive and fails to leverage the significant resources already invested in high-performing deterministic backbones. In this work, we adopt a training-efficient strategy to transform pre-trained deterministic models into probabilistic ones via retrofitting with a proper scoring rule: the Continuous Ranked Probability Score (CRPS). Crucially, this approach is architecture-agnostic: it applies the same adaptation mechanism across distinct model backbones with minimal code modifications. The method proves highly effective across different scales of pre-training: for models trained on single dynamical systems, we achieve 20-54% reductions in rollout CRPS and up to 30% improvements in variance-normalised RMSE (VRMSE) relative to compute-matched deterministic fine-tuning. We further validate our approach on a PDE foundation model, trained on multiple systems and retrofitted on the dataset of interest, to show that our probabilistic adaptation yields an improvement of up to 40% in CRPS and up to 15% in VRMSE compared to deterministic fine-tuning. Validated across diverse architectures and dynamics, our results show that probabilistic PDE modelling need not require retraining from scratch, but can be unlocked from existing deterministic backbones with modest additional training cost.

AI.tldr: Transform deterministic PDE backbones into probabilistic models by retrofitting with CRPS; architecture-agnostic, improves probabilistic metrics with modest training cost.
AI.motivation: Address lack of uncertainty estimates in deterministic PDE models; leverage pre-trained backbones to produce calibrated probabilistic predictions.
AI.method: Retrofitting via Continuous Ranked Probability Score (CRPS); compatible across architectures; code-minimal changes; evaluated on single-system pre-trained models and a PDE foundation model with retrofitting on target dataset.
AI.result: Rollout CRPS improved by 20-54%; VRMSE improved up to 30% for single-system; foundation model shows CRPS up to 40% improvement and VRMSE up to 15%.
AI.conclusion: Probabilistic PDE modelling can be achieved without starting from scratch; architecture-agnostic adaptation enables efficient uncertainty quantification with modest additional training.

title: TC-SSA: Token Compression via Semantic Slot Aggregation for Gigapixel Pathology Reasoning
summary: The application of large vision-language models to computational pathology holds great promise for diagnostic assistants but faces a critical computational bottleneck: the gigapixel scale of Whole Slide Images (WSIs). A single WSI typically contains over 105 patches, creating sequence lengths that exceed the constraints of standard Transformer architectures. Existing solutions often resort to spatial sampling, which risks discarding diagnostically critical evidence. To address this, we propose TC-SSA (Token Compression via Semantic Slot Aggregation), a learnable token compression framework that aggregates patch features into a fixed number of semantic slots. A gated routing module assigns patches to slots using sparse Top-2 routing, followed by weighted aggregation, enabling global slide coverage under a strict token budget. The resulting representation retains diagnostically relevant information while reducing the number of visual tokens to 1.7% of the original sequence. On the SlideBench(TCGA), our model achieves 78.34% overall accuracy and 77.14% on the diagnosis subset, outperforming sampling-based baselines under comparable token budgets. The method also generalizes to MIL classification, reaching AUC of 95.83% on TCGA-BRCA, 98.27% on TCGA-NSCLC and 79.80% on PANDA. These results suggest that learnable semantic aggregation provides an effective trade-off between efficiency and diagnostic performance for gigapixel pathology reasoning.

AI.tldr: A learnable token compression method (TC-SSA) aggregates gigapixel slide patches into a fixed number of semantic slots, enabling efficient WSI reasoning with little loss in diagnostic performance.
AI.motivation: WSIs contain millions of patches; standard Transformers cannot handle such long sequences. Sampling-based approaches risk losing crucial evidence. A learnable, fixed-token representation aims to preserve diagnostically relevant information under a strict token budget.
AI.method: TC-SSA (Token Compression via Semantic Slot Aggregation) uses a gated routing module with sparse Top-2 routing to assign patches to a fixed number of semantic slots, followed by weighted aggregation to produce a compact slide representation, reducing tokens to about 1.7% of the original. This achieves global slide coverage within a token budget.
AI.result: On SlideBench(TCGA), achieved 78.34% overall accuracy and 77.14% on the diagnosis subset, surpassing sampling-based baselines at similar token budgets. In MIL settings, achieved AUC 95.83% (TCGA-BRCA), 98.27% (TCGA-NSCLC), and 79.80% (PANDA).
AI.conclusion: Learnable semantic slot aggregation provides an effective efficiency-accuracy trade-off for gigapixel pathology reasoning, enabling scalable vision-language pathology models without severely compromising diagnostic performance.

title: Semantic Similarity is a Spurious Measure of Comic Understanding: Lessons Learned from Hallucinations in a Benchmarking Experiment
comment: 8 pages, 2 figures, 3 tables. Includes link to code
summary: A system that enables blind or visually impaired users to access comics/manga would introduce a new medium of storytelling to this community. However, no such system currently exists. Generative vision-language models (VLMs) have shown promise in describing images and understanding comics, but most research on comic understanding is limited to panel-level analysis. To fully support blind and visually impaired users, greater attention must be paid to page-level understanding and interpretation. In this work, we present a preliminary benchmark of VLM performance on comic interpretation tasks. We identify and categorize hallucinations that emerge during this process, organizing them into generalized object-hallucination taxonomies. We conclude with guidance on future research, emphasizing hallucination mitigation and improved data curation for comic interpretation.

AI.tldr: Preliminary benchmark reveals Vision-Language Models struggle with page-level comic interpretation and are prone to hallucinations; a generalized object-hallucination taxonomy is proposed, along with mitigation guidelines and data-curation recommendations.
AI.motivation: Enable blind/visually impaired users to access comics by advancing from panel-level to page-level understanding; address a gap where no end-to-end page-level system exists.
AI.method: Evaluate generative vision-language models on page-level comic interpretation tasks; analyze model outputs to identify hallucinations; categorize hallucinations into an object-hallucination taxonomy; provide guidance for future research and data curation.
AI.result: Established a preliminary benchmark for page-level comic interpretation; identified and organized hallucinations into a generalized object-hallucination taxonomy; offered actionable guidance on hallucination mitigation and data curation for comic interpretation.
AI.conclusion: Hallucination mitigation and targeted data curation are critical for reliable page-level comic understanding; future work should focus on robust evaluation and techniques to reduce hallucinations to support accessibility for blind/VI readers.

title: ConVibNet: Needle Detection during Continuous Insertion via Frequency-Inspired Features
summary:
Purpose: Ultrasound-guided needle interventions are widely used in clinical practice, but their success critically depends on accurate needle placement, which is frequently hindered by the poor and intermittent visibility of needles in ultrasound images. Existing approaches remain limited by artifacts, occlusions, and low contrast, and often fail to support real-time continuous insertion. To overcome these challenges, this study introduces a robust real-time framework for continuous needle detection.
  Methods: We present ConVibNet, an extension of VibNet for detecting needles with significantly reduced visibility, addressing real-time, continuous needle tracking during insertion. ConVibNet leverages temporal dependencies across successive ultrasound frames to enable continuous estimation of both needle tip position and shaft angle in dynamic scenarios. To strengthen temporal awareness of needle-tip motion, we introduce a novel intersection-and-difference loss that explicitly leverages motion correlations across consecutive frames. In addition, we curated a dedicated dataset for model development and evaluation.
  Results: The performance of the proposed ConVibNet model was evaluated on our dataset, demonstrating superior accuracy compared to the baseline VibNet and UNet-LSTM models. Specifically, ConVibNet achieved a tip error of 2.80+-2.42 mm and an angle error of 1.69+-2.00 deg. These results represent a 0.75 mm improvement in tip localization accuracy over the best-performing baseline, while preserving real-time inference capability.
  Conclusion: ConVibNet advances real-time needle detection in ultrasound-guided interventions by integrating temporal correlation modeling with a novel intersection-and-difference loss, thereby improving accuracy and robustness and demonstrating high potential for integration into autonomous insertion systems.

AI.tldr: ConVibNet extends VibNet to enable robust real-time continuous needle detection in ultrasound by exploiting temporal frame dependencies and a novel intersection-and-difference loss, achieving improved tip and angle accuracy on a curated dataset while preserving real-time inference.
AI.motivation: Need for reliable, real-time needle tracking during ultrasound-guided interventions despite poor visibility, artifacts, and occlusions; existing methods struggle with real-time continuous insertion.
AI.method: Extend VibNet to ConVibNet to leverage temporal information across frames for continuous tip and shaft angle estimation; introduce intersection-and-difference loss to enforce motion-consistent tip motion; curate a dedicated dataset for development and evaluation.
AI.result: ConVibNet achieves tip error 2.80±2.42 mm and angle error 1.69±2.00 deg, outperforming baseline VibNet and UNet-LSTM with a 0.75 mm improvement in tip localization, while maintaining real-time inference.
