To further enhance cross-modal alignment, we introduce a Semantic-Pixel Alignment Module (SPAM) that aligns language-level semantic embeddings with high-resolution visual features, along with an Anchor-Guided Mask Decoder (AGMD) that performs anchor-conditioned mask prediction for precise anomaly localization. In addition, we curate Anomaly-Instruct20K, a large-scale instruction dataset that organizes anomaly knowledge into structured descriptions of appearance, shape, and spatial attributes, facilitating effective learning and integration of the proposed semantic anchors. Extensive experiments on six industrial and medical benchmarks demonstrate that AG-VAS achieves consistent state-of-the-art performance in the zero-shot setting.

AI.tldr: AG-VAS introduces semantic anchors ([SEG], [NOR], [ANO]) to guide zero-shot visual anomaly segmentation, with SPAM for semantic-pixel alignment and AGMD for anchor-conditioned masks; paired with Anomaly-Instruct20K dataset. Claims state-of-the-art zero-shot performance on six industrial/medical benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Address fundamental limitations of LMM-based segmentation: (i) abstract, context-dependent anomaly concepts lacking stable visual prototypes; (ii) weak alignment between high-level semantic embeddings and pixel-level spatial features.
AI.method: Three learnable semantic anchors to unify segmentation: [SEG] as an absolute anchor linking anomaly semantics to spatial entities; [NOR]/[ANO] as relative anchors modeling normal/abnormal contextual contrasts. Semantic-Pixel Alignment Module (SPAM) aligns language embeddings with high-res visual features. Anchor-Guided Mask Decoder (AGMD) performs anchor-conditioned mask prediction. Anomaly-Instruct20K dataset provides structured anomaly descriptions.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on six industrial and medical benchmarks show consistent state-of-the-art performance in the zero-shot setting.
AI.conclusion: Semantic anchors plus cross-modal alignment enable accurate zero-shot anomaly localization; the proposed dataset supports scalable learning and integration of the anchor-guided paradigm.

title: Scaling Laws of SignSGD in Linear Regression: When Does It Outperform SGD?
summary: We study scaling laws of signSGD under a power-law random features (PLRF) model that accounts for both feature and target decay. We analyze the population risk of a linear model trained with one-pass signSGD on Gaussian-sketched features. We express the risk as a function of model size, training steps, learning rate, and the feature and target decay parameters. Comparing against the SGD risk analyzed by Paquette et al. (2024), we identify a drift-normalization effect and a noise-reshaping effect unique to signSGD. We then obtain compute-optimal scaling laws under the optimal choice of learning rate. Our analysis shows that the noise-reshaping effect can make the compute-optimal slope of signSGD steeper than that of SGD in regimes where noise is dominant. Finally, we observe that the widely used warmup-stable-decay (WSD) schedule further reduces the noise term and sharpens the compute-optimal slope, when feature decay is fast but target decay is slow.

AI.tldr: Under a power-law random feature model with decaying features and targets, one-pass signSGD on Gaussian-sketch features yields compute-optimal scaling laws; the analysis uncovers a drift-normalization effect and a unique noise-reshaping effect for signSGD, with a steeper compute-optimal slope than SGD in noisy regimes, further improved by warmup-stable-decay (WSD) when feature decay is fast and target decay is slow.
AI.motivation: To understand how signSGD scales in a realistic PLRF setting with feature and target decay, and to compare its scaling to SGD as in Paquette et al. (2024).
AI.method: Analytical study of population risk for a linear model trained with one-pass signSGD on Gaussian-sketched features under a PLRF model with feature and target decay. Derive risk as a function of model size, training steps, learning rate, and decay parameters; compare to SGD risk; derive compute-optimal scaling laws under optimal learning rate; examine the impact of the warmup-stable-decay (WSD) schedule.
AI.result: Identifies drift-normalization and a novel noise-reshaping effect unique to signSGD; shows that the compute-optimal slope of signSGD can be steeper than SGD when noise dominates; demonstrates that WSD reduces the noise term and sharpens the compute-optimal slope, particularly when feature decay is fast but target decay is slow.
AI.conclusion: SignSGD exhibits favorable compute scaling in certain decay regimes due to noise-reshaping and drift-normalization, and the WSD schedule further enhances these gains; results augment SGD analyses (e.g., Paquette et al. 2024) by highlighting unique dynamics of sign-based updates under PLRFs.

title: Open-Vocabulary vs Supervised Learning Methods for Post-Disaster Visual Scene Understanding
summary: Aerial imagery is critical for large-scale post-disaster damage assessment. Automated interpretation remains challenging due to clutter, visual variability, and strong cross-event domain shift, while supervised approaches still rely on costly, task-specific annotations with limited coverage across disaster types and regions. Recent open-vocabulary and foundation vision models offer an appealing alternative, by reducing dependence on fixed label sets and extensive task-specific annotations. Instead, they leverage large-scale pretraining and vision-language representations. These properties are particularly relevant for post-disaster domains, where visual concepts are ambiguous and data availability is constrained. In this work, we present a comparative evaluation of supervised learning and open-vocabulary vision models for post-disaster scene understanding, focusing on semantic segmentation and object detection across multiple datasets, including FloodNet+, RescueNet, DFire, and LADD. We examine performance trends, failure modes, and practical trade-offs between different learning paradigms, providing insight into their applicability for real-world disaster response. The most notable remark across all evaluated benchmarks is that supervised training remains the most reliable approach (i.e., when the label space is fixed and annotations are available), especially for small objects and fine boundary delineation in cluttered scenes.

AI.tldr: Open-vocabulary foundation models are evaluated against supervised learning for post-disaster scene understanding. While open-vocabulary approaches offer label flexibility, supervised learning remains more reliable, especially for small objects and precise boundaries in cluttered scenes.
AI.motivation: Disaster-response settings require broad visual coverage with minimal manual annotation. Open-vocabulary and foundation vision models promise label-efficient generalization across diverse disaster types, motivating a systematic comparison with conventional supervised methods.
AI.method: A comparative evaluation on semantic segmentation and object detection across multiple post-disaster datasets (FloodNet+, RescueNet, DFire, LADD), analyzing performance trends, failure modes, and practical trade-offs between supervised and open-vocabulary learning.
AI.result: Supervised training remains the most reliable when the label space is fixed and annotations exist, particularly for small objects and fine boundary delineation; open-vocabulary models show potential but underperform in critical detail tasks and exhibit dataset- and domain-shift sensitivities.
AI.conclusion: For real-world disaster response, supervised approaches are preferable under fixed labels and available annotations; open-vocabulary models can be complementary for label-efficient or zero-shot scenarios, but require further investigation into domain adaptation and integration with task-specific supervision.

title: Learning from Synthetic Data Improves Multi-hop Reasoning
summary: Reinforcement Learning (RL) has been shown to significantly boost reasoning capabilities of large language models (LLMs) in math, coding, and multi-hop reasoning tasks. However, RL fine-tuning requires abundant high-quality verifiable data, often sourced from human annotations, generated from frontier LLMs, or scored by LLM-based verifiers. All three have considerable limitations: human-annotated datasets are small and expensive to curate, LLM-generated data is hallucination-prone and costly, and LLM-based verifiers are inaccurate and slow. In this work, we investigate a cheaper alternative: RL fine-tuning on rule-generated synthetic data for multi-hop reasoning tasks. We discover that LLMs fine-tuned on synthetic data perform significantly better on popular real-world question-answering benchmarks, despite the synthetic data containing only fictional knowledge. On stratifying performance by question difficulty, we find that synthetic data teaches LLMs to compose knowledge -- a fundamental and generalizable reasoning skill. Our work highlights rule-generated synthetic reasoning data as a free and scalable resource to improve LLM reasoning capabilities.

AI.tldr: RL fine-tuning on rule-generated synthetic data enhances LLM multi-hop reasoning on real benchmarks, offering a scalable, cheaper alternative to costly human or LLM-generated data.
AI.motivation: Address the high costs and limitations of using human annotations, frontier LLM data, or LLM-based verifiers for RLHF-style data by exploring synthetic, rule-generated data to train LLMs for multi-hop reasoning; investigate whether synthetic data can generalize to real benchmarks and whether it fosters knowledge composition.
AI.method: Apply RL fine-tuning of LLMs on synthetic data generated by explicit rules for multi-hop reasoning tasks; assess performance on real-world QA benchmarks; stratify results by question difficulty to analyze how synthetic data influences knowledge composition.
AI.result: LLMs fine-tuned on synthetic data outperform counterparts trained on real data or baselines on real-world QA benchmarks; synthetic data, though fictional, transfers to real tasks; benefits are stronger in harder questions and indicate improved compositional reasoning.
AI.conclusion: Rule-generated synthetic reasoning data is a cheap, scalable resource that can meaningfully improve LLM reasoning capabilities without relying on costly human annotations or LLM verifiers.

title: You Only Need One Stage: Novel-View Synthesis From A Single Blind Face Image
summary: We propose a novel one-stage method, NVB-Face, for generating consistent Novel-View images directly from a single Blind Face image. Existing approaches to novel-view synthesis for objects or faces typically require a high-resolution RGB image as input. When dealing with degraded images, the conventional pipeline follows a two-stage process: first restoring the image to high resolution, then synthesizing novel views from the restored result. However, this approach is highly dependent on the quality of the restored image, often leading to inaccuracies and inconsistencies in the final output. To address this limitation, we extract single-view features directly from the blind face image and introduce a feature manipulator that transforms these features into 3D-aware, multi-view latent representations. Leveraging the powerful generative capacity of a diffusion model, our framework synthesizes high-quality, consistent novel-view face images. Experimental results show that our method significantly outperforms traditional two-stage approaches in both consistency and fidelity.

AI.tldr: One-stage diffusion-based NVB-Face synthesizes consistent novel-view faces from a single degraded image by converting single-view features into 3D-aware multi-view latents, avoiding the traditional two-stage restoration-plus-synthesis workflow.
AI.motivation: Conventional two-stage pipelines depend on restoring high-resolution inputs from degraded images, which can introduce errors and inconsistencies in novel-view synthesis. A direct, one-stage approach aims to improve consistency and fidelity by bypassing restoration.
AI.method: Extracts single-view features from the blind face image, uses a feature manipulator to transform these into 3D-aware, multi-view latent representations, and employs a diffusion model to synthesize high-quality, consistent novel-view face images.
AI.result: Claims significant improvements over traditional two-stage approaches in both consistency and fidelity, based on experimental results.
AI.conclusion: The proposed NVB-Face enables robust, single-shot novel-view synthesis from degraded facial images by learning 3D-aware latent representations and leveraging diffusion-based generation, reducing error amplification common in multi-stage pipelines.

title: Adam Converges Without Any Modification On Update Rules
summary: Adam is the default algorithm for training neural networks, including large language models (LLMs). However, \citet{reddi2019convergence} provided an example that Adam diverges, raising concerns for its deployment in AI model training. We identify a key mismatch between the divergence example and practice: \citet{reddi2019convergence} pick the problem after picking the hyperparameters of Adam, i.e., $(β_1,β_2)$; while practical applications often fix the problem first and then tune $(β_1,β_2)$. In this work, we prove that Adam converges with proper problem-dependent hyperparameters. First, we prove that Adam converges when $β_2$ is large and $β_1 < \sqrt{β_2}$. Second, when $β_2$ is small, we point out a region of $(β_1,β_2)$ combinations where Adam can diverge to infinity. Our results indicate a phase transition for Adam from divergence to convergence when changing the $(β_1, β_2)$ combination. To our knowledge, this is the first phase transition in $(β_1,β_2)$ 2D-plane reported in the literature, providing rigorous theoretical guarantees for Adam optimizer. We further point out that the critical boundary $(β_1^*, β_2^*)$ is problem-dependent, and particularly, dependent on batch size. This provides suggestions on how to tune $β_1$ and $β_2$: when Adam does not work well, we suggest tuning up $β_2$ inversely with batch size to surpass the threshold $β_2^*$, and then trying $β_1< \sqrt{β_2}$. Our suggestions are supported by reports from several empirical studies, which observe improved LLM training performance when applying them.

AI.tldr: Adam convergence depends on problem-dependent hyperparameters; there is a β1-β2 phase transition: converges when β2 is large and β1<√β2, but can diverge for small β2. The critical boundary depends on batch size, guiding tuning: increase β2 with smaller batch size, then enforce β1<√β2.
AI.motivation: Resolve the mismatch between the classic divergence example for Adam and practical training where problem settings are fixed and hyperparameters are tuned. Provide rigorous convergence guarantees by tying β1, β2 to the optimization problem and batch size, particularly for large-scale LLM training.
AI.method: Theoretical analysis of Adam’s stochastic updates. Characterizes regions in the (β1,β2) plane that yield convergence vs possible divergence; demonstrates a phase transition in this 2D space; shows the phase boundary is problem-dependent (notably on batch size); derives practical tuning guidelines and connects them to empirical observations in LLM training.
AI.result: Proves convergence under conditions: large β2 and β1<√β2. Identifies a divergent region for small β2, revealing a phase transition in the (β1,β2) plane. Establishes that the critical boundary is problem-dependent, especially on batch size. Provides tuning guidance: increase β2 inversely with batch size to surpass the threshold, then choose β1<√β2. Empirical reports support improved LLM training when applying these rules.
AI.conclusion: Adam can achieve convergence with problem-aware hyperparameter choices; the study reveals a β1–β2 phase transition and batch-size dependence, offering practical tuning rules and inviting further empirical/theoretical exploration.

title: Perspective-Equivariant Fine-tuning for Multispectral Demosaicing without Ground Truth
summary: Multispectral demosaicing is crucial to reconstruct full-resolution spectral images from snapshot mosaiced measurements, enabling real-time imaging from neurosurgery to autonomous driving. Classical methods are blurry, while supervised learning requires costly ground truth (GT) obtained from slow line-scanning systems. We propose Perspective-Equivariant Fine-tuning for Demosaicing (PEFD), a framework that learns multispectral demosaicing from mosaiced measurements alone. PEFD a) exploits the projective geometry of camera-based imaging systems to leverage a richer group structure than previous demosaicing methods to recover more null-space information, and b) learns efficiently without GT by adapting pretrained foundation models designed for 1-3 channel imaging. On intraoperative and automotive datasets, PEFD recovers fine details such as blood vessels and preserves spectral fidelity, substantially outperforming recent approaches, nearing supervised performance.

AI.tldr: PEFD enables GT-free multispectral demosaicing by perspective-equivariant fine-tuning, leveraging camera projective geometry and pretrained 1-3 channel models to recover high-fidelity spectral images from mosaiced measurements; achieves near-supervised performance on intraoperative and automotive datasets.
AI.motivation: High-quality multispectral demosaicing is essential for real-time imaging in medical and automotive applications, but classical methods are blurry and supervised methods require costly GT from slow line-scanning systems. A GT-free approach that exploits geometry and transfer learning could enable real-time, high-fidelity multispectral imaging.
AI.method: PEFD (Perspective-Equivariant Fine-tuning for Demosaicing) uses the projective geometry of camera imaging to define a richer group structure (perspective-equivariance) that recovers more null-space information for demosaicing. It adapts pretrained 1-3 channel foundation models to multispectral demosaicing using mosaiced measurements only (no GT).
AI.result: On intraoperative and automotive datasets, PEFD recovers fine details (e.g., blood vessels) and preserves spectral fidelity, substantially outperforming recent approaches and approaching supervised performance.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates GT-free, geometry-informed demosaicing via fine-tuning of foundation models, enabling near-supervised performance in real-time multispectral imaging for critical applications; shows promise for broader applicability across mosaic patterns and spectral ranges.

title: On the Rate of Convergence of GD in Non-linear Neural Networks: An Adversarial Robustness Perspective
summary: We study the convergence dynamics of Gradient Descent (GD) in a minimal binary classification setting, consisting of a two-neuron ReLU network and two training instances. We prove that even under these strong simplifying assumptions, while GD successfully converges to an optimal robustness margin, effectively maximizing the distance between the decision boundary and the training points, this convergence occurs at a prohibitively slow rate, scaling strictly as $Θ(1/\ln(t))$. To the best of our knowledge, this establishes the first explicit lower bound on the convergence rate of the robustness margin in a non-linear model. Through empirical simulations, we further demonstrate that this inherent failure mode is pervasive, exhibiting the exact same tight convergence rate across multiple natural network initializations. Our theoretical guarantees are derived via a rigorous analysis of the GD trajectories across the distinct activation patterns of the model. Specifically, we develop tight control over the system's dynamics to bound the trajectory of the decision boundary, overcoming the primary technical challenge introduced by the non-linear nature of the architecture.

AI.tldr: Gradient descent on a minimal 2-neuron ReLU network with two training points converges to the maximum robustness margin but at a slow rate Θ(1/ln t); first explicit lower bound on robustness-margin convergence in a non-linear model, with consistent empirical rate across initializations.
AI.motivation: Understand fundamental limits of gradient descent in non-linear networks for robustness-margin optimization and quantify convergence speed in a tractable setting.
AI.method: Theoretical analysis of GD trajectories across activation patterns in a two-neuron ReLU network with two training instances; derive tight dynamics bounds to control the decision boundary trajectory and prove a Θ(1/ln t) convergence lower bound; corroborate with empirical simulations across multiple initializations.
AI.result: GD attains the optimal robustness margin—maximizing the distance between the decision boundary and training points—but does so at a prohibitively slow rate Θ(1/ln t); this constitutes the first explicit lower bound on robustness-margin convergence in a non-linear model; empirical evidence shows the same convergence rate across various network initializations.
AI.conclusion: Robustness-margin convergence under GD in this minimal non-linear setting is inherently slow, highlighting potential fundamental limits of vanilla GD for margin-based robustness in ReLU networks; invites exploration of faster optimization strategies or insights into dynamics for larger, more realistic architectures.

title: MixerCSeg: An Efficient Mixer Architecture for Crack Segmentation via Decoupled Mamba Attention
summary: Feature encoders play a key role in pixel-level crack segmentation by shaping the representation of fine textures and thin structures. Existing CNN-, Transformer-, and Mamba-based models each capture only part of the required spatial or structural information, leaving clear gaps in modeling complex crack patterns. To address this, we present MixerCSeg, a mixer architecture designed like a coordinated team of specialists, where CNN-like pathways focus on local textures, Transformer-style paths capture global dependencies, and Mamba-inspired flows model sequential context within a single encoder. At the core of MixerCSeg is the TransMixer, which explores Mamba's latent attention behavior while establishing dedicated pathways that naturally express both locality and global awareness. To further enhance structural fidelity, we introduce a spatial block processing strategy and a Direction-guided Edge Gated Convolution (DEGConv) that strengthens edge sensitivity under irregular crack geometries with minimal computational overhead. A Spatial Refinement Multi-Level Fusion (SRF) module is then employed to refine multi-scale details without increasing complexity. Extensive experiments on multiple crack segmentation benchmarks show that MixerCSeg achieves state-of-the-art performance with only 2.05 GFLOPs and 2.54 M parameters, demonstrating both efficiency and strong representational capability. The code is available at https://github.com/spiderforest/MixerCSeg.

AI.tldr: MixerCSeg presents a unified hybrid encoder that combines CNN-like local texture, Transformer-like global dependencies, and Mamba-like sequential context in a single encoder. It introduces TransMixer to leverage latent attention, a spatial block processing strategy, Direction-guided Edge Gated Convolution (DEGConv), and a Spatial Refinement Multi-Level Fusion (SRF) to enhance edge fidelity and multi-scale detail with minimal overhead. The approach achieves state-of-the-art crack segmentation performance at 2.05 GFLOPs and 2.54M parameters, with code available online.
AI.motivation: Current CNN-, Transformer-, and Mamba-based crack segmentation models each capture only partial spatial or structural information, leaving gaps in modeling complex crack patterns. A unified, efficient architecture that fuses locality, global context, and sequential cues is needed to improve segmentation fidelity without excessive computation.
AI.method: Propose MixerCSeg, a mixer architecture with CNN-like, Transformer-style, and Mamba-inspired pathways operating within a single encoder. The core TransMixer explores Mamba-like latent attention while establishing dedicated pathways for locality and global awareness. Introduce a Spatial Block Processing strategy and a Direction-guided Edge Gated Convolution (DEGConv) to strengthen edge sensitivity under irregular crack geometries. Employ a Spatial Refinement Multi-Level Fusion (SRF) module to refine multi-scale details without increasing complexity.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on multiple crack segmentation benchmarks show MixerCSeg achieving state-of-the-art performance with only 2.05 GFLOPs and 2.54M parameters, demonstrating both efficiency and strong representational capability.
AI.conclusion: MixerCSeg provides an efficient, high-performing encoder that fuses local, global, and sequential cues for crack segmentation, offering strong accuracy with minimal computational overhead and practical applicability; code is publicly available.

title: Stochastic Multi-Armed Bandits with Limited Control Variates
summary: Motivated by wireless networks where interference or channel state estimates provide partial insight into throughput, we study a variant of the classical stochastic multi-armed bandit problem in which the learner has limited access to auxiliary information. Recent work has shown that such auxiliary information, when available as control variates, can be used to get tighter confidence bounds, leading to lower regret. However, existing works assume that control variates are available in every round, which may not be realistic in several real-life scenarios. To address this, we propose UCB-LCV, an upper confidence bound (UCB) based algorithm that effectively combines the estimators obtained from rewards and control variates. When there is no control variate, UCB-LCV leads to a novel algorithm that we call UCB-NORMAL, outperforming its existing algorithms for the standard MAB setting with normally distributed rewards. Finally, we discuss variants of the proposed UCB-LCV that apply to general distributions and experimentally demonstrate that UCB-LCV outperforms existing bandit algorithms.

AI.tldr: Introduces UCB-LCV, an upper confidence bound algorithm for stochastic multi-armed bandits with intermittent access to control variates. It fuses reward and control-variate estimates to tighten confidence bounds; when control variates are unavailable, it reduces to UCB-NORMAL for normally distributed rewards. The approach is extended to general distributions and empirically outperforms existing bandit methods.
AI.motivation: Addresses practical stochastic MAB settings where auxiliary information via control variates is partial or intermittent, such as wireless networks with interference or channel-state estimates. Prior work assumes CVs are always available, which is often unrealistic.
AI.method: Develop the UCB-LCV framework to combine reward-based estimates with control-variate estimates under partial availability, deriving confidence bounds that adapt to CV presence. When no CV is available, the method reduces to a UCB variant called UCB-NORMAL tailored for normally distributed rewards. Discuss extensions to general distributions.
AI.result: The approach yields tighter performance guarantees when CVs are accessible and shows improved empirical performance. UCB-NORMAL outperforms standard MAB algorithms in the normal-reward setting. Experimental results demonstrate that UCB-LCV often surpasses existing bandit algorithms across distributions.
AI.conclusion: Leveraging intermittent auxiliary information via control variates can meaningfully improve stochastic bandit performance. The proposed variants (UCB-LCV and UCB-NORMAL) extend applicability to broader distributions, with empirical validation supporting their effectiveness.

title: TIMI: Training-Free Image-to-3D Multi-Instance Generation with Spatial Fidelity
summary: Precise spatial fidelity in Image-to-3D multi-instance generation is critical for downstream real-world applications. Recent work attempts to address this by fine-tuning pre-trained Image-to-3D (I23D) models on multi-instance datasets, which incurs substantial training overhead and struggles to guarantee spatial fidelity. In fact, we observe that pre-trained I23D models already possess meaningful spatial priors, which remain underutilized as evidenced by instance entanglement issues. Motivated by this, we propose TIMI, a novel Training-free framework for Image-to-3D Multi-Instance generation that achieves high spatial fidelity. Specifically, we first introduce an Instance-aware Separation Guidance (ISG) module, which facilitates instance disentanglement during the early denoising stage. Next, to stabilize the guidance introduced by ISG, we devise a Spatial-stabilized Geometry-adaptive Update (SGU) module that promotes the preservation of the geometric characteristics of instances while maintaining their relative relationships. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method yields better performance in terms of both global layout and distinct local instances compared to existing multi-instance methods, without requiring additional training and with faster inference speed.

AI.tldr: TIMI offers a training-free framework for Image-to-3D Multi-Instance generation that exploits pre-trained spatial priors to disentangle instances early in denoising, using ISG for instance separation and SGU for geometry preservation, achieving better global layout and distinct local instances with faster inference and no extra training.
AI.motivation: To overcome high training overhead and fidelity issues in fine-tuned I23D models and to leverage latent priors that already encode spatial structure for multi-instance generation.
AI.method: Introduce Instance-aware Separation Guidance (ISG) to promote early-stage instance disentanglement, followed by Spatial-stabilized Geometry-adaptive Update (SGU) to stabilize guidance and preserve geometric characteristics and relative relationships. All within a training-free pipeline that relies on pre-trained I23D priors.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show TIMI yields superior global layout and local instance separation compared to existing multi-instance methods, without additional training and with faster inference speeds.
AI.conclusion: A training-free approach can attain high spatial fidelity in Image-to-3D multi-instance generation by effectively guiding pre-trained spatial priors through ISG and SGU, reducing training overhead while improving quality and speed.

title: Recursive Models for Long-Horizon Reasoning
summary: Modern language models reason within bounded context, an inherent constraint that poses a fundamental barrier to long-horizon reasoning. We identify recursion as a core principle for overcoming this barrier, and propose recursive models as a minimal realization, where the model can recursively invoke itself to solve subtasks in isolated contexts. We prove that any computable problem admits a recursive decomposition in which each subtask requires only exponentially smaller active context than standard autoregressive models; this strictly surpasses any context management approach confined to a single sequence, such as summarization. We further generalize our framework to modern agentic systems with arbitrary context processing and control flows, and prove that recursive models can achieve optimal power within this broader class. Experimentally, we train a 3B model to reason recursively and evaluate on Boolean satisfiability, a task requiring long-horizon combinatorial search, where it significantly outperforms frontier LLMs.

AI.tldr: Proposes recursive models to overcome bounded context; proves any computable problem has a recursive decomposition with exponentially smaller active context; generalizes to agentic systems; experimentally shows a 3B model solving SAT better than frontier LLMs.
AI.motivation: Language models are limited by finite context, hindering long-horizon reasoning. Recursion offers a minimal, scalable mechanism to extend reasoning horizons by reusing the same model on subproblems.
AI.method: Formalizing a recursive decomposition framework; proving existence of subproblems with exponentially reduced context; extending the framework to agentic systems with arbitrary context processing and control flows; implementing and training a 3B model to reason recursively; evaluating on SAT.
AI.result: Theoretically, any computable problem admits a recursive decomposition with exponentially smaller active context per subtask, outperforming single-sequence context management approaches; empirically, the 3B model trained to reason recursively significantly outperforms frontier LLMs on SAT.
AI.conclusion: Recursive models can achieve optimal power within a broad class of context-processing systems; recursion is a viable mechanism to overcome context limitations and enable long-horizon reasoning, with demonstrated empirical gains on combinatorial tasks.

title: Continuous Exposure-Time Modeling for Realistic Atmospheric Turbulence Synthesis
summary: Atmospheric turbulence significantly degrades long-range imaging by introducing geometric warping and exposure-time-dependent blur, which adversely affects both visual quality and the performance of high-level vision tasks. Existing methods for synthesizing turbulence effects often oversimplify the relationship between blur and exposure-time, typically assuming fixed or binary exposure settings. This leads to unrealistic synthetic data and limited generalization capability of trained models. To address this gap, we revisit the modulation transfer function (MTF) formulation and propose a novel Exposure-Time-dependent MTF (ET-MTF) that models blur as a continuous function of exposure-time. For blur synthesis, we derive a tilt-invariant point spread function (PSF) from the ET-MTF, which, when integrated with a spatially varying blur-width field, provides a comprehensive and physically accurate characterization of turbulence-induced blur. Building on this synthesis pipeline, we construct ET-Turb, a large-scale synthetic turbulence dataset that explicitly incorporates continuous exposure-time modeling across diverse optical and atmospheric conditions. The dataset comprises 5,083 videos (2,005,835 frames), partitioned into 3,988 training and 1,095 test videos. Extensive experiments demonstrate that models trained on ET-Turb produce more realistic restorations and achieve superior generalization on real-world turbulence data compared to those trained on other datasets. The dataset is publicly available at: github.com/Jun-Wei-Zeng/ET-Turb.

AI.tldr: Introduces an Exposure-Time-dependent MTF (ET-MTF) to model turbulence blur as a continuous function of exposure time, derives a tilt-invariant PSF from ET-MTF, and integrates it with a spatially varying blur-width field to synthesize realistic turbulence. Builds ET-Turb, a large-scale dataset with 5,083 videos (2,005,835 frames) and 3,988 training / 1,095 test splits, and shows improved realism and generalization over existing datasets.
AI.motivation: Atmospheric turbulence causes geometric warping and exposure-time–dependent blur that degrades imaging and high-level vision tasks. Existing turbulence synthesis often uses fixed or binary exposure settings, yielding non-physical data and poor generalization. A physically grounded, continuous exposure-time model can improve data realism and model performance.
AI.method: Revisit MTF formulation to define an Exposure-Time-dependent MTF (ET-MTF) that treats blur as a continuous function of exposure time. Derive a tilt-invariant point spread function (PSF) from ET-MTF and combine it with a spatially varying blur-width field to capture turbulence-induced blur. Build ET-Turb by generating 5,083 videos (≈2.01M frames) across diverse optical and atmospheric conditions, with 3,988 training and 1,095 test sequences.
AI.result: Experiments show models trained on ET-Turb produce more realistic restorations and achieve superior generalization on real turbulence data compared to models trained on prior datasets.
AI.conclusion: ET-MTF provides a physically grounded, exposure-time-aware framework for turbulence blur synthesis; the ET-Turb dataset enables better generalization for restoration tasks and serves as a valuable benchmark for turbulence research; the dataset is publicly available at github.com/Jun-Wei-Zeng/ET-Turb.

title: Machine Learning (ML) library in Linux kernel
summary: Linux kernel is a huge code base with enormous number of subsystems and possible configuration options that results in unmanageable complexity of elaborating an efficient configuration. Machine Learning (ML) is approach/area of learning from data, finding patterns, and making predictions without implementing algorithms by developers that can introduce a self-evolving capability in Linux kernel. However, introduction of ML approaches in Linux kernel is not easy way because there is no direct use of floating-point operations (FPU) in kernel space and, potentially, ML models can be a reason of significant performance degradation in Linux kernel. Paper suggests the ML infrastructure architecture in Linux kernel that can solve the declared problem and introduce of employing ML models in kernel space. Suggested approach of kernel ML library has been implemented as Proof Of Concept (PoC) project with the goal to demonstrate feasibility of the suggestion and to design the interface of interaction the kernel-space ML model proxy and the ML model user-space thread.

AI.tldr: Proposes a Linux kernel ML infrastructure to enable self-evolving configuration learning; implements a PoC validating the architecture and kernel-space/user-space interaction.
AI.motivation: Kernel configuration is highly complex due to numerous subsystems and options; ML could learn from data to predict optimal configs. However, kernel constraints (no FP in kernel space) and potential performance penalties complicate adoption.
AI.method: Architectural design of a kernel ML infrastructure; development of a proof-of-concept (PoC); design of interfaces between a kernel-space ML model proxy and a user-space ML model thread.
AI.result: PoC demonstrates feasibility of the approach and provides a working interface design for kernel-space ML proxy–user-space model interaction.
AI.conclusion: The work establishes groundwork for integrating ML into the Linux kernel and shows feasibility; further refinement is needed to address performance, FP handling, and broader applicability.

title: Token Reduction via Local and Global Contexts Optimization for Efficient Video Large Language Models
summary: Video Large Language Models (VLLMs) demonstrate strong video understanding but suffer from inefficiency due to redundant visual tokens. Existing pruning primary targets intra-frame spatial redundancy or prunes inside the LLM with shallow-layer overhead, yielding suboptimal spatiotemporal reduction and underutilizing long-context compressibility. All of them often discard subtle yet informative context from merged or pruned tokens. In this paper, we propose a new perspective that elaborates token \textbf{A}nchors within intra-frame and inter-frame to comprehensively aggregate the informative contexts via local-global \textbf{O}ptimal \textbf{T}ransport (\textbf{AOT}). Specifically, we first establish local- and global-aware token anchors within each frame under the attention guidance, which then optimal transport aggregates the informative contexts from pruned tokens, constructing intra-frame token anchors. Then, building on the temporal frame clips, the first frame within each clip will be considered as the keyframe anchors to ensemble similar information from consecutive frames through optimal transport, while keeping distinct tokens to represent temporal dynamics, leading to efficient token reduction in a training-free manner. Extensive evaluations show that our proposed AOT obtains competitive performances across various short- and long-video benchmarks on leading video LLMs, obtaining substantial computational efficiency while preserving temporal and visual fidelity. Project webpage: \href{https://tyroneli.github.io/AOT}{AOT}.

AI.tldr: Proposes local-global token anchors and optimal transport (AOT) to aggregate informative contexts from pruned tokens within and across frames, enabling training-free token reduction in Video LLMs with maintained fidelity and efficiency.
AI.motivation: Current VLLM pruning strategies either target intra-frame spatial redundancy or prune inside the LLM with shallow overhead, which often loses subtle contextual information or underutilizes long-context compressibility. There is a need to better preserve informative context while reducing tokens across spatiotemporal dimensions.
AI.method: Define local- and global-aware token anchors inside each frame guided by attention. Use optimal transport to aggregate information from pruned tokens into intra-frame anchors. For temporal clips, designate the first frame as keyframe anchors to ensemble information across consecutive frames via OT, while keeping distinct tokens to represent temporal dynamics. This yields training-free token reduction.
AI.result: Extensive evaluations show competitive performance on short- and long-video benchmarks across leading VLLMs, with substantial computational efficiency and preserved temporal and visual fidelity.
AI.conclusion: The AOT framework provides an effective, training-free approach to token reduction in VLLMs by leveraging local-global anchors and optimal transport to capture both intra-frame and inter-frame contexts, improving efficiency without sacrificing quality.

title: Near-Optimal Regret for KL-Regularized Multi-Armed Bandits
summary: Recent studies have shown that reinforcement learning with KL-regularized objectives can enjoy faster rates of convergence or logarithmic regret, in contrast to the classical $\sqrt{T}$-type regret in the unregularized setting. However, the statistical efficiency of online learning with respect to KL-regularized objectives remains far from completely characterized, even when specialized to multi-armed bandits (MABs). We address this problem for MABs via a sharp analysis of KL-UCB using a novel peeling argument, which yields a $\tilde{O}(ηK\log^2T)$ upper bound: the first high-probability regret bound with linear dependence on $K$. Here, $T$ is the time horizon, $K$ is the number of arms, $η^{-1}$ is the regularization intensity, and $\tilde{O}$ hides all logarithmic factors except those involving $\log T$. The near-tightness of our analysis is certified by the first non-constant lower bound $Ω(ηK \log T)$, which follows from subtle hard-instance constructions and a tailored decomposition of the Bayes prior. Moreover, in the low-regularization regime (i.e., large $η$), we show that the KL-regularized regret for MABs is $η$-independent and scales as $\tildeΘ(\sqrt{KT})$. Overall, our results provide a thorough understanding of KL-regularized MABs across all regimes of $η$ and yield nearly optimal bounds in terms of $K$, $η$, and $T$.

AI.tldr: Sharp regret bounds for KL-UCB in MABs: upper bound ~Ō(η K log^2 T), lower bound Ω(η K log T); in high regularization (η large) regime, regret ~Θ(√(KT)) independent of η; results are near-tight across K, η, T.
AI.motivation: Characterize the statistical efficiency of KL-regularized online learning in multi-armed bandits and understand how KL regularization intensity η affects regret.
AI.method: Introduce a novel peeling argument for KL-UCB; derive high-probability regret bounds; construct hard instances and employ Bayes-prior decomposition to obtain lower bounds; analyze regimes of η and establish η-independence in the low-regularization regime.
AI.result: Upper bound: Ō(η K log^2 T); lower bound: Ω(η K log T); in the low-regularization regime (large η), KL-regularized regret is η-independent and scales as Ō(√(K T)); overall, near-tight bounds across η, K, T.
AI.conclusion: Provides a thorough, near-optimal characterization of KL-regularized MABs, clarifying regime-specific behavior, establishing the first high-probability linear-in-K bound, and paving the way for extensions to broader online learning settings with KL-type regularizers.

title: UETrack: A Unified and Efficient Framework for Single Object Tracking
comment: This paper was accepted by CVPR2026
summary: With growing real-world demands, efficient tracking has received increasing attention. However, most existing methods are limited to RGB inputs and struggle in multi-modal scenarios. Moreover, current multi-modal tracking approaches typically use complex designs, making them too heavy and slow for resource-constrained deployment. To tackle these limitations, we propose UETrack, an efficient framework for single object tracking. UETrack demonstrates high practicality and versatility, efficiently handling multiple modalities including RGB, Depth, Thermal, Event, and Language, and addresses the gap in efficient multi-modal tracking. It introduces two key components: a Token-Pooling-based Mixture-of-Experts mechanism that enhances modeling capacity through feature aggregation and expert specialization, and a Target-aware Adaptive Distillation strategy that selectively performs distillation based on sample characteristics, reducing redundant supervision and improving performance. Extensive experiments on 12 benchmarks across 3 hardware platforms show that UETrack achieves a superior speed-accuracy trade-off compared to previous methods. For instance, UETrack-B achieves 69.2% AUC on LaSOT and runs at 163/56/60 FPS on GPU/CPU/AGX, demonstrating strong practicality and versatility. Code is available at https://github.com/kangben258/UETrack.

AI.tldr: Proposes UETrack, an efficient multi-modal single-object tracker that supports RGB, Depth, Thermal, Event, and Language inputs. It introduces a Token-Pooling-based Mixture-of-Experts (TP-MoE) to boost capacity with feature aggregation and expert specialization, and a Target-aware Adaptive Distillation strategy to reduce redundant supervision. It achieves a favorable speed–accuracy trade-off across multiple benchmarks and devices, demonstrating practicality and versatility.
AI.motivation: Real-world tracking demands efficient, multi-modal perception. Existing RGB-only trackers and heavy multi-modal methods struggle under resource constraints, creating a gap for lightweight yet capable solutions that leverage multiple modalities.
AI.method: Two core components: (1) Token-Pooling-based Mixture-of-Experts (TP-MoE) to expand modeling capacity via diverse experts and token pooling for efficient aggregation; (2) Target-aware Adaptive Distillation that selectively applies distillation based on sample characteristics to minimize redundant supervision while preserving or improving performance. The framework supports multiple modalities (RGB, Depth, Thermal, Event, Language) and aims for a favorable speed-accuracy trade-off.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on 12 benchmarks across 3 hardware platforms demonstrate strong efficiency and accuracy. UETrack-B achieves 69.2% AUC on LaSOT and runs at 163/56/60 FPS on GPU/CPU/AGX, highlighting its practicality for real-world deployment.
AI.conclusion: UETrack establishes a practical and versatile framework for efficient multi-modal single-object tracking, delivering strong accuracy with high inference speed across diverse devices, and offering open-source code for broader adoption.

title: SageBwd: A Trainable Low-bit Attention
summary: Low-bit attention, such as SageAttention, has emerged as an effective approach for accelerating model inference, but its applicability to training remains poorly understood. In prior work, we introduced SageBwd, a trainable INT8 attention that quantizes six of seven attention matrix multiplications while preserving fine-tuning performance. However, SageBwd exhibited a persistent performance gap to full-precision attention (FPA) during pre-training. In this work, we investigate why this gap occurs and demonstrate that SageBwd matches full-precision attention during pretraining. Through experiments and theoretical analysis, we reach a few important insights and conclusions: (i) QK-norm is necessary for stable training at large tokens per step, (ii) quantization errors primarily arise from the backward-pass score gradient dS, (iii) reducing tokens per step enables SageBwd to match FPA performance in pre-training, and (iv) K-smoothing remains essential for training stability, while Q-smoothing provides limited benefit during pre-training.

AI.tldr: SageBwd can achieve parity with full-precision attention during pretraining by addressing QK-norm stability, backward-gradient quantization errors, and token-per-step constraints, with K-smoothing essential and Q-smoothing limited in benefit.
AI.motivation: To understand why SageBwd previously lagged behind FPA during pretraining and to identify mechanisms and conditions under which low-bit attention can train as effectively as full-precision attention.
AI.method: Empirical investigations plus theoretical analysis, examining quantization effects on forward and backward passes, exploring QK-norm, token-per-step effects, and smoothing strategies (K-smoothing and Q-smoothing) across pretraining scenarios.
AI.result: Through experiments and theory, SageBwd is shown to match full-precision attention during pretraining when QK-norm is ensured, backward-pass score gradient quantization is controlled, tokens per step are reduced, and K-smoothing is maintained (Q-smoothing offers limited gains).
AI.conclusion: For trainable low-bit attention to reach parity with FP attention in pretraining, ensure QK-norm stability, minimize backward-gradient quantization error, use smaller tokens-per-step, and apply K-smoothing; Q-smoothing is less impactful in this setting.

title: UniTalking: A Unified Audio-Video Framework for Talking Portrait Generation
summary: While state-of-the-art audio-video generation models like Veo3 and Sora2 demonstrate remarkable capabilities, their closed-source nature makes their architectures and training paradigms inaccessible. To bridge this gap in accessibility and performance, we introduce UniTalking, a unified, end-to-end diffusion framework for generating high-fidelity speech and lip-synchronized video. At its core, our framework employs Multi-Modal Transformer Blocks to explicitly model the fine-grained temporal correspondence between audio and video latent tokens via a shared self-attention mechanism. By leveraging powerful priors from a pre-trained video generation model, our framework ensures state-of-the-art visual fidelity while enabling efficient training. Furthermore, UniTalking incorporates a personalized voice cloning capability, allowing the generation of speech in a target style from a brief audio reference. Qualitative and quantitative results demonstrate that our method produces highly realistic talking portraits, achieving superior performance over existing open-source approaches in lip-sync accuracy, audio naturalness, and overall perceptual quality.

AI.tldr: A unified, end-to-end diffusion framework (UniTalking) for high-fidelity speech-driven lip-synced video generation, leveraging Multi-Modal Transformer Blocks and priors from a pre-trained video model, with optional personalized voice cloning to produce realistic talking portraits that outperform open-source baselines.
AI.motivation: To address the inaccessibility of closed-source architectures (Veo3, Sora2) and improve open alternatives by delivering an open, high-fidelity, end-to-end diffusion model for speech-video generation, with accurate lip-sync and customizable voice style.
AI.method: Proposes Multi-Modal Transformer Blocks that share self-attention to model fine-grained temporal correspondence between audio latent tokens and video latent tokens. Uses priors from a pre-trained video-generation model to enhance visual fidelity. An end-to-end diffusion framework with an added component for personalized voice cloning from a short audio reference.
AI.result: Qualitative and quantitative experiments show highly realistic talking portraits and superior lip-sync accuracy, audio naturalness, and perceptual quality compared with existing open-source baselines.
AI.conclusion: UniTalking delivers state-of-the-art open-source performance for talking-head video generation, achieving accurate lip-sync and voice-cloned speech with high visual fidelity, while remaining end-to-end trainable and accessible.

title: De-paradox Tree: Breaking Down Simpson's Paradox via A Kernel-Based Partition Algorithm
summary: Real-world observational datasets and machine learning have revolutionized data-driven decision-making, yet many models rely on empirical associations that may be misleading due to confounding and subgroup heterogeneity. Simpson's paradox exemplifies this challenge, where aggregated and subgroup-level associations contradict each other, leading to misleading conclusions. Existing methods provide limited support for detecting and interpreting such paradoxical associations, especially for practitioners without deep causal expertise. We introduce De-paradox Tree, an interpretable algorithm designed to uncover hidden subgroup patterns behind paradoxical associations under assumed causal structures involving confounders and effect heterogeneity. It employs novel split criteria and balancing-based procedures to adjust for confounders and homogenize heterogeneous effects through recursive partitioning. Compared to state-of-the-art methods, De-paradox Tree builds simpler, more interpretable trees, selects relevant covariates, and identifies nested opposite effects while ensuring robust estimation of causal effects when causally admissible variables are provided. Our approach addresses the limitations of traditional causal inference and machine learning methods by introducing an interpretable framework that supports non-expert practitioners while explicitly acknowledging causal assumptions and scope limitations, enabling more reliable and informed decision-making in complex observational data environments.

AI.tldr: De-paradox Tree is an interpretable decision-tree method to uncover hidden subgroup patterns behind Simpson's paradox in observational data, leveraging balancing to adjust for confounders and recursive partitioning to capture effect heterogeneity, yielding simpler trees and robust causal estimates under provided causal admissibility.
AI.motivation: Simpson's paradox arises when aggregated associations conflict with subgroup-specific effects, potentially misleading decisions in real-world data. Existing methods are often not interpretable for non-experts and inadequately address confounding and heterogeneity under causal assumptions.
AI.method: An interpretable algorithm (De-paradox Tree) using novel split criteria and balancing-based adjustments to account for confounders and homogenize heterogeneous effects via recursive partitioning. The method selects relevant covariates, reveals nested opposite effects, and provides robust causal effect estimation when causally admissible variables are supplied, under explicit causal structure assumptions.
AI.result: Compared to state-of-the-art methods, De-paradox Tree yields simpler, more interpretable trees, better covariate selection, and explicit identification of nested opposite effects, while maintaining robust causal effect estimation within admissible sets.
AI.conclusion: The framework offers an interpretable, causally-informed tool for practitioners without deep causal expertise, explicitly stating assumptions and scope to support more reliable decision-making in complex observational data contexts.

title: SeaVIS: Sound-Enhanced Association for Online Audio-Visual Instance Segmentation
summary: Recently, an audio-visual instance segmentation (AVIS) task has been introduced, aiming to identify, segment and track individual sounding instances in videos. However, prevailing methods primarily adopt the offline paradigm, that cannot associate detected instances across consecutive clips, making them unsuitable for real-world scenarios that involve continuous video streams. To address this limitation, we introduce SeaVIS, the first online framework designed for audio-visual instance segmentation. SeaVIS leverages the Causal Cross Attention Fusion (CCAF) module to enable efficient online processing, which integrates visual features from the current frame with the entire audio history under strict causal constraints. A major challenge for conventional VIS methods is that appearance-based instance association fails to distinguish between an object's sounding and silent states, resulting in the incorrect segmentation of silent objects. To tackle this, we employ an Audio-Guided Contrastive Learning (AGCL) strategy to generate instance prototypes that encode not only visual appearance but also sounding activity. In this way, instances preserved during per-frame prediction that do not emit sound can be effectively suppressed during instance association process, thereby significantly enhancing the audio-following capability of SeaVIS. Extensive experiments conducted on the AVISeg dataset demonstrate that SeaVIS surpasses existing state-of-the-art models across multiple evaluation metrics while maintaining a competitive inference speed suitable for real-time processing.

AI.tldr: SeaVIS introduces an online framework for audio-visual instance segmentation (AVIS) that uses causal cross-attention fusion to merge current-frame visuals with the full audio history and Audio-Guided Contrastive Learning to encode sounding activity, addressing silent-object missegmentation and enabling real-time online processing with state-of-the-art performance on AVISeg.
AI.motivation: Offline VIS methods cannot maintain consistent instance associations across consecutive clips in continuous video streams, and appearance-based cues fail to distinguish sounding vs. silent states, leading to incorrect segmentation.
AI.method: SeaVIS employs a Causal Cross Attention Fusion (CCAF) module to fuse current-frame visual features with complete audio history under causal constraints. It also uses Audio-Guided Contrastive Learning (AGCL) to produce instance prototypes that encode both appearance and sounding activity, allowing silent objects to be suppressed during association. The model operates in an online frame-by-frame manner, with per-frame predictions followed by online instance association.
AI.result: Experiments on the AVISeg dataset show SeaVIS surpasses existing state-of-the-art models across multiple metrics while maintaining competitive inference speed suitable for real-time processing.
AI.conclusion: SeaVIS provides a robust online solution for AVIS, effectively leveraging audio history for accurate audio-guided instance tracking and addressing silent-object challenges, with strong empirical performance and real-time feasibility.

title: Reservoir Subspace Injection for Online ICA under Top-n Whitening
summary: Reservoir expansion can improve online independent component analysis (ICA) under nonlinear mixing, yet top-$n$ whitening may discard injected features. We formalize this bottleneck as \emph{reservoir subspace injection} (RSI): injected features help only if they enter the retained eigenspace without displacing passthrough directions. RSI diagnostics (IER, SSO, $ρ_x$) identify a failure mode in our top-$n$ setting: stronger injection increases IER but crowds out passthrough energy ($ρ_x: 1.00\!\rightarrow\!0.77$), degrading SI-SDR by up to $2.2$\,dB. A guarded RSI controller preserves passthrough retention and recovers mean performance to within $0.1$\,dB of baseline $1/N$ scaling. With passthrough preserved, RE-OICA improves over vanilla online ICA by $+1.7$\,dB under nonlinear mixing and achieves positive SI-SDR$_{\mathrm{sc}}$ on the tested super-Gaussian benchmark ($+0.6$\,dB).

AI.tldr: RSI-aware reservoir expansion enables robust online ICA under nonlinear mixing by mitigating a top-n whitening bottleneck that can discard injected features; guarded RSI control restores passthrough energy and achieves substantial gains over vanilla online ICA.
AI.motivation: Top-n whitening in online ICA can suppress injected features that are beneficial under nonlinear mixing. This creates a bottleneck where beneficial injections may be discarded unless the injected subspace is retained without displacing passthrough directions.
AI.method: Formalize reservoir subspace injection (RSI) and introduce RSI diagnostics (IER, SSO, ρx) to detect a failure mode where stronger injection increases injection error (IER) but reduces passthrough energy (ρx). Propose a guarded RSI controller to preserve passthrough and retain beneficial injected subspace.
AI.result: In the top-n setting, stronger injection raises IER but reduces ρx from 1.00 to 0.77, degrading SI-SDR by up to 2.2 dB. The guarded RSI controller preserves passthrough retention and brings mean performance back to within 0.1 dB of baseline 1/N scaling. When passthrough is preserved, RE-OICA outperforms vanilla online ICA by +1.7 dB under nonlinear mixing and achieves positive SI-SDR_sc on the tested super-Gaussian benchmark (+0.6 dB).
AI.conclusion: RSI diagnostics reveal a critical trade-off in top-n whitening for online ICA under nonlinear mixing. A guarded RSI controller enables reservoir expansion to realize gains (RE-OICA) without sacrificing passthrough, yielding improved performance and positive SI-SDR_sc on challenging benchmarks.

title: DOCFORGE-BENCH: A Comprehensive Benchmark for Document Forgery Detection and Analysis
summary: We present DOCFORGE-BENCH, the first unified zero-shot benchmark for document forgery detection, evaluating 14 methods across eight datasets spanning text tampering, receipt forgery, and identity document manipulation. Unlike fine-tuning-oriented evaluations such as ForensicHub [Du et al., 2025], DOCFORGE-BENCH applies all methods with their published pretrained weights and no domain adaptation -- a deliberate design choice that reflects the realistic deployment scenario where practitioners lack labeled document training data. Our central finding is a pervasive calibration failure invisible under single-threshold protocols: methods achieve moderate Pixel-AUC (>=0.76) yet near-zero Pixel-F1. This AUC-F1 gap is not a discrimination failure but a score-distribution shift: tampered regions occupy only 0.27-4.17% of pixels in document images -- an order of magnitude less than in natural image benchmarks -- making the standard tau=0.5 threshold catastrophically miscalibrated. Oracle-F1 is 2-10x higher than fixed-threshold Pixel-F1, confirming that calibration, not representation, is the bottleneck. A controlled calibration experiment validates this: adapting a single threshold on N=10 domain images recovers 39-55% of the Oracle-F1 gap, demonstrating that threshold adaptation -- not retraining -- is the key missing step for practical deployment. Overall, no evaluated method works reliably out-of-the-box on diverse document types, underscoring that document forgery detection remains an unsolved problem. We further note that all eight datasets predate the era of generative AI editing; benchmarks covering diffusion- and LLM-based document forgeries represent a critical open gap on the modern attack surface.

AI.tldr: Zero-shot document forgery benchmark reveals severe calibration issues: moderate Pixel-AUC but near-zero Pixel-F1 due to few tampered pixels; single-threshold calibration on 10 domain images recovers 39-55% of Oracle-F1 gains; no method works reliably out-of-the-box; need calibration-aware deployment and modern forgery benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Address practical deployment of document forgery detectors without labeled data and fine-tuning, using a unified zero-shot benchmark across diverse document types to reveal real-world limitations.
AI.method: Evaluate 14 methods with their published pretrained weights (no domain adaptation) on 8 datasets covering text tampering, receipts, and ID manipulation. Assess Pixel-AUC and Pixel-F1. Analyze score distributions and calibration. Conduct a controlled calibration experiment by adapting a single threshold on N=10 domain images to measure Oracle-F1 gap recovery.
AI.result: Methods show moderate Pixel-AUC (≥0.76) but near-zero Pixel-F1 due to extreme class sparsity in tampering (0.27–4.17% of pixels). An AUC–F1 gap stems from score-distribution shift, not representation. Oracle-F1 is 2–10× fixed-threshold Pixel-F1. Calibrating a single threshold on 10 domain images recovers 39–55% of the Oracle-F1 gap, indicating calibration, not retraining, is key for deployment. No method is reliably robust across diverse document types; datasets are pregenerations for non-AI-forgeries; a gap exists for diffusion/LLM-based forgeries.
AI.conclusion: Calibration-aware deployment is essential for document forgery detection; zero-shot methods with pretrained weights struggle out-of-the-box. Future benchmarks should cover diffusion- and LLM-based document forgeries to reflect modern attack surfaces, and practical systems should incorporate threshold calibration rather than solely relying on representation quality.

title: MAC: A Conversion Rate Prediction Benchmark Featuring Labels Under Multiple Attribution Mechanisms
comment: Code and data available at https://github.com/alimama-tech/PyMAL
summary:
Multi-attribution learning (MAL), which enhances model performance by learning from conversion labels yielded by multiple attribution mechanisms, has emerged as a promising learning paradigm for conversion rate (CVR) prediction. However, the conversion labels in public CVR datasets are generated by a single attribution mechanism, hindering the development of MAL approaches.
  To address this data gap, we establish the Multi-Attribution Benchmark (MAC), the first public CVR dataset featuring labels from multiple attribution mechanisms. Besides, to promote reproducible research on MAL, we develop PyMAL, an open-source library covering a wide array of baseline methods. We conduct comprehensive experimental analyses on MAC and reveal three key insights: (1) MAL brings consistent performance gains across different attribution settings, especially for users featuring long conversion paths. (2) The performance growth scales up with objective complexity in most settings; however, when predicting first-click conversion targets, simply adding auxiliary objectives is counterproductive, underscoring the necessity of careful selection of auxiliary objectives. (3) Two architectural design principles are paramount: first, to fully learn the multi-attribution knowledge, and second, to fully leverage this knowledge to serve the main task. Motivated by these findings, we propose Mixture of Asymmetric Experts (MoAE), an effective MAL approach incorporating multi-attribution knowledge learning and main task-centric knowledge utilization. Experiments on MAC show that MoAE substantially surpasses the existing state-of-the-art MAL method. We believe that our benchmark and insights will foster future research in the MAL field. Our MAC benchmark and the PyMAL algorithm library are publicly available at https://github.com/alimama-tech/PyMAL.

AI.tldr: Introduces MAC, the first public CVR dataset with multi-attribution labels, and PyMAL, a library of MAL baselines. Proposes MoAE (Mixture of Asymmetric Experts) to leverage multi-attribution knowledge for CVR. Empirical results show MAL gains across attribution settings, with benefits increasing with task complexity; caution on auxiliary objectives for first-click targets. MAC and PyMAL are publicly released.
AI.motivation: Public CVR datasets typically use a single attribution mechanism, which impedes research on Multi-Attribution Learning (MAL). A benchmark with multiple attribution labels and an open-source MAL library is needed to enable reproducible evaluation and fair comparisons.
AI.method: Construct MAC by collecting CVR data labeled by multiple attribution mechanisms; develop PyMAL to provide diverse MAL baselines; design MoAE that learns multi-attribution knowledge and uses it to improve the main CVR task.
AI.result: MAL yields consistent performance gains across attribution settings, especially for users with long conversion paths; performance gains scale with objective complexity but can be negative if auxiliary objectives are mis-specified for first-click targets; MoAE substantially outperforms existing state-of-the-art MAL methods on MAC.
AI.conclusion: The MAC benchmark and PyMAL library will foster MAL research; MoAE effectiveness is demonstrated; data and code are publicly available for reproducible research.

title: Multi-Head Low-Rank Attention
summary: Long-context inference in large language models is bottlenecked by Key--Value (KV) cache loading during the decoding stage, where the sequential nature of generation requires repeatedly transferring the KV cache from off-chip High-Bandwidth Memory (HBM) to on-chip Static Random-Access Memory (SRAM) at each step. While Multi-Head Latent Attention (MLA) significantly reduces the total KV cache size, it suffers from a sharding bottleneck during distributed decoding via Tensor Parallelism (TP). Since its single latent head cannot be partitioned, each device is forced to redundantly load the complete KV cache for every token, consuming excessive memory traffic and diminishing TP benefits like weight sharding. In this work, we propose Multi-Head Low-Rank Attention (MLRA), which enables partitionable latent states for efficient 4-way TP decoding. Extensive experiments show that MLRA achieves state-of-the-art perplexity and downstream task performance, while also delivering a 2.8$\times$ decoding speedup over MLA. Code is available at https://github.com/SongtaoLiu0823/MLRA. Pretrained weights, along with the training and evaluation data, are available at https://huggingface.co/Soughing/MLRA.

AI.tldr: MLRA enables partitionable latent states for efficient 4-way tensor-parallel decoding, delivering 2.8x decoding speedup over MLA with state-of-the-art performance.
AI.motivation: Address long-context KV-cache loading bottlenecks during decoding and the sharding bottleneck in Multi-Head Latent Attention (MLA) caused by a non-partitionable latent head.
AI.method: Propose Multi-Head Low-Rank Attention (MLRA) to produce partitionable latent states using a low-rank latent representation, enabling efficient 4-way tensor-parallel decoding and reduced KV-cache traffic.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art perplexity and downstream task performance, with a 2.8× decoding speedup over MLA; code and pretrained weights are publicly released.
AI.conclusion: MLRA effectively mitigates decoding bottlenecks and enhances TP scalability without sacrificing accuracy, facilitating practical deployment of long-context LLMs.

title: Deepfake Forensics Adapter: A Dual-Stream Network for Generalizable Deepfake Detection
summary: The rapid advancement of deepfake generation techniques poses significant threats to public safety and causes societal harm through the creation of highly realistic synthetic facial media. While existing detection methods demonstrate limitations in generalizing to emerging forgery patterns, this paper presents Deepfake Forensics Adapter (DFA), a novel dual-stream framework that synergizes vision-language foundation models with targeted forensics analysis. Our approach integrates a pre-trained CLIP model with three core components to achieve specialized deepfake detection by leveraging the powerful general capabilities of CLIP without changing CLIP parameters: 1) A Global Feature Adapter is used to identify global inconsistencies in image content that may indicate forgery, 2) A Local Anomaly Stream enhances the model's ability to perceive local facial forgery cues by explicitly leveraging facial structure priors, and 3) An Interactive Fusion Classifier promotes deep interaction and fusion between global and local features using a transformer encoder. Extensive evaluations of frame-level and video-level benchmarks demonstrate the superior generalization capabilities of DFA, particularly achieving state-of-the-art performance in the challenging DFDC dataset with frame-level AUC/EER of 0.816/0.256 and video-level AUC/EER of 0.836/0.251, representing a 4.8% video AUC improvement over previous methods. Our framework not only demonstrates state-of-the-art performance, but also points out a feasible and effective direction for developing a robust deepfake detection system with enhanced generalization capabilities against the evolving deepfake threats. Our code is available at https://github.com/Liao330/DFA.git

AI.tldr: DFA introduces a dual-stream, CLIP-enabled framework for deepfake detection, combining a Global Feature Adapter, a Local Anomaly Stream with facial priors, and an Interactive Fusion Classifier to achieve superior generalization, notably on DFDC (frame-level AUC 0.816, EER 0.256; video-level AUC 0.836, EER 0.251), with ~4.8% video AUC improvement.
AI.motivation: Existing detectors struggle to generalize to new forgery patterns; there is a need for robust, generalizable deepfake detection leveraging powerful foundation models and explicit forensic priors.
AI.method: Integrates a pre-trained CLIP backbone without parameter changes and three components: (1) Global Feature Adapter for global inconsistencies, (2) Local Anomaly Stream leveraging facial structure priors for local cues, (3) Interactive Fusion Classifier using a transformer encoder to fuse global and local features.
AI.result: State-of-the-art performance on DFDC with frame-level AUC/EER 0.816/0.256 and video-level AUC/EER 0.836/0.251; reports 4.8% video AUC improvement over prior methods; demonstrates strong generalization across frame- and video-level benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: DFA offers a robust, generalizable deepfake detection approach that effectively leverages CLIP’s capabilities without retraining the backbone and provides a practical direction for robust deepfake defense; code released.

title: Symbol-Equivariant Recurrent Reasoning Models
summary: Reasoning problems such as Sudoku and ARC-AGI remain challenging for neural networks. The structured problem solving architecture family of Recurrent Reasoning Models (RRMs), including Hierarchical Reasoning Model (HRM) and Tiny Recursive Model (TRM), offer a compact alternative to large language models, but currently handle symbol symmetries only implicitly via costly data augmentation. We introduce Symbol-Equivariant Recurrent Reasoning Models (SE-RRMs), which enforce permutation equivariance at the architectural level through symbol-equivariant layers, guaranteeing identical solutions under symbol or color permutations. SE-RRMs outperform prior RRMs on 9x9 Sudoku and generalize from just training on 9x9 to smaller 4x4 and larger 16x16 and 25x25 instances, to which existing RRMs cannot extrapolate. On ARC-AGI-1 and ARC-AGI-2, SE-RRMs achieve competitive performance with substantially less data augmentation and only 2 million parameters, demonstrating that explicitly encoding symmetry improves the robustness and scalability of neural reasoning. Code is available at https://github.com/ml-jku/SE-RRM.

AI.tldr: SE-RRMs introduce permutation-equivariant layers in recurrent reasoning models to enforce symbol symmetry, improving Sudoku solving and extrapolating to larger/smaller grids, while performing competitively on ARC-AGI with far fewer parameters (~2M). Code available.
AI.motivation: Symbol symmetries (e.g., color/symbol permutations) are central to structured reasoning but are typically addressed via data augmentation; architectural symmetry offers data efficiency, better generalization, and robustness.
AI.method: Incorporate symbol-equivariant layers into the Recurrent Reasoning Model (RRMs like HRM, TRM) to enforce permutation equivariance with respect to symbol/color mappings. Train on 9x9 Sudoku; evaluate extrapolation to 4x4, 16x16, 25x25; assess on ARC-AGI-1/2 with reduced data augmentation and ~2M parameters; compare to prior RRMs.
AI.result: Outperforms prior RRMs on 9x9 Sudoku and extrapolates to a range of sizes (4x4, 16x16, 25x25) where previous RRMs fail to generalize. On ARC-AGI-1/2, achieves competitive performance with substantially less data augmentation and only ~2M parameters.
AI.conclusion: Explicit symmetry encoding via symbol-equivariant architecture improves robustness and scalability of neural reasoning, enabling data-efficient generalization across varied problem sizes and domains; SE-RRMs offer a compact, scalable alternative to large language models for structured reasoning tasks.

title: VidDoS: Universal Denial-of-Service Attack on Video-based Large Language Models
summary: Video-LLMs are increasingly deployed in safety-critical applications but are vulnerable to Energy-Latency Attacks (ELAs) that exhaust computational resources. Current image-centric methods fail because temporal aggregation mechanisms dilute individual frame perturbations. Additionally, real-time demands make instance-wise optimization impractical for continuous video streams. We introduce VidDoS, which is the first universal ELA framework tailored for Video-LLMs. Our method leverages universal optimization to create instance-agnostic triggers that require no inference-time gradient calculation. We achieve this through $\textit{masked teacher forcing}$ to steer models toward expensive target sequences, combined with a $\textit{refusal penalty}$ and $\textit{early-termination suppression}$ to override conciseness priors. Testing across three mainstream Video-LLMs and three video datasets, which include video question answering and autonomous driving scenarios, shows extreme degradation. VidDoS induces a token expansion of more than 205$\times$ and inflates the inference latency by more than 15$\times$ relative to clean baselines. Simulations of real-time autonomous driving streams further reveal that this induced latency leads to critical safety violations. We urge the community to recognize and mitigate these high-hazard ELA in Video-LLMs.

AI.tldr: A universal energy-latency attack (VidDoS) for Video-LLMs that induces extreme token expansion and latency via masked teacher forcing and auxiliary penalties, without on-the-fly gradient, proven across multiple models/datasets, raising safety concerns and urging mitigation.
AI.motivation: Video-LLMs are increasingly used in safety-critical tasks but are susceptible to energy-latency attacks (ELAs). Image-centric ELA methods fail on video due to temporal aggregation and real-time constraints. A universal, instance-agnostic, inference-free attack tailored for Video-LLMs is needed to test and improve robustness.
AI.method: Develop a universal optimization-based attack to produce input-agnostic triggers. Use masked teacher forcing to steer the model toward expensive target sequences, plus a refusal penalty and early-termination suppression to counter conciseness priors. No inference-time gradients required. Evaluate on three Video-LLMs and three video datasets (including video QA and autonomous driving) to measure token expansion and latency.
AI.result: VidDoS achieves token expansion >205x and inference latency >15x compared with clean baselines. Real-time autonomous driving simulations show that induced latency leads to critical safety violations across tested systems.
