AI.conclusion: Decoder-only, sparse-depth-guided adaptation is an effective and practical approach for fast zero-shot depth completion, with robust gains across diverse environments.

title: Sketch2Colab: Sketch-Conditioned Multi-Human Animation via Controllable Flow Distillation
comment: Accepted to CVPR 2026 Main Conference (11 pages, 5 figures)
summary: We present Sketch2Colab, which turns storyboard-style 2D sketches into coherent, object-aware 3D multi-human motion with fine-grained control over agents, joints, timing, and contacts. Conventional diffusion-based motion generators have advanced realism; however, achieving precise adherence to rich interaction constraints typically demands extensive training and/or costly posterior guidance, and performance can degrade under strong multi-entity conditioning. Sketch2Colab instead first learns a sketch-driven diffusion prior and then distills it into an efficient rectified-flow student operating in latent space for fast, stable sampling. Differentiable energies over keyframes, trajectories, and physics-based constraints directly shape the student's transport field, steering samples toward motions that faithfully satisfy the storyboard while remaining physically plausible. To capture coordinated interaction, we augment the continuous flow with a continuous-time Markov chain (CTMC) planner that schedules discrete events such as touches, grasps, and handoffs, modulating the dynamics to produce crisp, well-phased human-object-human collaborations. Experiments on CORE4D and InterHuman show that Sketch2Colab achieves state-of-the-art constraint adherence and perceptual quality while offering significantly faster inference than diffusion-only baselines.

AI.tldr: Sketch2Colab introduces a sketch-driven diffusion prior distilled into a fast rectified-flow latent-space model for 3D multi-human motion guided by 2D storyboard constraints, complemented by a CTMC planner for discrete interaction events; it achieves state-of-the-art constraint adherence and faster inference on CORE4D and InterHuman.
AI.motivation: Enforcing rich interaction constraints in diffusion-based motion generation is costly and slow, especially under strong multi-entity conditioning. A fast, constraint-adherent pipeline is needed to convert storyboard sketches into physically plausible, coordinated 3D motions.
AI.method: 1) Learn a sketch-driven diffusion prior; 2) distill into an efficient rectified-flow student operating in latent space for fast sampling; 3) apply differentiable energies over keyframes, trajectories, and physics-based constraints to shape the transport field toward storyboard-adherent motion; 4) augment the continuous flow with a continuous-time Markov chain (CTMC) planner that schedules discrete events (touches, grasps, handoffs) to coordinate human-object-human interactions.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on CORE4D and InterHuman shows state-of-the-art constraint adherence and perceptual quality, with significantly faster inference than diffusion-only baselines.
AI.conclusion: Sketch2Colab provides a fast, physically plausible, and constraint-adherent solution for 3D multi-human motion generation from 2D sketches by combining a sketch-driven diffusion prior, a rectified-flow latent-space sampler, differentiable energies, and a CTMC-based event planner to manage coordinated interactions.

title: Downstream Task Inspired Underwater Image Enhancement: A Perception-Aware Study from Dataset Construction to Network Design
comment: Accepted for publication in IEEE TIP 2026
summary: In real underwater environments, downstream image recognition tasks such as semantic segmentation and object detection often face challenges posed by problems like blurring and color inconsistencies. Underwater image enhancement (UIE) has emerged as a promising preprocessing approach, aiming to improve the recognizability of targets in underwater images. However, most existing UIE methods mainly focus on enhancing images for human visual perception, frequently failing to reconstruct high-frequency details that are critical for task-specific recognition. To address this issue, we propose a Downstream Task-Inspired Underwater Image Enhancement (DTI-UIE) framework, which leverages human visual perception model to enhance images effectively for underwater vision tasks. Specifically, we design an efficient two-branch network with task-aware attention module for feature mixing. The network benefits from a multi-stage training framework and a task-driven perceptual loss. Additionally, inspired by human perception, we automatically construct a Task-Inspired UIE Dataset (TI-UIED) using various task-specific networks. Experimental results demonstrate that DTI-UIE significantly improves task performance by generating preprocessed images that are beneficial for downstream tasks such as semantic segmentation, object detection, and instance segmentation. The codes are publicly available at https://github.com/oucailab/DTIUIE.

AI.tldr: A downstream-task–aware underwater image enhancement framework (DTI-UIE) that uses a two-branch network with task-aware attention and a task-driven perceptual loss, plus a Task-Inspired UIE Dataset (TI-UIED), to improve semantic segmentation, object detection, and instance segmentation on underwater images.
AI.motivation: Underwater imagery suffers from blur and color shifts that hinder recognition; existing UIE targets human perceived quality and often fails to preserve high-frequency details needed for downstream tasks. There is a need to tailor enhancement to task performance.
AI.method: Two-branch network with a task-aware attention module for feature fusion; multi-stage training; task-driven perceptual loss guiding enhancement toward downstream task cues; TI-UIED constructed automatically using task-specific networks to provide task-relevant supervision; evaluation on multiple underwater vision tasks; code released.
AI.result: DTI-UIE improves downstream task performance (semantic segmentation, object/detection, instance segmentation) compared to baselines; demonstrates that task-aligned enhancement yields better recognition.
AI.conclusion: Aligns UIE with downstream recognition objectives; introduces TI-UIED for task-driven data; promising direction for robust underwater perception; potential extensions to efficiency and broader tasks.

title: Non-verbal Real-time Human-AI Interaction in Constrained Robotic Environments
summary: We study the ongoing debate regarding the statistical fidelity of AI-generated data compared to human-generated data in the context of non-verbal communication using full body motion. Concretely, we ask if contemporary generative models move beyond surface mimicry to participate in the silent, but expressive dialogue of body language. We tackle this question by introducing the first framework that generates a natural non-verbal interaction between Human and AI in real-time from 2D body keypoints. Our experiments utilize four lightweight architectures which run at up to 100 FPS on an NVIDIA Orin Nano, effectively closing the perception-action loop needed for natural Human-AI interaction. We trained on 437 human video clips and demonstrated that pretraining on synthetically-generated sequences reduces motion errors significantly, without sacrificing speed. Yet, a measurable reality gap persists. When the best model is evaluated on keypoints extracted from cutting-edge text-to-video systems, such as SORA and VEO, we observe that performance drops on SORA-generated clips. However, it degrades far less on VEO, suggesting that temporal coherence, not image fidelity, drives real-world performance. Our results demonstrate that statistically distinguishable differences persist between Human and AI motion.

AI.tldr: A real-time framework generating natural Human-AI non-verbal interaction from 2D keypoints; synthetic pretraining reduces motion errors, but a remaining realism gap persists, particularly with SORA vs VEO keypoints; temporal coherence is key.
AI.motivation: Address the debate on whether AI-generated body motion merely mimics surface features or can engage in natural non-verbal interaction; close the perception–action loop for real-time HAI.
AI.method: Propose four lightweight architectures capable of up to 100 FPS on NVIDIA Orin Nano to generate natural interaction from 2D body keypoints; train on 437 human clips; pretrain on synthetic sequences; evaluate on keypoints from SORA and VEO; measure motion errors.
AI.result: Synthetic pretraining reduces motion errors without sacrificing speed; reality gap remains; performance degrades more on SORA than VEO; temporal coherence rather than image fidelity drives performance; statistical differences between human and AI motion persist.
AI.conclusion: While real-time AI motion approaches human-like non-verbal interaction, it remains statistically distinguishable from human motion; synthetic data helps but cannot fully close the gap; performance depends on the temporal coherence of sequences.

title: Neural Operator-Grounded Continuous Tensor Function Representation and Its Applications
summary: Recently, continuous tensor functions have attracted increasing attention, because they can unifiedly represent data both on mesh grids and beyond mesh grids. However, since mode-$n$ product is essentially discrete and linear, the potential of current continuous tensor function representations is still locked. To break this bottleneck, we suggest neural operator-grounded mode-$n$ operators as a continuous and nonlinear alternative of discrete and linear mode-$n$ product. Instead of mapping the discrete core tensor to the discrete target tensor, proposed mode-$n$ operator directly maps the continuous core tensor function to the continuous target tensor function, which provides a genuine continuous representation of real-world data and can ameliorate discretization artifacts. Empowering with continuous and nonlinear mode-$n$ operators, we propose a neural operator-grounded continuous tensor function representation (abbreviated as NO-CTR), which can more faithfully represent complex real-world data compared with classic discrete tensor representations and continuous tensor function representations. Theoretically, we also prove that any continuous tensor function can be approximated by NO-CTR. To examine the capability of NO-CTR, we suggest an NO-CTR-based multi-dimensional data completion model. Extensive experiments across various data on regular mesh grids (multi-spectral images and color videos), on mesh girds with different resolutions (Sentinel-2 images) and beyond mesh grids (point clouds) demonstrate the superiority of NO-CTR.

AI.tldr: NO-CTR introduces neural operator-grounded mode-n operators to replace linear discrete mode-n products, enabling a truly continuous tensor function representation with nonlinear mapping from continuous core tensors to continuous outputs; it provides universal approximation and shows empirical superiority for data completion across meshes and beyond (including point clouds).
AI.motivation: Current continuous tensor representations are limited by mode-n products being discrete and linear, which can cause discretization artifacts and restrict expressivity. A continuous, nonlinear operator-based approach is proposed to unify representations across mesh grids and non-mesh data.
AI.method: Define neural operator-grounded mode-n operators that map a continuous core tensor function to a continuous target tensor function. Assemble these into NO-CTR, a continuous tensor function representation. Prove a universal approximation property: any continuous tensor function can be approximated by NO-CTR. Develop an NO-CTR-based multi-dimensional data completion model and validate on data types including multispectral images, color videos, Sentinel-2 imagery at varying resolutions, and point clouds.
AI.result: NO-CTR provides a more faithful representation of continuous tensor functions than traditional discrete or existing continuous representations. The authors establish a universal approximation theorem for NO-CTR. Empirically, the NO-CTR-based completion model demonstrates superior performance across diverse data regimes: regular grids (multispectral images, color videos), varying resolutions (Sentinel-2), and non-grid data (point clouds).
AI.conclusion: NO-CTR offers a genuine, nonlinear, continuous tensor function representation grounded by neural operators, bridging discrete and continuous perspectives, with theoretical universality and practical effectiveness for high-dimensional data completion.

title: Adaptive Confidence Regularization for Multimodal Failure Detection
summary: The deployment of multimodal models in high-stakes domains, such as self-driving vehicles and medical diagnostics, demands not only strong predictive performance but also reliable mechanisms for detecting failures. In this work, we address the largely unexplored problem of failure detection in multimodal contexts. We propose Adaptive Confidence Regularization (ACR), a novel framework specifically designed to detect multimodal failures. Our approach is driven by a key observation: in most failure cases, the confidence of the multimodal prediction is significantly lower than that of at least one unimodal branch, a phenomenon we term confidence degradation. To mitigate this, we introduce an Adaptive Confidence Loss that penalizes such degradations during training. In addition, we propose Multimodal Feature Swapping, a novel outlier synthesis technique that generates challenging, failure-aware training examples. By training with these synthetic failures, ACR learns to more effectively recognize and reject uncertain predictions, thereby improving overall reliability. Extensive experiments across four datasets, three modalities, and multiple evaluation settings demonstrate that ACR achieves consistent and robust gains. The source code will be available at https://github.com/mona4399/ACR.

AI.tldr: ACR tackles multimodal failure detection by leveraging confidence degradation (multimodal confidence lower than any unimodal branch) and introducing Adaptive Confidence Loss plus Multimodal Feature Swapping to generate failure-like samples, achieving robust gains across diverse datasets and modalities; code released.
AI.motivation: High-stakes multimodal systems (e.g., self-driving cars, medical diagnostics) require reliable failure detection and rejection mechanisms, beyond plain predictive accuracy.
AI.method: 1) Identify confidence degradation as a signal for failures where the multimodal prediction is less confident than at least one unimodal branch. 2) Adaptive Confidence Loss penalizes degradations during training to align multimodal confidence with unimodal confidences. 3) Multimodal Feature Swapping synthesizes challenging, failure-aware examples by swapping features across modalities to create outliers. 4) Train with synthetic failures to improve recognition and rejection of uncertain predictions.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation across four datasets and three modalities under various settings shows consistent, robust gains in failure-detection/rejection performance without sacrificing overall accuracy; source code will be released at the provided URL.
AI.conclusion: ACR provides a reliable framework for detecting and rejecting uncertain multimodal predictions, improving reliability in high-stakes scenarios; the approach combines a diagnostic loss tied to confidence degradation with synthetic failure generation to strengthen the model's out-of-distribution rejection capabilities.

title: Affine Correspondences in Stereo Vision: Theory, Practice, and Limitations
summary: Affine transformations have been recently used for stereo vision. They can be exploited in various computer vision application, e.g., when estimating surface normals, homographies, fundamental and essential matrices. Even full 3D reconstruction can be obtained by using affine correspondences. First, this paper overviews the fundamental statements for affine transformations and epipolar geometry. Then it is investigated how the transformation accuracy influences the quality of the 3D reconstruction. Besides, we propose novel techniques for estimating the local affine transformation from corresponding image directions; moreover, the fundamental matrix, related to the processed image pair, can also be exploited. Both synthetic and real quantitative evaluations are implemented based on the accuracy of the reconstructed surface normals. For the latter one, a special object, containing three perpendicular planes with chessboard patterns, is constructed. The quantitative evaluations are based on the accuracy of the reconstructed surface normals and it is concluded that the estimation accuracy is around a few degrees for realistic test cases. Special stereo poses and plane orientations are also evaluated in detail.

AI.tldr: Affine-geometry stereo review and method; improvements for local affine estimation from image directions; analyzes impact on 3D reconstruction; shows normals accuracy within a few degrees; validated on synthetic and real data including a multi-plane chessboard scene.
AI.motivation: To understand how affine transformation accuracy affects stereo 3D reconstruction and to enhance affine estimation in stereo pipelines, leveraging epipolar geometry and the fundamental matrix.
AI.method: Provide a survey of affine transformations and epipolar geometry; develop novel techniques to estimate local affine transforms from corresponding image directions; exploit the fundamental matrix for improved estimation; perform quantitative evaluations using reconstructed surface normals on synthetic and real data, including a three-planes-with-chessboard scene; test on special stereo poses and plane orientations.
AI.result: Affine-based reconstruction can achieve surface normals accurate to a few degrees under realistic conditions; local affine estimation improves accuracy; results validated on synthetic/real datasets with varied plane orientations.
AI.conclusion: Affine correspondences are a viable tool for 3D reconstruction and related tasks; accuracy in affine estimation directly impacts normal-based surface reconstruction; leveraging image-direction cues and the fundamental matrix enhances estimation.

title: FireRed-OCR Technical Report
summary: We present FireRed-OCR, a systematic framework to specialize general VLMs into high-performance OCR models. Large Vision-Language Models (VLMs) have demonstrated impressive general capabilities but frequently suffer from ``structural hallucination'' when processing complex documents, limiting their utility in industrial OCR applications. In this paper, we introduce FireRed-OCR, a novel framework designed to transform general-purpose VLMs (based on Qwen3-VL) into pixel-precise structural document parsing experts. To address the scarcity of high-quality structured data, we construct a ``Geometry + Semantics'' Data Factory. Unlike traditional random sampling, our pipeline leverages geometric feature clustering and multi-dimensional tagging to synthesize and curate a highly balanced dataset, effectively handling long-tail layouts and rare document types. Furthermore, we propose a Three-Stage Progressive Training strategy that guides the model from pixel-level perception to logical structure generation. This curriculum includes: (1) Multi-task Pre-alignment to ground the model's understanding of document structure; (2) Specialized SFT for standardizing full-image Markdown output; and (3) Format-Constrained Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO), which utilizes reinforcement learning to enforce strict syntactic validity and structural integrity (e.g., table closure, formula syntax). Extensive evaluations on OmniDocBench v1.5 demonstrate that FireRed-OCR achieves state-of-the-art performance with an overall score of 92.94\%, significantly outperforming strong baselines such as DeepSeek-OCR 2 and OCRVerse across text, formula, table, and reading order metrics. We open-source our code and model weights to facilitate the ``General VLM to Specialized Structural Expert'' paradigm.

AI.tldr: FireRed-OCR adapts general VLMs into pixel-precise OCR experts using a Geometry+Semantics data factory and a three-stage training curriculum, achieving state-of-the-art 92.94% on OmniDocBench v1.5 and outperforming OCR baselines.
AI.motivation: General-purpose vision–language models struggle with structural hallucinations on complex documents and lack high-quality structured OCR data. There is a need to convert broad VLM capabilities into reliable, pixel-accurate document parsers, especially for long-tail layouts and diverse document types.
AI.method: Introduce Geometry+Semantics Data Factory to synthesize a balanced, long-tail dataset via geometric feature clustering and multi-dimensional tagging. Employ a Three-Stage Progressive Training: (1) Multi-task Pre-alignment to ground structure understanding; (2) Specialized SFT for full-image Markdown output; (3) Format-Constrained GRPO (reinforcement learning) to enforce syntactic validity and structural integrity (tables, formulas, etc.). Base model is Qwen3-VL, with FireRed-OCR transforming it into a specialized structural expert.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on OmniDocBench v1.5 shows state-of-the-art overall score of 92.94%, with strong gains across text, formula, table, and reading order benchmarks, outperforming DeepSeek-OCR 2 and OCRVerse. Code and weights released to enable the General VLM to Specialized Structural Expert paradigm.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates a viable path from general VLMs to high-precision OCR engines through data synthesis and curriculum-based specialization, achieving substantial performance gains and offering open resources to foster broader adoption.

title: physfusion: A Transformer-based Dual-Stream Radar and Vision Fusion Framework for Open Water Surface Object Detection
summary:
Detecting water-surface targets for Unmanned Surface Vehicles (USVs) is challenging due to wave clutter,
  specular reflections, and weak appearance cues in long-range observations. Although 4D millimeter-wave
  radar complements cameras under degraded illumination, maritime radar point clouds are sparse and
  intermittent, with reflectivity attributes exhibiting heavy-tailed variations under scattering and
  multipath, making conventional fusion designs struggle to exploit radar cues effectively.
  We propose PhysFusion, a physics-informed radar-image detection framework for water-surface perception.
  The framework integrates: (1) a Physics-Informed Radar Encoder (PIR Encoder) with an RCS Mapper and
  Quality Gate, transforming per-point radar attributes into compact scattering priors and predicting
  point-wise reliability for robust feature learning under clutter; (2) a Radar-guided Interactive Fusion
  Module (RIFM) performing query-level radar-image fusion between semantically enriched radar features and
  multi-scale visual features, with the radar branch modeled by a dual-stream backbone including a
  point-based local stream and a transformer-based global stream using Scattering-Aware Self-Attention
  (SASA); and (3) a Temporal Query Aggregation module (TQA) aggregating frame-wise fused queries over a
  short temporal window for temporally consistent representations.
  Experiments on WaterScenes and FLOW demonstrate that PhysFusion achieves 59.7% mAP50:95 and 90.3% mAP50
  on WaterScenes (T=5 radar history) using 5.6M parameters and 12.5G FLOPs, and reaches 94.8% mAP50 and
  46.2% mAP50:95 on FLOW under radar+camera setting. Ablation studies quantify the contributions of PIR
  Encoder, SASA-based global reasoning, and RIFM.

AI.tldr: PhysFusion is a physics-informed radar-image fusion framework for water-surface perception on USVs, combining a PIR Encoder, RIFM with SASA, and TQA to robustly fuse radar and visual cues; achieves state-of-the-art results on WaterScenes and FLOW.
AI.motivation: Detecting water-surface targets is hampered by wave clutter, specular reflections, and sparse, heavy-tailed radar point clouds. Conventional fusion struggles to exploit radar cues under degraded sensing conditions; a physics-informed, temporally aware fusion approach is needed.
AI.method: PIR Encoder with an RCS Mapper and Quality Gate transforms per-point radar attributes into compact scattering priors and predicts point-wise reliability. The Radar-guided Interactive Fusion Module (RIFM) performs query-level radar-image fusion between semantically enriched radar features and multi-scale visual features, with a dual-stream radar backbone: a point-based local stream and a transformer-based global stream using Scattering-Aware Self-Attention (SASA). Temporal Query Aggregation (TQA) aggregates frame-wise fused queries over a short temporal window for temporal consistency.
AI.result: Experiments on WaterScenes and FLOW show strong performance: WaterScenes achieves 59.7% mAP50:95 and 90.3% mAP50 with T=5 radar history (5.6M parameters, 12.5G FLOPs). FLOW with radar+camera reaches 94.8% mAP50 and 46.2% mAP50:95. Ablation studies quantify the contributions of the PIR Encoder, SASA-based global reasoning, and RIFM.
AI.conclusion: PhysFusion demonstrates that physics-informed radar priors combined with radar-image fusion and temporal aggregation can significantly improve water-surface detection under challenging radar conditions, with clear contributions from PIR Encoder, SASA, and RIFM validated by ablations.

title: GroupEnsemble: Efficient Uncertainty Estimation for DETR-based Object Detection
comment: Accepted to IEEE IV 2026. 8 pages, 5 figures
summary:
Detection Transformer (DETR) and its variants show strong performance on object detection, a key task for autonomous systems. However, a critical limitation of these models is that their confidence scores only reflect semantic uncertainty, failing to capture the equally important spatial uncertainty. This results in an incomplete assessment of the detection reliability. On the other hand, Deep Ensembles can tackle this by providing high-quality spatial uncertainty estimates. However, their immense memory consumption makes them impractical for real-world applications. A cheaper alternative, Monte Carlo (MC) Dropout, suffers from high latency due to the need of multiple forward passes during inference to estimate uncertainty.
  To address these limitations, we introduce GroupEnsemble, an efficient and effective uncertainty estimation method for DETR-like models. GroupEnsemble simultaneously predicts multiple individual detection sets by feeding additional diverse groups of object queries to the transformer decoder during inference. Each query group is transformed by the shared decoder in isolation and predicts a complete detection set for the same input. An attention mask is applied to the decoder to prevent inter-group query interactions, ensuring each group detects independently to achieve reliable ensemble-based uncertainty estimation. By leveraging the decoder's inherent parallelism, GroupEnsemble efficiently estimates uncertainty in a single forward pass without sequential repetition. We validated our method under autonomous driving scenes and common daily scenes using the Cityscapes and COCO datasets, respectively. The results show that a hybrid approach combining MC-Dropout and GroupEnsemble outperforms Deep Ensembles on several metrics at a fraction of the cost. The code is available at https://github.com/yutongy98/GroupEnsemble.

AI.tldr: GroupEnsemble enables ensemble-like spatial uncertainty for DETR by running multiple independent query groups through the transformer decoder in a single forward pass, using attention masks to prevent inter-group interaction; a hybrid with MC-Dropout further improves performance at lower cost.
AI.motivation: Address the lack of spatial uncertainty in DETR and avoid the high cost of Deep Ensembles and MC-Dropout latency, enabling reliable detection uncertainty estimation for autonomous systems.
AI.method: During inference, feed several diverse groups of object queries to the DETR-style decoder. Each group is processed in isolation via a shared decoder with an attention mask that prevents inter-group interactions, producing independent detection sets. This enables ensemble-like uncertainty estimation in one forward pass. The approach leveraged the decoder's parallelism; a hybrid with MC-Dropout was also explored.
AI.result: Empirical validation on Cityscapes (driving scenes) and COCO (common scenes) shows that GroupEnsemble yields better uncertainty metrics than Deep Ensembles on several measures; combining MC-Dropout with GroupEnsemble further improves performance at a fraction of the cost.
AI.conclusion: GroupEnsemble provides an efficient, scalable means to estimate spatial uncertainty for DETR-like detectors, mitigating latency and memory concerns of traditional ensembles while preserving reliable uncertainty estimates; code is publicly available.

title: CTForensics: A Comprehensive Dataset and Method for AI-Generated CT Image Detection
summary: With the rapid development of generative AI in medical imaging, synthetic Computed Tomography (CT) images have demonstrated great potential in applications such as data augmentation and clinical diagnosis, but they also introduce serious security risks. Despite the increasing security concerns, existing studies on CT forgery detection are still limited and fail to adequately address real-world challenges. These limitations are mainly reflected in two aspects: the absence of datasets that can effectively evaluate model generalization to reflect the real-world application requirements, and the reliance on detection methods designed for natural images that are insensitive to CT-specific forgery artifacts. In this view, we propose CTForensics, a comprehensive dataset designed to systematically evaluate the generalization capability of CT forgery detection methods, which includes ten diverse CT generative methods. Moreover, we introduce the Enhanced Spatial-Frequency CT Forgery Detector (ESF-CTFD), an efficient CNN-based neural network that captures forgery cues across the wavelet, spatial, and frequency domains. First, it transforms the input CT image into three scales and extracts features at each scale via the Wavelet-Enhanced Central Stem. Then, starting from the largest-scale features, the Spatial Process Block gradually performs feature fusion with the smaller-scale ones. Finally, the Frequency Process Block learns frequency-domain information for predicting the final results. Experiments demonstrate that ESF-CTFD consistently outperforms existing methods and exhibits superior generalization across different CT generative models.

AI.tldr: CTForensics provides a dataset to evaluate generalization of CT forgery detectors across 10 generative methods; ESF-CTFD is a CNN that fuses wavelet, spatial, and frequency cues across three scales for robust CT forgery detection and shows strong generalization.
AI.motivation: There is a need for CT-specific forgery datasets and detectors because existing methods for natural images fail to capture CT-specific forgery artifacts and real-world generalization is limited.
AI.method: CTForensics dataset comprising ten diverse CT generative methods to assess generalization; ESF-CTFD network using a three-scale input with Wavelet-Enhanced Central Stem, Spatial Process Block for multi-scale feature fusion, and Frequency Process Block for frequency-domain analysis, culminating in a forgery prediction.
AI.result: Empirical results show ESF-CTFD outperforms existing methods and generalizes better across different CT generative models.
AI.conclusion: Providing a CT-specific forgery dataset and a multi-domain CNN detector enhances generalization and robustness of CT forgery detection in real-world settings.

title: Resolving Blind Inverse Problems under Dynamic Range Compression via Structured Forward Operator Modeling
summary: Recovering radiometric fidelity from unknown dynamic range compression (UDRC), such as low-light enhancement and HDR reconstruction, is a challenging blind inverse problem, due to the unknown forward model and irreversible information loss introduced by compression. To address this challenge, we first identify monotonicity as the fundamental physical invariant shared across UDRC tasks. Leveraging this insight, we introduce the \textbf{cascaded monotonic Bernstein} (CaMB) operator to parameterize the unknown forward model. CaMB enforces monotonicity as a hard architectural inductive bias, constraining optimization to physically consistent mappings and enabling robust and stable operator estimation. We further integrate CaMB with a plug-and-play diffusion framework, proposing \textbf{CaMB-Diff}. Within this framework, the diffusion model serves as a powerful geometric prior for structural and semantic recovery, while CaMB explicitly models and corrects radiometric distortions through a physically grounded forward operator. Extensive experiments on a variety of zero-shot UDRC tasks, including low-light enhancement, low-field MRI enhancement, and HDR reconstruction, demonstrate that CaMB-Diff significantly outperforms state-of-the-art zero-shot baselines in terms of both signal fidelity and physical consistency. Moreover, we empirically validate the effectiveness of the proposed CaMB parameterization in accurately modeling the unknown forward operator.

AI.tldr: Proposes CaMB-Diff, a plug-and-play diffusion framework for unknown dynamic range compression (UDRC) tasks that enforces a monotonic forward operator via cascaded monotonic Bernstein (CaMB) parameterization, yielding improved signal fidelity and physical consistency in zero-shot settings (low-light enhancement, low-field MRI, HDR).
AI.motivation: UDRC is a blind inverse problem with unknown forward model and irreversible information loss. A fundamental invariant across UDRC tasks is monotonicity, which can be exploited to constrain the forward operator.
AI.method: Introduce cascaded monotonic Bernstein (CaMB) operator to parameterize the forward model with monotonicity as a hard inductive bias. Integrate CaMB with a plug-and-play diffusion framework (CaMB-Diff) where the diffusion model acts as a geometric prior and CaMB models/corrects radiometric distortions through a physically grounded forward operator.
AI.result: CaMB-Diff achieves substantial improvements over state-of-the-art zero-shot baselines in signal fidelity and physical consistency across diverse UDRC tasks (low-light enhancement, low-field MRI enhancement, HDR reconstruction). Empirical validation confirms CaMB's effectiveness in accurately modeling the unknown forward operator.
AI.conclusion: Monotonicity is a fundamental invariant for UDRC; the CaMB parameterization provides a robust, physically grounded forward model that, when integrated with diffusion priors, yields stable, high-fidelity reconstructions in zero-shot settings.

title: Generative Visual Chain-of-Thought for Image Editing
summary: Existing image editing methods struggle to perceive where to edit, especially under complex scenes and nuanced spatial instructions. To address this issue, we propose Generative Visual Chain-of-Thought (GVCoT), a unified framework that performs native visual reasoning by first generating spatial cues to localize the target region and then executing the edit. Unlike prior text-only CoT or tool-dependent visual CoT paradigms, GVCoT jointly optimizes visual tokens generated during the reasoning and editing phases in an end-to-end manner. This way fosters the emergence of innate spatial reasoning ability and enables more effective utilization of visual-domain cues. The main challenge of training GCVoT lies in the scarcity of large-scale editing data with precise edit region annotations; to this end, we construct GVCoT-Edit-Instruct, a dataset of 1.8M high-quality samples spanning 19 tasks. We adopt a progressive training strategy: supervised fine-tuning to build foundational localization ability in reasoning trace before final editing, followed by reinforcement learning to further improve reasoning and editing quality. Finally, we introduce SREdit-Bench, a new benchmark designed to comprehensively stress-test models under sophisticated scenes and fine-grained referring expressions. Experiments demonstrate that GVCoT consistently outperforms state-of-the-art models on SREdit-Bench and ImgEdit. We hope our GVCoT will inspire future research toward interpretable and precise image editing.

AI.tldr: A unified GVCoT framework for native visual reasoning in image editing, with a large 1.8M dataset and progressive training, achieving state-of-the-art results on SREdit-Bench and ImgEdit.
AI.motivation: To address the lack of explicit spatial localization guidance in existing image-editing models and overcome dependence on text-only reasoning or tool-based CoT, by end-to-end optimizing visual reasoning and editing.
AI.method: Generate spatial cues to localize edit regions and jointly optimize visual tokens for reasoning and editing. Build GVCoT-Edit-Instruct (1.8M samples, 19 tasks). Progressive training: supervised fine-tuning to bootstrap localization, followed by reinforcement learning to refine reasoning and editing quality.
AI.result: GVCoT outperforms state-of-the-art models on SREdit-Bench and ImgEdit, with improved interpretability through visible reasoning traces and more precise edits.
AI.conclusion: End-to-end visual reasoning with generated spatial cues enhances precise image editing and suggests a path for more interpretable, data-efficient, visual-editing models.

title: Zero-shot Low-Field MRI Enhancement via Diffusion-Based Adaptive Contrast Transport
summary: Low-field (LF) magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) democratizes access to diagnostic imaging but is fundamentally limited by low signal-to-noise ratio and significant tissue contrast distortion due to field-dependent relaxation dynamics. Reconstructing high-field (HF) quality images from LF data is a blind inverse problem, severely challenged by the scarcity of paired training data and the unknown, non-linear contrast transformation operator. Existing zero-shot methods, which assume simplified linear degradation, often fail to recover authentic tissue contrast. In this paper, we propose DACT(Diffusion-Based Adaptive Contrast Transport), a novel zero-shot framework that restores HF-quality images without paired supervision. DACT synergizes a pre-trained HF diffusion prior to ensure anatomical fidelity with a physically-informed adaptive forward model. Specifically, we introduce a differentiable Sinkhorn optimal transport module that explicitly models and corrects the intensity distribution shift between LF and HF domains during the reverse diffusion process. This allows the framework to dynamically learn the intractable contrast mapping while preserving topological consistency. Extensive experiments on simulated and real clinical LF datasets demonstrate that DACT achieves state-of-the-art performance, yielding reconstructions with superior structural detail and correct tissue contrast.

AI.tldr: DACT is a zero-shot MRI reconstruction framework that uses a pre-trained high-field diffusion prior and a differentiable Sinkhorn optimal transport-based forward model to map low-field MRI to high-field MRI without paired data, addressing non-linear contrast shifts while preserving anatomical structure.
AI.motivation: Low-field MRI suffers from low SNR and tissue contrast distortion; reconstructing HF-quality images from LF data is ill-posed due to lack of paired data and unknown non-linear contrast transformation.
AI.method: Leverages a pre-trained HF diffusion prior to enforce anatomical fidelity and introduces a differentiable Sinkhorn optimal transport module to model and correct LF-to-HF intensity distribution shifts during reverse diffusion; couples a physically-informed adaptive forward model with the diffusion process for zero-shot learning.
AI.result: Extensive simulations and real clinical LF datasets show state-of-the-art performance, with reconstructions that exhibit superior structural detail and correct tissue contrast compared to baselines.
AI.conclusion: DACT effectively combines diffusion priors with an adaptive OT-based forward model to enable zero-shot LF-to-HF MRI reconstruction, preserving topology and improving tissue contrast.

title: LaST-VLA: Thinking in Latent Spatio-Temporal Space for Vision-Language-Action in Autonomous Driving
summary: While Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models have revolutionized autonomous driving by unifying perception and planning, their reliance on explicit textual Chain-of-Thought (CoT) leads to semantic-perceptual decoupling and perceptual-symbolic conflicts. Recent shifts toward latent reasoning attempt to bypass these bottlenecks by thinking in continuous hidden space. However, without explicit intermediate constraints, standard latent CoT often operates as a physics-agnostic representation. To address this, we propose the Latent Spatio-Temporal VLA (LaST-VLA), a framework shifting the reasoning paradigm from discrete symbolic processing into a physically grounded Latent Spatio-Temporal CoT. By implementing a dual-feature alignment mechanism, we distill geometric constraints from 3D foundation models and dynamic foresight from world models directly into the latent space. Coupled with a progressive SFT training strategy that transitions from feature alignment to trajectory generation, and refined via Reinforcement Learning with Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) to ensure safety and rule compliance. \method~setting a new record on NAVSIM v1 (91.3 PDMS) and NAVSIM v2 (87.1 EPDMS), while excelling in spatial-temporal reasoning on SURDS and NuDynamics benchmarks.

AI.tldr: LaST-VLA: a physically grounded latent spatio-temporal CoT for VLA in autonomous driving, achieving state-of-the-art on NAVSIM, SURDS, and NuDynamics via dual-feature alignment, progressive SFT, and GRPO-based RL.
AI.motivation: Address semantic-perceptual decoupling caused by explicit textual chain-of-thought by replacing discrete symbolic reasoning with a physically grounded latent spatio-temporal reasoning framework.
AI.method: Proposes Latent Spatio-Temporal VLA (LaST-VLA) with a dual-feature alignment mechanism that distills geometric constraints from 3D foundation models and dynamic foresight from world models into latent space. Employs a progressive Student-Forcing Training (SFT) strategy transitioning from feature alignment to trajectory generation, and uses Reinforcement Learning with Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) to enforce safety and rule compliance.
AI.result: Sets new records on NAVSIM v1 (91.3 PDMS) and NAVSIM v2 (87.1 EPDMS); demonstrates strong spatial-temporal reasoning on SURDS and NuDynamics benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Physically grounded latent CoT improves integration of perception and planning, enabling safer and more accurate autonomous driving performance, with effective latent reasoning that aligns geometry and dynamics in the latent space.

title: MAP-Diff: Multi-Anchor Guided Diffusion for Progressive 3D Whole-Body Low-Dose PET Denoising
summary: Low-dose Positron Emission Tomography (PET) reduces radiation exposure but suffers from severe noise and quantitative degradation. Diffusion-based denoising models achieve strong final reconstructions, yet their reverse trajectories are typically unconstrained and not aligned with the progressive nature of PET dose formation. We propose MAP-Diff, a multi-anchor guided diffusion framework for progressive 3D whole-body PET denoising. MAP-Diff introduces clinically observed intermediate-dose scans as trajectory anchors and enforces timestep-dependent supervision to regularize the reverse process toward dose-aligned intermediate states. Anchor timesteps are calibrated via degradation matching between simulated diffusion corruption and real multi-dose PET pairs, and a timestep-weighted anchor loss stabilizes stage-wise learning. At inference, the model requires only ultra-low-dose input while enabling progressive, dose-consistent intermediate restoration. Experiments on internal (Siemens Biograph Vision Quadra) and cross-scanner (United Imaging uEXPLORER) datasets show consistent improvements over strong CNN-, Transformer-, GAN-, and diffusion-based baselines. On the internal dataset, MAP-Diff improves PSNR from 42.48 dB to 43.71 dB (+1.23 dB), increases SSIM to 0.986, and reduces NMAE from 0.115 to 0.103 (-0.012) compared to 3D DDPM. Performance gains generalize across scanners, achieving 34.42 dB PSNR and 0.141 NMAE on the external cohort, outperforming all competing methods.

AI.tldr: MAP-Diff: a multi-anchor guided diffusion framework for progressive 3D whole-body PET denoising that uses intermediate-dose scans as trajectory anchors and timestep-weighted supervision to enforce dose-aligned reconstruction; demonstrates consistent gains over multiple baselines on internal and external datasets.
AI.motivation: Low-dose PET yields severe noise and quantitative degradation; conventional diffusion-based denoisers have unconstrained reverse trajectories that ignore the dose formation process. Incorporating clinically observed intermediate-dose states as trajectory anchors can regularize the reverse process to be dose-consistent.
AI.method: MAP-Diff introduces multiple trajectory anchors corresponding to intermediate-dose states. Anchors are calibrated via degradation matching between simulated diffusion corruption and real multi-dose PET pairs. A timestep-weighted anchor loss stabilizes stage-wise learning. At inference, ultra-low-dose input is denoised progressively to produce dose-consistent intermediate reconstructions.
AI.result: Internal dataset (Siemens Biograph Vision Quadra): PSNR 43.71 dB (+1.23 dB from 42.48), SSIM 0.986, NMAE 0.103 (−0.012). Cross-scanner external (United Imaging uEXPLORER): 34.42 dB PSNR, 0.141 NMAE; outperforms CNN/Transformer/GAN and diffusion baselines.
AI.conclusion: MAP-Diff delivers dose-aligned progressive denoising for 3D whole-body PET, generalizes across scanners, and yields quantitative improvements over strong baselines, demonstrating practical potential for clinical PET denoising pipelines.

title: BAWSeg: A UAV Multispectral Benchmark for Barley Weed Segmentation
summary: Accurate weed mapping in cereal fields requires pixel-level segmentation from UAV imagery that remains reliable across fields, seasons, and illumination. Existing multispectral pipelines often depend on thresholded vegetation indices, which are brittle under radiometric drift and mixed crop--weed pixels, or on single-stream CNN and Transformer backbones that ingest stacked bands and indices, where radiance cues and normalized index cues interfere and reduce sensitivity to small weed clusters embedded in crop canopies. We propose VISA (Vegetation-Index and Spectral Attention), a two-stream segmentation network that decouples these cues and fuses them at native resolution. The radiance stream learns from calibrated five-band reflectance using residual spectral-spatial attention to preserve fine textures and row boundaries that are attenuated by ratio indices. The index stream operates on vegetation-index maps with windowed self-attention to model local structure efficiently, state-space layers to propagate field-scale context without quadratic attention cost, and Slot Attention to form stable region descriptors that improve discrimination of sparse weeds under canopy mixing. To support supervised training and deployment-oriented evaluation, we introduce BAWSeg, a four-year UAV multispectral dataset collected over commercial barley paddocks in Western Australia, providing radiometrically calibrated blue, green, red, red edge, and near-infrared orthomosaics, derived vegetation indices, and dense crop, weed, and other labels with leakage-free block splits. On BAWSeg, VISA achieves 75.6% mIoU and 63.5% weed IoU with 22.8M parameters, outperforming a multispectral SegFormer-B1 baseline by 1.2 mIoU and 1.9 weed IoU. Under cross-plot and cross-year protocols, VISA maintains 71.2% and 69.2% mIoU, respectively. The BAWSeg data, VISA code, and trained models will be released upon publication.

AI.tldr: VISA is a two-stream segmentation network for pixel-level weed mapping in cereal fields, decoupling radiance and vegetation-index cues and fusing them at native resolution. Trained on BAWSeg, it achieves state-of-the-art mIoU/weed IoU with robust cross-plot/year performance and will release datasets/code.
AI.motivation: Need robust weed mapping across fields, seasons, and illumination where radiometric drift and canopy mixing degrade traditional thresholded indices and single-stream models.
AI.method: Two streams: radiance stream using calibrated five-band reflectance with residual spectral-spatial attention to preserve textures; index stream on vegetation-index maps using windowed self-attention, state-space layers for field-scale context, and Slot Attention for stable region descriptors. Fusion occurs at native resolution.
AI.result: On BAWSeg: 75.6% mIoU and 63.5% weed IoU with 22.8M parameters; outperforms SegFormer-B1 baseline by 1.2 mIoU and 1.9 weed IoU. Cross-plot mIoU 71.2%; cross-year mIoU 69.2%.
AI.conclusion: VISA effectively decouples radiometric and index cues to improve sensitivity to sparse weeds under canopy and maintains strong generalization across plots/years; BAWSeg provides a radiometrically calibrated, multi-spectral benchmark and the authors will release data/code.

title: MobileMold: A Smartphone-Based Microscopy Dataset for Food Mold Detection
comment: Accepted to ACM Multimedia Systems (MMSys'26). Dataset and code available at https://mobilemold.github.io/dataset/
summary: Smartphone clip-on microscopes turn everyday devices into low-cost, portable imaging systems that can even reveal fungal structures at the microscopic level, enabling mold inspection beyond unaided visual checks. In this paper, we introduce MobileMold, an open smartphone-based microscopy dataset for food mold detection and food classification. MobileMold contains 4,941 handheld microscopy images spanning 11 food types, 4 smartphones, 3 microscopes, and diverse real-world conditions. Beyond the dataset release, we establish baselines for (i) mold detection and (ii) food-type classification, including a multi-task setting that predicts both attributes. Across multiple pretrained deep learning architectures and augmentation strategies, we obtain near-ceiling performance (accuracy = 0.9954, F1 = 0.9954, MCC = 0.9907), validating the utility of our dataset for detecting food spoilage. To increase transparency, we complement our evaluation with saliency-based visual explanations highlighting mold regions associated with the model's predictions. MobileMold aims to contribute to research on accessible food-safety sensing, mobile imaging, and exploring the potential of smartphones enhanced with attachments.

AI.tldr: MobileMold is a new open smartphone-based microscopy dataset for food mold detection and food type classification, collected with clip-on microscopes across 4 smartphones and 3 microscopes in 11 food types (4,941 images). Baselines across several DL architectures show near-ceiling performance, with multi-task learning predicting both mold presence and food type, and saliency explanations to highlight mold regions, aiming to advance accessible food-safety sensing and mobile imaging.
AI.motivation: To enable accessible, low-cost mold inspection and food spoilage detection using smartphone-enabled microscopy by creating a diverse, real-world dataset and establishing strong baselines.
AI.method: Data collection of 4,941 handheld microscopy images across 11 food types, 4 smartphones, and 3 microscopes under diverse real-world conditions. Evaluation includes mold detection, food-type classification, and a multi-task setting. Multiple pretrained deep learning architectures with various augmentation strategies are used. Saliency-based visual explanations are provided to highlight mold regions.
AI.result: Achieved near-ceiling performance with accuracy 0.9954, F1 0.9954, MCC 0.9907 across tasks and architectures. Baselines for mold detection and food-type classification are established, and a multi-task model jointly predicts both attributes. Saliency maps enhance transparency.
AI.conclusion: MobileMold provides a valuable dataset and baselines for accessible food-safety sensing, mobile imaging, and use of smartphone attachments for microscopy, supporting research into mold detection and food classification with interpretable explanations.

title: PreSight: Preoperative Outcome Prediction for Parkinson's Disease via Region-Prior Morphometry and Patient-Specific Weighting
summary: Preoperative improvement rate prediction for Parkinson's disease surgery is clinically important yet difficult because imaging signals are subtle and patients are heterogeneous. We address this setting, where only information available before surgery is used, and the goal is to predict patient-specific postoperative motor benefit. We present PreSight, a presurgical outcome model that fuses clinical priors with preoperative MRI and deformation-based morphometry (DBM) and adapts regional importance through a patient-specific weighting module. The model produces end-to-end, calibrated, decision-ready predictions with patient-level explanations. We evaluate PreSight on a real-world two-center cohort of 400 subjects with multimodal presurgical inputs and postoperative improvement labels. PreSight outperforms strong clinical, imaging-only, and multimodal baselines. It attains 88.89% accuracy on internal validation and 85.29% on an external-center test for responder classification and shows better probability calibration and higher decision-curve net benefit. Ablations and analyses confirm the contribution of DBM and the patient-specific weighting module and indicate that the model emphasizes disease-relevant regions in a patient-specific manner. These results demonstrate that integrating clinical prior knowledge with region-adaptive morphometry enables reliable presurgical decision support in routine practice.

AI.tldr: PreSight predicts presurgical motor improvement for Parkinson's surgery using multimodal data with region-adaptive morphometry; high accuracy and calibration; provides interpretable, patient-specific explanations; outperforms baselines in two-center study.
AI.motivation: Preoperative forecasting of motor benefit is clinically important but challenging due to subtle imaging signals and patient heterogeneity; there is a need for models that integrate clinical priors with imaging and provide calibrated, interpretable predictions for presurgical decision-making.
AI.method: A presurgical outcome model, PreSight, that fuses clinical priors with preoperative MRI and deformation-based morphometry (DBM) and includes a patient-specific weighting module to adapt regional importance; end-to-end, calibrated predictions with explanations and disease-relevant region emphasis.
AI.result: Evaluated on a two-center cohort of 400 subjects; internal validation accuracy 88.89%, external-center test accuracy 85.29%; outperformed clinical, imaging-only, and multimodal baselines; improved probability calibration and decision-curve net benefit; ablation studies confirm DBM and weighting module contributions and patient-specific regional emphasis.
AI.conclusion: Integrating clinical priors with region-adaptive morphometry enables reliable presurgical decision support in routine practice, with robust cross-center performance and interpretable, calibrated predictions.

title: Robust White Blood Cell Classification with Stain-Normalized Decoupled Learning and Ensembling
summary: White blood cell (WBC) classification is fundamental for hematology applications such as infection assessment, leukemia screening, and treatment monitoring. However, real-world WBC datasets present substantial appearance variations caused by staining and scanning conditions, as well as severe class imbalance in which common cell types dominate while rare but clinically important categories are underrepresented. To address these challenges, we propose a stain-normalized, decoupled training framework that first learns transferable representations using instance-balanced sampling, and then rebalances the classifier with class-aware sampling and a hybrid loss combining effective-number weighting and focal modulation. In inference stage, we further enhance robustness by ensembling various trained backbones with test-time augmentation. Our approach achieved the top rank on the leaderboard of the WBCBench 2026: Robust White Blood Cell Classification Challenge at ISBI 2026.

AI.tldr: A stain-normalized decoupled training framework for robust WBC classification that tackles staining variability and severe class imbalance via (1) instance-balanced pretraining for transferable features, (2) class-aware rebalancing with a hybrid loss (effective-number weighting + focal modulation), and (3) test-time ensembling for robustness, achieving top performance on WBCBench 2026.
AI.motivation: Real-world WBC datasets suffer from substantial appearance variations due to staining and scanning, plus severe class imbalance where rare but clinically important cell types are underrepresented, necessitating robust, transferable representations and effective rebalancing strategies.
AI.method: Stage 1: train with instance-balanced sampling and stain normalization to learn transferable representations. Stage 2: rebalance the classifier using class-aware sampling and a hybrid loss combining effective-number weighting and focal modulation. Inference: ensemble multiple trained backbones with test-time augmentation to boost robustness.
AI.result: Achieved the top rank on the WBCBench 2026: Robust White Blood Cell Classification Challenge at ISBI 2026.
AI.conclusion: A decoupled training framework with stain normalization, instance-balanced pretraining, class-aware sampling, and a hybrid loss, complemented by test-time ensembling, yields state-of-the-art performance on a challenging WBC classification benchmark and demonstrates robustness to staining variations and class imbalance.

title: Process Over Outcome: Cultivating Forensic Reasoning for Generalizable Multimodal Manipulation Detection
summary: Recent advances in generative AI have significantly enhanced the realism of multimodal media manipulation, thereby posing substantial challenges to manipulation detection. Existing manipulation detection and grounding approaches predominantly focus on manipulation type classification under result-oriented supervision, which not only lacks interpretability but also tends to overfit superficial artifacts. In this paper, we argue that generalizable detection requires incorporating explicit forensic reasoning, rather than merely classifying a limited set of manipulation types, which fails to generalize to unseen manipulation patterns. To this end, we propose REFORM, a reasoning-driven framework that shifts learning from outcome fitting to process modeling. REFORM adopts a three-stage curriculum that first induces forensic rationales, then aligns reasoning with final judgments, and finally refines logical consistency via reinforcement learning. To support this paradigm, we introduce ROM, a large-scale dataset with rich reasoning annotations. Extensive experiments show that REFORM establishes new state-of-the-art performance with superior generalization, achieving 81.52% ACC on ROM, 76.65% ACC on DGM4, and 74.9 F1 on MMFakeBench.

AI.tldr: A framework REFORM reframes manipulation detection as forensic reasoning, trained via a three-stage curriculum, and backed by ROM, a large-scale reasoning-annotated dataset. It achieves state-of-the-art generalization across ROM, DGM4, and MMFakeBench.
AI.motivation: Existing manipulation detection methods focus on outcome-oriented manipulation-type classification, which lacks interpretability and generalization to unseen manipulation patterns. A reasoning-based approach is proposed to improve generalization by modeling forensic processes.
AI.method: Reform introduces REFORM, a three-stage curriculum: (1) induce forensic rationales; (2) align reasoning with final judgments; (3) refine logical consistency via reinforcement learning. It is supported by ROM, a large-scale dataset with rich reasoning annotations for training and evaluation.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation yields state-of-the-art generalization: 81.52% ACC on ROM, 76.65% ACC on DGM4, and 74.9 F1 on MMFakeBench.
AI.conclusion: Reasoning-driven, curriculum-based learning with RL improves generalization in multimodal manipulation detection; ROM enables robust evaluation and supports a shift from outcome-fitting to process-modeling.

title: FluxMem: Adaptive Hierarchical Memory for Streaming Video Understanding
comment: Accepted at CVPR 2026. Project page: https://yiwengxie.com/FluxMem/
summary: This paper presents FluxMem, a training-free framework for efficient streaming video understanding. FluxMem adaptively compresses redundant visual memory through a hierarchical, two-stage design: (1) a Temporal Adjacency Selection (TAS) module removes redundant visual tokens across adjacent frames, and (2) a Spatial Domain Consolidation (SDC) module further merges spatially repetitive regions within each frame into compact representations. To adapt effectively to dynamic scenes, we introduce a self-adaptive token compression mechanism in both TAS and SDC, which automatically determines the compression rate based on intrinsic scene statistics rather than manual tuning. Extensive experiments demonstrate that FluxMem achieves new state-of-the-art results on existing online video benchmarks, reaching 76.4 on StreamingBench and 67.2 on OVO-Bench under real-time settings, while reducing latency by 69.9% and peak GPU memory by 34.5% on OVO-Bench. Furthermore, it maintains strong offline performance, achieving 73.1 on MLVU while using 65% fewer visual tokens.

AI.tldr: FluxMem is a training-free, two-stage adaptive token compression framework for real-time streaming video understanding that reduces redundancy and resource use while maintaining high accuracy.
AI.motivation: To enable efficient online video understanding under real-time constraints by removing temporal and spatial redundancy without training data or manual tuning.
AI.method: A two-stage design: (1) Temporal Adjacency Selection (TAS) to drop redundant tokens across consecutive frames and (2) Spatial Domain Consolidation (SDC) to merge repetitive regions within each frame. Both stages employ self-adaptive, scene-statistics-driven compression rates, enabling dynamic adaptation to content.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art online performance on StreamingBench (76.4) and OVO-Bench (67.2) in real-time settings, with latency reduced by 69.9% and peak GPU memory by 34.5% on OVO-Bench. Maintains strong offline performance (73.1 on MLVU) with 65% fewer visual tokens.
AI.conclusion: FluxMem demonstrates that training-free, adaptive token compression across temporal and spatial dimensions yields strong online and offline performance with substantial resource savings, making it effective for efficient streaming video understanding.

title: LiftAvatar: Kinematic-Space Completion for Expression-Controlled 3D Gaussian Avatar Animation
summary: We present LiftAvatar, a new paradigm that completes sparse monocular observations in kinematic space (e.g., facial expressions and head pose) and uses the completed signals to drive high-fidelity avatar animation. LiftAvatar is a fine-grained, expression-controllable large-scale video diffusion Transformer that synthesizes high-quality, temporally coherent expression sequences conditioned on single or multiple reference images. The key idea is to lift incomplete input data into a richer kinematic representation, thereby strengthening both reconstruction and animation in downstream 3D avatar pipelines. To this end, we introduce (i) a multi-granularity expression control scheme that combines shading maps with expression coefficients for precise and stable driving, and (ii) a multi-reference conditioning mechanism that aggregates complementary cues from multiple frames, enabling strong 3D consistency and controllability. As a plug-and-play enhancer, LiftAvatar directly addresses the limited expressiveness and reconstruction artifacts of 3D Gaussian Splatting-based avatars caused by sparse kinematic cues in everyday monocular videos. By expanding incomplete observations into diverse pose-expression variations, LiftAvatar also enables effective prior distillation from large-scale video generative models into 3D pipelines, leading to substantial gains. Extensive experiments show that LiftAvatar consistently boosts animation quality and quantitative metrics of state-of-the-art 3D avatar methods, especially under extreme, unseen expressions.

AI.tldr: LiftAvatar is a diffusion-based framework that completes sparse monocular cues into a rich kinematic representation to drive high-fidelity, controllable 3D avatar animation; it leverages multi-granularity expression control and multi-reference conditioning to improve 3D consistency, robustness to unseen expressions, and facilitate distillation from large video models, acting as a plug-in for existing 3D avatar pipelines.
AI.motivation: Address the limited expressiveness and reconstruction artifacts of 3D Gaussian Splatting avatars caused by sparse monocular cues. By lifting incomplete observations into richer kinematic representations and leveraging multi-frame priors, LiftAvatar aims to improve reconstruction, animation quality, and 3D consistency, while enabling prior distillation from large-scale video generative models.
AI.method: A fine-grained, expression-controllable large-scale video diffusion Transformer conditioned on single or multiple reference images. Introduces (i) a multi-granularity expression control scheme that combines shading maps with expression coefficients for precise and stable driving, and (ii) a multi-reference conditioning mechanism that aggregates complementary cues from multiple frames. It is designed as a plug-and-play enhancer for existing 3D avatar pipelines, particularly targeting improvements in expressiveness and artifact reduction in Gaussian Splatting-based avatars.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show LiftAvatar consistently boosts animation quality and quantitative metrics of state-of-the-art 3D avatar methods, with notable gains under extreme or previously unseen expressions, and improved 3D consistency and controllability.
AI.conclusion: LiftAvatar effectively lifts incomplete observations into expressive, controllable 3D avatar animation, serving as a practical enhancer for current 3D avatar pipelines and enabling effective prior distillation from video generative models.

title: NICO-RAG: Multimodal Hypergraph Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Understanding the Nicotine Public Health Crisis
summary: The nicotine addiction public health crisis continues to be pervasive. In this century alone, the tobacco industry has released and marketed new products in an aggressive effort to lure new and young customers for life. Such innovations and product development, namely flavored nicotine or tobacco such as nicotine pouches, have undone years of anti-tobacco campaign work. Past work is limited both in scope and in its ability to connect large-scale data points. Thus, we introduce the Nicotine Innovation Counter-Offensive (NICO) Dataset to provide public health researchers with over 200,000 multimodal samples, including images and text descriptions, on 55 tobacco and nicotine product brands. In addition, to provide public health researchers with factual connections across a large-scale dataset, we propose NICO-RAG, a retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) framework that can retrieve image features without incurring the high-cost of language models, as well as the added cost of processing image tokens with large-scale datasets such as NICO. At construction time, NICO-RAG organizes image- and text-extracted entities and relations into hypergraphs to produce as factual responses as possible. This joint multimodal knowledge representation enables NICO-RAG to retrieve images for query answering not only by visual similarity but also by the semantic similarity of image descriptions. Experimentals show that without needing to process additional tokens from images for over 100 questions, NICO-RAG performs comparably to the state-of-the-art RAG method adapted for images.

AI.tldr: Introduces NICO: a large-scale multimodal dataset (>200k samples across 55 tobacco/nicotine brands) and NICO-RAG: a retrieval-augmented generation framework that uses hypergraphs to fuse image and text information for factual, efficient image-based QA, achieving competitive performance with reduced image-token processing.
AI.motivation: Public health concerns about nicotine/tobacco marketing innovations and the lack of comprehensive multimodal data to counter industry messaging; need for scalable, factual retrieval across large image-text datasets to support research.
AI.method: Construct NICO dataset with images and text descriptions for 55 brands (>200k samples). Develop NICO-RAG, a retrieval-augmented generation framework that retrieves image features without high LM-cost token processing, and organizes image- and text-extracted entities/relations into hypergraphs to enable factual responses. Retrieval considers both visual similarity and semantic similarity of image descriptions.
AI.result: NICO-RAG achieves results comparable to state-of-the-art image-adapted RAG on over 100 questions without processing additional image tokens, demonstrating efficient multimodal retrieval with factual grounding.
AI.conclusion: NICO provides a valuable public health resource and NICO-RAG offers an efficient, scalable approach for multimodal retrieval and factual QA, enabling researchers to better analyze and counter nicotine/tobacco product innovations.

title: WorldStereo: Bridging Camera-Guided Video Generation and Scene Reconstruction via 3D Geometric Memories
summary: Recent advances in foundational Video Diffusion Models (VDMs) have yielded significant progress. Yet, despite the remarkable visual quality of generated videos, reconstructing consistent 3D scenes from these outputs remains challenging, due to limited camera controllability and inconsistent generated content when viewed from distinct camera trajectories. In this paper, we propose WorldStereo, a novel framework that bridges camera-guided video generation and 3D reconstruction via two dedicated geometric memory modules. Formally, the global-geometric memory enables precise camera control while injecting coarse structural priors through incrementally updated point clouds. Moreover, the spatial-stereo memory constrains the model's attention receptive fields with 3D correspondence to focus on fine-grained details from the memory bank. These components enable WorldStereo to generate multi-view-consistent videos under precise camera control, facilitating high-quality 3D reconstruction. Furthermore, the flexible control branch-based WorldStereo shows impressive efficiency, benefiting from the distribution matching distilled VDM backbone without joint training. Extensive experiments across both camera-guided video generation and 3D reconstruction benchmarks demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach. Notably, we show that WorldStereo acts as a powerful world model, tackling diverse scene generation tasks (whether starting from perspective or panoramic images) with high-fidelity 3D results. Models will be released.

AI.tldr: WorldStereo introduces two geometric memory modules to align video diffusion outputs with 3D reconstruction, enabling precise camera control and multi-view consistency in video generation without joint training and with efficient, memory-guided attention.
AI.motivation: Reconstructing consistent 3D scenes from video diffusion outputs is challenged by limited camera controllability and content inconsistency across different viewpoints. A memory-augmented framework is needed to enforce global structure and local detail alignment while maintaining efficiency.
AI.method: Propose WorldStereo with two dedicated geometric memory modules: (1) global-geometric memory for precise camera control and coarse structure via incrementally updated point clouds; (2) spatial-stereo memory to constrain attention with 3D correspondences, guiding fine-grained detail capture. Includes a flexible control-branch and a distribution-matching distilled VDM backbone, enabling efficient, non-joint training.
AI.result: The framework achieves multi-view-consistent video generation under controlled camera trajectories and supports high-quality 3D reconstructions. It performs well across camera-guided and panoramic-from-perspective tasks, and demonstrates efficiency and strong world-model capabilities.
AI.conclusion: WorldStereo effectively bridges camera-guided video generation and 3D reconstruction, delivering high-fidelity 3D results across diverse tasks and inputs, with models to be released.

title: Kiwi-Edit: Versatile Video Editing via Instruction and Reference Guidance
summary: Instruction-based video editing has witnessed rapid progress, yet current methods often struggle with precise visual control, as natural language is inherently limited in describing complex visual nuances. Although reference-guided editing offers a robust solution, its potential is currently bottlenecked by the scarcity of high-quality paired training data. To bridge this gap, we introduce a scalable data generation pipeline that transforms existing video editing pairs into high-fidelity training quadruplets, leveraging image generative models to create synthesized reference scaffolds. Using this pipeline, we construct RefVIE, a large-scale dataset tailored for instruction-reference-following tasks, and establish RefVIE-Bench for comprehensive evaluation. Furthermore, we propose a unified editing architecture, Kiwi-Edit, that synergizes learnable queries and latent visual features for reference semantic guidance. Our model achieves significant gains in instruction following and reference fidelity via a progressive multi-stage training curriculum. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our data and architecture establish a new state-of-the-art in controllable video editing. All datasets, models, and code is released at https://github.com/showlab/Kiwi-Edit.

AI.tldr: Introduces a scalable data generation pipeline to convert video editing data into high-quality quadruplets, enabling RefVIE and RefVIE-Bench, and presents Kiwi-Edit, a unified editing model that uses learnable queries and latent features for instruction-reference-guided video editing, achieving state-of-the-art results.
AI.motivation: Address the scarcity and quality gap of paired training data for instruction-based video editing and the limitations of natural language for precise control; leverage reference guidance and generative-model scaffolds to scale data and improve editing fidelity.
AI.method: 1) Data pipeline converts existing video editing pairs into quadruplets and uses image generative models to synthesize reference scaffolds; 2) Build RefVIE dataset and RefVIE-Bench for evaluation; 3) Propose Kiwi-Edit architecture combining learnable queries with latent visual features to encode reference semantics; 4) Employ a progressive multi-stage training curriculum.
AI.result: Achieves significant improvements in instruction following and reference fidelity, setting new state-of-the-art on controllable video editing across experiments; comprehensive evaluations validate the dataset's utility and the model's effectiveness; all data, models, and code released at the listed GitHub repository.
AI.conclusion: The proposed scalable data generation pipeline and the Kiwi-Edit framework jointly advance controllable video editing by enabling higher-quality instruction-reference guidance and robust generalization; the RefVIE/RefVIE-Bench resources provide a strong foundation for future research in instruction-reference-based editing.

title: ORGAN: Object-Centric Representation Learning using Cycle Consistent Generative Adversarial Networks
summary: Although data generation is often straightforward, extracting information from data is more difficult. Object-centric representation learning can extract information from images in an unsupervised manner. It does so by segmenting an image into its subcomponents: the objects. Each object is then represented in a low-dimensional latent space that can be used for downstream processing. Object-centric representation learning is dominated by autoencoder architectures (AEs). Here, we present ORGAN, a novel approach for object-centric representation learning, which is based on cycle-consistent Generative Adversarial Networks instead. We show that it performs similarly to other state-of-the-art approaches on synthetic datasets, while at the same time being the only approach tested here capable of handling more challenging real-world datasets with many objects and low visual contrast. Complementing these results, ORGAN creates expressive latent space representations that allow for object manipulation. Finally, we show that ORGAN scales well both with respect to the number of objects and the size of the images, giving it a unique edge over current state-of-the-art approaches.

AI.tldr: A cycle-consistent GAN-based object-centric representation learner (ORGAN) that competes with state-of-the-art on synthetic data and outperforms on challenging real-world datasets, with scalable, manipulable latent representations.
AI.motivation: Object-centric representation learning aims to decompose scenes into individual objects in an unsupervised manner but current AE-dominated approaches may struggle with real-world scenes, especially with many objects and low visual contrast. A scalable method that yields manipulable latent slots is needed.
AI.method: ORGAN uses cycle-consistent Generative Adversarial Networks to induce object-centric latent representations. It factors scenes into object-level components, learns latent spaces per object, and enables manipulation of objects. The approach is claimed to scale with the number of objects and image size while handling challenging real-world data.
AI.result: On synthetic datasets, ORGAN achieves performance similar to other state-of-the-art methods. On more challenging real-world datasets with many objects and low contrast, ORGAN is the only method among those tested that performs effectively. The latent representations are expressive, enabling object manipulation.
AI.conclusion: ORGAN provides a competitive and scalable alternative to AE-centric object-centric methods, with demonstrated ability to handle complex real-world scenes and support object-level manipulation.

title: MMNavAgent: Multi-Magnification WSI Navigation Agent for Clinically Consistent Whole-Slide Analysis
summary: Recent AI navigation approaches aim to improve Whole-Slide Image (WSI) diagnosis by modeling spatial exploration and selecting diagnostically relevant regions, yet most operate at a single fixed magnification or rely on predefined magnification traversal. In clinical practice, pathologists examine slides across multiple magnifications and selectively inspect only necessary scales, dynamically integrating global and cellular evidence in a sequential manner.
