This mismatch prevents existing methods from modeling cross-magnification interactions and adaptive magnification selection inherent to real diagnostic workflows. To these, we propose a clinically consistent Multi-Magnification WSI Navigation Agent (MMNavAgent) that explicitly models multi magnification interaction and adaptive magnification selection. Specifically, we introduce a Cross-Magnification navigation Tool (CMT) that aggregates contextual information from adjacent magnifications to enhance discriminative representations along the navigation path. We further introduce a Magnification Selection Tool (MST) that leverages memory-driven reasoning within the agent framework to enable interactive and adaptive magnification selection, mimicking the sequential decision process of pathologists. Extensive experiments on a public dataset demonstrate improved diagnostic performance, with 1.45% gain of AUC and 2.93% gain of BACC over a non-agent baseline. Code will be public upon acceptance.

AI.tldr: Introduces MMNavAgent for multi-magnification whole-slide image navigation with Cross-Magnification Tool (CMT) and Magnification Selection Tool (MST) to simulate pathologist-like sequential magnification decisions, achieving improved AUC and BACC over a non-agent baseline.
AI.motivation: Current AI navigation for WSI diagnosis typically uses a single fixed magnification or predefined magnification traversal, failing to capture cross-magnification interactions and dynamic scale selection central to clinical workflow. The work aims to model multi-magnification interactions and adaptive magnification selection to better reflect how pathologists inspect slides.
AI.method: Proposes MMNavAgent consisting of: (1) Cross-Magnification navigation Tool (CMT) that aggregates contextual information from neighboring magnifications to enhance discriminative representations along the navigation path; (2) Magnification Selection Tool (MST) that uses memory-driven reasoning to enable interactive and adaptive magnification selection in a sequential decision process. Evaluations on a public dataset show improvements.
AI.result: Extensive experiments demonstrate performance gains of 1.45% in AUC and 2.93% in BACC over a non-agent baseline.
AI.conclusion: The framework offers clinically consistent multi-magnification navigation for WSI diagnosis and provides code upon acceptance, suggesting robustness and reproducibility of the approach.

title: A 3D mesh convolution-based autoencoder for geometry compression
summary: In this paper, we introduce a novel 3D mesh convolution-based autoencoder for geometry compression, able to deal with irregular mesh data without requiring neither preprocessing nor manifold/watertightness conditions. The proposed approach extracts meaningful latent representations by learning features directly from the mesh faces, while preserving connectivity through dedicated pooling and unpooling operations. The encoder compresses the input mesh into a compact base mesh space, which ensures that the latent space remains comparable. The decoder reconstructs the original connectivity and restores the compressed geometry to its full resolution. Extensive experiments on multi-class datasets demonstrate that our method outperforms state-of-the-art approaches in both 3D mesh geometry reconstruction and latent space classification tasks. Code available at: github.com/germainGB/MeshConv3D

AI.tldr: A 3D mesh convolution-based autoencoder for geometry compression that handles irregular meshes without preprocessing, learning latent representations from mesh faces, preserving connectivity via pooling/unpooling, and achieving state-of-the-art geometry reconstruction and latent-space classification across multi-class datasets.
AI.motivation: To enable robust compression and analysis of irregular 3D meshes without requiring preprocessing steps or watertight/manifold constraints, while maintaining meaningful latent representations.
AI.method: Introduce a 3D mesh convolution autoencoder that operates on mesh faces, with connectivity-preserving pooling/unpooling; encoder maps to a compact base mesh latent space; decoder reconstructs connectivity and geometry to full resolution; designed to work on irregular meshes.
AI.result: Outperforms state-of-the-art methods in both 3D mesh geometry reconstruction and latent-space classification across multi-class datasets; code provided at GitHub.
AI.conclusion: The proposed MeshConv3D-based autoencoder offers robust, preprocessing-free geometry compression for irregular meshes with transferable latent representations and strong reconstruction/classification performance.

title: Stereo-Inertial Poser: Towards Metric-Accurate Shape-Aware Motion Capture Using Sparse IMUs and a Single Stereo Camera
comment: The code, data, and supplementary materials are available at \url{https://sites.google.com/view/stereo-inertial-poser}. Accepted to ICRA 2026
summary: Recent advancements in visual-inertial motion capture systems have demonstrated the potential of combining monocular cameras with sparse inertial measurement units (IMUs) as cost-effective solutions, which effectively mitigate occlusion and drift issues inherent in single-modality systems. However, they are still limited by metric inaccuracies in global translations stemming from monocular depth ambiguity, and shape-agnostic local motion estimations that ignore anthropometric variations. We present Stereo-Inertial Poser, a real-time motion capture system that leverages a single stereo camera and six IMUs to estimate metric-accurate and shape-aware 3D human motion. By replacing the monocular RGB with stereo vision, our system resolves depth ambiguity through calibrated baseline geometry, enabling direct 3D keypoint extraction and body shape parameter estimation. IMU data and visual cues are fused for predicting drift-compensated joint positions and root movements, while a novel shape-aware fusion module dynamically harmonizes anthropometry variations with global translations. Our end-to-end pipeline achieves over 200 FPS without optimization-based post-processing, enabling real-time deployment. Quantitative evaluations across various datasets demonstrate state-of-the-art performance. Qualitative results show our method produces drift-free global translation under a long recording time and reduces foot-skating effects.

AI.tldr: A real-time Stereo-Inertial Poser uses a stereo camera and six IMUs to deliver metric-accurate, shape-aware 3D human motion at 200+ FPS, by resolving depth with stereo and fusing anthropometry-aware cues without post-processing.
AI.motivation: Monocular depth ambiguity leads to biased global translations; shape-agnostic estimates ignore body size variations; a real-time, drift-free, and shape-consistent mocap system is needed.
AI.method: One stereo camera plus six IMUs; direct 3D keypoint extraction from calibrated stereo, plus body shape parameter estimation; fusion of IMU data and visual cues for drift-compensated joint/root motion; a shape-aware fusion module harmonizes anthropometry with global translations; end-to-end pipeline with no optimization-based post-processing.
AI.result: Quantitative tests show state-of-the-art performance across datasets; qualitative results indicate drift-free global translation over long recordings and reduced foot-skating; capable of real-time deployment (>200 FPS).
AI.conclusion: Stereo-IMU fusion with a shape-aware module achieves metric-accurate and shape-aware 3D human motion capture in real time, overcoming monocular depth ambiguity and anthropometry limitations.

title: SimRecon: SimReady Compositional Scene Reconstruction from Real Videos
summary: Compositional scene reconstruction seeks to create object-centric representations rather than holistic scenes from real-world videos, which is natively applicable for simulation and interaction. Conventional compositional reconstruction approaches primarily emphasize on visual appearance and show limited generalization ability to real-world scenarios. In this paper, we propose SimRecon, a framework that realizes a "Perception-Generation-Simulation" pipeline towards cluttered scene reconstruction, which first conducts scene-level semantic reconstruction from video input, then performs single-object generation, and finally assembles these assets in the simulator. However, naively combining these three stages leads to visual infidelity of generated assets and physical implausibility of the final scene, a problem particularly severe for complex scenes. Thus, we further propose two bridging modules between the three stages to address this problem. To be specific, for the transition from Perception to Generation, critical for visual fidelity, we introduce Active Viewpoint Optimization, which actively searches in 3D space to acquire optimal projected images as conditions for single-object completion. Moreover, for the transition from Generation to Simulation, essential for physical plausibility, we propose a Scene Graph Synthesizer, which guides the construction from scratch in 3D simulators, mirroring the native, constructive principle of the real world. Extensive experiments on the ScanNet dataset validate our method's superior performance over previous state-of-the-art approaches.

AI.tldr: SimRecon introduces a perception-generation-simulation pipeline for cluttered scene reconstruction, adding two bridging modules—Active Viewpoint Optimization and Scene Graph Synthesizer—to improve visual fidelity and physical plausibility. Validated on ScanNet with superior performance over prior work.
AI.motivation: Conventional compositional reconstruction focuses on visual appearance and lacks robust generalization to real-world clutter; object-centric reconstruction must ensure both fidelity and physical plausibility when assembled in a simulator.
AI.method: A three-stage pipeline: (1) scene-level semantic reconstruction from video input; (2) single-object generation; (3) assembly in a simulator. Two bridging modules: Active Viewpoint Optimization (selects optimal 3D viewpoints to improve object completion) and Scene Graph Synthesizer (guides 3D-world construction from generated objects to ensure plausible scene graphs).
AI.result: Extensive experiments on the ScanNet dataset show superior performance over previous state-of-the-art methods.
AI.conclusion: Bridging perception, generation, and simulation with Active Viewpoint Optimization and Scene Graph Synthesizer yields more visually faithful and physically plausible cluttered scene reconstructions, enabling robust object-centric representations in simulators.

title: OnlineX: Unified Online 3D Reconstruction and Understanding with Active-to-Stable State Evolution
comment: Accepted by CVPR Finding 2026 (Project page: https://xiac20.github.io/OnlineX/)
summary: Recent advances in generalizable 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) have enabled rapid 3D scene reconstruction within seconds, eliminating the need for per-scene optimization. However, existing methods primarily follow an offline reconstruction paradigm, lacking the capacity for continuous reconstruction, which limits their applicability to online scenarios such as robotics and VR/AR. In this paper, we introduce OnlineX, a feed-forward framework that reconstructs both 3D visual appearance and language fields in an online manner using only streaming images. A key challenge in online formulation is the cumulative drift issue, which is rooted in the fundamental conflict between two opposing roles of the memory state: an active role that constantly refreshes to capture high-frequency local geometry, and a stable role that conservatively accumulates and preserves the long-term global structure. To address this, we introduce a decoupled active-to-stable state evolution paradigm. Our framework decouples the memory state into a dedicated active state and a persistent stable state, and then cohesively fuses the information from the former into the latter to achieve both fidelity and stability. Moreover, we jointly model visual appearance and language fields and incorporate an implicit Gaussian fusion module to enhance reconstruction quality. Experiments on mainstream datasets demonstrate that our method consistently outperforms prior work in novel view synthesis and semantic understanding, showcasing robust performance across input sequences of varying lengths with real-time inference speed.

AI.tldr: OnlineX: a feed-forward online 3D reconstruction framework that jointly models 3D appearance and language fields from streaming images, using a decoupled active/stable memory to reduce drift, enabling real-time novel view synthesis and semantic understanding.
AI.motivation: To address the lack of online, continuous reconstruction in generalizable 3D Gaussian Splatting, which currently relies on offline per-scene optimization. The drift arises from conflicting memory roles (active refreshing vs. stable long-term structure).
AI.method: Introduce a decoupled memory architecture with separate active and persistent stable states. Fuse information from the active state into the stable state. Jointly model visual appearance and language fields, augmented by an implicit Gaussian fusion module, enabling feed-forward online inference from streaming images.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on mainstream datasets shows consistent improvements over prior work in novel view synthesis and semantic understanding, with robustness across sequences of varying length and real-time inference speed.
AI.conclusion: OnlineX demonstrates effective, real-time online 3D reconstruction with high fidelity and stability by decoupling memory and jointly modeling appearance and language fields, suitable for online robotics, VR/AR, and related applications.

title: 3D Field of Junctions: A Noise-Robust, Training-Free Structural Prior for Volumetric Inverse Problems
summary: Volume denoising is a foundational problem in computational imaging, as many 3D imaging inverse problems face high levels of measurement noise. Inspired by the strong 2D image denoising properties of Field of Junctions (ICCV 2021), we propose a novel, fully volumetric 3D Field of Junctions (3D FoJ) representation that optimizes a junction of 3D wedges that best explain each 3D patch of a full volume, while encouraging consistency between overlapping patches. In addition to direct volume denoising, we leverage our 3D FoJ representation as a structural prior that: (i) requires no training data, and thus precludes the risk of hallucination, (ii) preserves and enhances sharp edge and corner structures in 3D, even under low signal to noise ratio (SNR), and (iii) can be used as a drop-in denoising representation via projected or proximal gradient descent for any volumetric inverse problem with low SNR. We demonstrate successful volume reconstruction and denoising with 3D FoJ across three diverse 3D imaging tasks with low-SNR measurements: low-dose X-ray computed tomography (CT), cryogenic electron tomography (cryo-ET), and denoising point clouds such as those from lidar in adverse weather. Across these challenging low-SNR volumetric imaging problems, 3D FoJ outperforms a mixture of classical and neural methods.

AI.tldr: Proposes a fully volumetric 3D Field of Junctions (3D FoJ) representation for volume denoising and as a training-free structural prior. It optimizes a junction of 3D wedges per patch with consistency across overlapping patches, usable via projected/proximal gradient methods as a drop-in prior for low-SNR volumetric inverse problems. Demonstrated on low-dose CT, cryo-ET, and lidar-like point clouds, outperforming classical and neural methods.
AI.motivation: Robust denoising of 3D volumes under high measurement noise while avoiding training-data dependence and hallucinations; preserve sharp edges and corners in 3D across diverse modalities.
AI.method: Introduce 3D FoJ: a representation that models each 3D patch as a junction of three wedges; enforce consistency between overlapping patches; adopt optimization schemes (projected or proximal gradient descent) to fit the 3D FoJ prior for denoising; handy as a drop-in prior for any low-SNR volumetric inverse problem without requiring training data.
AI.result: Empirical validation across low-SNR tasks: low-dose CT, cryo-ET, and lidar-like point clouds. 3D FoJ denoising outperforms a mix of classical and neural methods, demonstrating effective volume reconstruction and edge-preserving denoising.
AI.conclusion: 3D FoJ provides a powerful, training-free, structure-based prior for volumetric denoising and inverse problems. It preserves sharp geometric features, reduces hallucinations, and serves as a versatile drop-in denoising representation for various low-SNR 3D imaging modalities.

title: Bridging the gap between Performance and Interpretability: An Explainable Disentangled Multimodal Framework for Cancer Survival Prediction
summary: While multimodal survival prediction models are increasingly more accurate, their complexity often reduces interpretability, limiting insight into how different data sources influence predictions. To address this, we introduce DIMAFx, an explainable multimodal framework for cancer survival prediction that produces disentangled, interpretable modality-specific and modality-shared representations from histopathology whole-slide images and transcriptomics data. Across multiple cancer cohorts, DIMAFx achieves state-of-the-art performance and improved representation disentanglement. Leveraging its interpretable design and SHapley Additive exPlanations, DIMAFx systematically reveals key multimodal interactions and the biological information encoded in the disentangled representations. In breast cancer survival prediction, the most predictive features contain modality-shared information, including one capturing solid tumor morphology contextualized primarily by late estrogen response, where higher-grade morphology aligned with pathway upregulation and increased risk, consistent with known breast cancer biology. Key modality-specific features capture microenvironmental signals from interacting adipose and stromal morphologies. These results show that multimodal models can overcome the traditional trade-off between performance and explainability, supporting their application in precision medicine.

AI.tldr: DIMAFx is an explainable multimodal framework for cancer survival prediction that learns disentangled modality-specific and shared representations from histopathology WSIs and transcriptomics, achieving strong performance and interpretability via SHAP.
AI.motivation: Address the interpretability challenge of multimodal survival models by disentangling modality-specific and shared signals and enabling biologically meaningful explanations.
AI.method: DIMAFx learns disentangled latent spaces across histopathology and transcriptomics data, with SHAP-based explanations to reveal multimodal interactions; evaluated across multiple cancer cohorts, including a breast cancer case linking morphology and estrogen response.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance with improved representation disentanglement; provides interpretable insights into key multimodal interactions; identifies modality-shared features (e.g., morphology linked to late estrogen response) and modality-specific microenvironment signals (adipose/stromal morphologies).
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates that high predictive performance and interpretability are not mutually exclusive in multimodal cancer survival models, supporting their use in precision medicine.

title: GeoDiT: Point-Conditioned Diffusion Transformer for Satellite Image Synthesis
summary: We introduce GeoDiT, a diffusion transformer designed for text-to-satellite image generation with point-based control. Existing controlled satellite image generative models often require pixel-level maps that are time-consuming to acquire, yet semantically limited. To address this limitation, we introduce a novel point-based conditioning framework that controls the generation process through the spatial location of the points and the textual description associated with each point, providing semantically rich control signals. This approach enables flexible, annotation-friendly, and computationally simple inference for satellite image generation. To this end, we introduce an adaptive local attention mechanism that effectively regularizes the attention scores based on the input point queries. We systematically evaluate various domain-specific design choices for training GeoDiT, including the selection of satellite image representation for alignment and geolocation representation for conditioning. Our experiments demonstrate that GeoDiT achieves impressive generation performance, surpassing the state-of-the-art remote sensing generative models.

AI.tldr: GeoDiT introduces a diffusion transformer for text-to-satellite image generation with point-based conditioning (location + text per point), enabling semantically rich, annotation-friendly control with adaptive local attention. It achieves state-of-the-art results while maintaining simple inference.
AI.motivation: Current satellite image generators rely on pixel-level maps, which are costly to acquire and semantically limited. There is a need for flexible, annotation-friendly control signals that are easy to obtain and computationally efficient.
AI.method: A point-based conditioning framework where each point provides a spatial location and an associated textual description. An adaptive local attention mechanism regularizes attention scores based on point queries. The study also systematically compares domain-specific design choices (image representation for alignment, geolocation conditioning).
AI.result: GeoDiT achieves impressive generation performance, surpassing state-of-the-art remote sensing generative models. It enables flexible, annotation-friendly, and computationally simple inference for satellite image generation, with effective attention regularization.
AI.conclusion: Point-based conditioning combined with adaptive local attention is an effective strategy for controllable satellite image generation, achieving strong performance while reducing annotation burden and computational complexity.

title: HiFi-Inpaint: Towards High-Fidelity Reference-Based Inpainting for Generating Detail-Preserving Human-Product Images
summary: Human-product images, which showcase the integration of humans and products, play a vital role in advertising, e-commerce, and digital marketing. The essential challenge of generating such images lies in ensuring the high-fidelity preservation of product details. Among existing paradigms, reference-based inpainting offers a targeted solution by leveraging product reference images to guide the inpainting process. However, limitations remain in three key aspects: the lack of diverse large-scale training data, the struggle of current models to focus on product detail preservation, and the inability of coarse supervision for achieving precise guidance. To address these issues, we propose HiFi-Inpaint, a novel high-fidelity reference-based inpainting framework tailored for generating human-product images. HiFi-Inpaint introduces Shared Enhancement Attention (SEA) to refine fine-grained product features and Detail-Aware Loss (DAL) to enforce precise pixel-level supervision using high-frequency maps. Additionally, we construct a new dataset, HP-Image-40K, with samples curated from self-synthesis data and processed with automatic filtering. Experimental results show that HiFi-Inpaint achieves state-of-the-art performance, delivering detail-preserving human-product images.

AI.tldr: HiFi-Inpaint is a high-fidelity, reference-based inpainting framework for human-product images that uses Shared Enhancement Attention (SEA) and a Detail-Aware Loss (DAL) to preserve product details, supported by a new HP-Image-40K dataset, achieving state-of-the-art, detail-preserving results.
AI.motivation: The generation of human-product images requires meticulous preservation of product details, which is hard for existing methods due to limited large-scale data, inadequate focus on product detail preservation, and coarse supervision. Reference-based inpainting offers targeted guidance but still falls short in these aspects.
AI.method: HiFi-Inpaint introduces Shared Enhancement Attention (SEA) to refine fine-grained product features and Detail-Aware Loss (DAL) to enforce precise pixel-level supervision via high-frequency maps. A new dataset, HP-Image-40K, is constructed from self-synthesis data and filtered automatically to support training.
AI.result: Experimental results demonstrate state-of-the-art performance with enhanced detail preservation in human-product images, indicating effective guidance from product references and improved supervision.
AI.conclusion: The proposed HiFi-Inpaint advances high-fidelity, reference-guided inpainting for human-product images by focusing on product detail preservation through SEA and DAL, supported by a large-scale dataset that facilitates robust training and evaluation.
